source.title: Ultrahigh-throughput screening-assisted in vivo directed evolution for enzyme engineering.

initial_request.research_problem: Classical directed evolution is labor- and time-intensive due to manual steps and limited screening throughput.
initial_request.known_context: Classical directed evolution methods rely on manual operation for rounds of stepwise processes, including DNA extraction, in vitro mutagenesis, transformation and selection, which are usually labour intensive and limit the efficiency, scale and depth of evolution.
constraints: Must achieve high-throughput screening
Must enable continuous in vivo evolution
Must have tunable mutagenesis
quantifiable_goals: Increase mutation rate over wild-type
Achieve >20% activity improvement in target enzymes
Enable FACS-based screening

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Classical directed evolution is labor- and time-intensive due to manual steps and limited screening throughput.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need for an ultrahigh-throughput in vivo continuous directed evolution system with thermosensitive inducible tunability.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Temperature-controlled expression of error-prone Pol I can enable in vivo targeted mutagenesis of plasmids.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Develop an in vivo continuous evolution system based on error-prone DNA polymerase I (Pol I*) under control of a thermosensitive repressor.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Hypothesis formulated that thermosensitive repressor cI857 can regulate Pol I* expression.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need to evolve a tighter repressor to achieve better regulation.

evidence.quote_or_span: classical directed evolution methods rely on manual operation for rounds of stepwise processes, including DNA extraction, in vitro mutagenesis, transformation and selection, which are usually labour intensive and limit the efficiency, scale and depth of evolution

an ultrahigh throughput in vivo continuous directed evolution system with thermosensitive inducible tunability, which is based on error-prone DNA polymerase expression modulated by engineered thermal-responsive repressor cl857

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Wild-type cI857 repressor provides some temperature sensitivity but may not be tight enough.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need tighter regulation of Pol I* expression to minimize background mutagenesis at low temperature.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Evolving cI857 can yield a mutant with higher induction ratio.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Evolve cI857 to obtain cI857* with mutations ΔT57, A400T, T418A.
evolution_trajectory.observation: cI857* achieved tighter regulation; specific induction fold values not directly quoted in provided evidence.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Use cI857* to construct a two-plasmid system.

evidence.quote_or_span: Compared with wild-type cI857, the cI857 mutant (cI857\*) from the M1 strain has three changes in the DNA sequence, including two substitutions and one deletion ( $\Delta$ T57, A400T and T418A).
The fluorescence intensity of mutant M1 and wild-type strain after 3 h (a) and 6 h (b) of fermentation in the shake flask at 30 °C/37 °C.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: cI857* provides tight regulation, but a system architecture is needed.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to separate mutagenesis machinery from target gene to avoid interference.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A two-plasmid system with Pol I* on a low-copy plasmid and target gene on a ColE1 plasmid will enable targeted mutagenesis.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Construct pSC101 (low-copy) with pol I* under PR-cI857* control, and pET28a (ColE1 ori) with target gene.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Two-plasmid system built.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate mutagenesis regulation with reversion assay.

evidence.quote_or_span: Previously, a highly mutagenic DNA polymerase I (Pol I D424A I709N A759R, Pol I\*) was reported to erroneously replicate a region located downstream of the origin of replication (ori), relying on the Pol I specifically required for ColE1 plasmid replication in E.
Compared with wild-type cI857, the cI857 mutant (cI857\*) from the M1 strain has three changes in the DNA sequence, including two substitutions and one deletion ( $\Delta$ T57, A400T and T418A).

an ultrahigh throughput in vivo continuous directed evolution system with thermosensitive inducible tunability, which is based on error-prone DNA polymerase expression modulated by engineered thermal-responsive repressor cl857
cI857 is a temperature-sensitive mutant (cI A67T) of the cI repressor protein from $\lambda$ phage.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Two-plasmid system constructed but mutagenesis regulation unverified.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to confirm temperature-dependent mutagenesis and distance effect from ori.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Reversion of a TAA stop codon in antibiotic resistance genes will indicate mutation rate.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform reversion assay using β-lactamase and aadA with TAA stop codons at varying distances from ori.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Low reversion at 30°C, high at 37°C; mutation rate decreased with distance from ori; 41 times higher reversion rate at 250 bp.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Mutation rate still insufficient; consider disabling MMR.

evidence.quote_or_span: the distance from ori impacted the mutation rate
The fluorescence intensity of mutant M1 and wild-type strain after 3 h (a) and 6 h (b) of fermentation in the shake flask at 30 °C/37 °C.
Approximately 50 resistant reversion mutants at a distance of 250 bp from ori were randomly selected and from position 100 to 750 bp downstream of ori was sequenced.

TtgR–P $_{ttgABC}$ system, which is responsible for multidrug recognition and especially efficient in responding to different flavonoids, is a native transcription regulatory system from Pseudomonas putida DOT–T1E [41, 42].

evolution_trajectory.state_before: MutS60 strain constructed but combined effect with Pol I* unknown.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to determine optimal temperature and mutation rate with both Pol I* and MutS60.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Higher temperature will increase mutation rate due to Pol I* activity and MutS60 inactivation.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Measure reversion rate at various temperatures with Pol I* and MutS60.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Optimal plasmid mutation rate at 43°C: ~638-fold higher than wild-type with Pol I at 250 bp; mutation rate 6.77×10⁻⁸ per nt per generation; ~70-fold genomic mutation rate increase.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Characterize mutational spectrum and apply to enzyme evolution.

evidence.quote_or_span: approximately 638 times that of the wild-type strain with Pol I at 250 bp
the mutation rate of the E. coli genome was increased by about 70-fold during mutagenesis

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Mutagenesis system characterized, ready for enzyme evolution.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to demonstrate system can improve enzyme activity.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Iterative mutagenesis and enrichment will yield α-amylase variants with improved growth on starch.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Evolve BLA (α-amylase from Bacillus licheniformis) displayed on E. coli surface; perform 25 rounds of mutagenesis-enrichment.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Growth rate in enrichment medium increased with passages; after 1 round, OD600 reached 1.5 at 30 h.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Screen enriched library with droplet microfluidics.

evidence.quote_or_span: as the number of consecutive passages increased, the growth rate in enrichment medium showed a gradual increasing trend

b Growth of the experimental strain and the control strains cultured for 30 h in liquid enrichment medium containing 2% starch as the sole carbon source after mutagenesis, and the initial $OD_{600}$ was unified to 0.2.
4b, the $OD_{600}$ of cultures after 1 round of mutagenesis–enrichment was significantly higher than that of the control strains, reaching up to 1.5 at 30 h.
Compared with wild-type cI857, the cI857 mutant (cI857\*) from the M1 strain has three changes in the DNA sequence, including two substitutions and one deletion ( $\Delta$ T57, A400T and T418A).
Single cell droplets were then collected and incubated in a 1 mL syringe at 25 or 30 °C.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Enriched α-amylase library obtained, but need high-throughput screening.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to identify individual improved variants from large library.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Droplet microfluidic FACS can sort top variants based on fluorescence.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Encapsulate cells in droplets with fluorogenic substrate, sort top 0.1% fluorescent droplets from ~3,000,000 droplets.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 0.1% of droplets sorted; 72% of sorted cells showed >20% increased activity compared to wild-type.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Identify and validate top mutant.

evidence.quote_or_span: 0.1% of droplets with fluorescence signals exceeding the set threshold were sorted from approximately 3,000,000 droplets
Seventy-two percent of sorted cells showed over 20% increased activity compared with the wild-type stain
Single cell droplets were then collected and incubated in a 1 mL syringe at 25 or 30 °C.
1% of droplets with fluorescence signals exceeding the set threshold were sorted from approximately 3,000,000 droplets in a short time.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Sorted α-amylase variants show improved fluorescence, but specific mutations and activity need confirmation.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to identify the mutation responsible and quantify activity improvement.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: The N473Y mutation is responsible for activity increase.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Sequence sorted clones, retransform N473Y mutant, and measure fermentation activity.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Retransformed strain carrying mutant BLA (N473Y) showed 48.3% activity increase; attributed to modestly increased specific activity and stability.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Apply system to biosynthetic pathway evolution.

evidence.quote_or_span: the fermentation activity of retransformed strain carrying mutant BLA (N473Y) increased by 48.3%
the combined effects of modestly increased specific enzyme activity and stability may contribute to the overall improvement
med strain carrying mutant BLA (N473Y) increased by 48.3%, due to a nucleotide change at 217 bp downstream of ColE1 ori
the combined effects of modestly increased specific enzyme activity and stability may contribute to the overall improvement in enzymatic activity

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Mutagenesis system proven for single enzyme, but pathway evolution requires product-specific screening.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need a biosensor to couple resveratrol production to fluorescence for FACS.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: TtgR-based biosensor can specifically detect resveratrol and drive mCherry expression.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Develop TtgR-PttgABC biosensor with mCherry reporter.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Biosensor showed excellent specificity for resveratrol over p-coumaric acid; fluorescence correlated with resveratrol titer.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Use biosensor for FACS-based evolution of resveratrol pathway.

evidence.quote_or_span: To realize the in vivo evolution of these key enzymes, a biosensor-based FACS method was established
The induction was much lower in response to p-coumaric acid than to resveratrol, which demonstrated the excellent specificity of this sensor for resveratrol
The fluorescence intensity was positively correlated with the titer of resveratrol, which exhibited that the biosensor could be used in resveratrol biosynthesis pathway evolution
Different final concentrations of p-coumaric acid and resveratrol were added into LB medium for 24-deep-well-plate fermentation at 30 °C.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Biosensor validated, ready for pathway evolution.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to evolve resveratrol production strain and identify improved variants.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Iterative mutagenesis and FACS sorting will enrich cells with higher resveratrol production.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform 16 mutagenesis passages, sort top 0.592‰ fluorescent cells (5,000 cells), retransform top 11 plasmids for verification.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Over 60% of sorted cells showed improved fluorescence; top 11 strains retransformed.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Analyze mutations in improved strains.

evidence.quote_or_span: After 16 consecutive mutagenesis passages, the cells with high fluorescence intensity were sorted from the saturated cultures by flow cytometry. A total of 5,000 cells were collected at a 0.592‰ sorting threshold. Over 60% of the cells sorted from the libraries exhibited improved fluorescence intensity compared with the starting strain
The collected cells were incubated on LB plates containing 900 mg/L p-coumaric acid.
Plasmids were extracted from the top 11 strains with highest fluorescence intensity, and retransformed into wild-type E. coli BL21 (DE3) to verify the increase in resveratrol production.
About 170 single colonies with darkest red were selected for 24-deep-well-plate fermentation at 30 °C.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Improved resveratrol production strains isolated, but mutations unknown.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to identify mutations responsible for improved phenotype.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Mutations may be in enzyme genes or plasmid backbone.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Sequence plasmids from top strains; construct test plasmids with individual mutations.
evolution_trajectory.observation: All improved plasmids contained ColE1 ori C148T and Δ5526-6717 deletion (mpETDuet); no mutations in enzyme genes; mpETDuet led to higher resveratrol production via increased mRNA levels (4-fold for STS, 2.9-fold for double mutant).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Acknowledge limitations of the system.

evidence.quote_or_span: the relative mRNA level of STS in retransformed stains harboring mpETDuet was approximately 4 times higher than that in the wild type
the fluorescence intensity of mpETDuet was increased, by 28%
the double mutant (mpETDuet) was 2.9-fold mRNA level of the control
the deletion included the primosome assembly signal (pas) site, probably resulting in more durable Pol I replication and mutation in sequences located further from ori
Interestingly, two mutations, the ColE1 ori C148T mutation and the $\Delta$ 5526-6717 deletion, were identified in all plasmids; this double mutant was named mpETDuet.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: System demonstrated but with notable limitations.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Low mutation rate limits sequence space exploration; off-target genomic mutations occur; beneficial mutations may occur in non-coding regions.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: These limitations must be addressed for broader applicability.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Document limitations: low mutation rate, off-target genomic mutations requiring plasmid retransformation, and non-coding mutations masking protein improvements.
evolution_trajectory.observation: System has low mutation rate compared to advanced tools; genomic mutations occur; non-coding mutations can dominate.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Future work needed to overcome limitations.

evidence.quote_or_span: This limited mutational density had a negative impact on the exploration of sequence space and evolution speed.
The resulting undesirable mutations could occur in genes essential for cell survival and affect screening for beneficial mutations

The resulting mutants could bring desired phenotype changes unrelated to significant improvements in target protein properties, such as mutations occurring in the promoter, ribosome binding site and other regions associated with gene transcription and expression regulation, and thereby mask other variants carrying mutations within the protein-coding sequence

metrics: Percentage of cells with >20% activity increase
success_verification.final_conclusion: The ultrahigh-throughput screening-assisted in vivo directed evolution system successfully evolved α-amylase (N473Y, 48.3% improvement) and resveratrol pathway (1.7-fold improvement via plasmid backbone mutations), but limitations include low mutation rate, off-target genomic mutations, and non-coding mutations masking protein improvements.
limitations: Low mutation rate (6.77×10⁻⁸ per base per generation) limits sequence space exploration.
Off-target genomic mutations require plasmid retransformation between rounds.
Beneficial mutations may occur in non-coding regions rather than protein-coding sequences.

source.title: High-throughput screening of catalytically active inclusion bodies using laboratory automation and Bayesian optimization

initial_request.research_problem: No a priori prediction possible for best linker/tag combination for CatIBs, necessitating extensive screening.
initial_request.known_context: Catalytically active inclusion bodies (CatIBs) retain substantial catalytic activity but their formation and activity depend on the combination of aggregation-inducing tag, linker length/type, and N-/C-terminal fusion, with no general design rules.
constraints: High-throughput screening required due to large combinatorial space
Manual screening is labor-intensive (approx. 40 h for 48 variants)
input_data: 63 BsGDH-CatIB variants designed with different tags, linkers, and fusion orientations
quantifiable_goals: Identify best performing CatIB variant with >95% probability
Reduce manual workload by >80%
Achieve high reproducibility (RSD < 5%)

evolution_trajectory.state_before: No a priori prediction possible for best linker/tag combination for CatIBs, necessitating extensive screening.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which combination of aggregation-inducing tag, linker, and fusion orientation yields the best CatIB performance for BsGDH?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A semi-automated cloning workflow can rapidly generate a large library of CatIB variants for screening.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Implement a semi-automated cloning workflow using Golden Gate Assembly to generate 63 BsGDH-CatIB variants.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Semi-automated cloning workflow implemented for fast generation of 63 CatIB variants.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Cloning failures observed for some variants, requiring investigation.

evidence.quote_or_span: no a priori prediction is possible so far to indicate the best combination thereof. Consequently, extensive screening is required to find the best performing CatIB variant.
a semi-automated cloning workflow was implemented and used for fast generation of 63 CatIB variants
For the generation of 96 CatIB variants only 11 h of manual work are needed
Due to the successful automation of the CatIB screening, which enables high-throughput testing in the context of DBTL cycles, the workflow could be applied to analyze all 63 BsGDH-CatIB variants.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: 63 CatIB variants designed, cloning attempted.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Some variants may not be clonable due to non-viable phenotypes.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Failed cloning attempts indicate non-viable phenotypes.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Document cloning failures and proceed with successfully cloned variants.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 13 CatIB variants could not be cloned within three attempts, potentially due to non-viable phenotype.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Proceed to cultivation of successfully cloned variants.

evidence.quote_or_span: 13 CatIB variants could not be successfully cloned within three attempts, potentially due to a non-viable phenotype.
a semi-automated cloning workflow was implemented and used for fast generation of 63 CatIB variants

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Successfully cloned CatIB variants ready for cultivation.

evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: unknown
evolution_trajectory.decision: Observe and document the positional bias.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Systematic positional bias observed in BioLector backscatter values during cultivation at 15°C, with backscatter ranging from 60 to 80 a.u., mean 74.9 a.u., SD 4.5 a.u.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Hypothesize that temperature gradient from cooling fan caused bias.

evidence.quote_or_span: a systematic bias over the positions in the BioLector, which was observed during the second cultivation step at 15 °C
a systematic bias over the positions in the BioLector, which was observed during the second cultivation step at 15 °C
the final backscatter values across the FlowerPlate varied from approximately 60 to 80 a.u. with a mean and standard deviation of 74.9 a.u. and 4.5 a.u. respectively

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Positional bias observed at 15°C.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: What causes the positional bias?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Temperature gradient from cooling fan causes positional bias.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Formulate hypothesis about the cause of bias.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Hypothesized that temperature gradient from cooling fan caused positional bias.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Test hypothesis by changing cultivation temperature.

evidence.quote_or_span: The first challenge that was noted was a systematic bias over the positions in the BioLector, which was observed during the second cultivation step at 15 °C

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Hypothesized that temperature gradient causes bias.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to eliminate positional bias?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Changing cultivation temperature to constant 25°C will eliminate positional bias.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Change cultivation temperature to constant 25°C.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Cultivation temperature changed to 25°C for the whole process, excluding the cooling step.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate that bias is eliminated and CatIB formation/activity is maintained.

evidence.quote_or_span: changing the cultivation temperature
the cultivation temperature was changed to 25 °C for the whole process, excluding the cooling step

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Cultivation temperature changed to 25°C.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Does 25°C cultivation eliminate positional bias?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Constant 25°C cultivation eliminates positional bias.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Validate bias elimination at 25°C.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Positional bias eliminated; backscatter standard deviation reduced from 4.5 to 1.1 a.u.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate CatIB formation and activity at 25°C.

evidence.quote_or_span: the positional bias could thus be prevented by using 25 °C as cultivation temperature
the final backscatter values across the FlowerPlate varied from approximately 60 to 80 a.u. with a mean and standard deviation of 74.9 a.u. and 4.5 a.u. respectively

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Positional bias eliminated at 25°C.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Does 25°C cultivation maintain CatIB formation and enzymatic activity?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: CatIB formation and enzymatic activity are maintained at 25°C.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Validate CatIB formation and activity at 25°C.
evolution_trajectory.observation: CatIB formation and enzymatic activity confirmed at 25°C; some variants showed improved formation.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Optimize enzymatic assay for online measurement.

evidence.quote_or_span: the results indicate that cultivation at 25 °C overall has a positive influence on the CatIB formation rate for the BsGDH case study

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Optimized enzymatic assay implemented.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Is the optimized workflow reproducible?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: The optimized workflow will show high reproducibility.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Validate reproducibility with 42 biological replicates.
evolution_trajectory.observation: High reproducibility achieved with 1.9% RSD after 120 min, compared to 11.4% previously.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Integrate validated workflow with Bayesian optimization for iterative screening.

evidence.quote_or_span: high reproducibility with 1.9% relative standard deviation across 42 biological replicates

a high reproducibility of the new workflow was demonstrated with 42 biological replicates, lowering the relative standard deviation of fluorescence signals after 120 min to only 1.9% compared to 11.4% in a previous study

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Validated high-throughput workflow available.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to efficiently screen 63 variants to identify the best performer?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Combining the automated workflow with a Bayesian process model and Thompson sampling will enable efficient iterative screening.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Integrate Bayesian process model and Thompson sampling for experimental design.

tool_or_method.name: Bayesian process model and Thompson sampling

evolution_trajectory.observation: Combined automated workflow with Bayesian process model and Thompson sampling for iterative screening of 63 variants in three batch experiments of 48 candidates each.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Execute iterative screening and analyze results.

evidence.quote_or_span: the workflow was performed in combination with a Bayesian process model and Thompson sampling
allowed analysis of 63 BsGDH-CatIB variants within only three batch experiments
the process model was used to quantify various experimental effects and their influence on the reaction rate of each CatIB variant in the final activity assay
we chose to model their reaction in the activity assay with a first order mass action law
this results in an overall manual work time of 7 h for 48 variants. This is approximately 12% of the operator workload for the typical manual procedure

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Three rounds of iterative screening completed.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which variant is the best performer?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Thompson sampling will identify the best variant with >95% probability.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Analyze results after three rounds; identify top candidate.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Thompson sampling identified TDoT-PT-BsGDH as best performer with 98% probability after three rounds; inactive variants dropped early. Top four candidates had median reaction rate constants between 0.15 min^-1 and 0.19 min^-1.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate top candidate with a manual fourth round.

evidence.quote_or_span: Due to the probabilistic nature of the algorithm, two remaining variants, BsGDH-SG-L48KD, and CBDCell-PT-BsGDH, where not chosen in any round.
the top four candidates, TDoT-PT-BsGDH, BsGDH-PT-3HAMP, BsGDH-PT-CBDCell and BsGDH-PT-L6KD all have a median reaction rate constant between 0.15 min $^{-1}$ and 0.19 min $^{-1}$
Afterwards, TDoT-PT-BsGDH showed a 98% probability to be the best performer in the library (see Round 3), which exceed the termination criterion of $>95\%$ probability.

Due to the exploitation-exploration balancing, the Thompson sampling algorithm selected this variant in 50 biological replicates during the three screening rounds, leading to a statistically sound estimation of its reaction rate constant as the KPI for a library ranking.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: TDoT-PT-BsGDH identified as top candidate with 98% probability.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Is TDoT-PT-BsGDH truly the best performer? Are unseen variants weak?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A manual fourth round will confirm TDoT-PT-BsGDH as best and unseen variants as weak.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Conduct manual fourth round including previously unseen variants.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Manual fourth round confirmed TDoT-PT-BsGDH as best with >99% probability; unseen variants BsGDH-SG-L48KD and CBDCell-PT-BsGDH were weak performers.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Reflect on limitations of CatIB design rules.

evidence.quote_or_span: After the fourth round, it is clearly confirmed that TDoT-PT-BsGDH is the best performer in the library, which the algorithm determined with a probability of $>99\%$
the previously unseen variants BsGDH-SG-L48KD and CBDCell-PT-BsGDH did not lead to the successful formation of CatIBs and were weak performers not worth testing

evolution_trajectory.state_before: TDoT-PT-BsGDH confirmed as best performer.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Why is the top performer an N-terminal fusion, contrary to expectations?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: The result exemplifies the limited understanding of structure-function relationship for CatIB design.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Acknowledge limitation: no general design rules can be derived.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Top performer TDoT-PT-BsGDH has N-terminal tag, exemplifying limited understanding of structure-function relationship for CatIB design.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Conclude study.

evidence.quote_or_span: the top performer TDoT-PT-BsGDH is a variant with N-terminal addition of the tag, as such a perfect example of the still limited understanding of the structure–function relationship for CatIB design.
successful CatIB formation and activity highly depend on the combination of aggregation-inducing tag, length and type of the linker and N-/C-terminal fusion, allowing for no general conclusion

success_verification.final_conclusion: TDoT-PT-BsGDH identified as best CatIB variant with >99% probability; automated workflow reduced manual work by 88% and achieved 1.9% RSD.
limitations: Limited understanding of structure-function relationship for CatIB design; no general design rules can be derived.

source.title: Enzyme engineering: A synthetic biology approach for more effective library generation and automated high-throughput screening.

initial_request.research_problem: Develop a versatile method for generating and screening smart enzyme libraries to engineer Candida antarctica lipase A (Cal-A) for altered substrate selectivity between short-chain and long-chain fatty acid triglycerides.
initial_request.known_context: Existing smart library methods suffer from high cost, experimental demand, time consumption, and lack of flexibility in recombining mutations.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Existing smart library methods are costly, experimentally demanding, time-consuming, and inflexible for recombining mutations.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need for a flexible, cost-effective method to generate and screen smart libraries for enzyme engineering.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A synthetic biology approach using Golden Gate assembly can enable rapid generation of recombined functional diversity in Cal-A.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Divide Cal-A gene into three parts for independent mutagenesis.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Gene divided into part 1 (N-terminal), part 2 (tunnel region), part 3 (C-terminal).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Design mutagenesis strategies for each part.

evidence.quote_or_span: These methods typically suffer from one or more disadvantage including high cost, being experimentally demanding, requiring time consuming steps or, importantly, not providing flexibility in recombining mutations.
The three ‘parts’ of Cal-A were synthesized in a codon-optimized form

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Cal-A gene divided into three parts.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which mutagenesis strategies to apply to each part to explore substrate selectivity.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Targeted NDT saturation mutagenesis at Tyr93, Tyr183, Phe431 and random mutagenesis of the tunnel region will generate functional diversity.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Apply NDT saturation mutagenesis to Tyr93, Tyr183, Phe431; error-prone PCR to tunnel region (part 2).
evolution_trajectory.observation: NDT degeneracy chosen for three positions; error-prone PCR for tunnel region.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Design assembly method to recombine mutated parts.

evidence.quote_or_span: We chose NDT degeneracy at these three positions
the entire tunnel region (residues 211-350) was targeted for random mutagenesis

Based on further structural analysis (PDB identification code: 2VEO)[26] and proximity to the catalytic triad (within 5 Å from the hydroxyl oxygen of Ser183), residues Tyr93, Tyr183 and Phe431 were selected as potential hot-spots for mutagenesis.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Mutagenesis strategies defined.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need a seamless method to recombine mutated parts into expression vector.

evolution_trajectory.observation: Junctions designed with unique BsaI site; two SapI sites for ligation into daughter vector.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Generate libraries using the designed assembly.

evidence.quote_or_span: The junctions between parts were designed with a unique BsaI restriction site while two unique SapI restriction sites were designed to ligate the assembled gene into the daughter vector
When ready to be recombined, for example upon library generation, the parts were reassembled into the complete gene in an Electra daughter vector (pD441pelB, DNA2.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Golden Gate assembly designed.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Whether the assembly strategy can generate diverse libraries.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Golden Gate assembly will yield at least 10^3 transformants per library with various combinations.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Generate ten libraries with combinations of mutated and native parts.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Ten libraries obtained with at least 10^3 transformants each; one library (Tyr93-Phe431) initially failed with circularized plasmid.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Assess library quality and resolve failure.

evidence.quote_or_span: We thus rapidly obtained a variety of complex, mutated libraries, ready to bring forward to screening (Table 1).
In one instance (construction of library Tyr93-Phe431), use of the circularized daughter plasmid afforded no transformants. This issue was resolved by using the commercial, pre-cut daughter plasmid

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Libraries generated with one failure resolved.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Whether libraries have expected diversity and quality.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Sequencing will confirm expected diversity for NDT libraries and mutational load for random library.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Sequence pooled clones from NDT libraries and 20 clones from random library.
evolution_trajectory.observation: NDT libraries showed expected codon degeneracy; random library had total mutational load 34, 25% wild-type, average 1.65 mutations/clone, max 4.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Proceed to screening of libraries.

evidence.quote_or_span: The quality of each of the degenerate NDT libraries, whether resulting from mutation of one part or assembly of more than one mutated part, was routinely assessed by sequencing pooled clones.
codon degeneracy can be clearly visible in the DNA sequencing electropherogram (Fig 3), confirming the expected distribution of nucleotides instead of the original codon.
The quality of the randomly mutated library was assessed by sequencing 20 clones (Table 2).

The 20 clones carried a total mutational load of 34, with approximately 25% of the clones being wild-type and the majority of clones carrying two mutations (average = 1.65), for a maximum of 4 mutations per clone.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Library quality validated.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Whether variants show altered substrate selectivity between short-chain (C4) and long-chain (C18) triglycerides.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Automated whole-cell screening against tributyrin and olive oil/rhodamine will identify variants with altered selectivity.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Screen 735 clones from Cal-A libraries using liquid handler robot for halo-based assays.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Screening performed; wild-type active on both substrates without distinction.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Analyze screening results to identify selectivity patterns.

evidence.quote_or_span: The resulting Cal-A libraries expressed in E. coli BL21 (DE3) were screened to evaluate selectivity of hydrolase activity toward long versus short-chain triglycerides.
For the olive/oil rhodamine plates, the tributyrin was replaced with 2.5 g olive oil, and rhodamine was added at a concentration of 0.001% w/v.
A script was designed to pick up 20 μL of culture, column by column, from the inoculum plates and spot 8 μL of inoculum by lightly touching the agar surface.
A total of 735 clones were screened (Table 1).

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Screening completed.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which variants show altered selectivity and what are the mutation effects.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Variants with mutations at specific positions will show distinct selectivity profiles.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Analyze halo patterns to classify variants as long-chain preferring, short-chain preferring, or non-discriminating.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Variant B6 prefers olive oil; D4, D6, G9 prefer tributyrin; majority show no improved selectivity or lose activity.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Correlate mutations with activity and selectivity.

evidence.quote_or_span: variant B6 hydrolyzes olive oil preferentially over tributyrin, while variants D4, D6 and G9 show the inverse selectivity.
the wild-type was active with tributyrin and olive oil without distinction.

The majority of the variants do not show any improved selectivity compared to the wild-type Cal-A (e.g. A2, A3, the majority of row B, etc, relative to wild-type A11, starred) or they lose the ability to hydrolyze both of the substrates (A1, A4, A5, and so on).

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Variants with altered selectivity identified.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How mutations at Tyr93, Tyr183, Phe431 affect activity and selectivity.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Tyr93 mutation retains activity with long-chain preference; Tyr183 is crucial for activity; Phe431 tolerates mutation without selectivity change.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Calculate activity retention and selectivity preference for each position.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Tyr93: 89% active, long-chain preference; Tyr183: 10% active, short-chain preference; Phe431: 90% active, no discrimination.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate assembly method and note limitations.

evidence.quote_or_span: When Tyr93 was mutated, 89% of the clones retained activity and discriminated preferentially towards long-chain fatty acids.
In contrast, Tyr183 was crucial for activity: when mutated, only 10% of the variants retained activity, with a preference for the hydrolysis of short-chain fatty acids.
Phe431 tolerated mutation without little effect, as activity was maintained in 90% of the variants and no increased discrimination was found.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Standard Golden Gate assembly used for library generation.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Test simplified one-pot assembly for recombining mutant parts.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Simplified one-pot assembly had reduced success rate for mutant parts; standard method was used instead.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate standard method with colony PCR and sequencing.

evidence.quote_or_span: when we applied this simplified strategy to achieve the recombination of the mutant parts, a reduced success rate of ligation was achieved.
the use of our standard method (Fig 2 in main article) solved the problem.

evolution_trajectory.observation: 100% correct ligation products observed for recombined libraries.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Proceed to overall validation of the Golden Gate strategy.

evidence.quote_or_span: Upon screening more than 15 randomly-selected clones for each recombined library, we observed 100% correct ligation products by colony PCR.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Mutation effects analyzed and assembly validated.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Whether the Golden Gate strategy is suitable for rapid generation of functional diversity.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: The successful alteration of Cal-A selectivity validates the Golden Gate approach.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Conclude that Golden Gate strategy enables rapid generation of recombined functional diversity.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Results demonstrate suitability of Golden Gate strategy for rapid generation of recombined functional diversity.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Note limitations of screening method.

evidence.quote_or_span: These results demonstrate the suitability of the Golden Gate strategy to achieve rapid generation of readily recombined functional diversity.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Proof of concept validated.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Limitations of the screening assay.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Halo analysis is qualitative and not quantitative due to variability in colony size.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Acknowledge that the method is qualitatively robust but not quantitative.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Halo analysis gives yes/no activity response; robust qualitatively but not quantitative due to cell number variability.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Complete trajectory.

evidence.quote_or_span: Halo analysis is qualitative, giving a yes/no response as to activity of a variant toward a specific substrate.
The method is qualitatively robust, in that an active variant consistently shows a halo and a negative variant consistently does not

the current method does not lend itself to quantitative activity measurements because of factors including insufficient precision in the number of cells inoculated which affects colony size and thus appearance of the halo

validation_methods: Colony PCR and sequencing of recombined libraries
success_verification.final_conclusion: The Golden Gate strategy successfully generated recombined functional diversity, enabling identification of Cal-A variants with altered substrate selectivity, though the screening method is qualitative.
limitations: Halo analysis is qualitative, not quantitative
One library construction initially failed with circularized plasmid
Simplified one-pot assembly had reduced success rate for mutant parts

source.title: Active learning-assisted directed evolution.

initial_request.research_problem: Directed evolution (DE) is inefficient when mutations exhibit epistasis, limiting exploration to local regions and causing local optima.
initial_request.target_object: ParPgb protoglobin for non-native cyclopropanation reaction
initial_request.known_context: DE can be inefficient when mutations exhibit non-additive, or epistatic, behavior. DE is limited because screening for performance can only explore a small, local region of sequence space. Taking one mutational step at a time can cause the experiment to become stuck at a local optimum, especially on rugged protein fitness landscapes where mutation effects exhibit epistasis.
constraints: Combinatorial design space on five active-site residues (W56, Y57, L59, Q60, F89)
Objective: high total cyclopropanation yield and cis:trans selectivity for cis-2a product
input_data: Single-site saturation mutagenesis (SSM) data at five positions
Recombination data of best single mutants (DAYFW, DGMDW, DGMVW)
quantifiable_goals: Improve total cyclopropanation yield beyond 12% (parent ParLQ)
Achieve high cis:trans selectivity for cis-2a

evolution_trajectory.state_before: DE is inefficient when mutations exhibit epistasis, limiting exploration to local regions and causing local optima.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to explore protein sequence space more efficiently than standard DE when epistasis is present.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Active learning with uncertainty quantification can explore protein sequence space more efficiently than DE.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Develop Active Learning-assisted Directed Evolution (ALDE) workflow.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Active learning has the potential to unlock improved engineering outcomes by leveraging uncertainty quantification to choose which variants should be tested at each step.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Design the ALDE workflow to test the hypothesis.

evidence.quote_or_span: DE can be inefficient when mutations exhibit non-additive, or epistatic, behavior.
DE is limited because screening for performance can only explore a small, local region of sequence space.
taking one mutational step at a time can cause the experiment to become stuck at a local optimum, especially on rugged protein fitness landscapes where mutation effects exhibit epistasis
active learning has the potential to unlock improved engineering outcomes

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Hypothesis that active learning can improve DE efficiency.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need a concrete workflow to implement active learning for protein engineering.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: ALDE workflow: define combinatorial design space on k residues, start with simultaneous mutagenesis and initial screening, train ML model, use acquisition function to propose new variants, repeat.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Adopt ALDE workflow for experimental campaign.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Workflow steps: simultaneously mutate k residues, collect initial sequence-fitness data, train supervised ML model, apply acquisition function to rank sequences, repeat cycle until fitness optimized.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Apply ALDE to ParPgb system, starting with SSM to assess landscape.

evidence.quote_or_span: First, those k residues are simultaneously mutated, and an initial round of sequence-fitness data is collected in the wet lab.
The collected sequence-fitness data are then used to computationally train a supervised ML model that can predict sequence from fitness.
an acquisition function is applied to the trained model to rank all sequences in the design space
the cycle is repeated until fitness is sufficiently optimized

evolution_trajectory.state_before: ALDE workflow designed; need to assess ParPgb active-site landscape.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Whether single-site mutations can improve the objective (total yield and cis selectivity).
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Single-site saturation mutagenesis (SSM) at five active-site residues (W56, Y57, L59, Q60, F89) may identify beneficial mutations.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform SSM and screen variants by gas chromatography.
evolution_trajectory.observation: None of the screened mutants demonstrated a significant, desirable shift in the value of the objective.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: SSM failed; attempt recombination of best single mutants.

evidence.quote_or_span: Single-site saturation mutagenesis (SSM) was performed at these sites, and variants were screened by gas chromatography for their cyclopropanation products.
None of the screened mutants demonstrated a significant, desirable shift in the value of the objective
None of the screened mutants demonstrated a significant, desirable shift in the value of the objective (Fig. 2C)

evolution_trajectory.state_before: SSM failed to improve objective; best single mutants identified.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Whether recombination of best single mutants can yield effective proteins.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Recombination of best single mutants (DAYFW, DGMDW, DGMVW) may produce variants with high yield and cis selectivity.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Test recombination variants for cyclopropanation activity.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Various recombinations of ideal single mutations are not effective proteins for the desired objective (cis-trans Yield); no variant generated cis-2a with high yield and selectivity.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Recombination failure confirms epistasis; proceed with ALDE initial library.

evidence.quote_or_span: in our recombination studies of the single-site mutants with the highest fold-
Various recombinations of ideal single mutations are not effective proteins for the desired objective (cis-trans Yield)
we did not observe a variant which generated cis-2a with high yield and selectivity (Fig. 2D)

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Challenging epistatic landscape confirmed; need initial library for ALDE.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to construct initial library when zero-shot predictors are not correlated with objective.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Random selection from NNK library is appropriate because zero-shot predictors may not enrich useful variants.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Construct initial library via NNK mutagenesis at all five positions and randomly select 384 variants for screening.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Retrospective analysis showed objective not strongly correlated with conventional zero-shot predictors.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Screen initial library to gather sequence-fitness data.

evidence.quote_or_span: We elected to use random selection from this library because we did not know if any zero-shot predictors might enrich the starting library with useful variants
retrospective analysis of the initial library revealed that our objective is not strongly correlated with conventional zero-shot predictors

The optimization campaign started with (A) constructing an initial library with mutations at all five sites under study using NNK degenerate codons, randomly selecting 384 for screening for product formation, and mapping to sequences using LevSeq.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Initial library constructed; need sequence-fitness data.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: What sequence-fitness relationships exist in the initial library.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Screening will reveal variants with varying activity and selectivity; F89Y may be important for cis selectivity.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Screen 384 random variants for cyclopropanation activity and sequence via LevSeq.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Most variants active but trans-selective; F89Y mutation critical for cis selectivity in specific contexts.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Use initial screening data to train ML model for ALDE Round 1.

evidence.quote_or_span: the F89Y mutation was particularly important for inverting selectivity to favor the cis-2a, but only in the context of certain mutations at positions 56, 57, 59, and 60

The optimization campaign started with (A) constructing an initial library with mutations at all five sites under study using NNK degenerate codons, randomly selecting 384 for screening for product formation, and mapping to sequences using LevSeq.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Initial screening data available; need to select ML model and acquisition function.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which model and acquisition function perform best for ALDE.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: DNN ensemble with one-hot encoding and Thompson sampling will be most consistent based on computational simulations.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Use DNN ensemble with one-hot encoding and Thompson sampling for wet-lab campaign.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Based on extensive computational simulations, DNN ensemble with one-hot and Thompson sampling is the most consistent strategy.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Train model on initial library and propose Round 1 sequences.

evidence.quote_or_span: Based on our extensive computational simulations (described in the following section), we decided to use the DNN ensemble with one-hot encoding of the five targeted residues for model training and Thompson sampling as the acquisition function

For the wet-lab campaign, we trained the model with onehot encodings, the DNN ensemble with 5 models and bootstrapping using 90% of the available training data for each model, and Thompson sampling as the acquisition function.
These design choices correspond to the most consistent strategy based on the computational simulations.

Subsequent activity screening of these sequences in triplicate showed that nearly a third of Round 1 sequences met the objective better than the best variant in the initial, randomly selected set (Fig.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Model trained on initial library; Round 1 sequences proposed.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Whether ALDE can identify improved variants in Round 1.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Top predicted sequences will show improved yield and selectivity.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Synthesize and screen top 90 predicted sequences.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Best variant MKFNY (W56M Y57K L59F Q60N F89Y) achieved 93% total yield and 12:1 cis:trans selectivity.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Use Round 1 data to retrain model for Round 2.

evidence.quote_or_span: The best variant in the Round 1 library, MKFNY (W56M Y57K L59F Q60N F89Y), demonstrated a total cyclopropanation yield of 93% and a cis:trans selectivity ratio of 12:1

Subsequent activity screening of these sequences in triplicate showed that nearly a third of Round 1 sequences met the objective better than the best variant in the initial, randomly selected set (Fig.
The top 90 predicted sequences were again synthesized and tested exactly as before (Round 2, Fig.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Round 1 successful; model retrained with additional data.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Whether further rounds can improve yield and selectivity.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Round 2 will explore more sequence space and find even better variants.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Synthesize and screen top 90 predicted sequences for Round 2.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Top variant MPFDY achieved 99% total yield and 14:1 cis:trans selectivity; mutations not individually beneficial, demonstrating epistasis.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate best variant on substrate scope.

evidence.quote_or_span: the top-performing variant among these sequences (MPFDY) displayed a total cyclopropane yield of 99% and a 14:1 cis:trans selectivity ratio
None of the mutations in MPFDY obviously optimized the objective in the single-site mutagenesis studies (Fig. 2C); they work together, however, to deliver an optimal variant
The top 90 predicted sequences were again synthesized and tested exactly as before (Round 2, Fig.

After performing three rounds of ALDE (exploring only \~0.01% of the design space), the optimal variant has 99% total yield and 14:1 selectivity for the desired diastereomer of the cyclopropane product.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: MPFDY identified as top variant; need to assess broader applicability.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Whether Round 2 variants perform well across different substrates.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Round 2 variants will outperform parent ParLQ across multiple styrene derivatives.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Screen Round 2 variants against eight styrene derivatives.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Nearly all Round 2 variants higher yielding and more selective than ParLQ; top variants differ per substrate.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate ALDE performance via simulations on other proteins.

evidence.quote_or_span: for every substrate, nearly all of the Round 2 variants were higher yielding and more selective for their respective cis- diastereomers than the parent protein, ParLQ
the top-performing variants for each substrate differed in sequence from MPFDY

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Wet-lab success; need computational validation of ALDE over DE.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Whether ALDE consistently outperforms DE and random sampling across different proteins.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: ALDE significantly outperforms average DE and random sampling on GB1 and TrpB datasets.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Run simulations comparing ALDE, DE, and random sampling on GB1 and TrpB fitness landscapes.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Active learning significantly outperforms average DE and random sampling; DNN ensemble with one-hot and Thompson sampling most consistent.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Analyze role of uncertainty quantification.

evidence.quote_or_span: We conclude that active learning can significantly outperform the average performance of DE and random sampling, and results are consistent across the two different protein datasets.
Overall, the frequentist ensemble models perform the most consistently across different encodings.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: ALDE validated; need to understand role of uncertainty quantification.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Whether calibrated uncertainty quantification is necessary for ALDE performance.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Classical uncertainty quantification plays a more limited role than expected; calibration does not necessarily correlate with ALDE performance.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Analyze miscalibration areas and correlation with max fitness achieved.
evolution_trajectory.observation: DNN ensembles have lowest miscalibration areas, but performance in ALDE simulations is not necessarily correlated to calibration; classical uncertainty quantification plays limited role.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Conclude ALDE effectiveness and limitations.

evidence.quote_or_span: DNN ensembles have the lowest miscalibration areas, suggesting that they are the most calibrated and best models overall.
However, in this study, we find that performance in ALDE simulations (by max fitness achieved) is not necessarily correlated to how calibrated the uncertainties are for each model.
Overall, classical notions of uncertainty quantification seem to play a more limited role than expected in these real-world applications.

validation_methods: Wet-lab screening of Round 1 and Round 2 variants
Substrate scope screening on eight styrene derivatives
Computational simulations on GB1 and TrpB fitness landscapes
success_verification.final_conclusion: ALDE successfully improved total yield from 12% to 99% and cis:trans selectivity to 14:1 after exploring only ~0.01% of the design space. Simulations confirm ALDE outperforms average DE and random sampling.
limitations: Classical uncertainty quantification plays a more limited role than expected; calibration does not necessarily correlate with ALDE performance.

source.title: directed evolution of a cyclodipeptide synthase with new activities via label fr

initial_request.research_problem: Directed evolution of enzymes is limited by the lack of high-throughput screening (HTS) assays for new catalytic activities. Mass spectrometry (MS) is label-free and suitable for untargeted discovery but traditionally slow. The goal is to develop an automated, label-free MS screening workflow for directed evolution of new enzymatic activities, demonstrated by engineering a cyclodipeptide synthase (CDPS) to produce new diketopiperazine (DKP) products.
initial_request.known_context: CDPSs are recalcitrant targets for rational engineering, and large-scale screening of CDPS variants has not been reported due to lack of HTS assays.
constraints: High-throughput screening assay must be label-free and automated.
Must detect new DKP products not produced by wild-type AlbC.
input_data: List of 190 possible DKP molecules with theoretical m/z values (115–373 Da)
quantifiable_goals: Screen ~4500 epPCR clones within a week.
Identify at least one AlbC mutant producing a new DKP product.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: No high-throughput screening assay exists for new catalytic activities of CDPSs.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Lack of HTS assays limits directed evolution of CDPSs for new DKP products.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: An automated, label-free MS screening workflow can enable high-throughput directed evolution of CDPSs to produce new DKP products.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Develop an automated workflow integrating library creation, robotic cultivation, MALDI-ToF MS, and custom computational analysis.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Assay development for high-throughput mutant screening remains challenging, particularly for new catalytic activities. CDPSs are recalcitrant targets for rational engineering, and large-scale screening of CDPS variants has not been reported due to lack of HTS assays.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Design an automated workflow to address the screening bottleneck.

evidence.quote_or_span: assay development for high-throughput mutant screening remains challenging, particularly for new catalytic activities.
CDPSs are recognized as recalcitrant targets for rational engineering.
large-scale screening of CDPS variants has not been reported, possibly due to the lack of applicable HTS assays.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: No automated MS screening workflow exists for CDPS directed evolution.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to design a workflow that can create libraries, cultivate mutants, and perform label-free MS screening at high throughput.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: An integrated robotic workcell can automate library creation, cultivation, and MALDI-ToF MS analysis at 5 s per sample, enabling screening of thousands of clones.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Design workflow with SSM and epPCR libraries, robotic cultivation, and MALDI-ToF MS analysis; choose AlbC as proof-of-concept target.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Workflow designed to automatically create and cultivate SSM and epPCR libraries, robotically process culture supernatants for MALDI-ToF MS at 5 s per sample, and analyze data with custom algorithms.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Implement the workflow and validate with wild-type AlbC.

evidence.quote_or_span: Here we report an automated workflow for directed evolution of new enzymatic activities via high-throughput library creation and label-free MS screening.
For a proof of concept, we chose to engineer a cyclodipeptide synthase (CDPS) that synthesizes diketopiperazine (DKP) compounds with therapeutic potential.
site-saturation mutagenesis (SSM) and error-prone PCR (epPCR) libraries expressing CDPS mutants were automatically created and cultivated on an integrated work cell.
Culture supernatants were then robotically processed for matrix-assisted laser desorption/ionization time-of-flight (MALDI-ToF) MS analysis at a rate of 5 s per sample.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Workflow designed but not yet validated with actual samples.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to confirm that the automated MS workflow can detect known CDP products from wild-type AlbC.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: The automated workflow will detect the expected CDP products from WT AlbC-expressing E. coli, consistent with literature.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Run WT AlbC-expressing E. coli through the automated workflow and analyze MS data.

parameters.sample: WT AlbC-expressing E. coli culture supernatant

evolution_trajectory.observation: Known CDP products were detected, validating the workflow.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Proceed to screen mutant libraries for new products.

evidence.quote_or_span: These results validated our workflow to detect CDP products from microplate cultures of AlbC-expressing E. coli using MALDI-ToF MS.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Workflow validated with WT, but no new products identified yet.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: SSM libraries targeting binding pocket residues may yield new DKP products.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Mutating binding pocket residues via SSM will alter substrate specificity and produce new DKP products.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Create and screen 14 SSM libraries targeting binding pocket residues and some external residues.
evolution_trajectory.observation: No new CDP molecules were observed from the 14 SSM library mutants.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: SSM limited to predefined residues failed; need random mutagenesis across the whole protein.

evidence.quote_or_span: Unfortunately, we did not observe any new CDP molecules produced from the 14 SSM library mutants.
Unfortunately, we did not observe any new CDP molecules produced from the 14 SSM library mutants.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: SSM libraries failed to produce new products.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Random mutations throughout the whole protein may uncover new specificity determinants not predictable by rational design.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: epPCR libraries with random mutations will generate variants with altered substrate specificity, including new DKP products.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Design epPCR libraries with an average of 2 nucleotide changes per gene to introduce random mutations across the entire AlbC sequence.

tool_or_method.name: Error-prone PCR (epPCR) library design
tool_or_method.version: Agilent GeneMorph II Random Mutagenesis Kit

parameters.mutation_rate: on average 2 nucleotides per gene variant

evolution_trajectory.observation: epPCR libraries created with random mutations throughout the whole protein sequence.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Screen the epPCR libraries using the automated MS workflow.

evidence.quote_or_span: Therefore, we further turned to profile epPCR libraries that contain random mutations throughout the whole protein sequence.

Under optimized conditions using the Agilent GeneMorph II Random Mutagenesis Kit, on average 2 nucleotides were introduced per gene variant so that most epPCR library members contain no more than one amino acid change.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to screen a large number of epPCR clones to find rare variants with new activities.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Screening ~4500 epPCR clones will identify mutants producing new DKP products.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Screen around 4500 independent epPCR clones using the automated MS workflow.

source.title: selection finder selfi a computational metabolic engineering tool to enable dire

initial_request.research_problem: Directed enzyme evolution is bottlenecked by the lack of high-throughput selections to isolate desired variants.
initial_request.target_object: Selection pathways coupling enzymatic product to host survival
initial_request.known_context: Most directed evolution is limited by the inability to generically link any biochemical transformation to readouts like cell density or fluorescence.
constraints: Maximum 3 knockouts to limit computational cost
Biomass flux >= 10% wild-type max
Oxygen uptake bounds -1000 to 1000
Engineered enzyme flux bounds 0 to 1000
input_data: E. coli iAF1260 genome-scale metabolic model
quantifiable_goals: Guarantee minimum flux through consumption pathway
Rank pathways by flux, length, and number of knockouts

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Directed enzyme evolution lacks generic high-throughput selections.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: No computational framework exists to systematically construct selection pathways linking any enzymatic product to host survival.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A computational metabolic engineering framework can construct selection pathways from a desired enzymatic product to a cellular host and couple the pathway with cell survival.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Develop SelFi, a four-step computational framework.
evolution_trajectory.observation: SelFi is designed to identify selection pathways in four steps: construct consumption pathways, eliminate alternate carbon sources, identify knockout targets, rank pathways.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need to detail the four steps of SelFi.

evidence.quote_or_span: The biggest challenge in directed enzyme evolution is identifying high-throughput screens or selections to isolate the variant(s) with the desired property.
most directed evolution of enzymes are still largely limited by the inability to identify and implement selections or screens.

We present in this paper a computational metabolic engineering framework, Selection Finder (SelFi), to construct a selection pathway from a desired enzymatic product to a cellular host and to couple the pathway with cell survival.

SelFi identifies a selection pathway in four steps. In the first step, SelFi constructs traversal pathways to consume the desired enzymatic reaction product and convert it to a native metabolite within the cellular host.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: SelFi framework outlined with four steps.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to construct consumption pathways from product to host metabolite.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Reversing ProPath algorithm (retroProPath) can identify pathways from desired product to endogenous metabolite.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Implement Step 1: use retroProPath to construct consumption pathways.
evolution_trajectory.observation: retroProPath constructs traversal pathways from desired product to native metabolite.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need to couple pathway with survival by eliminating alternate carbon sources.

evidence.quote_or_span: We reverse ProPath's search direction to identify a pathway starting from a compound of interest to an endogenous metabolite within the host. We refer to the use of the algorithm in this reversed manner as retroProPath.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Consumption pathways constructed.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Pathway not yet coupled to survival; host can use other carbon sources.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Eliminating all organic carbon uptakes except the consumption pathway and CO2 will couple pathway to survival.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Implement Step 2: eliminate alternate carbon sources.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Carbon sources eliminated except consumption pathway and CO2.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Limitation: when reactant is native, eliminating carbon sources does not couple pathway to survival; need knockouts.

evidence.quote_or_span: SelFi therefore eliminates all organic carbon uptakes except for the uptake provided through the consumption pathway, and inorganic $CO_{2}$ , an essential waste product.
In some cases, the reactant of the specified enzymatic reaction is native to the host, and limiting carbon uptake to be only through the consumption pathway is not possible.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Carbon source elimination insufficient when reactant is native.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to guarantee minimum flux through consumption pathway via knockouts.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A sequential greedy knockout strategy can identify minimal set of knockouts to guarantee minimum flux.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Implement Step 3: sequential greedy knockout strategy with max 3 knockouts.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Knockout targets identified to guarantee minimum flux.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need to rank resulting pathways.

evidence.quote_or_span: In the second and third steps, SelFi identifies the minimal set of carbon sources and knockout targets required to link cell survival with the consumption pathway and to guarantee a minimum flux.
To identify possible knockout targets, SelFi utilizes a sequential greedy strategy.
SelFi explores knocking out one reaction at a time using FBA to calculate the minimum flux through the consumption pathway.

SelFi continues to find an additional knockout target that improves the flux, repeating this process until the maximum allowed number of knockouts, as specified by the user, is reached. In this work, we set the number of knockouts to three to limit the computational cost associated with evaluating each consumption pathway.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Knockout targets identified.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Multiple pathways possible; need to select best ones.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Ranking pathways by minimum consumption flux, pathway length, and number of knockouts will identify optimal selection pathways.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Implement Step 4: rank pathways based on flux, length, and knockouts.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Pathways ranked; shorter, higher-flux pathways with fewer knockouts preferred.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Apply SelFi to test cases.

evidence.quote_or_span: In the last step, SelFi ranks the resulting pathways based on flux, pathway length, and number of required knockouts.

Ideally, candidate pathways are chosen based on the guaranteed minimum consumption flux, pathway length, and number of required knockouts. Shorter, higher-consumption flux pathways with the smallest number of knockouts are preferable over others.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: SelFi framework fully designed.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to validate SelFi on real test cases.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: SelFi can identify effective selection pathways for diverse enzymatic products.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Apply SelFi to four test cases: xylitol, D-ribulose-1,5-bisphosphate, methanol, aniline using E. coli iAF1260 model.
evolution_trajectory.observation: SelFi applied to four test cases; retroProPath executed for 1000 iterations; all cofactors native; many pathways have zero minimum flux before knockouts.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Analyze effect of knockouts on flux.

evidence.quote_or_span: we applied SelFi to several test cases including desired products xylitol, D-ribulose-1,5-bisphosphate, methanol, and aniline that can be potentially synthesized through the action of engineered enzymes Xylose Reductase (XR), Phosphoribulokinase (PRK), Methane Monooxygenase (MMO), and Aromatic Amino acid Decarboxylase (AAD), respectively. We utilized the genome-scale model of E. coli metabol
we applied SelFi to several test cases including desired products xylitol, D-ribulose-1,5-bisphosphate, methanol, and aniline
We utilized the genome-scale model of E. coli metabolism (iAF1260)
A constraint is added to ensure that the biomass flux is equal to or greater than 10% of the maximum biomass flux rate of the wild type.
We executed retroProPath for 1000 iterations.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Test cases run; many pathways have zero minimum flux before knockouts.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to understand effect of knockouts on flux distribution.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Analyze flux changes after knockouts.
evolution_trajectory.observation: After knockouts, minimum consumption flux increases while maximum decreases; knockout process sets threshold on desired enzymatic activity.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Analyze consumption demand of terminating metabolites.

evidence.quote_or_span: after knockouts, the minimum consumption flux increases whereas the maximum consumption flux decreases.
the knockout process provides a mechanism to place a threshold on minimum desired enzymatic activity.

metabolite 4-aminobenzoate has low demand
the limited demand of end metabolite 4-aminobenzoate for biomass production (0.05 mmol/gDCW/h) explains the low flux rate for the aniline consumption pathway.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Demand analysis completed.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to validate SelFi predictions against known experimental results.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: SelFi can identify selection pathways that match previously validated experimental pathways.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Analyze xylitol/Xylose Reductase case in detail.
evolution_trajectory.observation: SelFi identified 7 pathways for xylitol; same 3 knockouts (R01432, R00086, R01518) increased minimum flux from 0 to ~111 mmol/gDCW/h. Pathway SP_X1 matches previously used selection pathway; SelFi identified two additional knockouts beyond the one used experimentally.

evidence.quote_or_span: SelFi identified seven consumption pathways for xylitol
the minimum flux through the consumption pathways, which is zero mmol/gDCW/h prior to applying any knockouts, is 110.27 mmol/gDCW/h or higher after knockouts.
SelFi identified two additional knockout targets to improve the selection and more strongly link growth rate to engineered XR activity.

the pathway from D-ribulose-1,5-bisphosphate to 3-phospho-D-glycerate was previously experimentally validated and confirmed in literature by Cai et al. (2014), but used for selection of a different enzyme – RuBisCO.
Among the identified pathways, the pathway from D-ribulose-1,5-bisphosphate to 3-phospho-D-glycerate was previously experimentally validated and confirmed in literature by Cai et al.

we applied SelFi to several test cases including desired products xylitol, D-ribulose-1,5-bisphosphate, methanol, and aniline that can be potentially synthesized through the action of engineered enzymes Xylose Reductase (XR), Phosphoribulokinase (PRK), Methane Monooxygenase (MMO), and Aromatic Amino acid Decarboxylase (AAD), respectively. We utilized the genome-scale model of E. coli metabol

evolution_trajectory.decision: Analyze D-ribulose-1,5-bisphosphate/Phosphoribulokinase case.
evolution_trajectory.observation: SelFi identified three consumption pathways; knockout targets: two for SP_D1, one for SP_D2 and SP_D3; additional knockouts did not improve flux. Pathway to 3-phospho-D-glycerate was previously experimentally validated for RuBisCO selection.

evidence.quote_or_span: SelFi identified three consumption pathways as shown in Tables 1 and 2 for D-ribulose-1,5-bisphosphate. The consumption pathways end at D-ribose 1,5-bisphosphate, 3-phospho-D-glycerate, or 2-phosphoglycolate.

For selection pathway $SP_{D1}$ , SelFi identified two knockout targets, while only one knockout target was identified for pathways $SP_{D2}$ and $SP_{D3}$ . Additional knockouts did not further improve the flux through the consumption pathways.

the pathway from D-ribulose-1,5-bisphosphate to 3-phospho-D-glycerate was previously experimentally validated and confirmed in literature by Cai et al. (2014), but used for selection of a different enzyme – RuBisCO.

evidence.quote_or_span: The amount of guaranteed minimum consumption flux after applying the knockout is slightly improved (0.001 mmol/gDCW/h), while the maximum consumption remains the same (0.01 mmol/gDCW/h).
