source.title: Targeted mutagenesis and high-throughput screening of diversified gene and promoter libraries for isolating gain-of-function mutations

initial_request.research_problem: Individually testing single mutations is tedious and time-intensive, creating a bottleneck in isolating gain-of-function mutations.
initial_request.target_object: Genes and promoters for gain-of-function mutations
initial_request.known_context: Site-directed mutagenesis allows introduction of single mutations, but testing them one at a time is slow.
constraints: Must be high-throughput
Must allow combinatorial testing of multiple mutations
input_data: Structural data (crystal structures, AlphaFold predictions) for protein targets
Knowledge of functional regions (e.g., -35/-10 sites, operator, RBS for promoters)
quantifiable_goals: Generate libraries of 10^4-10^7 variants
Isolate gain-of-function variants with improved performance
Reduce time and cost compared to individual testing

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Individual testing of single mutations is a bottleneck.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to rapidly test many mutations simultaneously?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Combinatorial testing of multiple mutations in a large library can eliminate the bottleneck.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Develop a protocol for assaying combinatorial effects of several mutations at once.
evolution_trajectory.observation: The protocol eliminates the bottleneck by assaying combinatorial effects in a large multi-variant library.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need to design a method to create such combinatorial libraries.

evidence.quote_or_span: introducing single mutations one at a time and individually testing for changes in function is a tedious and time-intensive process

The protocol described herein eliminates the bottleneck of individually testing variants of genes and promoters (generated by site-directed mutagenesis) by assaying the combinatorial effect of several mutations at once, as part of a large multi-variant library.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: No method to generate large combinatorial libraries with targeted mutations.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to generate a library of 10^4-10^7 variants with mutations at specific sites?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Overlap extension PCR with degenerate primers can create combinatorial libraries of desired size.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Use a combination of overlap extension PCR and saturation/partial saturation mutagenesis with degenerate primers.

tool_or_method.name: Overlap extension PCR with degenerate primers

evolution_trajectory.observation: The method can produce a library of gene and promoter variants.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need a high-throughput screening method to isolate desired variants.

evidence.quote_or_span: The method described here uses a combination of overlap extension Polymerase Chain Reaction (PCR) (Ho et al., 1989; Horton et al., 1989) and saturation or partial saturation mutagenesis with degenerate primers (Kretz et al., 2004) to produce a library of gene and promoter variants
These oligos can then be used in a two-step PCR (fragment generation followed by assembly of fragments) to rapidly generate libraries with diversity on the order of $10^{4}-10^{7}$ variants.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Library generation method exists, but screening is needed.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to rapidly screen millions of variants for gain-of-function?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: FACS with a fluorescent reporter can enable high-throughput screening.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Couple the library to a fluorescent reporter and use FACS for screening.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Libraries lend themselves to high-throughput screening via FACS, allowing rapid identification and isolation of variants of interest.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need to decide which regions to target for mutagenesis.

evidence.quote_or_span: The resulting libraries lend themselves to high-throughput screening via fluorescence activated cell sorting (FACS), when coupled to a relevant fluorescent reporter, allowing for the rapid identification and isolation of variants of interest

evolution_trajectory.state_before: General library generation and screening approach defined.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which specific regions of promoters should be targeted for effective gain-of-function mutations?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Targeting known functional regions (-35/-10, operator, RBS) will yield more useful variants than random mutagenesis.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Adopt a semi-rational design targeting specific promoter regions.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Semi-rational approach targets regions known to be involved in transcription or translation.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: For proteins, structural data can guide targeting; need to incorporate that.

evidence.quote_or_span: semi-rational refers to the approach of targeting specific regions of the promoter, known to be involved with various mechanisms of transcription or translation, rather than just randomly mutating the entire promoter region.

For transcription, the regions approximately 35 and 10 bases upstream of the transcriptional initiation site (referred to as -35/ -10 promoter sites) are particularly important for transcription initiation and therefore a single nucleotide mutation here can have dramatic effects (Einav and Phillips, 2019).

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Semi-rational design for promoters established; need analogous strategy for proteins.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to select residues for mutagenesis in proteins?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Structural data (crystal structures, AlphaFold) and in silico docking can guide targeted mutagenesis.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Use available structural information and computational tools to identify key residues.

tool_or_method.name: Structural analysis and in silico docking

evolution_trajectory.observation: Targeting only very relevant residues with computationally informed mutations can simplify library generation and reduce noise.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need to design the actual mutation library construction process.

evidence.quote_or_span: When targeting which sequences to randomize, one may take either a random, semi-rational or fully rational approach. This is frequently determined by the amount of information available for the protein of interest
Tertiary structures can also be predicted for proteins with a known sequence using computational approaches such as Rosetta (Rohl et al., 2004) or AlphaFold (Jumper et al., 2021).
In silico docking consisting of protein-protein or protein-ligand interactions (Lyskov and Gray, 2008; Lyskov et al., 2013) permits guided selection of amino acids involved in binding.

Targeting only very relevant residues with computationally informed mutations, or conservative mutations such as neutral drift mutations (Lynch and Hill, 1986), can simplify library generation, reduce library size, and decrease noise and workload.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Target residues identified; need to design primers and assembly strategy.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to introduce mutations at multiple sites and assemble full-length variants?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Fragmenting the sequence and using degenerate primers with overlap extension PCR will allow combinatorial assembly.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Design primers with degenerate bases at mutation sites, separate sequence into fragments, and assemble via overlap PCR.

tool_or_method.name: PCR-based site-directed mutagenesis with degenerate primers and overlap extension PCR

parameters.primer_design: degenerate bases at mutation sites, 20 bp overlaps
parameters.fragments: separated with mutation sites at ends

evolution_trajectory.observation: This approach introduces a variety of mutations at each site and enhances diversity by combinatorial assembly.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need to incorporate a substitution matrix to guide conservative mutations.

evidence.quote_or_span: A number of residues are targeted for mutagenesis around the area of interest
The protein sequence is then separated into several fragments with the mutation sites located at one end of each fragment.

Each fragment will be generated by PCR reactions consisting of the template to be mutated and a pair of primers. One of those primers (the mutation primer) includes degenerate bases in the middle to effect mutation.

The method described here introduces a variety of mutations at each mutation site by performing PCR-based, site directed mutagenesis, with primers containing degenerate bases at the desired mutation site.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Primer design strategy defined; need to bias mutations towards conservative changes.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to avoid entirely divergent mutations that are likely deleterious?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Using BLOSUM62 substitution matrix will guide conservative mutations.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Refer to BLOSUM62 matrix when designing degenerate primers.
evolution_trajectory.observation: BLOSUM62 is useful for generating variants that are dissimilar but not entirely divergent.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need to validate that in silico docking can successfully guide library design.

evidence.quote_or_span: When designing the primers, it is helpful to refer to an amino acid substitution matrix, such as the Block Substitution Matrix 62 (BLOSUM62) table

In the case of the BLOSUM62 matrix, the scores shown were determined based on sequences with an identity of 62% or less – making it useful for generating variants that are dissimilar to the starting sequence, but not entirely divergent

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Design principles established; need evidence that the approach works.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Has in silico guided library design been successfully used?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: In silico ligand docking can guide design of focused libraries for biosensor engineering.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Cite successful examples from literature.
evolution_trajectory.observation: In silico ligand docking in a comparative model was successfully used to engineer several biosensors for small molecules.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need to combine all components into a core method and address limitations.

evidence.quote_or_span: In silico ligand docking in a comparative model to guide the design of a focused library was successfully used to engineer several biosensors for small molecules (Jha et al., 2015; Jha et al., 2016; Shin et al., 2022).

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Individual components validated; need to integrate into a complete workflow.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to combine PCR mutagenesis, overlap PCR, and cloning efficiently?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Combining PCR-based site-directed mutagenesis, overlap extension PCR, and Gibson assembly offers superior flexibility and control.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Use Gibson assembly to clone the assembled library into a vector.
evolution_trajectory.observation: The combination is rare but offers superior flexibility, control of mutation bias, and ease of use.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need to acknowledge limitations of alternative methods and the method itself.

evidence.quote_or_span: The resulting gene or promoter library is then cloned into a vector by Gibson assembly
their combination is rare within the mutation library field, despite offering superior flexibility, control of mutation bias, and ease of use

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Core method defined; need to compare with existing methods.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How does this method compare to random mutagenesis and existing targeted methods?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Random mutagenesis produces more deleterious mutations; existing targeted methods have low library sizes.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Highlight advantages: reduced deleterious mutations and larger library sizes.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Random mutagenesis requires screening larger populations; SDM/SSM have restricted library sizes.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need to address a specific limitation: degenerate primer bias.

evidence.quote_or_span: Without designing a bias towards potentially useful mutations, it is expected that a much larger percentage of the mutations will be deleterious, which requires screening or selecting from a larger sampling of the population.

Targeted mutagenesis approaches, such as site directed mutagenesis (SDM) and site-saturation mutagenesis (SSM) have proven to be extremely useful, however, the relatively low library size that can be achieved through these methods has restricted their application in directed evolution approaches

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Degenerate primers may introduce bias due to differential annealing.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to ensure library quality and diversity?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Sequencing verification can detect bias and confirm desired mutations.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Include a verification step: sequence random clones or whole library.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Sequencing ensures quality assurance by verifying mutations and diversity.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need to acknowledge another limitation: requirement of prior knowledge.

evidence.quote_or_span: the different sequences may show differences in annealing during PCR resulting in a degree of bias

this protocol includes a verification step of either sequencing a random selection of clones isolated from the library (see Figure 6A), or sequencing DNA extracted from the whole library (see Figure 6B), for quality assurance

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Method is powerful but has limitations.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: What are the inherent limitations of this targeted approach?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: The method requires prior knowledge of the target and DNA synthesis cost can be high.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Acknowledge these limitations.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Method is limited by availability of knowledge about the targeted protein or promoter; DNA synthesis is still expensive for some applications.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Final validation through published research.

evidence.quote_or_span: This method is additionally limited by availability of knowledge about the targeted protein or promoter
Still, the high price, compared to other techniques, makes this technique appropriate for only some specific applications.

validation_methods: Published research papers employing the method
FACS-based screening and isolation of gain-of-function variants
metrics: Fold improvement in fluorescence or function
Percentage of deleterious vs. gain-of-function variants
success_verification.final_conclusion: The method successfully eliminates the bottleneck of individual testing by enabling combinatorial screening of large libraries, as demonstrated in multiple published studies across different microbial strains.
limitations: Requires prior knowledge of the target protein or promoter
Degenerate primers may introduce bias
DNA synthesis cost can be high for some applications

risk_notes: Paper is a methods/protocol paper, not a primary research article with new experimental data; trajectory is based on described methodology and cited validations.

Some coverage requirements (e.g., experimental timeline, FACS sorting strategy) are not explicitly included as steps but are referenced in evidence; they are implicitly covered in the design and screening steps.

source.title: High-throughput screening of spike variants uncovers the key residues that alter the affinity and antigenicity of SARS-CoV-2

source.title: ACIDES: on-line monitoring of forward genetic screens for protein engineering

initial_request.research_problem: Noisy NGS count data in forward genetic screens hinders accurate estimation of variant performance and convergence detection, necessitating a method for robust scoring, error estimation, and on-line monitoring.
initial_request.target_object: variant performance in forward genetic screens
initial_request.known_context: NGS data from forward genetic screens are vast and noisy, impeding estimation of variant performance. Scores need precise statistical error estimation to avoid winner's curse. It is hard to know when to end the experiment: too few rounds may select weak variants, too many rounds are costly.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: NGS data from forward genetic screens are vast and noisy, impeding estimation of variant performance.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Noisy NGS count data hinders accurate estimation of variant performance and convergence detection.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Combining statistical inference and in-silico simulations can improve performance estimation in library selection by attributing accurate statistical scores to individual variants.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Develop ACIDES, a method that combines statistical inference and in-silico simulations.
evolution_trajectory.observation: ACIDES is proposed to combine statistical inference and in-silico simulations to improve performance estimation.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need to design the statistical framework for selectivity estimation.

evidence.quote_or_span: the vast and noisy nature of the sequencing data impedes the estimation of the performance of individual variants

in order to rank the variants and to identify the best performing ones, the score should come with a precise estimation of its statistical error. As a consequence of the noise in the counts, some irrelevant variants might appear to be highly enriched (winner's curse)

when running DE over multiple rounds, it is hard to know when to end the experiment: performing too few rounds could lead to selection of weak variants, not representative of their true ranking. On the other hand, performing too many rounds is costly, time-consuming and even ethically questionable

we propose ACIDES that combines statistical inference and in-silico simulations to improve performance estimation in the library selection process by attributing accurate statistical scores to individual variants

evolution_trajectory.state_before: ACIDES is proposed to combine statistical inference and in-silico simulations.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to estimate selectivity and confidence intervals for each variant.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Using maximum likelihood estimation with a negative binomial noise model and softmax normalization will provide robust selectivity estimates and 95% confidence intervals.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Implement first step of ACIDES: estimate selectivity and 95% CI using MLE with negative binomial and softmax.
evolution_trajectory.observation: ACIDES estimates selectivity and 95% confidence interval for each variant.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate the negative binomial model against Poisson on plasmid library data.

evidence.quote_or_span: The first step of ACIDES estimates the selectivity of each individual variant present in the dataset (Fig. 1b) and its 95% confidence interval
our approach combines a robust inference framework (maximum likelihood estimation) with a better quantification of the NGS sampling noise
our approach benefits from a negative binomial distribution

source.title: Directed Evolution of Pseudomonas fluorescens Lipase Variants With Improved Thermostability Using Error-Prone PCR

source.title: NADPH biosensor-based identification of an alcohol dehydrogenase variant with improved catalytic properties caused by a single charge reversal at the protein surface

initial_request.research_problem: Improve catalytic properties of LbADH for 2,5-hexanedione reduction using NADPH biosensor-based FACS screening to overcome throughput limits.
initial_request.known_context: LbADH catalyzes NADPH-dependent reduction of prochiral ketones. Conventional screening methods are low-throughput, limiting directed evolution efforts.
constraints: Must use NADPH biosensor pSenSox for fluorescence-based detection
Screening must be performed in E. coli
quantifiable_goals: Identify LbADH variants with improved activity for 2,5-hexanedione reduction
Achieve higher fluorescence signal in biosensor assay compared to wild-type

evolution_trajectory.state_before: No high-throughput screening method available for LbADH variants.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to screen large LbADH libraries for improved 2,5-hexanedione reduction activity?

evolution_trajectory.decision: Design a FACS screening strategy with three positive selection steps followed by one negative selection step.
evolution_trajectory.observation: The entire screening process comprised three positive selection steps for cells showing high fluorescence after biotransformation of 2,5-hexanedione followed by a negative selection step. In the latter, cells showing high fluorescence independent of 2,5-hexanedione were excluded by collecting only those cells in the library that showed the same fluorescence as E. coli TOP10/pSenNeg cells lacking an active LbADH.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: The designed strategy needs to be implemented by generating a mutant library.

success_verification.final_conclusion: The FACS-based screening using the pSenSox biosensor successfully identified the LbADH^{K71E} variant with improved catalytic properties for 2,5-hexanedione reduction and broader substrate scope.
