evolution_trajectory.decision: Compare sp4 and sp5 energy differences.
evolution_trajectory.observation: sp4 minimized slightly better than sp5, contrary to expectation, especially for thymidine and raloxifene.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Select conjugate gradient with sp4 as default protocol.

evidence.quote_or_span: Although sp5 has been reported to be an improved version of sp4 [39], in our work sp4 minimized slightly better than sp5 in terms of energy differences between the initial and minimized structure, in particular for thymidine and raloxifene.
sp4 minimized slightly better than sp5 in terms of energy differences between the initial and minimized structure, in particular for thymidine and raloxifene.

evolution_trajectory.observation: Protocol selected based on performance and robustness.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate protocol against other force fields.

evidence.quote_or_span: Following the above observations, we chose conjugate gradient and sp4 as the minimization protocol for subsequent assessment of AMMOS.
we chose conjugate gradient and sp4 as the minimization protocol for subsequent assessment of AMMOS.
we accepted 2×500 iterations as an appropriate number of steps for the automatic AMMOS procedure

source.title: Hotspot Hunter: a computational system for large-scale screening and selection of candidate immunological hotspots in pathogen proteomes

initial_request.research_problem: Experimental identification of T-cell epitopes is laborious, costly, and impractical for large-scale screening across multiple HLA alleles and pathogen proteomes. Existing computational tools predict binding for single proteins and require manual integration, hindering systematic study of promiscuous epitopes and immunological hotspots.
initial_request.target_object: Immunological hotspots (clusters of promiscuous T-cell epitopes) in pathogen proteomes
initial_request.known_context: T-cell epitopes are peptides bound by MHC molecules; their experimental mapping is challenging due to large proteomes, MHC diversity, low epitope prevalence (~0.1–5%), high costs, and time-consuming assays. Existing prediction servers handle single proteins and lack hotspot prediction and large-scale analysis.
constraints: Must handle multiple HLA supertypes (A2, A3, B7, DR)
Must enable large-scale screening of pathogen proteomes
Must provide hotspot prediction and target selection
input_data: Protein sequences (single or multiple) from pathogen proteomes
quantifiable_goals: Achieve high predictive performance (Aroc) for each HLA supertype
Correctly predict known experimental hotspots
Enable identification of consensus hotspots across sequence variants

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Experimental T-cell epitope mapping is costly and low-throughput; existing computational tools are limited to single proteins and manual integration.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: No computational system exists for large-scale, automated prediction of immunological hotspots across multiple HLA supertypes and pathogen proteomes.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A computational system integrating ANN and SVM predictive engines can enable large-scale screening and selection of candidate immunological hotspots.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Design Hotspot Hunter with ANN and SVM as predictive engines, using soft computing integration for robust prediction.
evolution_trajectory.observation: ANN and SVM are chosen as complementary methods; soft computing integration is planned to combine their predictions.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need to compile training datasets for the predictive models.

evidence.quote_or_span: The high cost of peptide synthesis, limited access to human peripheral blood samples, and time-consuming experimental assays further add to the challenge.

Nearly all existing publicly available promiscuous T-cell epitope prediction servers perform prediction for a single protein sequence per submission, while those that accept multiple sequences are not tailor-made to predict hotspots; they rely on manual visualization techniques.

Given the large size of pathogen proteomes, it is painstaking to integrate the individual prediction results, and therefore impractical to use these tools for large-scale systematic study of promiscuous epitopes
Hotspot Hunter uses Artificial Neural Network (ANN) and Support Vector Machine (SVM) methods as predictive engines.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Predictive engines selected; no training data available.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need high-quality training datasets for four HLA supertypes (A2, A3, B7, DR).
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Compiling data from MHCPEP, IEDB, literature, and unpublished sources will provide sufficient training data; DR dataset from consistent experiments may be higher quality.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Compile training datasets: A2 (3050 peptides), A3 (2216), B7 (4102), DR (2396).

tool_or_method.name: Data compilation from MHCPEP, IEDB, literature, unpublished data

evolution_trajectory.observation: Datasets compiled; DR dataset expected higher quality due to consistent experimental conditions.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Build ANN models using the training data.

evidence.quote_or_span: These data were mainly from three sources, the MHCPEP database [24], IEDB [25], and published literature, and a set of HLA non-binding peptides (Brusic V., unpublished data).

Since the data originated from a single set of experiments, with relatively consistent experimental conditions, it is reasonable to expect that the DR training dataset is of higher quality relative to datasets aggregated from multiple sources.

The HLA-A2 training dataset had a total of 3050 (675 binders and 2375 non-binders) 9-mer peptides related to 15 variants of the HLA-A2 supertype (0201, 0202, 0203, 0204, 0205, 0206, 0207, 0208, 0209, 0210, 0211, 0214, 0217, 6802 and 6901).
The HLA-A3 training dataset had a total of 2216 (680 binders and 1536 non-binders) 9-mer peptides related to eight variants (0301, 0302, 1101, 1102, 3101, 3301, 3303 and 6801).
The HLA-DR training dataset had 2396 (448 binders and 1948 non-binders) 9-mer peptides related to six HLA-DRB1 variants (0101, 0301, 0701, 0801, 1101 and 1501).

The HLA-B7 training dataset had a total of 4102 (1258 binders and 2844 non-binders) 9-mer peptides related to 13 variants (0702, 1508, 3501, 3502, 3503, 5101, 5102, 5103, 5301, 5401, 5501, 5502 and 5601).

source.title: PreMode predicts mode-of-action of missense variants by deep graph representation learning of protein sequence and structural context

initial_request.research_problem: Predicting mode-of-action (molecular and genetic) of missense variants is challenging because existing methods focus on binary pathogenicity, which does not capture the multi-dimensional functional impacts. Mode-of-action is gene-specific, with limited labeled data per gene, and requires understanding of protein sequence and structural context.
initial_request.known_context: Pathogenicity prediction methods exist but do not capture complexity of functional and genetic effects.
constraints: Limited availability of mode-of-action data for most genes and protein families.
quantifiable_goals: Predict molecular mode-of-action (e.g., biochemical properties) with high Spearman correlation.
Predict genetic mode-of-action (gain/loss-of-function) with high AUC.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Pathogenicity prediction methods exist but do not capture multi-dimensional functional impacts.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Pathogenicity does not capture complexity of functional and genetic effects, limiting clinical utility.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Mode-of-action prediction should be gene-specific due to diverse protein functions; a universal predictor leads to conceptual ambiguity.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Define predictive tasks within individual proteins or protein families sharing similar functions.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Pathogenicity prediction insufficient; gene-specific mode-of-action needed.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Gene-specific hypothesis motivates curation of large mode-of-action datasets.

evidence.quote_or_span: pathogenicity does not capture the complexity of functional and genetic effects of variants
Such limitation reduced the utility of the methods in genetic analysis and clinical applications.

mode-of-action centers around how a variant disrupts the normal function of a protein. Given the inherent diversity of protein functions, attempting to define a universally applicable predictive task for all G/LoF variants across all proteins could lead to conceptual ambiguity.
we propose that such predictive tasks should be defined within the context of individual proteins or protein families that share similar functions.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Datasets curated but not characterized.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to understand structural and evolutionary differences between GoF and LoF variants.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: GoF and LoF variants have distinct structural and evolutionary properties.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform genome-wide comparison of GoF and LoF variant sites.
evolution_trajectory.observation: GoF variants more in low pLDDT regions and surfaces; LoF variants have bigger folding energy impact; both in conserved regions.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Characterization reveals data limitation challenge.

evidence.quote_or_span: GoF variants are relatively more likely to be located in regions with lower pLDDT (50 < pLDDT < 90) than LoF variants, and are relatively more likely to be on protein surfaces (rsa ≥ 25%)
LoF variants have an overall bigger impact on protein folding energy than GoF variants
both LoF and GoF variants are more likely to be in conserved regions than non-conserved regions

a Genome wide comparison of gain- and loss-of-function variant sites' pLDDT (AlphaFold2 prediction confidence), protein folding energy change, relevant solvent accessibility, and conservation in 2043 GoF, 7889 LoF variants across 1300 genes and 13704 benign variants from same set of genes, p values were calculated by two-sided Mann-Whitney test.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Characterization done, but data limitation identified.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Limited availability of mode-of-action data for most genes and protein families.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Data limitation is the main challenge for mode-of-action prediction.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Acknowledge limitation and design model with transfer learning.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Limited data per gene necessitates transfer learning.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Data limitation motivates design of PreMode with transfer learning.

evidence.quote_or_span: The main challenge is the limited availability of data for most genes and protein families.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Need a model that can leverage limited data and capture variant effects.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Existing methods do not capture direct and second-order impacts of amino acid alterations.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: SE(3)-equivariant graph neural networks with star-graph and KNN-graph can capture direct and second-order impacts.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Design PreMode architecture.

parameters.architecture: SE(3)-equivariant graph attention neural network with 4 layers
parameters.input_features: ESM2 embeddings (d=1280), amino acid frequencies from MSA (d=20), weighted covariance matrix, contact strength predictions

evolution_trajectory.observation: PreMode uses SE(3)-equivariant mechanisms on edge and node features through star-graph and KNN-graph.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Model design enables pretraining on pathogenicity.

evidence.quote_or_span: PreMode applies SE(3)-equivariant mechanisms on edge features and node features first through a star-graph that connects variant site with all other residues to capture the direct impacts of amino acid alterations on other residues, then through the k-nearest neighbor (KNN)-graph that connects each residue with its closest neighbor residues to capture the second order impacts
PreMode is an SE(3)-equivariant graph attention neural network with 4 layers.
First layer is a feature embedding layer that encodes the input features into latent dimensions.
Second and third layers are equivariant graph attention layers designed to model interactions between the connected nodes

evolution_trajectory.state_before: PreMode designed but not trained.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need pretraining on large-scale pathogenicity data to learn general variant effects.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Pretraining on pathogenicity will improve mode-of-action prediction.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Pretrain PreMode on 148,324 pathogenic/benign variants.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Achieved AUROC 0.932 on independent test set.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Pretrained model enables transfer learning for molecular mode-of-action.

evidence.quote_or_span: We collected a total of 148,324 missense variants for training, including 51,494 benign variants from PrimateAI $^{18}$ and 32,350 non-overlapping benign variants from ClinVar $^{59}$ with at least one-star non-conflict submits that labeled as “benign” or “likely-benign”; 64,480 “pathogenic” and “likely-pathogenic” variants from ClinVar database with at least one-star non-conflict submits $^{8}$ and non-overlapping variants from HGMD $^{41}$ (Table 2).

We collected 83,844 benign variants from ClinVar $^{59}$ and PrimateAI $^{18}$ in 13,748 genes, and 64,480 pathogenic variants from ClinVar $^{59}$ and HGMD $^{41}$ in 3703 genes (“Methods: Training and testing datasets”).

Instead, we used independent testing data for which the pathogenicity label was entirely based on statistical evidence, that is, 533 pathogenic missense variants in cancer hotspots from cBioportal $^{50,52,60}$ , and same number of benign variants in the same genes randomly selected from common variants in primates $^{18}$ .

For testing, we included 1066 missense variants, including 533 pathogenic missense variants from somatic missense hotspots in 153 cancer driver genes that not annotated in the training data; and 533 benign variants from the same genes, randomly selected from ClinVar and PrimateAI that not overlapping with training dataset (Table 2).

PreMode performed better in all the genes than baseline methods. It reached average AUC of 0.8–0.9 in genes RET, KCNJ11, CACNA1A and BRAF, average AUCs of 0.7–0.8 in SCN5A, SCN2A, TP53, and ABCC8

source.title: Metabolic engineering and adaptive laboratory evolution of Kluyveromyces Marxianus for lactic acid production

initial_request.research_problem: Engineering Kluyveromyces marxianus for efficient lactic acid production under low-pH conditions to reduce neutralization costs and enable use of cheap feedstocks.
initial_request.known_context: Current lactic acid production via LAB requires large amounts of neutralization agents and complex nutrients, limiting sustainability. S. cerevisiae is suboptimal due to Crabtree effect and low pH sensitivity.
constraints: Low-pH tolerance
Avoidance of Crabtree effect
Use of cheap feedstocks
quantifiable_goals: High lactic acid titer
High yield
Low-pH production

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Lactic acid production by LAB requires neutralization and complex nutrients; S. cerevisiae is suboptimal due to Crabtree effect and low pH sensitivity.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need for a robust, acid-tolerant, Crabtree-negative host for low-pH lactic acid production.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: K. marxianus is a promising alternative due to acid tolerance, fast growth, xylose utilization, and GRAS status.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Proceed with K. marxianus as host organism.
evolution_trajectory.observation: K. marxianus is acid tolerant (pH 3-8), can metabolize xylose, and has GRAS status.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Design a study to exploit K. marxianus diversity and engineering.

evidence.quote_or_span: it requires complex and expensive feedstocks and large amounts of neutralization agents for pH control during fermentation.
as a Crabtree-positive yeast, S. cerevisiae tends to favor ethanol production under high sugar concentrations

it is acid tolerant with a pH range for growth between 3 and 8

Variants were filtered using DP>40 (depth of coverage greater than 40), FS<40 (Fisher Strand Bias), MQ>40 (mapping quality), MQRankSum >-10, ReadPosRankSum >-8, QUAL>50 (variant quality score), and a variant frequency threshold>12.

we screen 168 different K. marxianus strains to identify 10 genetically distinct strains that show superior industrially-relevant phenotypes
we engineer each of the 10 strains to produce LA and further optimize production in the best candidates by using adaptive laboratory evolution (ALE) and process tuning

source.title: Protein engineering and iterative multimodule optimization for vitamin B6 production in Escherichia coli

initial_request.research_problem: Engineering Escherichia coli for efficient microbial production of vitamin B6 (pyridoxine) as a green alternative to chemical synthesis, overcoming low catalytic efficiencies of natural enzymes and tight metabolic regulation by PLP.
initial_request.known_context: Chemical synthesis of vitamin B6 uses toxic solvents (benzene, toluene) and poses safety risks. The natural DXP-dependent pathway enzymes (Epd, PdxA, PdxJ) have low catalytic efficiencies and tight regulation by PLP.
constraints: Avoid toxic solvents
Overcome PLP feedback inhibition
Maintain PLP supply for growth
input_data: Crystal structures of PdxA and PdxJ
quantifiable_goals: Increase PN titer
Achieve high productivity in fed-batch fermentation

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Chemical synthesis of vitamin B6 uses toxic solvents and poses safety risks. Natural DXP-dependent pathway enzymes have low catalytic efficiencies and tight regulation by PLP.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to relieve PLP feedback inhibition and toxicity to enable high-flux PN production.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Decoupling PN production from PLP-dependent growth can relieve feedback inhibition and toxicity, enabling higher flux.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Adopt a growth decoupling strategy by creating parallel metabolic pathways.
evolution_trajectory.observation: A growth decoupling strategy was proposed to rapidly accumulate biomass without inhibiting product formation.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Design parallel pathways by deleting pdxH to block PNP→PLP conversion.

evidence.quote_or_span: a growth decoupling strategy was used to rapidly accumulate biomass without inhibiting product formation

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Design of pdxH knockout to decouple pathways.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Will pdxH knockout cause growth defect due to PLP deficiency?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Knockout of pdxH will block PLP synthesis, causing poor growth unless PLP is supplied exogenously.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Knock out pdxH in E. coli MG1655 to create strain LL001 and test growth.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Elimination of the pdxH gene resulted in poor growth due to a lack of PLP and accumulation of PNP. Growth partially restored by 1 μM PL but not PN.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need to supply PLP for growth without restoring PNP→PLP conversion.

evidence.quote_or_span: Elimination of the pdxH gene resulted in poor growth due to a lack of PLP and accumulation of PNP
we first knocked out the native pdxH gene encoding PNP oxidase to obtain strain LL001.
Elimination of the pdxH gene resulted in poor growth due to a lack of PLP and accumulation of PNP
E. coli MG1655, with better performance, was used as the starting strain

evolution_trajectory.state_before: pdxH knockout strain LL001 shows poor growth due to PLP deficiency.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to supply PLP for growth without restoring PNP→PLP conversion.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Integrating B. subtilis pdxST (direct PLP synthesis) at pdxH locus will restore growth.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Integrate B. subtilis pdxST at pdxH locus with RBS optimization.
evolution_trajectory.observation: unknown
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Construct and test strain LL005.

evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Strain LL005 with pdxST will show growth similar to WT.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Construct LL005 and measure growth rate.
evolution_trajectory.observation: LL005 showed a growth rate similar to that of the WT strain.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Complete PN pathway by adding pdxP.

evidence.quote_or_span: LL005 showed a growth rate similar to that of the WT strain
the pdxP gene was inserted into the pta locus of E. coli in the above-described strain LL005

evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Mutations H136N, P245C, V149L, T165R/T285L will improve PdxA activity and PN titer.

evolution_trajectory.observation: Two rounds of PdxA redesign yield mutants with titers exceeding 2.0 mg/L. MD simulations show H136N improves substrate binding energy (-174.00 vs -163.16 kcal/mol).

evolution_trajectory.state_before: PdxA mutants improved; PdxJ remains rate-limiting.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can iterative computational redesign improve PdxJ?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Four rounds of iterative design will yield PdxJ mutants with significantly higher activity.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform four rounds of computational design and experimental validation for PdxJ.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Four rounds yield triple mutant E104T/I218L/G194C (PdxJ1) with 18-fold titer increase over control; 212 mutants constructed.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Improve upstream enzyme Epd via homolog mining.

evidence.quote_or_span: The yield of the strain expressing mutant E104T/I218L/G194C (LL239) was 18-fold higher than that of the control strain
A total of 212 mutants were constructed, including 26 single mutations, 62 double mutations, 60 triple mutations, and 64 quadruple mutations.
four rounds of computational designs were simulated by using the positive mutants from the previous round of experimental results as a template

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Downstream enzymes improved; upstream Epd is a bottleneck.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can genome mining identify a more efficient Epd homolog?

evolution_trajectory.decision: Screen Epd homologs in LL239 background.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Gni_Epd from Glaciecola nitratireducens gives 2.2-fold increase in PN titer (29 mg/L) due to improved catalytic efficiency for E4P and enhanced binding of E4P and NAD+.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Address serine pathway bottleneck and 4HTP toxicity via modular optimization.

evidence.quote_or_span: the gene from Glaciecola nitratireducens resulted in a 2.2-fold increase of the product titer, reaching 29 mg/L
This suggests that the increase in product yield was primarily attributed to the improved catalytic capacity of Gni\_Epd, as well as its enhanced binding and recruitment of E4P and NAD $^{+}$ .
3c), the catalytic efficiency of Gni\_Epd was significantly enhanced (Table 1), particularly for the substrate E4P (with an almost 3-fold improvement in $k_{\text{cat}}/K_{\text{m}}$ ).
The yield of the strain expressing mutant E104T/I218L/G194C (LL239) was 18-fold higher than that of the control strain (Supplementary Data 3), and this mutant protein was named PdxJ1.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Upstream and downstream enzymes improved; serine pathway competes for precursors.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can blocking serine pathway increase PN yield without harming growth?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Knockout of serA will increase PN yield but may reduce biomass; overexpressing serC may balance.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Knock out serA and test growth and PN yield; supplement glycine.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Knockout of serA increases PN yield but reduces biomass; growth not restored by serine (toxic), partially by glycine. Overexpress serC instead to balance growth and production.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Partition pathway into push-pull modules to manage 4HTP toxicity.

evidence.quote_or_span: the growth was found to be almost completely restored when 10 mM glycine was added

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Push-pull module design.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: What plasmid copy numbers balance expression and avoid toxicity?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Low-copy plasmid for push module (due to 4HTP toxicity), medium-high copy for pull module.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Test p15ASI (low-copy) for push and pRSFDuet-1 (medium-high) for pull.
evolution_trajectory.observation: The push module was optimally expressed from the low-copy plasmid p15ASI, while the medium-high copy plasmid pRSFDuet-1 was better suited for the pull module.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Tune promoter strength for downstream module.

evidence.quote_or_span: the push module was optimally expressed from the low-copy plasmid p15ASI, while the medium-high copy plasmid pRSFDuet-1 was better suited for the pull module
the upstream module required low expression because the intermediate 4HTP is toxic

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Copy numbers optimized; need to fine-tune expression of PdxA and PdxJ.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: What promoter strength maximizes PN titer without toxicity?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Moderate promoter P3 will give best PN titer.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Test a series of promoters for downstream module.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Moderate promoter P3 gives best PN titer (115 mg/L), avoiding too strong or too weak expression.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Combine best enzyme mutants under optimized expression.

evidence.quote_or_span: promoters with low transcriptional intensity generally increased the PN production, but it did not increase when we used a promoter with a lower intensity than P3
the strength of the P3 promoter may be more suitable for the expression of pdxA and pdxJ for PN production

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Optimized modules and promoters; best enzyme mutants available.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Will combining best PdxJ and PdxA mutants under P3 promoter achieve high titer?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Combining PdxJ1 and PdxA2 under P3 will synergistically increase PN titer.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Construct strain LL388 expressing PdxJ1 and PdxA2 under P3 promoter.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Combine best PdxJ mutant (PdxJ1) and PdxA mutant (PdxA2) under P3 promoter, achieving 453.78 mg/L PN in shake flasks, 48.86-fold over upstream-only strain.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate performance in fed-batch fermentation.

evidence.quote_or_span: PdxA2 (H136N) was the best PdxA mutant, and when expressed under the control of the P3 promoter, it enhanced the PN titer to 453.78 mg/L in shake flasks
produced 48.86-fold more PN than the strain expressing only the native upstream pathway

Overexpression of the up- or downstream module alone could slightly increase the PN titer, but they could significantly boost the titer when overexpressed in combination (approximately 48.86-fold increase if downstream module expression in the strain included the upstream module, and 247.78-fold conversely).

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Shake-flask titer of 453.78 mg/L achieved.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can high titer be maintained in controlled bioreactor conditions?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Fed-batch fermentation will increase PN production due to better control of nutrients and oxygen.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform fed-batch fermentation of LL388 in 5 L bioreactor.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Fed-batch fermentation of final strain LL388 produces 1409 mg/L PN in 48 h, productivity 29.16 mg/L/h, 3.1-fold higher than shake flask.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Final validation complete.

evidence.quote_or_span: After 48 h, 1409 mg/L PN was produced, which was 3.1-fold higher than in shake flask culture
equivalent to a productivity of 29.16 mg/L/h
The fed-batch culture was performed in a 5 L bioreactor with a 2 L working volume.
The DO level was controlled at 30% air saturation by coupling varying the agitation speed from 300 to 800 rpm.
PdxA2 (H136N) was the best PdxA mutant, and when expressed under the control of the P3 promoter, it enhanced the PN titer to 453.78 mg/L in shake flasks

success_verification.final_conclusion: Engineered E. coli strain LL388 produced 1409 mg/L PN in 48 h fed-batch fermentation, demonstrating successful decoupling of growth and production, enzyme engineering, and modular optimization.
limitations: Titer still below industrial requirements
4HTP toxicity not fully eliminated
Serine pathway manipulation caused growth defect

source.title: Analysis of protein secretion in Bacillus subtilis by combining a secretion stress biosensor strain with an in vivo split GFP assay

initial_request.research_problem: Activity-based screening methods are not universally applicable for detecting secreted proteins, limiting high-throughput optimization of recombinant protein secretion in Bacillus subtilis.
initial_request.target_object: Recombinant protein secretion in Bacillus subtilis
initial_request.known_context: Activity-based screening is limited to proteins with detectable enzymatic activity.
constraints: High-throughput compatible
Activity-independent detection
quantifiable_goals: Develop an activity-independent high-throughput method for monitoring secretion and stress
Optimize cutinase secretion using signal peptide library

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Activity-based screening methods are not universally applicable for detecting secreted proteins.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need for an activity-independent, high-throughput method to monitor secretion and stress.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Combining a split GFP assay with a secretion stress biosensor enables activity-independent, high-throughput monitoring of secretion and stress.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Develop a combined online monitoring system consisting of an in vivo split GFP assay and an mCherry-based secretion stress biosensor.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Hypothesis formulated based on known limitations of activity-based screening.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Design the secretion stress biosensor.

evidence.quote_or_span: However, not every protein can be detected by activity-based screening.
We therefore developed a combined online monitoring system, consisting of an in vivo split GFP assay for activity-independent target detection and an mCherry-based secretion stress biosensor.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Library constructed.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to evaluate secretion stress for different signal peptides.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Online monitoring of mCherry fluorescence in B. subtilis PAL5 will reveal secretion stress levels for Cut11 fused to SP_Pel, SP_Epr, SP_Bsn.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform online monitoring of secretion stress for Cut11 fused to three signal peptides (SP_Pel, SP_Epr, SP_Bsn) in B. subtilis PAL5 using BioLector.
evolution_trajectory.observation: All cultures showed similar growth; SP_Pel exhibited about 30% higher mCherry fluorescence.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Analyze correlation between enzymatic activity and secretion stress.

evidence.quote_or_span: All cutinase producing cultures showed similar growth behavior (Fig. 1A, inset), which allowed a direct comparison of mCherry fluorescence intensities
For the $SP_{Pel}$ variant, secretion stress increased slightly earlier and more strongly, resulting in about 30% higher mCherry fluorescence compared to the other cutinase-secreting strains.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Secretion stress data obtained.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Is there a correlation between enzymatic activity and secretion stress?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Enzymatic activity and secretion stress may not correlate for these signal peptides.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Compare enzymatic activities and secretion stress for SP_Pel, SP_Epr, SP_Bsn.
evolution_trajectory.observation: No correlation observed between enzymatic activity and secretion stress.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Develop an activity-independent detection method (split GFP).

evidence.quote_or_span: a correlation between the enzymatic activity and secretion stress was not observed

evolution_trajectory.state_before: No correlation between activity and stress.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need an activity-independent method to detect secreted protein.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: In vivo split GFP assay can detect secreted Cut11 by adding GFP1-10 to culture broth.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Develop in vivo split GFP assay for online detection of secreted Cut11 by adding GFP1-10 to culture broth during cultivation.
evolution_trajectory.observation: In vivo holo-GFP reconstitution enabled detection of secreted Cut11.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate split GFP assay against enzymatic activity.

evidence.quote_or_span: we tested the addition of GFP1-10 to the culture broth during cultivation thereby enabling in vivo holo-GFP reconstitution
GFP1-10 solution was added directly to the culture broth at a final concentration of 3% (v/v) after 16 h of cultivation

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Split GFP assay developed.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Does split GFP fluorescence correlate with enzymatic activity?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Split GFP fluorescence will show good correlation with enzymatic activity for Cut11 secretion.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Validate split GFP assay by comparing fluorescence with enzymatic activity using pNPP substrate.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Good correlation observed between split GFP fluorescence and enzymatic activity.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Evaluate simultaneous use of biosensor and split GFP assay.

evidence.quote_or_span: Using the split GFP-based detection assay, we demonstrated a good correlation between the amount of secreted cutinase and the enzymatic activity.
A good correlation was observed between split GFP fluorescence and the corresponding enzymatic activity determined with the substrate pNPP
A good correlation was observed between split GFP fluorescence and the corresponding enzymatic activity determined with the substrate pNPP (Fig. 2B).
The results therefore clearly demonstrate the applicability of the in vivo split GFP assay as an easy-to-perform and time-saving in vivo assay suitable for online detection of secreted enzymes.
By combining both methods, an activity-independent high-throughput method was created, that allowed optimization of Cut11 secretion.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Split GFP assay validated.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to evaluate simultaneous use of biosensor and split GFP assay with varying secretion levels.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Modulating spacer length will vary Cut11 secretion, allowing correlation analysis between split GFP and enzymatic activity.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Evaluate simultaneous use of mCherry secretion stress biosensor and split GFP assay by varying spacer length (4 to 12 nt) for SP_Pel, SP_Epr, SP_Bsn.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Simultaneous monitoring data collected; split GFP and enzymatic activity correlated.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Analyze correlation between split GFP and enzymatic activity.

evidence.quote_or_span: we evaluated the simultaneous use of the mCherry-based secretion stress biosensor strain B. subtilis PAL5 and the in vivo split GFP assay
By combining both methods, an activity-independent high-throughput method was created, that allowed optimization of Cut11 secretion.
# Construction of Cut11 signal peptide library
The plasmid pBS-4nt-SP $_{\text{Pel}}$ -Cut11 [29] was used to construct the Cut11 signal peptide library.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Simultaneous monitoring data collected.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: What is the quantitative correlation between split GFP and enzymatic activity?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Split GFP fluorescence will correlate with enzymatic activity, but sensitivity may decrease for low-performing variants.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Calculate correlation between split GFP fluorescence and enzymatic activities.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Good correlation found (r=0.85, R²=0.73), but sensitivity decreased for low-performing variants.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Analyze stress-activity correlation per signal peptide.

evidence.quote_or_span: the sensitivity of the in vivo split GFP assay decreased with inferior performing variants

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Signal peptide-specific correlations observed.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Why does SP_Pel show disproportionately high stress with longer spacers?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Disproportionally high increase in secretion stress for SP_Pel with spacer ≥7 nt may indicate improper protein folding, reducing efficient secretion.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Identify limitation: high stress with SP_Pel and spacer ≥7 nt suggests misfolding.
evolution_trajectory.observation: For spacer lengths ≥7 nt, increase in stress was higher than increase in cutinase amount/activity, indicating possible misfolding.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Screen signal peptide library to find optimal secretion.

evidence.quote_or_span: for spacer lengths≥7 nt the increase of secretion stress response was higher than the increase in cutinase amount and enzymatic activity
The disproportionally high increase in secretion stress may indicate improper protein folding at a significant rate, thereby reducing efficient protein secretion.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Limitation identified with SP_Pel.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to screen the full signal peptide library to identify high-secreting variants.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Screening the SP-Cut11 library with split GFP and stress biosensor will identify clones with high secretion and acceptable stress.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Screen 480 clones from SP-Cut11 library using split GFP assay and secretion stress biosensor.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Correlation between split GFP and activity (r=0.77, R²=0.61) but no correlation with stress.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate top clones from screening.

evidence.quote_or_span: a library of 173 different B. subtilis signal peptides fused to the cutinase was screened for enzymatically active secreted protein and secretion stress response
480 individual clones were analyzed with respect to the amount and activity of the secreted cutinase and the accompanying secretion stress
no correlation was observed between the secretion stress response and the amount/activity of the secreted cutinase
Screening of the signal peptide library again revealed a correlation between results obtained with the split GFP assay and enzymatic activity (r=0.77; $R^{2}=0.
77; $R^{2}=0.61$ ), although with reduced sensitivity for low-secreting variants.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Library screening completed.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to validate top clones from screening to confirm secretion performance.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Recultivating top clones as triplicates will confirm high secretion and identify signal peptides.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Select five clones with highest split GFP, recultivate as biological triplicates, and identify signal peptides.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Identified signal peptides SP_NamZ, SP_Mpr, SP_DacB; SP_DacB showed higher stress despite similar secretion.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Analyze overall relationship between stress and secretion.

evidence.quote_or_span: we selected five clones with the highest level of cutinase secretion based on the split GFP measurement that were subsequently recultivated as biological triplicates

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Top clones validated.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: What is the overall relationship between secretion stress and secretion levels?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Secretion stress only partially reflects secretion levels; protein misfolding competes with secretion.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Conclude that secretion stress partially reflects secretion levels, with misfolding causing high stress and low secretion.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Secretion stress response only partially reflects secretion levels; protein misfolding competes with effective secretion.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Acknowledge remaining limitations.

evidence.quote_or_span: the secretion stress response only partially reflects secretion levels
Protein misfolding competes with effective secretion leading to high-level stress and disproportionately low protein secretion.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Overall conclusion drawn.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: The exact relation between stress response and amount of secreted enzyme remains unclear.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Further studies are needed to elucidate the stress-secretion relationship.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Note limitation: the relation between stress response and amount of secreted enzyme remains to be elucidated.
evolution_trajectory.observation: The relation between stress response and amount of secreted enzyme or extracellular enzymatic activity remains to be elucidated in more detail.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: End of trajectory.

evidence.quote_or_span: the relation between stress response and amount of secreted enzyme or extracellular enzymatic activity remains to be elucidated in more detail

validation_methods: Correlation between split GFP fluorescence and enzymatic activity (pNPP assay)
Recultivation of top clones as biological triplicates
success_verification.final_conclusion: The combined split GFP and secretion stress biosensor system enables activity-independent high-throughput screening, but secretion stress only partially reflects secretion levels due to protein misfolding.
limitations: The relation between stress response and amount of secreted enzyme remains to be elucidated.
Sensitivity of split GFP assay decreased for low-performing variants.
Disproportionally high stress with SP_Pel and spacer ≥7 nt suggests misfolding.

source.title: Development and characterization of a glycine biosensor system for fine-tuned metabolic regulation in Escherichia coli

initial_request.research_problem: Lack of in vivo glycine biosensors in E. coli for dynamic metabolic regulation and directed evolution.
initial_request.known_context: Existing biosensors are insufficient in number and performance, limiting metabolic engineering applications. Few reports use glycine riboswitches for controlling indirectly related metabolic pathways.
constraints: Must function in E. coli
Must enable dynamic regulation and high-throughput screening
input_data: Natural glycine riboswitches from B. subtilis, S. pyogenes, V. cholerae, F. nucleatum
quantifiable_goals: Develop glycine-ON and -OFF riboswitches with improved dynamic range and repression
Achieve tunable metabolic regulation and directed evolution of enzymes

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Insufficient number and performance of biosensors, especially for glycine, limits metabolic engineering applications.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: No effective in vivo glycine biosensors available for dynamic regulation and directed evolution in E. coli.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Synthetic glycine-ON and -OFF riboswitches can be developed from natural riboswitches for metabolic regulation and directed evolution in E. coli.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Proceed to screen natural glycine riboswitches for functionality in E. coli.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Natural glycine riboswitches exist but have not been adapted for in vivo use in E. coli.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need to test natural riboswitches in E. coli to identify a functional starting point.

evidence.quote_or_span: the existing number and performance of biosensors is far from sufficient, limiting their potential applications in metabolic engineering
the number of currently available biosensors is still limited in comparison with the vast number of metabolite molecules

we developed the synthetic glycine-ON and -OFF riboswitches for metabolic regulation and directed evolution of enzyme in Escherichia coli

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Hypothesis formulated; need to identify a functional natural glycine riboswitch in E. coli.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Unknown which natural glycine riboswitch will function as an activator in E. coli.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: At least one natural glycine riboswitch will function as an activator in E. coli with a GFP reporter.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Screen four natural glycine riboswitches in E. coli using a GFP reporter.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Only the B. subtilis glycine riboswitch functioned as an activator to promote downstream gfp gene expression.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: B. subtilis riboswitch is functional; use it to construct a dual genetic selection system.

evidence.quote_or_span: only the glycine riboswitch from B. subtilis functioned as an activator to promote the downstream gfp gene expression

evolution_trajectory.state_before: B. subtilis glycine-ON riboswitch identified as functional in E. coli.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Construct tetA-based dual genetic selection cassette.
evolution_trajectory.observation: The tetA-based dual genetic selection system was successfully constructed using the B. subtilis glycine riboswitch.

evidence.quote_or_span: the tetA gene was fused with the gfp gene under the control of the B. subtilis glycine-ON riboswitch

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Dual selection system constructed.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to generate diversity in the riboswitch to improve its properties.

evidence.quote_or_span: the glycine riboswitch library and backbone fragment were assembled together via Gibson assembly and transformed into E. coli MG1655 competent cell after four rounds of error-prone PCR

By contrast, the E4GS strain (E4G2 harboring pZY48-glyOFF6-glyA, glyA gene was regulated by glyOFF6) exhibited a 104% increase of serine production (660.48 mg L $^{-1}$ ) with a yield of 85.

evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Coupling AGXT activity to glyOFF6-controlled tetA enables high-throughput screening for improved AGXT variants.

source.title: Adaptive laboratory evolution of β-caryophyllene producing Saccharomyces cerevisiae

initial_request.research_problem: Improve β-caryophyllene production in S. cerevisiae using adaptive laboratory evolution with oxidative stress as selective pressure.
initial_request.known_context: β-caryophyllene is a sesquiterpene with antioxidant properties. Rational engineering has been used to increase production, but additional targets may be identified through adaptive laboratory evolution.
constraints: Use oxidative stress as selective pressure
Work with S. cerevisiae strains
input_data: Parental strain YAG118 (ctt1Δ background with QHS1 and FPP overproduction cassette)
quantifiable_goals: Increase β-caryophyllene production (mg/g DCW)

evolution_trajectory.state_before: β-caryophyllene production in S. cerevisiae is limited; oxidative stress tolerance may be linked to production.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Unknown whether higher β-caryophyllene production confers a growth advantage under oxidative stress.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: In an oxidative environment, strains producing more β-caryophyllene (an antioxidant) will have a growth advantage.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Test correlation between β-caryophyllene production and H2O2 tolerance using constructed strains.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Hypothesis formulated based on antioxidant properties of β-caryophyllene.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need to construct strains with varying production levels to test hypothesis.

evidence.quote_or_span: The strategy is based on the hypothesis that in an oxidative environment, the strain that produces more of an antioxidant product will have a growth advantage.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Hypothesis formulated; need strains with varying β-caryophyllene production.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to create isogenic strains differing only in β-caryophyllene production to test correlation with H2O2 tolerance.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Strains with higher β-caryophyllene production will show better growth under H2O2 stress.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Construct strains YAG110, YAG111, YAG114, YAG115 in ctt1Δ background with different combinations of QHS1 and FPP overproduction cassette.

integrated_genes: FPP overproduction cassette (tHMG1, HMG2(K6R), UPC2-1, ERG20)

evolution_trajectory.observation: Strains constructed: YAG110 (non-producer), YAG114 (non-producer with FPP cassette), YAG111 (producer with QHS1), YAG115 (producer with QHS1 and FPP cassette).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Test growth of these strains under continuous H2O2 exposure.

evidence.quote_or_span: strains that produce varying levels of β-caryophyllene were constructed in the ctt1Δ strain, resulting in non-producers YAG110 (BY4741 ctt1Δ) and YAG114 (YAG110 with a genome-integrated farnesyl pyrophosphate [FPP] overproduction cassette), and producers YAG111 (YAG110 with the β-caryophyllene synthase QHS1 gene integrated in the genome) and YAG115 (YAG110 with QHS1 and FPP overproduction cassette integrated in the genome).

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Strains with varying β-caryophyllene production constructed.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Does higher β-caryophyllene production correlate with better growth under continuous H2O2 exposure?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: YAG115 (highest producer) will show growth at high H2O2 concentrations.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform continuous H2O2 exposure growth test on YAG110, YAG111, YAG114, YAG115.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Only YAG115 grew at 100 mM H2O2, with long lag phase and low final cell density.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Continuous exposure too harsh; shift to periodic H2O2 challenge for ALE.

evidence.quote_or_span: The highest producer, YAG115, was the only strain able to grow in 100 mM hydrogen peroxide, although with a very long lag phase and with a low final cell density.

strains that produce varying levels of β-caryophyllene were constructed in the ctt1Δ strain, resulting in non-producers YAG110 (BY4741 ctt1Δ) and YAG114 (YAG110 with a genome-integrated farnesyl pyrophosphate [FPP] overproduction cassette), and producers YAG111 (YAG110 with the β-caryophyllene synthase QHS1 gene integrated in the genome) and YAG115 (YAG110 with QHS1 and FPP overproduction cassette integrated in the genome).

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Continuous H2O2 exposure too harsh; need a selective pressure that allows gradual adaptation.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to apply oxidative stress to enrich for hyperproducers without killing all cells?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Periodic H2O2 challenge will allow survival and enrichment of hyperproducers.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Initiate ALE with periodic H2O2 challenge, splitting population into P1 and P2 with different stress ramp-up schedules.

tool_or_method.name: Adaptive laboratory evolution (ALE) with periodic H2O2 challenge

evolution_trajectory.observation: ALE initiated; populations P1 and P2 evolved under periodic H2O2 stress.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Monitor production over time in evolving populations.

evidence.quote_or_span: Using a 30-min exposure to 1 M hydrogen peroxide, we subjected four time point samples from the evolved population: P1 (day 4), P2 (day 8), P2 (day 11), P2 (day 14) and P2 (day 18) to screen for hyperproducers.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: ALE populations evolving under periodic H2O2 stress.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Do production levels increase in the evolving populations?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Production will increase over time in response to oxidative stress selection.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Measure β-caryophyllene production at multiple time points in P1 and P2.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Production peaks and declines observed; P2 showed higher production than P1.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Isolate hyperproducers from P2 using high H2O2 challenge.

evidence.quote_or_span: Using a 30-min exposure to 1 M hydrogen peroxide, we subjected four time point samples from the evolved population: P1 (day 4), P2 (day 8), P2 (day 11), P2 (day 14) and P2 (day 18) to screen for hyperproducers.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Hyperproducer mutants isolated; genetic basis unknown.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which mutations are responsible for increased production?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Genome sequencing will reveal mutations enriched in hyperproducers.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Sequence genomes of four P2 population time points, five best isolated mutants, and parental strain.
evolution_trajectory.observation: >200 mutations in population samples; 13 unique mutations selected from isolates for further analysis.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Reconstruct candidate mutations in parental strain to validate effect on production.

evidence.quote_or_span: Large numbers (>200) of mutations above the cutoff threshold were observed in the population samples.
a total of 13 unique mutations selected for further analyses

evolution_trajectory.state_before: 13 candidate mutations identified; their individual effects on production unknown.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which of the 13 mutations actually improve β-caryophyllene production?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Some of the identified mutations will increase production when reconstructed in the parental strain.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Reconstruct each of the 13 mutations individually in YAG118 using CRISPR-Cas9 and measure production.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Only STE6 T1025N (3.7-fold to 12.6 mg/g DCW) and MST27/tR(UCU)G1 intergenic (3-fold to 10.3 mg/g DCW) improved production.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Test if combining these two mutations has an additive effect.

evidence.quote_or_span: Only two mutations led to improved production, the mutation in STE6 gene and a mutation in intergenic region of between MST27/tR(UCU)G1
STE6 T1025N mutation improved production 3.7 fold to 12.6 mg/g DCW
The MST27/tR(UCU)G1 intergenic mutation improved production threefold to 10.3 mg/g DCW
CRISPR–Cas9 using the one plasmid-system with pCRCT developed by $[43]$ was used for site-directed mutagenesis to reconstruct identified mutations into YAG118.
The data showed that five (CDC39, tK(CUU)C/MAK32 int, RIF1 D709N, STE6 T1025N and YAL064W B/TDA8 int) out of 13 mutations did not impact growth under normal culture conditions (see Table 2).
five (CDC39, tK(CUU)C/MAK32 int, RIF1 D709N, STE6 T1025N and YAL064W B/TDA8 int) out of 13 mutations did not impact growth under normal culture conditions

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Two beneficial mutations identified; their combined effect unknown.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Does combining STE6 T1025N and MST27/tR(UCU)G1 intergenic mutations further increase production?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Combining the two mutations will have an additive or synergistic effect on production.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Construct a double mutant (STE6 T1025N + MST27/tR(UCU)G1 int) and measure production.

tool_or_method.name: CRISPR-Cas9 double mutagenesis and production assay

evolution_trajectory.observation: No further increase in production observed when mutations were combined.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Characterize the mechanism of STE6 T1025N mutation.

evidence.quote_or_span: No further increase in production was observed when these two mutations were combined.
The STE6 T1025N mutation was found only in mutant P11M1 and MST27/tR(UCU)G1 intergenic mutation was identified in all the isolated mutants sequenced.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: STE6 T1025N improves production; mechanism unknown.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Is STE6 T1025N a loss-of-function or gain-of-function mutation?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: STE6 T1025N is a gain-of-function mutation that enhances β-caryophyllene efflux.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform STE6 knockout and overexpression (wild-type and T1025N) experiments and measure production.
evolution_trajectory.observation: STE6 knockout and wild-type overexpression had no effect; STE6 T1025N overexpression increased production fourfold to 13.8 mg/g DCW, indicating gain-of-function.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Test specificity of STE6 T1025N for β-caryophyllene vs other sesquiterpenes.

evidence.quote_or_span: The STE6 knockout strain exhibited no change in production compared with the reference.
Production was also not impacted when the wild type STE6 was overexpressed.

However, when we overexpressed the mutated STE6 in the reference strain, $\beta$ -caryophyllene production increased fourfold to 13.8 mg/g DCW, suggesting the STE6 T1025N mutation is a gain-of-function mutation.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: STE6 T1025N is a gain-of-function mutation for β-caryophyllene; specificity unknown.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Does STE6 T1025N also enhance production of other sesquiterpenes like α-humulene?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: STE6 T1025N specifically affects β-caryophyllene transport and will not increase α-humulene production.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Overexpress STE6 T1025N in an α-humulene producer and measure production.
evolution_trajectory.observation: No increase in α-humulene production, suggesting specificity for β-caryophyllene.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Discuss limitations and mechanisms.

evidence.quote_or_span: No increase in production was observed in the strain that overexpresses STE6 T1025N compared to the reference

No increase in production was observed in the strain that overexpresses STE6 T1025N compared to the reference (Additional file 1: Figure S6), suggesting that the mutation specifically affects the transport of $\beta$ -caryophyllene and does not significantly influence the efflux of sesquiterpenes in general.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Beneficial mutations identified and partially characterized; mechanisms not fully understood.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Exact mechanisms of STE6 T1025N and MST27/tR(UCU)G1 intergenic mutations remain unclear; roles of other mutations not fully understood.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: STE6 T1025N may alter substrate recognition; MST27/tR(UCU)G1 intergenic mutation may affect gene regulation.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Discuss potential mechanisms based on mutation locations and literature.
evolution_trajectory.observation: STE6 T1025N is a missense mutation between transmembrane and ATP binding domains, potentially impacting substrate recognition. MST27/tR(UCU)G1 intergenic mutation is at 3' end of MST27, making impact difficult to ascertain. Both mutations found at very low frequencies in population.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Conclude study and note limitations.

evidence.quote_or_span: The STE6 T1025N mutation is a missense mutation, resulting in a change in amino acid from threonine to asparagine.

Since the mutation is located between the transmembrane and ATP binding domains on the predicted cytoplasmic portion of the protein, it potentially impacted substrate recognition, allowing increased export of β-caryophyllene.
However, as this mutation is in the intergenic region and at the 3' end of MST27, it is difficult to ascertain the exact impact of the mutation on the cell.
Interestingly, both the STE6 T1025N and MST27/tR(UCU)G1 intergenic mutations were found in very low frequencies in the population (below detection limit).

evolution_trajectory.state_before: 13 candidate mutations reconstructed; their effects on growth unknown.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Do the reconstructed mutations affect growth kinetics under normal conditions?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Some mutations may improve growth rate or reduce lag phase, contributing to fitness.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Measure growth kinetics of reconstructed mutants under normal culture conditions.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Five mutations (CDC39, tK(CUU)C/MAK32 int, RIF1 D709N, STE6 T1025N, YAL064W-B/TDA8 int) did not impact growth; FLO9 T288T, RNR3/FIS1 int, SNO2/SNZ2 int increased growth rate; DAN4 T200T, MDS3, MTL1 S257S, MST27/tR(UCU)G1 int, FLO9 T288T reduced lag phase.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Proceed to characterize beneficial mutations further.

evidence.quote_or_span: five (CDC39, tK(CUU)C/MAK32 int, RIF1 D709N, STE6 T1025N and YAL064W B/TDA8 int) out of 13 mutations did not impact growth under normal culture conditions
The FLO9 T288T, RNR3/FIS1 int, and SNO2/SNZ2 int mutations exhibited increased growth rates.
The DAN4 T200T, MDS3, MTL1 S257S, MST27/tR(UCU)G1 int, and FLO9 T288T mutations showed significant reduction in lag phase.

validation_methods: Reconstruction of individual mutations in parental strain
success_verification.final_conclusion: ALE with oxidative stress enriched for hyperproducers; STE6 T1025N and MST27/tR(UCU)G1 intergenic mutations identified as beneficial; STE6 T1025N is a gain-of-function mutation specific for β-caryophyllene; combining mutations did not further increase production.
limitations: Exact mechanism of STE6 T1025N and MST27/tR(UCU)G1 intergenic mutations remains unclear
Other mutations' roles not fully understood
Beneficial mutations found at very low frequencies in population

source.title: Identification of a novel metabolic engineering target for carotenoid production in Saccharomyces cerevisiae via ethanol-induced adaptive laboratory evolution

initial_request.research_problem: Difficulty in discovering new metabolic engineering targets for carotenoid accumulation in S. cerevisiae.
initial_request.known_context: Carotenoids are high-value compounds; existing engineering efforts have limited target discovery.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Difficulty in discovering new targets relevant to carotenoid accumulation in engineered S. cerevisiae.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Unknown metabolic targets that could enhance carotenoid accumulation.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Ethanol stress could be used as a driving force for ALE to increase carotenoid accumulation, based on the link between carotenoid yield, membrane engineering, and ethanol tolerance.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Use ethanol-induced ALE to evolve a carotenoid-producing strain.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Hypothesis formulated that ethanol stress might drive adaptation for increased carotenoid accumulation.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Design ALE experiment with ethanol stress.

evidence.quote_or_span: it was difficult to discover new targets that were relevant to the accumulation of carotenoids
we hypothesized that ethanol stress might be used as a driving force for the adaptation of yeast for increased carotenoids accumulation

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Hypothesis that ethanol stress can drive ALE for carotenoid accumulation.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to design an ALE protocol with appropriate ethanol concentrations and selection criteria.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Gradually increasing ethanol concentrations will select for mutants with enhanced carotenoid production.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Design ALE with sequential batch cultures in YPD with ethanol concentrations from 2% to 12%, screening for darkened colonies.
evolution_trajectory.observation: ALE protocol designed with gradually increasing ethanol and screening for darkened colonies.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Execute ALE to isolate evolved strains.

evidence.quote_or_span: Sequential passages and batch cultures were performed using the parental strain (BL03-D-4) and YPD media with different concentrations of ethanol
Gradually increasing (from 2 to 12% or from 4 to 12%) and specific concentrations (2%, 4%, 8%, and 10%) of ethanol were selected as the driving forces for the ALE

evolution_trajectory.state_before: ALE design ready for execution.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Whether ethanol stress will yield mutants with improved carotenoid production.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Ethanol stress will select for mutants with enhanced carotenoid accumulation.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform ALE and isolate evolved strain M3 after three subcultures at 10% ethanol based on darkened colony color.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Evolved strain M3 isolated with darkened colony color.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Characterize carotenoid yield of M3.

evidence.quote_or_span: Sequential passages and batch cultures were performed using the parental strain (BL03-D-4) and YPD media with different concentrations of ethanol
Gradually increasing (from 2 to 12% or from 4 to 12%) and specific concentrations (2%, 4%, 8%, and 10%) of ethanol were selected as the driving forces for the ALE

evolution_trajectory.state_before: M3 isolated but not yet characterized for carotenoid yield.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Quantify carotenoid yield improvement in M3.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: M3 will show increased carotenoid yield compared to parental strain.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform shake-flask fermentation in YPD and YPM media.
evolution_trajectory.observation: M3 showed 5.1-fold increase in carotenoid yield in YPD and 2.4-fold in YPM; yield reached 42.4 mg/g CDW in YPM.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Investigate genetic basis of improved yield.

evidence.quote_or_span: the carotenoid yield of M3 increased about 5.1- and 2.4-fold compared with BL03-D-4 in YPD and YPM media, respectively
After 96 h of incubation, the carotenoid yield of M3 reached 42.4 mg/g CDW in the YPM medium

evolution_trajectory.state_before: M3 shows improved carotenoid yield.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Whether further evolution can yield an even better strain.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A second round of evolution on M3 might produce a strain with higher carotenoid yield.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Subject M3 to another round of ALE using the same ethanol stress strategy.
evolution_trajectory.observation: No better strain was obtained from the second round of evolution.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Proceed to analyze growth and ethanol effects on M3.

evidence.quote_or_span: M3 was also turned into another round of evolution. However, no better strain was obtained using the above ALE strategy

evolution_trajectory.state_before: M3 shows improved carotenoid yield, but second round of evolution failed.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Determine if yield increase is due to growth or metabolic changes.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Yield increase is not due to growth enhancement.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Analyze growth and effect of ethanol supplementation.

tool_or_method.name: growth analysis and ethanol supplementation assay

evolution_trajectory.observation: Ethanol supplementation repressed carotenoid yield in M3; no significant difference in cell growth between BL03-D-4 and M3 without ethanol.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Proceed to whole-genome resequencing to find causal mutations.

evidence.quote_or_span: the additional supplement of ethanol had a remarkably repressive effect on the carotenoid yield of M3 especially under a high concentration of ethanol
there was no significant difference in cell growth between BL03-D-4 and M3 during the cultivation without ethanol

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Phenotypic characterization suggests genetic changes.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Identify mutations responsible for improved carotenoid yield.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: M3 harbors mutations that enhance carotenoid accumulation.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform whole-genome resequencing of BL03-D-4 and M3.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 14 SNPs and 100 INDELs detected in M3, including a loss-of-function mutation in PFK1.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Hypothesize that PFK1 mutation is causal.

evidence.quote_or_span: whole-genome resequencing of strain BL03-D-4 and M3 was performed using the Illumina HiSeq platform through paired-end sequencing
The average sequencing depths of the samples were 70 to 90
Overall, 14 single nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) and 100 insertion and deletions (INDEL) were detected in M3

evolution_trajectory.state_before: PFK1 loss-of-function mutation identified.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Whether PFK1 mutation is responsible for increased carotenoid yield.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: The loss-of-function mutation of PFK1 is related to the promoted carotenoid yield.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Test by reverse engineering PFK1 deletion.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Speculated that PFK1 mutation is causal.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate by constructing PFK1 deletion strain.

evidence.quote_or_span: we speculated that the loss-of-function mutation of PFK1 in M3 was related to the promoted carotenoid yield

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Hypothesis that PFK1 mutation improves carotenoid yield.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to confirm causality.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Deleting PFK1 in parental strain will recapitulate increased carotenoid yield.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Delete PFK1 in BL03-D-4 to create strain BE1 and measure carotenoid yield.
evolution_trajectory.observation: BE1 showed remarkably increased carotenoid yield relative to BL03-D-4, not significantly different from M3, fully recovering the evolved phenotype.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Investigate mechanism via transcriptomics.

evidence.quote_or_span: The reverse-engineered strain BE1 showed remarkably increased carotenoid yield relative to BL03-D-4, which was not significantly different compared to M3
fully recovering the evolved carotenoids phenotype

evolution_trajectory.state_before: PFK1 deletion confirmed as causal.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Understand transcriptomic changes underlying phenotype.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: PFK1 mutation alters expression of genes in glycolysis, PPP, and cell wall pathways.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform transcriptome analysis of BL03-D-4 and M3.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 37 up-regulated and 162 down-regulated genes; GO enrichment showed down-regulation of cell wall-related genes.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Analyze pathway-level changes.

evidence.quote_or_span: we performed a transcriptome analysis of BL03-D-4 and M3
down-regulated genes in M3 compared with BL03-D-4 through differential expression analysis (Fig. 4a, full lists are available in Additional file 3).
the GO enrichment analysis demonstrated that the differentially expressed genes were mainly involved in cell wall organization, and almost all genes were down-regulated in this study (Fig. 4b).
We defined the gene sets that showed greater than twofold differences in expression levels between BL03-D-4 and M3.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Transcriptome data obtained.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Unexpected down-regulation of oxidative PPP genes contradicts expected NADPH demand for carotenoid biosynthesis.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: PFK1 mutation leads to a new mechanism not reliant on increased NADPH via oxidative PPP.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Note limitation and propose alternative hypothesis.
evolution_trajectory.observation: First and second steps of oxidative PPP significantly down-regulated in M3, contradicting expectation.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Formulate new hypothesis about cell wall remodeling and carotenoid storage.

evidence.quote_or_span: the first and second steps of the oxidative PPP were significantly down-regulated in M3, which presented a remarkable improvement of carotenoid yield in our study (Fig. 4d).

Surprisingly, and in contrast, the first and second steps of the oxidative PPP were significantly down-regulated in M3, which presented a remarkable improvement of carotenoid yield in our study (Fig. 4d).

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Unexpected down-regulation of oxidative PPP.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Mechanism by which PFK1 mutation improves carotenoid yield.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: PFK1 mutation leads to cell wall remodeling, affecting membrane properties and carotenoid storage capacity.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Propose new mechanism for further investigation.

source.title: Efficient production of d-lactate from methane in a lactate-tolerant strain of Methylomonas sp. DH-1 generated by adaptive laboratory evolution

initial_request.research_problem: Lactate toxicity limits high-level lactate production in methanotrophs; prior titers from methane were <1 g/L.
initial_request.known_context: Methylomonas sp. DH-1 is a methanotroph lacking native LDH, suitable for optically pure lactate production. Lactate toxicity is a known bottleneck.
constraints: Lactate toxicity
Low prior titers (<1 g/L)
quantifiable_goals: Increase lactate tolerance
Achieve higher D-lactate titer from methane

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Lactate toxicity limits high-level production; prior titers <1 g/L.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Unknown lactate tolerance of Methylomonas sp. DH-1.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Wild-type Methylomonas sp. DH-1 has low lactate tolerance.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Test growth inhibition by lactate.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Cell growth severely inhibited; tolerance only up to 0.5 g/L lactate.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Low tolerance motivates adaptive laboratory evolution to improve tolerance.

evidence.quote_or_span: LA toxicity is one of the limiting factors for high-level production of LA.
The cell growth was severely inhibited by addition of LA, exhibiting low tolerance up to 0.5 g/L LA in the medium neutralized to pH 6.8 (Fig. 1b).
we performed adaptive laboratory evolution of Methylomonas sp. DH-1 by serially transferring cells to the medium with increasing concentrations of LA from 0.5 g/L to 8.0 g/L.
DH-1, generated by adaptive laboratory evolution was effective in LA production from methane.
DH-1 generated by adaptive laboratory evolution
DH-1 by adaptive laboratory evolution and generated an efficient D-LA-producing strain by introducing D-specific LDH gene into the evolved strain.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Wild-type tolerance only 0.5 g/L lactate.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need strains with higher lactate tolerance.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Serial transfer with increasing lactate concentrations will evolve tolerant strains.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform adaptive laboratory evolution (ALE).
evolution_trajectory.observation: Evolved strains JHM30 (tolerant to 3.0 g/L) and JHM80 (tolerant to 8.0 g/L) isolated.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Evolved strains obtained; need to identify genetic basis of tolerance.

evidence.quote_or_span: we performed adaptive laboratory evolution of Methylomonas sp. DH-1 by serially transferring cells to the medium with increasing concentrations of LA from 0.5 g/L to 8.0 g/L.
evolved strains JHM30 and JHM80 were selected, which could survive at 3.0 g/L and 8.0 g/L LA, respectively, during the evolution process.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Evolved strains JHM30 and JHM80 isolated.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Genetic mutations responsible for tolerance unknown.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Whole genome sequencing will reveal mutations associated with tolerance.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Sequence genomes of evolved strains.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 2-bp (TT) deletion in intergenic region upstream of watR operon in both strains; JHM80 also has nonsense mutation in fliE.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Candidate mutations identified; need to validate role of watR.

evidence.quote_or_span: DH-1 and identified that up-regulation of the watR gene due to the promoter mutation is partly responsible for the LA tolerance of JHM80.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: TT deletion in watR promoter identified.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Whether watR upregulation is responsible for tolerance.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Deletion of watR operon will abolish tolerance.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Delete watR operon genes in JHM80.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Deletion abolished lactate tolerance of JHM80.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Confirms watR role; test if overexpression alone is sufficient.

evidence.quote_or_span: deletion of all three genes abolished the LA tolerance of JHM80, suggesting that the elevated expression of watR is mainly responsible for the LA tolerance of JHM80

evolution_trajectory.state_before: watR deletion abolishes tolerance.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Whether watR overexpression alone increases tolerance in wild-type.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Overexpression of watR in wild-type will increase lactate tolerance.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Overexpress watR in wild-type strain.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Overexpression increased lactate tolerance; OE2 construct failed to recover sensitivity.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: watR validated as key tolerance factor; also test fliE mutation.

evidence.quote_or_span: Overexpression of the watR gene in wild-type strain also led to an increase in LA tolerance.
cells integrated with the OE1 and OE3 expression cassettes showed higher LA tolerance
overexpression of OE2 failed to recover the LA sensitivity of the wild-type strain

evolution_trajectory.state_before: fliE nonsense mutation also found in JHM80.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Whether fliE mutation contributes to tolerance.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: fliE deletion may improve tolerance.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Delete fliE in JHM30.
evolution_trajectory.observation: fliE deletion did not improve lactate tolerance of JHM30.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: fliE not responsible; focus on watR for engineering.

evidence.quote_or_span: fliE deletion did not improve the LA tolerance of JHM30

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Tolerant strain JHM80 available; no native LDH.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to introduce D-lactate production pathway.
