evolution_trajectory.observation: Strains constructed.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Compare D-lactate production of different LDH variants.

evidence.quote_or_span: 4 heterologous D form-specific LDH genes from L. jensenii (Lj1. LDH and Lj3.LDH), L. delbrueckii subsp. bulgaricus ATCC 11842 (Ld.LDH), L. mesenteroides subsp. mesenteroides ATCC 8293 (Lm.LDH) were introduced into the chromosome of JHM80 while deleting the glgA gene

evolution_trajectory.state_before: LDH-expressing JHM80 strains constructed.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which LDH variant yields highest D-lactate.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Lm.LDH and Ld.LDH will be most effective.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Measure D-lactate production in shake flasks.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Lm.LDH and Ld.LDH produced 192 and 187 mg/L D-lactate, respectively.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Lm.LDH selected for further engineering.

evidence.quote_or_span: JHM80 strains expressing Lm.LDH and Ld.LDH were more effective in LA production than the strains expressing the other LDH genes, producing 192 mg/L and 187 mg/L LA, respectively

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Lm.LDH identified as best variant.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Production level still low; need to combine tolerance and LDH.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: JHM80 with Lm.LDH and ΔglgA (JHM86) will produce higher titer than wild-type equivalent.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Construct JHM86 and compare with JHM14 (wild-type + Lm.LDH).

tool_or_method.name: Fed-batch fermentation with periodic methane feeding

suggesting that intracellular production of LA is still toxic in JHM80 strain.
further elucidation of LA tolerance mechanisms and metabolic pathways might be necessary to improve LA production.

source.title: A seamless and iterative DNA assembly method named PS-Brick and its assisted metabolic engineering for threonine and 1-propanol production

initial_request.research_problem: Lack of a simple DNA assembly method that is simultaneously iterative, seamless, and capable of handling repetitive sequences for efficient DBTL cycles in metabolic engineering.
initial_request.known_context: Current methods (BioBrick, Golden Gate, MoClo/Golden Braid) each lack one or more of the desired properties: iterative, seamless, repetitive sequence assembly.
constraints: Must be simple and convenient
Must handle repetitive sequences
Must be iterative
Must be seamless
quantifiable_goals: Achieve high assembly efficiency (10^4-10^5 CFU/μg DNA)
Achieve high accuracy (~90%)
Demonstrate iterative DBTL cycles for threonine overproduction
Achieve high threonine titer (target >40 g/L)

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Existing DNA assembly methods (BioBrick, Golden Gate, MoClo/Golden Braid) lack a simple technique that is simultaneously iterative, seamless, and capable of handling repetitive sequences.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: No single method combines iterative, seamless, and repetitive sequence assembly in a simple format.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Combining Type IIP and IIS restriction endonucleases in a single assembly method (PS-Brick) can achieve iterative, seamless, and repetitive sequence assembly.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Develop PS-Brick method using Type IIP and IIS REs.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Hypothesis formulated based on limitations of existing methods.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Design PS-Brick vectors and test the concept.

evidence.quote_or_span: DNA assembly scenarios encountered in metabolic engineering for the construction of amino acid and related compound producers, such as design-build-test-learn cycles, construction of precise genetic circuits and repetitive DNA molecules, usually require for iterative, scarless and repetitive sequence assembly methods, respectively.

Here, we developed a Type IIP and IIS RE-assisted method named PS-Brick that comprehensively takes advantage of the properties of PCR fragments and REs for iterative, seamless and repetitive sequence assembly.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Hypothesis formulated; no PS-Brick vectors exist.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to design vectors with appropriate restriction sites for iterative assembly.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Vectors pOB and pOM with entrance sites SphI/MlyI and SphI/BmrI can enable iterative assembly.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Construct pOB and pOM from pUC19 by removing internal BmrI and MlyI sites and inserting entrance sites.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Vectors pOB and pOM constructed successfully.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Optimize digestion conditions for PS-Brick assembly.

evidence.quote_or_span: One BmrI site and three MlyI sites located in the pUC19 vector backbones are removed through overlap extension PCR
the newly obtained vectors pOB and pOM from pUC19, containing the entrance sites SphI/MlyI and SphI/BmrI, respectively, were taken as the original PS-Brick vectors
BmrI (generating 1 nt cohesive end) and MlyI (generating blunt end) are used as Type IIS REs for the proof-of-concept of the PS-Brick assembly strategy (Fig. 1bc).

evolution_trajectory.state_before: PS-Brick vectors designed but digestion conditions not optimized.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Optimal digestion time for Type IIS REs and effect of SphI digestion time on assembly efficiency and accuracy unknown.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: 30 min digestion for BmrI and MlyI is sufficient; SphI digestion time does not significantly affect assembly.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Test cutting efficiency of BmrI and MlyI over 15-180 min; test SphI digestion time effect on assembly efficiency and accuracy.

tool_or_method.name: gel electrophoresis, colony counting, DNA sequencing

evolution_trajectory.observation: 30 min digestion chosen; SphI digestion time (15-180 min) did not affect assembly efficiency or accuracy (88.2-95.1%).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Identify cause of incorrect clones and compare with traditional cloning.

evidence.quote_or_span: The transform efficiency of Bmrl digested pOB vector and Mlyl digested pOM vector
The second cutting efficiency of Sphl was investigated through the assembly efficiency and correct ligation rate
The cutting efficiencies of the Type IIS REs BmrI and MlyI presented in vectors pOB and pOM were tested through gel electrophoresis with a cutting time ranging from 15 to 180 min.
The accuracy of PS-Brick assembly was measured by DNA sequencing of 20 clones and ranged from 88.2 to 95.1%
The original PS-Brick vector pOM containing the SphI/MlyI entrance site was cleaved by SphI and MlyI for 30 min in the same buffer.
The purified PCR products were digested with SphI only for 30 min, heat-inactivated at 60 °C for 20 min and then column-purified for ligation.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Optimization revealed some incorrect clones.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Cause of incorrect clones unknown.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Self-ligation of vector due to incomplete SphI digestion causes incorrect clones.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Investigate effect of 5'-phosphorylation of non-cut PCR end; suggest dephosphorylation to prevent self-ligation.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Self-ligation identified as cause; dephosphorylation suggested.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Compare PS-Brick performance with traditional cloning.

evidence.quote_or_span: The effect 5'-phosphorylation of the non-cut PCR end on the efficiency and accuracy of PS-Brick

evolution_trajectory.state_before: PS-Brick optimized; traditional cloning performance known.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How does PS-Brick compare to traditional Type IIP RE-based cloning?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: PS-Brick has similar efficiency and accuracy to traditional cloning but offers additional benefits.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Compare efficiency and accuracy of PS-Brick (BmrI and MlyI) with XbaI/SphI cloning.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Similar efficiency and accuracy observed. Blunt end/MlyI-based PS-Brick is completely seamless and sequence-independent; TA clone/BmrI-based PS-Brick offers more RE options.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Apply PS-Brick to first DBTL cycle for threonine production.

evidence.quote_or_span: Comparison of the efficiency and accuracy between PS-Brick and the traditional Type IIP RE-based clone method with XbaI and Sphl

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Bottleneck (asd) identified; threonine export may be limiting.

evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Overexpression of exporters (rhtA, rhtB, rhtC, yeaS) with standardized expression elements will increase threonine titer.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Use bicistronic design (BCD1) and mid-strength promoter to overexpress four exporters; test threonine production.

Second, P $_{T}$ and BCD1 [65, 66] were amplified as donor PCR fragments, and inserted in front of the ATG start codon of the four exporter genes in the last round assembled vectors, generating the corresponding vectors pACYC184-thrA $^{433}$ BC-asd-P $_{T}$ BCD1-rhtA/rhtB/rhtC/yeaS (Fig.

Four threonine exporters under the same standard initiation element (a mid-strength promoter (P $_{T}$ ) from a constitutive bacteriophage P $_{L}$ promoter library [65] along with a mid-strength bicistronic design (BCD1) RBS [66]) were assembled into the last round PS-Brick plasmids pACYC184-thrA $^{433}$ BC-asd through two cycles of PS-Brick reaction, respectively.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Threonine production improved but catabolism may still reduce yield.

evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Repetitive sgRNA arrays for tdh and ilvA deletion can be assembled via TA clone/BciVI PS-Brick, and double mutant will increase threonine.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Assemble repetitive sgRNA arrays using PS-Brick; perform CRISPR-Cas9 deletion of tdh and ilvA; test fed-batch production.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Double mutant strain obtained; fed-batch produced 45.71 g/L threonine, 28.7% higher than control (35.52 g/L).

Batch culture of this engineered strain in a 7.5-L fermenter allowed production of 45.71 g/L threonine, 28.7% higher than the production of control strains retaining the tdh and ilvA genes (35.52 g/L)
Following the genome editing protocol [70], we transformed ptargetET-tdh-ilvA into a strain expressing Cas9, and the double mutation strain E.

evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Assembling kivD and ADH2 in one PS-Brick cycle will enable 1-propanol production from threonine.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Conclude that future work should focus on obtaining more efficient enzymes.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: PS-Brick applied to multiple DBTL cycles and 1-propanol pathway.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Does PS-Brick successfully enable iterative, seamless, and repetitive sequence assembly for metabolic engineering?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: PS-Brick is the first restriction-ligation assembly method using both Type IIP and IIS REs, combining iterative and seamless strengths.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Summarize achievements: 45.71 g/L threonine via DBTL cycles; repetitive sgRNA assembly; 1-propanol pathway assembly.
evolution_trajectory.observation: PS-Brick successfully demonstrated for iterative DBTL cycles; first method combining both RE types for iterative seamless assembly.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Conclude study.

evidence.quote_or_span: An application of metabolic engineering to threonine production, including the release of feedback regulation, elimination of metabolic bottlenecks, intensification of threonine export and inactivation of threonine catabolism, was stepwise resolved in E. coli by rounds of “design-build-test-learn” cycles through the iterative PS-Brick paradigm, and 45.71 g/L threonine was obtained through fed-batch fermentation.

seamless cloning enabled precise in-frame fusion for codon saturation mutagenesis and bicistronic design, and the repetitive sequence cloning ability of PS-Brick enabled construction of tandem CRISPR sgRNA arrays for genome editing.
the PS-Brick framework is the first restriction-ligation assembly method using both Type IIP and IIS REs
we have developed a novel PS-Brick framework that enables iterative, seamless and sequence-repeat DNA assembly.

success_verification.final_conclusion: PS-Brick is the first restriction-ligation assembly method using both Type IIP and IIS REs, enabling iterative, seamless, and repetitive sequence assembly. It was successfully applied to threonine overproduction (45.71 g/L) through four DBTL cycles and to 1-propanol pathway assembly (1.35 g/L), though low enzyme activity limited 1-propanol yield.
limitations: Low 1-propanol titer due to insufficient KivD and ADH2 activity
PS-Brick unfit for combinatorial pathway engineering due to limitations in multi-part assembly
Need to eliminate internal restriction sites remains a general limitation for RE-based strategies

source.title: Dissecting the evolvability landscape of the CalB active site toward aromatic substrates

initial_request.research_problem: Engineering CalB lipase to improve activity toward bulky aromatic substrates (methyl cinnamate and methyl salicylate) due to poor native recognition, while overcoming limitations of high-throughput screening for transesterification.
initial_request.known_context: CalB has poor activity on bulky aromatic substrates; high-throughput screening for transesterification is difficult.
constraints: High-throughput screening for transesterification is challenging
Limited screening capacity
quantifiable_goals: Increase transesterification activity toward methyl cinnamate and methyl salicylate
Design smaller, smarter mutant libraries

evolution_trajectory.state_before: CalB has poor activity on bulky aromatic substrates and high-throughput screening for transesterification is difficult.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to efficiently engineer CalB for improved activity on bulky aromatics given screening limitations.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A combinatorial semi-rational approach (ISRD) combining virtual docking, iterative saturation mutagenesis, and RIN analysis can design smaller, smarter mutant libraries.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Adopt ISRD approach.
evolution_trajectory.observation: ISRD approach proposed to partly overcome screening challenge.

source.title: Enhancement of the catalytic activity of Isopentenyl diphosphate isomerase (IDI) from Saccharomyces cerevisiae through random and site-directed mutagenesis

initial_request.research_problem: IDI is a rate-limiting enzyme in the lycopene biosynthetic pathway with low activity, short half-life, and weak substrate affinity. The goal is to enhance IDI catalytic activity through protein engineering to improve lycopene production in E. coli.
initial_request.target_object: Isopentenyl diphosphate isomerase (IDI) from Saccharomyces cerevisiae
initial_request.known_context: IDIs are limited by low enzyme activity, short half-life and weak substrate affinity

evolution_trajectory.decision: Design a directed evolution strategy using error-prone PCR and a lycopene-dependent color screening assay.
evolution_trajectory.observation: A strategy was designed to optimize IDI activity through error-prone PCR and color screening.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Implement the designed strategy by performing error-prone PCR.

evolution_trajectory.observation: Three cycles of error-prone PCR were performed with library sizes of 10,000–15,000 strains.

evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Perform structural modeling to explain mechanistic basis of improvements.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Experimental improvements observed but structural rationale missing.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: What are the molecular mechanisms by which L141H, Y195F, and W256C enhance activity and stability?

evolution_trajectory.decision: Build homology model of IDI using E. coli IDI (2ICJ) as template and perform molecular docking with IPP.

source.title: A graph-based approach to construct target-focused libraries for virtual screening

initial_request.research_problem: Generic virtual screening libraries have low hit rates; target-focused libraries improve efficiency but often rely on known scaffolds, limiting novelty. The challenge is to generate novel, drug-like, synthetically accessible compounds tailored to a target using fragments from known actives while managing enormous chemical space.
initial_request.known_context: Widely used screening libraries like ZINC have very low probability of desired bioactivity; e.g., <4% of Abbott collection are potential kinase hinge-binders.
constraints: Must generate novel compounds without scaffold bias
Must be drug-like and synthetically accessible
Must handle enormous chemical space
quantifiable_goals: High reconstruction rate of known actives
High fraction of novel bioactive-like molecules
Reasonable synthetic accessibility scores

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Generic screening libraries have low hit rates (e.g., <4% for kinase hinge-binders), motivating target-focused libraries.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to generate novel, drug-like, synthetically accessible compounds tailored to a target without relying on known scaffolds.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Fragment-based synthesis from known actives can yield target-focused libraries with novel compounds by decomposing molecules into rigid fragments and linkers, then reconnecting them following connectivity patterns.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Develop eSynth, an exhaustive graph-based search algorithm to computationally synthesize new compounds by reconnecting fragments.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Organic compounds can be decomposed into rigid fragments connected by flexible linkers; tracking atomic connectivity allows reconstruction.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need to design fragment extraction and connectivity tracking.

evidence.quote_or_span: the vast majority of compounds in widely used screening libraries, such as the ZINC database, will have a very low probability to exhibit the desired bioactivity for a given protein
For instance, an internal analysis of the Abbott compound collection suggested that $<4\%$ of the compounds in their library have the potential to yield novel kinase hinge-binders $[9]$ .
Assuming that organic compounds are composed of sets of rigid fragments connected by flexible linkers, a molecule can be decomposed into its building blocks tracking their atomic connectivity.
we developed eSynth, an exhaustive graph-based search algorithm to computationally synthesize new compounds by reconnecting these building blocks following their connectivity patterns.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Hypothesis of fragment-based synthesis established.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to define fragment extraction rules and connectivity tracking.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Rigid fragments defined as ≥4 non-hydrogen atoms connected by non-rotatable bonds; linkers merged; connectivity tracked via atom types.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Implement fragment extraction: rigid fragments carry connectivity info (atom types), linkers carry only number of allowed contacts.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Fragments extracted with connectivity information; linkers merged when attached.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need to control search space via linker saturation and fragment consolidation.

evidence.quote_or_span: a rigid fragment is defined as a set of at least four non-hydrogen atoms connected by non-rotatable bonds
If two linker fragments are attached to each other, these will be connected to form a longer linker
A rigid fragment carries connectivity information indicating those atoms from which a rigid fragment was originally branched and the corresponding atom types it was connected to
linkers contain information only on the number of allowed contacts at every atom

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Fragment extraction defined.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Long linkers with extensive connectivity risk unmanageable search space; redundancy in fragments from multiple compounds.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Using unsaturated linkers (storing only original connections) and consolidating fragments will control search space.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Implement unsaturated linkers and fragment consolidation (merge identical fragments, retain all connectivity possibilities).

parameters.consolidation: merge identical fragments, retain all connectivity

evolution_trajectory.observation: Redundancy removed; search space narrowed.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Design exhaustive synthesis algorithms.

evidence.quote_or_span: long linkers with the extensive connectivity pose a risk of expanding the molecular search space to an unmanageable size
unsaturated linkers can also be built to store only the number of original connections, regardless of the maximum capacity of their atoms to create covalent bonds
Redundancy is removed from molecular fragments extracted from multiple compounds by consolidating the connectivity information and deleting identical moieties
if this fragment is connected to different atom types in distinct parent molecules, all possibilities should be retained

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Synthesis algorithms generate significant redundancy.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Memory constraints require memoryless deduplication.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Bloom filters with SMILES representation can efficiently deduplicate molecules.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Implement Bloom filters for deduplication; represent molecules as SMILES.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Bloom filters enable memoryless deduplication.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Test initial implementation.

evidence.quote_or_span: Using either Algorithm 1 or Algorithm 2 results in a significant redundancy present in synthesized molecules
eliminating molecular redundancy requires a memoryless technique; the synthesis requires a series of Bloom filters
a molecule M is represented using the Simplified Molecular-Input Line-Entry System (SMILES) specification

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Initial implementation of Algorithm 1.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Algorithm 1 may have memory/time limitations.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Algorithm 1 will be non-optimal due to exponential growth.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Run initial implementation and observe performance.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Sharp increase in generated molecules around levels 9-11; considerable wall time and memory limitations.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need to optimize synthesis.

evidence.quote_or_span: Our initial implementation of the molecular synthesis described in Algorithm 1 is non-optimal due to considerable limitations on the wall time and memory space.
we encounter this sharp increase of the number of generated molecules around levels 9 through 11

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Algorithm 1 failed due to exponential growth.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to reduce search space and improve efficiency.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Bounded algorithm (Algorithm 2), Bloom filters, unsaturated linkers, and Rule-of-Five filtering will optimize synthesis.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Implement Algorithm 2, Bloom filters, unsaturated linkers, and Rule-of-Five filtering.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Synthesis optimized.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need to analyze Rule-of-Five thresholds and descriptor estimation.

evidence.quote_or_span: we optimized the molecular synthesis by (1) implementing the bounded, level-based algorithm described in Algorithm 2, (2) reducing redundancy with Bloom filters, (3) restricting the connectivity using unsaturated linkers to narrow the width of the search space in order to gain depth, and (4) eliminating compounds violating the Rule-of-Five

source.title: Hydrogen overproducing nitrogenases obtained by random mutagenesis and high-throughput screening

initial_request.research_problem: Engineering nitrogenase for enhanced H2 production is limited by the lack of high-throughput screening methods.
initial_request.known_context: Nitrogenase produces H2 as a byproduct; previous genetic approaches have targeted hydrogenase and nitrogenase genes to improve H2 yield.
constraints: High-throughput screening method needed
Must work in Rhodobacter capsulatus
quantifiable_goals: Increase H2 production
Retain N2 fixation activity in some variants

evolution_trajectory.state_before: No high-throughput screening method for nitrogenase H2 production exists.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need a sensor strain that can report H2 production via fluorescence.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Deleting hupAB (uptake hydrogenase) and nifH will reduce background H2 consumption and nitrogenase activity, allowing a fluorescent reporter to detect H2 produced by variant nitrogenases.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Construct sensor strain S3 by deleting hupAB and nifH in R. capsulatus.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Deletion of hupAB eliminated uptake hydrogenase activity; S2 strain showed 15-fold higher β-galactosidase activity under diazotrophic conditions.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Optimized sensor strain enables screening of nifH variants.

evidence.quote_or_span: The deletion of hupAB genes in the RC25 strain completely eliminated its in vivo uptake hydrogenase activity (nil compared to $2504 \pm 450$ nmol $\mathrm{H}_2\mathrm{h}^{-1}\mathrm{OD}_{600}^{-1}$ in the wild-type strain).

First, the response to 10% exogenous $H_{2}$ improved 5-fold in the $\Delta$ hupAB strain S2 compared to in S1; second, the constitutive activation of PhupA was observed in the $\Delta$ hupT strain RC24-S1; and third, no response to $H_{2}$ was observed in the $\Delta$ hupR strain RC54-S1.
Figure 1C shows that the $\beta$ -galactosidase activity in S2 was 15-fold higher under diazotrophic conditions compared to non-diazotrophic conditions, validating its use as a biosensor.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Sensor strain S3 is available but no variant library exists.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need a diverse library of nifH variants to screen for enhanced H2 production.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Error-prone PCR can generate random nifH variants with an average of 5 amino acid changes.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Generate random nifH variant library by error-prone PCR.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Library of ~4×10^6 clones generated with no obvious mutational hotspots.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Library enables high-throughput screening.

evidence.quote_or_span: Random nifH variants (nifHv) having an average of 5 amino acid changes (Table S1) were generated by error-prone PCR, ligated into pRHB602, and introduced into E.
No obvious mutational hotspots were observed after sequencing 20 nifH variants per library.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: nifH variant library is ready for screening.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to identify variants with high fluorescence indicating high H2 production.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: FACS can sort cells with high fluorescence from the sensor strain expressing nifH variants.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Screen ~10^5 variants by FACS and sort high-fluorescence cells into 96-well plates.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Five H2-overproducing clones (V1-V5) identified.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Hits need secondary confirmation.

evidence.quote_or_span: Cells exhibiting high fluorescence levels were sorted and recovered in 96-well microplates containing YPS medium supplemented with Tc.
2A) were sorted by the cytometer into separate wells of 96-well microplates containing growth medium.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: FACS hits need validation.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: FACS fluorescence may not accurately reflect β-galactosidase activity.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: MUG-based fluorescence assay will confirm higher β-galactosidase activity in sorted clones.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform secondary MUG screen on sorted clones.
evolution_trajectory.observation: β-galactosidase activity was 2 to 12-fold higher in nifHv-S3 cultures; 85% of clones harbored nifHv gene.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Correlation validates screening method.

evidence.quote_or_span: 85% of the nifHv-S3 clones harbored an expression vector with a nifHv gene, as determined by PCR analysis.
The $\beta$ -galactosidase activity was 2 to 12-fold higher in the nifHv-S3 cultures than in the pRHB576-S3 strain (carrying wild-type nifH).

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Secondary screen results available.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to confirm that FACS primary screening correlates with secondary MUG screening.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Strong correlation between FACS and MUG results validates the high-throughput method.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Compare FACS and MUG data.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Strong correlation observed, validating FACS as a high-throughput screening method.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validated method can be used for further screening.

evidence.quote_or_span: The strong correlation of the results from the FACS primary screening and the secondary MUG screening in the 96-well format validates the use of flow cytometry as a high-throughput method to screen nifHv-S3 libraries.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Validated hits V1-V5 available.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to characterize H2 production and N2 fixation of variants.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Variants will show enhanced H2 production, possibly with trade-offs in N2 fixation.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Measure H2 and ethylene production in variants.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Variants show up to 4-fold higher H2 production; V3, V5, V7 lose N2 fixation; V7 has 6000-fold higher H2:ethylene ratio.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: V7's unique properties warrant further investigation.

evidence.quote_or_span: The ratio of $H_{2}$ to ethylene production in V7 strain was 6000-fold higher than in pRHB576-S3.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: V7 identified as a highly H2-overproducing variant.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to understand the genetic basis of V7's phenotype.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: V7 contains a frameshift mutation causing C-terminal truncation.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Sequence nifH-V7 and analyze mutations.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Frameshift at codon 172 results in a NifH protein lacking 124 amino acids at its C-terminus; also G32C and A107T substitutions.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Truncation may affect structure and function; modeling needed.

evidence.quote_or_span: A frame shift mutation was found in codon 172 of nifH-V7 that would result in a NifH protein lacking 124 amino acids at its C-terminus.
NifH-V7 would carry G32C and A107T amino acid substitutions.
In addition, NifH-V7 would carry G32C and A107T amino acid substitutions.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: V7 mutation known.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to assess structural impact of truncation.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Homology modeling can predict if V7 still forms a homodimer capable of ligating [4Fe-4S] clusters.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Generate 3-D models of wild-type and V7 NifH by homology modeling.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Both models form homodimers capable of ligating [4Fe-4S] clusters; RMSD 0.900 Å.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Modeling does not explain mechanism; limitations remain.

evidence.quote_or_span: 3-D model of R. capsulatus NifH and the V7 variant were generated by homology modeling (http://swissmodel.expasy.org/) using the A. vinelandii NifH structures as template (1NIP for wild-type NifH and 1G5P for NifH-V7, respectively).
Both models yielded NifH homodimer with capability to ligate $[4Fe-4S]$ clusters.
The overall protein overlap (4–171 amino acid residues) and an RMSD value of 0.900 Angstroms for the protein backbone atoms indicate very similar model architectures.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Structural models available.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Mechanism of V7's H+ reduction despite lacking ATP-binding residues is unknown; method limited to R. capsulatus.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: V7's truncation may enable H+ reduction without ATP hydrolysis, but mechanism is not elucidated.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Acknowledge limitations and need for further study.
evolution_trajectory.observation: V7 lacks 40% of wild-type sequence, a surprising finding; method not yet applied to other organisms.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Limitations highlight future directions.

evidence.quote_or_span: Perhaps the most interesting was the V7 variant, which lacks 124 amino acid residues at the C-terminus of the protein (Fig.
One of the selected $H_{2}$ -overproducing Fe protein variants lacked 40% of the wild-type amino acid sequence, a surprising finding for a protein that is highly conserved in nature.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: The paper has been published with a typographical error in the Conclusions section.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: A typographical error in the Conclusions section needs correction.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A corrigendum can correct the typographical error.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Issue a corrigendum to correct the typographical error in the Conclusions section.
evolution_trajectory.observation: The corrigendum corrects a typographical error in the Conclusions section.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: The correction ensures accuracy of the published article.

evidence.quote_or_span: This Article contains a typographical error in the Conclusions section.

validation_methods: Gas chromatography for H2 and ethylene
success_verification.final_conclusion: A high-throughput screening method was developed and validated, yielding H2-overproducing nitrogenase variants, including V7 with a large C-terminal truncation.
limitations: Mechanism of V7's H+ reduction not elucidated
Method limited to R. capsulatus

source.title: Selection of chromosomal DNA libraries using a multiplex CRISPR system

initial_request.research_problem: Extrachromosomal gene libraries in yeast suffer from high copy number variation, leading to variable expression and confounding selection. The goal is to develop a multiplex CRISPR-Cas9 system (CRISPRm) for efficient, markerless integration of DNA libraries into the yeast genome, enabling homogeneous expression and improved selections.
initial_request.known_context: CEN plasmids vary widely in copy number per cell, causing variable expression. Standard homologous recombination is too inefficient for markerless library integration.
constraints: Markerless integration
High-throughput library integration
Homogeneous expression
quantifiable_goals: Achieve nearly 100% barcode insertion efficiency at a single locus
Enable multiplex targeting with >40% efficiency for duplex and >15% for triplex
Demonstrate improved cellobiose utilization through directed evolution

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Extrachromosomal gene libraries in yeast suffer from high copy number variation, leading to variable expression and confounding selection. Standard homologous recombination is too inefficient for markerless integration of DNA libraries.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to achieve efficient, markerless chromosomal integration of DNA libraries to eliminate copy number variation.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: CRISPR-Cas9 can increase homology-directed repair efficiency by several 1000-fold, enabling high-throughput chromosomal integration.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Develop a multiplex CRISPR-Cas9 system (CRISPRm) for markerless integration of DNA libraries into the yeast genome.
evolution_trajectory.observation: The presence of the Cas9-produced DSB in genomic DNA can increase the rate of homology-directed repair (HDR) with linear DNA at the DSB locus by several 1000-fold.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Design a new sgRNA architecture to improve expression and stability for multiplexing.

evidence.quote_or_span: present directed evolution systems require the use of extrachromosomal gene libraries encoded in bacteriophage or plasmids, which suffer from high levels of copy number variation
common yeast centromere containing (CEN) plasmids vary widely in copy number per cell resulting in highly variable expression levels from cell to cell
The presence of the Cas9-produced DSB in genomic DNA can increase the rate of homology-directed repair (HDR) with linear DNA at the DSB locus by several 1000-fold

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Hypothesis that CRISPR-Cas9 can enhance HDR for chromosomal integration, but sgRNA stability and expression need optimization.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: sgRNA 5' end is susceptible to exonucleases, limiting abundance and multiplexing efficiency.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Fusing the sgRNA to the 3' end of the self-cleaving HDV ribozyme will protect the 5' end and allow flexible promoter use.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Design a new sgRNA architecture with HDV ribozyme fused to the 5' end.
evolution_trajectory.observation: The structured ribozyme would protect the 5' end of the sgRNA from 5' exonucleases.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Test various RNA polymerase III promoters for sgRNA expression with the new architecture.

evidence.quote_or_span: We reasoned that the structured ribozyme would protect the 5' end of the sgRNA from 5' exonucleases

evolution_trajectory.state_before: HDV ribozyme-sgRNA fusion designed, but optimal promoter for sgRNA expression unknown.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which RNA polymerase III promoter yields highest sgRNA expression and targeting efficiency?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: tRNA promoters will provide efficient sgRNA expression due to their natural processing.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Test various RNA polymerase III promoters for sgRNA expression in diploid yeast at the URA3 locus.
evolution_trajectory.observation: tRNA promoters resulted in nearly 100% efficient barcode insertion at the URA3 locus, while non-tRNA promoters were mostly ineffective.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Assess target sequence bias across multiple genomic loci using the best promoter.

evidence.quote_or_span: With a single sgRNA, we found tRNA sequences used as RNA polymerase III promoters resulted in nearly 100% efficient barcode insertion in diploid yeast at the URA3 locus, resulting in 5-fluororotic acid resistance (Boeke et al., 1984) whereas the non-tRNA promoters were mostly ineffective (Figure 3A; Supplementary file 1A).

evolution_trajectory.state_before: tRNA promoters identified as highly efficient for single-locus targeting, but target sequence bias unknown.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Does targeting efficiency vary across different genomic loci and guide sequences?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Targeting efficiency will be high for most loci, but some may require guide sequence optimization.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Target 11 unlinked yeast genes in diploid S288C cells using tRNA^Tyr promoter.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Targeting was highly efficient for every genomic target except LEU2, which was restored to 100% by selecting a different guide sequence (LEU2-2).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Extend to multiplex targeting of multiple loci simultaneously.

evidence.quote_or_span: We also assessed target sequence bias by targeting 11 unlinked yeast genes in diploid S288C yeast cells using tRNA $^{Tyr}$ as the RNA polymerase III promoter.

We found that targeting was highly efficient for every genomic target except LEU2, which at first was weak but was restored to 100% by selecting a different guide sequence (LEU2-2) as the sgRNA (Figure 3B).

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Single-locus targeting efficiency established, but multiplex targeting capability unknown.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can CRISPRm simultaneously target multiple unlinked genomic loci with high efficiency?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Multiplex targeting efficiency will be lower than single-locus but sufficient for library integration, and dependent on HDV ribozyme.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Test trans-multiplexed CRISPR-Cas9 by targeting two and three unlinked loci in diploid cells.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Duplex targeting efficiency was 43%, triplex 19%, and without HDV ribozyme duplex dropped to 3.5%.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate that HDV ribozyme increases sgRNA abundance, explaining improved multiplexing.

evidence.quote_or_span: We simultaneously targeted two and three unlinked genomic loci in diploid cells for loss-of-function mutagenesis by introducing a single plasmid expressing Cas9 and two or three sgRNAs with two or three gene-specific barcoded DNA molecules, respectively. The efficiency of duplex and triplex targeting (requiring four and six chromosome cuts, respectively) with two sgRNAs (URA3 and LYP1) or three (URA3, LYP1 and COX10) was 43% and 19%, respectively (Figure 3D; Supplementary file 1A).

This multiplexed efficiency was dependent on the presence of the HDV ribozyme 5' of the guide sequence as duplex targeting dropped to 3.5% in cells with sgRNA lacking the 5' HDV ribozyme (Figure 3D; Supplementary file 1A).

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Multiplex targeting efficiency shown to depend on HDV ribozyme, but mechanism unclear.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Does the HDV ribozyme increase intracellular sgRNA abundance?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: HDV ribozyme increases sgRNA abundance by protecting from degradation, and sgRNA abundance is rate-limiting for CRISPR targeting.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Measure relative cellular abundance of sgRNAs with and without HDV ribozyme by RT-qPCR.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Presence of the ribozyme increased intracellular sgRNA abundance by sixfold.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Apply cis-multiplexing for in vivo assembly of functional genes from multiple fragments.

evidence.quote_or_span: We measured the relative cellular abundance of sgRNAs expressed by $P_{SNR52}$ , with and without the 5' HDV ribozyme, by reverse transcription quantitative polymerase chain reaction (RT-qPCR) and found that the presence of the ribozyme increases the intracellular abundance of the sgRNAs by sixfold (Figure 3E), consistent with the model that sgRNA abundance is rate limiting for CRISPR/Cas targeting (Hsu et al., 2013).

evolution_trajectory.state_before: sgRNA abundance validated as rate-limiting, enabling complex multi-fragment assembly.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can CRISPRm assemble a functional gene from multiple overlapping PCR products in vivo?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Cis-multiplexing (targeting all copies of a locus) will allow efficient assembly of a selectable marker from three fragments.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Test in vivo assembly of a nourseothricin-resistance gene from three overlapping PCR products at the URA3 locus.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 85% efficiency in diploid S288C and 70% in polyploid ATCC4124.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Use CRISPRm for directed evolution of cdt-1 transporter to improve cellobiose utilization.

evidence.quote_or_span: We tested the efficiency of in vivo assembly of a functional nourseothricin-resistance (Nat $^{R}$ ) gene from three overlapping PCR products encoding a transcription promoter, protein open reading frame (ORF) and transcription terminator at a single locus using cis-multiplexing in the diploid S288C strain.

We found 85% efficiency of targeting and assembly in both copies of the URA3 locus in diploid yeast S288C cells, and 70% in polyploid ATCC4124 by using the tRNA $^{Pro}$ sequence as the sgRNA promoter (Figure 3C; Supplementary file 1A)

evolution_trajectory.state_before: CRISPRm enables efficient multi-fragment assembly, suitable for library integration.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can CRISPRm be used for directed evolution of a transporter to improve cellobiose utilization?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Error-prone PCR library of cdt-1 alleles integrated into the chromosome will allow selection of improved variants.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform in vivo selection for improved cellobiose utilization by integrating an error-prone PCR library of cdt-1 into the URA3 locus.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Identified strains with 2.6-fold and 1.7-fold increased cellobiose utilization over wild type.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Combine beneficial mutations to test for synergistic effects.

evidence.quote_or_span: To select for improved cellobiose utilizing strains we used error-prone PCR to amplify the cdt-1 gene. We co-transformed the library of mutated cdt-1 alleles, along with overlapping dsDNA fragments for the promoter and terminator, for assembly and integration into the URA3 locus (ura3:: $P_{PGK1}$ -cdt-1- $T_{ADH1}$ library) of a diploid strain with a previously integrated $\beta$ -glucosidase gh1-1 gene (lyp1:: $P_{TDH3}$ -gh1-1- $T_{CYC1}$ ) using CRISPRm.

We then grew the transformants in medium containing cellobiose as the sole carbon source to select for functional cdt-1 alleles and identified one strain with 2.6-fold (homozygous cdt-1 N209S/I354N/S360P/T406S/W531L allele) and one strain with 1.7-fold (homozygous cdt-1 Q45H/F262Y/F533Y allele) increased cellobiose utilization capacity over wild type cdt-1 (Figure 4A).

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Two cdt-1 mutant alleles identified with improved cellobiose utilization, but potential for synergy unknown.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can combining mutations from different alleles yield synergistic improvements?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Introducing the N209S mutation into the Q45H/F262Y/F533Y background will create a quadruple mutant with enhanced activity.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Use CRISPRm to introduce the N209S mutation into the cdt-1 Q45H/F262Y/F533Y background.
evolution_trajectory.observation: The quadruple mutant resulted in synergistic cellobiose utilization with 3.9-fold increased growth over wild-type.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate improved mutants in anaerobic fermentations.

evidence.quote_or_span: The quadruple mutant cdt-1 allele resulted in synergistic cellobiose utilization with 3.9-fold increased growth over strains expressing wild-type cdt-1 (Figure 4A).

We then grew the transformants in medium containing cellobiose as the sole carbon source to select for functional cdt-1 alleles and identified one strain with 2.6-fold (homozygous cdt-1 N209S/I354N/S360P/T406S/W531L allele) and one strain with 1.7-fold (homozygous cdt-1 Q45H/F262Y/F533Y allele) increased cellobiose utilization capacity over wild type cdt-1 (Figure 4A).

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Improved cdt-1 mutants show enhanced growth on cellobiose plates, but performance in industrially relevant conditions unknown.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Do the selected cdt-1 alleles enable efficient cellobiose fermentation under anaerobic conditions?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Chromosomally integrated mutant cdt-1 alleles will support complete cellobiose fermentation, unlike wild-type.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Test selected cdt-1 alleles in anaerobic fermentations using cellobiose as the sole carbon source.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Wild-type cdt-1 integrated into the chromosome barely supported fermentation, while mutants enabled complete fermentation.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Discuss limitations of the selection method.

evidence.quote_or_span: We next tested the selected cdt-1 alleles in anaerobic fermentations using cellobiose as the sole carbon source.
We found that wild-type cdt-1 integrated into the chromosome was barely able to support cellobiose fermentation (Figure 4C).

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Successful directed evolution and validation, but potential confounding factors in selection.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Could the selection have enriched for sequences that improve mRNA stability or translation efficiency rather than protein function?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: In vivo selection may enrich for non-coding mutations affecting expression, not just protein activity.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Acknowledge limitation that selection may have enriched for sequences conferring improved mRNA stability or translation efficiency.
evolution_trajectory.observation: It is possible that selection enriched for sequences that confer improved mRNA stability or translation efficiency, due to the in vivo nature of the selection.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Note strain-dependent promoter efficiency as another limitation.

evidence.quote_or_span: it is also possible that selection enriched for sequences that confer improved mRNA stability or translation efficiency, due to the fact that the selection was carried out in vivo.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: CRISPRm efficiency shown to vary with tRNA promoter and strain background.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Is the choice of tRNA promoter universally effective across different yeast strains?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: tRNA promoter efficiency is strain-dependent, limiting generalizability.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Report that tRNA^Tyr was inefficient in ATCC4124 though effective in S288C diploid, indicating strain-dependent promoter activity.
evolution_trajectory.observation: tRNA^Tyr was inefficient at targeting in ATCC4124 though effective in S288C diploid, indicating strain-dependent promoter choice impacts multiplexing.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Conclude the trajectory.

evidence.quote_or_span: Notably, tRNA $^{Tyr}$ was inefficient at targeting in ATCC4124 though it was effective in S288C diploid (Supplementary file 1A), indicating that the choice of tRNA used for the expression of the HDV-sgRNA impacts multiplexing in a strain-dependent manner.

validation_methods: Anaerobic fermentation with cellobiose as sole carbon source
metrics: Complete fermentation of cellobiose by mutant strains
success_verification.final_conclusion: CRISPRm enables efficient, markerless chromosomal integration of DNA libraries, eliminating copy number variation. Directed evolution of cdt-1 yielded mutants with up to 3.9-fold improved cellobiose utilization, enabling complete anaerobic fermentation.
limitations: Selection may enrich for sequences improving mRNA stability or translation efficiency, not just protein function.
Choice of tRNA promoter impacts multiplexing in a strain-dependent manner; tRNA^Tyr inefficient in ATCC4124.

source.title: Integrating Statistical Predictions and Experimental Verifications for Enhancing Protein-Chemical Interaction Predictions in Virtual Screening

initial_request.research_problem: Predicting protein-chemical interactions for virtual screening without 3D structural data, while reducing false positives when screening large compound libraries.
initial_request.known_context: One-layer SVM approach previously developed for predicting protein-chemical interactions, but suffers from many false positives in comprehensive predictions.
constraints: No 3D structural data available
Must handle large compound libraries
quantifiable_goals: Reduce false positive rate in comprehensive predictions
Achieve high precision in virtual screening

evolution_trajectory.state_before: One-layer SVM produces many false positives in comprehensive predictions.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to reduce false positives while maintaining recall?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A two-layer SVM architecture with carefully designed negative data can reduce false positives.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Propose two strategies: two-layer SVM and reasonable negative data design.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Conceptual design of two-layer SVM and negative data design to address false positives.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need to implement first-layer SVM models.

evidence.quote_or_span: it suffered from the problem of predicting many false positives when comprehensive predictions were conducted.
we describe two strategies, namely two-layer SVM and reasonable negative data design, which are used for the purpose of reducing the number of false positives

evolution_trajectory.state_before: First-layer SVM models generated.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to combine first-layer outputs and design negatives to reduce false positives?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A second-layer SVM using first-layer outputs as inputs, trained on designed negatives with RBF kernel, will improve precision.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Construct second-layer SVM using outputs of first-layer models as inputs, with RBF kernel, and design negatives using expansion rules (min, mlt, mle, max).
evolution_trajectory.observation: Second-layer SVM designed to use first-layer outputs; negative data design uses expansion rules based on SVM outputs.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Evaluate precision improvement in computational experiments.

evidence.quote_or_span: The second-layer SVM directly utilizes the outputs of the first-layer SVM models as inputs.
The second-layer SVM model was constructed from the whole DrugBank dataset and reasonably designed negatives, which are described in detail later, on the basis of the RBF kernel

the negative samples were extended sequentially according to the four expansion rules min, mlt, mle and max by using the outputs of the prediction model constructed from positive samples and tentative negative samples

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Precision improvement shown in computational experiments.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How much does the method reduce predictions in comprehensive screening?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Two-layer SVM with max dataset will drastically reduce predictions compared to one-layer SVM with random negatives.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform comprehensive prediction on three proteins using PubChem.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Number of candidates discovered by max dataset in allpos two-layer SVM was about one fiftieth of that by random negative dataset in one-layer SVM.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Reduced predictions enable feasible experimental verification.

evidence.quote_or_span: the number of candidates discovered by using the max dataset in the allpos two-layer SVM approach was about one fiftieth of the number of chemical compounds predicted by using the random negative dataset in the one-layer SVM

evolution_trajectory.state_before: ~50-fold reduction in predictions achieved.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Do the predicted interactions hold experimentally?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Predicted interactions for androgen receptor will be validated experimentally.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Test purchasable compounds, including known drugs, for androgen receptor binding.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Precision of 67% (4/6) and accuracy of 67% (8/12) obtained.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Use experimental feedback to reconstruct model.

evidence.quote_or_span: a precision of 67% (4/6) and an accuracy of 67% (8/12) were obtained

evolution_trajectory.state_before: First experimental verification completed with 67% precision.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to incorporate experimental feedback to improve predictions?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Reconstructing the prediction model using feedback, including misclassified compounds, will improve performance.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Reconstruct prediction model using results of first experimental verification.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Prediction model reconstructed utilizing first experimental verification results.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Design new model with steroid-like compounds as negatives for antagonist search.

evidence.quote_or_span: By utilizing the results of the first experimental verification, the prediction model was reconstructed.
a precision of 67% (4/6) and an accuracy of 67% (8/12) were obtained

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Steroid-like negative model constructed.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to control prediction increase when incorporating feedback?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: An additional first-layer SVM model with a weighting factor in the second-layer SVM can control prediction increase with slight recall decrease.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Use additional first-layer SVM model with sufficient weighting factor in second-layer SVM.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Use of additional model with weighting factor controlled prediction increase with slight recall decrease.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Perform second experimental verification.

evidence.quote_or_span: the use of the additional model with a sufficient weighting factor controlled the increase of the predictions with a slight decrease of the recall rate

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Feedback-reconstructed models ready.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Does the reconstructed model yield improved predictions?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Second experimental verification will confirm predictions from reconstructed models.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Test purchasable candidates predicted by reconstructed models.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Precision of 60% (3/5) achieved.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate biological validity on diverse datasets.

evidence.quote_or_span: With this threshold, 59% accuracy (10/17) and 57% precision (8/14) were achieved while our method obtained an overall 65% accuracy (11/17; 8/12 in the first experimental verification and 3/5 in the second experimental verification) and 64% precision (7/11; 4/6 in the first experimental verification and 3/5 in the second experimental verification).

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Experimental verifications completed.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Does the statistical approach capture biological binding properties?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: High prediction performance on diverse protein families will support biological validity.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Evaluate prediction performance on diverse datasets (nuclear receptors, GPCRs, ion channels, enzymes).
evolution_trajectory.observation: More than 0.85 AUC and 80% accuracy obtained for diverse datasets.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Confirm dependence on biological binding with shuffled datasets.

evidence.quote_or_span: more than 0.85 AUC and an accuracy of 80% were obtained for diverse datasets

evolution_trajectory.state_before: High performance on diverse datasets shown.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Is the prediction accuracy dependent on biological binding properties?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Prediction accuracy will decrease proportionally with the fraction of valid samples in shuffled artificial datasets.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Create shuffled artificial datasets with varying fractions of biologically valid samples and measure accuracy.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Prediction accuracy was proportional to the content rate of biologically valid samples.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Demonstrate statistical significance and scalability.

evidence.quote_or_span: the prediction accuracy was proportional to the content rate of the biologically valid samples

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Experimental verifications and biological validation completed.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Is the virtual screening precision statistically significant?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: The precision of virtual screening is statistically significant with extremely small P-values.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Calculate P-values for virtual screening precision.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Extremely small P-values prove the significance of the virtual screening and its precision.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Demonstrate genome-wide screening capability.

evidence.quote_or_span: These extremely small P-values prove the significance of the virtual screening and its precision in the drug discovery process.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Statistical significance demonstrated.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can the method scale to genome-wide virtual screening?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: The statistical method enables genome-wide virtual screening of >19M compounds, infeasible with docking methods.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform virtual screening of more than 19 million chemical compounds from PubChem for any proteins in genome-wide scale.
evolution_trajectory.observation: The method can perform a virtual screening of more than 19 million chemical compounds from the PubChem Compound database for any proteins in genome-wide scale, infeasible with existing docking methods.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Re-evaluate against ligand-based virtual screening.

evidence.quote_or_span: our method can perform a virtual screening of more than 19 million chemical compounds from the PubChem Compound database for any proteins in genome-wide scale and this immense screening task would be infeasible to accomplish with any of the existing docking methods.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Genome-wide screening capability shown.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How does the method compare to ligand-based virtual screening?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Proposed methods outperform ligand-based virtual screening in recall rate at higher ranks.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Re-evaluate using experimentally verified compounds and compare recall rate at ranks higher than 500 against LBVS.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Proposed methods achieved a higher recall rate at ranks higher than 500 compared to a conventional Ligand Based Virtual Screening method.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Conclude overall approach.

evidence.quote_or_span: our proposed methods (“one-layer SVM”, “two-layer SVM-subpos” and “two-layer SVM-allpos”) achieved a higher recall rate at ranks higher than 500 compared to a conventional Ligand Based Virtual Screening method (“only compound SVM” in Figure 6A).

success_verification.final_conclusion: The two-layer SVM with designed negatives significantly reduces false positives, achieving high precision in virtual screening, validated experimentally and biologically, and outperforms ligand-based virtual screening.
limitations: Experimental verification limited to androgen receptor
Small number of compounds tested experimentally
Dependence on availability of known interaction data

source.title: WISDOM-II: Screening against multiple targets implicated in malaria using computational grid infrastructures

initial_request.research_problem: Malaria drug resistance necessitates discovery of new drugs targeting multiple proteins. Virtual high-throughput screening (vHTS) is cost-effective but computationally intensive. WISDOM-II extends to multiple targets (PfGST, PfDHFR, PvDHFR) to counteract resistance, leveraging grid computing for large-scale docking of 4.3M compounds from ZINC.
initial_request.target_object: Multiple malaria targets: PfGST, PfDHFR (wild-type and quadruple mutant), PvDHFR (wild-type and double mutant)
initial_request.known_context: WISDOM-I successfully screened 42 million compounds against plasmepsins, identifying novel scaffolds confirmed in vitro. Drug resistance in DHFR arises from point mutations. GST inhibition impairs detoxification and antioxidant capacity.
constraints: Computational intensity of large-scale virtual screening
Need for validated target structures and docking parameters
quantifiable_goals: Identify novel inhibitors against multiple malaria targets
Achieve successful re-docking (RMSD < 2 Å for PfDHFR)

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Malaria drug resistance is a major problem; single-target drugs are insufficient.

evolution_trajectory.observation: Targets selected: PfGST (detoxification), PfDHFR (folate pathway, mutations cause resistance), PvDHFR (also important for vivax malaria).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need to prepare target structures and optimize docking parameters.

three scaffolds were identified, one of them is guanidino scaffold, which is likely to be novel

parameters.minimization: 500 steps H-only, 5000 steps all-atom, 50 ps MD at 300K, 5000 steps final minimization

evolution_trajectory.observation: Structures prepared: PfGST (1Q4J, active site 8 Å around GTX), PfDHFR (missing residues modeled, two conserved waters kept), PvDHFR (similarly prepared).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need to select compound database and docking software.

the position of missing residues from Asp87 to Asn90 was modelled with Modeller software
All water molecules in the crystal structure were removed except for two conserved waters

evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Proceed with validated parameters for PfGST and PfDHFR; PvDHFR excluded from further screening.

evidence.quote_or_span: Parameter 8 (maximum allowed overlap volume between protein and ligand surface: 100 Å³) gave the best results

Re-docking against minimized structures with the same parameter sets gave best results. All the parameter sets reproduced the actual binding mode of the ligand, further made interactions to key amino acids and RMS deviation were less than 2 Å.
Parameter 8 (maximum allowed overlap volume between protein and ligand surface: 100 Å³) gave the best results in terms of docking score, docking conformation and interactions to key amino acids.

The significant achievement on the grid side is the improved WISDOM production environment, which enabled smooth and successful deployment of docking jobs on the grid. The major difference between our previous deployments (WISDOM-I and the data challenge on avian flu) is the automatic resubmission of the docking jobs on the grid.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Parse docking results into MySQL database storing scores and atom-to-atom interactions.

evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Conclude study and outline future directions.

evidence.quote_or_span: One major bottleneck in large scale screening experiments is the handling of large data output of these experiments.
this problem is addressed by parsing the results into a MySQL database, storing the docking score as well as atom-to-atom interaction between the protein and ligand.

validation_methods: Re-docking of co-crystallized ligands to validate docking parameters
success_verification.final_conclusion: WISDOM-II successfully screened 4.3 million compounds against multiple malaria targets, identifying diverse hits with promising interactions for PfGST. PvDHFR re-docking failed, excluding it from screening. Data handling remains a bottleneck, addressed by MySQL database.

source.title: Rational computational design and development of an immunogenic multiepitope vaccine incorporating transmembrane proteins of Fusobacterium necrophorum

initial_request.research_problem: Fusobacterium necrophorum causes severe human and livestock diseases with rising antimicrobial resistance and no effective vaccines, motivating computational design of a multi-epitope vaccine targeting transmembrane proteins.
initial_request.known_context: Rising antimicrobial resistance and lack of effective vaccines for F. necrophorum infections.
constraints: Computational design only; no experimental validation performed.
Epitopes must be non-allergenic, non-toxic, and antigenic.
quantifiable_goals: Design a multi-epitope vaccine with high antigenicity (VaxiJen >0.4).
Achieve >99% global population coverage.
Induce robust immune response in silico.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: No effective vaccines for F. necrophorum; rising antimicrobial resistance.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need for alternative therapeutic strategies against F. necrophorum.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Transmembrane proteins of F. necrophorum are promising vaccine targets due to immune accessibility and roles in virulence.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Focus on transmembrane proteins for vaccine design.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Transmembrane proteins are accessible to the immune system and involved in adhesion, invasion, and virulence.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Select specific transmembrane proteins with high antigenicity.

evidence.quote_or_span: The rise in antimicrobial resistance and the absence of effective vaccines underscore the need for alternative therapeutic strategies.
Transmembrane proteins present promising targets for vaccination due to their accessibility to the immune system and their involvement in bacterial adhesion, invasion, and virulence

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Hypothesis that transmembrane proteins are good vaccine targets.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which specific transmembrane proteins to select?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Proteins with high antigenicity (>0.4), non-allergenicity, non-toxicity, and roles in virulence are suitable.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Select four transmembrane proteins: outer membrane efflux protein, SecA, FtsH, SecF.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Four proteins met criteria from 2722 total proteins.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Predict epitopes from selected proteins.

evidence.quote_or_span: These proteins were chosen based on their high antigenicity scores (>0.4), non-allergenicity and non-toxicity, along with their functional roles in bacterial virulence, including adhesion, invasion, and biofilm formation.
From a total of 2722, four transmembrane proteins were selected based on their antigenicity and biological significance.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Four transmembrane proteins selected.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to identify immunogenic epitopes for B-cells, MHC-I, and MHC-II.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Epitopes with high binding affinity and low percentile rank will be immunogenic.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Use Bepipred 2.0 for B-cell epitopes, ANN 4.0 for MHC-I (IC50 <100 nM, rank <1%), NN-align for MHC-II (IC50 <100 nM).
evolution_trajectory.observation: Selected epitopes meeting criteria.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Assemble epitopes into a vaccine construct.

evidence.quote_or_span: Only epitopes with IC50 values < 100 nM and percentile ranks < 1% were selected
The cut-off for selection was based on IC50 values < 100 nM and a low percentile rank
two epitopes selected from each protein based on their low percentage rank with their corresponding IC50 values, specifically those less than 100
The chosen epitopes were based on the antigenicity score exceeding 0.4 and an evaluation of their non-allergenic properties.
Yellow lines show the engineered disulfide bonds predicted by Disulfide by Design 2.0

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Epitopes predicted.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to link epitopes and enhance immunogenicity?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Using specific linkers and adding adjuvant will create a stable, immunogenic construct.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Link B-cell epitopes with AYY, MHC-I with KK, MHC-II with AAY; add beta-defensin adjuvant via GPGPG, and 6xHis tag.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Vaccine construct assembled.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate antigenicity, allergenicity, toxicity of construct.

evidence.quote_or_span: The linkers GPGPG, KK, AAY, and AYY were used to join the selected epitopes in the final vaccine construct
Eight MHC Class-I epitopes were linked using KK, eight MHC Class-II were connected via AAY and four B cell epitopes were joined using AYY linkers
a beta defensin protein sequence was added to the N-terminal N using a GPGPG linker
a 6X Histidine tag was incorporated at the C-terminus

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Vaccine construct designed.

The antigenicity was 0.7293, which is greater than the threshold level of 0.4 as predicted by VaxiJen server.
Toxipred server confirmed that the vaccine construct is non-toxic.

The antigenicity score of our vaccine construct was 0.7293 (VaxiJen analysis), which classifies it as highly immunogenic. Also, the population coverage estimates showed values of 99.39%, ensuring the applicability across different ethnicities.

source.title: Modification of the active centre of nattokinase to enhance its thermostability using a strategy based on molecular dynamics simulation, steered dynamics simulation, and conservative prediction

initial_request.research_problem: Nattokinase has poor thermostability limiting industrial use. Existing modification methods have not targeted the active center due to high conservation and risk of inactivation.
initial_request.known_context: Thermostability modification studies of nattokinase have not addressed its active centre region, while active centre modifications using directed evolution tend to result in a higher workload and a high percentage of negative results.
constraints: Modifications must not inactivate the enzyme
Must overcome activity-stability trade-off
quantifiable_goals: Enhance thermostability
Maintain or increase activity
Reduce screening workload

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Nattokinase thermostability is poor, and active center modification is challenging due to high conservation and risk of inactivation.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: No rational design strategy exists for active center thermostability modification that overcomes activity-stability trade-off.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A rational design strategy targeting the active center can overcome the activity-stability trade-off and reduce screening workload.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Propose a rational design strategy (MSC) combining MD, SMD, and conservation analysis.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Rational design strategy is hypothesized to effectively solve the problems.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Design the MSC strategy steps.

evidence.quote_or_span: The current thermostability modification strategies for nattokinase do not involve modification of the catalytic centre.

Modifying the active centre of an enzyme is a more challenging process than modifying other regions. This is due to the fact that the amino acid residues in the active centre of an enzyme are highly conserved and interact extensively with the substrate. This makes it more likely that the enzyme will become inactivated following a mutation in this region.

Thermostability modification studies of nattokinase have not addressed its active centre region, while active centre modifications using directed evolution tend to result in a higher workload and a high percentage of negative results.

It is therefore evident that a rational design strategy to enhance the thermostability of the enzyme by modifying its active centre represents an effective means of solving the aforementioned problems.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Hypothesis that rational design can overcome trade-off.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to define specific computational steps to identify candidate sites.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Combining MD at different temperatures, SMD, and conservation analysis will pinpoint active center sites affecting thermostability.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Design MSC strategy: 1) MD to find flexible sites, 2) SMD to eliminate substrate-interacting residues, 3) conservation analysis to exclude strictly conserved residues.
evolution_trajectory.observation: MSC strategy designed with three sequential filters.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Execute MD simulations to identify flexible active center residues.

evidence.quote_or_span: a new strategy based on the combination of molecular dynamics simulation at different temperatures, steered molecular dynamics simulation, and sequence conservative analysis (MSC) was designed in this study.

The initial identification of candidate sites with high flexibility changes was achieved through a comparative analysis of RMSF alterations in the active centre of nattokinase under varying temperature conditions. This was followed by the utilisation of steered molecular dynamics simulation to eliminate sites involved in substrate interactions upon substrate entry into the enzyme's active centre. Subsequently, the highly conserved sites were further excluded through a sequence conservative analysis.

it has become imperative to devise a rational design scheme for the stability modification of the active centre of nattokinase that reduces the intensity of screening work while improving the prediction accuracy.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: MSC strategy designed.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which active center residues show high flexibility change with temperature?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Residues with ΔRMSF > 0.05 Å between 298K and 328K are candidates for thermostability modification.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Run MD simulations at 298K and 328K and compute RMSF differences.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 20 active center residues identified with ΔRMSF > 0.05 Å.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Apply SMD to eliminate residues interacting with substrate during entry.

evidence.quote_or_span: 20 amino acid residues (33–34, 62–63, 66–68, 93–127, 154, 216–218, 224) having RMSF differences greater than 0.05 Å.

Our previous study showed that the activity and thermostability-enhanced nattokinase T174V mutant had similarly reduced flexibility in the region of positions 167–176 under 328 K simulation conditions, which is consistent with the results of the present study, suggesting that conformational stabilisation of this region facilitates $Ca^{2+}$ binding, which in turn enhances thermostability (17).

evolution_trajectory.state_before: 20 candidate residues identified.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Some residues may be essential for substrate binding; mutating them could inactivate enzyme.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Residues that interact with substrate during entry should be excluded.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform SMD of substrate docking and eliminate residues with prolonged hydrophobic interactions.
evolution_trajectory.observation: L96, L126, G154, M222 eliminated due to prolonged hydrophobic interactions with substrate.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Apply conservation analysis to exclude strictly conserved residues.

evidence.quote_or_span: L96, L126, G154 and M222 each have prolonged hydrophobic interactions with the substrate when the substrate is stretched from binding to the enzyme active centre to 15 Å from the enzyme active centre
20 amino acid residues (33–34, 62–63, 66–68, 93–127, 154, 216–218, 224) having RMSF differences greater than 0.05 Å.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform conservation analysis and exclude residues with conservation score 9.
