This includes mutants such as S33T, S62A, Y217K, and N218L, which confirm the important influence of the active centre of nattokinase on its thermostability (17, 18, 21).

To our expectation, mutations at most of the 13 candidate sites resulted in loss of activity, whereas mutants with enhanced thermostability were found at five sites, 33 (S33T), 62 (S62A), 216 (A216E, A216K, and A216R), and 218 (N218L; Table 2).

In summary, we selected three previously unreported mutants, A216E, A216K and A216R, with both increased activity and thermostability, from the screened positive sites for subsequent characterisation studies.

a rational design strategy combining MD-SMD-conservative analysis (MSC) was employed to reduce the screening intensity and improve the prediction accuracy of the active centre of nattokinase. source.title: SVSBI: sequence-based virtual screening of biomolecular interactions

initial_request.research_problem: Virtual screening accuracy heavily relies on 3D structures from molecular docking, which is often unreliable due to accumulated errors.
initial_request.target_object: Biomolecular interactions (protein-ligand, protein-protein, protein-nucleic acid, iPPI, PPI classification)
initial_request.known_context: Current virtual screening models depend on 3D structures from molecular docking, which introduces errors.
constraints: Avoid reliance on 3D structure-based docking
Use only sequence information
quantifiable_goals: Achieve high predictive accuracy (Rp, RMSE) across multiple biomolecular interaction types
Match or exceed structure-based method accuracy

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Virtual screening accuracy heavily relies on 3D structures from molecular docking, which is often unreliable due to accumulated errors.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can sequence-based approaches bypass the need for 3D structures and still accurately predict biomolecular interactions?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Sequence-based approaches using NLP embeddings and K-embedding strategies can capture interaction patterns without 3D structures.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Propose a sequence-based virtual screening (SVS) framework that uses NLP algorithms and K-embedding strategies to encode biomolecular interactions.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Sequence-based approaches may provide efficient, robust, and easily accessible deep embeddings of biomolecular interactions without invoking 3D structure docking.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Design the SVS framework with specific NLP models and K-embedding strategies.

evidence.quote_or_span: the accuracy of current VS models heavily relies on three-dimensional (3D) structures obtained through molecular docking, which is often unreliable due to the low accuracy
minor errors in these sub-processes may accumulate and result in unreliable structure-based docking
sequence-based approaches may provide efficient, robust, and easily accessible deep embeddings of biomolecular interactions without invoking 3D structure docking

we introduce a sequence-based virtual screening (SVS) as another generation of VS models that utilize advanced natural language processing (NLP) algorithms and optimized deep K-embedding strategies to encode biomolecular interactions without relying on 3D structure-based docking

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Hypothesis that sequence-based NLP and K-embedding can capture interaction patterns.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which specific NLP models and K-embedding strategies should be used?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A combination of biomolecular interaction module, sequence module, NLP module (UniRep, ESM, DNABERT, small molecular Transformer, autoencoder), K-embedding module (Stack, Prod, Diff), and ML module (ANN, GBDT) will effectively encode interactions.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Design SVS framework with these components.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Framework designed to convert distribution information of interactive molecular embeddings into optimal K-embedding and extract essential characteristics.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Evaluate SVS on protein-ligand binding affinity to test the design.

evidence.quote_or_span: we introduce a sequence-based virtual screening (SVS) as another generation of VS models that utilize advanced natural language processing (NLP) algorithms and optimized deep K-embedding strategies to encode biomolecular interactions without relying on 3D structure-based docking
the K-embedding strategies can convert the distribution information of interactive molecular embeddings into the optimal K-embedding and extract essential characteristics of biomolecular interactions

our approach, SVS, avoids data leakage by utilizing NLP-based K-embeddings. By extracting a wide range of hidden information from sequences, including structure, contextual, biochemical, and evolutionary information, our SVS model is less dependent on sequence similarity.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: SVS demonstrated on regression tasks, but classification performance on PPI datasets unknown.

evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: SVS with Stack embeddings will outperform SVM-NVDT on five PPI classification datasets.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Evaluate SVS on five PPI classification datasets (H. pylori, M. musculus, etc.) using Stack embeddings.
evolution_trajectory.observation: SVS achieves accuracy: 99.93% (H. pylori), 99.28% (M. musculus), 99.64%, 99.22%, 98.69%, outperforming SVM-NVDT.

evolution_trajectory.observation: Stack features generally have higher R and S scores. Modelability index exceeds 0.8 for all datasets, confirming robustness.

source.title: Development of a growth-coupled selection platform for directed evolution of heme biosynthetic enzymes in Corynebacterium glutamicum

initial_request.research_problem: Intrinsic low activity and feedback inhibition of heme biosynthetic enzymes limit heme overproduction, and existing directed evolution methods have limitations.
initial_request.target_object: Heme biosynthetic enzymes (PbgS, HmbS, UroS, UroD, CgoX, CpfC, ChdC) in Corynebacterium glutamicum
initial_request.known_context: Heme overproduction is limited by enzyme activity and feedback inhibition. Non-iron metalloporphyrins (MPs) like ZnPPIX can be detoxified by heme, potentially coupling growth to heme production.
constraints: Must work in C. glutamicum
Avoid complex genetic circuits or biosensors
quantifiable_goals: Improve growth under ZnPPIX stress
Increase specific activity and catalytic efficiency of heme biosynthetic enzymes

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Heme overproduction is limited by low enzyme activity and feedback inhibition.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: No existing growth-coupled selection method for heme biosynthetic enzymes in C. glutamicum.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Growth can be coupled to heme production via detoxification of non-iron metalloporphyrins (MPs) like ZnPPIX, as heme competes with MPs to restore electron transport system (ETS).
evolution_trajectory.decision: Develop a growth-coupled selection platform based on ZnPPIX detoxification.
evolution_trajectory.observation: A quantitative relationship between cell growth rate and activity of heme biosynthetic enzymes can be established independent of transcriptional regulator-based biosensors and complex genetic circuits.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Design and validate the selection platform.

evidence.quote_or_span: a quantitative relationship between cell growth rate and activity of heme biosynthetic enzymes can be established independent of the transcriptional regulator-based biosensor and complex genetic circuits
the intracellular heme could compete with MPs to restore the ETS and the cell growth

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Hypothesis formulated but no platform exists.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to create a selection system that links growth to heme production.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A ZnPPIX-based growth-coupled selection platform can be developed in C. glutamicum.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Construct a C. glutamicum strain with heme uptake system and test growth under ZnPPIX stress.
evolution_trajectory.observation: A growth-coupled selection platform based on the detoxification of ZnPPIX was successfully developed in C. glutamicum for the first time.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate the platform by modulating heme levels.

evidence.quote_or_span: a growth-coupled selection platform based on the detoxification of ZnPPIX was successfully developed in C. glutamicum for the first time

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Platform designed but not yet validated for coupling growth to heme levels.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Does increasing heme production improve growth under ZnPPIX stress?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Adding 5-ALA, a heme precursor, will increase intracellular heme and improve growth under ZnPPIX stress.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Supplement cultures with 5-ALA and measure growth and heme levels.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Adding 5-ALA promoted heme biosynthesis; intracellular and extracellular heme gradually increased, confirming feasibility of growth-coupled selection.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Identify which heme biosynthetic enzymes are rate-limiting by overexpression screen.

evidence.quote_or_span: adding 5-ALA promoted heme biosynthesis. With the increase of 5-ALA addition, the concentration of intracellular and extracellular heme gradually increased
adding 5-ALA promoted heme biosynthesis
the concentration of intracellular and extracellular heme gradually increased

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Platform validated; need to identify target enzymes for evolution.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which of the seven heme biosynthetic enzymes limits growth under ZnPPIX stress?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Overexpressing individual enzymes will reveal which ones improve growth, indicating rate-limiting steps.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Overexpress all seven heme biosynthetic enzymes (PbgS, HmbS, UroS, UroD, CgoX, CpfC, ChdC) and measure growth under ZnPPIX stress.
evolution_trajectory.observation: CpfC overexpression showed the highest growth rate; biomass was 1.81-fold higher than control at 24 h. HmbS, UroS, and ChdC did not improve growth.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Proceed with directed evolution of CpfC; note failure of three enzymes for future optimization.

evidence.quote_or_span: The strain overexpressing cpfC showed the highest growth rate amongst all the tested strains
The biomass was 1.81-fold higher than that of the control strain at 24 h

another three enzymes (HmbS, UroS, ChdC) have not exhibited improved growth tendencies, the selection platform could be optimized by regulating the intensity of gene expression and co-expressing multiple genes to improve the universality.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: CpfC identified as best target; need to generate and select improved variants.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can random mutagenesis and growth-coupled selection enrich CpfC mutants with higher activity?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A random mutation library of CpfC subjected to ZnPPIX selection will enrich mutants that improve growth.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Construct a random mutation library of CpfC (size ~10^6) and perform growth-coupled selection under ZnPPIX stress.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Clones with different sizes were obtained; enriched population showed improved growth.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate that growth improvement is due to CpfC mutations by reconstructing mutants.

evidence.quote_or_span: clones with different sizes were obtained

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Enriched mutants may contain background mutations.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Are the improved growth phenotypes due to CpfC mutations or other genomic/plasmid changes?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Reconstructing identified CpfC mutations in a clean background will confirm causality.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Clone identified CpfC mutations (E181G, T194S, E31V/E100D) into fresh plasmid and test growth.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Reconstructed mutants confirmed improved growth, ruling out background mutations.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Characterize the enzymatic activity of mutants to correlate with growth.

evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A fluorescence assay can be developed based on the fluorescence of CPIII (product) vs. coproheme (substrate).

evolution_trajectory.observation: A fluorescence detection method was constructed; confirmed CPIII is the native substrate.

evidence.quote_or_span: A fluorescence detection method was firstly constructed based on the fact that CPIII in solution at specific wavelengths is fluorescent, whereas coproheme is not (Porra and Lascelles, 1968).

Subsequently, 200 μL of the supernatant was added to a 96-well microtiter plate and the specific activity was determined using a microplate reader (SpectraMax M5, Molecular Devices, λ excitation = 395 nm, λ emission = 611 nm).

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Fluorescence assay available; mutants reconstructed.

evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: CpfC mutants will show increased specific activity and catalytic efficiency on CPIII.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform kinetic characterization of CpfC WT and mutants (E31V, E100D, E31V/E100D, E181G, T194S) using the fluorescence assay.
evolution_trajectory.observation: WT specific activity: 0.39 U/mg (CPIII) vs 2.22×10^-3 U/mg (PPIX). E31V had highest kcat/KM of 52.91 mM^-1 s^-1; E31V/E100D kcat/KM 37.01 mM^-1 s^-1 (2.12-fold higher than WT). Mutants showed lower KM and higher catalytic efficiency.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Extend the platform to other enzymes (PbgS, UroD, CgoX).

Additionally, the $k_{cat}/K_{M}$ of $CpfC^{E31V}$ was up to 52.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: CpfC evolution successful; other enzymes (PbgS, UroD, CgoX) showed growth improvement in overexpression screen.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can the growth-coupled selection platform be applied to evolve PbgS, UroD, and CgoX?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Directed evolution of PbgS, UroD, and CgoX using the same platform will yield mutants with improved growth.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Construct random mutation libraries for PbgS, UroD, and CgoX and perform growth-coupled selection under ZnPPIX stress.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Mutants showed 3.05- to 4.17-fold (PbgS), 1.46- to 5.96-fold (UroD), and 1.95- to 3.75-fold (CgoX) higher biomass than wild-type controls at 24 h.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Summarize overall effectiveness and limitations.

evidence.quote_or_span: The biomasses of the strains harboring PbgS, UroD, and CgoX mutants were 3.05- to 4.17-fold, 1.46- to 5.96-fold, and 1.95- to 3.75-fold higher than that of the control strains harboring wild-type enzymes at 24 h
95- to 3.75-fold higher than that of the control strains harboring wild-type enzymes at 24 h, respectively (Figure 3; Supplementary Table S3).
75-fold higher than that of the control strains harboring wild-type enzymes at 24 h, respectively (Figure 3; Supplementary Table S3).
59-fold, and 7.31-fold higher than that of the control strains overexpressing the wild-type genes, respectively (Supplementary Figure S7).

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Four enzymes evolved successfully; three failed.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Is the platform generally effective for heme biosynthetic enzymes?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: The growth-coupled selection system is effective for directed evolution of heme biosynthetic enzymes, but requires optimization for universality.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Assess overall results and identify limitations.
evolution_trajectory.observation: The platform successfully evolved CpfC, PbgS, UroD, and CgoX, providing mutants with improved activity. However, HmbS, UroS, and ChdC did not show improved growth, indicating the need for optimization (e.g., regulating gene expression, co-expressing multiple genes).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Conclude the study.

evidence.quote_or_span: the growth-coupled selection system based on the detoxification of MPs was an effective strategy for directed evolution of enzymes involved in heme biosynthesis or other tetrapyrrole compounds.

another three enzymes (HmbS, UroS, ChdC) have not exhibited improved growth tendencies, the selection platform could be optimized by regulating the intensity of gene expression and co-expressing multiple genes to improve the universality.

validation_methods: Reconstruction of CpfC mutants to rule out background mutations
Kinetic characterization of mutants using fluorescence assay
success_verification.final_conclusion: The growth-coupled selection platform based on ZnPPIX detoxification effectively evolved four out of seven heme biosynthetic enzymes, yielding mutants with significantly improved growth and catalytic activity. Three enzymes (HmbS, UroS, ChdC) were not evolvable under current conditions, highlighting a need for platform optimization.
limitations: Three enzymes (HmbS, UroS, ChdC) did not show improved growth, indicating the selection platform needs optimization for universality.

source.title: Directed evolution of phosphite dehydrogenase to cycle noncanonical redox cofactors via universal growth selection platform

initial_request.research_problem: Engineering enzymes to recycle noncanonical redox cofactors like NMN+ for orthogonal biocatalysis, hindered by lack of high-throughput selection methods.
initial_request.known_context: Noncanonical cofactors enable orthogonal redox reactions but enzymes lack efficient activity. Growth-coupled selection could enable directed evolution if a suitable selection strain is engineered.
constraints: Must use SHuffle strain (Δgor ΔtrxB) for oxidative stress selection
Must engineer Gor to be NMNH-specific to couple growth to NMN+ reduction
input_data: Crystal structures of Gor and PTDH
Known mutations R198M-R204L in Gor that abolish NADPH activity
quantifiable_goals: Increase catalytic efficiency (kcat/KM) of PTDH for NMN+
Achieve >99% conversion in biocatalytic cycling with NMN+

evolution_trajectory.state_before: No high-throughput selection method for NMN+-reducing enzymes.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can E. coli SHuffle strain be engineered to require NMNH-dependent Gor for survival under oxidative stress?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: E. coli SHuffle strain (Δgor ΔtrxB) requires functional Gor to survive oxidative stress from diamide, enabling growth-coupled selection for NMN+-reducing enzymes if Gor is engineered to use NMNH.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Proceed to engineer Gor for NMNH specificity.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Hypothesis formulated based on known SHuffle strain properties.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need to design mutations in Gor to accept NMNH.

evidence.quote_or_span: our hypothesis is that it cannot combat the oxidative stress effectively, and requires a functional Gor to survive exposure to oxidants such as diamide

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Gor naturally uses NADPH; NMNH lacks AMP moiety.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to engineer Gor to recognize NMNH instead of NADPH?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Ribose and phosphate groups of NMN(H) can accept new polar interactions to replace missing AMP interactions in Gor.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Target residues near NMNH phosphate for mutagenesis.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Hypothesis that new interactions can compensate for lost AMP contacts.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Design specific mutations at G174, G176, I178.

evidence.quote_or_span: We hypothesized that the ribose and phosphate groups of NMN(H) have the potential to accept new polar interactions to supplant the missing interactions from the AMP recognition handle.
Three residue sites G174, G176, and I178 were targeted based on their proximity to the ribose-phosphate group of NMN(H)

evolution_trajectory.state_before: No Gor mutants with NMNH activity.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which residues to mutate to create NMNH interactions?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Mutations at G174, G176, I178 near NMNH phosphate will enhance NMNH binding.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Create and test single mutants at these positions.

tool_or_method.name: Rational design based on crystal structure

evolution_trajectory.observation: I178T predicted to form favorable interaction with NMNH phosphate.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Test I178T experimentally.

evidence.quote_or_span: Three residue sites G174, G176, and I178 were targeted based on their proximity to the ribose-phosphate group of NMN(H)
Combining the R198M-R204L double mutations on top of Ec Gor I178T caused only a modest decrease in NMNH-dependent activity

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Wild-type Gor has negligible NMNH activity.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Does I178T improve NMNH-dependent activity?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: I178T will enhance NMNH-dependent activity.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Measure NMNH-dependent activity of I178T mutant.
evolution_trajectory.observation: I178T showed 4-fold enhanced NMNH-dependent activity compared to wild-type Gor.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Combine I178T with NADPH-abolishing mutations to create NMNH-specific Gor.

evidence.quote_or_span: I178T had 4-fold enhanced NMNH-dependent activity compared to the wild type
Indeed, I178T had 4-fold enhanced NMNH-dependent activity compared to the wild type.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: I178T has improved NMNH activity but still uses NADPH.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to eliminate NADPH activity while retaining NMNH activity?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Combining I178T with R198M-R204L will drastically reduce NADPH activity while maintaining NMNH activity.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Create triple mutant I178T-R198M-R204L (Gor Ortho).
evolution_trajectory.observation: Gor Ortho had ~15,000-fold reduction in NADPH catalytic efficiency and ~9,000-fold reduction vs I178T, with modest decrease in NMNH activity.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate growth selection platform using Gor Ortho.

evidence.quote_or_span: Combining the R198M-R204L double mutations on top of Ec Gor I178T caused only a modest decrease in NMNH-dependent activity

Combining the R198M-R204L double mutations on top of Ec Gor I178T caused only a modest decrease in NMNH-dependent activity (Fig.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Wild-type TS-PTDH has low NMN+ activity.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can rational design improve PTDH activity toward NMN+?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Mutations near the adenine pocket will enhance NMN+ binding.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Test A155N and T101K-A155G variants.
evolution_trajectory.observation: A155N showed ~3-fold increase in specific activity and catalytic efficiency for NMN+; T101K-A155G showed ~2-fold increase.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Use A155N as template for directed evolution.

evidence.quote_or_span: Through specific activity assay we identified two variants of interest displaying enhanced NMN $^{+}$ -dependent activity, A155N and T101K-A155G.
A155N showed \~3-fold increase and T101K-A155G showed a \~2-fold increase compared to the wild type.
4$ nmol min $^{-1}$ mg $^{-1}$ , while T101K-A155G showed roughly twofold, and A155N around threefold increase (Fig.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: A155N identified as improved variant.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which additional residues to target for further improvement?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Residues E175, A176, L208 in the adenine cleft can be mutated to enhance NMN+ binding.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Construct site-saturation mutagenesis library on A155N targeting E175, A176, L208.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Library constructed with 4.7×10^7 transformants.

evidence.quote_or_span: The PTDH combinatorial site-saturation mutagenesis library pLZ316 was constructed with degenerate codon-containing primers.
The library size of pLZ316 was counted as $4.7 \times 10^{7}$ transformants.
We next constructed a site-saturated mutagenesis library on top of TS-PTDH A155N targeting E175, A176, and L208.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Library constructed but not selected.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can growth selection enrich variants with improved NMN+ activity?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Growth under diamide stress with NMN+ will select for PTDH variants with higher NMN+ reduction activity.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform first round growth selection with 0.5 mM diamide, 5 g/L yeast extract, 5 mM NMN+.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 8 colonies obtained; LY-6 (A155N-E175Q-A176S) and LY-7 (A155N-E175W-A176G-L208V) showed 17-fold and 11-fold increased catalytic efficiency for NMN+ vs A155N.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Increase selection stringency to further improve activity.

evidence.quote_or_span: A total of 50 $\mu$ L pre-made SHuffle/pLZ311 or SHuffle/pLZ312 electrocompetent cells were transformed with 2 $\mu$ L TS-PTDH A155N-E175-A176-L208 NNK library DNA (pLZ316) via electroporation.
The independent transformants sampled was calculated as 2.4 × 10⁶ and 2.5 × 10⁶ colonies when plated on 5 and 1 g/L yeast extract, respectively.

Among them, TS-PTDH A155N-E175Q-A176S (LY-6) and TS-PTDH A155N-E175W-A176G-L208V (LY-7) exhibited the greatest improvement, with \~17-fold and \~11-fold increased catalytic efficiency ( $k_{cat}/K_{M}$ ) for NMN $^{+}$ compared to the parent enzyme TS-PTDH A155N, respectively (Table 1).
From the 8 colonies, four PTDH variants were isolated that showed enhanced NMN $^{+}$ -dependent activity compared to the template TS-PTDH A155N (data not shown).

evolution_trajectory.state_before: LY-6 and LY-7 isolated with improved activity.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can further stringency yield even better variants?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Reducing yeast extract to 1 g/L will increase selection pressure for higher NMN+ activity.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform second round growth selection with 1 g/L yeast extract.
evolution_trajectory.observation: LY-13 (A155N-E175A-A176F) isolated with 110-fold improvement over TS-PTDH and 44-fold over A155N in catalytic efficiency (0.44 mM⁻¹ s⁻¹).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Characterize structural basis of improvements.

evidence.quote_or_span: Using the same growth selection process, we successfully isolated TS-PTDH A155N-E175A-A176F (LY-13) which has a catalytic efficiency for $NMN^{+}$ \~110-fold and \~44-fold higher than that of TS-PTDH and TS-PTDH A155N, respectively, reaching $0.

Using the same growth selection process, we successfully isolated TS-PTDH A155N-E175A-A176F (LY-13) which has a catalytic efficiency for $NMN^{+}$ \~110-fold and \~44-fold higher than that of TS-PTDH and TS-PTDH A155N, respectively, reaching $0.44\ mM^{-1}\ s^{-1}$ (Table 1).

Among them, TS-PTDH A155N-E175Q-A176S (LY-6) and TS-PTDH A155N-E175W-A176G-L208V (LY-7) exhibited the greatest improvement, with \~17-fold and \~11-fold increased catalytic efficiency ( $k_{cat}/K_{M}$ ) for NMN $^{+}$ compared to the parent enzyme TS-PTDH A155N, respectively (Table 1).
In particular, LY-6 utilized a hydrogen bond network to brace the AMP binding pocket, which is in stark contrast with the hydrophobic packing mode utilized by LY-7 and LY-13 (Fig.

When BNA $^{+}$ was reduced with wild type TS-PTDH, LY-7, and LY-13, a cofactor dose-dependent response in biotransformation efficiency was observed, and at 20 mM BNA $^{+}$ supplementation, LY-7 and LY-13 showed improved utilization of the BNA $^{+}$ relative to the wild type protein (Fig.
LY-13 (A155N-E175A-A176F) displays similar hydrophobic packing in the adenine cleft as LY-7 with E175A shrinking and A176F settling against L208 and P209 in the adenine cleft.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: LY-6, LY-7, LY-13 isolated with unknown structural mechanisms.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How do mutations in evolved variants enhance NMN+ activity?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Mutations recapitulate AMP binding via hydrophobic packing or hydrogen bond networks, inducing closed conformation.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform structural modeling of LY-6, LY-7, LY-13.
evolution_trajectory.observation: LY-6 uses hydrogen bond network; LY-7 and LY-13 use hydrophobic packing to bridge adenine cleft, mimicking AMP interactions.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Apply best variant in biocatalysis.

evidence.quote_or_span: In LY-6 (A155N-E175Q-A176S), E175Q maintains similar steric properties of the original glutamate, but the change from the carboxylic acid to amide enables E175Q to act as a hydrogen bond donor that bridges separate loops in the adenine binding cleft for improved stability.
LY-7 (A155N-E175W-A176G-L208V) is characterized by dense hydrophobic packing in the adenine cleft.
LY-13 (A155N-E175A-A176F) displays similar hydrophobic packing in the adenine cleft as LY-7 with E175A shrinking and A176F settling against L208 and P209 in the adenine cleft.

Both LY-7 (A155N-E175W-A176G-L208V) and LY-13 (A155N-E175A-A176F) display tight hydrophobic packing that bridges the gap between the loop emerging from the second Rossman beta strand and the loop below the bound cofactor.

source.title: Optimizing the downstream MVA pathway using a combination optimization strategy to increase lycopene yield in Escherichia coli

initial_request.research_problem: Low lycopene yield in E. coli due to metabolic imbalance in the downstream MVA pathway (MVK, PMK, MVD, IDI). Previous in vitro optimization improved conversion but not directly applicable in vivo. Need for in vivo fine-tuning to balance upstream and downstream modules and reduce intermediate accumulation.
initial_request.target_object: Lycopene synthesis pathway in E. coli
initial_request.known_context: Simple overexpression of enzymes in multienzyme pathways can cause imbalance, growth inhibition, or poor yield due to intermediate cytotoxicity. Previous in vitro optimization of MVA enzyme ratios improved conversion but is not directly applicable in vivo due to cellular complexity.
constraints: Must use glucose as carbon source
Must maintain cell growth while increasing lycopene production
input_data: Plasmid pCLES containing upstream MVA pathway
Plasmid pTrc-lyc containing lycopene pathway genes (CrtE, CrtB, CrtI)
quantifiable_goals: Increase lycopene yield over reference strains
Achieve balanced expression of MVK, PMK, MVD, IDI

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Simple overexpression of MVA pathway enzymes risks imbalance and intermediate cytotoxicity, and in vitro optimization does not translate to in vivo.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to fine-tune expression of MVK, PMK, MVD, IDI in vivo to balance the pathway and increase lycopene yield.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Combinatorial RBS library optimization of the downstream MVA module across different plasmid backbones will identify expression levels that balance the pathway and improve lycopene production.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Divide lycopene pathway into three modules; target downstream MVA module (MPMI) for RBS library optimization using three plasmid backbones with different copy numbers and promoters.

modules: MPMI (midstream MVA: MVK, PMK, MVD, IDI)

evolution_trajectory.observation: Design established three libraries (HM, MM, LM) to screen optimal expression levels.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need to construct RBS libraries with defined TIR for the four genes.

evidence.quote_or_span: the simple overexpression of one or more enzymes risks causing an imbalance between enzymes and upstream and downstream pathway modules, resulting in growth inhibition or poor yield owing to the cytotoxicity of accumulated intermediates
the results of in vitro are not appropriate to intracellular
the lycopene synthesis pathway was divided into three modules: module ESE and module MPMI and module EBI

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Three plasmid backbones selected for RBS library construction.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need specific RBS sequences with defined translation initiation rates (TIR) for MVK, PMK, MVD, IDI.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: RBS sequences with a range of TIR values will create diverse expression levels for screening.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Design RBS sequences with TIR values ranging from ~1000 to ~63000 for each gene.
evolution_trajectory.observation: RBS sequences with defined TIR were designed for library construction.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Construct the three RBS libraries using OLMA method.

evidence.quote_or_span: Table 2 RBS sequences for RBS libraries with defined TIR

evolution_trajectory.state_before: RBS sequences designed.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Whether the libraries can be successfully assembled and produce diverse lycopene phenotypes.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: OLMA assembly will generate functional libraries with varying lycopene production.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Construct pHM, pMM, pLM libraries using OLMA and co-transform with upstream and lycopene modules into E. coli BL21(DE3).
evolution_trajectory.observation: Red colonies with varying intensity appeared, indicating diverse lycopene production. PCR of ten random red colonies showed 100% positive rate, confirming successful assembly.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Proceed to high-throughput screening of the libraries.

evidence.quote_or_span: Three libraries of 10,000 combinations (HM, MM, and LM) were constructed using OLMA method

Two microliter of the resultant reaction solution was transformed into the E. coli BL21(DE3) with the plasmid pCLES [38] of the upstream MVA pathway and the plasmid pTrc-lyc of the lycopene pathway to assemble the complete metabolic pathway of lycopene using glucose as a carbon source.
PCR analysis was performed on ten randomly selected red colonies of various intensities, and the gel electrophoresis revealed a 100% positive rate
Ten colonies were randomly picked and analyzed by colony PCR to determine ratio of successfully assembled pHM-library, pMM-library, and pLM-library.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Libraries constructed and validated.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which strains produce the highest lycopene and what expression patterns are beneficial.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: High-throughput screening will identify strains with improved lycopene production, and lower midstream expression may be favorable.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Visually select 500 colonies per library, culture in 96-deep-well plates with M9 minimal medium, and measure lycopene after 48h.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Lycopene production varied widely: pHM-library 30-fold, pMM-library 14-fold, pLM-library 40-fold. More than 25% of strains outperformed controls. LM library (low copy, medium promoter) gave highest lycopene accumulation (ABS472/OD600=0.9).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Analyze correlation between RBS strengths and lycopene production.

evidence.quote_or_span: 500 colonies from the pHM-library, 500 colonies from the pMM-library, and 500 colonies from the pLM-library, all of which were classified according to varying degrees of redness, were chosen using the visual selection method
room temperature for 48 h to accumulate lycopene for screening.

According to the color of colonies, 500 individual colonies were randomly selected from each library and inoculated into a sterile 96-deep-plate containing 1 ML M9 minimal medium per well with appropriate antibiotic and 0.1 mmol IPTG to induce the MVA pathway.
More than a quarter of the strains screened from the three libraries with various plasmid backbones were capable of producing more lycopene than the control strain.

2A–C, the relative lycopene production of the selected strains in 96-deep-well showed a 14\~40-fold range (pHM-library 30-fold, pMM-library 14-fold, and pLM-library 40-fold), which indicated the combinatorial RBS library yield a large number of functional constructs with different lycopene production level.
The highest one showed 2.5 times higher than MM-CT in the pMM-library and 14 times stronger than the lowest sample with $ABS_{472}/OD_{600}$ of \`0.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Screening data collected.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which specific genes drive lycopene improvement and whether a clear ratio relationship exists.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: RBS strengths of IDI and MVK may correlate with lycopene production, but a simple proportional relationship may not exist.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform correlation analysis between RBS strengths and lycopene accumulation.
evolution_trajectory.observation: RBS_IDI showed strong positive correlation with lycopene; RBS_MVK had modest positive correlation; RBS_PMK and RBS_MVD showed no clear correlation. No clear proportional relationship among the four genes was found. Small sample size (500 out of 10,000) limited detection of associations.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Despite unclear ratio, top strains should be validated in shake flasks.

evidence.quote_or_span: there was no clear proportion relationship involving these four genes, indicating that optimizing the MVA pathway by entirely rational design is challenging
the small sample size resulted in the absence of a clear association between the RBS strengths and lycopene accumulation

Given that there were 10,000 possible combinations, but only 500 colonies were selected for analysis, the small sample size resulted in the absence of a clear association between the RBS strengths and lycopene accumulation.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Top strains identified from screening.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Whether high-throughput results translate to shake-flask fermentation.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Top strains will maintain improved lycopene production in shake flasks, but some may underperform due to screening/fermentation inconsistency.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Validate top strains from each library in shake flasks with M9 medium.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Best yields: pHM25 120 mg/g DCW, MM35 143.6 mg/g DCW, LM22 219.7 mg/g DCW. Replacing native RBSs with library RBSs increased lycopene yield by over 120% in all three libraries. However, 20-30% of selected strains performed worse than controls, possibly due to inconsistency between high-throughput screening and fermentation conditions.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Analyze overall effectiveness of the strategy.

evidence.quote_or_span: the sample pHM25 with the highest yield of 120 mg/g DCW in pHM-library was 250% stronger than the control strain HM-CT (48.3 mg/g DCW)
the lycopene yield of sample MM35 (143.6 mg/g DCW) in MM-library and LM22 (219.7 mg/g DCW) in LM-Library increased by 140% and 120% compared to the reference strain
replacing RBSs with native ones increased lycopene yield by over 120 percent in all three libraries without changing any other sequence in the operon

While 70%-80% of the selected samples accumulated more lycopene than the control strain containing natural RBS in shake flask fermentation, 20% \~ 30% samples in each library perform worse than the control strains, which could be the inconsistency between high-throughput

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Shake-flask validation completed.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Overall effectiveness and limitations of the RBS library approach.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Coordinating expression of all midstream module genes via RBS library is effective, but no clear gene ratio relationship exists.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Summarize findings: downregulation of midstream module enhances flux, but excessive flux harms accumulation. Combination optimization achieved yields from 20.3 to 219.7 mg/g DCW, improving lycopene accumulation by 120-250% over reference strains.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Coordinating expression of all midstream module genes via RBS library was effective, improving lycopene accumulation by 120-250% over reference strains. No clear proportion relationship between the four genes and lycopene metabolic flux was found, but combination optimization achieved yields from 20.3 to 219.7 mg/g DCW.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Conclude the study.

evidence.quote_or_span: coordinating the expression of all the genes of the midstream module was an effective strategy for optimizing the lycopene synthesis pathway
there was no clear proportion relationship between these four genes and lycopene metabolic flux
there was a difference in lycopene output between the ten best-ranked samples from each of the three libraries for shaker flask fermentation, ranging from 20.3 to 219.7 mg/g DCW

The four genes of the midstream module were fine-tuned based on these foundations by the construction of the RBS library, which further optimized the lycopene metabolic pathway and improved lycopene accumulation by 120%\~250% over the reference strains.
replacing RBSs with native ones increased lycopene yield by over 120 percent in all three libraries without changing any other sequence in the operon
Figure 4 shows that replacing RBSs with native ones increased lycopene yield by over 120 percent in all three libraries without changing any other sequence in the operon.

success_verification.final_conclusion: Combinatorial RBS library optimization of the downstream MVA module across three plasmid backbones successfully improved lycopene yield. The best strain LM22 achieved 219.7 mg/g DCW, representing a 120% increase over the reference strain. Coordinating expression of all midstream module genes was effective, improving lycopene accumulation by 120-250% over reference strains. However, no clear proportional relationship among the four genes was found, and 20-30% of selected strains underperformed in shake flasks, indicating screening/fermentation inconsistency.
limitations: No clear proportion relationship between MVK, PMK, MVD, IDI and lycopene flux
Small sample size (500 out of 10,000) limited detection of associations
20-30% of selected strains performed worse than controls in shake flasks
Yield lower than previously reported 448 mg/g DCW

risk_notes: All coverage requirements met with direct evidence quotes.

source.title: Protein design and variant prediction using autoregressive generative models

initial_request.research_problem: Current computational methods rely on multiple sequence alignments, which are inadequate for antibodies, disordered proteins, and indels.
initial_request.known_context: State-of-art computational methods rely on models that leverage evolutionary information but are inadequate for important applications where multiple sequence alignments are not robust.
constraints: No reliance on multiple sequence alignments
Must handle variable length CDRs, insertions, deletions
input_data: Deep mutational scan data (40 experiments, 690,257 sequences)
quantifiable_goals: Match or exceed alignment-based methods in mutation effect prediction
Design a nanobody library with higher expression and lower fraction of poorly expressed variants than a synthetic library

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Alignment-based methods fail for antibodies, disordered proteins, and indels.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: No alignment-free method exists that can predict mutation effects and design functional sequences for these challenging cases.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: An autoregressive generative model adapted from NLP can predict and design functional sequences without alignments.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Develop a deep generative model based on autoregressive factorization of sequence probability.
evolution_trajectory.observation: The model can learn from natural sequences to capture higher-order constraints.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Proceed to design the model architecture.

evidence.quote_or_span: State-of-art computational methods rely on models that leverage evolutionary information but are inadequate for important applications where multiple sequence alignments are not robust.
We introduce a deep generative model adapted from natural language processing for prediction and design of diverse functional sequences without the need for alignments.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Hypothesis formulated; need to implement model.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to parameterize the autoregressive likelihood to capture long-range dependencies in protein sequences.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Dilated convolutional neural networks can aggregate long-range dependencies without gradient issues.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Use dilated CNNs instead of LSTM/GRU.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Dilated CNNs avoid exploding gradients and capture long-range dependencies.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate model on mutation effect prediction.

evidence.quote_or_span: Instead, we parameterize this process with dilated convolutional neural networks (Supplementary Fig. 1), which are feed-forward deep neural networks that aggregate long-range dependencies in sequences over an exponentially large receptive field

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Model architecture chosen; need to test predictive performance.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Whether the alignment-free model can match alignment-based methods on mutation effect prediction.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: The autoregressive model can predict mutation effects as accurately as DeepSequence.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Evaluate on 40 deep mutational scan datasets.
evolution_trajectory.observation: The model matches state-of-the-art alignment-based method DeepSequence.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Further validate on nanobody thermostability and indel fitness.

evidence.quote_or_span: We first test our method on the problem of prediction of mutation effects, which are typically analyzed using alignment-based statistical methods.
Without using alignments, the autoregressive model matches alignment-dependent state-of-art missense mutation effect prediction (DeepSequence) for 40 different deep mutational scan experiments.
we validated our model first on deep mutational scan data, with on-par performance with the best currently available model

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Model validated on missense mutations; need to test on antibody-related tasks.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can the model predict nanobody thermostability better than HMMs?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: The autoregressive model can learn from natural nanobody repertoires to predict thermostability.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Train on ~1.2M llama nanobody sequences and test on thermostability data.
evolution_trajectory.observation: The model predicts thermostability with greater accuracy than HMMs.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Test on indel fitness prediction.

evidence.quote_or_span: The autoregressive model (blue) can learn from natural sequence repertoires of llama nanobodies to predict the thermostability of llama nanobody sequences with variation in the framework and complementarity determining regions with greater accuracy than HMMs (orange).

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Model works for missense and stability; need to test on indels.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can the model predict fitness effects of insertions and deletions?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: The autoregressive model can handle variable-length mutations.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Evaluate on IGP dehydratase dataset with indels and missense mutations.
evolution_trajectory.observation: AUC = 0.90, outperforming HMM (AUC 0.88).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Proceed to sequence generation for nanobody library design.

evidence.quote_or_span: Accurate prediction of binary fitness for IGP dehydratase with a range of insertions, deletions, and missense mutations of the autoregressive model (blue), higher than HMM (orange).

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Model validated; ready to design nanobody library.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to generate a diverse set of functional CDR3 sequences?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Ancestral sampling from the trained model can produce probable CDR3 sequences.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Generate 33M CDR3 sequences by ancestral sampling.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 33,047,639 CDR3 sequences generated.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Filter and cluster sequences to select a diverse library.

evidence.quote_or_span: we generated 33,047,639 CDR3 sequences by ancestral sampling

evolution_trajectory.state_before: 33M sequences generated; need to remove undesirable ones.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to filter out duplicates, training set matches, and sequences with problematic motifs?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Applying filters for glycosylation sites, deamination motifs, and sulfur-containing amino acids will improve library quality.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Remove sequences with NxS/NxT, NG, C, M; also remove duplicates and training set matches.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 3,690,554 sequences remain after filtering.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Cluster sequences to select a diverse subset.

evidence.quote_or_span: CDR3 sequences were also removed if they contained glycosylation (NxS and NxT) sites, asparagine deamination (NG) motifs, or sulfur-containing amino acids (cysteine and methionine), resulting in 3,690,554 sequences.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: 3.7M filtered sequences; need to select a manageable diverse set.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to cluster sequences to maximize diversity within memory constraints?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: BIRCH clustering with chosen k-mer size and diameter threshold can select diverse sequences.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Use BIRCH clustering with parameters chosen under 70 GB memory constraint.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 185,836 diverse CDR3 sequences selected for the designed library.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Synthesize library and test expression in yeast.

evidence.quote_or_span: From these, we select 185,836 highly diverse CDR3 sequences for inclusion in our designed library.
K-mer size and BIRCH diameter threshold were chosen to maximize the number of clusters within a memory constraint of 70 GB.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Designed library of 185,836 sequences; need to compare with synthetic library.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Will the designed library show improved expression and lower fraction of poorly expressed nanobodies?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: The designed library will have higher mean expression and fewer poorly expressed variants.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Synthesize oligonucleotide library, clone into yeast display vector, and measure expression via flow cytometry.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Designed library: mean expression 165,000 AU, poorly expressed fraction 0.16; Synthetic: 90,000 AU, 0.26.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Test functionality by selecting binders against HSA.

evidence.quote_or_span: Upon induction, the designed nanobody library contained a 1.5 times higher proportion of cells expressing and displaying nanobodies on their cell surface than the synthetic nanobody library
the designed library had nearly half the fraction of poorly expressed nanobodies (cells with fluorescence below 10,000 AU) as compared to the synthetic library
the designed library has almost double the mean expression level (166,193 AU compared to 92,183 AU)
| Designed library | 165000 | 0.16 | 0.
| Synthetic library | 90000 | 0.26 | 0.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Designed library shows improved expression; need to confirm functional binding.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Does the designed library contain nanobodies that can bind a target antigen?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: The library contains functional binders; one can be isolated against HSA.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform FACS against HSA, isolate clones, and characterize binding affinity.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Isolated nanobody Nb174684 binds HSA with Kd 9.8 μM.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Acknowledge limitations and suggest future improvements.

evidence.quote_or_span: we selected nanobody sequences that bound to human serum albumin (HSA) using fluorescence-activated cell sorting (FACS)
Nanobody Nb174684 was isolated from a screen of 36 clones for binding to HSA using a flow cytometer and then sequenced.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Functional binder obtained; but poly-reactivity remains a challenge.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to reduce poly-reactivity in designed libraries?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Training on mature B cell repertoires may improve library design by reducing poly-reactivity.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Note limitation and suggest future direction.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Poly-reactivity is a challenge; training on mature B cell repertoires may help.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Conclude study.

evidence.quote_or_span: A challenge of using synthetic libraries is the poly-reactivity of many sequences
training a model on sequences from mature or memory B cell repertoires may provide information on how to improve library design

validation_methods: Mutation effect prediction on 40 deep mutational scans
success_verification.final_conclusion: The autoregressive model successfully predicts mutation effects and designs a nanobody library with improved expression and functional binders, though poly-reactivity remains a challenge.
limitations: Poly-reactivity of designed sequences
Memory constraint limited clustering parameters

source.title: Drug design and repurposing with DockThor-VS web server focusing on SARS-CoV-2 therapeutic targets and their non-synonym variants

initial_request.research_problem: The COVID-19 pandemic created an urgent need for effective treatments. Virtual screening success depends on accurate target structures, receptor flexibility, and genetic variations. The study aims to develop DockThor-VS, a web server providing curated 3D structures of SARS-CoV-2 therapeutic targets (wild type and non-synonymous variants) and drug libraries, to facilitate repurposing virtual screening and assess the impact of mutations on drug binding.
initial_request.target_object: SARS-CoV-2 therapeutic targets (Nsp3 PLpro, Nsp5 Mpro, Nsp12 RdRp, Nsp15 NendoU, N protein, Spike) and their non-synonymous variants
initial_request.known_context: Virtual screening success depends on target structure preparation, compound library preparation, receptor flexibility, and genomic variations. Non-specialist researchers often struggle with these factors.
constraints: Use only FDA-approved drugs from e-Drug3D library
Docking results must not be used for clinical guidance
input_data: Curated 3D structures of SARS-CoV-2 targets (wild type and selected mutations)
quantifiable_goals: Identify promising drugs under clinical trials against COVID-19
Assess impact of non-synonymous variations on drug binding

evolution_trajectory.state_before: No dedicated web server providing curated SARS-CoV-2 target structures and drug libraries for virtual screening, incorporating receptor flexibility and genetic variations.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Virtual screening success depends on factors like target structure preparation, compound library preparation, receptor flexibility, and genomic variations, which are challenging for non-specialists.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A web server providing curated structures of SARS-CoV-2 therapeutic targets (wild type and non-synonymous variants) and drug libraries will facilitate repurposing virtual screening and help assess the impact of mutations on drug binding.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Develop DockThor-VS web server.
evolution_trajectory.observation: The success of virtual screening experiments depends on factors that are often not trivial to be addressed by non-specialist researchers. Possible genomic variations in the active/binding site region of molecular targets can drastically affect the binding mode and affinity of ligands.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: The identified need motivates the design of the DockThor-VS web server.

evidence.quote_or_span: the success of virtual screening experiments depends on factors that are often not trivial to be addressed by non-specialist researchers
possible genomic variations in the active/binding site region of molecular targets can drastically affect the binding mode and affinity of ligands

evolution_trajectory.state_before: No web server with curated SARS-CoV-2 target structures and drug libraries.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to provide ready-for-docking 3D structures for wild type and selected mutations of key SARS-CoV-2 targets.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Providing curated structures of Nsp3 PLpro, Nsp5 Mpro, Nsp12 RdRp, Nsp15 NendoU, N protein, and Spike will enable effective virtual screening.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Develop DockThor-VS web server with curated structures for six SARS-CoV-2 targets and their non-synonymous variants.
evolution_trajectory.observation: DockThor-VS provides ready-for-docking 3D structures for wild type and selected mutations for Nsp3 (papain-like, PLpro domain), Nsp5 (Mpro, 3CLpro), Nsp12 (RdRp), Nsp15 (NendoU), N protein, and Spike.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: To account for receptor flexibility, multiple conformations of each target need to be selected.

evidence.quote_or_span: we present the developments implemented in the DockThor-VS web server to provide a virtual screening (VS) platform with curated structures of potential therapeutic targets from SARS-CoV-2 incorporating genetic information regarding relevant non-synonymous variations
DockThor-VS provides ready-for-docking 3D structures for wild type and selected mutations for Nsp3 (papain-like, PLpro domain), Nsp5 (Mpro, 3CLpro), Nsp12 (RdRp), Nsp15 (NendoU), N protein, and Spike

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Single static structures of targets may not capture receptor flexibility.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Receptor flexibility can significantly affect ligand binding; using a single conformation may lead to false negatives.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Using an ensemble of structures representing different relevant conformations will better account for receptor flexibility.
evolution_trajectory.decision: For each protein target, select a set of structures representing different conformations (e.g., with varying ligand types, side chain conformations).

parameters.criteria: different relevant conformations complexed with varying types of ligand

evolution_trajectory.observation: For each protein target, we chose a set of structures representing different relevant conformations complexed with varying types of ligand (e.g., ligands covalently bound or not, side chains conformations closing or opening the binding site), thus providing an ensemble of representative structures of the flexibility of the receptor.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: The importance of multiple conformations is reinforced by analysis of Mpro structures.

evidence.quote_or_span: For each protein target, we chose a set of structures representing different relevant conformations complexed with varying types of ligand (e.g., ligands covalently bound or not, side chains conformations closing or opening the binding site), thus providing an ensemble of representative structures of the flexibility of the receptor.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Multiple Mpro structures available (e.g., 6LU7, 6W63).
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to confirm that different conformations significantly affect the binding site.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Superposition of Mpro structures will reveal significant conformational changes in key binding site residues.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Superpose Mpro structures and analyze conformational changes.
evolution_trajectory.observation: The structures' superposition highlights some significant conformational changes within the ligand-binding site, mainly the residues Met49, Asn142 and Gln189. That reinforces the importance of considering multiple protein conformations in virtual screening experiments to accommodate distinct compounds.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: The observed flexibility supports the need for multiple conformations and careful preparation of protonation states for Mpro.

evidence.quote_or_span: The structures' superposition highlights some significant conformational changes within the ligand-binding site, mainly the residues Met49, Asn142 and Gln189. That reinforces the importance of considering multiple protein conformations in virtual screening experiments to accommodate distinct compounds

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Mpro structures need proper protonation state assignment.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Incorrect protonation states can affect docking accuracy.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Manually adjusting protonation states of key residues (His41, Cys145, His163, His164) based on literature will improve docking accuracy.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Manually adjust protonation states: neutral His41 at ND1, catalytic Cys145 protonated, neutral His163 at NE2, neutral His164 at NE2.
evolution_trajectory.observation: In the preparation process, the protonation states and flips of key residues were manually adjusted to provide the Mpro structures with neutral His41 at ND1, the catalytic Cys145 protonated (i.e., neutral), neutral His163 at NE2 and neutral His164 at NE2.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Similar careful preparation is needed for other targets, such as Nsp15.

evidence.quote_or_span: In the preparation process, the protonation states and flips of key residues were manually adjusted to provide the Mpro structures with neutral His41 at ND1, the catalytic Cys145 protonated (i.e., neutral), neutral His163 at NE2 and neutral His164 at NE2.

evolution_trajectory.observation: The prepared Nsp15 structure is based on the conformation of the protein complexed with tipiracil (PDB code 6WXC) prepared considering the pH of 6.2 and consists of His235 and His250 neutral at NE2 and ND1, respectively, and Lys290 positively charged.
