validation_methods: Experimental testing of top predicted mutants
success_verification.final_conclusion: innov'SAR with FFT successfully predicted improved enantioselective variants of ANEH, with experimental E-values up to 253, surpassing previous best LW202 (E=115). The method captured epistasis and required only 7% of the fitness landscape for training.
limitations: Application limited to recombining mutations present in the training set; predicting non-learned mutations remains future work.

source.title: Droplet-based microfluidic high-throughput screening of heterologous enzymes secreted by the yeast Yarrowia lipolytica

initial_request.research_problem: Lack of rapid and generalized high-throughput screening (HTS) systems for secreted enzymes in directed evolution, due to reliance on cytoplasmic expression in E. coli requiring lysis.
initial_request.target_object: Secreted heterologous enzymes (xylanase, cellobiohydrolase, endoglucanase, protease) from Aspergillus niger expressed in Yarrowia lipolytica
initial_request.known_context: Most droplet-based microfluidic systems use cytoplasmic expression in E. coli, requiring lysis or membrane transport for enzyme assay.
constraints: Enzymes must be secreted
Compatible fluorescent assays required
quantifiable_goals: Detect enzyme activity in droplets with low variability
Screen mutant libraries for thermostable variants

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Lack of rapid and generalized HTS systems is a major bottleneck for directed evolution of biocatalysts. Most droplet-based microfluidic systems use cytoplasmic expression in E. coli, requiring lysis.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need for an extracellular expression system to avoid lysis step in droplet-based microfluidic HTS.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Y. lipolytica's secretion abilities can be exploited to build an extracellular expression system for droplet-based microfluidic HTS, avoiding lysis.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Use Y. lipolytica as host for secreted enzyme expression.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Y. lipolytica is known for excellent secretion abilities.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Design expression constructs for target enzymes.

evidence.quote_or_span: The lack of rapid and generalized high-throughput screening (HTS) systems has proven to be a major bottleneck when it comes to creating biocatalysts via directed evolution and protein engineering approaches.
most of the enzymes screened to date are expressed within the cytoplasm of Escherichia coli cells, which means that a lysis step must take place inside the droplets for enzyme activity to be assayed.
we take advantage of the excellent secretion abilities of the yeast Yarrowia lipolytica to describe a highly efficient expression system particularly suitable for the droplet-based microfluidic HTS.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Hypothesis to use Y. lipolytica secretion for HTS.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to express and secrete target enzymes in Y. lipolytica.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Codon-optimized A. niger genes integrated at zeta docking platform will yield high secretion.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Design expression cassettes for five A. niger hydrolytic genes (xlnb, xlnc, cbha, egla, pep1) in Y. lipolytica JMY2566 with zeta docking platform.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Five genes were codon-optimized and overexpressed in Y. lipolytica strain JMY2566 with a zeta docking platform for consistent integration.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Test expression and secretion.

evidence.quote_or_span: The five genes were codon optimized and overexpressed in Y. lipolytica strain JMY2566
This strain possesses a zeta docking platform that favors integration at that specific locus

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Expression constructs designed.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Will the enzymes be overproduced and secreted?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Enzymes will be secreted as major proteins in supernatant.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Grow transformants and analyze crude supernatant by SDS-PAGE.
evolution_trajectory.observation: All five enzymes were successfully overproduced and secreted as major proteins in crude supernatant without extensive optimization.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate enzyme activity.

evidence.quote_or_span: Each of the target enzymes was successfully overproduced and secreted as a major protein in the crude supernatant.
All the enzymes demonstrated high expression levels without requiring any extensive optimization of growth conditions.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Enzymes secreted.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Are the secreted enzymes active?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Secreted enzymes retain activity.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Assay activity with fluorogenic substrates.
evolution_trajectory.observation: All encoded proteins were found to be active after secretion. Endoglucanase A (egla) displayed low activity on cellobiose substrate, likely due to substrate specificity.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Proceed to droplet encapsulation despite egla limitation.

evidence.quote_or_span: All encoded proteins were found to be active after secretion.
JMY4343 (egla) displayed a low activity with this substrate, seemingly due to the endocellulase activity of endoglucanase A

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Active secreted enzymes confirmed.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can Y. lipolytica cells be encapsulated and grown in droplets?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Optimized encapsulation conditions will allow single-cell growth and secretion.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Encapsulate single Y. lipolytica cells in 20 pl droplets with continuous stirring; produce droplets at 2500–3000/s.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Droplets produced at 2500–3000/s; cells grew and secreted enzymes after 16 h incubation.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Detect enzyme activity in droplets.

evidence.quote_or_span: cell suspensions were also continuously stirred during the encapsulation process to prevent cell sedimentation
Droplets were produced at a rate of 2500 to 3000 per second via hydrodynamic flow focusing
Droplets were collected off chip in a glass vial and incubated for 16 h at 28 °C to allow cell growth and enzyme secretion.
the droplets contained dozens of cells after incubation, showing that Y. lipolytica was able to replicate in the droplets.

4 JMY4346 (xlnb) and JMY4510 (xlnc) strains activity in droplets 2D histograms showing the blue fluorescence (xylanase activity) and the red fluorescence (barcoding) of a binary 20 pl droplets emulsion containing JMY4346 (xlnb) and JMY4510 (xlnc) strains analyzed in the integrated screening device just after injection of the xylanase substrate (a) and after on-chip incubation (b).

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Cells grown in droplets.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can xylanase activity be detected with low variability?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Xylanase activity will show low CV due to uniform expression and controlled incubation.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Picoinject xylobiose-based coumarin substrate, incubate on-chip, and measure fluorescence; use sulforhodamine B for optical encoding.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Xylanase activity of two strains (xlnb and xlnc) was detected with low variability (CV 3%). Positive droplet percentage for xlnb was 2.7%.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Test other enzymes.

evidence.quote_or_span: Both strains showed remarkably low variability in their signal distributions.
coefficient of variation [CV] of 3%
For JMY4346 (xlnb) strain, a positive droplet population showing xylanase activity (2.7% of the droplets [theoretically 3% if $\lambda = 0.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Low variability observed for xylanase.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Why is variability low?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Low variability is due to Y. lipolytica expression system and delay line design.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Attribute low variability to constitutive promoter and uniform incubation time.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Low variability attributed to Y. lipolytica expression system and precise control of incubation time via delay line design.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Proceed to other enzymes.

evidence.quote_or_span: This narrow phenotypic distribution at the single cell level within a monoclonal population is highly desirable for enzymatic screening applications and is due to a combination of two factors.
the expression system developed in Y. lipolytica under a strong constitutive promoter is characterized by low variability in expression levels
the delay line structure design, made of constrictions and expansions, ensured that average droplet residence time, and hence incubation time, displayed little variance

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Xylanase detection successful.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can cellobiohydrolase and protease be detected?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Cellobiohydrolase and protease will show activity with comparable narrow signal distribution.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Assay cellobiohydrolase (cbha) and protease (pep1) with respective fluorogenic substrates.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Cellobiohydrolase and protease activities were detected with comparable narrow signal distribution, demonstrating efficient and reliable detection.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Proceed to library screening.

evidence.quote_or_span: Each strain showed hydrolytic activity against the respective substrate with comparable narrow signal distribution, while no activity was detected for the other strains.
These results demonstrate that our droplet-based microfluidic HTS system is an efficient and reliable method for detecting endoxylanase, cellobiohydrolase, and protease activity.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Xylanase detection robust.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to create mutant libraries for thermostability screening.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Error-prone PCR libraries of xlnc will yield variants with altered thermostability.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Generate error-prone PCR libraries of xlnc gene with low (library A, ~2 mutations/kb) and medium (library B, >4 mutations/kb) mutation frequencies.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Libraries A and B generated.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Screen libraries with heat shock.

evidence.quote_or_span: Libraries were generated from xlnc gene using error-prone PCR.
We built libraries of error-prone xlnc variants using a two-step PCR amplification according to [30].

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Libraries ready.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can thermostable variants be enriched by heat shock?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Heat shock at 90 °C for 30 s will inactivate less stable variants, enriching thermostable ones.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Encapsulate libraries, apply heat shock (90 °C, 30 s), and assay xylanase activity before and after.

tool_or_method.name: Integrated microfluidic screening device with heat shock module

evolution_trajectory.observation: Before heat shock, 27% of clones in library A and 9% in library B displayed xylanase activity; none had greater activity than wild-type. After heat shock, 1% (8 clones) in library A and 0.17% (1 clone) in library B had higher activities than wild-type.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate hits by microtiter plate and sequencing.

evidence.quote_or_span: Using the droplet-based microfluidic HTS system described above, both libraries were analyzed before and after a heat shock of 90 °C for 30 s
The JMY4510 (xlnc) wild-type, library A, and library B were encapsulated in 20 pl droplets as described above.
To generate the heat shock, the droplets were run through PTFE tubing (0.56 mm × 1.07 mm) immersed in a 90 °C water bath. The flow rate was set such that residence time within the tubing was 30 s.
The droplets were then collected in a glass vial and reloaded into the integrated screening device to assay post-heat-shock levels of xylanase activity.

After exposure to heat shock, 1% of the clones (8 clones) in library A were found to have higher activities than the wild-type xylanase (>mean wild-type activity + 3σ]), while this percentage was 0.17% (1 clone) for library B.

Before exposure to heat shock, 27% of the clones (216 clones) in library A and 9% of the clones (54 clones) in library B displayed xylanase activity. None of these clones were found to have greater activity than wild-type xylanase (>mean wild-type activity + 3σ).

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Putative thermostable hits identified.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Are the hits truly thermostable? What mutations do they carry?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Microtiter plate retest will confirm thermostability; sequencing will reveal mutations.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Isolate 480 clones from library A, retest in microtiter plates, and sequence six confirmed thermostable mutants.

tool_or_method.name: Microtiter plate assay and Sanger sequencing

evolution_trajectory.observation: Microtiter plate screening identified six thermostable mutants, similar to droplet-based HTS results. Sequencing revealed 1–6 point mutations (average 2.5) with higher density at N-terminus.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Conclude platform capabilities and limitations.

evidence.quote_or_span: To evaluate the sensitivity of our droplet-based microfluidic HTS system as compared to more classical, lower-throughput microtiter plate methods, 480 clones were isolated from library A and placed in 96-well plates.
The sequencing of the mutated gene in the six clones reveals that they contain from 1 to 6 point mutations (average of 2.5) at the amino acid level
Mutations are located all along the protein, with a higher density at the N-terminus

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Platform validated for xylanase thermostability screening.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: What are the limitations of the platform?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Platform is limited to secreted enzymes with compatible fluorescent assays; some enzymes show lower signal.
evolution_trajectory.decision: State limitations based on egla low activity and the need for compatible fluorescent substrates.
evolution_trajectory.observation: The platform is limited to secreted enzymes with compatible fluorescent assays; endoglucanase A had low activity on cellobiose substrate.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: None (final step).

evidence.quote_or_span: JMY4343 (egla) displayed a low activity with this substrate, seemingly due to the endocellulase activity of endoglucanase A
Each strain showed hydrolytic activity against the respective substrate with comparable narrow signal distribution, while no activity was detected for the other strains.
These results demonstrate that our droplet-based microfluidic HTS system is an efficient and reliable method for detecting endoxylanase, cellobiohydrolase, and protease activity.

validation_methods: Sanger sequencing of six thermostable mutants
metrics: Coefficient of variation (CV) for xylanase activity: 3%
Hit rate for thermostable variants: 6 out of 480 clones in library A
success_verification.final_conclusion: A droplet-based microfluidic HTS system using Y. lipolytica secretion was developed and validated for xylanase thermostability screening, with limitations for some enzymes.
limitations: Limited to secreted enzymes with compatible fluorescent assays
Endoglucanase A had low activity on cellobiose substrate

source.title: Engineering of mCherry variants with long Stokes shift, red-shifted fluorescence, and low cytotoxicity

initial_request.research_problem: Engineering mCherry variants with low cytotoxicity and altered spectral properties (long Stokes shift and red-shifted fluorescence) for live-cell imaging.
initial_request.known_context: mCherry is a widely used red fluorescent protein but exhibits cytotoxicity in E. coli, limiting its utility. mCherry2 is a less cytotoxic variant with similar spectra and slightly higher brightness.
constraints: Low cytotoxicity
Long Stokes shift or red-shifted fluorescence
quantifiable_goals: Long Stokes shift variant with excitation ~450 nm and emission ~610 nm
Red-shifted variant with excitation ~600 nm and emission ~630 nm
Brightness comparable to or better than existing RFPs
Low cytotoxicity comparable to mCherry2

evolution_trajectory.state_before: mCherry exhibits cytotoxicity in E. coli, limiting its utility as a fluorescent protein.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need for a less cytotoxic template to engineer new spectral variants.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: mCherry2, a less cytotoxic variant with similar spectra and slightly higher brightness, can serve as a suitable template for directed evolution.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Select mCherry2 as the starting template for engineering long Stokes shift and red-shifted variants.
evolution_trajectory.observation: mCherry2 shows similar growth rates to EGFP in bacterial cytotoxicity assay, indicating lower cytotoxicity than mCherry.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: With a low-cytotoxicity template chosen, hypothesis-driven mutagenesis can be performed to introduce long Stokes shift.

evidence.quote_or_span: bacteria expressing mCherry2 (Fig 2D) showed similar growth rates with bacteria expressing EGFP in the time frame of this test, suggesting they had lower cytotoxicity than mCherry
Due to the high brightness and low cytotoxicity, mCherry2 was chosen as the template for subsequent development of long Stokes shift and red-shifted variants.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: mCherry2 selected as template; no long Stokes shift variant exists.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to introduce a long Stokes shift into mCherry2?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Introducing an excited state proton transfer (ESPT) pathway via mutations at residues 161 and 163 will create a long Stokes shift variant.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform site-directed saturation mutagenesis at residues 161 and 163 of mCherry2.
evolution_trajectory.observation: The best variant, LSSmCherry0.1 (I161S/Q163E), exhibits blue-shifted excitation but mixed green/red emission.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: The mixed emission indicates failure to achieve pure long Stokes shift; further mutagenesis needed to suppress unwanted fluorescence.

evidence.quote_or_span: site-directed saturation mutagenesis was performed at residues 161 and 163 of mCherry2 (numbered according to DsRed) in an effort to introduce an excited state proton transfer (ESPT) pathway

further spectroscopic characterization of LSSmCherry0.1 revealed that a substantial portion of the protein formed a green fluorescent chromophore, and a substantial amount of short Stokes shift red fluorescence co-existed with the long Stokes shift fluorescence

saturation mutagenesis was performed at position 143, which is in the close vicinity of the chromophore. The variant with the highest ratio of long Stokes shift fluorescence relative to short Stokes shift and green fluorescence was mCherry2-W143L/I161S/Q163E, which was designated as LSSmCherry0.2.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: LSSmCherry0.1 has undesirable green and short Stokes shift red fluorescence.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to eliminate the unwanted fluorescence species?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Mutating position 143, near the chromophore, may suppress green and short Stokes shift fluorescence.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform saturation mutagenesis at position 143 on LSSmCherry0.1.
evolution_trajectory.observation: The variant mCherry2-W143L/I161S/Q163E (LSSmCherry0.2) shows exclusively long Stokes shift red fluorescence.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: LSSmCherry0.2 is a pure long Stokes shift variant but may need brightness improvement.

evidence.quote_or_span: saturation mutagenesis was performed at position 143, which is in the close vicinity of the chromophore. The variant with the highest ratio of long Stokes shift fluorescence relative to short Stokes shift and green fluorescence was mCherry2-W143L/I161S/Q163E, which was designated as LSSmCherry0.2.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: LSSmCherry0.2 has pure long Stokes shift but unknown brightness.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can brightness be improved while maintaining long Stokes shift and low cytotoxicity?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Multiple rounds of error-prone PCR (EP-PCR) and screening will yield brighter variants.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Subject LSSmCherry0.2 to multiple rounds of EP-PCR and screen for brightness and low cytotoxicity.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Final variant LSSmCherry1 (mCherry2-A6T/W143L/I161S/Q163E/A217S) obtained with λex=460 nm, λem=610 nm, and brightness calculated as 10.1 from ε=35,000 M⁻¹cm⁻¹ and Φ=0.29.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Characterize cytotoxicity of LSSmCherry1 to assess if low cytotoxicity is retained.

evidence.quote_or_span: These efforts ultimately led to a long Stokes shift (LSS)-mCherry variant ( $\lambda_{ex} = 460$ nm and $\lambda_{em} = 610$ nm) and a red-shifted (RDS)-mCherry variant ( $\lambda_{ex} = 600$ nm and $\lambda_{em} = 630$ nm).
The molar extinction coefficient ( $\varepsilon$ ) and quantum yield ( $\Phi$ ) of LSSmCherry1 was determined to be 35,000 M $^{-1}$ cm $^{-1}$ and 0.29, respectively (Table 1).
The final variant, LSSmCherry1 (equivalent to mCherry2-A6T/W143L/I161S/Q163E/A217S), has a 160 nm Stokes shift, with an excitation maximum at 450 nm and an emission maximum at 610 nm (Fig 1C and 1D).

saturation mutagenesis was performed at position 143, which is in the close vicinity of the chromophore. The variant with the highest ratio of long Stokes shift fluorescence relative to short Stokes shift and green fluorescence was mCherry2-W143L/I161S/Q163E, which was designated as LSSmCherry0.2.
The variant with the highest ratio of long Stokes shift fluorescence relative to short Stokes shift and green fluorescence was mCherry2-W143L/I161S/Q163E, which was designated as LSSmCherry0.

In the attempt to further improve mCherry2-I161S/I163E by elimination of the undesirable green and short Stokes shift red fluorescence, saturation mutagenesis was performed at position 143, which is in the close vicinity of the chromophore.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: LSSmCherry1 has desired spectral properties.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Does LSSmCherry1 retain the low cytotoxicity of mCherry2?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: LSSmCherry1 may have lost low cytotoxicity during directed evolution.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform bacterial cytotoxicity assay on LSSmCherry1.
evolution_trajectory.observation: LSSmCherry1 shows cytotoxicity similar to mCherry, indicating loss of mCherry2's low cytotoxicity.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: The loss of low cytotoxicity is a significant drawback; parallel efforts on red-shifted variants may yield better outcomes.

evidence.quote_or_span: The bacterial cytotoxicity assay demonstrated that RDSmCherry1 has cytotoxicity similar to that of mCherry and LSSmCherry1

evolution_trajectory.state_before: During screening of the 161/163 library for long Stokes shift variants, some colonies appeared blue/purple.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Could these unusual colonies represent red-shifted variants?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: The blue/purple colonies may contain red-shifted fluorescent proteins.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Characterize the blue/purple colonies from the 161/163 library.
evolution_trajectory.observation: A variant mCherry2-I161G/Q163G (RDSmCherry0.1) is identified with red-shifted excitation at 598 nm and emission at 625 nm.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: RDSmCherry0.1 is a promising red-shifted variant; further red-shift may be achieved by additional mutations.

evidence.quote_or_span: it was noticed that a number of colonies appeared blue or purple to visual inspection
Gene sequencing revealed this variant to be mCherry2-I161G/Q163G
spectral analysis confirmed that it had a substantially red shifted fluorescence excitation and emission maxima at 598 nm and 625 nm
During screening of the mCherry2 161X/163X library it was noticed that a number of colonies appeared blue or purple to visual inspection.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: RDSmCherry0.1 has red-shifted spectra but further red-shift is desired.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can π-π stacking interactions further red-shift the fluorescence?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Introducing π-π stacking via mutations at positions 195 and 197 will further red-shift fluorescence.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform saturation mutagenesis at positions 195 and 197 on RDSmCherry0.1.
evolution_trajectory.observation: The best variant RDSmCherry0.2 (V195C/I197Y) shows modest red-shift to λex=600 nm, λem=630 nm.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: The red-shift is modest; further rounds of evolution may enhance it.

evidence.quote_or_span: positions 197 and 195 were randomized by saturation mutagenesis
Screening for the most red-shifted mutant in the 195X/197X library led to the identification of mCherry2-I161G/Q163G/V195C/I197Y
this variant was only modestly red-shifted relative to RDSmCherry0.1, with an excitation maximum at 600 nm and an emission maximum at 630 nm

evolution_trajectory.state_before: RDSmCherry0.2 has modest red-shift.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can additional rounds of evolution further red-shift the spectra?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Multiple rounds of saturation mutagenesis and EP-PCR will yield further red-shifted variants.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Subject RDSmCherry0.2 to multiple rounds of saturation mutagenesis and EP-PCR.
evolution_trajectory.observation: RDSmCherry0.5 obtained with λex=604 nm, λem=636 nm, but exhibits undesirable blue fluorescence (λex=400 nm, λem=462 nm).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: The blue fluorescence is an artifact that needs to be eliminated.

evidence.quote_or_span: Multiple additional rounds of saturation mutagenesis and EP-PCR produced RDSmCherry0.5 with excitation maximum further red-shifted to 604 nm and emission maximum shifted to 636 nm
it was found that this variant exhibited a distinctive blue fluorescence with excitation at 400 nm and emission at 462 nm

evolution_trajectory.state_before: RDSmCherry0.5 has blue fluorescence artifact.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: What causes the blue fluorescence?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Mutations at positions 195 and 197 are responsible for the blue fluorescence.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Analyze the mutational origin of blue fluorescence by comparing RDSmCherry0.5 with RDSmCherry0.1.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Blue fluorescence is attributed to mutations at 195 and 197, as RDSmCherry0.1 lacks it.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: To eliminate blue fluorescence, re-mutagenize positions 195/197 on RDSmCherry0.5.

evidence.quote_or_span: This led us to speculate that the mutations at position 195 and 197 were responsible for the undesirable blue fluorescence
5 had 7 mutations: Val16Ser, Ala44Cys, Ala145Pro, Ile161Gly, Gln163Gly, Val195Cys, and Ile197Tyr.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Blue fluorescence in RDSmCherry0.5 linked to positions 195/197.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can the blue fluorescence be eliminated while retaining far-red fluorescence?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Saturation mutagenesis at 195/197 on RDSmCherry0.5, screening for maximal far-red and minimal blue, will yield a pure red-shifted variant.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform saturation mutagenesis at 195 and 197 on RDSmCherry0.5 with dual screening.
evolution_trajectory.observation: RDSmCherry1 (mCherry2-V16S/A44C/A145P/I161G/Q163G/V195C) identified; reversion I197Y eliminates blue fluorescence, yielding λex=600 nm, λem=630 nm.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Characterize cytotoxicity of RDSmCherry1.

evidence.quote_or_span: another round of saturation mutagenesis on 195 and 197 was performed on the template of RDSmCherry0.5 with screening for maximal far-red fluorescence and minimal blue fluorescence
Thorough screening of this library led to the identification of mCherry2-V16S/A44C/A145P/I161G/Q163G/V195C, which was designated as RDSmCherry1
Reversion of Tyr197 to Ile in the final round of engineering was associated with the complete disappearance of the blue fluorescence and an increase in red intensity

These efforts ultimately led to a long Stokes shift (LSS)-mCherry variant ( $\lambda_{ex} = 460$ nm and $\lambda_{em} = 610$ nm) and a red-shifted (RDS)-mCherry variant ( $\lambda_{ex} = 600$ nm and $\lambda_{em} = 630$ nm).
Screening for the most red-shifted mutant in the 195X/197X library led to the identification of mCherry2-I161G/Q163G/V195C/I197Y, which was designated RDSmCherry0.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: RDSmCherry1 has desired spectral properties.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Does RDSmCherry1 retain low cytotoxicity?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: RDSmCherry1 may have lost low cytotoxicity during directed evolution.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform bacterial cytotoxicity assay on RDSmCherry1.
evolution_trajectory.observation: RDSmCherry1 has cytotoxicity similar to mCherry, indicating loss of mCherry2's low cytotoxicity.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Both LSSmCherry1 and RDSmCherry1 fail to maintain low cytotoxicity; overall utility is limited.

evidence.quote_or_span: The bacterial cytotoxicity assay demonstrated that RDSmCherry1 has cytotoxicity similar to that of mCherry and LSSmCherry1
indicating that the decreased cytotoxicity of mCherry2 was lost during directed evolution

evolution_trajectory.state_before: LSSmCherry1 and RDSmCherry1 have been engineered but with cytotoxicity issues.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How do these variants compare to existing red fluorescent proteins?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: The new variants may not be competitive with existing RFPs in brightness or cytotoxicity.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Compare LSSmCherry1 and RDSmCherry1 with existing RFPs like mCardinal and TagRFP657.
evolution_trajectory.observation: LSSmCherry1 and RDSmCherry1 are not improved over existing RFPs in brightness or cytotoxicity, limiting immediate utility.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: The variants may serve as templates for further engineering but are not immediately useful.

evidence.quote_or_span: Similarly, red-shifted variants of the sea anemone-derived mKate RFP, including mCardinal ( $\lambda_{ex} = 604$ nm and $\lambda_{em} = 659$ nm) [30] and TagRFP657 ( $\lambda_{ex} = 611$ nm and $\lambda_{em} = 657$ nm) [31], have been developed.

These efforts ultimately led to a long Stokes shift (LSS)-mCherry variant ( $\lambda_{ex} = 460$ nm and $\lambda_{em} = 610$ nm) and a red-shifted (RDS)-mCherry variant ( $\lambda_{ex} = 600$ nm and $\lambda_{em} = 630$ nm).

validation_methods: Spectral characterization (excitation/emission maxima, extinction coefficient, quantum yield)
metrics: Bacterial growth rate relative to EGFP
success_verification.final_conclusion: LSSmCherry1 and RDSmCherry1 were successfully engineered with desired spectral properties but lost the low cytotoxicity of the mCherry2 template, and are not competitive with existing RFPs.
limitations: Both variants exhibit cytotoxicity similar to mCherry, losing the low cytotoxicity advantage of mCherry2.
Brightness of RDSmCherry1 (5.0) is lower than some existing far-red proteins.
No in vivo imaging validation beyond bacterial cytotoxicity.

source.title: Adaptive laboratory evolution of Corynebacterium glutamicum towards higher growth rates on glucose minimal medium

initial_request.research_problem: Improve growth rate of C. glutamicum on glucose minimal medium via ALE and compare evolvability of wild-type ATCC 13032 and prophage-free MB001.
initial_request.known_context: C. glutamicum is an important biotechnological platform strain. MB001 is a prophage-free variant of ATCC 13032.
constraints: Glucose minimal medium (CGXII)
30°C incubation
quantifiable_goals: Increase growth rate on glucose minimal medium
Compare adaptive behavior of ATCC 13032 and MB001

evolution_trajectory.state_before: C. glutamicum ATCC 13032 and MB001 have uncharacterized growth rate potential on glucose minimal medium.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: It is unknown whether the prophage-free MB001 has a growth advantage over wild-type ATCC 13032 and how both strains will adapt under ALE.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: ALE on glucose minimal medium will improve growth rates, and MB001 may show different adaptive behavior due to lack of prophages.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform comparative ALE of ATCC 13032 and MB001 on glucose minimal medium.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Goal defined: improve growth rate and compare evolvability.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need to establish baseline competitive fitness between strains.

evidence.quote_or_span: In this work, we performed a comparative adaptive laboratory evolution experiment of the important biotechnological platform strain Corynebacterium glutamicum ATCC 13032 and its prophage-free variant MB001 towards improved growth rates on glucose minimal medium.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: No prior competitive fitness data between ATCC 13032 and MB001.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Does MB001 have a growth advantage over wild-type?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: MB001 may outcompete wild-type due to reduced metabolic burden from prophages.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Conduct competitive growth experiment with fluorescently labeled strains.
evolution_trajectory.observation: After three serial transfers, MB001 showed significant competitive advantage.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Motivates comparative ALE to see if advantage persists under selection.

evidence.quote_or_span: already after three serial transfers a significant competitive advantage for strain MB001 (for both reporter strains, vice versa) was observed

we observed no significant differences in the adaptive behavior, mutation frequency or transposon activity of C. glutamicum ATCC 13032 wild type and its prophage-free derivative MB001 in our comparative ALE experiment

In this work, we performed a comparative adaptive laboratory evolution experiment of the important biotechnological platform strain Corynebacterium glutamicum ATCC 13032 and its prophage-free variant MB001 towards improved growth rates on glucose minimal medium.
no significant differences, neither in the mutation frequency nor in transposon activity, were observed between ATCC 13032 and MB001
no significant differences, neither in the mutation frequency nor in transposon activity, were observed between ATCC 13032 and MB001

evolution_trajectory.state_before: MB001 shows initial competitive advantage; ALE design needed.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Optimal ALE protocol to compare strains and achieve growth improvement.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Repetitive batch cultivation in 48-well plates with 90 transfers (~630 generations) will select for faster growth.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Design ALE with six independent cell lines per strain, 90 serial transfers in CGXII glucose minimal medium.
evolution_trajectory.observation: ALE design established; first 20 transfers included iron-depleted medium to test prophage induction, but discontinued due to no effect.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Execute ALE and monitor fitness changes.

evidence.quote_or_span: Overall, six independent cell lines for each strain were evolved by repetitive batch cultivation using 48-well plates incubated at 30°C
serially transferred (90 times) from the stationary phase into fresh medium
The evolution experiment started with six biological replicates of each strain
In total, 90 serial transfers were performed during this ALE experiment.

by genome sequencing and fitness tests), two cell lines that originated from different biological replicates of the two strains were used from the first (100 and 140 generations) and last two time points (540 and 630 generations).
two cell lines that originated from different biological replicates of the two strains were used from the first (100 and 140 generations) and last two time points (540 and 630 generations).

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Fitness leap observed; genetic changes unknown.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which mutations are responsible for improved growth?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Genome sequencing of evolved populations will reveal causative mutations.

no significant differences, neither in the mutation frequency nor in transposon activity, were observed between ATCC 13032 and MB001

from the repeated ALE experiment one cell line of each strain were examined after 50 and 80 generations.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Multiple potentially causative mutations identified.

evidence.quote_or_span: the pyk gene, encoding a pyruvate kinase, was one of the most frequently affected genes with five different mutations
these pyk mutations already appeared in an early stage of the experiment (after 100 & 140 generations) in low frequencies and displayed a competitive dynamic progression within the population
analysis of the sequencing data revealed the phosphofructokinase gene fruK as another important key target
one of the two fruK SNPs (T6I) occurred independently at different time points in the two prophage-free cell lines

It is noteworthy that one of the two fruK SNPs (T6I) occurred independently at different time points in the two prophage-free cell lines whereas mutation R71L was found only in strain UBm showing the highest growth rate of all isolated clones.

evidence.quote_or_span: only observed at later stages in the ALE experiment suggesting epistatic interactions of corA mutations supporting the evolution towards higher growth rates on glucose

evidence.quote_or_span: The reintegration of selected pyk and fruK mutations resulted in an increased glucose consumption rate and ptsG expression causative for the accelerated growth on glucose minimal medium

this mutant showed the strongest impact on growth

success_verification.final_conclusion: ALE successfully improved growth rates after ~100 generations. Key mutations in pyk, fruK, and corA were identified. source.title: Development of a genetically programed vanillin-sensing bacterium for high-throughput screening of lignin-degrading enzyme libraries

initial_request.research_problem: Lack of high-throughput screening methods for direct detection of vanillin production limits directed evolution of lignin-degrading enzymes.
initial_request.known_context: Robust methods for detecting the conversion of lignin to desired small molecules are not available.
constraints: Must detect vanillin directly from lignin degradation
Must be compatible with high-throughput screening
input_data: RNAseq data of E. coli exposed to vanillin
quantifiable_goals: Detect vanillin at concentrations relevant to enzyme activity
Achieve sufficient fold induction for FACS-based screening

evolution_trajectory.state_before: No robust high-throughput method exists for directly detecting vanillin from lignin degradation.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to transduce vanillin concentration into a measurable signal for high-throughput screening.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A vanillin-inducible promoter can be used to create a biosensor that transduces vanillin concentration to reporter gene expression.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Develop a genetically encoded biosensor using a vanillin-inducible promoter driving a fluorescent reporter.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Development of a biosensor that can detect a lignin degradation product by transducing the metabolite concentration to reporter gene expression would be useful.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Identify candidate vanillin-inducible promoters from transcriptomic data.

evidence.quote_or_span: robust methods for detecting the conversion of lignin to desired small molecules are not available
Development of a biosensor that can detect a lignin degradation product by transducing the metabolite concentration to reporter gene expression would be useful

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Hypothesis formulated; need to identify and clone vanillin-responsive promoters.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which promoters are up-regulated by vanillin and can drive sufficient reporter expression.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Putative promoter regions (first 300 bp upstream) of top seven up-regulated genes from RNAseq can function as vanillin-inducible promoters.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Clone the first 300 bp upstream of each of the top seven up-regulated genes into a pUC19-based plasmid with vGFP and a strong g10 RBS.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Seven plasmid constructs were designed, each with a different putative promoter region upstream of vGFP.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Transform constructs into E. coli and screen for vanillin-induced fluorescence.

evidence.quote_or_span: The putative promoter regions of the top seven up-regulated genes were arbitrarily predicted to be located within the first 300 bp of the non-coding region immediately upstream of the up-regulated genes

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Seven plasmid constructs designed; need to test their response to vanillin.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which construct yields the highest fold induction and lowest background.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: At least one of the seven constructs will show significant vanillin-dependent fluorescence increase.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Transform E. coli BL21 with each construct, induce with 5 mM vanillin, and measure fluorescence.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Lcb5 (yeiW promoter) showed 4.3-fold induction; Lcb4 (proA promoter) showed high background and only 1.8-fold induction.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Select Lcb5 as the VSC biosensor for further characterization; note Lcb4 as a failure.

evidence.quote_or_span: Seven live cell biosensors (Lcb1–Lcb7) were developed by transforming chemically competent E. coli BL21 cells with seven plasmid constructs

The biosensor Lcb5 constructed with the putative promoter region upstream of the yeiW gene showed higher fluorescence enhancement (4.3 fold) compared to Lcb4 that was made with the putative promoter region upstream of the proA gene.
Lcb4 showed high background fluorescence with 1.8-fold fluorescence enhancement.

Response of the biosensor to 5 mM vanillin was not abruptly affected by the presence of 5 mg/ml alkaline kraft lignin alone or in combination with 5 mM each of acetosyringone, acetovanillone, guaiacol, syringaldehyde and vanillyl alcohol.

To study its performance in real lignin-degrading condition, the biosensor was also treated with two mixtures: (1) 5 mg/ml alkaline kraft lignin and 5 mM vanillin and (2) 5 mg/ml alkaline kraft lignin and 5 mM each of acetosyringone, acetovanillone, guaiacol, syringaldehyde, vanillyl alcohol and vanillin.
vanillin inhibited the growth of the biosensor at 5 mM concentration but the cells recovered from initial toxicity and entered log phase after 6 h.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Lcb5 selected as VSC biosensor; need to determine its sensitivity and dynamic range.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: What is the detection limit and dose-response profile of the VSC biosensor.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: The biosensor will show a dose-dependent increase in fluorescence with vanillin concentration.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Test VSC biosensor with vanillin concentrations from 0 to 5 mM and measure fluorescence by FACS.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Dose-dependent response observed; detection limit of 200 µM vanillin by FACS.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate specificity against lignin and related compounds.

evidence.quote_or_span: The VSC biosensor was then tested against different concentrations of vanillin to determine its detection threshold.
detecting as low as 200 $\mu$ M vanillin by FACS

Response of the biosensor to 5 mM vanillin was not abruptly affected by the presence of 5 mg/ml alkaline kraft lignin alone or in combination with 5 mM each of acetosyringone, acetovanillone, guaiacol, syringaldehyde and vanillyl alcohol.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Biosensor sensitivity established; need to ensure it responds specifically to vanillin.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Does the biosensor cross-react with lignin, vanillin analogues, or other chemicals.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: The biosensor is highly specific to vanillin and will not respond to lignin or analogues.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Test biosensor response to lignin, lignin model compound, DMSO, vanillin analogues, and non-specific toxic chemicals.

parameters.tested_compounds: lignin, lignin model compound, DMSO, vanillin analogues, non-specific toxic chemicals

evolution_trajectory.observation: No major response elicited by any of the tested compounds; biosensor is highly specific to vanillin.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Test performance in complex mixtures mimicking real lignin degradation conditions.

evidence.quote_or_span: The biosensor is highly specific to vanillin and no major response is elicited by the presence of lignin, lignin model compound, DMSO, vanillin analogues or non-specific toxic chemicals
This biosensor does not show any cross-reactivity to lignin, vanillin analogues or non-specific toxic chemicals.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Specificity validated; need to assess performance in the presence of lignin and other degradation products.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Will the biosensor function accurately in complex mixtures containing lignin and multiple degradation products.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: The biosensor response to vanillin will not be significantly affected by the presence of lignin and other degradation products.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Treat biosensor with two mixtures: (1) lignin + vanillin, (2) lignin + all degradation products + vanillin, and measure fluorescence.

parameters.mixture_1: 5 mg/ml alkaline kraft lignin + 5 mM vanillin
parameters.mixture_2: 5 mg/ml alkaline kraft lignin + 5 mM each of acetosyringone, acetovanillone, guaiacol, syringaldehyde, vanillyl alcohol, vanillin

evolution_trajectory.observation: Fluorescence in mixtures was comparable to vanillin alone, with only 5–10% reduction.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Test toxicity of lignin degradation products on the biosensor.

evidence.quote_or_span: To study its performance in real lignin-degrading condition, the biosensor was also treated with two mixtures: (1) 5 mg/ml alkaline kraft lignin and 5 mM vanillin and (2) 5 mg/ml alkaline kraft lignin and 5 mM each of acetosyringone, acetovanillone, guaiacol, syringaldehyde, vanillyl alcohol and vanillin.

Response of the biosensor to 5 mM vanillin was not abruptly affected by the presence of 5 mg/ml alkaline kraft lignin alone or in combination with 5 mM each of acetosyringone, acetovanillone, guaiacol, syringaldehyde and vanillyl alcohol.
about 5–10% less fluorescence was observed when the sensor was treated with the mixtures in comparison to treatment with vanillin alone

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Biosensor performs well in mixtures; need to ensure it can survive and function in the presence of toxic degradation products.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Are lignin degradation products toxic to the biosensor at relevant concentrations.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: The biosensor will tolerate lignin degradation products up to 20 mM, with vanillin causing only temporary growth inhibition.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Grow biosensor in the presence of various lignin degradation products at concentrations up to 20 mM and monitor growth.
evolution_trajectory.observation: No major toxicity up to 20 mM; vanillin inhibited growth at 5 mM but cells recovered after 6 h; no recovery at 10 mM or higher within 9 h.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Analyze vanillin's antimicrobial mode to understand recovery.

evidence.quote_or_span: Toxicity of various lignin degradation products and the non-specific chemical acrylic acid was determined by growing the live cell biosensor in the presence of various concentrations of each chemical.
vanillin inhibited the growth of the biosensor at 5 mM concentration but the cells recovered from initial toxicity and entered log phase after 6 h.
the biosensing cells could not recover within the study time (9 h) from growth inhibition at vanillin concentrations of 10 mM or higher
No major lignin degradation product showed significant toxicity towards the biosensor when treated with up to 20 mM concentration.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Vanillin causes temporary growth inhibition; need to understand the nature of this effect.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Is vanillin's antimicrobial effect bacteriostatic or bactericidal, and how does this impact biosensor viability.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Vanillin's growth inhibition is bacteriostatic, allowing cells to recover and remain viable for sensing.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Interpret growth recovery data in the context of known antimicrobial mechanisms.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Microbial growth inhibition by vanillin is bacteriostatic in nature rather than bactericidal.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Acknowledge limitation that biosensor has not been demonstrated in actual enzyme screening.

evidence.quote_or_span: microbial growth inhibition by vanillin is bacteriostatic in nature rather than bactericidal

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Biosensor characterized; need to identify limitations and propose future applications.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: The biosensor has not been tested in actual enzyme screening; detection limit may be insufficient for low-activity enzymes.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: The biosensor can be used as a host cell for screening lignin-degrading enzymes, but its detection range may limit selection of certain mutants.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Propose the biosensor as a host cell for screening, while noting the detection limit as a potential limitation.
evolution_trajectory.observation: The biosensor could potentially be used for screening, but may not select mutants producing very low (<0.5 mM) or very high (>5 mM) vanillin.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Conclude with proposal for future use.

evidence.quote_or_span: This engineered E. coli cell could potentially be used as a host cell for screening lignin-degrading enzymes
We propose the use of this biosensor as a host cell for screening of lignin-degrading enzymes from randomized libraries and metagenomic samples.
may be a limitation in selecting for mutants that can produce very low (<0.5 mM) or very high (>5 mM) concentrations of vanillin.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Biosensor validated for specificity, sensitivity, and robustness; ready for proposed application.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can the biosensor be recommended as a host cell for high-throughput screening of lignin-degrading enzymes.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: The VSC biosensor is suitable as a host cell for screening lignin-degrading enzymes from randomized libraries and metagenomic samples.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Propose the biosensor for screening applications based on accumulated evidence.
evolution_trajectory.observation: The biosensor is proposed as a host cell for screening lignin-degrading enzymes.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: End of trajectory.

evidence.quote_or_span: We propose the use of this biosensor as a host cell for screening of lignin-degrading enzymes from randomized libraries and metagenomic samples.

metrics: Specificity (no cross-reactivity to lignin or analogues)
Tolerance (up to 20 mM of degradation products)
success_verification.final_conclusion: A vanillin-sensing biosensor (VSC) was developed using the yeiW promoter, demonstrating high specificity, dose-dependent response, and robustness in lignin degradation mixtures, and is proposed as a host cell for high-throughput screening of lignin-degrading enzymes.
limitations: Not yet demonstrated in actual enzyme screening experiments
Detection limit of 200 µM may be insufficient for low-activity enzymes
Vanillin toxicity at high concentrations (>5 mM) may limit dynamic range

source.title: Thermal stability and kinetic constants for 129 variants of a family 1 glycoside hydrolase reveal that enzyme activity and stability can be separately designed

initial_request.research_problem: Lack of large datasets with quantitative kinetic constants and thermal stability measurements hinders evaluation of enzyme design algorithms.
initial_request.known_context: Existing databases like ProTherm lack enzyme kinetic data; large-scale mutational studies often measure activity only qualitatively or convolve stability and activity.
constraints: Single point mutations only
Must be experimentally tractable (129 mutants)
quantifiable_goals: Generate a dataset of quantitative kinetic constants (kcat, KM, kcat/KM) and thermal stability (Tm) for a large set of enzyme variants
Evaluate whether stability and activity can be separately designed

evolution_trajectory.state_before: No large dataset exists with both quantitative kinetic constants and thermal stability for enzyme variants.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which single point mutations of BglB should be selected for experimental characterization to cover diverse effects?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A combination of alanine scanning, random selection, and student-designed mutations via Foldit will yield a diverse set of mutants for functional and stability profiling.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Select 129 single point mutants from 8,455 possible using three approaches: alanine scanning of active-site residues, random selection near active site, and Foldit-based student designs.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Three sets of mutants were chosen: alanine scanning of residues within 12 Å of pNPG, random mutations within 12 Å, and Foldit student-designed mutations.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Selected mutants need to be produced and purified for experimental characterization.

evidence.quote_or_span: To select a subset of the 8,455 possible single point mutations to the native BglB sequence that could be experimentally characterized, three approaches were taken.
First, all residues within 12 Å of the modeled pNPG were individually mutated to alanine.
Second, a subset of mutants was chosen at random by selecting a random mutation to residues within 12 Å of the active site.

Third, a model of the BglB-pNPG complex was loaded into Foldit, a graphical user interface to Rosetta, point mutations to the protein were modeled, and a subset were chosen by students learning about molecular modeling.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: 129 BglB mutants designed but not yet produced.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can all 129 mutants be expressed and purified in soluble form?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Most mutants will express in E. coli, but some may fail due to folding defects.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Express all 129 mutants in E. coli BLR(DE3) and purify via Ni-NTA affinity chromatography.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 92 mutants expressed as soluble protein; 37 mutants did not express after at least 2 attempts.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Soluble mutants proceed to functional assays; non-expressing mutants analyzed for folding rule consistency.

evidence.quote_or_span: Individual purified plasmid constructs were transformed into chemically competent Escherichia coli BLR (DE3) cells
Protein purity was assessed using 4–12% gradient SDS-PAGE (Life Technologies) and total protein yield determined by A280 using a BioTek Epoch spectrophotometer.
Of 129 mutant proteins produced, 92 expressed and purified as soluble protein

evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Thermal challenge followed by residual activity measurement will yield functional Tm values for most mutants.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform thermal stability assay: incubate at 8 temperatures (30–50°C, 2.5°C steps) for 30 min, measure residual activity with pNPG, fit to logistic equation to determine Tm.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Kinetic constants for some BglB mutants previously measured; reproducibility and new mutants need characterization.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Are kinetic measurements reproducible, and what are the kinetic constants for newly characterized mutants?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Kinetic assays will yield reproducible kcat, KM, and kcat/KM values, with some mutants showing significant changes.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Measure kinetic constants for 10 new mutants and compare with previous wild-type replicates.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Strong agreement with previous wild-type replicates (≤4% difference); notable mutant N220Y showed 10-fold decrease in KM.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Combine kinetic and stability data to identify specific trade-off and independent modulation examples.

evidence.quote_or_span: The most notable change in kinetic constants within the new mutants characterized in this publication is the 10-fold decrease (indicating more sensitive enzyme response to substrate concentration) for computationally-designed mutant N220Y.
The limit of detection of our assay for $k_{cat}/K_{M}$ is $10 M^{-1} min^{-1}$ , and the maximum $k_{cat}/K_{M}$ in the data set is $1,570,000 M^{-1} min^{-1}$ (N220Y).

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Tm and kinetic data available for 79 mutants.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Do specific mutations show trade-offs or independent modulation of stability and activity?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Some mutations will increase Tm while decreasing kcat (trade-off), while others will alter one parameter without affecting the other (independent modulation).
evolution_trajectory.decision: Identify mutants with notable changes in Tm and kinetic constants.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Examples: N404C (Tm +2.75°C, kcat -10-fold), W120F (Tm +2.6°C, kcat -9-fold), R240A (Tm unchanged, kcat +10-fold), Q313R (Tm +2.2°C, kcat within 10%), N220Y (Tm +1.9°C, KM -10-fold).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Statistical analysis needed to generalize the relationship between stability and activity across all mutants.

evidence.quote_or_span: The mutation R240A does not change $T_{m}$ , but increases $k_{cat}$ by 10-fold (see Fig 2), while Q313R increases $T_{m}$ by 2.
For the Michaelis constant ( $K_{M}$ ) a decrease of 10-fold in mutant N220Y is accompanied by an increase in $T_{m}$ by 1.
Similarly, the mutation W120F increased functional $T_{m}$ by 2.

To evaluate the ability of molecular modeling software to predict thermal stability of mutants in our data set, we modeled each of the 129 point mutations to the BglB sequence using three approaches: 1) an enzyme-specific algorithm termed RosettaDesign [11], 2) an algorithm for predicting $\Delta \Delta G$ of point mutations to proteins termed Rosetta $\Delta \Delta G$ [12], and 3) an algorithm for predicting $\Delta \Delta G$ of mutations to proteins using FoldX [9].

Second, mutations were generated and scored using the Rosetta ddg\_monomer application, with recommended settings previously validated on experimental data [20], and the results were averaged across all 50 iterations.

source.title: Engineering of Methylation State Specific 3xMBT Domain Using ELISA Screening

initial_request.research_problem: The wild-type 3xMBT domain binds both mono- and di-methylated lysine peptides with similar affinity, limiting its utility as a methylation-state-specific probe. The goal is to engineer 3xMBT variants that exclusively recognize di-methylated lysine.
initial_request.known_context: 3xMBT is a methyl-lysine binding domain that recognizes mono- and di-methylated lysines but not unmodified or tri-methylated lysines.

Must eliminate binding to mono-methylated lysine

quantifiable_goals: Achieve exclusive binding to di-methylated peptides

evolution_trajectory.observation: T411L binding specificity was similar to NNS3F5, confirming T411L alone confers di-methyl specificity.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: T411L validated; perform structural modeling to rationalize specificity.

evidence.quote_or_span: the binding specificity to different peptides of recombinant T411L 3XMBT mutant was compared to the WT and the NNS3F5 recombinant 3XMBT proteins (Fig 5D and S3 Fig).

evolution_trajectory.state_before: T411L experimentally confirmed to confer di-methyl specificity.

evolution_trajectory.observation: T411L mutation increases hydrophobicity, excludes water molecule, disfavors mono-methyl binding while maintaining di-methyl affinity.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Mechanism rationalized; note limitation that further engineering needed for other methylation states.

evidence.quote_or_span: The human SCML2 MBT repeats in a complex with histone H2A peptide (monomethyl lysine, pdbID-4EDU)[18] structure was modified according to its electron density or mutated using Coot [19].

The structure of the Lethal(3)malignant brain tumor-like protein 1 3-MBT repeats (dimethyl lysine analog, PDBID-1OYX) [20] was overlapped on 4EDU using the least square fit in Coot to allow the correct orientation of the dimethyl lysine.

Analysis of the binding site of the T411L mutant reveals that the mutation to leucine can significantly increase the hydrophobicity of the binding site, leading to an exclusion of the water molecule and consequently to disfavored binding to mono-methylated lysine
our data supports a model by which these structural changes can lead to a decreased affinity to mono-methylated lysine while maintaining affinity to the di-methylated lysine.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Engineered 3xMBT variants specifically recognize di-methylated lysine.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: The current engineering only addresses di-methyl specificity; other methylation states remain.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Further engineering of methylated lysine binding domains using the ELISA approach can lead to domains specific for mono- or tri-methylated proteins.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Note limitation and future direction.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Further engineering needed for domains specific to mono- or tri-methylated proteins.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Limitation acknowledged; future work can extend the approach.

evidence.quote_or_span: We envision that further engineering of methylated lysine binding domains using the ELISA approach can lead to a repertoire of domains capable of specific recognition of mono-methylated, di-methylated or tri-methylated proteins in the cell.

metrics: Binding signal ratio di-methyl/mono-methyl >3.5-fold over WT
success_verification.final_conclusion: Engineered 3xMBT variants, particularly T411L, specifically recognize di-methylated lysine, with structural basis elucidated.
limitations: Further engineering needed for domains specific to mono- or tri-methylated proteins.

source.title: Better prediction of functional effects for sequence variants

initial_request.research_problem: Improving prediction of functional effects of sequence variants (effect vs. neutral) over previous SNAP method, motivated by personalized health needs and challenges of limited/imbalanced training data and sequence similarity.
initial_request.known_context: Existing methods focus on disease, not molecular function change. SNAP predecessor exists.
constraints: Limited and imbalanced training data
Sequence similarity may cause overfitting
quantifiable_goals: Improve two-state accuracy over SNAP
Achieve high AUC on ALL dataset

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Need to distinguish functional from neutral sequence variants for personalized health; existing methods focus on disease, not molecular function change.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Existing methods do not predict molecular function change directly; SNAP predecessor may be improved.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A neural network classifier trained on diverse variant data and many features can better distinguish effect from neutral variants.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Design SNAP2 as a neural network classifier to predict molecular function change, not disease.
evolution_trajectory.observation: SNAP2 designed to improve over SNAP by screening many features and refining datasets.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need to compile training data for the classifier.

evidence.quote_or_span: Elucidating the effects of naturally occurring genetic variation is one of the major challenges for personalized health and personalized medicine.

In contrast, the current version of our SNAP (Screening for Non-Acceptable Polymorphisms) method, SNAP2, does not predict the variant effect as “disease or not” but rather as “change of molecular function or not”.

Here, we introduce SNAP2, a novel neural network based classifier that improves over the state-of-the-art in distinguishing between effect and neutral variants. Our method's improved performance results from screening many potentially relevant protein features and from refining our development data sets.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: SNAP2 design requires training data.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need diverse and balanced training data with careful class assignment.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Combining PMD, EC, disease variants, and protein-specific studies will provide sufficient training data.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Compile training data from PMD, EC, OMIM, HumVar, LacI, and HIV-1 protease; assign effect/neutral classes.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Compiled ALL dataset with 101,515 variants (40,478 neutral, 61,037 effect) in 9,744 proteins.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need cross-validation strategy to avoid overfitting due to sequence similarity.

evidence.quote_or_span: PMD annotations with ‘no change’ (‘=’) qualification (function equivalent to wild-type) were assigned to the ‘neutral’ class, while variants with any level of increase (‘+’, ‘++’, ‘+++’) or decrease (‘-’, ‘- -’, ‘- - -’) in function were assigned to the ‘effect’ class.
First, $PMD + EC + Disease$ were compiled into one comprehensive set termed ALL with 101,515 variants (40,478 neutral, 61,037 effect) in 9,744 proteins.
We extracted 22,858 human disease-associated variants in 3,537 proteins from OMIM [24] and HumVar [25].
This approach identified 51,817 variants (neutral: 13,638, effect: 38,179) in 4,061 proteins.
We also included data from comprehensive studies of particular proteins, namely LacI repressor from Escherichia coli [31] (4,041 variants) and the HIV-1 protease [32] (336 variants).

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Training data compiled; risk of overfitting due to sequence similarity.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Proteins with similar sequences may bias cross-validation.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Clustering proteins by sequence similarity and splitting clusters into folds will provide unbiased cross-validation.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Use PSI-BLAST (E<10^-3) single linkage clustering; 10-fold cross-validation with random cluster split.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Cross-validation strategy established to avoid overfitting.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need to define input features for the neural network.

evidence.quote_or_span: We also split the PMD data into two subsets: one containing only human mutations ( $PMD\_HUMAN$ ; 9,657 variants in 678 human sequences) and one consisting of all others ( $PMD\_NON-HUMAN$ ; 42,160 variants in 3,383 sequences).
First, $PMD + EC + Disease$ were compiled into one comprehensive set termed ALL with 101,515 variants (40,478 neutral, 61,037 effect) in 9,744 proteins.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Cross-validation strategy defined; need input features.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which features best distinguish effect from neutral variants?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Evolutionary information, biophysical properties, structural features, and new annotations will improve prediction.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Include PSIC profiles, AAindex, binding residues, disorder, co-evolution, Pfam/PROSITE annotations; use sliding windows up to 21 residues.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Feature set defined including evolutionary, structural, and biophysical features.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need to select optimal features and architecture.

evidence.quote_or_span: features: global features (amino acid composition, secondary structure and solvent accessibility composition, and protein length), PSI-BLAST [27] profiles and deltas, PSIC [12] profiles and deltas (differences between mutant and wild-type residue annotations; see Methods for details), residue flexibility, sequence and variant profiles, disorder, secondary structure and relative solvent accessibility and their deltas, physicochemical properties (charge, hydrophobicity, volume, and their deltas), contact potential pr

network included the following features: global features (amino acid composition, secondary structure and solvent accessibility composition, and protein length), PSI-BLAST [27] profiles and deltas, PSIC [12] profiles and deltas (differences between mutant and wild-type residue annotations; see Methods for details), residue flexibility, sequence and variant profiles, disorder, secondary structure and relative solvent accessibility and their deltas, physicochemical properties (charge, hydrophobicity, volume, and thei
each feature could be selected in a window of w = 1,5,9,13,17, or 21 consecutive residues around the observed variant at the center of the window.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Feature set defined; need to select optimal subset and architecture.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Not all features may be useful; architecture parameters need tuning.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Greedy bottom-up feature selection with backward elimination and heuristic architecture optimization will yield best performance.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform greedy feature selection per network using AUC on cross-training set; optimize hidden nodes, learning rate, momentum.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Feature selection and architecture optimization completed.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Evaluate final model performance via cross-validation.

evidence.quote_or_span: As an exhaustive screening of the entire parameter space was not intended, we heuristically selected parameter combinations for optimization: learning rate 0.005-0.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Model trained with selected features and architecture.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Does SNAP2 outperform existing methods?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: SNAP2 will achieve higher accuracy and AUC than SNAP, PolyPhen-2, and SIFT.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Cross-validate SNAP2 on ALL dataset and compare to other methods.
evolution_trajectory.observation: SNAP2 achieved 83% two-state accuracy, AUC 0.905, outperforming SNAP (0.880), PolyPhen-2 (0.853), SIFT (0.838).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Analyze performance by organism and variant difficulty.

evidence.quote_or_span: Cross-validated on >100k experimentally annotated variants, SNAP2 significantly outperformed other methods, attaining a two-state accuracy (effect/neutral) of 83%.
SNAP2 (dark blue, AUC = 0.905) outperforms its predecessor SNAP (light blue, AUC = 0.880), PolyPhen-2 (orange, AUC = 0.853) and SIFT (green, AUC = 0.838)

evolution_trajectory.observation: SNAP2 slightly better on non-human; neutral variants harder across methods.

evidence.quote_or_span: The F-measure for predicting effect ( $F_{effect}$ , Eqn. 3), the two state-accuracy (Q2, Eqn. 4), and the Matthew's correlation coefficient (MCC, Eqn. 5) were slightly higher for SNAP2 when tested on the non-human than on the human set

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Performance varies by organism; neutral variants harder.

evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: SNAP2's use of non-alignment features like co-evolution will improve difficult case prediction.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Analyze performance on ALL-difficult subset and examine feature contributions.
evolution_trajectory.observation: SNAP2 correctly predicted 69% of difficult cases vs SNAP 53%, SIFT 41%; improvement partly from non-alignment features.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Compare to naive method combination.

evidence.quote_or_span: SNAP2 outperformed others on the difficult cases, correctly predicting 69%, as compared to SNAP with 53% and SIFT with 41%
one important source of SNAP2 improvement for difficult cases originates from its use on various pieces of information, not just alignments
For these difficult cases, our new method SNAP2 significantly outperformed SNAP (set ALL-difficult: Q2(snap2) = 69%, Q2(snap) = 53%) and SIFT (Q2(sift) = 41%).

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to predict for orphan proteins without alignments.

evolution_trajectory.observation: SNAP2_noali achieves Q2 around 68% from sequence alone for 6.5 million orphan families.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate on independent datasets.

evidence.quote_or_span: This feature reduces prediction runtime by over two orders of magnitude, enables cross-genome comparisons, and renders our new method as the best solution for the 10-20% of sequence orphans.
we developed a classifier that still achieves a two-state accuracy Q2 around 68% from sequence alone even for these 6.5 million orphan families.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: SNAP2 and SNAP2_noali developed.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need independent validation of SNAP2 performance.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: SNAP2 will perform well on LacI and HIV-1 protease datasets.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Test SNAP2 on independent LacI (4,041 variants) and HIV-1 protease (336 variants) datasets.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Q2 = 78% for LacI, Q2 = 72% for HIV-1 protease.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Acknowledge limitations in comparison and data.

However, they provide independent evidence to estimate the performance of SNAP2: Q2 = 78% for 4,041 LacI variants and Q2 = 72% for 336 HIV-1 variants.
We also included data from comprehensive studies of particular proteins, namely LacI repressor from Escherichia coli [31] (4,041 variants) and the HIV-1 protease [32] (336 variants).

evolution_trajectory.decision: Note limitations in comparison and data quality.

success_verification.final_conclusion: SNAP2 significantly outperforms SNAP, PolyPhen-2, and SIFT on variant effect prediction, with 83% accuracy and AUC 0.905. SNAP2_noali provides reasonable accuracy for orphan proteins. Limitations include comparison issues and potential performance plateau.
