source.title: Combining Rational and Random Strategies in β-Glucosidase Zm-p60.1 Protein Library Construction

initial_request.research_problem: How to efficiently construct a complete protein library at a highly conserved position when saturation mutagenesis unexpectedly yields many active variants, requiring a combined rational and random strategy.
initial_request.known_context: Previous work showed that mutation W373K severely impairs catalytic activity, suggesting W373 is critical.
constraints: Low transformation efficiency of E. coli strain
Codon bias in NNM degeneracy
Cost considerations for mutagenesis and screening
quantifiable_goals: Complete library of all 18 amino acid variants at position 373
Assess activity of all variants on natural and artificial substrates

evolution_trajectory.state_before: W373 is highly conserved; previous W373K mutant has severely impaired activity.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Unknown whether other amino acids at position 373 can retain activity.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: W373 is critical for activity, so saturation mutagenesis will yield few active variants.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Test hypothesis by saturation mutagenesis at W373.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Expected one or a few functional variants based on previous work.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Design saturation mutagenesis library to test hypothesis.

evidence.quote_or_span: On the basis of our previous work we expected to find one or a few functional variants of the weakly catalyzing mutant W373K [7,8].

Thus, although their catalytic activity was significantly impaired, we have demonstrated that variation at position 373 is possible, without complete loss of activity, and that variants with mutations at the position could be used in efforts to modulate and explain $\beta$ -glucosidases' specificity.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Hypothesis that W373 is critical.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to create a library covering all possible amino acids at position 373 except Met and Trp.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: NNM codon degeneration will generate a diverse library excluding Met and Trp.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Use NNM degeneracy primers for saturation mutagenesis.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Library designed with 32 codon variants encoding 18 amino acids and 2 stop codons.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Construct library and screen for activity.

evidence.quote_or_span: A library of Zm-p60.1 mutants with variations at the Trp373 position was created using the following set of primers introducing NNM degeneracy:
Mutagenesis by NNM codon degeneration yields 32 codon variants, encoding 18 amino acids and 2 stop codons.

Thus, although their catalytic activity was significantly impaired, we have demonstrated that variation at position 373 is possible, without complete loss of activity, and that variants with mutations at the position could be used in efforts to modulate and explain $\beta$ -glucosidases' specificity.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Library designed, transformation into E. coli.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Low transformation efficiency may limit library size.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Despite low efficiency, some clones will be obtained for screening.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Proceed with screening of obtained colonies.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Number of colonies was low due to low transformation efficiency.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Screen available clones for activity.

evidence.quote_or_span: The number of colonies was low due to the low transformation efficiency of the engineered E. coli strain.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Low number of colonies obtained.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Activity of clones unknown.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Most clones will be inactive if W373 is critical.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Screen 21 clones on XGlc plates.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 20 of 21 clones were active; one inactive clone had an unintended stop codon at position 206.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Sequence active clones to identify variants.

evidence.quote_or_span: The clone with no glucosidase activity proved to be a proline variant with an additional stop codon introduced at position 206 by an unknown error in the mutagenesis procedure.
However, most clones proved to be active, for example 20 of 21 clones initially obtained displayed various levels of $\beta$ -glucosidase activity.

Therefore, we applied saturation mutagenesis followed by activity screening to identify other amino acid residues at the W373 position that could be compatible with the composition of $\beta$ -glucosidase Zm-p60.1 and catalytic effects of the substitutions.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: 20 active clones identified.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which amino acid variants are present and at what frequencies?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Sequencing will reveal multiple variants with frequencies reflecting codon usage.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Sequence the 20 active clones.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 10 unique amino acid variants identified: Leu (5), Pro (3), Arg (3), Ser (2), Ala (2), Gly (2), His (1), Thr (1), Asp (1), Phe (1).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Assess completeness of library and need for further screening.

evidence.quote_or_span: We identified 10 variants at the 373 position: Leu (five copies); Pro and Arg (each in three copies); Ser, Ala and Gly (each in two copies); His, Thr, Asp and Phe (all in one copy).

The cost-effectiveness of the mutagenesis strategies was compared using estimates for (a) SDM and sequencing based on costs of commercial oligonucleotide synthesis + mutagenesis (by a QuikChange kit) + DNA isolation (by a Qiagen kit) + commercial sequencing (ca. 100 USD per variant in total), and b) screening only, based on DNA isolation (by a Qiagen kit) + commercial sequencing (ca. 20 USD per variant in total).

evolution_trajectory.state_before: 10 variants found, 8 missing (Cys, Glu, Ile, Met, Asn, Gln, Val, Tyr).
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How many clones must be screened to find remaining variants?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Monte Carlo simulation will show a sharp increase in screening effort for the last variants.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Run Monte Carlo simulation with 1,000,000 iterations.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Sharp increase in clones needed for the last quarter of library construction.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Compare cost of continued screening vs. site-directed mutagenesis.

evidence.quote_or_span: we determined theoretical average numbers of clones needed to find new variants and complete the library in computer simulations (Figure 2).
The simulations show that there is a sharp increase in numbers of clones needed for the last quarter of library construction

The simulation used to calculate theoretical average numbers of clones that needed to be screened for a new hit using each codon degeneration strategy was based on Monte Carlo method (scripts are enclosed as a File S6) implemented in Python 2.7.3 with 1 000 000 iterations simulating random clone picking.
File S6 The simulation used to calculate theoretical average numbers of clones that needed to be screened for a new hit based on Monte Carlo method.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Simulation shows screening for missing variants is inefficient.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which strategy is more cost-effective for completing the library?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: SDM is more cost-effective than screening for the missing variants.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform cost analysis comparing SDM (~100 USD/variant) vs. screening (~20 USD/variant).
evolution_trajectory.observation: SDM becomes cost-effective when many clones must be screened to find a new variant; SDM ~100 USD/variant, screening ~20 USD/variant.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Propose combined strategy.

evidence.quote_or_span: The cost-effectiveness of the mutagenesis strategies was compared using estimates for (a) SDM and sequencing based on costs of commercial oligonucleotide synthesis + mutagenesis (by a QuikChange kit) + DNA isolation (by a Qiagen kit) + commercial sequencing (ca. 100 USD per variant in total), and b) screening only, based on DNA isolation (by a Qiagen kit) + commercial sequencing (ca. 20 USD per variant in total).

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Random screening inefficient for complete coverage; SDM cost-effective for missing variants.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to efficiently complete the library?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A combined random and rational strategy will efficiently generate all variants.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Propose combined strategy: random mutagenesis (NNM) plus SDM for missing variants.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Ideal strategy combines random mutagenesis with SDM to generate missing variants.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Implement SDM to create missing variants.

evidence.quote_or_span: we propose that an ideal strategy should combine random mutagenesis with site-directed mutagenesis (SDM) to generate missing variants (Figure 3).

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Missing variants: Cys, Glu, Ile, Met, Asn, Gln, Val, Tyr.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can missing variants be created by SDM?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: SDM with specific primers will generate the missing variants.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Use QuikChange kit for individual SDM reactions.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Missing variants created; expression problems encountered for Leu and Ser variants.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Optimize codons for problematic variants.

evidence.quote_or_span: Finally, to complete the library we created missing variants using the QuikChange kit again in individual reactions with the same template and following primers –

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Expression problems with Leu and Ser variants.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Codon usage may affect expression.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Changing codons to TCC for Ser and CTG for Leu will improve expression.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform codon optimization for Leu and Ser.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Expression problems resolved.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Express and purify all variants for activity assays.

evidence.quote_or_span: Due to expression problems with Leu and Ser variants their codons were changed to TCC for Ser and CTG for Leu.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: All 18 amino acid variants expressed and purified.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Are all variants active on natural and artificial substrates?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: All variants will retain some activity, but at reduced levels.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Test activity on tZOG (1 mM) and pNPG.
evolution_trajectory.observation: All variants hydrolyzed both substrates, but at reduced levels compared to wild-type.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Characterize pH optima of selected variants.

evidence.quote_or_span: In surprising contrast to the high conservation at this position all enzyme variants were able to hydrolyze the substrates, albeit at reduced levels (Figure 4).
All purified enzymes were tested for their activity towards the natural substrate trans-zeatin-O-β-D-glucoside (tZOG, OlChemIm, Olomouc, Czech Republic) at a concentration of 1 mM.
Mutagenesis by NNM codon degeneration yields 32 codon variants, encoding 18 amino acids and 2 stop codons.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: All variants active but impaired.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Do substitutions affect pH optimum?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: pH optimum may shift for some variants.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Analyze pH profiles of most (W373H) and least (W373K) active variants.
evolution_trajectory.observation: W373H pH optimum shifted from 5.5 to 6.0; W373K similar to wild-type.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Conclude on tolerance of variation at position 373.

evidence.quote_or_span: File S1 The pH optimum analysis of the most and least active variants (W373H and W373K).
We found no significant difference between the W373K mutant and WT pH profiles, but the pH optimum of the W373H mutant shifted from 5.5 to 6.

Thus, although their catalytic activity was significantly impaired, we have demonstrated that variation at position 373 is possible, without complete loss of activity, and that variants with mutations at the position could be used in efforts to modulate and explain $\beta$ -glucosidases' specificity.
coli expression strain, all 20 variants at position 373 were expressed, purified to homogeneity and tested for catalytic effect.
We identified 10 variants at the 373 position: Leu (five copies); Pro and Arg (each in three copies); Ser, Ala and Gly (each in two copies); His, Thr, Asp and Phe (all in one copy).
These results suggest that mutagenesis at the 373 position can influence the optimum pH of Zm-p60.1, but this effect alone is not the cause of the activity reductions.

validation_methods: Enzyme assays on tZOG and pNPG
metrics: Number of unique variants (10 initially, 18 after SDM)
Hydrolysis rates on tZOG and pNPG
success_verification.final_conclusion: All 18 amino acid variants at position 373 are active but with significantly reduced activity, demonstrating that variation is tolerated but not optimal. The combined random and rational strategy efficiently completed the library.
limitations: All variants have significantly impaired catalytic activity compared to wild-type.
Low transformation efficiency limited initial library size.
Expression problems required codon optimization for Leu and Ser.

source.title: An Evolution-Based Approach to De Novo Protein Design and Case Study on Mycobacterium tuberculosis

initial_request.research_problem: Physics-based force fields are inaccurate for protein fold recognition, limiting design of medium-to-large proteins.
initial_request.target_object: Protein sequences that fold into desired structures
initial_request.known_context: Existing physics-based methods (PBM) often fail to correctly recognize protein folds due to force field inaccuracies.
constraints: Design sequences must fold into target scaffold structures
Homologous templates excluded from I-TASSER folding simulations
quantifiable_goals: Improve foldability over PBM (lower RMSD, higher TM-score)
Achieve soluble, well-folded proteins in experimental validation

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Physics-based force fields are inaccurate for protein fold recognition, limiting design of medium-to-large proteins.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to design protein sequences that reliably fold into target structures without relying solely on inaccurate physics-based force fields.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Using evolutionary information from structure families can guide sequence design more robustly than physics-based methods.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Propose a novel method that uses evolutionary information, in the form of sequence profiles from structure families, to guide sequence design.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Evolutionary information from structure families may provide more robust guidance for sequence design.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Develop a computational method that implements this hypothesis.

evidence.quote_or_span: the inaccuracy of the force-field design, which cannot accurately describe atomic interactions or correctly recognize protein folds
We propose a novel method which uses evolutionary information, in the form of sequence profiles from structure families, to guide the sequence design.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Hypothesis that evolutionary information can guide sequence design.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need a concrete computational method that combines evolutionary profiles with physics-based packing.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A structural profile-guided Monte Carlo search with a combined energy function (profile match + FoldX) can design sequences that fold to the target.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Develop the Evolution-Based Method (EBM) consisting of structural profile construction and profile-guided Monte Carlo sequence space searching simulation.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Method developed: structural profiles from analogs, Monte Carlo search with combined energy, clustering for final sequence.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate that I-TASSER can discriminate folded from unfolded designs.

evidence.quote_or_span: The outline of our protein design procedure is shown in Figure 1, which consists of structural profile construction and the profile-guided Monte Carlo sequence space searching simulation.
The energy function of the MC search consists of two parts. The first part counts for the alignment match of the sequence decoy with the target structural profile
The second part of the energy function contains a physics-based force field from FoldX V3.0b5
For each target, ten independent Monte Carlo runs, each starting from a different random sequence, are performed.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: EBM method developed; need to validate I-TASSER as a foldability discriminator.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Whether I-TASSER folding simulations can reliably distinguish folded from unfolded designs.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: I-TASSER estimated TM-score and RMSD correlate with actual values and can separate folded and unfolded designs.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Test I-TASSER on previously designed sequences with known experimental folding outcomes.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Estimated TM-score and RMSD highly correlated with actual values (correlation 0.91 and 0.80). Average TM-score 0.718 (folded) vs 0.551 (unfolded); RMSD 3.9 Å vs 6.8 Å.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Use validated I-TASSER to compare EBM and PBM foldability.

evidence.quote_or_span: the estimated TM-score and RMSD of the I-TASSER predictions for the designed proteins are highly correlated with the actual TM-score and RMSD, with a correlation coefficient of 0.91 and 0.80, respectively.
the average TM-scores (RMSDs) are 0.718 (3.9 Å) and 0.551 (6.8 Å), respectively

evolution_trajectory.state_before: I-TASSER validated as foldability discriminator.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Whether EBM-designed sequences show improved foldability over PBM.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: EBM sequences will have lower RMSD and higher TM-score than PBM sequences in I-TASSER folding simulations.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Design sequences for 87 test proteins using EBM and PBM, then fold with I-TASSER.
evolution_trajectory.observation: EBM: RMSD 2.12 Å, TM-score 0.87. EBM shows improved foldability.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Analyze TM-score distribution to understand PBM unreliability.

evidence.quote_or_span: the RMSD and TM-score for the EBM designed sequences are 2.12 Å and 0.87, respectively, which demonstrated an improved foldability by EBM
The method is tested on the design experiment of 87 non-homologous single-domain proteins covering different fold classes.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: EBM outperforms PBM on average, but PBM shows divergent TM-scores.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Why PBM sometimes fails dramatically.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: PBM TM-score distribution is bimodal, indicating unreliability; EBM improves low-scoring cases.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Examine TM-score distributions and individual cases.
evolution_trajectory.observation: PBM TM-scores either very high (>0.85) or very low (<0.35). EBM improves TM-scores for low-scoring PBM cases.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Investigate structural feature improvements (secondary structure, torsion, solvation).

evidence.quote_or_span: the distribution of TM-scores of the PBM designed sequences is more divergent, i.e. the TM-scores are either very high (>0.85) or very low (<0.35)

evolution_trajectory.state_before: EBM improves foldability; need to understand structural basis.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How EBM improves secondary structure, torsion angles, and solvation.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: EBM reduces normalized relative errors (NRE) in secondary structure, backbone torsion, and solvent accessibility compared to PBM.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Calculate NRE for secondary structure, Φ/Ψ angles, and solvent accessibility.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Secondary structure NRE: EBM 0.33 vs PBM 2.40 (7x lower). Improvement due to profile smoothening and SS energy terms.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Analyze dependence on structural analogs and TM-score cutoff.

evidence.quote_or_span: The NRE of designed sequence by the EBM is 0.33, which is seven times lower than that by the PBM method

The reduction in the secondary structure error for the EBM method is mainly due to the smoothening effects introduced by the structural profiles and the single-sequence based SS energy terms, which significantly increase the short-range residue cooperation that are usually missed in the physics-based force fields.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: EBM performance established; need to understand parameter sensitivity.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How EBM performance depends on the number of structural analogs and TM-score cutoff.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Higher TM-score cutoff for analog selection improves design accuracy.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Group test proteins by TM-score cutoff of structural analogs and compare RMSD.
evolution_trajectory.observation: RMSD: 1.46 Å (cutoff>0.7), 2.57 Å (>0.6), 2.84 Å (>0.5). Higher cutoff yields better designs.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Perform ablation study to assess contribution of evolution vs physics terms.

evidence.quote_or_span: the average RMSD of the I-TASSER models for the first group of 46 proteins is 1.46 Å, which is lower than the two other groups based on the lower TM-score cutoffs (2.57 Å and 2.84 Å).

the designs for the thioredoxin, CISK-PX and TIF1 domains showed strongly shielded methyl shifts between 0.5 and -1.0 ppm and had well-resolved peaks in the amide region, features that are indicative of proteins possessing stable folds.

In reality, recent studies have demonstrated that the current PDB library is approaching to completeness and almost all the proteins, including random homopolypeptides, can have protein analogs with similar folds (TM-score>0.5) in the PDB [62–64].

We found that 32% of residues in the conserved positions in the EBM sequences are identical to that in the target and 44% of residues in the conserved regions are highly homologous (with a BLOSUM62 mutation score>0.5) to the corresponding residues in the target.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: MTb designs generated; need to assess foldability.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Whether MTb designs fold as well as test set designs.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: MTb designs will have higher RMSD due to larger protein sizes, but performance consistent with test set when controlling for length.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Fold MTb designs with I-TASSER and compute RMSD.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Average RMSD 3.28 Å for all, 2.57 Å for proteins ≤200 residues. Consistent with test set performance.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Analyze ligand-binding properties of MTb designs.

evidence.quote_or_span: The average RMSD of the I-TASSER models is, however, relatively higher (=3.28 Å)
If we exclude the proteins of length >200 residues, the average RMSD of the I-TASSER models is reduced to 2.57 Å

evolution_trajectory.state_before: MTb designs fold well; need to assess functional potential.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Whether designed MTb proteins retain ligand-binding capabilities.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: EBM-designed MTb proteins will have comparable or better ligand-binding predictions than targets.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Use COFACTOR to predict binding partners and binding sites.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 62% of designed proteins have binding partners (vs 59% targets). Average 6.2 binding sites per design vs 5.5 per target.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Experimentally validate selected EBM designs.

evidence.quote_or_span: 62% of the EBM designed proteins have binding partners with a high confidence score
COFACTOR detects slightly less (59%) binding partners
the EBM designed sequences have on average more binding sites (6.2 per protein) than the target proteins (5.5 per protein)

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Computational results promising; need experimental confirmation.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Whether EBM-designed proteins actually fold in vitro.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: EBM-designed proteins will be soluble and exhibit stable tertiary structure.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Select five designs (hnRNPK, thioredoxin, CISK-PX, LOV2, TIF1) for expression, purification, and characterization by CD and NMR.
evolution_trajectory.observation: All five soluble with secondary structure. Three (thioredoxin, CISK-PX, TIF1) have stable tertiary fold by NMR. Two (hnRNPK, LOV2) lack stable fold.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Characterize folding stability of successful designs.

evidence.quote_or_span: five sequences were randomly selected from the design pool and subjected to experimental validation. The results showed that all the designed proteins are soluble with distinct secondary structure and three have well ordered tertiary structure
all the designed domains successfully expressed and were soluble

the hnRNPK and Lov2 designs lacked upfield methyl chemical shifts (-1.0-0.5 ppm) and had sparse features in the protein amide range (5.5–10.0 ppm) (Figures 7F and 7I), which suggest that they do not possess a stable fold.

the designs for the thioredoxin, CISK-PX and TIF1 domains showed strongly shielded methyl shifts between 0.5 and -1.0 ppm and had well-resolved peaks in the amide region, features that are indicative of proteins possessing stable folds.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Three designs show stable fold; need to quantify stability.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Folding free energies of successful designs.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Thioredoxin and CISK-PX designs have measurable folding free energies.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform urea denaturation experiments on thioredoxin and CISK-PX.
evolution_trajectory.observation: ΔG: thioredoxin -16.1 kJ/mol, CISK-PX -29.6 kJ/mol.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Conclude with overall assessment and limitations.

evidence.quote_or_span: Free energies of folding for the thioredoxin and CISK-PX domains were calculated by linear regression from the data points available and determined to be -16.1 and -29.6 kJ/mol, respectively [76].

evolution_trajectory.state_before: EBM method developed and validated; need to assess computational cost.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Computational cost of including FoldX in the EBM pipeline.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Including FoldX significantly increases simulation time due to side-chain calculations.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Measure average simulation time with and without FoldX.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Average simulation time without FoldX is 3.8 hours; including FoldX increases time to 16.5 hours.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Note limitation in conclusions.

evidence.quote_or_span: the average simulation time of our method without using FoldX is 3.8 hours, while including FoldX increases the time for the program to 16.5 hours

validation_methods: I-TASSER folding simulations with homologous templates excluded
Experimental validation by CD and NMR spectroscopy
Urea denaturation for folding free energy
metrics: Normalized relative error (NRE) for secondary structure, torsion angles, solvent accessibility
success_verification.final_conclusion: EBM significantly improves foldability over PBM, with average RMSD 2.12 Å and TM-score 0.87 on test set. Evolution terms are major driver. Large-scale application to 243 M. tuberculosis proteins successful. Experimental validation: 3/5 designs have stable folds. Limitations include high computational cost with FoldX (16.5 hours) and failure of two designs to fold stably.
limitations: Computation time high when including FoldX (16.5 hours vs 3.8 hours without)
Two out of five experimentally tested proteins (hnRNPK, LOV2) lacked stable fold
I-TASSER folding accuracy decreases for larger proteins

source.title: Structural insights from random mutagenesis of Campylobacter jejuni oligosaccharyltransferase PglB

initial_request.research_problem: Elucidate residues of the periplasmic domain of Campylobacter jejuni PglB important for N-glycosylation to improve engineered glycoprotein production for conjugate vaccines.
initial_request.target_object: Campylobacter jejuni oligosaccharyltransferase PglB periplasmic domain
initial_request.known_context: PglB catalyzes transfer of lipid-linked oligosaccharides to asparagine residues in acceptor proteins. The periplasmic domain contains the WWDYG catalytic motif and a proposed MIV motif for acceptor peptide binding. A crystal structure of PglB from C. lari is available.
constraints: Medium-throughput screening required for mutant library analysis
E. coli expression system
input_data: PglB wild-type and inactive W458A-D459A mutant
quantifiable_goals: Identify residues critical for glycosylation activity
Find PglB variants with improved glycoprotein yield

evolution_trajectory.state_before: No medium-throughput screening method available for glycoprotein detection in E. coli periplasm.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need for a method to screen PglB mutant libraries for glycoprotein production.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A sandwich ELISA using anti-EPA capture and anti-CP5 detection antibodies can specifically detect EPA-CP5 glycoconjugates in periplasmic extracts.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Develop a sandwich ELISA for glycoprotein detection.
evolution_trajectory.observation: ELISA absorbance values were reproducibly and significantly higher for wild-type PglB samples than for inactive PglB samples. Coefficient of variation below 5%.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: ELISA validated, but background signals were higher with low-copy plasmid, requiring optimization.

evidence.quote_or_span: A method for detection of glycoproteins in the periplasm of E.coli which is suitable for medium throughput screening has not yet been described, but is required for the analysis of pglB mutant libraries.
The ELISA setup with the best signal-to-noise ratio for periplamic extracts of E. coli comprised anti-EPA capturing antibodies and anti-CP5 detection antibodies (sandwich ELISA).
ELISA absorbance values were reproducibly and significantly higher for samples from wild-type PglB strains compared to samples from isogenic control strains expressing inactive PglB (Figure 1B).
The coefficient of variation of ELISA absorbance values was below 5% of the mean value for periplasmic extracts of strains expressing either wild-type or inactive PglB.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: ELISA showed higher background with low-copy pglB plasmid.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Low-copy plasmid pGVXN114 gave higher background signals, reducing assay sensitivity.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Switching to a medium-copy plasmid will reduce background and improve signal-to-noise ratio.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Switch to medium-copy plasmid pGVXN112Kan for PglB expression.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Background signals were higher with low-copy plasmid than with medium-copy plasmid.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Medium-copy plasmid chosen for further development of 96-well screening.

evidence.quote_or_span: Background signals (PglB mut) were higher when the low copy number pglB expression plasmid was used than when the medium copy number, pACT3 derived plasmid was used (Figure 1B).
lcp: low copy number plasmids pGVXN114 (wt) and pGVXN115 (mut), mcp: medium copy number plasmids pGVXN112Kan (wt) and pGVXN113Kan (mut), mut: inactive PglB W458A-D459A, host strain: E.
Based on the positive results with the sandwich ELISA assay for shake flask samples, we developed a 96-well cultivation and screening method for glycoprotein production in E. coli.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Sandwich ELISA validated in shake flasks with medium-copy plasmid.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to scale down to 96-well format for medium-throughput screening of mutant libraries.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A 96-well deep well plate cultivation with sucrose-lysozyme extraction and sandwich ELISA can reliably distinguish wild-type from inactive PglB.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Develop 96-well DWP-ELISA screening method.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Clear separation between wild-type (average absorbance 0.402, CV 5.1%) and inactive PglB (0.275, CV 4.5%) clones.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validated screening system enables random mutagenesis and library screening.

evidence.quote_or_span: Based on the positive results with the sandwich ELISA assay for shake flask samples, we developed a 96-well cultivation and screening method for glycoprotein production in E. coli.
we developed a 96-well cultivation and screening method for glycoprotein production in E. coli.
The ELISA signals of all wells containing the wild-type PglB strain were clearly separated from those wells which contained the inactive control strain (Additional file 1).
The average absorbance (coefficient of variation) was 0.402 (5.1%) and 0.275 (4.5%) for wild-type and inactive PglB, respectively.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: 96-well screening system validated.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which residues in the periplasmic domain are critical for activity?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Random mutagenesis of the periplasmic domain will reveal functionally important residues.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform error-prone PCR on the periplasmic domain (H423 to I713) and screen for inactive clones.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Library generated with average 0.8 amino acid changes. Inactive clones identified by ELISA.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Inactive clones need to be sequenced to identify mutations.

evidence.quote_or_span: On average 0.8 amino acid changes were introduced within the periplasmic domain of PglB (range 0–2)
Based on the positive results with the sandwich ELISA assay for shake flask samples, we developed a 96-well cultivation and screening method for glycoprotein production in E. coli.

Sandwich ELISA read-outs for EPA-CP5 glycoproteins and final optical densities (600 nm) of 90 wells inoculated with individual clones of E. coli CLM24 (pGVXN345-pGVXN150-pGVXN112Kan) (wt PglB, open symbols) and 6 distributed wells inoculated with E. coli CLM24 (pGVXN345-pGVXN150-pGVXN113Kan) (inactive PglB, closed symbols).
The majority of clones was inactive (77 ± 8% of samples with an ELISA absorbance below 10% of wt, n = 6 96-well plates)
Plasmids pGVXN112Kan and pGVXN113Kan were constructed by PCR-sub cloning of the sequence coding for wt PglB with C terminal HA tag and inactive PglB-HA W458A-D459A from
we developed a 96-well cultivation and screening method for glycoprotein production in E. coli.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Inactive clones isolated from epPCR library.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: What mutations cause inactivation? Are they in known motifs?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Inactivating mutations will cluster in functionally important regions like WWDYG and MIV motifs.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Sequence 92 inactive clones and analyze mutation distribution.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 27% of clones had expected negative mutations (WWDYG, stop, frameshift, MIV, Pro).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Many inactive clones may have lost expression; need to check PglB protein levels.

evidence.quote_or_span: Sequencing of 92 inactive clones without prior analysis of PglB expression showed that 27% of the clones harbored mutations with expected negative effects on expression and/or activity of PglB

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Many inactive clones may have lost PglB expression.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: What fraction of inactive clones actually express PglB?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Anti-HA Western blot will reveal expression levels and identify clones with inactivating mutations but normal expression.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Screen 410 inactive clones by anti-HA Western blot.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 68.5% no expression, 20.5% weak expression, 11% wild-type level expression.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Focus on expressing but inactive clones to identify critical residues.

evidence.quote_or_span: When 410 inactive clones of epPCR libraries were screened by anti-HA Western blot analysis, it was found that the majority (68.5%) had lost the ability to produce any PglB, while 84 (20.5%) exhibited weak expression and only 45 (11%) produced PglB at similar levels as the wild-type strain.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Expressing but inactive clones identified.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which specific residues in the WWDYG motif and adjacent loop are critical?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Mutations in the WWDYG motif and the flexible loop R465-G481 will abolish activity.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Identify inactivating single amino acid substitutions in expressing clones.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Inactivating mutations found in WWDYG motif (Y460N, G461D) and loop (R465H, D469L, D475F/Y, G481S/N). V432F reduced activity by 89%.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Map mutations onto crystal structure to interpret structural roles.

evidence.quote_or_span: A second region where inactivating mutations clustered was R465 to G481. D475 and G481 were mutated in several clones with strong expression of PglB
Mutation V432F reduced background-corrected EPA-CP5 signals by 89% without any negative effect on expression of PglB
Change of G481 to either S or N abolished formation of glycoconjugates completely in spite of wild-type levels of PglB
In addition to mutations Y460N and G461D within the WWDYG motif, four other amino acid changes lead to a complete loss of glycosylation activity: R465H, D469L, D475F and D475Y.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Critical residues identified in loop D475-G481 and WWDYG motif.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How do these residues contribute structurally to activity?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: The loop D475-G481 is flexible and interacts with glycan substrate; G481 acts as helix breaker.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Map inactivating mutations onto PglB_C.lari crystal structure.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Loop D475-G481 is in close proximity to WWDYG motif; G481 likely a helix breaker; loop believed to interact with sugar moieties.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Test roles of specific loop residues H479 and L480 by site-directed mutagenesis.

evidence.quote_or_span: the region V464 to G481 immediately adjacent to this motif is also highly important
these amino acids form a flexible loop in close proximity to the side chains of tryptophan and aspartic acid residues of the WWDYG motif
this loop is believed to interact with sugar moieties of the glycan substrate
The highly conserved residue G481, which was hit several times in inactive epPCR mutants, could function as a helix breaker

The inactivating mutations at G481 identified in our epPCR experiment presumably induce structural changes which disturb the interaction of loop residues such as H479 and D475 with the glycan substrate.
The importance of the loop D475-G481 is further backed by the phenotype of the H479Q variant generated in this study.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Loop D475-G481 implicated in glycan binding; H479 and L480 are part of this loop.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Are H479 and L480 critical for activity?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: H479 is important for activity; L480 is not, based on structural orientation.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Create site-directed mutants H479Q and L480A and measure glycoprotein yield by ELISA.
evolution_trajectory.observation: H479Q reduced glycoprotein yield >5-fold (16 ± 2.6% of wild-type). L480A had no effect (118 ± 0.04% of wild-type).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: MIV motif underrepresented in epPCR; need focused mutagenesis to explore its role.

evidence.quote_or_span: the yield of glycoprotein was reduced more than five-fold as determined by sandwich ELISA (16 ± 2.6% of background-corrected wild-type signals, n = 3)
removal of the aliphatic side chain of the adjacent, non-conserved residue L481 by mutation to alanine had no effect on activity at all.
This is in agreement with the spatial orientation of the side chain of the equivalent residue L486 in $PglB_{C.lari}$ pointing away from the acceptor peptide (Figure 5B).

By contrast, we did not observe any significant difference between variant L480A and wild-type $PglB_{Cj}$ in EPA-CP5 and PglB levels (band intensities on anti-HA Western blots: 127 ± 28% of wild-type, background-corrected ELISA signals: 118 ± 0.
The importance of the loop D475-G481 is further backed by the phenotype of the H479Q variant generated in this study.

The inactivating mutations at G481 identified in our epPCR experiment presumably induce structural changes which disturb the interaction of loop residues such as H479 and D475 with the glycan substrate.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: MIV motif (M568, I571, V575) hit only once in epPCR; its role unclear.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: What amino acids are tolerated at I571 and V575? Can we find improved variants?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Only hydrophobic residues will be tolerated at I571 and V575 due to interaction with acceptor peptide.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform cassette mutagenesis of I571 and V575 using NDT degenerate codons and screen for activity.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 77 ± 8% of clones inactive. Only 12 active clones out of 590 screened. No gain-of-function mutants found.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Characterize active variants to understand sequence-activity relationship.

evidence.quote_or_span: Randomization was achieved with mutagenic primers bearing NDT degenerate codons
The majority of clones was inactive (77 ± 8% of samples with an ELISA absorbance below 10% of wt, n = 6 96-well plates)
Only 12 out of 590 screened library clones produced signals within the range of wild-type control clones

The so-called MIV motif of bacterial N-oligosaccharyltransferases (M568, I571, V575 in $PglB_{Cj}$ , Figure 6A) which is assumed to interact with the acceptor peptide was hit only once in the epPCR experiment.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Active MIV variants isolated from cassette mutagenesis.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How do specific substitutions affect activity?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Conservative hydrophobic substitutions (I571V) will retain activity; larger or polar substitutions will reduce it.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Quantify glycoprotein yield of active variants by ELISA and Western blot.
evolution_trajectory.observation: I571V and I571V-V575I like wild-type. I571F-V575L reduced 3-fold. I571C-V575I moderate reduction.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Overall limitations: only one mutation outside W457-G481 found; no improved variants.

evidence.quote_or_span: No significant difference to wild-type PglB was found for variants I571V and I571V-V575I
The level of EPA-CP5 detected by ELISA was reduced by a factor of three in variant I571F-V575L
The double mutant I571C-V575I was characterized by a parallel, moderate reduction in the levels of both EPA-CP5 and PglB
Wild-type isoleucine and valine at position 571 exhibited the same phenotype.
In only one inactive clone with strong expression of pglB an amino acid change was found which mapped to a region outside of W457 to G481.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: All results analyzed; no improved variants found.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Why were no improved variants found? What are the limitations?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Screening numbers too low, other factors limiting, or need for simultaneous changes in membrane domain.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Acknowledge limitations and suggest future strategies.
evolution_trajectory.observation: No improved variants found. Possible reasons: low screening numbers, other limiting factors (e.g., precursor biosynthesis), need for simultaneous changes in membrane domain. Only one mutation (V432F) found outside W457-G481 among expressing inactive clones.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Conclude study with structural insights and recommendations.

evidence.quote_or_span: We did not detect any PglB variants with increased yields of glycoprotein in our libraries.
Most likely, the number of screened epPCR clones was too low to find improved variants, which are expected to be very rare.

we cannot exclude the possibility that protein glycosylation in the applied $E. coli$ system was limited by other factors than OST activity, e.g., biosynthesis of undecaprenyl-pyrophosphate linked polysaccharide precursors.
It might well be that an increase in catalytic activity is not possible without simultaneous changes both in the periplasmic and membrane domains of PglB $_{Cj}$ .

It would be interesting to study whether glycosylation of the D-Q-N-R-T site is more affected than that of the other site and whether a change of -1 Q to amino acids with shorter side chains could compensate for the negative effect of PglB $_{Cj}$ V432F.
In only one inactive clone with strong expression of pglB an amino acid change was found which mapped to a region outside of W457 to G481.

validation_methods: Anti-HA Western blot for PglB expression
Structural mapping onto PglB_C.lari crystal structure
success_verification.final_conclusion: The flexible loop D475-G481 adjacent to the WWDYG motif is critical for PglB activity, likely involved in glycan binding. H479 is important, while L480 is not. The MIV motif requires hydrophobic residues at I571 and V575 for activity. No improved PglB variants were found, possibly due to limited screening numbers or other limiting factors.
limitations: Only one mutation (V432F) found outside W457-G481 among expressing inactive clones, limiting broader structure-function insights.
No improved PglB variants found; screening numbers may be too low, or other factors limit glycosylation.
High proportion of mutations caused loss of PglB expression, complicating identification of purely catalytic mutations.

source.title: Identification of Sequence Variants in Genetic Disease-Causing Genes Using Targeted Next-Generation Sequencing

initial_request.research_problem: Develop and validate a targeted NGS method for efficient, low-cost identification of sequence variants in genetic disease-causing genes, overcoming limitations of Sanger sequencing and whole-genome NGS.
initial_request.known_context: Sanger sequencing is laborious, expensive, and time-consuming for multiple genes; whole-genome NGS is too costly for routine resequencing.
constraints: Low cost
High throughput
High sensitivity and specificity
input_data: DNA from 7 patients with 6 genetic diseases
quantifiable_goals: High coverage of targeted regions (≥30×)
High concordance with known variants (≥95%)
Identification of disease-causing mutations

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Sanger sequencing is laborious and expensive for multiple genes; whole-genome NGS is too costly for routine resequencing.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need for a cost-effective, high-throughput method to sequence multiple disease genes simultaneously.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A custom targeted capture array combined with NGS can efficiently enrich and sequence 193 disease genes, enabling variant detection at lower cost.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Design a custom NimbleGen solid-phase capture array targeting 3382 exons of 193 genes, with sample barcoding for multiplexing.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Designed array to capture 1.54 Mb of target sequence.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Proceed to sequencing of patient and control samples to evaluate performance.

evidence.quote_or_span: When multiple genes need to be analyzed for mutations simultaneously, the gathering of information becomes laborious, expensive and time consuming using the Sanger method.
Although NGS technology can be used to analyze gene variants with high throughput screening, its high cost still prevents its application in whole human genome resequencing projects.
we have designed a custom high-density, solid-phase array (Roche NimbleGen, Inc., Madison, WI, USA) to capture 193 distinct genes involved in 103 different genetic diseases.
the DNA adapter-ligated and indexed fragments from 10 libraries were pooled and hybridized to the capture array for 72 h.

To discover DNA variants of disease-causing genes involved in genetic disorders, we designed a unique high-density capture array to capture the 3382 exons and adjacent intron sequences of 193 genes causing distinct hereditary diseases (approximately 1.54 Mb, Table S1) that have a higher incidence in the Chinese population.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Custom capture array designed; no sequencing data yet.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Whether the targeted capture and sequencing will yield sufficient coverage and uniformity across all exons.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Targeted DNA-HiSeq on Illumina HiSeq2000 will produce high enrichment and coverage for the 193 genes.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Sequence 7 patient samples and 100 controls using the capture array and HiSeq2000 (paired-end 90bp).
evolution_trajectory.observation: 74% uniquely mapped reads; 99.54% of target covered at ≥30×; average exon depth 410×.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: High coverage enables accurate variant calling; proceed to accuracy estimation using YH DNA.

evidence.quote_or_span: we selected 7 samples from seven patients with six different genetic diseases resulting from six disease-causing genes and 100 samples from normal human adults as controls.
99.54% of the targeted region was covered in at least a 30-fold depth in each sample
The average coverage depth for exons was 410-fold, and the highest coverage depth for exons was 1000-fold
74% of the yielded clean reads could be uniquely matched to target regions, and 99.54% of the targeted region was covered in at least a 30-fold depth in each sample (Figure 2).

we sequenced 193 genes in DNA from 100 blood samples from 100 human adults and constructed our own polymorphism database for these 193 genes. We then considered the variants that did not overlap with our annotated SNPs (with an allele frequency more than 5%) for the 193 genes as candidate novel mutations for further analysis

To discover DNA variants of disease-causing genes involved in genetic disorders, we designed a unique high-density capture array to capture the 3382 exons and adjacent intron sequences of 193 genes causing distinct hereditary diseases (approximately 1.54 Mb, Table S1) that have a higher incidence in the Chinese population.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: High coverage achieved; variant calling accuracy unknown.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to establish variant calling criteria and estimate accuracy against a known reference.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Using criteria of ≥10 reads, >14% variant reads, Q>20 will yield high concordance with dbYH for YH DNA.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Apply initial variant calling criteria to YH DNA and compare with dbYH.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Found 1301 SNVs; 95.70% (1245/1301) concordance with dbYH.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Concordance is high but can be improved; investigate variant ratio distribution to refine heterozygous threshold.

evidence.quote_or_span: we found 1301 SNVs of 193 genes in YH DNA using Targeted DNA-HiSeq, of which 95.70% (1245/1301) were also identified in dbYH

The variants were considered to be candidate variants according to the following criteria: (1) the sites were supported by at least 10 reads; (2) the variant sequence was supported by more than $14\%$ of the total reads; and (3) the quality value was greater than 20 per base [18].

Figure S1 Estimation of the accuracy of Targeted DNA-HiSeq according to the following criteria: (1) sites are supported by at least 10 reads; (2) variant sequences are supported by more than 14% of the total reads for the heterozygous genotype; and (3) a quality value of more than 20 per base was obtained.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Initial accuracy 95.70% with >14% variant ratio threshold.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Some discordant variants may be due to suboptimal heterozygous threshold.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Raising the heterozygous variant ratio threshold to >20% will improve concordance by reducing false positives.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Analyze variant ratio distribution; set new threshold at >20% for heterozygous calls.
evolution_trajectory.observation: With >20% threshold, found 1281 SNVs; concordance improved to 96.8% (1240/1281).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Refined criteria will be applied to patient samples for variant calling.

evidence.quote_or_span: a new criterion was used to identify candidate variants. Based on these new standards, we found 1281 SNVs of 193 genes in YH DNA (C1) using Targeted DNA-HiSeq, of which 96.8% (1240/1281) were also identified in dbYH
6\%$ (1240/1245) of common variants (Figure S1B), suggesting that candidate variants should be supported by more than $20\%$ of the total reads

To further ascertain the appropriate ratio of candidate variants in heterozygous genotypes, we compared the distribution ratio of candidate variants in heterozygous genotypes between common variants (1245 variants) and specific variants (56 variants) (Figure S1A).

The results showed that the ratio of candidate variants in heterozygous genotypes was more than $20\%$ in $99.6\%$ (1240/1245) of common variants (Figure S1B), suggesting that candidate variants should be supported by more than $20\%$ of the total reads

Figure S1 Estimation of the accuracy of Targeted DNA-HiSeq according to the following criteria: (1) sites are supported by at least 10 reads; (2) variant sequences are supported by more than 14% of the total reads for the heterozygous genotype; and (3) a quality value of more than 20 per base was obtained.

(B) The distribution of ratio of candidate variants with heterozygous genotypes between common variants (1245 variants) and specific variants (56 variants) showed that the ratio of candidate variants with heterozygous genotypes was greater than 20% in 99.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Refined variant calling criteria established.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to identify variants in patient samples and filter for disease-causing mutations.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Applying refined criteria and a filtering strategy will identify candidate disease mutations.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Call variants in 7 patients using BWA, SOAPsnp, GATK; filter against known databases and custom control polymorphism database.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Identified 6317 variants (5734 SNVs, 583 indels); after filtering, 79 candidate mutations (29 known, 41 novel, 9 indels).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Examine candidate mutations for disease relevance.

evidence.quote_or_span: We finally identified 6317 variants, including 583 indels and 5734 substitutes.

we sequenced 193 genes in DNA from 100 blood samples from 100 human adults and constructed our own polymorphism database for these 193 genes. We then considered the variants that did not overlap with our annotated SNPs (with an allele frequency more than 5%) for the 193 genes as candidate novel mutations for further analysis
Out of the 79 total candidate mutations, 7 mutations occurred in 6 disease-causing genes

We then considered the variants that did not overlap with our annotated SNPs (with an allele frequency more than 5%) for the 193 genes as candidate novel mutations for further analysis, including 29 known substitutions, 41 novel substitutions and 9 novel indels.
Among these variants, 84.4% (4841/5734) were termed as “known SNVs”, which were well known SNPs that had been deposited in the dbSNP database, HGMD, LSMD, and 1000 human genome dataset.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: 79 candidate mutations identified.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which of these are true disease-causing mutations?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Mutations in known disease genes for the patients' phenotypes are likely causative.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Correlate candidate mutations with patient phenotypes; identify known and novel mutations.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Identified 7 disease-causing mutations in 6 genes: 6 known (SPG11 insT, ATP7B 2810detT, HBB delTTCT, HBB IVS2+654C>T) and 2 novel (STS G367R, FBN1 C59R), plus DMD exon 1 deletion detected by read depth.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate mutations with orthogonal methods.

evidence.quote_or_span: the median read depth for the first exon of the DMD gene was 0 in P7, whereas the median depth for first exon of the DMD gene in the proband's mother (C3) was 334 fold
A homozygous substitution mutation (c.1099G>A; p.G367R) was found in exon 8 of the STS gene
A substitution mutation (c.175T>C, p.C59R) was found in exon 3 of the FBN1 gene
SIFT prediction demonstrated that these two mutations were in fact damaging mutations

In the two patients suffering from $\beta$ -thalassemia, we identified two different mutations in the $\beta$ -subunit of the hemoglobin gene (HBB, MIM# 141900): 126\_129delCTTT (269 variants and 421 wild types reads) in patient P5 and IVS2+654C>T (130 of 255 reads) in patient P6 [23,24].
Out of the 79 total candidate mutations, 7 mutations occurred in 6 disease-causing genes

evolution_trajectory.state_before: 7 candidate disease mutations identified.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to confirm mutations are not artifacts.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Sanger sequencing and real-time PCR will confirm the mutations.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Validate all 7 mutations by Sanger sequencing; validate DMD deletion by real-time PCR.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Sanger sequencing confirmed all 7 mutations with 100% concordance; real-time PCR confirmed DMD exon 1 deletion (P7 RA~0, mother C3 RA~0.444).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Method validated; assess limitations.

evidence.quote_or_span: the relative amplification level (RA level) was almost zero (0.032%) in P7, while the RA level was 444 (44.4%) in C2 when the value of C2 was normalized to 1000 (100%)
There were significant differences between C2 and P7 as well as between C3 and P7 (p<0.001)
The results showed complete consistency between the two methods
Figure 6. Verification of identified disease-causing mutations by the classical Sanger sequencing method.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: 7 disease mutations validated; 72 additional candidate mutations remain.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Why are there many candidate mutations not linked to disease?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Incomplete polymorphism database leads to false positives.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Analyze remaining candidate mutations; assess database completeness.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 72 additional candidate mutations in 49 other genes suggest incomplete polymorphism database; average 13.1 potential mutations per patient.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Highlight limitation for future improvement.

evidence.quote_or_span: there were an additional 72 candidate mutations in 49 genes unrelated to the six diseases
72 potential mutations in 43 genes were identified in the same seven specimens by our method
our annotated genetic polymorphism database does not present the highest possible level of representativeness and comprehensiveness
Each patient carried 10 to 17 (average 13.1) potential mutations that were not in the seven disease-causing genes according to the OMIM database.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: High overall coverage, but some exons may have low coverage.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: GC content may cause coverage bias.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Exons with extreme GC content will have reduced coverage due to PCR amplification bias.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Analyze coverage vs. GC content; identify poorly covered exons.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 36% of exons with GC>75% or <25% had depth <30×; only 14% of exons with GC>80% captured well; 29 exons consistently low coverage. Likely due to PCR amplification efficiency.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Note limitation for future optimization.

evidence.quote_or_span: When the GC content was greater than 80%, only 14% of the exons could be successfully captured and/or sequenced to a median depth of greater than 30 fold
ike behavior and decreased whenever the GC content of the exon was too high or low.
Table S4 The list of 29 exons with a median depth of less than 30-fold across the ten samples.
More than 36% of exons with a high GC content ( $>75\%$ ) or low GC content ( $<25\%$ ) presented a sequencing depth less than 30 fold (Figure S2A and S2B).

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Array-based capture method used successfully but with practical drawbacks.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: The solid-phase array hybridization process is time-consuming and inflexible.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Array-based hybridization is slower and less adaptable than solution-based methods.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Note the limitation of array-based hybridization for clinical and optimization purposes.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Hybridization to solid-phase array takes 65-72 hours, slower than solution-based (~24h); array redesign is required for probe changes, hindering optimization.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Acknowledge limitation for future method development.

evidence.quote_or_span: Hybridization to a solid-phase array takes almost three days (65–72 hours) and is a time-consuming process, making array-based hybridization technology slightly slower for clinical diagnoses compared with solution-based approaches, which require approximately 24 hours.
array-based methods are hampered with regard to further optimization due to their technological complexity. If a probe has to be added or changed, the whole array must be synthesized de novo.

success_verification.final_conclusion: Targeted DNA-HiSeq successfully identified 7 disease-causing mutations with high accuracy, but false positives from incomplete polymorphism database, GC bias, and array-based hybridization limitations remain.
limitations: Incomplete polymorphism database leads to false positives (72 additional candidates)
GC content bias reduces coverage in extreme GC exons (36% with depth <30×)
Array-based hybridization is time-consuming (65-72h) and less flexible

source.title: Accelerated enzyme engineering by machine-learning guided cell-free expression

initial_request.research_problem: Enzyme engineering is limited by low-throughput directed evolution that misses epistatic interactions and focuses on single transformations.
initial_request.known_context: Traditional directed evolution methods are low-throughput and often miss epistatic interactions. Selection methods focus on single transformations, limiting the collection of positive and negative sequence-function relationships.
constraints: Screening throughput is limited
Need to capture epistatic interactions
Must handle multiple distinct chemical reactions
quantifiable_goals: Improve activity over wild-type McbA for multiple amide products
Demonstrate ML-guided platform can outperform traditional ISM

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Enzyme engineering is limited by low-throughput directed evolution that misses epistatic interactions and focuses on single transformations.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to rapidly generate and use large datasets of sequence-function relationships for predictive design.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A high-throughput, ML-guided platform integrating cell-free DNA assembly, cell-free gene expression, and functional assays can rapidly map fitness landscapes and optimize enzymes for multiple reactions.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Develop an ML-guided cell-free platform to address the limitations of traditional directed evolution.
evolution_trajectory.observation: The platform concept is proposed to overcome low throughput and capture epistatic interactions.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need to explore the reaction space of McbA to identify target reactions for engineering.

evidence.quote_or_span: Enzyme engineering is limited by the challenge of rapidly generating and using large datasets of sequence-function relationships for predictive design.
screening strategies are generally low throughput, which constrains resampling mutations in iterative site saturation mutagenesis campaigns and can miss epistatic interactions
selection methods for directed evolution focus on “winning” enzymes for a single transformation, which limits the ability to collect positive and negative sequence-function relationships

we develop a machine learning (ML)-guided platform that integrates cell-free DNA assembly, cell-free gene expression, and functional assays to rapidly map fitness landscapes across protein sequence space and optimize enzymes for multiple, distinct chemical reactions.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Hypothesis of ML-guided cell-free platform proposed.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which amidation reactions can McbA catalyze, and which should be targeted for engineering?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: McbA has broad substrate promiscuity that can be exploited for pharmaceutical amide synthesis.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Explore the amidation reaction space of wild-type McbA by evaluating enzymatic substrate promiscuity with 1100 unique reactions.
evolution_trajectory.observation: McbA showed activity on a wide range of substrates, identifying target reactions for engineering.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need to implement a cell-free workflow to rapidly generate mutant libraries for the identified targets.

evidence.quote_or_span: We apply this platform to engineer amide synthetases by evaluating substrate preference for 1217 enzyme variants in 10,953 unique reactions.

Through efforts to build ML models and all ISM rounds, we explored the sequence-function landscape of McbA by assessing 2856 variants of McbA (1217 variants of which were for ML models), 1100 possible amide products, and 12,584 substrate pair-mutant reactions.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Target reactions identified from McbA substrate promiscuity screen.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to rapidly generate sequence-defined protein libraries for McbA without laborious cloning?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Cell-free DNA assembly and cell-free gene expression can streamline mutant library generation.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Implement a cell-free protein synthesis workflow to rapidly generate sequence-defined protein libraries for McbA.

tool_or_method.name: Cell-free DNA assembly and gene expression

evolution_trajectory.observation: Cell-free workflow successfully generated McbA mutant libraries, avoiding laborious cloning steps.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: With the library generation method in place, conduct a hot spot screen of single mutants for initial target reactions.
