evolution_trajectory.state_before: Cell-free workflow established for mutant library generation.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which single mutations are beneficial for the target reactions moclobemide, metoclopramide, and cinchocaine?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A hot spot screen of single mutants across key residues will identify beneficial mutations.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Conduct hot spot screen (HSS) of 1216 single mutants across 64 residues for three target reactions.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Multiple residues with positive impact on each target reaction were identified.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: In parallel, run traditional ISM campaigns for benchmarking and to generate higher-order mutants.

evidence.quote_or_span: Our HSS of these residues (64 residues × 19 amino acids = 1216 total single mutants) revealed multiple residues that had a positive impact on moclobemide (Fig.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: HSS data collected for three target reactions.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How do traditional ISM campaigns perform on these targets, and can they provide higher-order mutants for benchmarking?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: ISM can iteratively accumulate beneficial mutations to generate improved variants.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Run traditional iterative saturation mutagenesis (ISM) campaigns for moclobemide, metoclopramide, and cinchocaine.
evolution_trajectory.observation: ISM campaigns for moclobemide and metoclopramide yielded higher-order mutants with improved activity, but cinchocaine campaign failed to find beneficial mutations beyond a double mutant.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: The failure for cinchocaine motivates the need for ML models to capture epistatic interactions.

evidence.quote_or_span: In parallel, we conducted a more traditional directed evolution campaign on each amide product (moclobemide, metoclopramide, and cinchocaine) via iterative saturation mutagenesis (ISM).
This would provide valuable higher order mutations to validate and benchmark model performance given our objective of extrapolating from single to higher order mutations.
The campaign for cinchocaine was more difficult to navigate and we failed to observe beneficial mutations beyond a double mutant, despite taking multiple ISM paths (Fig. S14).
This result (i.e., running into dead ends during ISM) supported the need to include ML models in our framework that might capture epistatic interactions.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: ISM failure for cinchocaine and availability of HSS single mutant data.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to predict higher-order mutants that capture epistatic interactions using only single mutant data?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Augmented ridge regression models using single mutant data and zero-shot fitness predictions can extrapolate to higher-order mutants.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Design augmented ridge regression ML models with variant features concatenating site-specific amino acid encodings and zero-shot fitness predictions.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Model architecture designed to leverage single mutant data for predicting higher-order mutants.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Train and evaluate the models retrospectively on moclobemide and metoclopramide data to select the best configuration.

evidence.quote_or_span: The key idea was to use single mutant data from the HSS to extrapolate higher order mutants with increased activity (Fig. 3A).

To achieve this goal, we chose to fit augmented ridge regression models with our data—as such models are simple and have been previously shown to outperform more sophisticated models for protein engineering
McbA variant feature representations consisted of site-specific amino acid encodings concatenated with a zero-shot fitness prediction
Ridge regression models were augmented following the code accompanying the elegant work of Hsu et al.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Augmented ridge regression models designed.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which zero-shot predictor and encoding strategy yields the best predictive performance?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: EVmutation with Georgiev encodings will provide the best augmentation for ridge regression.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Train and evaluate ML models retrospectively on moclobemide and metoclopramide data, using NDCG and Spearman correlation to select best zero-shot predictor (EVmutation) and encoding (Georgiev).
evolution_trajectory.observation: Augmented models outperformed ridge regression alone; EVmutation with Georgiev encodings selected as best configuration.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Analyze whether full site saturation data (n=77) provides more predictive power than reduced libraries.

evidence.quote_or_span: Training and hyperparameter tuning were performed using single mutant data (n=77) from the HSS (top four hot spots; Fig. 3B, C).
The augmented models outperformed the ridge regression model alone when evaluated with NDCG.
Model evaluation and selection were first performed retrospectively by using the assay-labeled datasets from our moclobemide and metoclopramide engineering campaigns.
Spearman correlation coefficient and NDCG were used to select the best zero-shot predictor and encoding strategy, with a preference given to NDCG.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Best model configuration selected (EVmutation + Georgiev).
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Does using the full site saturation dataset (n=77) improve predictive power compared to reduced libraries?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Full site saturation data provides more predictive power than reduced libraries.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Compare model performance using full site saturation dataset vs. reduced libraries; confirm augmented models outperform ridge regression alone.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Full site saturation dataset (n=77) provides more predictive power; augmented models outperform ridge regression alone.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Proceed to experimentally test top ML predictions for the three target reactions.

evidence.quote_or_span: this analysis indicated that utilizing all the data gathered from the site saturation dataset provides more predictive power
we decided to use the site saturation dataset and the augmented EVmutation model with Georgiev encodings given the strong predictive performance
Training and hyperparameter tuning were performed using single mutant data (n=77) from the HSS (top four hot spots; Fig. 3B, C).

This can likely be attributed to the nature of the rich datasets mostly containing mutants with non-zero activity (64/77 for moclobemide and 62/77 for metoclopramide), preventing “holes” in training sets $^{14}$ .

evolution_trajectory.state_before: ML model trained and validated retrospectively.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can the ML model predict higher-order mutants with improved activity for moclobemide, metoclopramide, and cinchocaine?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Top 25 ML predictions will be enriched in high-activity variants, some surpassing ISM quadruple mutants.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Experimentally test top 25 ML predictions for moclobemide, metoclopramide, and cinchocaine.
evolution_trajectory.observation: ML-predicted variants enriched in high activity; some surpassed ISM quadruple mutants.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Analyze specific mutants to understand how ML outperforms ISM.

evidence.quote_or_span: the augmented ML model was able to predict McbA variants enriched in high activity for each amide product when tested experimentally, some even surpassing qm-McbA from both moclobemide and metoclopramide ISM campaigns

3 mM NADH, 50 mM 4-chlorobenzoic acid, 15 U/mL pyruvate kinase and lactate dehydrogenase enzyme mix (Sigma-Aldrich P0294), 25 U/mL myokinase (Sigma-Aldrich 475941), and various concentrations (50–200 μg/mL) of the studied McbA variant.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Experimental validation of ML predictions completed.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Why did ML predictions outperform ISM in some cases?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: ML models can identify superior mutants overlooked by ISM and avoid path dependencies.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Analyze specific mutants (e.g., A424S for metoclopramide, A205L for cinchocaine) to understand ML advantages.
evolution_trajectory.observation: ML model identified superior mutants overlooked by ISM (e.g., A424S for metoclopramide) and avoided path dependencies (e.g., A205L for cinchocaine).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Apply the ML-guided framework to six additional pharmaceutical compounds to test generalization.

evidence.quote_or_span: the best predicted mutant for metoclopramide contained a mutation (A424S) that was superseded in the HSS by a more active mutation (A424T) carried forward in ISM, indicating the model found a superior mutant that would have been overlooked using ISM alone

The best predicted variant for cinchocaine had significantly higher activity than the best single mutation on its own and surprisingly contained a mutation (A205L) that decreased activity compared to wt-McbA in the HSS

evolution_trajectory.state_before: ML-guided framework validated on initial three targets.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Does the ML-guided framework generalize to other pharmaceutical compounds?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: The framework can be applied to new targets by selecting hot spots, training ML models, and testing top predictions.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Apply ML-guided framework to six additional pharmaceutical compounds: select four hot spots, train ML model, test top 24 predictions, and compare best variant to wild-type.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Best predicted mutant always outperformed best rational design, with 1.6- to 34-fold improvement over wild-type.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Identify limitations of the approach, including screening bottlenecks and low signal-to-noise issues.

evidence.quote_or_span: For each compound, the best predicted mutant always outperformed the best rational design
select four hot spots, and train our ML model to predict higher order mutants with increased activity
For each reaction, the top 24 predictions were tested, and the best variant was expressed, purified, and compared to wt-McbA activity.
We observed increases in yields ranging from 1.6-fold to 34-fold over wt-McbA for the six compounds we tested

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Framework successfully applied to six additional compounds.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: What are the limitations of the current approach?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Screening remains a bottleneck, and low signal-to-noise in HSS can lead to flat fitness landscapes that are difficult to model.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Acknowledge limitations: screening bottleneck (LC-MS throughput may not suit all enzymes), and some mutants show modest improvements due to low signal-to-noise in HSS.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Screening remains a bottleneck; LC-MS provides manageable solution but may not suit all enzymes. Some mutants show modest improvements due to low signal-to-noise in HSS.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: These limitations inform future improvements to the platform.

evidence.quote_or_span: Some mutants gave only modest improvements, which may be an artifact of low signal-to-noise in the hot spot screens for some of the target compounds that were only detectable by MS. This can lead to flat fitness landscapes that are more difficult to model.
experimental screening methods for biocatalytic reactions remain a bottleneck
LC-MS provided a manageable solution, as has been found in other examples

validation_methods: Experimental testing of top ML predictions
Application to six additional pharmaceutical compounds
success_verification.final_conclusion: The ML-guided cell-free platform successfully engineered McbA variants with improved activity for multiple amide products, outperforming traditional ISM in several cases and demonstrating generalization to new targets.
limitations: Screening remains a bottleneck; LC-MS throughput may not suit all enzymes
Low signal-to-noise in HSS can lead to flat fitness landscapes and modest improvements

source.title: Rapid response to fast viral evolution using AlphaFold 3-assisted topological deep learning

initial_request.research_problem: Fast viral evolution outpaces experimental DMS and 3D structure determination, hindering timely forecasting of dominant variants.
initial_request.target_object: SARS-CoV-2 RBD-ACE2 binding free energy changes upon mutation
initial_request.known_context: Experimental DMS and 3D structures are bottlenecks for rapid response to viral evolution. The earliest experimental DMS on SARS-CoV-2 RBD mutations was not available until September 2020, nine months after the outbreak.
constraints: High-quality 3D structures are required for TDL-based predictions, but are often delayed during pandemics.
input_data: Four experimental SARS-CoV-2 RBD-ACE2 DMS datasets (6M0J-RBD-1, 6M0J-RBD-2, 7T9L-RBD, 7XB0-RBD)
quantifiable_goals: Maintain predictive performance (PCC, RMSE) when substituting experimental structures with AF3-predicted structures
Extend to new variants (e.g., HK.3) with acceptable accuracy

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Experimental DMS and 3D structures are bottlenecks for rapid response to viral evolution.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can AF3-predicted structures substitute experimental structures for TDL-based DMS prediction without significant performance loss?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: AF3-predicted structures can substitute experimental structures for TDL-based DMS prediction.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Propose an AF3-assisted multi-task topological Laplacian (MT-TopLap) strategy to bypass experimental structure determination.
evolution_trajectory.observation: AF3 can predict 3D structures of RBD-ACE2 complexes, enabling feature extraction without experimental structures.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Design the MT-TopLap model integrating features from AF3 structures.

evidence.quote_or_span: the DMS experiments are typically a bottleneck in response to fast evolving viruses
the earliest experimental DMS on the SARS-CoV-2 spike protein RBD mutations was not available until September 2020, nine months after the outbreak
We propose an AlphaFold 3 (AF3)-assisted multi-task topological Laplacian (MT-TopLap) strategy to address this need.

we leverage AF3's predicted structures to extract persistent Laplacian features. As such, we can speed up in-silico DMS predictions by bypassing the experimental determination of PPI complex structures.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Hypothesis formulated; need a model that can use AF3 structures for DMS prediction.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to integrate topological, auxiliary, and sequence features for multi-task learning of BFE changes?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Integrating persistent Laplacian, auxiliary, and pre-trained transformer features in a multi-task deep learning framework will accurately predict BFE changes upon mutation.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Design MT-TopLap with persistent Laplacian features from AF3 structures, auxiliary features, and ESM-2 transformer embeddings, trained on SKEMPI 2.0 with multi-task learning.

parameters.features: persistent Laplacian (0D: 1872, higher-D: 420), auxiliary, ESM-2 transformer

evolution_trajectory.observation: Model architecture defined; features extracted from AF3-generated complexes.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate the model on four experimental DMS datasets.

evidence.quote_or_span: MT-TopLap integrates persistent Laplacian, auxiliary, and pre-trained transformer features to predict BFE changes upon RBD DMS mutations.

SKEMPI 2.0 is used as training data with a multi-task deep learning approach that simultaneously learns single-site mutational patterns with experimental BFE changes and DMS profiles with experimental enrichment ratios.

the AF3 generated 3D SARS-CoV-2 RBD-ACE2 complex is used to generate persistent Laplacian features and auxiliary features. Persistent Laplacian features provide a topological representation, using element-and site-specific atom sets to simplify the structural complexity of protein–protein complexes and encode vital biological information into topological invariants.

The ESM-2 pre-trained transformer is used to convert amino acid sequence of the PPI into a pre-trained transformer embedding. All these features are concatenated and fed into MT-TopLap to predict the BFE change upon mutation.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: MT-TopLap designed; need to test on SARS-CoV-2 DMS data.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How does AF3-assisted MT-TopLap perform compared to using experimental structures on multiple DMS datasets?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: AF3-assisted MT-TopLap will maintain performance close to that using experimental structures.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Validate on four experimental RBD-ACE2 DMS datasets (6M0J-RBD-1, 6M0J-RBD-2, 7T9L-RBD, 7XB0-RBD) using both AF3 and PDB structures with 10-fold cross-validation.
evolution_trajectory.observation: AF3-assisted MT-TopLap achieved PCC and RMSE values close to those using PDB structures across all datasets.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Quantify the performance difference between AF3 and experimental structures.

evidence.quote_or_span: We validate our AF3-assisted MT-TopLap approach with four experimental RBD-ACE2 DMS datasets of SARS-CoV-2 and its variants

we first collected four experimental RBD-ACE2 DMS datasets, each paired with an experimental 3D SARS-CoV-2 RBD-ACE2 complex structure. Meanwhile, we also create alternative AF3 3D RBD-ACE2 complex structures from the AlphaFold 3 Server.

we perform dataset-level 10-fold cross-validation on four experimental SARS-CoV-2 RBD-ACE2 datasets. The learning rate is set to 0.0001 and 500 epochs are used for the pre-training while 200 epochs are used for the validation step.
The four experimental DMS datasets comprise of two datasets from the original SARS-CoV-2 RBD-ACE2 DMSs (6M0J-RBD-1, 6M0J-RBD-2) [7, 45], and datasets from the BA.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Validation completed; need to assess the impact of using AF3 structures.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: What is the quantitative performance drop when using AF3 structures instead of experimental ones?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: The performance drop will be minimal, justifying the use of AF3 structures for rapid response.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Compute average PCC decrease and RMSE increase across the four datasets.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Average PCC decreased by 1.1% and RMSE increased by 9.3% compared to using PDB structures.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Highlight best-performing dataset and consider fine-tuning for new variants.

evidence.quote_or_span: AF3-assisted MT-TopLap maintains strong performance in predicting BFE changes upon DMS mutations, with an average decrease of only 1.1% in Pearson correlation coefficient (PCC) and an average increase of only 9.3% in RMSE, compared to the use of experimental RBD-ACE2 structures.
AF3-assisted MT-TopLap only displayed an average decrease of 1.1% in PCC when compared to using Protein Data Bank (PDB) structures.
the four SARS-CoV-2 RBD-ACE2 datasets displayed an average of 9.3% increase in RMSE when validated with AF3-assisted MT-TopLap.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Overall performance quantified; best dataset identified.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which dataset shows the best performance with AF3 structures?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: The 7XB0-RBD dataset will yield the highest PCC and lowest RMSE.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Report best performance on 7XB0-RBD with AF3 structures.
evolution_trajectory.observation: PCC of 0.968 and RMSE of 0.338 kcal/mol, only 0.6% lower PCC than using PDB structures.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Extend to new variant HK.3 via transfer learning.

evidence.quote_or_span: AF3-assisted MT-TopLap also achieved the best performance in the validation test for 7XB0-RBD with a PCC of 0.968, only 0.6% lower than the performance using PDB complexes.
AF3-assisted MT-TopLap's predicted BFE changes for 7XB0-RBD obtained the lowest RMSE of 0.338 kcal/mol

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Model validated on existing variants; need to adapt to emerging variant HK.3.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can AF3-assisted MT-TopLap be fine-tuned with limited experimental DMS data for a new variant?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Transfer learning with latest experimental DMS data will enable accurate prediction for HK.3.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Use transfer learning to fine-tune AF3-assisted MT-TopLap with HK.3 DMS data and AF3-predicted structure.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Model updated with HK.3 data; ready for validation.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate fine-tuned model on HK.3 DMS.

evidence.quote_or_span: To enhance AF3-assisted MT-TopLap's capabilities using experimental DMS data, we use transfer learning to update AF3-assisted MT-TopLap by training it with latest experimental DMS data.
we use the AlphaFold Server to predict the 3D structure of HK.3 variant RBD-ACE2 complex.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Fine-tuned model for HK.3; need to assess prediction accuracy.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How well does the fine-tuned model predict BFE changes for HK.3 mutations?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: The fine-tuned model will achieve acceptable accuracy.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform 10-fold cross-validation on HK.3 DMS data.

parameters.pre-training: SARS-CoV-2 RBD-ACE2 datasets and S8338 dataset

evolution_trajectory.observation: PCC of 0.81 and RMSE of 1.10 achieved.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Analyze predictions by structural regions and mutation types.

evidence.quote_or_span: AF3-assisted MT-TopLap achieves a PCC of 0.81 and RMSE of 1.10 for this fine-tuning validation.
The predicted BFE changes are obtained by performing a 10-fold cross-validation after MT-TopLap has been pre-trained with the SARS-CoV-2 RBD-ACE2 datasets and the S8338 dataset.
The patterns of the predicted DMS is observed to resemble the patterns of the converted BFE changes with a PCC of 0.81 and an RMSE of 1.10.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: HK.3 validation completed; further analysis of predictions.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How do predictions vary across structural regions and mutation types?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Predictions will be more accurate for binding interface mutations and will align with experimental patterns across amino acid types.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Analyze BFE changes by structural regions (support, core, rim) and by mutation types (20 amino acid types, charged, polar, hydrophobic).
evolution_trajectory.observation: High PCCs for binding interface regions (0.947, 0.943, 0.931); predicted BFE changes by mutation types align with experimental data, though predicted variance is lower.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Track variant evolution using predicted BFE changes.

evidence.quote_or_span: The combined prediction results for different residue region types for four AF3-assisted SARS-CoV-2 RBD-ACE2 datasets according to Fig. 1(a) with PCCs of 0.947, 0.943, and 0.931 for the binding interfaces: support, core, and rim respectively.
the variance of the predicted BFE changes are generally lower than the variance of the experimental values

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Regional and mutation type analysis done; apply to variant tracking.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can predicted BFE changes explain the emergence of new variants like KP.3?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Predicted BFE changes will reflect mutational effects that drive variant dominance.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Analyze BFE changes for key mutations in KP.3 (Q493E, L455S, F456L).
evolution_trajectory.observation: Q493E alone decreases BFE, but combined with L455S and F456L reverses effect, increasing BFE by 0.455 kcal/mol.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Conclude overall validation and limitations.

evidence.quote_or_span: in the current KP.3 variant, when Q493E is combined with L455S and F456L mutations, the effect has reversed thus promoting an increase in BFE changes
This is consistent with Figure 4 where a positive BFE change of $0.455\mathrm{kcal / mol}$ due to Q493E mutation led to the KP.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: All analyses completed; need to summarize overall performance and limitations.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Does AF3-assisted MT-TopLap provide a robust and rapid alternative to experimental structure-based prediction?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: AF3-assisted MT-TopLap achieves robust performance with minimal deviation from experimental structures and extends to new variants.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Conclude that AF3-assisted MT-TopLap is effective, but note the limitation of requiring high-quality structures.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Robust performance on four DMS datasets (avg. 1.1% PCC drop, 9.3% RMSE increase) and successful extension to HK.3 (PCC 0.81). Limitation: still requires 3D structures, which may be delayed.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Finalize case.

evidence.quote_or_span: With 3D PPI complexes generated by AF3, AF3-assisted MT-TopLap achieves robust predictive performance, showing minimal deviation from results obtained using high-quality experimental 3D structures.
Our model's success extends to new variants, including the SARS-CoV-2 HK.3 variant, where it achieved a Pearson correlation coefficient (PCC) of 0.81.
MT-TopLap requires the 3D structures of new viral variants to achieve accurate predictions. Such structures are typically not available until many months later during pandemics.

AF3-assisted MT-TopLap maintains strong performance in predicting BFE changes upon DMS mutations, with an average decrease of only 1.1% in Pearson correlation coefficient (PCC) and an average increase of only 9.3% in RMSE, compared to the use of experimental RBD-ACE2 structures.

validation_methods: 10-fold cross-validation on four experimental DMS datasets
Comparison of AF3-assisted vs. PDB structure-based predictions
Transfer learning and validation on HK.3 variant
success_verification.final_conclusion: AF3-assisted MT-TopLap maintains robust predictive performance with only a 1.1% average PCC decrease and 9.3% RMSE increase compared to experimental structures, and successfully extends to the HK.3 variant with PCC 0.81, demonstrating its potential for rapid response to viral evolution.
limitations: TDL requires high-quality 3D structures, which may still be delayed during pandemics.
Predicted BFE changes have lower variance than experimental values.
Performance on HK.3 (PCC 0.81) is lower than on training variants.

source.title: Multiple factors based adaptive laboratory evolution strategy enhances DHA production in Aurantiochytrium sp. PKU#Mn16 by rewiring key metabolic pathways

initial_request.research_problem: Wild-type Aurantiochytrium sp. PKU#Mn16 has low DHA yield due to poor adaptation to low pH during fermentation.
initial_request.known_context: Wild-type strains isolated from natural environments typically fail to meet commercial demands for DHA yields, partly because they are poorly adapted to the decreased pH conditions encountered during fermentation.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Wild-type Aurantiochytrium sp. PKU#Mn16 fails to meet commercial DHA yields due to poor adaptation to low pH during fermentation.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Unknown whether multi-factor acclimation (low pH, low temperature, high DO) can synergistically enhance DHA production through complex metabolic rewiring.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Multi-factor acclimation (low pH, low temperature, high DO) will synergistically enhance DHA production through complex metabolic rewiring.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Proceed with multi-factor ALE design.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Hypothesis formulated that multi-factor acclimation for DHA improvements would involve complex metabolic rewiring.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Design orthogonal experimental ALE conditions to test hypothesis.

evidence.quote_or_span: wild-type strains isolated from natural environments typically fail to meet commercial demands for DHA yields, partly because they are poorly adapted to the decreased pH conditions encountered during fermentation.
we hypothesize multi-factor acclimation for DHA improvements would involve complex metabolic rewiring that awaits dissection.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: 12 ALE conditions designed.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to implement ALE process to generate adapted strains.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Staged acidic ALE process with pH decreasing from 5.5 to 3.5 over 75 passages will yield acid-tolerant strains.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Execute ALE process over 150 days.
evolution_trajectory.observation: ALE process completed, yielding 12 endpoint strains.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Characterize endpoint strains for biomass, TFA, and DHA.

evidence.quote_or_span: The entire ALE process involves 75 passages over a period of 150 days.

we used orthogonal experimental design to combine two levels of temperature (16 °C and 28 °C), two levels of DO indicated by the shaking speed (170 rpm and 230 rpm), and three acid types (citric acid, acetic acid, and hydrochloric acid) to obtain 12 combinations of ALE conditions

evolution_trajectory.state_before: 12 ALE endpoint strains obtained.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to measure DCW, TFA, DHA under standard conditions.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: ALE strains will show altered biomass and DHA production compared to wild-type.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Culture wild-type and 12 ALE strains under standard conditions for 72 h.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Biomass, TFA, and DHA measured for all strains.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Analyze effect of acid type on biomass.

evidence.quote_or_span: the wild-type strain and 12 ALE endpoint strains (Additional file 1: Table S1) were cultured under standard fermentation conditions (28 °C, 170 rpm, M4 medium) in triplicate. After 72 h of cultivation, these three indicators were measured.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Biomass data collected for all strains.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Determine which acid type yields highest biomass.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Citric acid acclimated strains will show higher biomass due to organic acid tolerance.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Compare biomass across acid types.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Citric acid acclimated strains showed higher biomass than other acids, indicating organic acid tolerance role.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Evaluate effect of additional stresses (low temperature, high DO) on biomass.

evidence.quote_or_span: Strains acclimated with citric acid generally showed higher biomass than those acclimated with other acids
This indicates that adaptability to specific acid types, rather than just a low pH value, contributes to improving biomass yield, suggesting a role of organic acid tolerance and/or remobilization

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Citric acid strains show highest biomass.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Determine if low temperature or high DO further increase biomass.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Low temperature or high DO alone will not further increase biomass beyond citric acid alone.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Compare biomass of citric acid strains with and without additional stresses.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Low temperature or high DO alone did not further increase biomass beyond citric acid alone.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Check TFA levels under these conditions.

evidence.quote_or_span: Compared with using citric acid alone as the acclimation condition, the addition of low-temperature or high-DO stress alone cannot further enhance the biomass accumulation ability.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Low temperature/high DO did not increase biomass.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Determine if these stresses affect TFA.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Low temperature and high DO increase TFA despite no biomass increase.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Analyze TFA data for strains with additional stresses.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Low temperature and high DO increased TFA but not biomass, confirming they enhance fatty acid production via cellular physiology.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Examine DHA/TFA fraction.

evidence.quote_or_span: applying low-temperature or high-DO stress alone significantly increased the TFA concentration in the ALE strain (Fig. 1A). This outcome starkly contrasts with the previous biomass results, further confirming that low-temperature and high-DO conditions enhance fatty acid production by maintaining normal cellular physiology and energy metabolism

evolution_trajectory.state_before: TFA increased by low temperature/high DO.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Determine effect on DHA/TFA fraction.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: DHA/TFA fraction may decrease in most ALE strains.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Calculate DHA/TFA fraction for all strains.
evolution_trajectory.observation: DHA/TFA fraction decreased in most ALE strains; only citric and acetic acid at normal conditions increased DHA fraction (42.3% wild-type, 47.0% citric, 51.6% acetic).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Select best strain for transcriptomic analysis.

evidence.quote_or_span: the DHA/TFA fraction in most ALE strains decreased compared to that in the wild-type strain (42.3%), and increased in only two strains that were obtained through the citric acid (47.0%) and acetic acid (51.6%) acclimation at the normal temperature and oxygen level

evolution_trajectory.state_before: ALE_AC_11 identified as optimal strain.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to understand transcriptional changes underlying improved DHA production.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Transcriptomic analysis of ALE_AC_11 vs wild-type at 36 h and 48 h will reveal key metabolic rewiring.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform RNA-seq on ALE_AC_11 and wild-type at two time points.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 23,118 DEGs identified at 36 h.

source.title: Transcriptome analysis and reverse engineering verification of SNZ3Val125Ile and Pho3Asn134Asp revealed the mechanism of adaptive laboratory evolution to increase the yield of tyrosol in Saccharomyces cerevisiae strain S26-AE2

initial_request.research_problem: Excessive metabolic engineering in S. cerevisiae S26 increased tyrosol but impaired growth, limiting industrial applicability. The goal was to restore growth and further improve tyrosol yield via adaptive laboratory evolution (ALE), then identify and validate the underlying genetic and transcriptomic mechanisms.
initial_request.known_context: S26 is an engineered strain with high tyrosol production but reduced growth rate.
constraints: Industrial applicability requires both high tyrosol yield and robust growth.
quantifiable_goals: Restore growth rate
Increase tyrosol yield beyond current levels

evolution_trajectory.state_before: S. cerevisiae S26, engineered for high tyrosol, suffers from impaired growth, limiting industrial use.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can adaptive laboratory evolution (ALE) restore growth and further improve tyrosol production in S26?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: ALE can restore growth and further improve tyrosol production in S26.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Proceed with ALE experiment.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Hypothesis formulated based on known potential of ALE to improve microbial traits.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Design ALE protocol to test hypothesis.

evidence.quote_or_span: Although excessive metabolic modification increases the production of tyrosol, it also causes a decrease in the growth rate of yeast.
this study attempted to restore the growth of S. cerevisiae through adaptive evolution and further improve tyrosol production.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Hypothesis that ALE can improve growth and tyrosol production in S26.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to design an ALE protocol that applies selective pressure for improved growth on glucose.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Stepwise increase in glucose concentration will select for mutants with enhanced glucose utilization and growth.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Implement ALE with automatic passaging at OD600=15 and stepwise glucose increase (20→40→80→125 g/L).
evolution_trajectory.observation: Protocol designed to gradually increase glucose concentration over three stages.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Execute ALE experiment.

evidence.quote_or_span: The initial glucose concentration of the evolution medium was 20 g/L, and the amount of added glucose gradually increased during the process of evolution.

In stage 2, the glucose concentration was increased from 40 to 80 g/L. A total of 20 passages were conducted in this stage, and strain S26-AE2 was obtained. The strain was passaged once every 11.75 h on average
In stage 3, the glucose content increased from 80 to 125 g/L, and the strain was passaged once every 11.95 h on average. The evolutionary strain in stage 3 was named S26-AE3.
In stage 1, the glucose concentration was increased to 40 g/L, and the strain was named S26-AE1. At this stage, the average passage time was 16 h.

SNZ3 contained mutations at amino acid residues 118, 122, and 125; synonymous mutations were identified at amino acids 118 and 122, and a nonsynonymous mutation was identified at amino acid residues 125.

As shown in Table 1, SNZ3 contained mutations at amino acid residues 118, 122, and 125; synonymous mutations were identified at amino acids 118 and 122, and a nonsynonymous mutation was identified at amino acid residues 125.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: ALE protocol designed with stepwise glucose increase.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Will the ALE protocol yield evolved strains with improved growth and tyrosol production?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Evolved strains will exhibit shorter passage times and improved fermentation performance.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform ALE in three stages, isolating strains S26-AE1, S26-AE2, S26-AE3.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Three evolved strains obtained with decreasing passage times: S26-AE1 avg 16 h, S26-AE2 avg 11.75 h, S26-AE3 avg 11.95 h.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Evaluate fermentation performance of evolved strains.

evidence.quote_or_span: In stage 1, the glucose concentration was increased to 40 g/L, and the strain was named S26-AE1. At this stage, the average passage time was 16 h.

In stage 2, the glucose concentration was increased from 40 to 80 g/L. A total of 20 passages were conducted in this stage, and strain S26-AE2 was obtained. The strain was passaged once every 11.75 h on average
In stage 3, the glucose content increased from 80 to 125 g/L, and the strain was passaged once every 11.95 h on average. The evolutionary strain in stage 3 was named S26-AE3.
After the adaptive laboratory evolution of S. cerevisiae S26, three evolutionary strains were obtained.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Evolved strains S26-AE1, S26-AE2, S26-AE3 isolated.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which evolved strain shows the best improvement in growth and tyrosol production?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: S26-AE2 will exhibit the highest biomass and tyrosol yield due to adaptation to 80 g/L glucose.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform fermentation in 100 g/L glucose and compare biomass and tyrosol production.
evolution_trajectory.observation: S26-AE2 achieved 17.82 g DCW/L biomass (15.33% higher than S26) and 817.83 mg/L tyrosol (69.20% higher than S26-AE1).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Select S26-AE2 for transcriptome analysis to understand mechanisms.

evidence.quote_or_span: the biomass of strain S26-AE2 was 17.82 g DCW/L at 48 h, which was 15.33% higher than that of strain S26.
the tyrosol production of S26-AE2 improved significantly, especially at 48 h, and its tyrosol production reached 817.83 mg/L, which was 69.20% higher than the highest yield of strain S26-AE1.
The biomass of strain S26-AE2 reached 17.82 g DCW/L in the presence of 100 g/L glucose, which was 15.33% higher than that of S26, and its tyrosol production reached 817.83 mg/L.
In stage 3, the glucose content increased from 80 to 125 g/L, and the strain was passaged once every 11.95 h on average. The evolutionary strain in stage 3 was named S26-AE3.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: S26-AE2 identified as best performer in fermentation.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: What transcriptomic changes underlie the improved growth and tyrosol production in S26-AE2?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Transcriptome analysis will reveal upregulation of central metabolism and downregulation of glucose repression.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform RNA-seq on S26-AE2 vs S26 under 20 g/L and 100 g/L glucose.
evolution_trajectory.observation: In 20 g/L glucose: 36 upregulated, 1 downregulated gene. In 100 g/L glucose: 9 upregulated, 16 downregulated genes. Notably, HXK2 expression decreased under 100 g/L glucose, suggesting reduced glucose repression. Pyruvate synthesis genes (PGK1, GPM1, GPM2, ENO1/ENO2, PYK1) and TCA cycle genes were upregulated.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Identify causal mutations via SNP analysis.

evidence.quote_or_span: Compared with S26, S26-AE2 presented 36 upregulated genes and 1 downregulated gene in the presence of 20 g/L glucose
Compared with the original strain S26, the evolutionary strain S26-AE2 had 9 upregulated genes and 16 downregulated genes in the presence of 100 g/L glucose
The transcriptome analysis revealed that, upon exposure to 100 g/L glucose, the S26-AE2 strain may reduce the transcriptional regulation of glucose repression through decreased HXK2 expression.
we speculated that after adaptive evolution, the decreased expression of HXK2 in strain S26-AE2 reduced the transcriptional regulation of glucose repression in cells

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Transcriptome analysis revealed key expression changes.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which genetic mutations are responsible for the evolved phenotype?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: SNP analysis will identify nonsynonymous mutations in genes related to metabolism.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform whole-genome sequencing and SNP calling on S26-AE2.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 19 SNPs identified, including nonsynonymous mutations in SNZ3 (Val125Ile) and PHO3 (Asn134Asp).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Predict functional impact of mutations via molecular docking.

evidence.quote_or_span: The results revealed 19 SNP mutations in S26-AE2-100vsS26-20, S26-AE2-100vsS26-AE2-20, and S26-100vsS26-20.
five genes with SNP mutations, namely, MAL32, SNZ3, DOT6, HXT3, and PHO3

SNZ3 contained mutations at amino acid residues 118, 122, and 125; synonymous mutations were identified at amino acids 118 and 122, and a nonsynonymous mutation was identified at amino acid residues 125.
PHO3 was mutated at residues 132, 134, and 420, and only amino acid residues 420 exhibited a nonsynonymous mutation.

# Transcriptome analysis and reverse engineering verification of SNZ3 $^{Val125Ile}$ and Pho3 $^{Asn134Asp}$ revealed the mechanism of adaptive laboratory evolution to increase the yield of tyrosol in Saccharomyces cerevisiae strain S26-AE2

evolution_trajectory.state_before: SNZ3 Val125Ile and PHO3 Asn134Asp mutations identified.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Do these mutations affect substrate binding affinity?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Mutations improve binding to substrates, enhancing enzyme activity.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform molecular docking of mutant proteins with substrates.
evolution_trajectory.observation: SNZ3 Val125Ile improved binding to D-ribose 5-phosphate (Vina score -5.7). PHO3 Asn134Asp improved binding to thiamine phosphate (Vina score -6.6).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate SNZ3 mutation effect via reverse engineering.

evidence.quote_or_span: After SNZ3 mutation, the Vina score for the interaction with the small-molecule D-ribose 5-phosphate decreased to -5.7
the interaction of the PRO51, PRO363, MET365, and GLY366 residues with small molecules was reduced, and the binding of the ALA348, ILE398, and CYS408 residues was increased

The docking results showed that after the introduction of the $\mathrm{PHO3}^{\mathrm{Asn134Asp}}$ mutation, its Vina score for the interaction with the small-molecule substance thiamine phosphate decreased to $-6.6$ , which indicated that the affinity between the receptor and the ligand improved (Table S3).
Molecular docking and reverse engineering verified the effectiveness of the SNZ3 $^{Val125Ile}$ mutation in increasing tyrosol production.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Molecular docking suggests SNZ3 Val125Ile enhances substrate binding.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Does the SNZ3 Val125Ile mutation alone increase tyrosol production?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Introducing SNZ3 Val125Ile into S26 will increase tyrosol yield.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Construct S26-SNZ3 Mut strain via CRISPR-Cas9 and test tyrosol production.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Under 100 g/L glucose, tyrosol production by SNZ3 mutant increased by 17.01% compared to control S26 at 72 h.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Test double mutant to see additive effects.

evidence.quote_or_span: Under 100 g/L glucose culture, tyrosol production by the SNZ3 mutant increased by 17.01% compared with that of the control strain S26 at 72 h
Reverse engineering demonstrated that a single-nucleotide mutation in SNZ3 could increase the concentration of tyrosol.
Furthermore, the amount of tyrosol produced by strain S26 with the SNZ3 $^{Val125Ile}$ mutation increased by 17.01% compared with that of the control strain S26 following exposure to 100 g/L glucose.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: SNZ3 single mutant validated.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Does combining SNZ3 and PHO3 mutations further improve tyrosol?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Double mutant (SNZ3+PHO3) will show additive or synergistic increase in tyrosol.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Construct S26-PHO3 Mut+SNZ3 Mut and measure tyrosol.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Double mutant increased tyrosol by only 11.37% compared to control S26 at 72 h, less than SNZ3 single mutant.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Analyze limitations and unexplored mutations.

evidence.quote_or_span: the tyrosol production of the SNZ3 and PHO3 comutants increased by 11.37% compared with that of the control strain S26 at 72 h

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Double mutant underperformed; other SNP genes not tested.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: What are the limitations of the current study?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: PHO3 single mutant effect not clearly reported; other SNP genes (MAL32, DOT6, HXT3) not explored; mechanism remains hypothetical without direct enzyme activity measurements.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Acknowledge limitations and suggest future work.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Study validated SNZ3 but PHO3 role unclear; other mutations may contribute; enzyme assays needed.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Conclude study.

evidence.quote_or_span: we detected mutations in the key genes PHO3 and SNZ3
Our molecular docking results revealed that mutations in these genes enhance the interaction between proteins and small molecules.

success_verification.final_conclusion: ALE successfully restored growth and increased tyrosol production in S26-AE2. SNZ3 Val125Ile mutation was validated to increase tyrosol by 17.01%, but double mutant with PHO3 did not show additive effect. Transcriptome and SNP analyses provided mechanistic insights, but direct enzyme activity measurements are lacking.
limitations: PHO3 single mutant effect not clearly reported
Other SNP genes (MAL32, DOT6, HXT3) not explored
Mechanism remains hypothetical without direct enzyme activity measurements

source.title: Combined machine learning models, docking analysis, ADMET studies and molecular dynamics simulations for the design of novel FAK inhibitors against glioblastoma

initial_request.research_problem: Design novel FAK inhibitors for glioblastoma by integrating machine learning, docking, ADMET, and MD simulations to overcome limitations of small-dataset QSAR models.
initial_request.known_context: GBM is highly aggressive with poor prognosis (median survival ≤15 months). FAK is a promising target. Existing QSAR models use small datasets (e.g., 22 compounds) with limited applicability.
constraints: Use only computational methods; no wet-lab experiments.
Models must be built on >1000 data points.
input_data: ChEMBL U-87 MG cell activity dataset
quantifiable_goals: Build ML models with R² > 0.8 for FAK inhibition prediction.
Identify novel FAK inhibitors with pIC50 > 6.0 in both protein and cell models.
Filter candidates through ADMET and MD validation.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: GBM has poor prognosis; FAK is a promising target but existing QSAR models are limited by small datasets.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can a larger dataset ML model enable rapid screening of FAK inhibitors for GBM?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A larger dataset ML model can enable rapid screening of FAK inhibitors for GBM.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Proceed to design an integrated computational framework.
evolution_trajectory.observation: GBM is highly aggressive with poor prognosis; FAK is a promising target; existing QSAR models use small datasets (e.g., 22 compounds) with limited applicability.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Hypothesis motivates design of integrated framework.

evidence.quote_or_span: Gliomas, particularly glioblastoma (GBM), are highly aggressive brain tumors with poor prognosis and high recurrence rates.
the prognosis for GBM patients has seen only marginal improvement over the past few decades. The median survival time remains disappointingly low, typically not exceeding 15 months
One promising target is Focal adhesion kinase (FAK), a key regulator of tumor progression currently in clinical trials for glioma treatment.

the current QSAR models used to predict FAK inhibition activity have relatively small construction datasets, typically comprising only tens to hundreds of compounds, which somewhat limits the widespread applicability of the models.
Wang et al. utilized 22 FAK inhibitors to develop two types of satisfactory 3D-QSAR models

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Hypothesis that larger dataset ML can improve screening.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to integrate ML, docking, ADMET, and MD for FAK inhibitor design?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: An integrated framework of ML models, docking, ADMET, and MD simulations can identify novel FAK inhibitors.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Design framework: build ML models with multiple algorithms and fingerprints, validate docking, screen ChemDiv library, filter by ML and ADMET, validate by MD.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Framework proposed: ML models (LightGBM, RF, SVR, KNN, PLS, LASSO, XGBoost) with PaDEL fingerprints, 80:20 ratio, 10-fold CV; docking with AutoDock Vina; ADMET with ADMETlab 2.0; MD with Gromacs.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Framework design specifies ML model building with multiple algorithms and fingerprints.

evidence.quote_or_span: we propose a comprehensive research framework that integrates machine learning models, docking analysis, ADMET studies, and molecular dynamics simulations to facilitate the design of novel FAK inhibitors against glioblastoma
The regression models leveraged LightGBM, Random Forest, SVR, KNN, PLS, LASSO, and XGBoost algorithms.
The datasets were split into training and independent test sets, maintaining an 80:20 ratio. Ten-fold Cross-validation was implemented during model training

evolution_trajectory.state_before: ML model design with multiple algorithms and fingerprints.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which fingerprint combination yields best predictive performance?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Combined CDK, CDK extended, and substructure count fingerprints will improve model accuracy.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Evaluate fingerprint combinations; select best for FAK model.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Combined CDK, CDK extended, and substructure count fingerprints gave best performance; LightGBM achieved test set R²=0.892, MAE=0.331, RMSE=0.467.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Optimal fingerprint selection yields final FAK model performance.

evidence.quote_or_span: The outcomes indicated that using a single molecular fingerprint resulted in lower predictive performance compared to combined molecular fingerprints, emphasizing the complementarity of combined fingerprints in enhancing the representation of relevant molecular structural information.
The model achieved the highest predictive accuracy when integrating combined CDK, CDK extended, and substructure fingerprint counts.
Although combining all molecular fingerprints could yield similar results, the computational efficiency of utilizing these three fingerprints guided the selection for subsequent model development.
It is noteworthy that the LightGBM algorithm produces the lowest MAE and RMSE values, highlighting its superior predictive accuracy.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: FAK protein model built.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can the same approach build a predictive model for U-87 MG cell activity?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: The same optimal fingerprint combination and LightGBM will yield a good cell-level model.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Build cell-level model for U-87 MG using same fingerprints and LightGBM.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Cell-level model achieved test set R²=0.789, MAE=0.395, RMSE=0.536.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: After building models, docking validation is performed.

evidence.quote_or_span: Table 2 illustrates the evaluation results of the machine learning models using cross-validation on the test set with the LightGBM approach.
The results demonstrate that the highest model performance is attained when CDK, CDK extended fingerprints, and substructure fingerprint counts are employed as input variables.
employing the LightGBM algorithm minimizes MAE and RMSE values, indicating superior predictive performance
These results indicate that molecular fingerprint models based on LightGBM are proficient in predicting the activity ( $pIC_{50}$ ) of compounds against U-87 MG glioblastomas.

source.title: Scanning the active center of formolase to identify key residues for enhanced C1 to C3 bioconversion

source.title: Engineering Bacillus subtilis J46 for efficient utilization of galactose through adaptive laboratory evolution

initial_request.research_problem: Bacillus subtilis J46 grows poorly on galactose (specific growth rate ~0.03 h⁻¹), limiting its use on soybean biomass.
initial_request.known_context: B. subtilis has inherent pathways for galactose metabolism but poor growth on galactose.
constraints: Use adaptive laboratory evolution (ALE) to enhance galactose utilization
Elucidate genetic and metabolic changes
quantifiable_goals: Increase specific growth rate on galactose
Identify causal mutations
Validate in soybean medium

evolution_trajectory.state_before: B. subtilis J46 grows poorly on galactose, limiting its use on soybean biomass.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can adaptive laboratory evolution enhance galactose utilization in B. subtilis?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: ALE can enhance galactose utilization in B. subtilis.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform ALE on B. subtilis J46 in galactose-containing medium.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Study aimed to boost galactose utilization using ALE and elucidate genetic changes.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Design ALE experiment with appropriate medium and conditions.

evidence.quote_or_span: Efficient utilization of galactose by microorganisms can lead to the production of valuable bio-products and improved metabolic processes.
While Bacillus subtilis has inherent pathways for galactose metabolism, there is potential for enhancement via evolutionary strategies.
This study aimed to boost galactose utilization in B. subtilis using adaptive laboratory evolution (ALE) and to elucidate the genetic and metabolic changes underlying the observed enhancements.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Hypothesis that ALE can enhance galactose utilization.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to design ALE conditions that select for improved galactose utilization.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Gradual reduction of yeast extract supplementation will force adaptation to galactose as sole carbon source.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Use M9 minimal medium with 2% galactose, initially supplemented with yeast extract (0.11% or 1% at different stages), then phased out; transfer every 24 h.
evolution_trajectory.observation: ALE performed with yeast extract phased out to achieve growth without supplementation.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Execute ALE and monitor growth.

evidence.quote_or_span: Initially, 0.11 percent yeast extract was added to the minimal medium to boost the growth of J46. By progressively lowering the supplementation, growth without supplementation was eventually attained.
Batch cultures were manually transferred to fresh medium every 24 h.
1% yeast extract was initially added to M9 minimal medium containing 2% (w/v) galactose. After 1000 generations, cultivation was carried out without the addition of yeast extract.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: ALE design established.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Will ALE produce a strain with significantly improved growth on galactose?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Prolonged ALE will yield evolved strains with increased specific growth rate on galactose.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Run ALE for ~5000 generations and isolate evolved strain BSGA14.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Evolved strain BSGA14 achieved a tenfold increase in specific growth rate compared to wild-type. Significant fitness increase confirmed between 1000 and 2500 generations.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Identify genetic basis of improved growth.

evidence.quote_or_span: The strains of B. subtilis underwent multiple rounds of adaptive laboratory evolution (approximately 5000 generations) in an environment that favored the use of galactose.
The significant increase in fitness on galactose was confirmed between 1000 and 2500 generations of ALE.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Evolved strain BSGA14 shows tenfold growth increase.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: What mutations are responsible for the improved phenotype?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Whole-genome sequencing will reveal causal mutations.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform whole-genome resequencing of BSGA14.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 63 SNPs identified, with key mutations in araR (H226R) and glcR (frameshift 243InsA).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Analyze allele frequencies over time to correlate with fitness increase.

evidence.quote_or_span: Upon selecting the evolved strain BSGA14, a comprehensive whole-genome sequencing revealed the presence of 63 single nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs).
Two of them, located in the coding sequences of the genes araR and glcR, were found to be the advantageous mutations after reverse engineering.
A total of 63 SNP mutations were identified in the BSGA14 strain
Conversely, the frequencies of $araR^{H226R}$ and $glcR^{243InsA}$ mutations surged from 0 to over 90%.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Measure allele frequencies of araR and glcR mutations across generations.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Mutation frequencies of araR and glcR surged from 0 to >90% between generations 1000-2000, coinciding with growth rate increase.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Formulate mechanistic hypothesis for araR mutation.

evidence.quote_or_span: a significant increment was recorded between the 1000 to 2000 generation span, which aligned with the escalation of the specific growth rate

source.title: Expanding the DNA editing toolbox: Novel lambda integrase variants targeting microalgal and human genome sequences

initial_request.research_problem: Lambda integrase is efficient for large DNA integration but limited by intrinsic sequence specificity to cognate attB sites. The goal is to engineer variants targeting pseudosites in microalgae and human genomes to expand the DNA editing toolbox.
initial_request.known_context: Intrinsic sequence specificities of recombinases curtail their use to DNA sequences with sufficient homology to endogenous target motifs.
constraints: Must target exogenous sequences in Phaeodactylum tricornutum and Nannochloropsis oceanica
Must achieve specificity switch away from native attB
quantifiable_goals: Improve recombination efficiency on non-cognate substrates
Achieve >1000-fold specificity switch

evolution_trajectory.observation: Intrinsic sequence specificities curtail their use to DNA sequences with sufficient homology to endogenous target motifs.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Hypothesis leads to design of directed evolution targeting microalgal sequences.

evidence.quote_or_span: intrinsic sequence specificities curtail their use to DNA sequences with sufficient homology to endogenous target motifs
Extensive engineering is therefore required to broaden applicability and robustness.

evidence.quote_or_span: Expressed and purified C7 showed 345-fold improvement over C3 for recombination of attNanno1 substrates and 1097-fold improvement for recombination of HTN substrates (Fig 8A).
The results showed that with 750ng and 1000ng of transfected target vector, both junctions can be generated by nested PCR, which was verified by PCR product sequencing

evolution_trajectory.state_before: C7 shows high activity and specificity switch.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: What is the mechanistic basis for the specificity switch conferred by N99D and H329R?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: N99D enables interaction with cytosine in variant sites, and H329R forms salt bridge with D351 stabilizing tetramer.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform MD simulations on Int C7 and related complexes.
evolution_trajectory.observation: A favourable network of interactions promotes binding and activity of Int variants comprising N99D in the DNA core binding domain to substrates with the cytosine to guanine transversion. H329R potentially introduces up to 4 intra-subunit salt bridges that could stabilize tetramer binding to DNA and favour recombination of non-cognate sequences.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: MD simulations provide mechanistic explanation for specificity switch of C7.

evidence.quote_or_span: This large network of interactions was observed to be stable for >75% of the simulation time and is likely a key determinant of the observed specificity switch.

MD simulations provide additional insight into the orthogonal phenotypes observed in this study. A favourable network of interactions promotes binding and activity of Int variants comprising N99D in the DNA core binding domain to substrates with the cytosine to guanine transversion.

H329 in the Int catalytic domain is distal to bound DNA. Mutation to R329 potentially introduces up to 4 intra-subunit salt bridges that could stabilize tetramer binding to DNA and favour recombination of non-cognate sequences.
The combined H329R and N99D mutations present in C7 therefore result in loss of the N99-DG8 interaction but improved R329-D351 interaction (attB-Int $^{C7}$ complex, Fig 10, S4 and S6A Figs).
However, the H329R mutation resulted in an R329 –D351 salt bridge interaction that was stable for 60% of the simulation time for the attB-Int $^{C3\_H329R}$ complex (S4 and S5B Figs).

evolution_trajectory.decision: Analyze recombination outcomes from cell-based experiments.
evolution_trajectory.observation: This indicates that contrary to expectations on thermodynamic grounds, Int-C7 surprisingly catalyzed intermolecular recombination more efficiently than intramolecular recombination.

evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Final conclusion and limitations.

these findings indicated that the locus can be considered a safe harbor site favorable for transgene expression

evolution_trajectory.decision: Acknowledge limitations of the study.

success_verification.final_conclusion: Directed evolution yielded Int C7 with N99D and H329R mutations, achieving >1000-fold specificity switch for attNanno1 and attHNanno substrates. Int C7 enabled single-copy genomic integration at a safe harbor locus in human cells with sustained transgene expression. Microalgae targeting failed likely due to DNA uptake/expression issues.
