source.title: A high-throughput multiparameter screen for accelerated development and optimization of soluble genetically encoded fluorescent biosensors

initial_request.research_problem: Developing and optimizing soluble genetically encoded fluorescent biosensors is challenging due to low-throughput, single-parameter screening methods.
initial_request.known_context: Existing biosensor screens are limited in the number of conditions that can be tested, hindering multiparameter optimization.
constraints: Soluble biosensors only
Cannot evaluate kinetics within GSBs
quantifiable_goals: Increase screening throughput and content by orders of magnitude
Develop a lactate biosensor with large response and low pH sensitivity

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Existing biosensor screens are limited in the number of conditions that can be tested, hindering multiparameter optimization.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to increase screening throughput and enable multiparameter optimization for biosensor engineering.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Combining droplet microfluidics and automated two-photon fluorescence lifetime imaging (2p-FLIM) will increase screening content and throughput by orders of magnitude.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Design the BeadScan platform.
evolution_trajectory.observation: The BeadScan platform was designed to combine droplet microfluidics and automated 2p-FLIM.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need to address low protein expression in initial GSB method.

evidence.quote_or_span: existing screens are still limited in the number of conditions that can be tested against the biosensor

we have surmounted these limitations, increasing screening content and throughput by orders of magnitude, by combining droplet microfluidics and automated two-photon fluorescence lifetime imaging (2p-FLIM)

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Initial GSB method yields nanomolar protein expression, insufficient for direct fluorescence detection.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Protein expression levels are too low for biosensor detection without enzymatic amplification.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Increasing DNA copy number per bead will boost protein expression to detectable levels.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Attempt existing bead PCR methods to achieve high copy numbers of full-length amplicons.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Existing bead PCR methods failed to reliably produce beads loaded with high copy numbers of full-length amplicons; only 0.1–1% of the resulting DNA beads were capable of driving strong expression of target sensors in IVTT droplets.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Develop a new bead PCR method to overcome these limitations.

evidence.quote_or_span: The enzymes are at nanomolar levels in the GSBs and their activity is visualized with fluorogenic substrates; but without the advantage of enzymatic amplification in the assay, fluorescent biosensors are difficult to detect at these low concentrations.

In our hands, these methods failed to reliably produce beads loaded with high copy numbers of full-length amplicons; only 0.1–1% of the resulting DNA beads were capable of driving strong expression of our target sensors in IVTT droplets.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Existing bead PCR methods failed to produce high-copy DNA beads.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need a reliable method to generate beads with high copy numbers of full-length amplicons.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Amplifying DNA free in solution in droplets and then immobilizing on streptavidin beads will yield high copy numbers.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Develop a new bead PCR method: amplify DNA free in solution in droplets with biotinylated 3' primer, then immobilize on streptavidin beads via microfluidic merging.
evolution_trajectory.observation: New method achieved >200,000 copies per bead.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Optimize DNA loading to prevent protein aggregates.

evidence.quote_or_span: To overcome these limitations, we instead allowed template DNA to be amplified free in solution in droplets containing a biotinylated 3' primer, and then subsequently immobilized the PCR products on streptavidin beads.
we chose to immobilize the amplified DNA on beads, purify the DNA beads and then use a minimal volume to deliver them into IVTT droplets

evolution_trajectory.state_before: High-copy DNA beads achieved, but risk of protein aggregates.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Uncontrolled DNA loading may cause protein aggregation.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Limiting DNA loading to ~100,000 copies by pre-blocking streptavidin sites and using limiting biotinylated 3' primer will prevent aggregates.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Optimize DNA loading on beads.
evolution_trajectory.observation: DNA loading limited to ~100,000 copies by pre-blocking streptavidin sites; limiting biotinylated 3' primer ensures only fully extended amplicons are captured.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Select a scaffold for lactate biosensor.

evidence.quote_or_span: we used a limiting concentration of the 3' primer to ensure the complete extension of all biotinylated primer molecules, so that only fully extended amplicons are captured on the streptavidin beads.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: High-copy DNA beads ready for library screening.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need a suitable scaffold for a lactate biosensor.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: TlpC dCACHE domain may undergo large conformational change upon lactate binding, making it a good scaffold.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Select TlpC as scaffold and fuse with T-Sapphire (TS) to create TlpC-TS.
evolution_trajectory.observation: TlpC selected as an untested scaffold; reasoning that lactate binding may generate a large conformational change between N- and C-termini.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Generate and screen linker library.

evidence.quote_or_span: we selected an untested scaffold for our biosensor: the extracellular dCACHE domain of the bacterial chemotaxis protein, TlpC from H. pylori.
we reasoned that lactate binding may generate a large conformational change between the N- and C- termini, which are in close proximity

evolution_trajectory.state_before: TlpC-TS scaffold designed.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Optimal linker composition and length unknown.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Screening a large linker library will identify variants with large lactate-induced lifetime changes.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Generate a library of 49,152 TlpC-TS linker variants and screen using BeadScan.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Two high-contrast biosensors isolated (TlpC-TS #1537 and #1059) with inverted lifetime changes (ΔLT = 0.2 ns and 0.3 ns) and physiological affinities (Kapp = 0.19 ± 0.13 mM and 0.66 ± 0.11 mM).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Characterize pH sensitivity of TlpC-TS variants.

evidence.quote_or_span: we generated a library of TlpC-TS biosensor variants that sampled 49,152 different linkers, varying both length and composition
Initial screen of the TlpC-TS lactate biosensor library, showing data from individual biosensor variants captured in GSBs.
Average photon counts within each ROI are plotted against the maximum change in lifetime between 0 and 10 mM lactate; each data point represents a single GSB.
Two GSBs with large lifetime changes were selected (dotted boxes).
5, 90 mM KCl, 10 mM NaCl and combined with Ni-NTA nanospheres (Kisker-biotech, PPS-0.2NI-NTA) and sterile 50% glycerol to final concentrations of 1.

source.title: Structure based pharmacophore modeling, virtual screening, molecular docking and ADMET approaches for identification of natural anti-cancer agents targeting XIAP protein

initial_request.research_problem: XIAP overexpression inhibits apoptosis, leading to cancer. Existing synthetic XIAP antagonists have side effects.
initial_request.known_context: XIAP is an anti-apoptotic protein; synthetic inhibitors exist but cause side effects.
constraints: Identify natural compounds
Low toxicity
quantifiable_goals: Binding affinity better than -6.8 kcal/mol
Non-toxic (LD50 > 2000 mg/kg)

evolution_trajectory.decision: Pursue in silico identification of natural XIAP inhibitors.

source.title: Benchmarking the performance of Bayesian optimization across multiple experimental materials science domains

initial_request.research_problem: Lack of comprehensive benchmarking of Bayesian optimization across diverse experimental materials science domains to guide surrogate model and acquisition function selection.
initial_request.known_context: Bayesian optimization has been successfully applied in materials science, but few studies have evaluated its efficiency across a broad range of experimental materials domains.
constraints: Use only existing experimental datasets
Simulate optimization campaigns via pool-based active learning
quantifiable_goals: Compare BO algorithms against random baseline using enhancement factor and acceleration factor
Identify best surrogate model and acquisition function combinations

evolution_trajectory.state_before: No comprehensive benchmarking of BO across experimental materials domains exists.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: It is unclear which surrogate models and acquisition functions perform best across diverse materials systems.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: GP with anisotropic kernels and RF will have comparable performance in BO, and both will outperform GP with isotropic kernels.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Test this hypothesis by benchmarking BO algorithms on multiple experimental datasets.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Hypothesis stated based on prior knowledge and preliminary observations.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need to design a benchmarking framework to test the hypothesis.

evidence.quote_or_span: surrogate models such as Gaussian Process (GP) with anisotropic kernels and Random Forest (RF) have comparable performance in BO, and both outperform the commonly used GP with isotropic kernels

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Hypothesis formulated but no experimental design exists.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need a framework to simulate BO-guided optimization campaigns and compare algorithms.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A pool-based active learning framework can effectively simulate experimental optimization and enable benchmarking.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Design a pool-based active learning framework using five experimental datasets, with batch size 1 and n=2 random initial experiments.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Framework designed to iteratively select experiments from a pool, mimicking real optimization.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Select surrogate models and acquisition functions to integrate into the framework.

evidence.quote_or_span: We utilize a pool-based active learning framework to approximate experimental materials optimization processes.
We also adapt metrics such as enhancement factor and acceleration factor to quantitatively compare performances of BO algorithms against that of a random sampling baseline.
Figure 2 shows the pool-based active learning benchmarking framework we use to simulate materials optimization campaigns guided by BO algorithms in each materials system.
At each learning cycle of the framework, instead of selecting a larger batch, only one new experiment is obtained.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Framework designed, but surrogate models and acquisition functions not specified.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which surrogate models and acquisition functions to include?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: RF, GP with isotropic kernels, and GP with ARD are representative surrogate models; EI, PI, LCB are common acquisition functions.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Select RF, GP with isotropic kernels, GP with ARD as surrogate models; EI, PI, LCB as acquisition functions.

tool_or_method.name: Surrogate model and acquisition function selection

evolution_trajectory.observation: Selected models and functions cover a range of complexity and assumptions.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Integrate into active learning framework with specific experimental settings.

evidence.quote_or_span: For hyperparameters of GP, we choose kernels from Matérn52, Matérn32, Matérn12, radial basis function (RBF), and multilayer perceptron (MLP).
For hyperparameters of GP ARD, we not only have the above kernel choices from GP, but also use ARD, which allows GP to keep anisotropic kernels.
We then pair the selected surrogate model with one of three acquisition functions, including expected improvement (EI), probability of improvement (PI), and lower confidence bound (LCB)

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Framework ready, but datasets have intrinsic BO guidance.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Could prior BO guidance bias benchmarking results?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Datasets partially collected with BO may favor certain algorithms, but the impact is limited.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Note limitation and proceed with rigorous statistical evaluation.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Four of five datasets had BO guiding experiments partially; this is a potential bias.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Conduct experiments with 50 ensembles and 10 five-fold splits to ensure robustness.

evidence.quote_or_span: while all datasets were gathered from relatively high-throughput experimental systems, P3HT/CNT, AgNP, Perovskite, and AutoAM had BO guiding the selection of subsequent experiments partially through the materials optimization campaigns

The aggregated performances of the BO algorithms derived from 50 averaged runs resulting from 10 random five-fold splits using the 50 original ensembles, is compared against a statistical random search baseline, and we can quantitatively evaluate its performance via active learning metrics defined in the sections below.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Limitation noted, experiments not yet run.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to evaluate BO algorithms with statistical rigor.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: 50 ensembles and 10 random five-fold splits will provide reliable performance estimates.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Run BO algorithms with 50 ensembles and 10 random five-fold splits, compare to random baseline.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Aggregated performances computed and compared against random search baseline.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Analyze results on Crossed barrel dataset as a case study.

evidence.quote_or_span: Each BO algorithm is evaluated for 50 ensembles with 50 independent random seeds governing the initialization of experiments.

The aggregated performances of the BO algorithms derived from 50 averaged runs resulting from 10 random five-fold splits using the 50 original ensembles, is compared against a statistical random search baseline, and we can quantitatively evaluate its performance via active learning metrics defined in the sections below.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Experiments completed, results on Crossed barrel dataset available.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How do RF, GP ARD, and GP isotropic compare on Crossed barrel?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: RF and GP ARD will outperform GP isotropic, with RF excelling early and GP ARD later.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Analyze learning curves and Top% metrics.
evolution_trajectory.observation: RF initially faster, GP ARD leads after Top%=0.46; both outclass GP isotropic.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Evaluate acquisition function performance.

evidence.quote_or_span: RF initially excels at lower learning cycles, while GP with ARD takes the lead after Top% = 0.46.
Both GP with ARD and RF outclass GP without ARD.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Surrogate model comparison done, acquisition function impact unknown.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Is PI a viable alternative to EI?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: PI will underperform EI consistently.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Compare PI and EI performance across datasets.
evolution_trajectory.observation: PI consistently worse than EI.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Quantify sample efficiency of top surrogate models.

evidence.quote_or_span: The performance of BO algorithms using the probability of improvement (PI) as acquisition function has also been evaluated, but its performance was quite consistently worse than EI

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Qualitative comparison done, need quantitative sample efficiency.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How many samples do GP ARD, RF, and GP isotropic need to reach Top%=0.8?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: GP ARD and RF will require far fewer samples than GP isotropic.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Measure number of samples to reach Top%=0.8 on Crossed barrel.
evolution_trajectory.observation: GP ARD ~75, RF ~85, GP isotropic ~170 samples out of 600.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Investigate optimization paths of GP ARD and RF.

evidence.quote_or_span: GP with ARD and RF reach that goal by evaluating approximately 75 and 85 candidates out of the total of 600, whereas GP without ARD needs about 170 samples out of 600.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Sample efficiency known, but optimization paths unexplored.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Do GP ARD and RF explore the design space similarly?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Despite comparable performance, they follow divergent paths.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Compute Jaccard similarity of selected experiments over iterations.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Jaccard similarity drops quickly, indicating very different optimization paths.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate findings across all five datasets.

evidence.quote_or_span: we can see that the Jaccard similarity value drops as quickly as the statistically least overlapping paths, indicating that despite the fact that GP with ARD and RF were trained on the same initial experiments at the onset, they follow very different paths in the materials design space.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Case study on Crossed barrel complete, need cross-dataset validation.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Do the findings generalize to other materials systems?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: GP ARD and RF will consistently outperform GP isotropic across all datasets.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Compare EF_max across all five datasets for different surrogate models.
evolution_trajectory.observation: GP ARD and RF comparable and outperform GP isotropic in most datasets.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Explain why GP isotropic underperforms.

evidence.quote_or_span: Across most, if not all, of the investigated datasets, it was observed quite consistently that the performance of BO algorithms using GP with ARD and RF as surrogate models were comparable, and both outperform those using GP without ARD in most datasets.
We demonstrate that when paired with the same acquisition functions, RF as a surrogate model can compete with GP with ARD, and both outperform GP without ARD.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: GP isotropic underperformance observed, but reason unclear.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Why does GP isotropic fail?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Single lengthscale cannot capture distinct feature contributions.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Analyze kernel properties.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Isotropic kernel uses one lengthscale, conflicting with varying feature importance.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Discuss trade-offs between GP ARD and RF.

evidence.quote_or_span: The reason GP without ARD shows worse performance as follows: it will have a single lengthscale in an isotropic kernel as a scaling parameter controlling GP's kernel function, which is at odds with the fact that each input feature has its distinct contribution to the objective.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: GP ARD and RF both effective, but practical considerations needed.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which surrogate model is more practical?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: GP ARD is more robust, but RF has practical advantages.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Summarize trade-offs: GP ARD robust, RF easier to use.
evolution_trajectory.observation: RF free from distribution assumptions, lower time complexity, easier hyperparameter tuning.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Conclude benchmarking study.

evidence.quote_or_span: GP with anisotropic kernel has shown to be more robust as a surrogate model across most design spaces, yet RF also warrants more consideration because of it being free from distribution assumptions, having lower time complexities, and requiring less effort in initial hyperparameter selection.

Instead of devoting a nontrivial experimental budget to finding the best kernel for GP using adaptive kernels $^{49}$ , automating kernel selection $^{50}$ or keeping a library of kernels available via online learning, RF is an easier off-the-shelf option that allows one to make fewer structural assumptions about unfamiliar materials domains.

validation_methods: Cross-dataset validation across five experimental materials systems
Comparison against random search baseline using enhancement factor and acceleration factor
Statistical evaluation with 50 ensembles and 10 random five-fold splits
success_verification.final_conclusion: GP with ARD and RF are comparable and consistently outperform GP with isotropic kernels across diverse experimental materials domains. RF offers practical advantages such as no distribution assumptions and easier hyperparameter tuning.
limitations: Four of five datasets were partially collected with BO guidance, potentially biasing results
Only three acquisition functions tested
Limited to five specific materials systems

source.title: ECNet is an evolutionary context-integrated deep learning framework for protein engineering

initial_request.research_problem: Protein engineering requires accurate prediction of functional fitness for unseen variants, but existing ML methods have limited accuracy because they capture general sequence contexts not specific to the target protein.
initial_request.known_context: Existing ML algorithms for protein engineering have limited accuracy because the general sequence contexts they capture are not specific to the protein being engineered.
constraints: Limited labeled DMS data
Need to model epistasis
quantifiable_goals: Improve Spearman correlation for fitness prediction
Generalize from low-order to higher-order mutants

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Existing ML algorithms for protein engineering have limited accuracy because they capture general sequence contexts not specific to the target protein.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Whether co-evolution information can be used to infer epistasis or fitness of proteins.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Co-evolution information can be used to infer epistasis or fitness of proteins.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Investigate the relationship between co-evolution of residue pairs and fitness of double mutants.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Hypothesized that co-evolution information can be used to infer epistasis or fitness of proteins.

evidence.quote_or_span: because the general sequence contexts they capture are not specific to the protein being engineered, the accuracy of existing machine learning algorithms is rather limited
we hypothesized that co-evolution information can also be used to infer epistasis or fitness of proteins

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Hypothesized that co-evolution information can infer epistasis or fitness.

evolution_trajectory.observation: Found Spearman correlation 0.35 between pairwise dependency strength and double mutant fitness.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Extend analysis to more DMS datasets and examine effect of homologous sequence depth.

evidence.quote_or_span: We found that the strength of pairwise dependencies correlated with the fitness of double mutants (Spearman correlation 0.35; Fig. 1a).

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Observed moderate correlation (0.35) for one protein.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Whether the correlation generalizes across proteins and depends on MSA depth.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Change of dependency strength can predict fitness across DMS datasets, and correlation increases with more homologous sequences.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Use change of dependency strength to predict fitness across multiple DMS datasets and analyze trend with number of homologous sequences.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Spearman correlations ranged from 0.1 to 0.5; observed trend of increasing correlation with more homologous sequences.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Design a model that integrates local evolutionary context from homologous sequences and global context from protein language models.

evidence.quote_or_span: We found that the predictions correlated with experimental data with a Spearman correlation ranging from 0.1 to 0.5 (Fig. 1b).

we observed a trend of increasing correlation score if a protein has more homologous sequences, presumably because abundant homologous sequences lead to a more accurately fitted direct coupling analysis model
We found that the strength of pairwise dependencies correlated with the fitness of double mutants (Spearman correlation 0.35; Fig. 1a).

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Co-evolution information shows predictive power but limited accuracy; correlation depends on MSA depth.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to integrate local evolutionary context (residue-residue epistasis) with global evolutionary context (from protein language models) for improved fitness prediction.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Integrating local evolutionary context from homologous sequences with global evolutionary context from protein language models will improve fitness prediction.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Design ECNet to combine local evolutionary context (via direct coupling analysis) and global evolutionary context (via protein language model, e.g., TAPE).
evolution_trajectory.observation: Designed ECNet architecture integrating local and global evolutionary contexts.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Specify the neural network architecture and training procedure.

evidence.quote_or_span: This algorithm integrates local evolutionary context from homologous sequences that explicitly model residue-residue epistasis for the protein of interest with the global evolutionary context that encodes rich semantic and structural features from the enormous protein sequence universe.
ECNet integrates global and local evolutionary contexts to represent the protein sequence of interest.
a direct coupling analysis model is used to capture the dependencies between residues in protein sequences, which encodes the local evolutionary context.
we integrated general protein sequence representations from unsupervised protein contextual language models

evolution_trajectory.state_before: ECNet conceptual design established.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to specify the neural network architecture and training details.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A BiLSTM with self-attention and fully-connected layers, trained with MSE loss and ensemble of three models, will effectively model sequence-to-function mapping.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Use BiLSTM with self-attention and fully-connected layers, train with MSE loss, and ensemble three replicas.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Architecture specified: recurrent neural network (BiLSTM) with self-attention, ensemble of three models.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Address skewed training data bias by adding auxiliary classification objective.

evidence.quote_or_span: a recurrent neural network model, trained on the fitness data of screened variants, is used for the sequence-to-function modeling with both representations

To improve the model’s robustness and prediction accuracy, we used an ensemble approach to output the prediction, in which three replicas of ECNet models were trained using the same hyperparameters and training data, and their output scores were averaged as the final prediction.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Basic ECNet architecture defined, but training data may be skewed (e.g., only 18% positive variants in Envision TEM-1).
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Skewed training data may bias the model towards negative predictions.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Adding an auxiliary classification objective will mitigate bias from skewed data.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Introduce auxiliary classification objective.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Added auxiliary classification objective to address skewed training data bias.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Benchmark ECNet against existing methods on Envision dataset.

evidence.quote_or_span: We addressed this issue by introducing an auxiliary classification objective.

For example, in the Envision dataset $^{35}$ , only 18% of TEM-1 variants are more active than the wild-type sequence (positive effects) while the remaining are less active than the wild-type sequence (negative effects).

evolution_trajectory.state_before: ECNet with auxiliary loss ready for benchmarking.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How ECNet performs compared to the approach of Yang et al on the Envision dataset.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: ECNet will outperform the approach of Yang et al on the Envision dataset.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Train ECNet on 80% of DMS data and test on 20%, using Spearman correlation as metric.
evolution_trajectory.observation: ECNet consistently outperformed the approach of Yang et al on all 12 datasets, with a relative improvement ranging from 16 to 60% in Spearman correlation.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Compare ECNet to unsupervised methods on DeepSequence dataset.

evidence.quote_or_span: ECNet consistently outperformed the approach of Yang et al on all the 12 datasets, with a relative improvement ranging from 16 to 60% in terms of the achieved Spearman correlation
For each protein, we used 80% of the DMS data to train ECNet or other methods and the remaining 20% data to evaluate the model's performance.
Spearman correlation was used as the evaluation metric.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: ECNet outperformed the approach of Yang et al on Envision dataset.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How ECNet compares to unsupervised methods (EVmutation, DeepSequence, Autoregressive) on DeepSequence dataset.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: ECNet, as a supervised predictor, will outperform unsupervised methods on the DeepSequence dataset.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform five-fold cross-validation on DeepSequence dataset, using Spearman correlation as metric.
evolution_trajectory.observation: ECNet outperformed EVmutation, DeepSequence, and Autoregressive across almost all proteins.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Perform ablation and robustness analyses to validate contributions of joint representations and noise tolerance.

evidence.quote_or_span: ECNet, predicting the mutation effects using a supervised predictor, outperformed these methods across almost all proteins
For ECNet, we performed five-fold cross-validation on this dataset and reported the average performance over all the five folds.
We used Spearman correlation as the evaluation metric.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: ECNet demonstrated superior performance in benchmarks.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Whether joint representations are necessary and how robust ECNet is to data noise.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Joint representations outperform individual ones, and ECNet is robust to data noise.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Conduct ablation study comparing joint vs. individual representations, and test robustness by perturbing 10% of training data.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Joint representations outperformed individual ones; test correlation decreased only 2% with 10% noise.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Test generalization from single to double mutants.

evidence.quote_or_span: a model using joint representations outperformed a model using any individual representation
ECNet's test correlation only decreased by 2% when the training data was perturbed by 10%

evolution_trajectory.state_before: ECNet validated on single mutant predictions.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Whether ECNet can generalize from single mutants to predict fitness of double mutants.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: ECNet trained on single mutant data can predict double mutant fitness.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Train ECNet on single mutant data only and test on double mutants for six proteins.
evolution_trajectory.observation: ECNet achieved Spearman correlations ranging from 0.73 to 0.94 on six proteins.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate ECNet's ability to prioritize higher-order mutants in wet-lab experiments.

evidence.quote_or_span: We then trained our prediction model using single mutant data only and tested its performance on double mutants.
ECNet achieved Spearman correlations ranging from 0.73 to 0.94 for the six proteins

evolution_trajectory.state_before: ECNet shows strong generalization from single to double mutants in silico.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Whether ECNet can prioritize novel higher-order TEM-1 variants with improved ampicillin resistance in wet-lab.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: ECNet-prioritized TEM-1 variants will show improved ampicillin resistance.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Train ECNet on low-order TEM-1 DMS data, predict fitness of 146 inhibitor-resistant variants and random variants, prioritize 37 variants, and test in E. coli.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Most prioritized variants showed improved fitness as compared to the wild type.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Confirm generalization to higher-order mutants and discuss limitations.

evidence.quote_or_span: most of the variants prioritized by ECNet demonstrated improved fitness as compared to the wild type
We trained an ECNet model and used it to predict the fitness of 146 TEM-1 inhibitor-resistant variants and a set of randomly generated variants.
For every of the 146 TEM-1 inhibitor-resistant variants, we generate ten random variants that have the same mutated sites
In total, we used ECNet to identify 37 TEM-1 variants that were likely to demonstrate improved fitness

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Wet-lab results show improved ampicillin resistance for ECNet-prioritized variants.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Whether ECNet generalizes from low-order to higher-order mutants.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: ECNet generalizes from low-order to higher-order mutants.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Validate that ECNet prioritized novel higher-order TEM-1 mutants with improved resistance, despite being trained on single and consecutive double mutants.
evolution_trajectory.observation: ECNet prioritized novel and higher-order TEM-1 mutants that showed improved resistance against ampicillin.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Acknowledge limitations regarding MSA and DMS data dependence.

evidence.quote_or_span: Despite being trained on the data of single mutants and consecutive double mutants, ECNet prioritized novel and higher-order TEM-1 mutants that showed improved resistance against ampicillin.

validation_methods: Benchmarking against the approach of Yang et al on Envision dataset
Comparison with unsupervised methods (EVmutation, DeepSequence, Autoregressive) on DeepSequence dataset
Ablation study on joint vs. individual representations
Generalization test from single to double mutants
Wet-lab validation of prioritized TEM-1 variants
metrics: Hit rate at different ampicillin concentrations
success_verification.final_conclusion: ECNet integrates local and global evolutionary contexts to accurately predict protein fitness, outperforms existing methods, generalizes from low-order to higher-order mutants, and successfully prioritizes novel functional variants in wet-lab experiments.
limitations: ECNet's prediction performance is impacted by the MSA characteristics and DMS data properties.
The exploration-exploitation trade-off of training data also influenced the model performance.

source.title: A High-Throughput Screening System Based on Fluorescence-Activated Cell Sorting for the Directed Evolution of Chitinase A

initial_request.research_problem: Developing a FACS-based high-throughput screening system for directed evolution of chitinase A that overcomes leakage of the hydrophobic fluorescent product 4MU from double emulsions.
initial_request.known_context: FACS-based screening in double emulsions is limited by leakage of hydrophobic products like 4MU, causing false negatives and false positives.
constraints: Must use commercially available substrate 4MUTC
Must maintain cell viability during expression and screening
quantifiable_goals: Achieve enrichment of positive cells
Identify mutants with improved activity over wild-type

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Hydrophobic product 4MU leaks from double emulsions, causing signal decline and crosstalk, limiting FACS-based screening for chitinase.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to prevent 4MU leakage from emulsion compartments to enable reliable FACS-based screening.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Cyclodextrins can trap hydrophobic 4MU to prevent leakage from emulsion compartments.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Test cyclodextrins as retention agents for 4MU in emulsion systems.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Cyclodextrins are known to form inclusion complexes with hydrophobic molecules, potentially retaining 4MU in the aqueous phase.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Verify the hypothesis by testing cyclodextrins in biphasic and emulsion systems.

evidence.quote_or_span: we observed a decline in the level of fluorescent product if the emulsions were incubated for longer time (Figure 1d), probably reflecting the hydrophobic nature of 4MU, which can diffuse out of the internal water phase of the emulsion system through the oil phase and, potentially, into other droplets.

we considered this as a model to overcome the diffusion of a hydrophobic product from the emulsion compartments and therefore increase the range of substrates that can be used with FACS-based screening for other enzyme classes.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Hypothesis that cyclodextrins can trap 4MU; initial ChiA activity assay verified in MTP format.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to confirm that cyclodextrins effectively retain 4MU in water phase and prevent leakage in emulsions.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A 100-fold molar excess of HCD will retain >95% of 4MU in the water phase.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Test different cyclodextrins (HCD, MCD) in biphasic mixture and single oil-in-water emulsions.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 100-fold molar excess of HCD retains >95% of 4MU in water phase; single emulsion confirms HCD retains 4MU better than no cyclodextrin or MCD.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Incorporate HCD into double emulsion design and add quenching system to further reduce crosstalk.

evidence.quote_or_span: a 100-fold molar excess of HCD over 4MU keeps more than 95% of 4MU in the water phase

The presence of positive (green droplets) in the P2-Q1 quarter confirmed that 4MU entered the oil system in the absence of CDs but was retained more efficiently when HCD was present in the water phase

evolution_trajectory.state_before: HCD effectively retains 4MU in single emulsions; double emulsion system still prone to crosstalk from leaked 4MU.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Leaked 4MU in outer phase can still cause false positives; need to quench external fluorescence.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Adding HCD to outer phase and HRP/H2O2 quenching system will minimize crosstalk from leaked 4MU.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Optimize double emulsion with HCD in inner and outer phases plus HRP/H2O2 quenching system.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Optimized double emulsion system designed with HCD in both phases and HRP/H2O2 quenching in outer phase.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Test the optimized system with a reference library to assess enrichment and viability.

evidence.quote_or_span: we also added HCD to the external water phase of the double emulsions to provide insurance

Using the optimized system containing HCD in the inner phase, and HCD, HRP, and $H_{2}O_{2}$ in the outer phase, we prepared reference libraries containing 5% ChiA-positive cells mixed with 95% nonexpressing cells and compartmentalized them in double emulsions.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Optimized double emulsion system ready for testing with reference library containing 5% ChiA-positive cells.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Will the optimized system allow enrichment of positive cells without toxicity issues?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Sorting the reference library will yield enriched positive cells.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Sort reference library with 5% positive cells using optimized double emulsion system.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Recovered fewer than 300 colonies, none positive; presorting plating showed <1% positive, indicating ChiA toxicity.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Investigate cause of low recovery; test viability at different expression times.

evidence.quote_or_span: we recovered fewer than 300 colonies from the plates and none of them were positive
despite the initial mix containing 5% positive cells, less than 1% of cells recovered from the presorting plate were positive

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Sorting failure due to low recovery of positive cells; suspected ChiA toxicity.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Is long-term ChiA expression bactericidal? What expression time maintains viability?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Long-term (18 h) ChiA expression kills E. coli; shorter expression (2–6 h) will maintain viability.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Test viability of E. coli expressing ChiA for different durations.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 18 h ChiA expression kills >99% of cells (less than 0.6% recovery); 2–6 h expression maintains viability.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Reduce expression time to 4 h and repeat sorting.

evidence.quote_or_span: the expression of ChiA was bactericidal to E. coli cells after 18 h, resulting in the recovery of less than 0.6% of the colonies
cells expressing ChiA for 2–6 h, confirming that the main bactericidal factor was the long-term expression of ChiA
We therefore reduced the chiA expression time to 4 h, which does not affect cell viability but still allows the detection of ChiA activity.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Viability data shows 4 h expression is non-toxic and allows activity detection.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Will reduced expression time enable successful enrichment of positive cells?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Reducing expression time to 4 h will allow enrichment of ChiA-positive cells without toxicity.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Reduce chiA expression time to 4 h and sort mutant library.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Achieved 17-fold enrichment of positive cell population.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate improved mutants by secondary screening.

evidence.quote_or_span: We therefore reduced the chiA expression time to 4 h, which does not affect cell viability but still allows the detection of ChiA activity.
After sorting the positive events, we achieved a 17-fold enrichment of the positive cell population.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Enriched mutant library obtained after sorting.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which mutants show improved activity compared to wild-type?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Secondary screening will identify mutants with higher chitinase activity.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform secondary screening of 300 colonies in MTP format.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Identified 12 mutants with higher activity than wild-type ChiA, one of which (DH08) was 100% more active.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Analyze mutations structurally to understand basis of improvement.

evidence.quote_or_span: This is the first report describing an ultrahigh-throughput screening system for improving chitinase activity
We identified 12 mutants with higher activity than wild-type ChiA, one of which (DH08) was 100% more active.
we recovered fewer than 300 colonies from the plates and none of them were positive

evolution_trajectory.state_before: 12 improved mutants identified; no crystal structure available for ChiA.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Where are the mutations located relative to the active site?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Mutations are likely distant from the active site, affecting activity indirectly.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Build homology model of ChiA based on 1edq template (30% similarity) and map mutations.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Mutations positioned >5 Å from active site, in loops or edges of secondary structures.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Analyze mutation distribution and types across variants.

evidence.quote_or_span: A crystal structure for ChiA is not yet available. We therefore constructed a homology model for the enzyme based on the 1edq sequence, which has 30% similarity to ChiA.
the mutations obtained in this study are positioned at a distance of more than 5 Å from the active site
Despite these limitations, the current version of the assay enabled the recovery of 12 improved ChiA variants, in most cases featuring mutations in surface loops far from the active site.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Mutations mapped on homology model; need to understand mutation patterns.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Are there any recurring mutations or hotspots? What types of mutations are present?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Mutations are distributed across the sequence with no clustering; multiple mutations per variant complicate interpretation.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Analyze mutation frequency, types (conservative, charge-changing, proline-related), and distribution.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Only one mutation (E389D) shared among two mutants; rest unique, no clustering; 49 total mutations across 12 variants, including 14 conservative and 16 charge-changing substitutions.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Discuss limitations of the study.

evidence.quote_or_span: Only one mutation (E389D) was found in two different mutants (AB01 and GM). The rest of the mutations were unique and distributed over the entire sequence of the protein. We did not observe any clustering or any regions with a higher mutational load.
Three of our mutants (P190, P436T, and S441P) involved exchanges that either removed or introduced proline residues
We identified 16 such substitutions: K16I, K24N, W42R, D110N, K128E, H130N, D220Y, R147L, N192K, G303D, V304D, D427N, R448C, N469D, G516R, and R574Q.
All our variants contained multiple mutations, making it difficult to speculate on the importance of individual residues
The 12 variants we identified feature a total of 49 mutations.
Despite these limitations, the current version of the assay enabled the recovery of 12 improved ChiA variants, in most cases featuring mutations in surface loops far from the active site.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Improved mutants identified but activity improvements capped at ~2-fold.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Why are activity improvements limited?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Toxicity of ChiA in E. coli limits recovery of highly active mutants.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Conclude that toxicity remains an issue and propose future strategies.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Mutants never exceeded wild-type activity by more than twofold; toxicity likely caps improvements. Future work: shorter induction, cell-free systems.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Acknowledge additional limitations of the study.

evidence.quote_or_span: the mutants recovered from the library never exceeded the wild-type activity by more than twofold, suggesting that toxicity remains an issue for enzymes with much higher levels of activity
We are considering several strategies to overcome this drawback, including the testing of even shorter induction periods and the use of cell-free in vitro coupled transcription–translation systems

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Homology model used for structural interpretation; limitations noted.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How reliable is the structural interpretation given low model accuracy?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Low homology model accuracy (30% similarity) and unmodeled N-/C-terminal regions limit structural interpretation.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Plan single mutant analysis and MD simulations to validate findings.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Model accuracy limited; single mutant evaluation and MD simulations underway.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Conclude the study.

evidence.quote_or_span: The evaluation of single mutants is currently underway, combined with molecular dynamics simulations.
A crystal structure for ChiA is not yet available. We therefore constructed a homology model for the enzyme based on the 1edq sequence, which has 30% similarity to ChiA.

validation_methods: Secondary screening in MTP format with wild-type and negative controls
metrics: Relative activity compared to wild-type (up to 100% increase)
success_verification.final_conclusion: A FACS-based screening system using HCD and HRP/H2O2 quenching was developed, enabling 17-fold enrichment and identification of 12 improved ChiA mutants, though toxicity limited improvements to ~2-fold.
limitations: Toxicity of ChiA in E. coli limits recovery of highly active mutants
Low homology model accuracy (30% similarity) limits structural interpretation
Multiple mutations per variant hinder identification of causative residues

source.title: Combining mechanistic and machine learning models for predictive engineering and optimization of tryptophan metabolism

initial_request.research_problem: Metabolic engineering requires accurate genotype-to-phenotype predictions, but mechanistic models need a priori knowledge and high-quality data, while ML models need large, high-quality datasets.
initial_request.known_context: Mechanistic models require a priori knowledge and high-quality data; ML models require large datasets of high quality.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Metabolic engineering lacks accurate genotype-to-phenotype predictions due to limitations of mechanistic and ML models.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can combining mechanistic and ML models enable accurate genotype-to-phenotype predictions?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Combining mechanistic and machine learning models can enable accurate genotype-to-phenotype predictions.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Proceed with a combined approach.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Hypothesis formulated.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Design overall approach to test hypothesis.

evidence.quote_or_span: Here we show that mechanistic and machine learning models can be combined to enable accurate genotype-to-phenotype predictions.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Hypothesis formulated.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to implement the combined approach?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Using a genome-scale model, efficient library construction, and high-throughput biosensor-enabled screening will train ML algorithms for predictive engineering.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Adopt overall approach: GSM for target identification, combinatorial library construction, biosensor-based phenotyping, and ML training.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Overall design established.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Perform computational target prediction using GSM.

evidence.quote_or_span: We use a genome-scale model to pinpoint engineering targets, efficient library construction of metabolic pathway designs, and high-throughput biosensor-enabled screening for training diverse machine learning algorithms.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Gene targets predicted.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which specific genes to include in combinatorial library?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Selecting CDC19, TKL1, TAL1, PCK1, and PFK1 will effectively modulate tryptophan pathway precursors.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Select four genes from modeling (CDC19, TKL1, TAL1, PCK1) and add PFK1 based on literature.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Five genes selected for library.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Define library design space with promoters.

evidence.quote_or_span: CDC19, TKL1, TAL1, and PCK1 were initially selected as targets for combinatorial library construction

while not being predicted as a target by the constraint-based modeling approach, the PFK1 gene, encoding the alpha subunit of heterooctameric phosphofructokinase (PFK1), catalyzing the irreversible conversion of fructose 6-phosphate to fructose 1,6-bisphosphate (FBP), was selected, as insufficient activity of this enzyme is known to cause divergence of carbon flux toward the PPP across different kingdoms

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Five genes selected.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to vary expression of these genes?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Using six promoters per gene from a set of 30 promoters will create a 7776-membered combinatorial library.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Define library design space: five genes each controlled by six promoters from a set of 30 promoters selected from transcriptomics data mining.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 7776-membered combinatorial library design space defined.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Construct library via one-pot assembly.

evidence.quote_or_span: We define a 7776-membered combinatorial library design space, based on five genes selected from GSM simulations and a priori biological understanding, each controlled by six different promoters from a set of 30 promoters selected from transcriptomics data mining.

we mined transcriptomics datasets for the selection of promoters to control the expression of the five target genes. Here, we focused on well-characterized and sequence-diverse promoters to ensure rational designs spanning large absolute levels of promoter activities, and limit the risk of recombination within strain designs and loss of any genetic elements

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Library design defined.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can the library be efficiently constructed?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: One-pot transformation with 38 parts will assemble 7776 unique designs at a single genomic locus.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform one-pot transformation of platform strain with 38 parts.

parameters.parts: 38 (30 promoters, 5 ORFs, HIS3 ORF, 2 homology regions)

evolution_trajectory.observation: Library of 7776 possible designs assembled at targeted genomic locus.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Assess library assembly fidelity.

evidence.quote_or_span: we transformed in one-pot the platform strain with 38 different parts (30 promoters, 5 ORFs, HIS3 ORF, and 2 homology regions) for 7776 unique 20 kb 13-parts assemblies at the targeted genomic locus

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Library constructed.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: What is the quality of the assembled library?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Genotyping will reveal assembly fidelity and coverage.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Genotype library colonies and estimate coverage via Monte Carlo simulation.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 82% correct assembly, 3.7% duplicate genotypes, estimated 48% coverage (3759 unique genotypes).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Proceed to high-throughput phenotyping despite partial coverage.

evidence.quote_or_span: genotyping identified 380 out of 461 (82%) of the sufficiently growing strains to be correctly assembled with only 9 out of 245 (3.7%) of the fully filtered library genotypes observed in duplicates
the expected number of unique genotypes among all library colonies was calculated to be 3759, equaling estimated library coverage of 48% (3759/7776)

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Library quality assessed.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: What are the tryptophan biosynthesis rates of library strains?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: High-throughput phenotyping using a GFP biosensor will quantify tryptophan biosynthesis rates.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Measure GFP and OD time series for 507 colonies in triplicate at 82 time points.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Collected 124,722 data points; GFP synthesis rate range 43.7–255.7 MFI/h, average CV 4.3%.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Train ML models on phenotyping data.

evidence.quote_or_span: we measured time series data of OD and GFP at 82 time points in triplicates for all 507 colonies (that is 480 from the library and 27 from the control strains), covering a total of 124,722 data points

we chose this interval for determining the GFP synthesis rate as a proxy for tryptophan biosynthesis rate. The average GFP synthesis rate of all quality-controlled strains (see below) was observed to vary between 43.7 and 255.7 MFI/h (approximately sixfold; Fig. 3f), with an average standard error of the mean of 6.6 MFI/h corresponding to an average coefficient of variation for the mean values of 4.3%
we collect >124,000 experimental time series data points derived from fluorescent read-outs of an engineered tryptophan biosensor encoded into >500 different strain designs.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Phenotyping data collected.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How well can ML models predict tryptophan biosynthesis rate?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: ML models will achieve test MAE around 11% of full range, with decreasing improvement as data increases.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform cross-validation of ART and EVOLVE models.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Test MAE 21.4 MFI/h (ART) and 22.4 MFI/h (EVOLVE), which is approximately 11% of the full range (43.7–255.7 MFI/h); MAE decrease rate declines with more data.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Use models to recommend new strain designs.

evidence.quote_or_span: the average cross-validated MAE (test MAE) was 21.4 and 22.4 MFI/h for ART and EVOLVE model approaches, respectively
The test MAE decreased systematically with the size of the dataset, yet the decrease rate declined markedly as more data was added.
Mean absolute error (MAE) from model training and testing as a function of the number of genotypes in the dataset.
The test MAE decreased considerably with the number of genotypes in the dataset, this decrease was similar for both models.

we chose this interval for determining the GFP synthesis rate as a proxy for tryptophan biosynthesis rate. The average GFP synthesis rate of all quality-controlled strains (see below) was observed to vary between 43.7 and 255.7 MFI/h (approximately sixfold; Fig. 3f), with an average standard error of the mean of 6.6 MFI/h corresponding to an average coefficient of variation for the mean values of 4.3%

evolution_trajectory.state_before: ML models trained.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to generate improved strain designs?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Exploitative (ART) and explorative (EVOLVE) strategies will propose 30 designs each.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Use ART in exploitative mode and EVOLVE in explorative mode to recommend 30 designs each.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Two sets of 30 prioritized designs generated.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Construct recommended strains.

evidence.quote_or_span: we used an exploitative approach implemented in the ART model and an explorative one adopting the EVOLVE algorithm to recommend two sets of 30 prioritized designs

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Recommendations generated.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can recommended strains be constructed?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Same genome engineering approach will construct recommended strains, but PGK1 promoter designs may fail.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Construct 19 explorative and 24 exploitative designs; note failure of PGK1 promoter designs.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 19 explorative and 24 exploitative designs successfully built; 8 designs with PGK1 promoter failed.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate performance of constructed strains.

evidence.quote_or_span: we used the same genome engineering approach as for library construction (Fig. 2a) to successfully construct 19 individual assemblies of the explorative recommendations and 24 individual assemblies of the exploitative recommendations.
we were not able to construct any of the eight designs with the PGK1 promoter
we were not able to construct any of the eight designs with the PGK1 promoter

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Recommended strains constructed.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Do recommended strains outperform library strains?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Best recommendation will show significant improvement in GFP synthesis rate, titer, and productivity.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Measure GFP synthesis rate, tryptophan titer, and productivity of recommended strains.
evolution_trajectory.observation: SP606 achieved 106% higher GFP synthesis rate than best library strain, 74% titer increase, 43% productivity increase.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Evaluate ML prediction accuracy on recommended strains.

evidence.quote_or_span: the best recommendation (SP606) having a measured GFP synthesis rate 106% higher
We observed the strain SP606 having a 74 and 43% improvement in tryptophan titer and productivity, respectively, compared to the best strain design from the library sample (SP271).
In total, we managed to increase tryptophan titers and productivity by up to 74% and 43%, respectively, compared to an already improved reference strain ( $ARO4^{K229L}$ and $TRP2^{S65R, S76L}$ ).

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Validation completed.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: What are the limitations of the GSM approach?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: GSMs predict many targets and may miss effective ones due to lack of regulatory information.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Note limitation: GSMs predict many targets and may miss effective ones (e.g., PFK1) due to lack of regulatory information, requiring manual filtering.
evolution_trajectory.observation: GSMs predict many targets and may miss effective ones (e.g., PFK1) due to lack of regulatory information, requiring manual filtering.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Consider future improvements.

evidence.quote_or_span: GSMs are able to guide metabolic engineering using various constraint-based algorithms, which, however, predict a large number of potential targets and may also miss some effective ones (e.g., PFK1 in our study), due to the lack of other information beyond metabolism, e.g., regulation in GSMs.
manual efforts are currently needed to filter out less relevant targets and add intuitively promising ones based on existing knowledge.

validation_methods: tryptophan titer and productivity via HPLC
success_verification.final_conclusion: Best recommendation SP606 achieved 106% higher GFP synthesis rate, 74% titer increase, and 43% productivity increase over best library strain.
limitations: ML predictions for recommended strains were noticeably worse than for library strains, highlighting extrapolation challenge.
GSMs predict many targets and may miss effective ones (e.g., PFK1) due to lack of regulatory information, requiring manual filtering.

source.title: Using deep mutational scanning to benchmark variant effect predictors and identify disease mutations

initial_request.research_problem: Unbiased benchmarking of computational variant effect predictors (VEPs) using deep mutational scanning (DMS) as independent ground truth to overcome training data circularity and overfitting.
initial_request.known_context: Many VEPs exist but are trained/evaluated on different datasets, making direct comparison difficult due to training data circularity.
constraints: DMS datasets must have at least 5% coverage of all possible mutations
VEPs must be publicly available or have precomputed scores
quantifiable_goals: Rank VEPs by Spearman correlation with DMS scores
Assess DMS reproducibility (average correlation > 0.5)
Identify top VEP for human proteins
Compare DMS and VEPs in distinguishing ClinVar pathogenic from gnomAD benign variants

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Many VEPs exist but are trained/evaluated on different datasets, making direct comparison difficult due to training data circularity.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: No unbiased benchmark exists to compare VEPs without training data circularity.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: DMS experiments can provide independent quantitative phenotypic measurements to benchmark VEPs.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Use 31 previously published DMS experiments to benchmark 46 VEPs.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Identified 31 DMS datasets with ≥5% coverage and 46 VEPs from databases and precomputed scores.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need to assess DMS reproducibility before using as benchmark.

evidence.quote_or_span: Such predictors are often trained and evaluated using different variant datasets, making a direct comparison between VEPs difficult.

In this study, we use 31 previously published deep mutational scanning (DMS) experiments, which provide quantitative, independent phenotypic measurements for large numbers of single amino acid substitutions, in order to benchmark and compare 46 different VEPs.
To identify DMS datasets, we performed a literature search for papers presenting such experiments with available data.
For inclusion in this analysis, we required at least 5% coverage of all possible mutations

evolution_trajectory.state_before: 31 DMS datasets collected but reproducibility unknown.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: DMS measurements may vary between experiments on the same protein.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: DMS is reproducible enough to serve as a benchmark.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Calculate Spearman correlation between functional scores of DMS sets for the same protein.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Average correlation 0.66 (range 0.34–0.94).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Reproducibility supports using DMS as benchmark; proceed to correlate with VEP predictions.

evidence.quote_or_span: To assess the reproducibility of DMS and its viability as a benchmark, we calculated the Spearman's correlation coefficient between the functional scores of each DMS set in the same protein.
The average correlations observed over all pairs of analyses was 0.66.
correlations from 0.94 (bla(a)/(b)) to 0.34 (PTEN(a)/(b))

In this study, we use 31 previously published deep mutational scanning (DMS) experiments, which provide quantitative, independent phenotypic measurements for large numbers of single amino acid substitutions, in order to benchmark and compare 46 different VEPs.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: DMS reproducibility assessed; VEP predictions available.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which VEPs best predict DMS scores?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: VEPs that capture evolutionary constraints will correlate best with DMS.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Calculate Spearman rank correlation between DMS scores and VEP predictions for human and non-human proteins.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Computed Spearman rank correlations between DMS scores and predictions of 46 VEPs across 31 DMS datasets.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Analyze which VEP ranked top overall.

evidence.quote_or_span: we calculated the Spearman's rank correlation between the independent DMS scores for each protein and the predictions of every VEP
we calculated the Spearman's rank correlation between the independent DMS scores for each protein and the predictions of every VEP

In this study, we use 31 previously published deep mutational scanning (DMS) experiments, which provide quantitative, independent phenotypic measurements for large numbers of single amino acid substitutions, in order to benchmark and compare 46 different VEPs.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Correlations computed between DMS and VEPs.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which VEP is the top performer?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: DeepSequence may rank top due to its unsupervised approach.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Examine ranking of VEPs by correlation, focusing on DeepSequence.
evolution_trajectory.observation: DeepSequence ranked top overall, especially for human proteins (top 5 in 7/13 datasets); bootstrapping confirmed significance (p<0.001).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Investigate DeepSequence's failure on viral proteins and robustness.

evidence.quote_or_span: DeepSequence was the overall top performing method for predicting DMS results in the human proteins, showing the highest correlations out of all predictors for ADRB2, CALM1 and PTEN(b), and ranking within the top five predictors for 7 of the 13 DMS datasets.

we used a bootstrapping approach and recalculated the ranking by re-sampling all DMS datasets with replacement 1000 times. Strikingly we found that DeepSequence always ranked the highest, showing that it is statistically better than all other predictors (p<0.001).

evolution_trajectory.state_before: DeepSequence top overall but performance on viral proteins unknown.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Does DeepSequence perform well on all protein types?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: DeepSequence may fail on proteins with low sequence diversity.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Examine DeepSequence performance on viral proteins.
evolution_trajectory.observation: DeepSequence performed poorly on viral proteins, ranking second to last, due to insufficient sequence diversity in alignments.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Check if DeepSequence's top ranking is robust to hyperparameter optimization.

evidence.quote_or_span: DeepSequence performs poorly for viral proteins, ranking second to last out of all predictors tested. This is consistent with the original publication, where the creators report poor performance on viral proteins due to insufficient sequence diversity within the alignments used

evolution_trajectory.state_before: DeepSequence top but may be overfit to its training proteins.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Is DeepSequence's success due to hyperparameter optimization on seen proteins?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: DeepSequence's performance is not simply due to hyperparameter optimization.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Repeat ranking excluding proteins from original DeepSequence study.
evolution_trajectory.observation: DeepSequence still ranked top, suggesting robustness to hyperparameter optimization.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Compare structural vs sequence-based VEPs.

evidence.quote_or_span: we repeated the ranking process using only DMS datasets from proteins that were not included in the original DeepSequence study (Table EV9). Importantly, DeepSequence still ranks top among all predictors, suggesting that its apparent success is not simply due to hyperparameter optimisation.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Sequence-based VEPs like DeepSequence perform well; structural VEPs also exist.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Do structural predictors outperform sequence-based methods?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Structural information may improve predictions, but evolutionary conservation dominates.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Compare performance of structural predictors (PolyPhen-2, S3D-PROF, SNP&GOs3D, DEOGEN2, MPC) with top sequence-based methods.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Structural predictors did not outperform top sequence-based methods; in proteins with partial structural coverage, performance difference between structured and unstructured regions was comparable for structural and sequence-based methods.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Evaluate DMS and VEPs for disease variant classification.

evidence.quote_or_span: overall they do not perform better than the top-performing sequence-based methods. We also find that, in proteins with partial coverage of high-resolution structures, the difference in performance between areas of structural coverage and areas with no coverage is comparable between structural methods and pure sequence-based methods
It is interesting that the inclusion of protein structural models did not appear to be particularly useful for the VEPs.
It may be that the value of evolutionary information simply dwarfs any contribution from the inclusion of structure
the inclusion of structural information does not markedly improve the performance of VEPs

While evolutionary conservation is widely accepted to be the most predictive feature used in variant effect prediction, some VEPs also integrate features derived from experimentally determined protein structures (PolyPhen-2, S3D-PROF, SNP&GOs3D, DEOGEN2 and MPC).

evolution_trajectory.state_before: VEPs ranked by DMS correlation; disease variant classification ability unknown.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can DMS or VEPs better distinguish pathogenic from benign variants?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: DMS may outperform VEPs in disease variant classification.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform ROC analysis using ClinVar pathogenic and gnomAD benign variants for six human proteins.
evolution_trajectory.observation: DMS outperformed all VEPs for 4/6 human proteins (P53, TPK1, CALM1, HRAS). For PTEN and BRCA1, VEPs performed comparably or better.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Identify which DMS phenotypes perform best.

evidence.quote_or_span: an experimental DMS metric performed better than any of the 46 VEPs for four of the six human proteins
ROC AUC values for DMS datasets and VEPs in distinguishing between pathogenic missense variants from ClinVar and putatively benign variants from gnomAD for six human disease genes (A-F).
PTEN ClinVar: 108, gnomAD: 78
P53 ClinVar: 157, gnomAD: 53

evolution_trajectory.state_before: DMS outperforms VEPs in disease classification; different DMS assay types exist.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which DMS assay type is most effective for disease mutation identification?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Competitive growth assays may be superior because they reflect general loss of function.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Compare performance of competitive growth assays with other DMS phenotypes and computational predictors.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Competitive growth assays ranked above all computational predictors for three of four proteins where available (BRCA1, HRAS, PTEN).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Acknowledge limitations of DMS for variant prioritization.

evidence.quote_or_span: those DMS experiments based upon competitive growth assays appear to perform particularly well, ranking above all computational predictors for three of the four proteins where they are available.
Growth rate is likely to be a very general experimental phenotype that will reflect any loss of function occurring at a molecular level.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: DMS shows promise but has known limitations.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: What are the limitations of using DMS for variant effect prediction?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: DMS coverage is limited, and some assays miss certain disease mechanisms.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Discuss limitations: small fraction of human residues covered, some assays miss dominant-negative effects (e.g., PTEN VAMP-seq).
evolution_trajectory.observation: DMS applicability limited by small fraction of human protein residues covered; PTEN VAMP-seq misses dominant-negative effects.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Conclude with recommendations.

evidence.quote_or_span: the phenotypic screen for PTEN(a) measures protein abundance in the cell by fluorescence of EGFP bound to the protein (Matreyek et al, 2018). This technique, called VAMP-seq, identifies thermodynamically unstable variants; however, this may fail to capture disease mechanisms acting through interaction disruption and loss or gain of function unrelated to destabilisation.
it was noted in this study that dominant-negative variants were not significantly different from wild type
The applicability of DMS data for direct variant prioritisation is currently limited by the small fraction of human protein residues for which DMS experiments have been performed.

validation_methods: Spearman correlation between DMS and VEP predictions
ROC analysis for disease variant classification
success_verification.final_conclusion: DeepSequence is the top VEP overall, especially for human proteins, but fails on viral proteins due to low sequence diversity. DMS outperforms VEPs in disease variant classification for most proteins, with competitive growth assays being the best DMS phenotype. However, DMS coverage is limited, and some assays miss certain disease mechanisms.
limitations: DMS coverage limited to small fraction of human residues
Some DMS assays miss disease mechanisms (e.g., PTEN VAMP-seq misses dominant-negative effects)
DeepSequence performance depends on MSA database representation

source.title: Ensemble-based enzyme design can recapitulate the effects of laboratory directed evolution in silico

initial_request.research_problem: De novo enzymes have low catalytic efficiencies, requiring directed evolution to improve activity. The study aims to understand how directed evolution alters conformational ensembles to enhance catalysis, and to use these insights to improve computational enzyme design by incorporating ensemble modeling of crystallographic data.
initial_request.known_context: De novo enzymes have been successfully designed but exhibit low catalytic efficiencies. Directed evolution can improve activity, but the structural basis is not fully understood.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: De novo enzymes have been successfully designed but exhibit low catalytic efficiencies.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: The structural basis for how directed evolution improves catalytic efficiency is not fully understood.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Room-temperature crystal structures of HG-series Kemp eliminases will reveal conformational changes that enhance catalysis.
evolution_trajectory.decision: State the problem and propose solving room-temperature structures.
evolution_trajectory.observation: De novo enzymes have low catalytic efficiencies, requiring directed evolution to improve activity.
