evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Counter-selection will identify Van2 with altered specificity to vanillin.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform counter-selection and characterize isolated mutants.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Van2 (I110V, M113S, N114A) responded to vanillin but not PCA. GC-MS confirmed vanillin metabolism to vanillyl alcohol, which did not induce, validating vanillin detection.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Improve Van2 performance by reducing aldehyde reduction.

evidence.quote_or_span: the mutant Van2 was identified to respond to vanillin, but not the parental effector PCA
Investigation of vanillin detection was made by GC-MS to determine if the signal was coming from vanillin itself or a metabolic conversion product by E. coli.
no fluorescent signal was observed for the latter, validating the vanillin detection by the Van2 system

An induction test was then made with increasing concentrations of vanillyl alcohol and no fluorescent signal was observed for the latter, validating the vanillin detection by the Van2 system (Additional file 1: Figure S4B).
PcaV N114A, PcaV M113S and PcaV I110V N114A led to reduced PCA detection without ligand specificity change.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Van2 biosensor validated but vanillin metabolism reduces performance.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can reducing aldehyde reduction improve Van2 performance?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Using RARE strain will improve Van2 biosensor performance.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Test Van2 in RARE strain and characterize performance with various aldehydes.
evolution_trajectory.observation: RARE strain improved Van2 biosensor performance; Van2 detected vanillin with 7.7-fold dynamic range and other aldehydes with >6-fold at 2.5 mM.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Characterize biophysical properties of Van2.

evidence.quote_or_span: the RARE strain had the best performance and was used for further performance characterisation of the Van2 biosensor

All strains displayed enhanced output signal in response to vanillin compare to the wild-type, however the RARE strain had the best performance and was used for further performance characterisation of the Van2 biosensor (Additional file 1: Figure S4C).

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Van2 shows higher basal expression than PcaV.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Why does Van2 have higher basal expression?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Van2 has lower DNA affinity than PcaV.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Measure DNA binding affinity of PcaV and Van2 by SPR.
evolution_trajectory.observation: SPR showed Van2 has lower DNA affinity (Kd 86.7 nM) than PcaV (5.1 nM), explaining higher basal expression.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Deconvolute contributions of individual mutations.

evidence.quote_or_span: PcaV showed experimental affinity to the probe with a Kd of 5.1 nM
Van2 showed a Kd of 86.7 nM, lower than the observed PcaV affinity
PcaV showed experimental affinity to the PV Bio probe with Kd of 5.05 nM. Van2 showed a Kd of 86.7 nM.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Van2 has three mutations (I110V, M113S, N114A).
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which mutations are essential for vanillin detection and repression?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: S113 and A114 are key for vanillin detection, V110 for repression.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform point mutational analysis of Van2 mutations.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Point mutational analysis revealed S113 and A114 are essential for vanillin detection, while V110 is important for maintaining repression in the absence of effector.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Test further mutations at position 110.

evidence.quote_or_span: the amino acids S113 and A114 have a major role on the detection of vanillin and the amino acid V110 is important to maintain repression in the absence of effector
Intermediate mutants with one or two point mutations ranging from PcaV (I110, M113, N114) to Van2 (V110, S113, A114) were generated by site-directed mutagenesis.
The mutants were tested in the biosensor system with increasing concentrations of vanillin, PCA and vanillic acid (Fig. 5a).
The substitution of the amino acid I110 individually (PcaV I110V) did not affect the performance of the biosensor or the ligand specificity.
Specifically the amino acids S113 and A114 appear to play a role in the detection of vanillin by the PcaV double mutant (M113S N114A) and by Van2.

Docking of the models for Van2 and PcaV M113S N114A with vanillin showed, in the 2nd best docked conformation, an interacting with S113 of Van2 via a hydrogen bond between the aldehyde functional group of vanillin and the hydroxyl group of the serine.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: V110 identified as important for repression.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Is V110 optimal for vanillin response?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Other amino acids at position 110 may improve performance.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Test further point mutations at position 110 (Van3-Van6).
evolution_trajectory.observation: Further point mutations at position 110 (Van3-Van6) all show high basal expression and poor vanillin response (<2-fold), indicating V110 is optimal.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Conclude on Van2 specificity and limitations.

evidence.quote_or_span: The overall performance for vanillin detection was lower for all the new Van aTF mutants (<2-fold) compared to Van2 (>6-fold)

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Van2 shows in vivo specificity for vanillin over PCA.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Why does Van2 not respond to PCA in vivo?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: In vitro, Van2 still responds to PCA, but in vivo it does not, likely due to physiological processes such as membrane transport selectivity.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Compare in vitro and in vivo PCA response.
evolution_trajectory.observation: In vitro, Van2 still responds to PCA, but in vivo it does not, likely due to physiological processes such as membrane transport selectivity.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Acknowledge limitation.

evidence.quote_or_span: The absence of an in vivo response to PCA by the Van2 biosensor could most likely be explained by physiological processes such as membrane transport selectivity affecting the intracellular availability of the effector inside the cell.

success_verification.final_conclusion: Van2 biosensor detects vanillin and other aromatic aldehydes with up to 7.7-fold dynamic range in RARE strain, but has higher basal expression due to lower DNA affinity and in vitro PCA response indicates physiological limitations.
limitations: Vanillin metabolism reduces performance in wild-type E. coli
Narrow dynamic range compared to parental PCA biosensor
In vitro PCA response contradicts in vivo specificity

source.title: Generation of an E. coli platform strain for improved sucrose utilization using adaptive laboratory evolution

initial_request.research_problem: Engineered E. coli K-12 strains grow slowly on sucrose, limiting industrial use. ALE was applied to improve growth rate and sucrose uptake, aiming to generate platform strains comparable to E. coli W.
initial_request.target_object: E. coli K-12 MG1655 engineered with csc genes (MGcscBKA, MGcscBKAp) and E. coli W
initial_request.known_context: Only a few E. coli strains metabolize sucrose, and those that can are typically slow growing or pathogenic. Previous engineered strains had slow growth and instability.
constraints: Use of M9 minimal medium with 20 g/L sucrose
Serial transfer during exponential phase
~40 days of evolution
input_data: Starting strains: MGcscBKA, MGcscBKAp, E. coli W
quantifiable_goals: Increase growth rate on sucrose
Improve sucrose uptake rate
Achieve growth comparable to E. coli W

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Only a few E. coli strains metabolize sucrose, and those that can are typically slow growing or pathogenic. Previous engineered strains had slow growth and instability.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can adaptive laboratory evolution improve the growth rate and sucrose uptake of engineered E. coli K-12 strains to levels comparable to E. coli W?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Serial passage under sucrose selection will enrich for mutations that enhance sucrose utilization and growth rate.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform ALE on engineered K-12 strains and E. coli W in sucrose minimal medium.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Only a few characterized Escherichia coli strains are able to metabolize sucrose, and those that can are typically slow growing or pathogenic strains. Most of the previous approaches to engineer E. coli to use sucrose as a carbon source were hampered by slow growth rates and phenotypic instability.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Design strains with integrated csc genes for ALE.

evidence.quote_or_span: only a few characterized Escherichia coli strains are able to metabolize sucrose, and those that can are typically slow growing or pathogenic strains
Most of the previous approaches to engineer E. coli to use sucrose as a carbon source were hampered by slow growth rates and phenotypic instability

Moreover, the developed platform strains are suitable as fermentation chassis with an extended capability to also rapidly utilize glucose to the same level as strains evolved to grow solely on glucose $[22]$ ; (2) this study establishes a transferable sucrose utilization cassette for use in K-12 strains where sucrose utilization is not native; and (3) the identification of key reproducibly-occurring mutations using multiple parallel replicate ALE experiments.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: E. coli K-12 MG1655 cannot utilize sucrose.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to introduce sucrose utilization genes into K-12.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Chromosomal integration of the csc gene cluster from E. coli W will enable sucrose metabolism.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Integrate cscB, cscK, cscA at the 186 attB site using pOSIP-KO, creating MGcscBKA and MGcscBKAp (with cscB Q353H SNP).
evolution_trajectory.observation: Two constructs inserted into the K-12 MG1655 host genome differed by a SNP mutation in the cscB gene, resulting in the residue change Q353H, and were labeled as MGcscBKA and MGcscBKAp.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Proceed to ALE with these strains.

evidence.quote_or_span: the integration site of csc DNA sequence was into E. coli 186 attB site using pOSIP-KO integration module

The two constructs inserted into the K-12 MG1655 host genome differed by a SNP mutation in the cscB gene, resulting in the residue change Q353H, and were labeled as MGcscBKA and MGcscBKAp (see Additional file 2: Text S1 for a detailed description).
MGcscBKA derivative with a variant SNP in cscB Q353H was abbreviated MGcscB-KAp [16].
coli strains; two genetic constructs of K-12 MG1655 with csc gene cassette MGcscBKA and MGcscBKAp and E.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Engineered strains MGcscBKA, MGcscBKAp, and E. coli W are available.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Will these strains adapt to faster growth on sucrose?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Serial passage in sucrose minimal medium will select for faster-growing mutants.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Evolve three independent populations of each strain in M9 + 20 g/L sucrose, with serial transfers during exponential phase, for ~40 days.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Bacterial cells were serially passaged during exponential growth phase for approximately 40 days using an automated liquid-handler platform. Three independent populations of each starting strain were evolved in batch and in parallel under the strict selection pressure of continuous exponential growth for approximately 40 days.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Analyze growth rate improvements.

evidence.quote_or_span: Bacterial cells were serially passaged during exponential growth phase for approximately 40 days using an automated liquid-handler platform
Three independent populations of each starting strain were evolved in batch and in parallel under the strict selection pressure of continuous exponential growth for approximately 40 days
coli strains: a K-12 MG1655cscBKA, b K-12 MG1655cscBKAp, and c W were evolved on 20 g/L sucrose minimal medium.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: ALE completed for all populations.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Did growth rates increase?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Evolved populations will show higher growth rates on sucrose.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Measure growth rates of endpoint clones on sucrose M9.
evolution_trajectory.observation: There was one observed fitness jump (i.e., increase in growth rate) for all the populations across all independent ALE replicates. The increases in growth rates on sucrose M9 were between 1.17 and 1.57-fold for MGcscBKA clones, 1.25–1.38-fold for MGcscBKAp, and 1.06–1.22-fold for E. coli W.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Note one clone without improvement; test growth on glucose.

evidence.quote_or_span: There was one observed fitness jump (i.e., increase in growth rate) for all the populations across all independent ALE replicates.
The increases in growth rates on sucrose M9 were between 1.17 and 1.57-fold for MGcscBKA clones, 1.25–1.38-fold for MGcscBKAp, and 1.06–1.22-fold for E. coli W

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Most clones improved, but one MGcscBKA clone (#2) did not.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Why did clone #2 not improve?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Clone #2 may have acquired no beneficial mutations or a detrimental mutation.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Report the outlier.
evolution_trajectory.observation: There was one isolated clone that had the same fitness, i.e. growth rate, as the starting strain from MGcscBKA (the ‘#2’ clone).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Proceed to test growth on glucose.

evidence.quote_or_span: there was one isolated clone that had the same fitness, i.e. growth rate, as the starting strain from MGcscBKA (the ‘#2’ clone)

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Growth improvements observed on sucrose.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Are the mutations sucrose-specific or generally beneficial?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: If mutations are generally beneficial, evolved clones will also grow faster on glucose.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Measure growth rates of evolved clones on glucose M9.
evolution_trajectory.observation: All clones demonstrated an increased growth rate over the start strains without any significant changes to the final cellular density (gCDW/L). The vast majority of the sucrose-evolved strains also displayed a fast growth phenotype on glucose is an indication that most mutations acquired during the sucrose ALE experiment were beneficial for growth on both sugars.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Sequence genomes to identify mutations.

evidence.quote_or_span: all clones demonstrated an increased growth rate over the start strains without any significant changes to the final cellular density (gCDW/L)

the vast majority of the sucrose-evolved strains also displayed a fast growth phenotype on glucose is an indication that most mutations acquired during the sucrose ALE experiment were beneficial for growth on both sugars

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Evolved clones show improved growth.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: What mutations are responsible?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Whole genome sequencing will reveal causal mutations.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Sequence endpoint clones and populations.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Endpoint population samples were streaked on agar plates to select single clones for whole genome resequencing. Whole genome sequencing was used to determine the genetic basis of the improved fitness phenotypes. The most predominantly mutated genes or intergenic regions identified across multiple independent replicates in the evolved K-12 strains were related to the RNA polymerase subunits rpoB and rpoC, as well as orotate phosphoribosyltransferase, pyrE. Strain-specific adaptive mutations in E. coli W clones were predominantly affecting a metabolic regulation pathway targeting the csc operon and cell wall biosynthesis through rod shape determining proteins.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Compare mutations between K-12 and W.

evidence.quote_or_span: Endpoint population samples were streaked on agar plates to select single clones for whole genome resequencing
Whole genome sequencing was used to determine the genetic basis of the improved fitness phenotypes

The most predominantly mutated genes or intergenic regions identified across multiple independent replicates in the evolved K-12 strains were related to the RNA polymerase subunits rpoB and rpoC, as well as orotate phosphoribosyltransferase, pyrE.

Strain-specific adaptive mutations in E. coli W clones were predominantly affecting a metabolic regulation pathway targeting the csc operon and cell wall biosynthesis through rod shape determining proteins.

Whole genome sequencing of evolved clones and populations revealed that two genetic regions were frequently mutated in the K-12 strains; the global transcription regulatory genes rpoB and rpoC, and the metabolic region related to a pyrimidine biosynthetic deficiency in K-12 attributed to pyrE expression.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Mutations identified in K-12 and W.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Do K-12 and W share adaptive strategies?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Different genetic backgrounds may lead to distinct mutational paths.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Compare mutation sets.
evolution_trajectory.observation: There were no shared key mutations found between the K-12 and E. coli W experiments.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Analyze K-12 mutations for parallel evolution and known effects.

evidence.quote_or_span: there were no shared key mutations found between the K-12 and E. coli W experiments

evolution_trajectory.state_before: K-12 mutations in rpoB, rpoC, pyrE-rph identified.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Are these mutations known to be beneficial in other contexts?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: rpoB/rpoC mutations are common in K-12 ALE; pyrE-rph mutations alleviate pyrimidine starvation.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Review literature and analyze mutation patterns.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Mutations in rpoB and rpoC genes have previously been found repeatedly across all different K-12 ALE experiments, indicating a very high level of parallel evolution. K-12 strains are known to possess a frame shift in rph which leads to pyrimidine starvation on minimal media and can be alleviated by mutations.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate causality via reverse engineering.

evidence.quote_or_span: mutations in rpoB and rpoC genes have previously been found repeatedly across all different K-12 ALE experiments, indicating a very high level of parallel evolution
K-12 strains are known to possess a frame shift in rph which leads to pyrimidine starvation on minimal media and can be alleviated by mutations
Interestingly, mutations in rpoB and rpoC genes have previously been found repeatedly across all different K-12 ALE experiments, indicating a very high level of parallel evolution [22, 32, 33].
The Escherichia coli K-12 "wild types" W3110 and MG1655 have an rph frameshift mutation that leads to pyrimidine starvation due to low pyrE expression levels.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Candidate mutations identified.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Do these mutations directly cause fitness improvement?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Introducing these mutations into the parental strain will recapitulate the fitness advantage.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Construct single and double mutants (pyrE-rph deletion, rpoB E672K, rpoB S621F, rpoC T1045P) and measure growth.
evolution_trajectory.observation: The comparative analysis of reverse engineered strains showed that mutated genes identified in this ALE study conferred a fitness advantage over the starting strain. When multiple mutations were present in a strain, such double mutant strains conferred a higher fitness advantage over single mutants with the identical mutations. Specifically, a strain with a mutation in the intragenic region between pyrE/rph genes and in rpoB (E672K) had higher fitness than strains with either single mutations.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Test if fitness advantage is general across carbon sources.

evidence.quote_or_span: The comparative analysis of reverse engineered strains showed that mutated genes identified in this ALE study conferred a fitness advantage over the starting strain
when multiple mutations were present in a strain, such double mutant strains conferred a higher fitness advantage over single mutants with the identical mutations
Specifically, a strain with a mutation in the intragenic region between pyrE/rph genes and in rpoB (E672K) had higher fitness than strains with either single mutations.
All rpoC mutations originated from this study, as well as the rpoB S621F with the pyrE-rph deletion mutation (i.
There was one co-occurrence of a rpoC mutation, T1045P (ACC→CCC), which was observed in experiments starting from both K-12 strains.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Reverse engineered mutants show fitness advantage on sucrose.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Is the advantage specific to sucrose?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Mutations are generally beneficial, so advantage should also be seen on glucose.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Measure growth of reverse engineered strains on glucose.
evolution_trajectory.observation: The fitness advantage of all tested mutations over the starting strain was observed when grown on M9 with either sucrose or glucose as carbon sources, and the fitness level increases for a given mutated strain were roughly similar when grown on sucrose or glucose.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Test evolved clones on industrially relevant sugarcane juice.

evidence.quote_or_span: the fitness advantage of all tested mutations over the starting strain was observed when grown on M9 with either sucrose or glucose as carbon sources, and the fitness level increases for a given mutated strain were roughly similar when grown on sucrose or glucose

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Evolved clones validated in defined medium.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Will the improved phenotypes translate to a complex feedstock?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Evolved clones will show improved growth and sucrose uptake on sugarcane juice.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Characterize endpoint clones on sugarcane juice (SCJ) medium.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Each endpoint clone was characterized on a raw feedstock, sugarcane juice (SCJ) medium. Overall, there was an increase in the SUR for the K-12 MG1655 and E. coli W clones. Best K-12 clone had 1.72-fold growth increase and 1.40-fold uptake increase. E. coli W clones had 1.4-fold uptake increase but no significant growth increase.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Note limitations: E. coli W growth not improved, acetate production, single batch.

evidence.quote_or_span: Extracellular metabolites and physiological properties
each endpoint clone was characterized on a raw feedstock, sugarcane juice (SCJ) medium
Overall, there was an increase in the SUR for the K-12 MG1655 and E.

validation_methods: Growth assays on sucrose, glucose, and sugarcane juice
success_verification.final_conclusion: ALE successfully improved sucrose utilization in engineered K-12 strains, with key mutations in rpoB, rpoC, and pyrE-rph validated as causal. Evolved K-12 clones showed improved growth and uptake on sugarcane juice, while E. coli W clones improved uptake but not growth rate.
limitations: E. coli W did not show significant growth rate increase on SCJ.
Some clones had high acetate production.
Only one batch of SCJ tested.
One MGcscBKA clone (#2) showed no improvement.

source.title: Accelerated directed evolution of dye-decolorizing peroxidase using a bacterial extracellular protein secretion system (BENNY)

initial_request.research_problem: Enhance H2O2 tolerance of dye-decolorizing peroxidase 4 (DyP4) from Pleurotus ostreatus using directed evolution, accelerated by a bacterial extracellular protein secretion system (BENNY).
initial_request.known_context: DyP4 is a haem peroxidase with potential for lignin valorization but is sensitive to H2O2 inactivation. Methionine oxidation was hypothesized as a cause.
constraints: Must maintain proper folding and activity
Directed evolution must be high-throughput
input_data: Crystal structure of DyP4 F194Y variant (PDB 6FSK)
quantifiable_goals: Improve H2O2 tolerance (IC50)
Maintain or improve catalytic efficiency (kcat/Km)

evolution_trajectory.state_before: DyP4 is sensitive to H2O2, limiting its application.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Methionine oxidation may cause H2O2-mediated inactivation.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Substituting methionine residues (M43, M77, M253) with leucine or phenylalanine will improve H2O2 tolerance.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Create methionine-substituted variants (M43L, M77L, M253L, M253F) to test the hypothesis.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Four methionine-substituted variants were created.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Variants need to be tested for H2O2 tolerance.

evidence.quote_or_span: In an attempt to enhance the $H_{2}O_{2}$ tolerance of DyP4, we created 4 methionine-substituted variants (M43L M77L M253L and M253F).

To construct M43L (primers M43L-F and M43L-R), M253L (primers M253L-F and M253L-R) and M253F (primers M253F-F and M253F-R) variants, partially overlapping primers and Q5 high-fidelity DNA polymerase (New England Biolabs) were used in a 2-stage PCR.
To construct M77L variant (primers M77L-F and M77L-R), DNA polymerase used was substituted with PfuUltra high-fidelity DNA polymerase AD (Agilent Technologies).
All methionine-substituted variants were created using pET-24a(+)-DyP4 as template.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Methionine-substituted variants created but not yet characterized for H2O2 tolerance.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Whether methionine substitution improves H2O2 tolerance is unknown.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Methionine substitution will increase H2O2 tolerance.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Test H2O2 tolerance of M43L, M77L, M253L, M253F variants.
evolution_trajectory.observation: All variants were properly folded and active, but none showed improved H2O2 tolerance.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Rational design failed; switch to directed evolution approach.

evidence.quote_or_span: In an attempt to enhance the $H_{2}O_{2}$ tolerance of DyP4, we created 4 methionine-substituted variants (M43L M77L M253L and M253F).

To construct M43L (primers M43L-F and M43L-R), M253L (primers M253L-F and M253L-R) and M253F (primers M253F-F and M253F-R) variants, partially overlapping primers and Q5 high-fidelity DNA polymerase (New England Biolabs) were used in a 2-stage PCR.
To construct M77L variant (primers M77L-F and M77L-R), DNA polymerase used was substituted with PfuUltra high-fidelity DNA polymerase AD (Agilent Technologies).
All methionine-substituted variants were created using pET-24a(+)-DyP4 as template.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Rational methionine substitution failed to improve H2O2 tolerance.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need a high-throughput method to screen for improved variants.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: An OsmY-based bacterial extracellular protein secretion system (BENNY) can streamline directed evolution by enabling extracellular secretion and bypassing cell lysis.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Design and construct OsmY-DyP4 fusion for secretion in E. coli BL21(DE3).
evolution_trajectory.observation: OsmY-DyP4 fusion successfully secreted into culture medium, bypassing induction and cell disruption.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate the BENNY-assisted assay for high-throughput screening.

evidence.quote_or_span: To accelerate DyP4 evolution, we used a bacterial extracellular protein secretion system (BENNY) based on the Escherichia coli osmotically-inducible protein Y (OsmY).
this is the first report of applying OsmY to streamline directed evolution workflow and to direct extracellular secretion of a haem protein such as DyP4.
To streamline the DyP4 directed evolution workflow, we fused E. coli OsmY to the N-terminus of DyP4 and investigated protein secretion
By using OsmY as secretory carrier and fusion protein expression in AIM, we bypassed both (i) protein induction (i.e. addition of IPTG inducer) and (ii) cell disruption

evolution_trajectory.state_before: BENNY system designed but not validated for high-throughput screening.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to assess assay variability and edge effects in 96-well format.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: BENNY-assisted ABTS assay in 96-well plates will have acceptable variability for screening.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Validate assay by measuring coefficient of variance (CV) and edge effects.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Apparent CV 8%, true CV 22% (11% excluding border wells); edge effects identified.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Assay is suitable for screening with precautions for edge effects; proceed to library design.

evidence.quote_or_span: Based on ABTS assay, we obtained an apparent coefficient of variance (CV) of 8% for OsmY-DyP4
If all bordering wells were excluded, the apparent and true CVs were reduced to 4% and 11%, respectively.
In the 1st and 2nd rounds, we screened 558 clones [2 96-well plates from each epPCR condition (H, M, L)].
coli as protein expression host, protein variants are often expressed in 96-well format.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Validated BENNY-assisted assay ready for screening.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to generate diverse mutant libraries for directed evolution.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Three epPCR conditions (H, M, L) with varying error rates will produce libraries with a range of mutation frequencies suitable for directed evolution.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Design epPCR conditions targeting only DyP4 sequence.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Three epPCR conditions designed to generate libraries with high, medium, and low mutation rates.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Use these conditions to generate libraries for screening.

evidence.quote_or_span: Three error-prone polymerase chain reaction (epPCR) conditions were used in this study: high (H), medium (M) and low (L) error rates.
we created random mutagenesis libraries of OsmY-DyP4 using 3 epPCR conditions (H, M and L).
mutations were only targeted to the sequence encoding for DyP4, leaving the OsmY sequence unaltered.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: epPCR libraries designed.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to screen for variants with improved H2O2 tolerance.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Screening at 4 mM H2O2 (16× optimal) will select for variants with enhanced tolerance.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform 3 rounds of epPCR screening with increasing H2O2 concentration (4 mM) and use best variants as parents for next round.

tool_or_method.name: Directed evolution by epPCR and screening

evolution_trajectory.observation: Screening yielded progressively improved variants; best variants OsmY-1D2 (round 1), OsmY-2A5 (round 2), and OsmY-3F6 (round 3) were identified.

initial_request.research_problem: Computationally design improved variants of Arabidopsis thaliana ent-copalyl diphosphate synthase (ent-CPS) to enhance gibberellin biosynthesis and potentially boost tanshinone production, without experimental validation.
initial_request.known_context: ent-CPS catalyzes the conversion of geranylgeranyl diphosphate (GGPS) to ent-copalyl diphosphate, a key step in gibberellin biosynthesis. Improved ent-CPS activity could increase endogenous hormone levels and boost tanshinone yield in transformed plants.

source.title: Mosaic RAS/MAPK variants cause sporadic vascular malformations which respond to targeted therapy

initial_request.research_problem: Sporadic vascular malformations (VMs) have high morbidity and limited treatment options, and a substantial proportion of patients have no mutations in known associated genes.
initial_request.known_context: Therapeutic options are severely limited, and multidisciplinary management remains challenging, particularly for high-flow arteriovenous malformations (AVM).

evolution_trajectory.observation: Hypothesis formulated that mosaic-activating variants in RAS/MAPK pathway genes cause VM.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Design study to test hypothesis using ultradeep sequencing.

evidence.quote_or_span: Sporadic vascular malformations (VMs) are complex congenital anomalies of blood vessels that lead to stroke, life-threatening bleeds, disfigurement, overgrowth, and/or pain.

source.title: Integrating rare genetic variants into pharmacogenetic drug response predictions

initial_request.research_problem: Current pharmacogenetic predictions rely on common variants and miss rare variants, leaving substantial unexplained inter-individual variability in drug response. The study aims to systematically evaluate the contribution of rare genetic variants in pharmacogenes to functional variability and drug response predictions.
initial_request.known_context: Over 90% of genetic variability in pharmacogenes is rare, but its impact on drug pharmacokinetics has not been systematically evaluated.
constraints: Variants with less than 10,000 called high-quality alleles were not considered.
Only exonic variants were included, with six non-exonic variants from 1000 Genomes added.
input_data: Exome sequencing data from 60,706 unrelated individuals from ExAC
quantifiable_goals: Quantify the contribution of rare variants to functional variability in pharmacogenes
Assess the impact of rare variants on drug response predictions for five drugs

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Pharmacogenetic predictions rely on common variants, but over 90% of genetic variability in pharmacogenes is rare and its impact is unknown.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: The contribution of rare genetic variants in pharmacogenes to functional variability and drug response has not been systematically evaluated.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Rare genetic variants in pharmacogenes significantly contribute to functional variability and can improve drug response predictions.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Systematically evaluate rare variant contribution using exome data and a computational prediction framework.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Over 90% of pharmacogene variability is rare, but its impact is unassessed.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Design a study to map rare variants and predict their functional impact.

evidence.quote_or_span: over 90% of the overall genetic variability in pharmacogenes is allotted to rare genetic variants, but the impact of rare genetic variability on drug pharmacokinetics has not been systematically evaluated

evolution_trajectory.state_before: The impact of rare variants is unknown.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to map rare variants in pharmacogenes and predict their functional effects.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Using exome data from 60,706 individuals and an ADME-optimized prediction framework will reveal the importance of rare variants.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Analyze exome data from ExAC for 208 pharmacogenes and apply a computational prediction framework with 92% sensitivity and 95% specificity.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Study designed to map variability and estimate importance of rare vs common variants.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Identify variants in the 208 pharmacogenes.

evidence.quote_or_span: To understand the global importance of rare pharmacogenetic gene variants, we mapped the variability in 208 pharmacogenes by analyzing exome sequencing data from 60,706 unrelated individuals and estimated the importance of rare and common genetic variants using a computational prediction framework optimized for pharmacogenetic assessments.
Human sequencing data from 60,706 unrelated individuals was obtained from the Exome Aggregation Consortium (ExAC) database
Variants with less than 10,000 called high-quality alleles were not considered.
the model achieved 92% sensitivity and 95% specificity for loss-of-function and functionally neutral variants, respectively

evolution_trajectory.state_before: 69,923 variants identified, mostly rare.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Whether rare variants are enriched for functional effects compared to common variants.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Rare variants are enriched for deleterious functional effects.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Compare functional predictions for rare vs common missense variants.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Rare pharmacogenetic variants were strongly enriched in mutations predicted to cause functional alterations.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Quantify functional variants per individual.

evidence.quote_or_span: rare pharmacogenetic variants were strongly enriched in mutations predicted to cause functional alterations
Rare genetic polymorphisms in pharmacogenes are enriched in variants predicted to cause functional alterations.
57,773 (83%) of these 69,923 variants we identified were novel
we identified 69,923 variants distributed across transporter genes

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Rare variants enriched for functional effects.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How many functional variants does each individual carry, and what fraction are rare?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Individuals carry a substantial number of rare functional variants.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Calculate average functional variants per individual and rare fraction.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Average 40.6 functional variants per individual, 4.4 (10.8%) rare.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Design drug-specific prediction integration.

evidence.quote_or_span: each individual harbored on average a total of 40.6 putatively functional variants
Rare variants accounted for 4.4 (10.8%) of these functional variants
each individual was found to harbor on average 4.4 rare functional variants

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Rare functional variants are present in individuals.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to incorporate rare variants into drug response predictions.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Integrating literature-based gene importance with genetic variability data will reveal drug-specific rare variant impacts.
evolution_trajectory.decision: For five drugs (warfarin, simvastatin, voriconazole, olanzapine, irinotecan), estimate relative importance of genes from literature and combine with variant data.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Method proposed to include rare variants into predictions for five drugs: warfarin, simvastatin, voriconazole, olanzapine, irinotecan.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Apply to warfarin.

evidence.quote_or_span: we considered it of importance to include rare variants into predictions of drug response.
We first estimated the relative importance of different genetic factors for drug metabolism phenotypes of the specific drugs based on extensive literature analysis.
we integrated these evaluations with our genetic variability data to derive assessments of the impact of rare genetic variants

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Method designed for drug-specific predictions.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Impact of rare variants on warfarin metabolism.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Rare variants in CYP2C9 and ABCB1 significantly affect warfarin metabolism.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Analyze rare variant contribution to warfarin-related genes.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Rare CYP2C9 variants: 2.1% allele frequency, 18.4% of functional variability; ABCB1: 1.3% frequency. Major impact on S-warfarin, minor on R-warfarin.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Apply to simvastatin.

evidence.quote_or_span: rare SNVs have a major impact on hepatic S-warfarin metabolism.
2.1% of CYP2C9 alleles are predicted to harbor rare variants with deleterious effects, accounting for 18.4% of the genetically encoded functional differences in CYP2C9 activity
rare variants with functional consequences in 1.3% of ABCB1 alleles

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Warfarin analysis complete.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Impact of rare variants on simvastatin myopathy risk.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Rare variants in SLCO1B1 and other genes add to common variant risk.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Analyze rare variant contribution to simvastatin-related genes.
evolution_trajectory.observation: SLCO1B1 rare variants: 1.2% frequency, 8.7% of variability; ABCB1: 1.3%, CYP3A4: 1.1%, ABCG2: 0.8%.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Apply to voriconazole.

evidence.quote_or_span: rare deleterious variants with an aggregated frequency of 1.2%, which are estimated to jointly explain 8.7% of the genetic basis of SLCO1B1 variability

rare variants are expected to contribute substantially to the metabolism and transport of simvastatin with an aggregated rare functional variant frequency of 1.3, 1.1, and 0.8% for ABCB1, CYP3A4, and ABCG2
rare variants with functional consequences in 1.3% of ABCB1 alleles

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Simvastatin analysis complete.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Impact of rare variants on voriconazole metabolism.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Rare CYP2C19 variants contribute but common variants dominate.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Analyze rare variant contribution to voriconazole-related genes.
evolution_trajectory.observation: CYP2C19 rare variants: 1.6% frequency, 4.4% of variability; common variants explain larger fraction.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Apply to olanzapine.

evidence.quote_or_span: rare deleterious variants with an aggregated frequency of 1.6%
rare variants are estimated to account for 4.4% of the overall genetic variability of CYP2C19 function

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Voriconazole analysis complete.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Impact of rare variants on olanzapine metabolism.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Rare variants dominate UGT1A4 and UGT2B10 but not CYP2D6, FMO3, CYP1A2.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Analyze rare variant contribution to olanzapine-related genes.
evolution_trajectory.observation: UGT1A4: 100% of variability from rare variants; UGT2B10: 47%; CYP2D6: 6.3%; FMO3: 5.5%; CYP1A2: 1.6%.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Apply to irinotecan.

evidence.quote_or_span: Rare variants substantially contribute to UGT1A4 and UGT2B10 variability and are predicted to account for 100 and 47% of the genetically encoded inter-individual variability in the functionality of these genes
we estimate that rare variants only explain 6.3, 5.5, and 1.6% of the variability in CYP2D6, FMO3, and CYP1A2, respectively.
The aggregated frequency of rare deleterious alleles in CES1 and CES2 were 1.5 and 0.4%, respectively, accounting for 19.6 and 100% of the functional genetic variants in these genes.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Drug-specific analyses complete.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Overall contribution of rare variants to drug metabolism phenotypes.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Rare variants explain a substantial part of unexplained variability, with gene- and drug-specific contributions.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Summarize findings across all drugs.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Rare variants contribute 18.4% for warfarin CYP2C9, >40% for irinotecan transport, 1.6-8.7% for others. Highly drug- and gene-specific.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Acknowledge limitations.

evidence.quote_or_span: Rare genetic variants contribute substantially to pharmacogenomic variability.
overall genetic variability as well as the fraction of functional variation that is allotted to rare variants differs considerably between genes.
our evaluations indicate that rare genetic variants in pharmacogenes have the potential to explain a substantial part of the unexplained genetic variability in drug metabolism phenotypes.

Rare variants are estimated to contribute significantly to the inter-individual variability of warfarin pharmacokinetics and irinotecan toxicity accounting for 18.4% of deleterious CYP2C9 alleles and >40% of the variability in irinotecan transport

the relative importance of rare variants is expected to be lower for the metabolism of simvastatin, voriconazole, and olanzapine for which rare variants only contribute between 1.6 and 8.7% of the key metabolic and/or transport processes

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Overall conclusion of substantial rare variant contribution.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Limitations of the study: exclusion of non-coding variants, rare copy number variations, and pathway interdependency.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: These limitations may affect the accuracy of predictions.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Acknowledge limitations and their potential impact.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Study does not consider synonymous/UTR variants, rare copy number variations (up to 1% of LoF alleles), and cannot directly translate genetic variability to effectiveness due to pathway interdependency and sparse interaction data.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Propose future work.

evidence.quote_or_span: we do not consider here variants with functional impacts that do not result in changes of the gene product, such as the synonymous variants rs1045642 (ABCB1 I1145I) and rs1128503 (ABCB1 G412G) or the UTR variant rs717620 in ABCC2.

rare copy number variations in pharmacogenes, accounting for up to 1% of all loss-of-function alleles, are an additional source of genetic variability with relevance for drug metabolism phenotypes that are commonly not considered by computational functionality prediction algorithms
For many drugs, genetic variability cannot be directly translated into effectiveness or ADR susceptibility due to the interdependency of different metabolic pathways

data regarding the effects of such gene-gene or gene-drug interactions is sparse, which complicates predictions of drug effectiveness or safety even when rare genetic variants are incorporated into the analyses

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Limitations acknowledged.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to improve predictions for clinical use.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: PBPK modeling and NGS with advanced computational methods can refine predictions.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Propose future directions.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Further work in PBPK modeling and integration of NGS with advanced computational prediction could refine individualized predictions and contribute to routine care.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Conclude study.

evidence.quote_or_span: further work particularly in physiologically based pharmacokinetic (PBPK) modeling is necessary to reliably predict treatment outcomes for the individual patient

current NGS technology in combination with more advanced computational prediction methods could already today facilitate the refinement of individualized predictions regarding drug efficacy and its propensity to cause ADRs and thereby to contribute to the implementation of pharmacogenetic markers into routine care

validation_methods: Computational prediction framework with 92% sensitivity and 95% specificity
metrics: Percentage of functional variability explained by rare variants
Aggregated frequency of rare deleterious alleles
success_verification.final_conclusion: Rare genetic variants have the potential to explain a substantial part of unexplained genetic variability in drug metabolism phenotypes, with gene- and drug-specific contributions. For some drugs (e.g., warfarin, irinotecan), rare variants are major contributors; for others (e.g., simvastatin, voriconazole, olanzapine), their impact is smaller but still notable.
limitations: Exclusion of synonymous, UTR variants, and rare CNVs
Inability to directly translate genetic variability to effectiveness due to pathway interdependency
Sparse gene-gene and gene-drug interaction data

source.title: Efficient molecular evolution to generate enantioselective enzymes using a dual-channel microfluidic droplet screening platform

initial_request.research_problem: Directed evolution of enantioselective enzymes is limited by low-throughput screening methods that cannot directly measure enantioselectivity.
initial_request.known_context: Existing droplet microfluidic systems (FADS) use only one substrate and cannot screen for enantioselectivity. Traditional chiral chromatography is low-throughput (<10^4 colonies/day) and costly.
constraints: High-throughput screening required for large mutant libraries
Need to simultaneously evaluate activity toward two enantiomers
quantifiable_goals: Improve enantioselectivity (E_S) over wild-type
Achieve >100-fold improvement in enantioselectivity

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Directed evolution of enantioselective enzymes is limited by screening throughput (<10^4 colonies/day) and costly chiral chromatography. Existing droplet microfluidic systems (FADS) use only one substrate and cannot screen for enantioselectivity.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: No high-throughput method exists to directly screen for enantioselectivity in enzyme libraries.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A dual-channel microfluidic droplet screening platform can simultaneously evaluate two substrates, enabling high-throughput screening for enantioselectivity.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Design a dual-channel microfluidic droplet screening (DMDS) platform with two working modes: cooperative mode for activity and biased mode for enantioselectivity.
evolution_trajectory.observation: DMDS platform designed with dual-fluorescence detection/sorting to evaluate two reactions simultaneously. Cooperative mode uses two substrates with same reactive group but different fluorophores to select for catalytic activity. Biased mode uses two substrates differing in reactive groups and fluorophores for positive/negative selection to screen enantioselectivity.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Platform design enables high-throughput screening; next, generate and screen a mutant library for improved activity.

evidence.quote_or_span: Screening such huge mutagenesis libraries for the directed evolution of enzymatic enantioselectivity has been especially challenging, owing to the requirement of performing chiral chromatography, a high-cost and labor-intensive analytical process.
existing droplet technologies lose some of this promise when one considers that they lack the ability to screen for enzymatic enantioselectivity.
Our system is capable of evaluating two reaction channels simultaneously, through the use of a dual-fluorescence detection/sorting microfluidic device.
we define this two selection substrate screening mode as the “DMDS cooperative mode” (sorting the double-positive population in Fig. 1g).

evolution_trajectory.state_before: DMDS platform designed but not yet tested on a real library.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to generate a mutant library and screen for improved activity toward (S)-ibuprofen.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Error-prone PCR on wild-type afest will generate variants with improved activity that can be enriched by DMDS cooperative mode.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Generate Library 1 (~2 million mutants) by error-prone PCR on wild-type afest and screen in cooperative mode at 0.3 cells/droplet, retaining top 0.2%.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 18 million droplets screened in <4 h; top 0.2% retained.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: High occupancy may cause co-encapsulation artifacts; perform enrichment at lower occupancy.

evidence.quote_or_span: The first round of random mutagenesis used the wild-type afest gene as a template. Error-prone PCR was used to generate a variant library comprised of approximately 2 million mutants that harbored between 2 and 4 nucleotide mutation sites per variant (Library 1).
the DMDS platform was operated in the cooperative mode to improve the catalytic activity of AFEST toward (S)-ibuprofen.
The first round of random mutagenesis used the wild-type afest gene as a template.
Error-prone PCR was used to generate a variant library comprised of approximately 2 million mutants that harbored between 2 and 4 nucleotide mutation sites per variant (Library 1).
cells were encapsulated at a high occupancy (0.3 cells per droplet). When using the DMDS cooperative mode, only 0.2% of the most active variants were retained.
we screened 18 million droplets (in <4 h).

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Library 1 enriched but potential co-encapsulation artifacts due to high occupancy.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Co-encapsulation at 0.3 cells/droplet may lead to false positives.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Reducing occupancy to 0.05 cells/droplet will prevent co-encapsulation artifacts and allow more stringent enrichment.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform enrichment iteration at 0.05 cells/droplet, retaining top 2%.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 7.2×10^5 mutants droplets screened in <10 min; top 2% retained.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Enriched library ready for further diversification; perform DNA shuffling of positive mutants.

evidence.quote_or_span: with the goal of preventing artifacts resulting from co-encapsulation at high cell occupancy, we reduced the cell occupancy to 0.05 cells per droplet. Our screen with this low occupancy retained the top 2% of the most active variants.
In this manner, $7.2 \times 10^{5}$ mutants droplets were screened in <10 min (3× coverage of our condensed library.
cells were encapsulated at a high occupancy (0.3 cells per droplet). When using the DMDS cooperative mode, only 0.2% of the most active variants were retained.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Enriched pool of active variants from Library 1.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to recombine beneficial mutations to further improve activity.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: DNA shuffling of nine mutation sites from positive mutants will generate variants with higher activity.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Generate Library 2 (~500,000 variants) by DNA shuffling of nine mutation sites and screen in cooperative mode.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Library 2 generated and screened; best mutant Q30 (I209V/D211G/L257P) identified.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Q30 shows improved activity; use as template for further random mutagenesis.

evidence.quote_or_span: The next round of directed evolution of afest consisted of shuffling of these nine mutation sites, generating a DNA shuffling library comprised of approximately 500,000 variants (Library 2).
6b), a three-site mutant, Q30 (I209V/D211G/L257P), was identified as the best performing mutant variant (Supplementary Fig.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Q30 mutant with improved activity but unknown enantioselectivity.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to further improve activity; enantioselectivity may be compromised.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Error-prone PCR on Q30 will yield variants with further improved activity.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Generate Library 3 (2 million variants) by error-prone PCR on Q30 and screen in cooperative mode.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Mutant IE9 (L13H/F203E/G206E/L284F) showed 2-fold activity improvement over Q30 but enantioselectivity decreased.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Activity improved but enantioselectivity decreased; switch to biased mode to screen for enantioselectivity.

evidence.quote_or_span: We next used the gene encoding Q30 as a template to generate another error-prone PCR-based random mutagenesis library (Library 3). This library was comprised of 2 million variants with 2–4 mutated sites per variant.

It harbors an additional four mutation sites (L13H/F203E/G206E/L284F) compared to Q30, and its activity of IE9 toward (S)-ibuprofen-pNP ester is improved 2-fold (and thus cumulatively \~4-fold over wild-type AFEST).

evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Screening in biased mode will select for variants with increased activity toward (S)-substrate and decreased activity toward (R)-substrate.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Switch to DMDS biased mode; generate Library 4 (~0.5 million mutants) by error-prone PCR on IE9 and screen.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Mutants 7F9 (F90Y, E_S=1.6) and 5G9 (G88S, E_S=3.6) identified with improved enantioselectivity.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Mutations in loop 87-90 suggest structural basis for enantioselectivity; perform saturation mutagenesis on these residues.

evidence.quote_or_span: To further improve the enantioselectivity of AFEST, we expanded the screening strategy to include both increased activity toward (S)-ibuprofen ester and decreased activity toward (R)-ibuprofen ester. To accomplish this, we switched from the DMDS cooperative mode to the DMDS biased mode.

Using the gene encoding IE9 as a template, we used error-prone PCR to generate a random mutagenesis library (Library 4) comprised of about 0.5 million mutant variants with an average of 2 mutation sites per variant.
6$ ); the 5G9 variant had a G88S amino acid substitution and improved enantioselectivity for (S)-ibuprofen ester ( $E_{S}=1.
This screening identified two positive mutants: compared to the IE9 variant, the 7F9 variant had a F90Y amino acid substitution and increased enantioselectivity for (S)-ibuprofen ester ( $E_{S}=1.

we generated two types of libraries: single-site-saturation mutagenesis libraries (four libraries, one for each of residues 87–90) and combinational saturation mutagenesis libraries (six of these: 87/88, 87/89, 87/90, 88/89, 88/90, and 89/90). These libraries were combined into a pooled library (Library 5) comprised of about 10,000 variants

The final screening library was a site-saturation mutagenesis library (Library 5) targeting the four AFEST residues: 87, 88, 89, and 90; both single-site and two-site combinations were generated, yielding a total of 10,000 site-saturation mutant variants.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Mutations in loop 87-90 (G88S, F90Y) improve enantioselectivity.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Optimal combination of residues in loop 87-90 for enantioselectivity is unknown.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Saturation mutagenesis of residues 87-90 will identify optimal combinations for enantioselectivity.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Design Library 5: saturation mutagenesis of residues 87-90, both single-site and two-site combinations, ~10,000 variants.

source.title: A synthetic microbial biosensor for high-throughput screening of lactam biocatalysts

initial_request.research_problem: Develop a high-throughput screening biosensor for lactam biocatalysts to enable discovery of enzymes that cyclize ω-amino fatty acids into lactams.
initial_request.known_context: Lactams are important industrial chemicals; existing screening methods are low-throughput. NitR from Alcaligenes faecalis is a known lactam-responsive transcription factor.
constraints: Must be high-throughput
Must be specific for lactams
Must function in E. coli
quantifiable_goals: Detect ε-caprolactam at low concentrations
Enable screening of large metagenomic libraries
Discover novel cyclase enzymes

evolution_trajectory.state_before: No high-throughput biosensor for lactam biocatalysts exists.

evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: NitR can be used as a specific lactam-responsive TF in a biosensor.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Test NitR responsiveness to ε-caprolactam and other effectors.
evolution_trajectory.observation: NitR is known to regulate nitrile degradation operon; hypothesized to respond to lactams.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need to construct a genetic circuit with NitR and a reporter to test responsiveness.

evidence.quote_or_span: we examined the applicability of the A. faecalis nitR regulatory subunit (nitrile degradation operon) in the development of the CL-GESS by assessing the induction of nitA gene expression in the presence of various effector molecules.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Initial genetic circuit with NitR and reporter may not be optimal.

evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Truncating P_nitA and optimizing promoter/RBS for NitR will improve sensor performance.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform promoter truncation and combinatorial optimization of promoter/RBS.

parameters.promoters: BioBrick promoters with known relative strength

evolution_trajectory.observation: Identified minimal functional P_nitA (v3) and best combination J23114-B0034 (v4) with highest fold change.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Characterize sensitivity and specificity of optimized sensor v4.

evidence.quote_or_span: Systematic combinatorial analysis and optimization of NitR expression was carried out using constitutive BioBrick promoters with known relative strength

evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: CL-GESSv4 will detect ε-caprolactam with high sensitivity and specificity.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Measure fluorescence response to varying concentrations of ε-caprolactam and test specificity against related compounds.
evolution_trajectory.observation: CL-GESSv4 detected ε-caprolactam down to 500 μM with linear response up to 30 mM; showed specificity for lactams and related compounds but not biosynthetic intermediates.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Sensitivity needs improvement for screening applications; consider directed evolution of NitR.

evidence.quote_or_span: The minimal concentration of ε-caprolactam required to activate NitR was 500 μM; moreover, a tight correlation was observed between fluorescence intensity and ε-caprolactam concentration.

CL-GESSv4 sensed ε-caprolactam, ε-caprolactone, cyclohexanone, N-acetylcaprolactam, δ-valerolactam, δ-valerolactone, benzonitrile, and isovaleronitrile, whereas detected none of the intermediates in the proposed ε-caprolactam biosynthesis pathway

evolution_trajectory.observation: Library constructed and screened; putative hits selected after three rounds.

The minimal concentration of ε-caprolactam required to activate NitR was 500 μM; moreover, a tight correlation was observed between fluorescence intensity and ε-caprolactam concentration.

success_verification.final_conclusion: A synthetic microbial biosensor (CL-GESS) was developed and optimized through promoter engineering and directed evolution of NitR, enabling high-throughput screening of metagenomic libraries. This led to the discovery of CF3HBD, a novel cyclase that converts 6-ACA to ε-caprolactam without cofactors. Structural and mutagenesis studies identified key catalytic residues and yielded a Y152A mutant with 3-fold improved activity.
limitations: Low background signal persists in CL-GESS, possibly due to leaky expression or transcriptional noise, limiting sensitivity.

source.title: Discovery of Mcl-1 inhibitors from integrated high throughput and virtual screening

initial_request.research_problem: Discovering small-molecule inhibitors of the anti-apoptotic protein Mcl-1, a challenging PPI target with a flat, hydrophobic binding interface.
initial_request.known_context: Protein-protein interactions (PPIs) are challenging targets due to flat, hydrophobic interfaces, leading to drug leads with poor physicochemical properties.
constraints: Small-molecule inhibitors
Selective for Mcl-1 over other anti-apoptotic proteins
quantifiable_goals: Identify compounds with binding affinity to Mcl-1
Achieve selectivity over Bfl-1, Bcl-2, Bcl-xL
Demonstrate on-target cell death in Bax/Bak-dependent manner

evolution_trajectory.state_before: PPIs are challenging targets due to flat, hydrophobic interfaces, leading to drug leads with poor physicochemical properties.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: No small-molecule Mcl-1 inhibitors with good drug-like properties have been identified.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Integrating high-throughput and computational screening can enable discovery of small-molecule PPI inhibitors for Mcl-1.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Proceed with integrated HTS and virtual screening approach.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Hypothesis formulated based on known challenges of PPI targets.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Design dual-readout HTS assay to test hypothesis.

evidence.quote_or_span: Many of these protein interfaces are hydrophobic in nature, so the developed drug leads usually display hydrophobicity themselves, with high molecular weight, low solubility, and poor oral bioavailability, representing unfavourable physicochemical properties.
we are describing an integrated high-throughput and computational screening approach to enable the discovery of small-molecule PPI inhibitors of the antiapoptotic protein, Mcl-1.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Hypothesis formulated; need to design screening assay.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to capture different binding profiles of Mcl-1 inhibitors.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Using two different BH3 peptides (Noxa and Bid) will capture diverse binding profiles.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Design dual-readout HTS assay with Noxa and Bid BH3 peptides.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Assay designed to screen 102,255 compounds using two labelled BH3 peptides.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Execute HTS to identify hits.

evidence.quote_or_span: For the HTS campaign, 102,255 compounds were screened using two different labelled BH3 peptides derived from the Noxa and Bid pro-apoptotic proteins.
These two peptides were selected because of their different binding profile and potency against anti-apoptotic proteins.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: HTS assay designed; ready for screening.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to identify initial hit compounds from HTS.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: HTS will yield a set of hit compounds that bind Mcl-1.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform HTS and collect hit data.
evolution_trajectory.observation: HTS yielded 875 hits from Noxa, 509 from Bid, with 170 common, totaling 1,214 hit compounds.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Filter hits using computational docking and pharmacophore models.

evidence.quote_or_span: Noxa and Bid BH3 labelled peptides yielded a different number of hits that were identified, 875 and 509 compounds, respectively, with 170 common compounds, obtaining the total number of 1,214 hit compounds.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: 1,214 HTS hits identified; need to prioritize for validation.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to select compounds that mimic key interactions with Mcl-1.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Computational docking and pharmacophore modeling can identify compounds that mimic conserved interactions with Mcl-1.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform docking and pharmacophore filtering of HTS hits.

evolution_trajectory.observation: Docking and pharmacophore analysis performed; 67 compounds selected based on binding modes and interactions.

evidence.quote_or_span: the identified 1,214 hits were analysed by in silico and molecular docking studies.

Based on the Noxa pharmacophore model, the grid box of Mcl-1 for the docking studies was defined by the Phe 270 (in the P2 pocket), Val 220 (in the P3/P4 pocket), Val 216 (in the P4 pocket), as well as the charged Arg 263 residue.
Glide 5.6 SP of the Schrödinger suite was used to dock the remaining 1,214 compounds into the Mcl-1 active site
Schrödinger's IFD was also employed for the docking studies to consider the flexibility of the Mcl-1 protein
Through the evaluation of binding modes and Mcl-1 protein interactions, 67 compounds were selected.
