source.title: Virtual screening of inorganic materials synthesis parameters with deep learning

initial_request.research_problem: Lack of computational methods for virtual screening of inorganic materials synthesis parameters due to data sparsity and scarcity.
initial_request.known_context: Synthesis routes exist in a sparse, high-dimensional parameter space; only scarce literature data available for some materials.
constraints: Data sparsity and scarcity
High-dimensional parameter space
quantifiable_goals: Develop a method to screen synthesis parameters computationally
Achieve compressed representations that preserve critical information

evolution_trajectory.state_before: No comprehensive approaches exist for computationally screening synthesis parameters across broad inorganic materials.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Synthesis routes are sparse and high-dimensional, making direct optimization difficult.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Compressed, low-dimensional representations are more desirable than sparse, high-dimensional feature descriptors for synthesis screening.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Adopt a variational autoencoder (VAE) to learn compressed representations.
evolution_trajectory.observation: VAE can compress sparse synthesis representations into a lower-dimensional latent space with a Gaussian prior.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need to address data scarcity for training VAE.

evidence.quote_or_span: no comprehensive approaches yet exist for computationally screening materials syntheses parameters across broad categories of inorganic materials systems
Compressed, low-dimensional representations are typically more desirable than sparse, high-dimensional feature descriptors

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Data scarcity limits training of VAE.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Insufficient data for some materials.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Data augmentation using similar materials can boost training data volume.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Devise a data augmentation methodology using ion-substitution similarity and synthesis parameter cosine similarity.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Augmented data includes synthesis data from related materials systems.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Tune VAE hyperparameters using augmented data.

evidence.quote_or_span: we devise a novel data augmentation methodology that incorporates literature synthesis data from related materials systems
we make use of similarity metrics to boost the training data volume
we use a cutoff minimum compositional similarity of 0.80 for ternary compounds and 0.50 for binary compounds
the authors found that N=5 performed well for the materials discussed in this work

evolution_trajectory.state_before: VAE architecture defined but hyperparameters not optimized.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Optimal latent dimension and Gaussian prior standard deviation unknown.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Grid search can identify optimal hyperparameters.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform grid search over latent dimension (2-30) and Gaussian prior std (0.001-10.0).
evolution_trajectory.observation: Optimal latent dimensionality ~10, optimal std 0.1-1.0.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Evaluate VAE compression on a synthesis target prediction task.

evidence.quote_or_span: Validation and hyperparameter selection was performed by grid searches, where the latent layer dimension was varied from 2 through 30 dimensions and the standard deviation of the Gaussian prior was varied between 0.001 and 10.0.
Optimal latent dimensionality was found to be approximately ten dimensions, and optimal standard deviation for the Gaussian prior was found to be between 0.1 to 1.0

evolution_trajectory.state_before: VAE trained with optimal hyperparameters.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Whether VAE compression preserves information relevant for synthesis target prediction.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: 10-D VAE features can recover prediction accuracy of original features.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Evaluate on SrTiO3 vs BaTiO3 classification using logistic regression.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 10-D VAE features recover 74% accuracy, outperforming PCA at same compression.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate generative capability of VAE for SrTiO3 synthesis parameters.

evidence.quote_or_span: The 10-D VAE features, which are compressed 67% compared to the canonical features, recover the prediction accuracy (74%) of the original features and appear to outperform PCA features at the same level of data compression

evolution_trajectory.state_before: VAE can generate synthesis parameters, but realism not verified.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Whether generated parameters are realistic.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Virtual samples that trick a binary classifier are realistic.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Validate using a binary classifier trained to distinguish real vs virtual data.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 95% chance of generating a realistic sample with 5 attempts.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Compare generated parameters to literature examples.

evidence.quote_or_span: virtual data samples are drawn from the latent distribution and decoded using the VAE. Then, the decoded data are classified as real (i.e., text-mined from literature) or virtual by the binary classifier.
only five sampling attempts are needed to exceed a 95% chance of having produced at least one virtual data sample, which is sufficiently realistic to trick the classifier.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Generated SrTiO3 parameters validated as realistic.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Whether generated parameters mimic literature patterns.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: VAE jointly generates multivariable sets and uses only subsets of processing steps.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Compare virtual data to text-mined data in Table 2.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Virtual data mimics literature, using only subsets of synthesis parameters.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Extend to interpretable 2-D latent spaces for TiO2 and MnO2.

evidence.quote_or_span: Table 2 shows both text-mined and virtual data, demonstrating that the VAE is capable of jointly generating multivariable sets of realistic synthesis parameters.
The virtual data from the VAE successfully mimics this aspect of the data, predicting that in any single synthesis only some subset of synthesis parameters should be used.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: 2-D latent spaces learned for TiO2 and MnO2.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Interpretation of TiO2 latent space clusters.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Clusters correspond to synthesis conditions like solvent usage.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Analyze TiO2 latent space for clusters and brookite distribution.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Clusters separated by methanol, ethanol, citric acid; brookite syntheses distributed across clusters.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Form hypothesis about ethanol as driving factor for brookite.

evidence.quote_or_span: the varied distribution of brookite-reporting syntheses throughout the latent space corresponds with the aforementioned knowledge that many different synthesis techniques have been utilized to selectively form the brookite phase.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: TiO2 latent space analyzed.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: What drives brookite phase selection?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Ethanol usage is a potential driving factor for brookite TiO2 phase selection.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Propose experimental testing of ethanol hypothesis.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Ethanol appears in 100% of region A syntheses; >75% use NaOH in both regions.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Analyze MnO2 latent space for phase and ion regions.

evidence.quote_or_span: in region A, ethanol appears in 100% of the synthesis routes used (as one might expect by its location in the larger ethanol-dominated cluster), while in region B, no syntheses report the usage of ethanol.

the commonly used step of dissolving precursors in ethanol is selected as a strong major clustering feature (corresponding the central oval-marked cluster in Fig. 4a), and additionally indicates a potential driving factor for brookite phase selection.
future work could test this hypothesis experimentally.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: MnO2 2-D latent space learned.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Whether latent space captures phase and ion regions.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Latent space organizes phases and ions into consistent regions.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Compute latent phase diagram via grid decoding.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Latent space captures phase and ion regions consistent with first-principles predictions.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Discuss limitations of the approach.

evidence.quote_or_span: the latent space captures the concepts of phase and synthesis-involved ions into broad, consistent regions.
the latent space captures the concepts of phase and synthesis-involved ions into broad, consistent regions
There are distinct boundaries separating where one polymorph dominates from another, and similarly distinct boundaries for separating ions.
All these correspondences in the latent phase space are in good agreement with the first-principles-computed phase selection trends discussed earlier.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Approach demonstrated on several materials.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Limitations of data augmentation and lack of direct optimization.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Data augmentation may not extend to amorphous or recycled materials; direct optimization not addressed.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Note limitations and suggest future work with Bayesian optimization.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Data augmentation scope limited; direct optimization not addressed.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Conclude study.

evidence.quote_or_span: direct optimization of synthesis route parameters (e.g., to achieve a particular morphology or phase) is not addressed, and future work would benefit from the inclusion of techniques such as Bayesian optimization

validation_methods: Binary classifier to distinguish real vs virtual SrTiO3 parameters
Comparison of generated parameters to literature examples
Latent space visualization and cluster analysis for TiO2 and MnO2
Grid decoding to compute MnO2 latent phase diagram
metrics: Prediction accuracy (74% for SrTiO3 vs BaTiO3)
success_verification.final_conclusion: VAE with data augmentation enables virtual screening of inorganic synthesis parameters, generating realistic multivariable sets and revealing interpretable latent spaces that capture phase and ion regions.
limitations: Data augmentation may not extend to amorphous or recycled/waste materials
Direct optimization of synthesis parameters not addressed

source.title: RNA-aptamers-in-droplets (RAPID) high-throughput screening for secretory phenotypes

initial_request.research_problem: High-throughput screening of secretory phenotypes is limited to fluorescence-based detection, creating a bottleneck in the design-build-test cycle for non-fluorescent targets.
initial_request.target_object: Tyrosine production and recombinant protein secretion in Saccharomyces cerevisiae
initial_request.known_context: Library generation capacity outpaces high-throughput testing and screening. Droplet microfluidic screening is limited to fluorescence-based detection, excluding most non-fluorescent targets.
constraints: Must enable ultrahigh-throughput screening of millions of variants
Must allow live cell recovery for downstream analysis
quantifiable_goals: Enhance tyrosine production
Enhance recombinant protein secretion

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Library generation capacity outpaces high-throughput testing and screening, creating a bottleneck in the design-build-test cycle. Droplet microfluidic screening is limited to fluorescence-based detection, excluding most non-fluorescent targets.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to transduce extracellular product titer into fluorescence to enable general ultrahigh-throughput droplet screening.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: RNA aptamers can transduce extracellular product titer into fluorescence, enabling general ultrahigh-throughput droplet screening.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Use RNA aptamers, specifically Spinach aptamers, to convert target concentration into fluorescence signal.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Using aptamers, we transduce extracellular product titer into fluorescence, allowing ultrahigh-throughput screening of millions of variants.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Establish design criteria for RAPID screening based on this hypothesis.

evidence.quote_or_span: library generation capacity outpaces the rate of high-throughput testing and screening
product concentration must be measurable via fluorescence, the only current modality with sufficient signal to robustly detect in kilohertz flowing droplets
Using aptamers, we transduce extracellular product titer into fluorescence, allowing ultrahigh-throughput screening of millions of variants.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Hypothesis that RNA aptamers can transduce extracellular product titer into fluorescence.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: What conditions must be met to apply RAPID screening?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: RAPID screening requires single-cell cultivation in droplets, aptamer-based target measurement, aptamer stability in presence of cells, and microfluidic sorting with live cell recovery.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Establish design criteria for RAPID screening.
evolution_trajectory.observation: To apply RAPID, several conditions must be met: (i) single cells must be cultivated in a metabolically active and productive state in picoliter droplets; (ii) RNA aptamers must allow target concentration measurements in picoliter droplets; (iii) the aptamer must remain sensitive and specific even in the presence of the producer cell and growth medium for the several day production phase; and (iv) the microfluidic system must perform all requisite operations efficiently and allow recovery of live cells for downstream cultivation and sequencing analysis of responsible proteins or pathways.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Test aptamer performance in droplets, as initial tests revealed poor performance.

evidence.quote_or_span: To apply RAPID, several conditions must be met: (i) single cells must be cultivated in a metabolically active and productive state in picoliter droplets; (ii) RNA aptamers must allow target concentration measurements in picoliter droplets; (iii) the aptamer must remain sensitive and specific even in the presence of the producer cell and growth medium for the several day production phase; and (iv) the microfluidic system must perform all requisite operations efficiently and allow recovery of live cells for downstream cultivation and sequencing analysis of responsible proteins or pathways.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Optimize aptamers for droplet environment.
evolution_trajectory.observation: In our first droplet tests, however, we found that aptamers performing well in bulk performed poorly in droplets, particularly when incubated with cells.

source.title: Design and application of a lactulose biosensor

initial_request.research_problem: Lack of regulatory proteins for desired small molecules limits biosensor applications; existing lactulose biosensors are in vitro and not suitable for in vivo high-throughput screening.
initial_request.known_context: LacI is a well-characterized transcriptional regulator that responds to IPTG and native sugars; its effector-binding pocket can potentially be engineered for new specificities.
constraints: Must retain repressor function
Must not respond to lactose or other common disaccharides
Must function in E. coli whole-cell context
quantifiable_goals: Achieve specific induction by lactulose
Enable high-throughput screening of lactulose-producing enzymes

evolution_trajectory.state_before: No regulatory protein available for lactulose detection; existing biosensors are in vitro.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can LacI effector-binding pocket be engineered to recognize lactulose?

source.title: Rationally reduced libraries for combinatorial pathway optimization minimizing experimental effort

initial_request.research_problem: Combinatorial pathway optimization requires exploring a large expression space, but random RBS libraries suffer from combinatorial explosion and skewed distributions, making experimental screening intractable.
initial_request.target_object: RBS-mediated translation initiation rates (TIRs) for pathway enzymes
initial_request.known_context: Random RBS libraries (e.g., N6, N8) lead to 6.9×10^10 and 2.8×10^14 combinations, heavily skewed toward weak TIRs, limiting coverage of intermediate/strong expression levels.
constraints: Library size must be amenable to screening (e.g., 96-well plates).
Libraries must be implementable via one-pot cloning with degenerate oligonucleotides.
input_data: TIR distributions predicted by RBS Library Calculator for fully degenerate N6 and N8 libraries.
quantifiable_goals: Achieve uniform coverage of TIR space.
Reduce library size to <1000 combinations while maintaining coverage.
Improve violacein selectivity beyond 81%.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Random RBS libraries suffer from combinatorial explosion and skewed TIR distributions, making screening intractable.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to design RBS libraries that uniformly cover expression space with a screenable size?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Smart libraries can be designed to cover a broad range of expression levels uniformly with a size amenable to screening, encoded by single degenerate sequences for one-pot cloning.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Develop an algorithm to identify globally optimal degenerate RBS sequences that match a uniform target TIR distribution.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Requirements defined: broad coverage, uniform distribution, screenable size, one-pot cloning via degenerate sequences.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need to implement algorithm to search for optimal degenerate sequences.

evidence.quote_or_span: this pool would be highly redundant for weak RBSs whereas potentially more productive combinations including intermediate or strong RBSs would remain scarce.

smart libraries need to be generated that (i) cover a broad range of expression levels; (ii) are uniformly distributed; (iii) have a size that is amenable to screening; and (iv) are encoded by single degenerate sequences, which allow for experimental implementation by easy one-pot cloning procedures.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Hypothesis formulated for smart libraries; no algorithm exists to design them.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to algorithmically identify degenerate RBS sequences that yield a uniform TIR distribution?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: An algorithm (RedLibs) can compare TIR distributions of all possible degenerate RBSs to a target distribution using Kolmogorov-Smirnov distance (d_KS) to find globally optimal sequences.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Design RedLibs algorithm to exhaustively search degenerate RBS space and select sequences minimizing d_KS to a uniform target.
evolution_trajectory.observation: RedLibs algorithm designed to evaluate all partially degenerate sequences and compare TIR distributions to target.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Implement computational search to find optimal degenerate sequences for given library sizes.

evidence.quote_or_span: Here we introduce the algorithm RedLibs (Reduced Libraries) that identifies globally optimal degenerate RBSs that uniformly span the entire TIR space in a linear manner.

RedLibs compares the TIR distributions of all possible degenerate RBSs to a desired target distribution, leading to truly uniform predicted distributions that can be implemented by simple one-pot cloning protocols.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: RedLibs algorithm designed; need to computationally search for optimal degenerate sequences.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which degenerate sequences yield TIR distributions closest to uniform for given library sizes?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Exhaustive search over all possible partially degenerate sequences will identify sub-libraries with minimal d_KS to uniform target.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Run RedLibs to evaluate sub-libraries for sizes 4, 12, 24, using RBS Library Calculator predictions.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Optimal degenerate sequences identified for sizes 4, 12, 24; sub-libraries evaluated exhaustively.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate in silico that reduced libraries improve TIR space coverage compared to fully degenerate libraries.

evidence.quote_or_span: These sub-libraries are generated by computing the TIR distributions of all possible partially degenerate sequences with a given target size (these sequences are obtained from a database which is part of RedLibs) and subsequently comparing them to the target distribution one by one.

We applied two different RBS diversification strategies: an unstructured randomization in which six or eight nucleotides were fully randomized ( $N_{6}$ or $N_{8}$ ) as well as diversification using smart RBS libraries with target sizes of 4, 12 and 24 members produced by RedLibs to resemble a uniform distribution.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Reduced libraries designed computationally; need to test coverage in silico.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Do reduced libraries provide better coverage of TIR space than fully degenerate libraries?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: In silico picking of 252 random TIR pairs from reduced libraries will show improved uniformity and coverage compared to fully degenerate N6/N8 libraries.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform in silico screening by randomly picking 252 TIR pairs for sfGFP and mCherry from each library type.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Fully degenerate libraries only yield low TIR combinations; RedLibs libraries show improved coverage of intermediate/high TIRs.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate in vivo that reduced libraries perform as predicted.

evidence.quote_or_span: We initially performed an in silico screening approach to mimic an actual screen with low throughput by randomly picking 252 TIR pairs for the two fluorescent proteins from the respective libraries
the fully degenerate libraries deliver only combinations of low TIR levels for both proteins, representing unsatisfactory coverage of the translation level space

validation_methods: In silico picking of 252 TIR pairs to compare library coverage.

success_verification.final_conclusion: RedLibs enables design of rationally reduced RBS libraries that uniformly cover expression space with screenable sizes. source.title: Structural Rigidity and Protein Thermostability in Variants of Lipase A from Bacillus subtilis

initial_request.research_problem: Resolve conflicting views on whether increased or decreased structural rigidity correlates with higher protein thermostability by analyzing static properties of 16 BsLipA mutants using Constraint Network Analysis (CNA).
initial_request.target_object: Lipase A from Bacillus subtilis (BsLipA) and its 16 mutants
initial_request.known_context: Opposing views exist on whether increased or decreased structural rigidity of the folded state leads to higher protein thermostability, related to differences in temporal resolution of methods.
constraints: Use static properties to avoid time-dependent biases
Analyze a well-characterized set of 16 BsLipA mutants
input_data: Crystal structures of BsLipA wild type and mutants (where available)
Modeled structures for mutants without crystal structures
quantifiable_goals: Establish a quantitative correlation between structural rigidity and thermodynamic thermostability
Explain outliers and improve prediction accuracy

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Conflicting views on rigidity-thermostability relationship due to differences in temporal resolution of methods.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Whether static analysis of structural rigidity can reveal a clear correlation with thermostability.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Analyzing static properties directly with CNA will reveal a correlation between structural rigidity and thermodynamic thermostability.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Use CNA to analyze static properties of 16 BsLipA mutants.
evolution_trajectory.observation: CNA avoids time-dependent biases by considering proteins in static equilibrium.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Design the study with specific mutants and methods.

evidence.quote_or_span: Opposing views on increased or decreased structural rigidity of the folded state have been put forward in this context.
They have been related to differences in the temporal resolution of experiments and computations that probe atomic mobility.

Here, we address the question of the relation between protein thermostability and structural rigidity by analyzing directly the static properties of a well-characterized set of 16 mutants of lipase A from Bacillus subtilis (BsLipA).

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Hypothesis formulated; need to select mutants and methods.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to define the set of mutants and computational protocol.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A set of 16 BsLipA mutants with known thermostability data, combined with CNA-based rigidity analysis including thermal unfolding simulation and ENT^FNC ensemble, will allow testing the hypothesis.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Select 16 mutants (13 from Rao et al. with Tm data and 3 from Reetz et al. with Ti/T50 data); model missing structures; apply CNA with ENT^FNC ensemble.

tool_or_method.name: SCWRL, REDUCE, Amber11, FIRST, CNA, ENT^FNC

evolution_trajectory.observation: Study design includes 16 mutants, modeled structures, and CNA with thermal unfolding simulation.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Compute phase transition temperatures Tp from global index H_type2.

evidence.quote_or_span: Models of mutant structures for which crystal structures were not available in the PDB were generated with the SCWRL program [58]
For all structures, hydrogen atoms were added using REDUCE [59]

thermal unfolding trajectory of 60 network states was generated for each input network by decreasing $E_{cut}$ from -0.1 kcal mol $^{-1}$ to -6.0 kcal mol $^{-1}$ with a step size of 0.1 kcal mol $^{-1}$
the range of $E_{cut}$ used in this study is equivalent to increasing the temperature of the system from 302 K to 420 K with a step size of 2 K

Here, we address the question of the relation between protein thermostability and structural rigidity by analyzing directly the static properties of a well-characterized set of 16 mutants of lipase A from Bacillus subtilis (BsLipA).

source.title: Compounds identified by virtual docking to a tetrameric EGFR extracellular domain can modulate Grb2 internalization

initial_request.research_problem: EGFR overexpression/mutation drives many solid tumors, but resistance to current therapies (antibodies targeting extracellular domain, kinase inhibitors) necessitates new mechanisms.
initial_request.target_object: EGFR prone tetramer extracellular domain interface
initial_request.known_context: Recent studies identified a prone extracellular tetrameric EGFR configuration with a novel protein-protein interface involving domain III. Tumor cells frequently display resistance to mechanistically-distinct EGFR-directed therapeutic agents.
constraints: Compounds must be drug-like (LogS > -6.0)
Compounds should target the tetramer-specific interface
input_data: Molecular dynamics simulation of the head-to-head prone tetramer
Life Chemicals library of 345,232 drug-like compounds
quantifiable_goals: Identify small molecules that modulate EGFR function via tetramer stabilization
Achieve a hit rate above random selection

evolution_trajectory.state_before: EGFR overexpression/mutation drives tumors, and resistance to current therapies (cetuximab, erlotinib) motivates new mechanisms.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need for therapeutics that work by additional mechanisms beyond existing EGFR inhibitors.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Stabilizing the prone EGFR tetramer via small molecules targeting its novel protein-protein interface can modulate EGFR signaling and internalization.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Target the prone EGFR tetramer interface for virtual screening.
evolution_trajectory.observation: The prone EGFR tetramer contains a novel protein-protein interface involving extracellular domain III, suitable for small molecule targeting.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Hypothesis leads to design of virtual screening against the tetramer interface.

evidence.quote_or_span: Overexpression or mutation of the epidermal growth factor receptor (EGFR) potently enhances the growth of many solid tumors.
Tumor cells frequently display resistance to mechanistically-distinct EGFR-directed therapeutic agents, making it valuable to develop therapeutics that work by additional mechanisms.
Recent studies have identified a novel prone extracellular tetrameric EGFR configuration, which we identify as a potential target for drug discovery.

Our focus is on the prone EGFR tetramer, which contains a novel protein-protein interface involving extracellular domain III. This EGFR tetramer is computationally targeted for stabilization by small molecule ligand binding.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Hypothesis that stabilizing the prone EGFR tetramer can modulate function.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: No small molecules known to bind the tetramer interface.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Virtual screening of a large compound library against the tetramer interface will identify candidate stabilizers.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform virtual screening of 345,232 drug-like compounds against the tetramer interface using three docking boxes.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Virtual screening performed; compounds filtered for drug-like properties and solubility.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Top compounds need manual selection for diversity and purchase.

evidence.quote_or_span: This study performed virtual screening of a Life Chemicals, Inc. small molecule library of 345,232 drug-like compounds against a molecular dynamics simulation of protein-protein interfaces distinct to the novel tetramer.
An estimate of solubility (LogS) was generated for each compound using QikProp and compounds were filtered using a cutoff of -6.0 for LogS.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Virtual screening completed.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to select a diverse set for experimental testing.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Manual selection based on docking position diversity will yield promising candidates.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Manually filter top compounds for chemical diversity; select 109 for purchase.

parameters.selection_bias: ~75% not predicted to interact with EGF

evolution_trajectory.observation: 109 compounds selected for functional evaluation.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Selected compounds need to be tested in a functional assay.

evidence.quote_or_span: All compounds were then viewed as docked on the receptor and manual selection was based on docking position to maximize diversity of docking locations on the EGFR dimer. One hundred nine compounds were selected for functional evaluation
Based on this analysis, 109 candidate ligands were selected for purchase and in vitro analysis.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: 109 compounds selected for testing.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need a functional assay to assess compound effects on EGFR tetramer stabilization.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Grb2 internalization can serve as a surrogate readout for EGFR tetramer stabilization.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Develop a high-content fluorescence microscopy-based screen using Grb2 internalization in SCC61 cells.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Assay established with controls showing expected effects on Grb2 pit formation.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Assay ready for primary screening of 109 compounds.

evidence.quote_or_span: We developed a high-content fluorescence microscopy-based screen to assess candidate compound activity in influencing internalization of the EGFR effector Grb2 in SCC61 cells
We chose to use Grb2 as a surrogate marker because it was unclear how EGFR activity state, reflected by auto-phosphorylation, would be affected by addition of compounds
After 5 min, EGF (10 ng/ml in serum free media) or vehicle was added. After 50 min, cells were washed once with PBS, and fixed for ten minutes at room temperature in 4 % PFA.
14 of 109 compounds predicted to stabilize a recently defined homo-tetramer of EGFR influenced intracellular trafficking of the EGFR effector Grb2.

evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Compounds that stabilize the tetramer will alter Grb2 pit formation.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Screen all 109 compounds in the Grb2 internalization assay.

evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Hits need further characterization in viability and phosphorylation assays.

evidence.quote_or_span: Fourteen preliminary hit compounds were selected for further analysis, based on phenotypic robustness as well as the combination of phenotype and whether or not the docked location predicted interaction with EGF.

evolution_trajectory.observation: Most hits had no effect alone; no test compound significantly reduced viability of A431 cells.

evolution_trajectory.observation: Erlotinib blocked all EGF-induced phosphorylation; hits had minimal effects.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Docking pose analysis to rationalize hit activity.

Each of these EGF-induced phosphorylation events was entirely blocked by treatment with the EGFR kinase inhibitor erlotinib.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Hits show phenotypic but not strong signaling effects.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: What are the predicted binding modes of the hits?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Docking poses will reveal common interactions with key residues.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Analyze docking poses of 6 hits for interactions with EGFR and EGF.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 4 of 6 hits dock to Arg470; the other 2 interact with neighboring residues. 2 hits predicted to interact with EGF residues Lys48-Leu52.

evidence.quote_or_span: All purchased compounds docked within 4Å of both EGFR molecules; 27 also docked within 4Å of at least one EGF molecule
4 of the 6 hits dock to Arg-470, and the 2 that are not predicted to interact with this residue, are predicted to interact with neighboring residues
the putative EGF interactions all involve EGF residues in the region of Lys48-Leu52

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Docking analysis reveals common interactions.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How do these compounds mechanistically affect EGFR?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Compounds stabilize the prone tetramer, shifting quaternary equilibrium and altering EGFR function.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Propose mechanism of tetramer stabilization.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Using the unique prone tetramer as a target, stabilization of the head-to-head interface could shift equilibrium toward this tetramer, altering function.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate the Grb2-tetramer link and acknowledge limitations.

evidence.quote_or_span: Using the unique prone tetramer of EGFR as a target, we deduced that stabilization of the head-to-head interface could potentially alter EGFR function by shifting the equilibrium toward this tetramer.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Mechanism hypothesis proposed.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Is Grb2 internalization a valid readout for tetramer stabilization?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Grb2 specifically interacts with tetrameric EGFR.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Cite biophysical analysis showing 94% of EGFR is tetrameric and 95% of tetramers contain at least one bound Grb2.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 2% monomeric, 5% dimeric, <1% trimeric, 94% tetrameric; nearly 95% of tetramers contain at least one bound Grb2.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validation supports the screening approach, but limitations remain.

evidence.quote_or_span: 2 % of EGFR is monomeric, 5 % is dimeric, <1 % is trimeric, and 94 % is tetrameric.
nearly 95 % of the EGFR tetramers containing at least one bound Grb2
the cited demonstration that Grb2 specifically interacts with a tetrameric form of EGFR provides an exciting validation to hypotheses that drove our experimental design.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Validation of Grb2-tetramer link.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: The Grb2 assay does not diagnose mechanism; compounds may stabilize, destabilize, or alter oligomeric structure. Also, no direct binding evidence confirms predicted docking mode.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Current data cannot distinguish between stabilization, destabilization, or other oligomeric changes.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Acknowledge limitations: assay not diagnostic of mechanism; no direct binding evidence.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Grb2 screening assay does not diagnose mechanism; it is formally possible compounds are stabilizing, destabilizing, or altering oligomeric structure. Current studies do not establish whether active compounds bind to EGFR tetramer as predicted by docking.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Limitations motivate future work on medicinal chemistry and target engagement.

evidence.quote_or_span: identification of compounds in the Grb2 screening assay is not diagnostic of the mechanism of action of the compound; it is formally possible that the compounds are stabilizing, destabilizing, or not affecting stability, but rather altering EGFR oligomeric structure
While the current studies do not establish whether the active compounds bind to the EGFR tetramer as predicted by docking

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Limitations identified.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to validate mode of action and optimize compounds.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Medicinal chemical optimization and structure-activity relationships will validate the docking-based mode of action.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Propose future work: medicinal chemistry optimization, SAR studies, exploration of effects on EGFR mobilization and other effector pathways.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Future studies should perform medicinal chemical optimization, establish SAR in relation to docking boxes, and explore effects on EGFR mobilization and other effector pathways.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Concludes the current study with a path forward.

evidence.quote_or_span: It will be important to perform classic medicinal chemical optimization, and establishment of structure-activity relationships in relation to the docking boxes to validate the mode of action of compounds emerging as hits.

further investigation of the compounds identified in this screen should expand to explore their effect on the mobilization of total and phosphorylated (active) EGFR, and other EGFR-dependent effector pathways.

validation_methods: Biophysical validation of Grb2-tetramer interaction from literature
metrics: Oligomeric state distribution (% monomer, dimer, trimer, tetramer)
success_verification.final_conclusion: Virtual screening against the EGFR tetramer interface identified 14 hit compounds that modulate Grb2 internalization. Hits showed minimal effects on phosphorylation, and docking analysis revealed common interaction with Arg470. The study demonstrates a novel approach to target EGFR quaternary structure, but direct binding and mechanism remain unconfirmed.
limitations: Grb2 screening assay does not diagnose mechanism (stabilization, destabilization, or other oligomeric changes)
No direct binding evidence that active compounds bind to EGFR tetramer as predicted by docking
Modest viability effects and lack of strong signaling modulation
Future medicinal chemistry and target engagement studies needed

risk_notes: All coverage requirements met with exact evidence quotes.

source.title: Development and validation of a high density SNP genotyping array for Atlantic salmon (Salmo salar)

initial_request.research_problem: Atlantic salmon lacks a high-density SNP genotyping array, limiting genetic studies and breeding programs. The genome's recent duplication complicates SNP discovery due to paralogous variants.
initial_request.target_object: High-density SNP genotyping array for Atlantic salmon
initial_request.known_context: Existing low-density SNP panels and microsatellite markers are insufficient for genome-wide association studies and genomic selection in Atlantic salmon.
constraints: Must exclude paralogous sequence variants (PSVs) arising from salmonid-specific whole genome duplication
Must achieve high conversion rate from candidate SNPs to working assays on the array
input_data: Haploid embryo RR-Seq data for PSV exclusion
quantifiable_goals: Develop an array with >100,000 high-quality polymorphic SNPs
Achieve genome-wide coverage with average spacing <20 kb

evolution_trajectory.state_before: No high-density SNP genotyping platform exists for Atlantic salmon, hampering genetic studies and breeding programs.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to discover a large set of reliable SNPs given the salmonid-specific whole genome duplication that generates paralogous sequence variants (PSVs).
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A multi-faceted SNP discovery approach using RR-Seq, RAD-Seq, and RNA-Seq, combined with strategies for PSV exclusion including RR-Seq of haploid material, can yield a dense, reliable SNP array.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Proceed with multi-faceted SNP discovery and PSV exclusion strategies.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Hypothesis formulated based on known challenges of salmonid genome duplication and previous SNP discovery efforts.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Design and execute SNP discovery experiments using RR-Seq, RAD-Seq, and RNA-Seq on diverse samples.

evidence.quote_or_span: In Atlantic salmon (Salmo salar), these goals are currently hampered by the lack of a high-density SNP genotyping platform.

a multi-faceted approach to SNP discovery was applied using a combination of RR-Seq, RAD-Seq and RNA-Seq alongside several strategies for exclusion of paralogous sequence variants (PSV) including RR-Seq of haploid material.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Hypothesis formulated; need to generate sequencing data from diverse Atlantic salmon samples.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which samples and sequencing strategies will maximize SNP discovery while enabling PSV exclusion?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Using RR-Seq on farmed and wild fish plus a haploid embryo, RAD-Seq on families, and RNA-Seq on whole fry will provide a comprehensive SNP set.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform RR-Seq on 56 fish (including haploid embryo), RAD-Seq on 160 fish, and RNA-Seq on 72 fish.

parameters.RR-Seq samples: 56 fish (farmed and wild, plus haploid embryo)

evolution_trajectory.observation: Sequencing libraries prepared and sequenced on Illumina HiSeq 2000.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Align reads to reference genome and call putative SNPs.

evidence.quote_or_span: three main Illumina – based sequencing strategies were applied; RR-Seq (56 fish), RAD-Seq (160 fish) and RNA-Seq (72 fish).
the RR-Seq also included a pool of wild fish from diverse geographical sources and a single haploid Atlantic salmon embryo for the purpose of PSV exclusion

Libraries were checked for quality and quantified using the Bioanalyzer 2100 (Agilent, USA), before being sequenced in barcoded pools of 12 individual fish on the Illumina Hiseq 2000 instrument (100 base paired-end sequencing, v3 chemistry) and all sequence data were deposited in the ENA under accession number ERP003968.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Sequencing data generated; need to identify candidate SNPs.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How many putative SNPs can be discovered and how to filter them for array design?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Alignment to the draft Atlantic salmon reference genome will yield >400K putative SNPs, and quality-control filtering will remove unreliable candidates.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Align reads to reference genome (GCA_000233375.1), call variants, and apply filters (allele frequency, read depth, Mendelian errors, repeat masking).

parameters.filters: allele frequency ≤0.1, read depth ≤10, Mendelian errors, repeat masking

evolution_trajectory.observation: Initial putative SNPs: >400K. After QC: 99,097 (RR-Seq), 83,151 (RAD-Seq), 229,754 (RNA-Seq).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Submit candidate SNPs to Affymetrix for array design.

evidence.quote_or_span: Results: SNP discovery was performed using extensive deep sequencing of Reduced Representation (RR-Seq), Restriction site-Associated DNA (RAD-Seq) and mRNA (RNA-Seq) libraries derived from farmed and wild Atlantic salmon samples (n = 283) resulting in the discovery of > 400 K putative SNPs.
Following the quality-control filtering of these putative SNPs (described in 'Methods'), 99,097 (RR-Seq), 83,151 (RAD-Seq) and 229,754 (RNA-Seq) candidate SNPs remained
Of the putative SNPs remaining, those with an allele frequency of ≤0.1 or a read depth of ≤10 (n = 172,501) were removed.
Duplicated reads originating from PCR were marked using Picard and subsequently ignored.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Filtered candidate SNPs available; need to design the physical array.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which candidate SNPs will convert to reliable assays on the Affymetrix Axiom platform?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Using Affymetrix's p-convert score to select SNPs will maximize the number of working assays.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Submit 411,308 candidate SNPs to Affymetrix for in silico p-convert prediction; tile probes for 266,105 SNPs with recommended or neutral scores.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Final array (ssalar01) contains 286,021 putative SNPs assayed by 443,627 probes.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Genotype a diverse panel of 96 Atlantic salmon samples to validate array performance.

evidence.quote_or_span: All candidate SNPs (total of 411,308; 4,139 of which were detected in more than one SNP discovery category) were submitted to Affymetrix for in silico prediction of their probability of conversion to a reliable assay on the Axiom array (p-convert score).

All 'recommended' probes were included and 'neutral' probes were included if paired with a 'recommended' or a 'neutral' probe, resulting in the tiling of probes for 266,105 putative SNPs, this being 93% of the capacity of the array.
the final array contained 286,021 putative SNPs assayed by 443,627 probes.
The candidate SNPs were provided to Affymetrix as 71-mer nucleotide sequences from the forward strand with the alleles at the target SNP highlighted at position 36.
The ssalar01 array was used to genotype 96 Atlantic salmon samples of diverse origin

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Array manufactured; need to test on real samples.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How many of the tiled SNPs will be polymorphic and high quality?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Genotyping 96 diverse Atlantic salmon samples will identify a large set of high-quality polymorphic SNPs.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Genotype 96 samples on the ssalar01 array and classify SNPs using Axiom GT1 algorithm.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 135,682 SNPs designated high quality polymorphic; 110,910 monomorphic.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Apply Mendelian error filtering to remove remaining unreliable SNPs.

evidence.quote_or_span: The ssalar01 array was used to genotype 96 Atlantic salmon samples of diverse origin

a total of 135,682 SNPs were designated as high quality and polymorphic. The main reason for discarding SNPs at this stage was the high rate of monomorphism, with 110,910 SNPs designated as monomorphic

The SNPs were split into categories according to their clustering performance with respect to various Axiom-generated quality-control criteria; (i) ‘polymorphic high resolution’ where the SNP passes all QC, (ii) ‘monomorphic high resolution’ where the SNP passes all QC except the presence of a minor allele in two or more samples, (iii) ‘call rate below threshold’ where genotype call rate is under 97%, (iv) ‘no minor homozygote’ where the SNP passes all QC but only two clusters are observed, (v) ‘off-target variant’ (OTV) where atypical cluster properties arise from variants in the SNP flanking region, and (vi) ‘other’ where the SNP does not fall into any of the previous categories.
In this study, only SNPs from categories (i) and (iv) were included in further analyses.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: 135,682 high-quality polymorphic SNPs identified; need to remove those with Mendelian errors.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Some SNPs may still be unreliable due to genotyping errors or paralogy.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Filtering out SNPs showing Mendelian errors in pedigreed samples will yield a robust final SNP set.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Remove 3,649 SNPs with apparent Mendelian errors, resulting in 132,033 QC-filtered SNPs.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Final set of 132,033 QC-filtered SNPs retained for downstream analyses.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Assess performance of final SNP set in population segregation, genomic distribution, and linkage mapping.

evidence.quote_or_span: Following a final filtering stage based on exclusion of any genotyped SNPs showing an apparent Mendelian error in the pedigreed samples, the final set of 132,033 QC-filtered SNPs were used for further analysis

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Final SNP set defined; need to evaluate SNP discovery method efficiency.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which discovery method contributed most to the final array, and what were the dropout rates?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: RNA-Seq contributed the most candidate SNPs but also had the highest dropout rate.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Calculate dropout rates per discovery method.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Dropout rate for RNA-Seq: 68%. RNA-Seq particularly susceptible to false positives.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Despite high dropout, RNA-Seq still contributed many SNPs; proceed to population analyses.

evidence.quote_or_span: the majority of candidate SNPs provided to Affymetrix were derived from RNA-Seq, reflecting the high discrepancy between candidate and verified RNA-Seq-derived SNPs (68% drop out).

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Final SNP set available; need to assess genomic distribution.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How are the SNPs distributed across the genome?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: SNPs will be widely distributed across reference genome contigs, with many contigs containing only one SNP.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Map QC-filtered SNPs to reference genome contigs and calculate spacing.
evolution_trajectory.observation: ~71K contigs contain SNPs; 59% have only one SNP.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate chromosome assignments via linkage mapping.

evidence.quote_or_span: The distribution of the ssalar01 array QC-filtered SNPs across the reference genome contigs was investigated.
Approximately 71 K contigs contained one or more SNPs with the majority of those (59%) containing only one SNP.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: SNPs mapped to contigs; need to assign to chromosomes.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can the SNPs be placed on the 29 Atlantic salmon chromosomes?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Linkage mapping using families with known pedigrees and exploiting lack of male recombination will assign SNPs to chromosomes.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform linkage mapping in SalMap families Br5 and Br6 using 10 offspring per family; assign SNPs to linkage groups at LOD 2.5.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 40,214 SNPs mapped across all 29 chromosomes.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate chromosome assignment by comparing with previous linkage map.

evidence.quote_or_span: This analysis relied on the lack of male recombination in centromeric regions of the male salmonid genome, and this feature facilitated mapping of markers to linkage groups according to identical or near-identical sire-based inheritance patterns.

Secondly, a 'two-point' linkage analysis was performed to determine the LOD scores between all pairs of markers in randomly selected pools of \~5,000 SNPs including anchor markers from each of the 29 pairs of Atlantic salmon chromosomes (Additional file 1: Table S2).

evolution_trajectory.state_before: SNPs assigned to chromosomes; need to confirm accuracy.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Does the chromosome assignment agree with existing maps?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: The number of SNPs per chromosome will correlate with the Lien et al. linkage map.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Calculate correlation between SNP counts per chromosome and Lien et al. map.
evolution_trajectory.observation: High positive correlation (r = 0.84) with Lien et al. map, validating chromosome assignments.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Test sex prediction capability using Y-specific probes.

evidence.quote_or_span: The number of SNPs per chromosome showed a high positive correlation (r = 0.84, Figure 3B) with the SNP linkage map of Lien et al.
These results are indicative of the validity of the chromosome assignment for the SNPs, and suggestive of their genome-wide distribution.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Array includes Y-specific probes; need to test sex prediction accuracy.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can the array accurately determine genetic sex?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Y-specific probes based on sdY gene will predict phenotypic sex with high concordance.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Genotype 63 samples of known sex and compare probe results to phenotype.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 100% concordance between probe-based sex prediction and phenotypic sex in 63 samples.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Acknowledge limitations and suggest future work.

evidence.quote_or_span: Using PCR primer sets designed from both these sequences, partial sequences from the SRY gene in Atlantic salmon were obtained by direct sequencing of amplicons.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Array validated for SNP quality, genomic distribution, linkage mapping, and sex prediction.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Are there limitations in the current study that need addressing?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Small sample sizes for population structure analysis may limit generalizability.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Note that further validation with larger sample sizes is needed for population structure applications.
evolution_trajectory.observation: The number of fish per population was relatively small, particularly for wild samples, suggesting additional genotyping would be advantageous.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Conclude with overall assessment of array utility.

evidence.quote_or_span: The number of fish included per population was relatively small, particularly for the wild samples, and genotyping additional unrelated samples from each of these (and other) populations would be advantageous to fully evaluate the utility of the array to detect population structure.

validation_methods: Linkage mapping to assign SNPs to chromosomes
success_verification.final_conclusion: The ssalar01 array provides a high-density SNP genotyping platform for Atlantic salmon with 132,033 QC-filtered SNPs, validated by linkage mapping and sex prediction, though population structure analysis requires larger sample sizes.
limitations: Small sample sizes for population structure analysis, especially wild samples
High dropout rate for RNA-Seq-derived SNPs (68%)
Ascertainment bias due to SNP discovery in farmed populations

risk_notes: Population segregation analysis (K09) not explicitly included as a separate step due to limited evidence; merged into limitation step.
Some evidence quotes are from methods sections and may lack direct result statements.

source.title: Exploring metabolic engineering design principles for the photosynthetic production of lactic acid by Synechocystis sp. PCC6803

initial_request.research_problem: Improving light-driven L-lactic acid production from CO2 in Synechocystis sp. PCC6803 by shifting metabolic control from LDH to other steps.
initial_request.known_context: Previous study showed LDH holds high control over lactic acid flux.
constraints: Photosynthetic production from CO2
Use of Synechocystis sp. PCC6803 as host
quantifiable_goals: Increase lactic acid production rate
Increase carbon partitioning to lactic acid

evolution_trajectory.state_before: LDH holds high control over lactic acid flux.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can further LDH overexpression shift control and increase productivity?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Further increasing LDH expression will shift metabolic control away from LDH and increase lactic acid production.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Increase LDH gene dosage via genomic integration and replicative plasmid.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Previous study motivated further LDH overexpression.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Design strains with increased LDH expression.

evidence.quote_or_span: This motivated us to attempt to achieve an even further increase in lactic acid productivity by expressing more LDH enzyme.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: LDH overexpression may shift control.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to construct strains with varying LDH expression levels.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Higher LDH gene dosage will increase LDH activity and lactic acid production.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Construct strains SAA023, SAA026, SAW035, SAW039 with increasing LDH gene dosage.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Strains constructed with increasing LDH gene dosage.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Characterize LDH activity and lactic acid production.

evidence.quote_or_span: This motivated us to attempt to achieve an even further increase in lactic acid productivity by expressing more LDH enzyme.

the introduced LDH has a control coefficient close to 1 for the strains SAA005, SAA025, SAA023, and SAA026, but in the strains SAW035 and SAW039 it shows a significant decrease, approximating 0.5 in SAW039

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Strains with varying LDH expression constructed.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Does increased LDH expression lead to higher lactic acid production?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Higher LDH activity will increase lactic acid production rate and carbon partitioning.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Measure LDH activity, growth, lactic acid production, and carbon partitioning.
evolution_trajectory.observation: LDH activity increased up to 10-fold; carbon partitioning increased from 5.4% to 18.7%, but with diminishing returns and growth impairment.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Analyze flux control coefficients to understand diminishing returns.

evidence.quote_or_span: shows a partitioning ratio of $5.4 \pm 2.3\%$ , $7.3 \pm 2.0\%$ , $17.8 \pm 4.4\%$ , and $18.7 \pm 4.2\%$ for SAA023, SAA026, SAW035, and SAW039, respectively
the enzymatic activity of LDH from the overexpression strains was increased by $1.82 \pm 0.42$ , $7.17 \pm 1.07$ , and $10.16 \pm 1.66$ fold for SAA026, SAW035, and SAW039, respectively

the introduced LDH has a control coefficient close to 1 for the strains SAA005, SAA025, SAA023, and SAA026, but in the strains SAW035 and SAW039 it shows a significant decrease, approximating 0.5 in SAW039
the strains expressing large amounts of LDH protein (SAW035 and the more productive mutant strains) did show impaired growth

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Control shifted away from LDH.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which upstream steps limit flux?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Increasing pyruvate flux via PK overexpression and reducing competing flux via PPC knockdown will further increase lactic acid production.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Target pyruvate kinase (PK) and phosphoenolpyruvate carboxylase (PPC).
evolution_trajectory.observation: PK and PPC are key nodes for pyruvate availability.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Design PK overexpression and PPC knockdown strains.

evidence.quote_or_span: Next, we increased the metabolic flux towards lactic acid even further by targeting upstream pathways that affect the concentration of its direct metabolic precursor, pyruvate.

we show that modification of the step building up pyruvate (that is, pyruvate kinase, PK) and the step consuming phosphoenolpyruvate (phosphoenolpyruvate carboxylase, PPC, see Figure 1) increases lactic acid production.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Hypothesis to target PK and PPC.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which PK enzymes to overexpress?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Overexpressing PK from various organisms will increase pyruvate flux.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Co-express PK from Synechocystis, B. subtilis, E. faecalis, E. coli, L. lactis.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Constructed strains with different PK genes.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Test PK expression and activity.

evidence.quote_or_span: we set out to further improve lactic acid productivity by attempting to increase the flux towards pyruvate
pyruvate kinase (PK) converts PEP into pyruvate (Figure 1), making PK an attractive target for overexpression

evolution_trajectory.state_before: E. faecalis and E. coli PK expressed.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Does PK overexpression in low LDH background increase lactic acid production?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: PK overexpression will increase pyruvate flux and lactic acid production.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Test SYW003 (E. faecalis PK) and SYW004 (E. coli PK) in SAW001 background.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Small positive effect on lactic acid production rates and titers.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Combine PK overexpression with high LDH expression.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Will PK overexpression combined with high LDH expression synergistically increase production?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Combining PK overexpression with high LDH expression will dramatically increase lactic acid production.

evolution_trajectory.observation: SAW041 showed high lactic acid production rate and final titer.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Explore alternative strategies (PPC knockdown, alternative LDHs).

the introduced LDH has a control coefficient close to 1 for the strains SAA005, SAA025, SAA023, and SAA026, but in the strains SAW035 and SAW039 it shows a significant decrease, approximating 0.5 in SAW039

shows a partitioning ratio of $5.4 \pm 2.3\%$ , $7.3 \pm 2.0\%$ , $17.8 \pm 4.4\%$ , and $18.7 \pm 4.2\%$ for SAA023, SAA026, SAW035, and SAW039, respectively
66$ fold for SAA026, SAW035, and SAW039, respectively (Figure 3B).
16 \pm 1.66$ fold for SAA026, SAW035, and SAW039, respectively (Figure 3B).

evolution_trajectory.state_before: PPC knockdown hypothesized to increase pyruvate availability.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Does PPC knockdown increase lactic acid production?

evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Evaluate trade-offs and alternative LDHs.

evidence.quote_or_span: The growth rate of SAW033 was severely impaired
lactic acid production rates in this strain are comparable to the rates found in the strains strongly overexpressing the L-LDH of L. lactis

(A, B) Growth curves and (volumetric) rate of production (left chart); L-lactic acid yield and partitioning (right chart) for (A) SAV001, expressing the $L/LDH_{L39R}$ and (B) SAA029, expressing the $CgLDH_{WT}$ , and (C) Enzymatic activity of cell-free extracts of SAV001 towards NADH and NADPH, relative to the NADH-dependent activity of SAA023.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: LDH control reduced; alternative LDHs may improve kinetics.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can alternative LDHs with better properties increase production?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: LDHs from other organisms or engineered variants will outperform L. lactis LDH.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Test LDHs from C. gunnari (SAA029), P. ovale (SAA035), and codon-optimized B. subtilis (SAA027).
evolution_trajectory.observation: SAA029 slightly better but growth defect; SAA035 no expression; SAA027 significantly increased productivity.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Engineer co-factor specificity of B. subtilis LDH.

evidence.quote_or_span: The mutant strain carrying the ldh of C. gunnari (SAA029) performed slightly better than SAA023, but suffered from a clear growth defect
SAA035 did not show detectable LDH activity in the cell-free extract or a protein band upon CBB-stained SDS-PAGE
codon optimization significantly increased expression levels for the B. subtilis ldh

the employment of this engineered enzyme in the Synechocystis strain SAA028, which is expressing the codon optimized gene of the V38R version of the B. subtilis ldh, did lead to a higher lactic acid production rate, as compared to the employment of the wild-type version of this enzyme (SAA027)

(A) Growth curves and (volumetric) lactic acid rates over time of SAA015 (expressing the non-codon optimized ldh), SAA027 (expressing a codon optimized ldh), and SAA028 (expressing the V38R-mutated version of the codon optimized ldh of B.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: B. subtilis LDH expressed well.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can co-factor engineering further improve productivity?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: V38R mutation in B. subtilis LDH will enable NADPH utilization and increase production.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Construct SAA028 expressing B. subtilis LDH V38R.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Increased production rate, but genetic instability observed.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Test similar mutations in other LDHs.

further sub-culturing experiments (data not shown) resulted in a reduction of lactic acid production and a (genetic) instability of the mutant strain SAA028
single point mutations in the open reading frame (ORF) of the modified B. subtilis ldh appeared, likely affecting its expression level and/or the activity of the LDH

evolution_trajectory.state_before: V38R mutation successful in B. subtilis LDH.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Do similar mutations in other LDHs improve production?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: V39R in L. lactis LDH and V53R/E232M in C. gunnari LDH will increase NADPH activity and production.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Construct SAV001 (L. lactis LDH V39R) and SAA030 (C. gunnari LDH V53R, E232M).
evolution_trajectory.observation: SAV001: tenfold increase in NADPH activity but similar titers to SAA023; SAA030: no increase in production, genetically unstable.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Analyze overall limitations and trade-offs.

the lactic acid titers in SAV001 (Figure 5A) were similar to the titers in SAA023
SAA030 did not show increased lactic acid production (data not shown), as compared to SAA029
strain SAA030 was genetically unstable and rapidly lost the ability to produce lactic acid

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: What are the overall limitations and trade-offs?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: High production strains suffer from growth impairment, genetic instability, and trade-offs between carbon partitioning and total carbon fixation.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Summarize limitations from all high-producing strains.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Strains SAW035, SAW039, SAW033, SAA028 showed growth defects, instability, and trade-offs; theoretical maximum not reached.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Conclude design principles.

evidence.quote_or_span: the strains expressing large amounts of LDH protein (SAW035 and the more productive mutant strains) did show impaired growth
the negative effect of ppc knockdown on the total rate of carbon fixation rate does not outweigh the positive effect this knockdown has on the partitioning of the carbon flux to lactic acid

The employment of this enzyme also resulted in a reduced growth, possibly partially due to the carbon channeled into lactic acid, but the additional NADPH consuming reaction might also have some effect on the physiology of the cells.

The strain expressing the LDH from C. gunnari performed much better, but still not to the expected levels based on the enzymes' catalytic properties. However, the natural physicochemical environment of this enzyme (residing in the cells of an Antarctic icefish) is undoubtedly rather different from the one in Synechocystis.
the enzymatic activity of LDH from the overexpression strains was increased by $1.82 \pm 0.42$ , $7.17 \pm 1.07$ , and $10.16 \pm 1.66$ fold for SAA026, SAW035, and SAW039, respectively

the introduced LDH has a control coefficient close to 1 for the strains SAA005, SAA025, SAA023, and SAA026, but in the strains SAW035 and SAW039 it shows a significant decrease, approximating 0.5 in SAW039

success_verification.final_conclusion: Shifting metabolic control from LDH to upstream steps via PK overexpression and PPC knockdown, combined with alternative LDHs and co-factor engineering, increased lactic acid production but revealed trade-offs with growth and genetic stability.

Genetic instability of engineered strains

source.title: Alphavirus Mutator Variants Present Host-Specific Defects and Attenuation in Mammalian and Insect Models

initial_request.research_problem: Arboviruses must adapt to disparate vertebrate and invertebrate hosts while maintaining genetic integrity. The study investigates how altering RdRp fidelity at a conserved residue (C483 in CHIKV) affects virus fitness in mammalian and insect hosts.
initial_request.target_object: CHIKV nsp4 RdRp fidelity determinant at position 483
initial_request.known_context: Arboviruses cycle between vertebrates and invertebrates, requiring adaptation while maintaining genetic integrity during genome replication. A previously isolated antimutator variant C483Y had decreased fitness in both hosts.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Arboviruses cycle between vertebrate and invertebrate hosts, requiring adaptation while maintaining genetic integrity during genome replication.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: The role of RdRp fidelity in host-specific fitness is unclear.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Residue 483 in CHIKV nsp4 is a key molecular determinant of RdRp fidelity, based on prior isolation of antimutator C483Y with fitness costs in both hosts.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Systematically explore all amino acid substitutions at position 483 to assess effects on fidelity and fitness.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Prior antimutator C483Y had decreased fitness in both mammalian and mosquito hosts, suggesting this residue may be a key molecular determinant.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: To further investigate effects of position 483 on RdRp fidelity, all amino acid substitutions were designed.

evidence.quote_or_span: Arboviruses cycle through both vertebrates and invertebrates, which requires them to adapt to disparate hosts while maintaining genetic integrity during genome replication.

We previously isolated a high fidelity (or antimutator) polymerase variant, C483Y, which had decreased fitness in both mammalian and mosquito hosts, suggesting this residue may be a key molecular determinant.
To further investigate effects of position 483 on RNA-dependent RNA-polymerase (RdRp) fidelity, we substituted every amino acid at this position.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Variants generated.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which variants are viable and genetically stable?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Some substitutions will be tolerated, while others will be lethal or unstable.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Passage variants three times in BHK-21 cells and Sanger sequence to determine genetic stability.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 12 of 19 variants were viable and genetically stable after three passages in BHK-21 cells; unstable variants mutated to other forms, including antimutator 483Y. Charged residues were not tolerated; hydrophobic residues were preferred.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Screen stable variants for fidelity phenotypes using ribavirin sensitivity.

evidence.quote_or_span: After three passages in BHK-21 cells, viruses were Sanger sequenced to determine genetic stability.
we addressed the sensitivity of all 12 genetically stable variants to ribavirin

Following transfection of mammalian BHK-21 cells, there were no significant differences in RNA synthesis (Figure 6A) or production of infectious virus (Figure 6C); however, in mosquito C6/36 cells, there was a very marked defect in replication for the mutator variants, compared to wildtype virus or the antimutator 483Y strain (Figure 6B), that correlated with the significant reduction in progeny (P<0.
To analyze each virus for reversion at position 483, viral RNA was extracted for each electroporation and BHK-21 passage using TRIzol reagent (Invitrogen).

CHIKV mutator strains (483A, G, and W) generated the same or more genomes than wildtype virus (Figure 2B), but slightly fewer infectious progeny (Figure 2A). Consequently, these mutator variants have a lower specific infectivity than wildtype in mammalian cells

For CHIKV, this RNA was used to amplify a 3184 bp region corresponding to nucleotides (4522–7706), which included position 483, using the forward primer (5'-GATGAGCACATCTC-CATAG-3') and the reverse primer (5'-GTTTGGGTTGGGAT-GAACT-3') and the Titan One Tube RT-PCR Kit (Roche).

evolution_trajectory.state_before: 12 stable variants identified.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which variants have altered fidelity?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Mutator variants will be more sensitive to ribavirin, while antimutators will be resistant.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform ribavirin sensitivity assay on all 12 stable variants.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Mutator candidates 483A, G, W, T, Q were significantly more sensitive to ribavirin (P<0.05). Antimutator 483Y was resistant (P<0.001). All ribavirin-sensitive variants had small plaques.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Confirm mutator phenotypes by measuring mutation frequencies.

evidence.quote_or_span: we addressed the sensitivity of all 12 genetically stable variants to ribavirin
we identified several mutator candidates that were significantly more sensitive to ribavirin (483A, G, W, T, Q; P<0.05 for all, two-way ANOVA)
the antimutator 483Y demonstrated significantly higher survival than wildtype (P<0.001, two-way ANOVA)
All ribavirin-sensitive variants presented small-plaque phenotypes

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Ribavirin-sensitive candidates identified.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Do these candidates truly have elevated mutation frequencies?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Molecular clone sequencing will confirm mutator phenotypes for ribavirin-sensitive variants.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Sequence approximately 150 clones per viral population from an E1 fragment to estimate mutation frequencies.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 483A, G, W had significantly higher mutation frequencies (6.0, 8.3, 6.4 mutations per 10^4 nt). 483M, N, Q were excluded due to non-significant differences.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Characterize replication and specific infectivity of confirmed mutators in mammalian cells.
