evolution_trajectory.state_before: Confirmed mutators 483A, G, W.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How do mutators affect replication and specific infectivity in mammalian cells?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Mutators will produce more RNA genomes but fewer infectious particles, resulting in lower specific infectivity.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Measure infectious titers and RNA genomes in BHK-21 cells.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Mutators 483A, G, W produced similar or more RNA genomes but fewer infectious particles, resulting in lower specific infectivity. Antimutator 483Y showed no significant difference.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Test mutator attenuation in a mouse model.

evidence.quote_or_span: CHIKV mutator strains (483A, G, and W) generated the same or more genomes than wildtype virus (Figure 2B), but slightly fewer infectious progeny (Figure 2A). Consequently, these mutator variants have a lower specific infectivity than wildtype in mammalian cells
In mammalian BHK-21 cells, mutator variants produce near wildtype-like titers of infectious particles (Figure 8D), and the same amounts of extracellular RNA genomes (Figure 8E).

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Mutators show lower specific infectivity in BHK-21 cells.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Are mutators attenuated in vivo in a mammalian host?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Mutator strains will be attenuated in mice, with lower viral loads and maintained elevated mutation frequencies.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Infect C57BL/6 mice with mutators and measure viral loads and mutation frequencies in tissues.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Mutator strains were attenuated in mice, with significantly lower viral loads in muscle, blood, brain, and liver. In vivo mutation frequencies remained elevated, correlating with attenuation (483W highest).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Assess mutator replication in mosquito cells, as arboviruses cycle between hosts.

evidence.quote_or_span: Viral loads were significantly lower for all three mutator strains in each tissue (Figure 3A)
higher mutation frequencies in vivo correlated with increased attenuation (Figure 3B)

Following transfection of mammalian BHK-21 cells, there were no significant differences in RNA synthesis (Figure 6A) or production of infectious virus (Figure 6C); however, in mosquito C6/36 cells, there was a very marked defect in replication for the mutator variants, compared to wildtype virus or the antimutator 483Y strain (Figure 6B), that correlated with the significant reduction in progeny (P<0.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Mutators attenuated in mice.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How do mutators replicate in mosquito cells?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Mutators may exhibit host-specific replication defects in mosquito cells.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Infect mosquito cell lines (C6/36, Aag2, U4.4) with mutators and measure infectious progeny and RNA genomes.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Mutators exhibited a severe host-specific replication defect with reduced infectious progeny and RNA genomes. Mutation frequency differences were negated.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Determine if the defect is due to lethal mutagenesis or a direct replication defect.

evidence.quote_or_span: the mutator strains 483A, G, and W presented significantly lower infectious progeny in C6/36 (P<0.001 for all, two-way ANOVA; Figure 4A), Aag2 (P<0.05 for 483A and G, two-way ANOVA; Figure 4B) and U4.4 (P<0.05 for 483A and W, P<0.01 for 483G, two-way ANOVA; Figure 4C) cells

the significant differences that existed between mutators and wildtype in mammalian cells were negated in mosquito cells, as evidenced by molecular clone sequencing (Figure 5A) and whole-genome deep sequencing (Figure 5B)
There were no significant differences found between WT and 483G.
In C6/36 cells, mutator sgRNA was undetectable or greatly reduced.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Replication defect in mosquito cells is due to RNA synthesis defect.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Will mutators be attenuated in live mosquitoes, or will reversion restore fitness?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Mutators may revert to wildtype or other replication-competent variants in mosquitoes.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Infect Aedes albopictus and Aedes aegypti mosquitoes with mutators and assess infection, dissemination, and transmission.
evolution_trajectory.observation: No significant defect in infection, dissemination, or transmission was observed. Sequencing revealed partial or total reversion of mutator variants to wildtype or other replication-competent variants.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate findings in a related alphavirus (SINV) to test generalizability.

evidence.quote_or_span: no significant defect was observed in either Aedes species for any of the variants
sequencing of virus populations from mosquitoes revealed partial or total reversion of the fidelity-altering residues at position 483

evolution_trajectory.state_before: CHIKV mutators revert in mosquitoes.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Is the conserved cysteine a general fidelity determinant for alphaviruses?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Generating analogous mutators in SINV (482A, 482G) will confirm the conserved role of this residue.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Generate SINV mutators 482A and 482G and confirm mutator phenotypes by ribavirin sensitivity and mutation frequency.
evolution_trajectory.observation: SINV mutators 482A and G were confirmed as mutators by ribavirin sensitivity and mutation frequency (WT 3.3, 482A 6.0, 482G 6.9 per 10,000 nt). The conserved cysteine is a general fidelity determinant for alphaviruses.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Test SINV mutators for host-specific replication and in vivo attenuation.

evidence.quote_or_span: the mutation frequencies determined by molecular clone sequencing confirmed the mutator phenotypes
this conserved residue is a general fidelity determinant for the alphaviruses
Both showed significantly higher sensitivity than wildtype SINV (for 482A, at least P<0.01, for 482G, P<0.05, two-way ANOVA; Figure 8B).

the mutation frequencies determined by molecular clone sequencing confirmed the mutator phenotypes suggested by ribavirin screening (Figure 8C): in comparison to wildtype that presented 3.3 mutations per 10,000 nucleotides, 482A presented 6.0, and 482G presented 6.9 (P<0.05, $\chi^{2}$ test).

evolution_trajectory.state_before: SINV mutators confirmed.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Do SINV mutators exhibit the same host-specific replication defect and mouse attenuation?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: SINV mutators will replicate near wildtype in BHK-21 cells but have significant defects in C6/36 cells, and 482G will be attenuated in mice.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Test SINV mutator replication in BHK-21 and C6/36 cells, and assess virulence in mice.
evolution_trajectory.observation: SINV mutators replicated near wildtype in BHK-21 cells but had significant titer and RNA genome reductions in C6/36 cells. In mice, 482G increased survival (91% vs 50%) and reduced paralysis (36% vs 100%) with lower brain titers.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Test SINV mutators in Drosophila, where no replication defect is expected, to see if attenuation can occur without reversion.

evidence.quote_or_span: In mammalian BHK-21 cells, mutator variants produce near wildtype-like titers of infectious particles (Figure 8D), and the same amounts of extracellular RNA genomes (Figure 8E).

the SINV mutators presented more significant drops in virus titers in mosquito C6/36 cells (P<0.001, two-way ANOVA; Figure 8F), that correlated with a significant decrease in extracellular RNA genomes (P<0.001, two-way ANOVA; Figure 8G).

We inoculated 8-day old mice with wildtype and 482G SINV strains, and observed significantly higher survival in mice infected with the mutator (91% compared to 50% for the wildtype, P=0.0474; Figure 9A).
only 36% of mice inoculated with 482G exhibited complete hind limb paralysis, compared to 100% of mice infected with wildtype SINV (P<0.0001, $\chi^{2}$ test; Figure 9A).

evolution_trajectory.state_before: SINV mutators show host-specific defect and mouse attenuation.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can mutators be attenuated in insects without reversion if there is no replication defect?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: In Drosophila S2 cells, SINV mutators will replicate normally, and in flies, 482G will be attenuated without reversion.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Test SINV mutator replication in Drosophila S2 cells and infect fruit flies with 482G.
evolution_trajectory.observation: In S2 cells, SINV mutators replicated normally without replication defect. In injected flies, 482G was attenuated without reversion, demonstrating proof of principle that mutators can be attenuated in insects.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Acknowledge limitation of linking mutation frequency to mutation rate.

evidence.quote_or_span: the mutator strains were replication competent, generating virus titers (Figure 10A) and RNA genome copies (Figure 10B) at levels comparable to wildtype virus.
mutator 482G presented significantly lower titers than wildtype on day 3 and 5 (P<0.01, Student's t-test; Figure 10C).
Sequencing of virus from 482G-infected flies at day 3 and 5 confirmed that no reversion had occurred.
These results indicate that in principle, mutators can be attenuated in insects.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Mutator attenuation demonstrated in mammalian and insect models.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Mutation frequency measurements are not directly linked to actual mutation rates or in vitro biochemical fidelity.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Future work should quantitatively link mutation frequencies to mutation rates.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Acknowledge limitation and propose future direction.
evolution_trajectory.observation: A future challenge will be to quantitatively link the measurements of mutation frequencies to actual mutation rates and in vitro biochemical fidelity.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Conclude the study.

evidence.quote_or_span: A future challenge will be to quantitatively link the measurements of mutation frequencies (average mutations per nucleotide sequenced) performed in this work to actual mutation rates (average mutations per nucleotide site per replication)

success_verification.final_conclusion: Altering RdRp fidelity at a conserved cysteine residue in alphaviruses produces host-specific effects: mutators are attenuated in mammalian hosts and Drosophila (without reversion), but exhibit severe replication defects in mosquito cells leading to reversion in mosquitoes. The conserved cysteine is a general fidelity determinant for alphaviruses.
limitations: Mutation frequency measurements are not directly linked to actual mutation rates or in vitro biochemical fidelity.

source.title: A new method to customize protein expression vectors for fast, efficient and background free parallel cloning

initial_request.research_problem: Need for a fast, efficient, and background-free parallel cloning method to customize protein expression vectors for screening across multiple hosts (E. coli, insect cells, HEK293E cells).
initial_request.known_context: Existing parallel cloning methods often suffer from background colonies and limited flexibility in tag and host combinations.
constraints: Must work across E. coli, insect cells, and HEK293E cells
Must achieve zero background colonies
Must support high-throughput parallel cloning
quantifiable_goals: 100% counterselection efficiency
>95% cloning efficiency
Zero background colonies

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Need for an efficient parallel cloning method to screen constructs, tags, and hosts for protein production.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Existing cloning methods produce background colonies, reducing throughput and reliability.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Strong constitutive expression of ccdB can provide 100% counterselection, eliminating background colonies.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Design a ccdB cassette with strong constitutive promoter for counterselection.
evolution_trajectory.observation: ccdB introduced into the vector series was designed for strong constitutive expression in order to suppress growth in non-resistant cells at 100% efficiency.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Hypothesis leads to design of ccdB cassette.

evidence.quote_or_span: In order to handle all these different expression constructs, an efficient parallel cloning method was required.
ccdB introduced into the vector series was designed for strong constitutive expression in order to suppress growth in non-resistant cells at 100% efficiency.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Hypothesis that strong constitutive ccdB expression can provide 100% counterselection.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to achieve strong constitutive expression of ccdB in E. coli.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Using the OmpA promoter (llp5 variant) and a Shine-Dalgarno sequence will drive strong constitutive ccdB expression.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Insert llp5 promoter variant and Shine-Dalgarno sequence upstream of ccdB coding sequence.
evolution_trajectory.observation: We inserted the respective llp5 promoter variant and a Shine-Dalgarno sequence upstream of the ccdB coding sequence (Figure 2A) to ensure translation.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Design enables construction of first vectors.

evidence.quote_or_span: We inserted the respective $llp5$ promoter variant and a Shine-Dalgarno sequence upstream of the $ccdB$ coding sequence (Figure 2A) to ensure translation.
ccdB introduced into the vector series was designed for strong constitutive expression in order to suppress growth in non-resistant cells at 100% efficiency.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Designed ccdB cassette with llp5 promoter and Shine-Dalgarno sequence.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to construct actual vectors with the ccdB cassette for counterselection.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Cloning Llp5-ccdB into pETM vectors will generate functional pCoofy vectors.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Ligate Llp5-ccdB HindIII fragment into HindIII-linearized pETM14, pETM22, pETM33, pETM44.
evolution_trajectory.observation: We first cloned Llp5-ccdB into pETM14, pETM22, pETM33 and pETM44 to generate pCoofy1, 2, 3 and 4.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Vectors enable establishment of cloning procedure.

evidence.quote_or_span: We first cloned Llp5-ccdB into pETM14, pETM22, pETM33 and pETM44 to generate pCoofy1, 2, 3 and 4
pCoofy E.coli expression vectors were generated by ligating the Llp5-ccdB HindIII restriction enzyme fragment from pPCRSript-ccdB into HindIII linearized pETM vectors.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: First pCoofy vectors (pCoofy1, 2, 3 and 4) constructed.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to establish a parallel cloning procedure with high efficiency.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: SLIC cloning with recA protein will achieve >95% cloning efficiency.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Test SLIC cloning using eGFP with recA protein addition.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Addition of recA raised overall cloning efficiency to >95%.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: High-throughput cloning validates the method.

evidence.quote_or_span: Addition of recA raised overall cloning efficiency to >95%.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: SLIC cloning procedure established with >95% efficiency.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Attempts to further optimize the protocol did not improve efficiency.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Variations in PCR cycles, primer length, vector/insert ratio, and denaturation/renaturation steps may improve cloning efficiency.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Test several variations to the basic SLIC protocol.

variations: extended LP1 and LP2 primer length
molar ratio of vector and insert
5 min 95°C denaturation followed by slow renaturation

evolution_trajectory.observation: We tested several other variations to the basic protocol, however none of these further improved cloning efficiency.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Proceed with high-throughput cloning using established protocol.

evidence.quote_or_span: We tested several other variations to the basic protocol, however none of these further improved cloning efficiency

We tested several other variations to the basic protocol, however none of these further improved cloning efficiency: number of PCR cycles, PCR without extension step, extended LP1 and LP2 primer length for vector and insert PCR amplification, amount of vector and insert, molar ratio of vector and insert, 5 min 95°C denaturation of vector and insert mix followed by slow renaturation at 22° (data not shown).
Addition of recA raised overall cloning efficiency to >95%.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: SLIC cloning protocol finalized with >95% efficiency.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to validate the method with a large number of diverse inserts.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: The pCoofy vectors with ccdB counterselection will enable high-throughput cloning with zero background.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Clone more than 250 inserts into various pCoofy vectors.
evolution_trajectory.observation: We have effectively cloned more than 250 inserts into all different vectors of the pCoofy series. As we never observed any background, we were not concerned if clone numbers were low, as the clone was correct in almost all cases.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate vectors with eGFP before target protein expression.

evidence.quote_or_span: We have effectively cloned more than 250 inserts into all different vectors of the pCoofy series.
As we never observed any background, we were not concerned if clone numbers were low, as the clone was correct in almost all cases (Figure 4).
Addition of recA raised overall cloning efficiency to >95%.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Some vectors may yield unexpectedly low expression.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Validating each new vector with eGFP will identify and remove low-expression vectors.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Validate every new vector with eGFP for cloning and small-scale expression; remove vectors with unexpectedly low expression.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Prior to use for requested target proteins, every new vector was validated with eGFP for cloning and small scale expression in the respective host. In case the expression level was unexpectedly low, we removed the vector from the list.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Use validated vectors for target protein expression in E. coli.

evidence.quote_or_span: Prior to use for requested target proteins, every new vector was validated with eGFP for cloning and small scale expression in the respective host. In case the expression level was unexpectedly low, we removed the vector from the list.
We have effectively cloned more than 250 inserts into all different vectors of the pCoofy series.

evolution_trajectory.observation: In agreement with the eGFP expression data, MBP had a major impact on protein solubility. Pil mutants expression levels range between 50% and 80%, but only His6-MBP-Pil was soluble after tag removal.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Parallel screening enables host switching to insect cells for problematic proteins.

evidence.quote_or_span: In agreement with the eGFP expression data, MBP had a major impact on protein solubility (Figure 5B).
coli expression of two Pil protein mutants in pCoofy1, 2, 3, and 4 (Figure 5C): expression levels range between 50% - and 80%, of target protein in E.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: E. coli expression showed MBP improves solubility, but some proteins (e.g., Vasp) degraded.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Some proteins require eukaryotic expression hosts for proper folding or to avoid degradation.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Insect cell expression will rescue proteins that fail in E. coli.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Express Vasp, ODC, and Top1 in insect cells (SF9, High Five) using baculovirus vectors.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Vasp expressed in SF9 cells without degradation. ODC required insect cells for co-expression. Top1 showed higher expression with His6-MBP than His6-GST.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Moving to HEK293E cells reveals purification problem.

evidence.quote_or_span: expressed both constructs in SF9 cells without any signs of degradation

-Vasp (lanes 6 to 10) (UniProt: P50552) in HighFive cells after infection with P2 virus stock; 48 h expression, 2000 fold virus dilution (lanes 1 and 6); 72 h expression at 20.000 (lanes 2 and 7), 2000 (lanes 3 and 8), 200 (lanes 4 and 9) and 40-fold (lanes 5 and 10) virus dilution, respectively; coomassie stained (B) Expression of His $_{6}$ -ODC (NCBI RefSeq NP\_002530) in HighFive cells after infection with BLIC; lanes 1–4 BLIC at 1:500, 1:1000, 1:2000, 1:4000 dilution respectively; coomassie stained (C) Express

000 (lanes 2 and 7), 2000 (lanes 3 and 8), 200 (lanes 4 and 9) and 40-fold (lanes 5 and 10) virus dilution, respectively; coomassie stained (B) Expression of His $_{6}$ -ODC (NCBI RefSeq NP\_002530) in HighFive cells after infection with BLIC; lanes 1–4 BLIC at 1:500, 1:1000, 1:2000, 1:4000 dilution respectively; coomassie stained (C) Expression of Topoisomerase I (UniProt P04786) fused to His $_{6}$ GST-topisomerase I (lanes 1 to 4) and His $_{6}$ MBP (lanes 5 to 7) in High Five cells.
Protein purification from HEK293E cells in our hands is not very efficient using standard immobilized metal affinity purification.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Successful expression in insect cells, but need to extend to HEK293E cells.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Standard IMAC purification is not efficient in HEK293E cells.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Alternative C-terminal purification tags will improve purification from HEK293E cells.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Introduce alternative C-terminal purification tags (S, His10, HPC, CBP, OneStrep) into pTT5 via ccdB-C-tag cassette.
evolution_trajectory.observation: We therefore have introduced alternative C-terminal purification tags into pTT5. The pCoofy HEK expression vector was derived from pTT5 by ligating the ccdB-C-tag cassette.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate purification tags in HEK293E cells.

evidence.quote_or_span: Protein purification from HEK293E cells in our hands is not very efficient using standard immobilized metal affinity purification.
We therefore have introduced alternative C-terminal purification tags into pTT5

The pCoofy HEK expression vector was derived from pTT5. The vector was linearized with EcoRI / NotI and ligated to the ccdB - C - tag cassette, which was PCR amplified with EcoRI / NotI primer extensions.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Alternative C-terminal tags introduced into pTT5-based pCoofy vectors.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to identify the best purification tag for yield and purity in HEK293E cells.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: OneStrep and His10 tags with stringent imidazole washes will give best yield and purity.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Test purification of eGFP fusions with different C-terminal tags from HEK293E cells.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Best yield and purity were obtained for eGFP-One Strep and eGFP-His10 when washed stringently with 50mM and 80mM imidazole. We have shown effective protein expression and purification from pCoofy40 vectors.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Method ready for parallel cloning strategy across hosts.

evidence.quote_or_span: we have shown effective protein expression and purification from pCoofy40 vectors that can now be included in our parallel cloning strategy
Best yield and purity were obtained for eGFP-One Strep and eGFP-His $_{10}$ when washed stringently with 50mM and 80mM imidazole (Figure 7B).
Protein purification from HEK293E cells in our hands is not very efficient using standard immobilized metal affinity purification.

validation_methods: eGFP expression assay for each new vector
Small-scale test expression in appropriate host
success_verification.final_conclusion: The pCoofy vector series with ccdB counterselection enables fast, efficient, and background-free parallel cloning for protein expression screening across E. coli, insect cells, and HEK293E cells. MBP tag improves solubility; OneStrep and His10 tags enable effective purification from HEK293E cells.
limitations: ccdB survival strains required for vector propagation
Vector size increase due to ccdB cassette
Occasional ccdB inactivation observed
Standard IMAC inefficient in HEK293E cells without optimized tags

source.title: Directed evolution of an E. coli inner membrane transporter for improved efflux of biofuel molecules

initial_request.research_problem: Biofuel toxicity limits microbial production yield; engineering native E. coli efflux pump AcrB via directed evolution to improve efflux of biofuel molecules like n-octane.
initial_request.known_context: n-Octane lacks chromophore, preventing high-throughput spectroscopic efflux assay. Hypothesis that solvent resistance correlates with growth and efflux rate.
constraints: No high-throughput assay for n-octane efflux
Need to maintain adequate selection pressure
quantifiable_goals: Increase n-octane efflux rate
Improve growth under hydrocarbon stress

evolution_trajectory.state_before: n-Octane lacks chromophore, preventing high-throughput spectroscopic efflux assay.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to select for improved AcrB variants without a direct high-throughput efflux assay.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Solvent resistance correlates with growth and efflux rate, enabling competitive growth selection.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Use competitive growth under hydrocarbon stress to enrich for superior AcrB mutants.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Hypothesis formulated that cells with better efflux will dominate under n-octane stress.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Design selection conditions with n-octane and 1-octanol.

evidence.quote_or_span: n-octane has no chromophore, thus efflux rate of n-octane from E. coli cannot be conveniently determined spectroscopically in real time.

Based on the hypothesis that solvent resistance correlates to increased growth and efflux rate, subjecting E. coli cells expressing a mixture of AcrB variants to the toxicity of n-octane would enable the superior mutants to dominate the population

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Hypothesis that growth under hydrocarbon stress can select for improved efflux.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to establish selection conditions that apply adequate pressure.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Basal AcrB expression with n-octane will provide sufficient selection pressure; 1-octanol with IPTG induction will be a more toxic surrogate.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Design two selection schemes: n-octane at basal expression, and 1-octanol with IPTG induction.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Selection designs established.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate selection conditions with a mixed culture experiment.

evidence.quote_or_span: competitive growth enrichment of the mutant libraries with n-octane would be performed at basal expression level. Under this condition, the growth rate of cells with wild-type AcrB n-octane efflux efficiency is sufficiently lower than the maximum growth rate achievable by the cells.
competitive growth enrichment of the mutant libraries with 1-octanol was performed using induced cells with 0.050% of the substrate.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Selection designs for n-octane and 1-octanol established.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Whether the selection conditions can effectively enrich for AcrB-expressing cells.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Mixed culture of JA300A/pMW119 and JA300A/pAcrB will enrich to only pAcrB under hydrocarbon stress.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform mixed culture experiment under hydrocarbon stress.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Plasmids from control cultures contained both pMW119 and pAcrB; under hydrocarbon stress, only pAcrB was recovered.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Develop a quantitative efflux assay to measure n-octane efflux.

evidence.quote_or_span: Plasmids isolated from the control cultures consisted of both pMW119 and pAcrB while those extracted from cultures grown under the selection pressure of the hydrocarbons contained only pAcrB

The efflux rate of n-octane by AcrB was calculated by taking the difference between the n-octane release rate constants of JA300A/pMW119 (expressing no AcrB) and JA300A/pAcrB (expressing wild-type AcrB)

evolution_trajectory.state_before: No high-throughput assay for n-octane efflux.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need a method to quantify n-octane efflux rate.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A discontinuous GC-based assay can measure intracellular n-octane over time.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Develop GC-based assay to determine n-octane efflux rate.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Efflux rate constant calculated as difference between n-octane release rate constants of strains with and without AcrB.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Generate mutant libraries for directed evolution.

evidence.quote_or_span: the n-octane efflux rate was determined by a discontinuous assay using gas chromatography to quantify the intracellular n-octane over time

The efflux rate of n-octane by AcrB was calculated by taking the difference between the n-octane release rate constants of JA300A/pMW119 (expressing no AcrB) and JA300A/pAcrB (expressing wild-type AcrB)

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Selection conditions validated and efflux assay developed.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to create genetic diversity in acrB.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Error-prone PCR can generate diverse acrB mutant libraries.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Generate eight mutant libraries of acrB by mutagenic PCR.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Eight libraries of ~20,000 variants each were generated and cloned into pMW119.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Enrich libraries using n-octane selection.

evidence.quote_or_span: Eight mutant libraries of acrB were generated by mutagenic PCR and cloned into pMW119. Each library consisted of approximately 20,000 variants

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Mutant libraries generated.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Whether n-octane selection can enrich for improved AcrB variants.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: n-Octane enrichment will select for variants with enhanced efflux.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform library enrichment using n-octane.
evolution_trajectory.observation: n-Octane could not apply sufficient selection pressure; enriched libraries dominated by plasmids with mutant lac operator, not improved AcrB mutants.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Switch to 1-octanol enrichment to overcome insufficient selection pressure.

evidence.quote_or_span: Library enrichment was initially performed using n-octane.
n-octane could not apply sufficient selection pressure to enable differentiation of variants with good performance from the average ones.

the libraries were dominated by plasmids with mutant lac operator expressing AcrB variants with average performance

success_verification.final_conclusion: Directed evolution of AcrB yielded variants 1G1 and 1G2 with over 40% increased n-octane efflux. Beneficial mutations N189H, T678S, Q737L, M844L were identified, with N189H and T678S showing synergy. Mutations are distant from the substrate path, validating the directed evolution approach. No increased antibiotic resistance was observed.
limitations: Crystal structures would be required to validate mechanistic hypotheses (EV_C003_E07_01).

source.title: Directed evolution of a cellobiose utilization pathway in Saccharomyces cerevisiae by simultaneously engineering multiple proteins

initial_request.research_problem: Inefficient cellobiose utilization in S. cerevisiae limits ethanol productivity; existing heterologous pathways are suboptimal, and transcriptional engineering alone cannot overcome inherent protein inefficiencies.
initial_request.target_object: cellobiose utilization pathway (β-glucosidase gh1-1 and cellodextrin transporter cdt-1)
initial_request.known_context: Heterologous expression of β-glucosidase and cellodextrin transporter enables cellobiose utilization, but no method is as efficient as glucose assimilation.
constraints: Single-copy plasmid-based expression
Constitutive promoters
Screening limited to ~10^4 colonies due to transformation efficiency
quantifiable_goals: Increase specific growth rate on cellobiose
Increase cellobiose consumption rate
Increase ethanol productivity

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Heterologous cellobiose utilization pathway is less efficient than glucose assimilation.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Transcriptional engineering alone cannot overcome inherent protein inefficiencies; simultaneous multi-protein engineering may yield synergistic improvements.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Simultaneously engineering multiple proteins in the pathway via directed evolution can yield mutants with balanced flux and synergistic improvements.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Apply directed evolution to both β-glucosidase (gh1-1) and cellodextrin transporter (cdt-1) simultaneously.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Hypothesis formulated based on limitations of existing approaches.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Design library construction strategy to test hypothesis.

evidence.quote_or_span: no method is as efficient as glucose assimilation, which is one of the most rapid sugar utilization rates
we have investigated a complementary strategy for pathway engineering, wherein we simultaneously modify multiple proteins within the context of the pathway and cellular metabolism
The gh1-1 and cdt-1 genes were subjected to error prone PCR with error rates of 2–3 basepair mutations per gene, resulting in one amino acid mutations per protein on average.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Hypothesis formulated; need to create mutant library of both proteins.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to generate and screen a library of mutant pathways with sufficient diversity.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Error-prone PCR mutagenesis of gh1-1 and cdt-1, co-assembled via DNA Assembler into single-copy plasmid, will yield a library with ~1 amino acid substitution per protein and theoretical diversity of 9.9×10^6.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Construct library using error-prone PCR (2-3 bp mutations per gene) and DNA Assembler, targeting ~10^4 screened colonies.

parameters.mutation_rate: 2-3 bp per gene, ~1 amino acid substitution per protein

evolution_trajectory.observation: Library constructed with theoretical diversity 9.9×10^6, but only ~10^4 screened due to transformation efficiency limits.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Screen library for improved growth on cellobiose agar plates.

evidence.quote_or_span: screening to be based on colony size from agar plates
Considering the combination of both genes, the theoretical total number of mutant pathways was $9.9 \times 10^{6}$ [35].
The library size obtained was $10^{4}$ , consistent with libraries previously generated by this method [15,36].
The gh1-1 and cdt-1 genes were subjected to error prone PCR with error rates of 2–3 basepair mutations per gene, resulting in one amino acid mutations per protein on average.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Library constructed; need to identify mutants with improved cellobiose utilization.

evolution_trajectory.observation: R2 showed 47% increase in specific growth rate, 49% increase in cellobiose consumption, and 64% increase in ethanol productivity compared to wild-type.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate that improvements are due to protein activity, not expression changes.

evidence.quote_or_span: a 49% increase in cellobiose consumption and a 64% increase in ethanol productivity

The gh1-1 and cdt-1 genes were subjected to error prone PCR with error rates of 2–3 basepair mutations per gene, resulting in one amino acid mutations per protein on average.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Multiple mutations accumulated; need to determine individual contributions.

evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: L173H in β-glucosidase and C82S in transporter are key drivers of improved phenotype.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Construct and test strains with individual and combined mutations.
evolution_trajectory.observation: L173H increased specific growth rate; C82S increased growth rate and ethanol productivity. Combined mutants showed synergistic effects.

evidence.quote_or_span: the L173H mutation was attributed to increased specific growth rate, while the C82S mutation increased both the growth rate and ethanol productivity.
Specifically, the L173H mutation was attributed to increased specific growth rate, while the C82S mutation increased both the growth rate and ethanol productivity.

Characterization of the strains expressing the mutations which conferred these improved phenotypes illustrated that the highest specific growth rates on cellobiose were achieved only when the $\beta$ -glucosidase L173H mutation is present.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Key mutations identified; need to measure direct effects on enzyme activity.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform in vitro β-glucosidase assays with cellobiose and p-NPG substrates.
evolution_trajectory.observation: L173H increased cellobiose activity by ~90%, decreased p-NPG activity by 34%. Double mutant L173H+H23L increased cellobiose activity by >150% over WT.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Perform transporter uptake assays.

evidence.quote_or_span: The L173H mutant increased the specific activity towards cellobiose by nearly 90% compared to the wild-type.
the L173H mutant decreased hydrolytic activity towards p-NPG by 34%.

The $\beta$ -glucosidase combined mutant L173H and H23L increased cellobiose hydrolytic activity by over 150% compared to the wild-type enzyme, illustrating additive effects from the combined mutations (Table 2).

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Transporter mutations identified; need to measure uptake activity.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How do mutations affect cellodextrin transporter activity?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: D433G and C82S increase transporter activity.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform cellodextrin transporter uptake assays.
evolution_trajectory.observation: D433G increased activity by 18%, C82S by 48%, double mutant by 60% over wild-type.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Rationalize mutations with structural models.

evidence.quote_or_span: The single mutation D433G conferred an 18% increase in cellodextrin transporter activity.
the single mutant protein containing C82S increased the transporter activity by 48%.
The combination of D433G and C82S mutations increased the protein activity over wild-type by 60%

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Enzyme and transporter activities characterized; need structural insights.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How do mutations mechanistically improve function?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: L173H forms a hydrogen bond with cellobiose; H23L is a peripheral activity enhancer. D433G alleviates steric hindrance; C82S affects substrate interaction or complex stability.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Build homology model of β-glucosidase and perform HMMTOP analysis of transporter.
evolution_trajectory.observation: L173H predicted to form direct hydrogen bond to cellobiose C1 hydroxyl. H23L on periphery. Transporter has 12 transmembrane helices; D433G in large outside loop, C82S in first transmembrane helix. No crystal structure available for transporter, limiting mechanistic understanding.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate overall strategy.

evidence.quote_or_span: When the residue was mutated to H173, the model predicted a direct hydrogen bond to the hydroxyl group of the C1 atom of the cellobiose
It is possible that this new hydrogen bond could increase the affinity of the enzyme for cellobiose by firmly keeping the substrate in place during hydrolysis
H23 is located on the periphery of the enzyme, distant from the active site
Based on the predicted structure, the cellodextrin transporter is comprised of 12 transmembrane helices with one large inside loop and one large outside loop.
The D433G mutation is predicted to be located on the large outside loop and the C82S mutation is predicted to be in the first transmembrane helix.
the combined D433G and C82S mutations of the cellodextrin transporter increased the specific activity by 60% compared to the wild-type.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: All data collected; need to confirm strategy success.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Does simultaneous multi-protein engineering yield synergistic improvements?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: The directed evolution strategy successfully optimized the pathway through synergistic mutations.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Summarize findings and validate strategy.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Simultaneous engineering of β-glucosidase and cellodextrin transporter yielded synergistic improvements in cellobiose utilization and ethanol productivity.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: None; trajectory complete.

evidence.quote_or_span: We successfully optimized the hydrolytic cellobiose utilization pathway through a new directed evolution strategy: simultaneously engineering multiple proteins within the context of the pathway.

By applying directed evolution to the entire pathway, engineered proteins can be found which synergistically improve the phenotype either through a balanced flux of the pathway or through overall protein activity improvement.

validation_methods: qPCR and Western blot to confirm comparable expression levels
In vitro enzyme and transporter assays
success_verification.final_conclusion: Simultaneous directed evolution of multiple proteins in the cellobiose utilization pathway yielded synergistic improvements, validating the strategy.
limitations: No crystal structure available for cellodextrin transporter, limiting mechanistic understanding.
Library screening limited to ~10^4 colonies, far below theoretical diversity.
Third round of evolution failed to yield further improvements.

source.title: Semi-rational engineering of cellobiose dehydrogenase for improved hydrogen peroxide production

initial_request.known_context: CDH oxidizes cellobiose and reduces electron acceptors including oxygen to produce H2O2, but its oxygen reactivity is low.

source.title: Fidelity Variants of RNA Dependent RNA Polymerases Uncover an Indirect, Mutagenic Activity of Amiloride Compounds

initial_request.research_problem: Uncover the indirect mutagenic activity of amiloride compounds and understand how different RdRp fidelity variants resist it.
initial_request.known_context: Amiloride compounds inhibit CVB3 replication; high-fidelity RdRp variants can resist RNA mutagens.
constraints: Use CVB3 as model system
Focus on RdRp fidelity variants
quantifiable_goals: Identify RdRp variants with altered fidelity
Determine mutation frequencies under drug treatment
Elucidate mechanism of amiloride mutagenesis

evolution_trajectory.state_before: No high-fidelity CVB3 RdRp variants known.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Whether CVB3 can evolve high-fidelity RdRp variants that resist RNA mutagens.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Passaging CVB3 in ribavirin or 5-azacytidine will select for high-fidelity RdRp variants.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform a screen for high-fidelity variants by selecting for resistance to mutagenic base analogs.
evolution_trajectory.observation: A screen was performed for high fidelity variants of CVB3 by selecting for resistance to ribavirin and 5-azacytidine.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Screen led to isolation of a candidate variant.

evidence.quote_or_span: we performed a screen for high fidelity variants of CVB3 by selecting for resistance to the mutagenic base analogs, ribavirin and 5-azacytidine.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Screen initiated; no variant identified.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which specific mutation(s) arise and confer resistance?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A specific RdRp mutation will emerge and confer resistance to mutagens.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Sequence passaged virus populations and confirm resistance by reverse genetics.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Wild type CVB3 acquired a C→U mutation resulting in A372V in RdRp after passage in ribavirin (by passage 10) and AZC (by passage 20). The role of A372V in resistance was confirmed by introducing the mutation back into the CVB3 wild type cDNA infectious clone and comparing growth under mutagen treatment.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: A372V variant isolated and confirmed; next characterize its fidelity.

evidence.quote_or_span: Wild type CVB3 acquired a new CtoU mutation resulting in an Alanine to Valine change at position 372 of the RdRp (A372V) after passage in both ribavirin (by passage 10) and AZC (by passage 20)
The role of A372V in resistance was confirmed by introducing the mutation back into the CVB3 wild type cDNA infectious clone

Wild type CVB3 acquired a new CtoU mutation resulting in an Alanine to Valine change at position 372 of the RdRp (A372V) after passage in both ribavirin (by passage 10) and AZC (by passage 20), that did not arise in untreated control passages.

The role of A372V in resistance was confirmed by introducing the mutation back into the CVB3 wild type cDNA infectious clone and comparing the growth of A372V to wild type virus treated with 3 RNA mutagens with different nucleotide structures: ribavirin (300 $\mu$ M), AZC (300 $\mu$ M) and 5-fluorouracil (FU, 150 $\mu$ M).

evolution_trajectory.state_before: A372V variant isolated and confirmed resistant to mutagens.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Does A372V have higher fidelity than wild type?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A372V is a high-fidelity variant with lower mutation frequency.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Determine mutation frequency of A372V and wild type populations.
evolution_trajectory.observation: The A372V population presented a mutation frequency approximately 2-fold lower than wild type (P=0.0225).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Higher fidelity suggested; confirm biochemically.

evidence.quote_or_span: The A372V population, on the other hand, presented a mutation frequency that was approximately 2-fold lower than wild type (P=0.0225)
(A) The mutation frequencies of wild type and A372V viruses grown in the presence of 400 $\mu$ M amiloride were determined, shown as the mean number of mutations per $10^{4}$ nucleotides sequenced.
05 statistical significance of difference between WT and A372V mutation frequencies.
A372V is a higher fidelity RdRp variant that generates virus populations with lower basal mutation frequencies than wild type populations.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: A372V shows lower mutation frequency in cells.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Is the higher fidelity intrinsic to the RdRp enzyme?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Purified A372V RdRp will exhibit higher fidelity in vitro.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform in vitro biochemical fidelity assay with purified RdRp.
evolution_trajectory.observation: The observed rate of GMP misincorporation showed a 2-fold difference between wild type and A372V RdRp. In vitro biochemical assays confirm the higher fidelity of A372V.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: A372V confirmed as high-fidelity; dual resistance to amiloride noted, leading to hypothesis.

evidence.quote_or_span: the observed rate of GMP misincorporation showed a 2-fold difference between wild type and A372V RdRp
In vitro biochemical assays confirm the higher (A372V) and lower (S299T) incorporation fidelities of CVB3 variants.
Quantification of relative incorporation product at 2 and 5 minutes reaction time.
Table 1. Incorporation kinetics and relative fidelity of CVB3 RdRp variants.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: A372V is high-fidelity and also resistant to amiloride.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Why does a high-fidelity variant resist amiloride, a non-mutagenic drug?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Amiloride has a previously unknown mutagenic activity.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Propose that amiloride is mutagenic and test this hypothesis.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Since the same mutation confers resistance to RNA mutagens and to amiloride, we hypothesized that amiloride has a previously unknown mutagenic activity.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Test alternative explanation (replicative capacity) before accepting mutagenesis hypothesis.

evidence.quote_or_span: Since the same mutation confers resistance to RNA mutagens and to amiloride, we hypothesized that amiloride has a previously unknown mutagenic activity.

It is perhaps for this reason that passage of wild type virus in $Mg^{2+}$ and $Mn^{2+}$ (that mimics only the mutagenic, and not the RdRp inhibitory, activity of amiloride), resulted in the selection of the high fidelity A372V variant over S299T (Figure 6G).

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Hypothesis: amiloride is mutagenic; alternative: A372V replicates better under amiloride.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Could increased replicative capacity explain A372V resistance to amiloride?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A372V does not have heightened replicative capacity compared to wild type.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Compare one-step growth kinetics and viral RNA levels of WT and A372V ± amiloride.

tool_or_method.name: One-step growth curve and northern blot

evolution_trajectory.observation: No significant differences in virus production kinetics were observed for wild type and A372V grown in the absence of amiloride. Heightened replicative capacity was thus not responsible for the resistance of A372V to amiloride.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Replicative capacity ruled out; directly test mutagenesis hypothesis.

evidence.quote_or_span: No significant differences in virus production kinetics were observed for wild type and A372V grown in the absence of amiloride
Heightened replicative capacity was thus not responsible for the resistance of A372V to amiloride.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Amiloride mutagenesis hypothesis untested.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Does amiloride increase mutation frequency of wild type CVB3?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Amiloride treatment will increase mutation frequency of wild type, and A372V will show lower frequencies.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Measure mutation frequencies of WT and A372V with and without amiloride.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Amiloride treatment increased mutation frequency of wild type CVB3; A372V showed lower basal and induced frequencies. Treatment with amiloride increased, but did not bias, the natural mutation profile.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Amiloride mutagenesis confirmed; validate generality with another high-fidelity variant.

evidence.quote_or_span: (A) The mutation frequencies of wild type and A372V viruses grown in the presence of 400 $\mu$ M amiloride were determined, shown as the mean number of mutations per $10^{4}$ nucleotides sequenced.
(C) Mutation profiles of wild type and A372V populations grown in the absence of drug or presence of 400 $\mu$ m ribavirin or amiloride in (A).
treatment with amiloride increased, but did not bias, the natural mutation profile.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Amiloride mutagenesis shown for CVB3.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Is amiloride mutagenesis specific to CVB3 or general?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A high-fidelity poliovirus variant will also resist amiloride mutagenesis.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Test poliovirus G64S high-fidelity variant for amiloride resistance.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Previous studies showed that G64S was resistant to ribavirin, AZC and FU; replicated with similar kinetics to wild type poliovirus; generated virus populations with lower basal mutation frequencies and presented a higher fidelity phenotype in biochemical incorporation assays.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Generality confirmed; investigate another amiloride-resistant variant with different properties.

evidence.quote_or_span: Previous studies showed that G64S was resistant to ribavirin, AZC and FU; replicated with similar kinetics to wild type poliovirus in one-step growth curves and northern blot analysis; generated virus populations with lower basal mutation frequencies and presented a higher fidelity phenotype in biochemical incorporation assays $[14,15,16,17,18]$ .

evolution_trajectory.state_before: S299T variant previously isolated for amiloride resistance.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: What is the fidelity and mutagenesis resistance profile of S299T?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: S299T may have altered fidelity and distinct resistance mechanism.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Characterize S299T fidelity in vitro and mutation frequency ± amiloride.

tool_or_method.name: In vitro fidelity assay and mutation frequency analysis

evolution_trajectory.observation: S299T RdRp incorporated more GMP over time than wild type, consistent with decreased fidelity. Unlike A372V, the mutation frequency of S299T did not suggest higher fidelity; its mutation frequency was unchanged by amiloride.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: S299T has lower fidelity but complete resistance to amiloride mutagenesis; investigate its resistance mechanism.

evidence.quote_or_span: Unlike A372V, the mutation frequency of S299T did not suggest higher fidelity compared to wild type (Figure 5F).
As shown in Figure 2, S299T RdRp incorporated more GMP over time than wild type RdRp, consistent with the S299T mutation decreasing RdRp fidelity (Table 1).
To confirm the lower fidelity phenotype conferred by the S299T mutation, an in vitro biochemical assay examining the relative levels of misincorporation with the purified S299T enzyme was performed.

Finally, to examine the potential effects of the S299T mutation on RdRp fidelity, infections were performed in the presence of ribavirin, amiloride, EIPA, or under mock-treatment and mutation frequencies were determined for each population.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: S299T characterized as lower fidelity but amiloride mutagenesis-resistant.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How does S299T resist amiloride if not by high fidelity?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: S299T resists both RNA synthesis inhibition and mutagenesis by amiloride.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Compare RNA synthesis inhibition and mutagenesis resistance of S299T and A372V.
evolution_trajectory.observation: S299T resists amiloride's antiviral activity better than wild type; its RNA synthesis is less inhibited and mutation frequency unchanged. A372V only resists mutagenesis via higher fidelity, while S299T resists both inhibition and mutagenesis.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Two distinct resistance mechanisms identified; investigate dose-response to understand primary vs secondary effects.

evidence.quote_or_span: Hence, while A372V resists the mutagenic activity of amiloride by increasing RdRp fidelity; S299T partially resists RNA replication inhibition and this mutagenic effect entirely.
The S299T variant seems to resist amiloride on both fronts: RNA synthesis is less inhibited (Figure 5E) and its mutation frequency is unchanged (Figure 5F).
Unlike S299T, this variant is solely resistant to the mutagenic antiviral activity since its RNA synthesis is as inhibited by amiloride as wild type virus (Figure 3E).
(A) S299T resists amiloride's antiviral activity better than wild type virus.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Ion hypothesis untested.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Do high extracellular Mg2+ or Mn2+ increase mutation frequency and select for fidelity variants?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: High Mg2+ or Mn2+ will increase mutation frequency of WT and A372V but not S299T, and will select for A372V.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Grow viruses in high Mg2+/Mn2+ media, measure mutation frequencies, and passage virus to observe selection.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Treatment with Mg2+ and Mn2+, but not Na+ or Ca2+, increased mutation frequency of wild type and A372V, but not S299T. Wild type virus passaged in MnCl2 acquired and fixed the A372V mutation by passage 5-7.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Ion hypothesis supported; conclude mechanism and resistance strategies.

evidence.quote_or_span: To examine the potential role of altered intracellular cation concentrations in the observed mutagenic activity, wild type, A372V and S299T viruses were grown in cell media supplemented with high concentrations of either NaCl, $CaCl_{2}$ , $MgCl_{2}$ or $MnCl_{2}$ and the resulting mutation frequencies were determined.
(D–F) Treatment with Mg $^{2+}$ and Mn $^{2+}$ , but not Na $^{+}$ or Ca $^{2+}$ , increase the mutation frequency of wild type (D) and A372V (E), but not S299T (F).

Interestingly, wild type virus that was grown in the presence of $MnCl_{2}$ had acquired (passage 5) and fixed (passage 7 onward) the same high fidelity A372V mutation that was selected by RNA mutagen (Figure 1) and amiloride [8] passage.
wild type virus was serially passaged in the presence of either compound and the RdRp regions of the passaged virus populations were sequenced at the intervals indicated

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Main conclusions drawn.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: What are the limitations of this study?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: The study has limitations that should be acknowledged.
evolution_trajectory.decision: State the limitations: mutagenic effect moderate, unclear in vivo relevance, unknown direct ion targets, relative contribution of mutagenesis vs. RNA synthesis inhibition not fully quantified.
evolution_trajectory.observation: The mutagenic effect is moderate, in vivo relevance unclear, direct ion targets unknown, and relative contribution of mutagenesis vs. RNA synthesis inhibition not fully quantified.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Limitations acknowledged.

evidence.quote_or_span: Biochemical assays have shown that poliovirus RdRp activity in the presence of $Mn^{2+}$ results in higher mutation frequencies[28,29] and if S299T is $Mn^{2+}$ dependent, it may explain the higher basal mutation frequency relative to wild type virus.

success_verification.final_conclusion: Amiloride compounds have an indirect mutagenic activity mediated by changes in intracellular Mg2+ and Mn2+ concentrations. Two distinct resistance mechanisms exist: increased RdRp fidelity (A372V) and insensitivity to ion-induced mutagenesis (S299T).
limitations: Mutagenic effect is moderate
In vivo relevance unclear
Direct ion targets unknown
Relative contribution of mutagenesis vs. RNA synthesis inhibition not fully quantified

source.title: Directed evolution of a filamentous fungus for thermotolerance

initial_request.research_problem: M. anisopliae has low thermotolerance (upper limit ~32°C), limiting its use as a biocontrol agent in hot climates and against insects exhibiting behavioral fever.
initial_request.known_context: Most strains display little to no growth between 35–37°C.
constraints: No a priori knowledge of required mutations
quantifiable_goals: Select for variants displaying robust growth at 37°C

evolution_trajectory.state_before: M. anisopliae has low thermotolerance, limiting biocontrol efficacy.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Unknown genetic basis for thermotolerance; traditional engineering approaches are impractical.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Directed evolution via the Evolugator™ continuous culture system can select for thermotolerant variants without a priori knowledge of required mutations.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Use the Evolugator™ to apply gradual temperature increase as a selective pressure.
evolution_trajectory.observation: The Evolugator™ takes advantage of a natural selection-adaptation strategy.

evidence.quote_or_span: None of the strains were pathogenic or able to cause mortality in hosts at 36°C.

However, when insects infected at 36°C were subsequently placed at 28°C, the hosts were rapidly killed by all three fungal strains, indicating that the wild type and adapted strains remained viable at 36°C, but could not cause pathogenicity and death.

Despite their confirmed thermotolerance, the adapted variants did not show increased virulence at 36–37°C, indicating that the ability to grow in vitro at 36–37°C does not necessarily mean that in vivo growth and pathogenesis will occur.

Assays confirmed that thermotolerant variants were, indeed, entomopathogenic, albeit with complex alterations in virulence parameters such as lethal dose responses ( $LD_{50}$ ) and median survival times ( $ST_{50}$ ).

evolution_trajectory.decision: Conclude that directed adaptation was successful and that pathogenicity can be retained or recovered.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Successful directed adaptation of M. anisopliae for thermotolerance using Evolugator™; complex phenotypes in non-selected traits but pathogenicity retained.

evidence.quote_or_span: We report the successful directed adaption of a eukaryotic organism, namely the filamentous fungus, M. anisopliae, for increased thermotolerance using a novel continuous culture machine whose operation is fundamentally different from chemostats and other current methods.

Selected mutants displayed complex phenotypes with respect to non-selected attributes such as conidiation and virulence, although both parameters could be retained or recovered while maintaining the thermotolerant phenotype.

validation_methods: ITS1 and Pr1 sequencing to confirm strain identity
Insect bioassays for pathogenicity and virulence
success_verification.final_conclusion: Successful directed adaptation of M. anisopliae for thermotolerance using the Evolugator™. Selected mutants displayed complex phenotypes in non-selected traits, but pathogenicity was retained or recovered.
limitations: Genetic basis of thermotolerance unknown
Uncertain if thermal barriers are concrete or artifacts
No increased virulence at high temperatures in vivo

source.title: Ancient mtDNA Genetic Variants Modulate mtDNA Transcription and Replication

initial_request.research_problem: Determining whether mtDNA haplogroup-defining mutations in regulatory regions have functional consequences or are evolutionary silent hitchhikers.
initial_request.target_object: mtDNA haplogroup-defining mutations in regulatory regions
initial_request.known_context: mtDNA haplogroups are defined by sets of mutations, but it is unclear which are functional and which are silent hitchhikers.
constraints: Inability to perform site-directed mutagenesis in mtDNA
input_data: >2500 complete human mtDNAs representing all major populations worldwide
quantifiable_goals: Identify functional effects of haplogroup-defining mutations on mtDNA transcription and replication

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Unclear which mutations within mtDNA haplogroups are functional and which are silent hitchhikers.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: The functional implications of haplogroup-defining mutations in regulatory regions are unknown.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Haplogroup-defining mutations occurring within regulatory motifs of mtDNA could affect transcription and replication.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Screen >2500 mtDNAs for variants in regulatory regions and experimentally test selected variants.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Hypothesis formulated based on known roles of regulatory motifs.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need to design a study to test the hypothesis.

evidence.quote_or_span: it is not clear which of the mutations within the haplogroup carry functional implications and which are “evolutionary silent hitchhikers”.
hypothesizing that haplogroup-defining mutations occurring within regulatory motifs of mtDNA could affect these processes.

We thus screened >2500 complete human mtDNAs representing all major populations worldwide for natural variation in experimentally established protein binding sites and regulatory regions comprising a total of 241 bp in each mtDNA.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Hypothesis formulated but no experimental plan.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to identify candidate variants and design experimental assays.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Screening >2500 mtDNAs will reveal variants in regulatory regions; selected variants can be tested using EMSA, in vitro transcription, and cybrid analysis.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Screen >2500 complete human mtDNAs for natural variation in 241 bp of regulatory regions, then test selected variants.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Study design established: screen >2500 mtDNAs, then EMSA, in vitro transcription, cybrid analysis.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Execute the screening step.

evidence.quote_or_span: We thus screened >2500 complete human mtDNAs representing all major populations worldwide for natural variation in experimentally established protein binding sites and regulatory regions comprising a total of 241 bp in each mtDNA.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Study designed, screening not yet performed.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Frequency and nature of variants in regulatory regions unknown.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Screening will identify point mutations, some of which are haplogroup-defining.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform computational screening of >2500 mtDNAs.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 77/241 sites showed point mutations; 74% non-fixed, 26% haplogroup-defining.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Analyze evolutionary conservation of mutated sites.

evidence.quote_or_span: Our screen revealed 77/241 sites showing point mutations that could be divided into non-fixed (57/77, 74%) and haplogroup/sub-haplogroup-defining changes (i.e., population fixed changes, 20/77, 26%).

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Screening identified 77 mutated sites.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Whether variants are under-represented in conserved positions.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Highly conserved positions will have fewer human variants.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform evolutionary conservation analysis comparing conserved vs. changed positions.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Highly significant under-representation of variants in conserved positions (4/54 vs 16/46, p=0.0009).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Select candidate variants for experimental testing based on conservation and haplogroup association.
