Our screen revealed 77/241 sites showing point mutations that could be divided into non-fixed (57/77, 74%) and haplogroup/sub-haplogroup-defining changes (i.e., population fixed changes, 20/77, 26%).

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Candidate variants identified: C295T (haplogroup J) and C242T (sub-haplogroup J1b2).
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Whether these variants affect TFAM binding.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: C295T and C242T may alter TFAM binding affinity.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Test TFAM binding using EMSA with oligonucleotide probes containing variants.
evolution_trajectory.observation: C295T increased TFAM binding; C242T showed no significant effect.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Test whether altered TFAM binding affects in vitro transcription.

evidence.quote_or_span: we employed Electrophoresis Mobility Shift Analysis (EMSA). Specifically, we tested for the effect of transition variants defining Caucasian haplogroup J (C295T) and a sub-haplogroup of J, J1b2
EMSA analysis of double-stranded oligonucleotides spanning each of the two different TFAM binding sites, revealed increased TFAM binding to the C295T variant
this increase in binding was observed at multiple different TFAM to DNA ratios (10/1 and 25/1 molar ratios are shown in Figure 2)
no significant difference with C242T variant (data not shown)

evolution_trajectory.state_before: C295T increases TFAM binding; C242T does not.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Whether altered TFAM binding affects in vitro transcription.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: C295T will increase L-strand transcription, especially of a shorter transcript near CSB1.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform in vitro run-off transcription using templates with haplogroup J (C295T), J1b2 (C295T+C242T), and haplogroup H (wild-type).
evolution_trajectory.observation: Haplogroup J templates showed a trend toward increased L-strand transcription; shorter ~160 nt transcript near CSB1 significantly increased (p=0.034).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate in vivo using cybrids with different mtDNA backgrounds.

evidence.quote_or_span: The variant defining Caucasian haplogroup J (C295T) increased the binding of TFAM (Electro Mobility Shift Assay) and the capacity of in vitro L-strand transcription, especially of a shorter transcript that maps immediately upstream of conserved sequence block 1 (CSB1)

Both mutant templates showed a trend toward increased LSP transcription activity when either the full-length or shorter product was analyzed, but only in the case of shorter, $\sim$ 160 transcript from the haplogroup J template (C295T) was this difference statistically significant (p-value 0.

Both mutant templates showed a trend toward increased LSP transcription activity when either the full-length or shorter product was analyzed, but only in the case of shorter, $\sim$ 160 transcript from the haplogroup J template (C295T) was this difference statistically significant (p-value 0.034).

Notably, the J1b2 construct showed increased transcription activity, although its additional mutation (C242T) did not significantly affect TFAM binding (data not shown), suggesting that the more functional variant is C295T that defines haplogroup J as a whole.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: In vitro transcription increased for haplogroup J templates.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Whether these effects are observed in a cellular context.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Haplogroup J cybrids will show altered mtDNA copy number and/or transcript levels compared to haplogroup H.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Generate cybrids with haplogroup J and H mtDNA backgrounds and measure mtDNA copy number and transcript levels.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Haplogroup J cybrids had >2 fold increase in mtDNA copy number vs H; no consistent difference in steady-state transcript levels.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Interpret the combined results to infer functional mechanism.

evidence.quote_or_span: cybrids (i.e., cells sharing the same nuclear genetic background but differing in their mtDNA backgrounds) harboring haplogroup J mtDNA had a >2 fold increase in mtDNA copy number, as compared to cybrids containing haplogroup H, with no apparent differences in steady state levels of mtDNA-encoded transcripts.

Two genes (i.e. ND1 and ND4L) exhibited reduced steady state levels in only one of the haplogroup J cybrids (designated 3861), whereas the other H- and L-strand transcripts showed no significance difference among the tested cybrids
both haplogroup J cybrids had more than twice as many mtDNAs than did any of the haplogroup H cybrids

Cybrids haplogroup assignment was verified by PCR RFLP of selected polymorphic sites and sequencing and HVR1 and HVR2: cybrids 3861 and 1106 belong to haplogroup J1 and J1b2, respectively, while cybrids CB, LR, NN, SB and TL belong to haplogroup H.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Cybrids show increased copy number but not transcript levels.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Mechanism linking C295T to replication and transcription.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: C295T may affect mtDNA replication efficiency, supported by elevated copy number; shorter transcript may relate to replication priming.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Interpret results in context of known roles of CSB1 and TFAM in replication priming.
evolution_trajectory.observation: C295T likely impacts replication efficiency; elevated copy number supports increased replication capacity.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Address discrepancy between in vitro transcription and in vivo transcript levels.

evidence.quote_or_span: it is quite possible that, in addition to its effects on transcription documented herein, the C295T variant also could impact mtDNA replication efficiency
The elevated mtDNA copy number in haplogroups J versus H cybrids support the possibly increased replication capacity

Since the region surrounding CSB1 and CSB2 has been implicated in various aspects of transcriptional priming of mtDNA replication [24,25,26], and given the involvement of TFAM in mitochondrial transcription and replication [16], it is quite possible that, in addition to its effects on transcription documented herein, the C295T variant also could impact mtDNA replication efficiency.
The elevated mtDNA copy number in haplogroups J versus H cybrids support the possibly increased replication capacity of haplogroups J.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Interpretation of replication effect, but discrepancy with transcript levels.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Why in vitro transcription increase is not reflected in steady-state transcript levels.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Post-transcriptional regulation or uncoupling of copy number and transcription may explain the discrepancy.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Analyze possible explanations: steady-state levels affected by post-transcriptional regulation; increased copy number does not necessarily increase transcription.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Discrepancy likely due to post-transcriptional regulation and uncoupling of copy number and transcription.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Acknowledge limitations and conclude.

evidence.quote_or_span: Measurements of mtDNA transcript levels by real time PCR reflect the steady state level of mRNA products, which is affected by both transcriptional and post-transcriptional regulation.

it has recently been shown that increased copy numbers do not necessarily lead to increased transcription [28], suggesting that our in vitro transcription results reflect differences in replication rather than transcription

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Functional effect of C295T observed, but evolutionary context unclear.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Whether low human variability at C295T supports functional constraint despite poor cross-species conservation.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Low human variability at C295T (single fixation event) suggests functional constraint.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Analyze phylogenetic distribution of C295T and its conservation across species.
evolution_trajectory.observation: C295T underwent only a single fixation event in human phylogeny (haplogroup J), suggesting functional constraint despite poor cross-species conservation.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Acknowledge limitation of site-directed mutagenesis.

evidence.quote_or_span: Since the C295T variant defines haplogroup J and was not fixed in any other human mtDNA lineage, it is possible that the observed functional effect can be partially attributed to its phenotypic effect.

Nevertheless, the C295T nucleotide position underwent only a single fixation event during human phylogeny in the branch leading to haplogroup J, suggesting that, although poorly conserved, the low human variability at this nucleotide position is in line with its functional potential.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Discrepancy explained, but limitations remain.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Inability to directly test specific mutations in mtDNA.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Site-directed mutagenesis would be needed to confirm causal role of C295T.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Acknowledge limitation: current inability to perform site-directed mutagenesis in mtDNA.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Major obstacle: cannot perform site-directed mutagenesis in mtDNA.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Conclude with summary of findings and limitations.

evidence.quote_or_span: A major obstacle interfering with the analysis of specific mtDNA mutations is the current inability to perform site-directed mutagenesis within mtDNA.

The variant defining Caucasian haplogroup J (C295T) increased the binding of TFAM (Electro Mobility Shift Assay) and the capacity of in vitro L-strand transcription, especially of a shorter transcript that maps immediately upstream of conserved sequence block 1 (CSB1), a region associated with RNA priming of mtDNA replication.

success_verification.final_conclusion: The C295T variant defining haplogroup J increases TFAM binding and in vitro transcription, and is associated with elevated mtDNA copy number in cybrids, suggesting a functional impact on mtDNA replication. However, steady-state transcript levels are not consistently altered, possibly due to post-transcriptional regulation. Direct mutagenesis is needed for confirmation.
limitations: Inability to perform site-directed mutagenesis in mtDNA
Small sample size: only 2 haplogroup J cybrid lines vs 5 haplogroup H lines
Inconsistent gene-specific transcript variations

risk_notes: Small sample size in cybrid experiments may limit generalizability.
Lack of direct mutagenesis prevents definitive causal attribution.

source.title: Laboratory Evolution of Fast-Folding Green Fluorescent Protein Using Secretory Pathway Quality Control

initial_request.research_problem: GFP folding inefficiency in E. coli; existing variants (frGFP, sfGFP) show limited fluorescence difference in cytoplasm, hindering directed evolution.
initial_request.known_context: Previous folding-enhanced variants (frGFP, sfGFP) showed only a 65% increase in geometric mean fluorescence in cytoplasm, insufficient for FACS-based directed evolution.
constraints: Must use SecYEG secretory pathway quality control for selection
Must maintain fluorescence function
quantifiable_goals: Achieve large fluorescence difference between folded and unfolded variants when targeted to SecYEG
Isolate superfolded variants with improved folding kinetics

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Cytoplasmic expression of frGFP and sfGFP showed only a 65% increase in geometric mean fluorescence, insufficient for FACS-based directed evolution.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Insufficient fluorescence difference in cytoplasm to discriminate folding variants.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: SecYEG-mediated quality control can discriminate folding and be used to engineer superfolded proteins.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Test targeting of sfGFP and frGFP to SecYEG via different signal peptides.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Hypothesis formulated that native cellular quality control pathways can exert evolutionary pressure to improve protein folding.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need to experimentally validate that SecYEG targeting yields large fluorescence differences.

evidence.quote_or_span: there was only a small increase (65%) in the geometric mean fluorescence of cells expressing sfGFP relative to frGFP

we explored the hypothesis that native cellular quality control pathways exert sufficient evolutionary pressure to improve protein folding properties beyond those that are normally sufficient for solubility and function within a cell.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Hypothesis that SecYEG quality control can discriminate folding.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Unknown whether SecYEG targeting yields sufficient fluorescence difference between folded and unfolded GFP variants.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Targeting sfGFP and frGFP to SecYEG via signal peptides will result in large fluorescence differences due to differential folding and degradation.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Construct fusions of sfGFP and frGFP with ssDsbA, ssMBP, ssPhoA and measure fluorescence.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 67-, 47-, and 24-fold greater fluorescence for ssDsbA-sfGFP, ssMBP-sfGFP, ssPhoA-sfGFP relative to corresponding frGFP fusions.

source.title: SPACIER: on-demand polymer design with fully automated all-atom classical molecular dynamics integrated into machine learning pipelines

initial_request.research_problem: On-demand polymer design is hindered by data scarcity and the difficulty of automating all-atom MD simulations for property prediction.
initial_request.target_object: polymers with desired properties (e.g., high refractive index, high Abbe number)
initial_request.known_context: Existing polymer databases like PoLyInfo have limited data (e.g., below 30 samples for thermal conductivity). ML predictors are interpolative and cannot extrapolate to novel polymers. Automating all-atom MD simulations for polymers faces technical barriers.
constraints: Polymers must be synthesizable in one step from commercially available monomers.
Virtual library limited to 101,487 candidates from SMiPoly.
input_data: Experimental data from PoLyInfo and other sources for calibration.
Virtual polymer library generated by SMiPoly.
quantifiable_goals: Detect polymers in target property regions within 20-30 BO cycles.
Cross empirical Pareto frontier for refractive index and Abbe number.
Achieve good agreement between experimental and calculated properties (e.g., refractive index within 0.01, Abbe number within 0.0).

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Polymer design is limited by scarce experimental data and interpolative ML predictors.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Lack of data and inability of ML to extrapolate to novel polymers.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Data scarcity is a major challenge in data-driven polymer research.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Identify data scarcity as a key problem.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Data scarcity is a major challenge; existing databases have very limited polymer data.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need to address technical barriers in automating MD simulations.

evidence.quote_or_span: A major challenge in data-driven materials research is the lack of data resources.
the scarcity of data on polymeric materials is remarkable.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: SPACIER framework established.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to specify BO method and polymer representation.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Use pool-based BO with GP and force-field kernel mean descriptor.
evolution_trajectory.observation: With a given library of virtual polymers, a pool-based BO was applied to identify promising candidates.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Generate virtual polymer library.

evidence.quote_or_span: With a given library of virtual polymers generated as described later, a pool-based BO was applied to identify promising candidates with the desired properties.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: No virtual library of synthesizable polymers exists.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need a diverse set of candidate polymers for BO.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: SMiPoly can generate a large virtual library of one-step synthesizable polymers.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Use SMiPoly with 22 reaction rules to generate virtual polymers.
evolution_trajectory.observation: SMiPoly generated a virtual polymer library.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Set up multi-objective BO acquisition functions.

evidence.quote_or_span: SMiPoly is a virtual polymer generator that implements 22 polymerization reaction rules

evolution_trajectory.decision: Acknowledge limitation.
evolution_trajectory.observation: It remains unclear to what extent RadonPy-calculated properties are quantitatively predictive for other properties and polymer systems.

evidence.quote_or_span: it remains unclear to what extent the RadonPy-calculated properties are quantitatively predictive for other properties and polymer systems.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Synthesis failure due to insolubility.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to avoid poorly soluble polymers in virtual library?

validation_methods: Experimental synthesis and characterization of designed polymers (P1, mpPh-PTU).
Comparison of experimental and calculated refractive indices and Abbe numbers.
metrics: Refractive index agreement (e.g., P1: exp 1.64 vs sim 1.63).
Abbe number agreement (e.g., P1: exp 32.0 vs sim 32.0).

success_verification.final_conclusion: SPACIER successfully integrates automated MD with BO to design polymers with desired optical properties, autonomously learning design principles like sulfonyl groups. Experimental validation confirmed good agreement for P1, but synthesis of P2 failed due to insolubility. Limitations include restricted structural diversity and unclear generalizability of RadonPy predictions.
limitations: Only a few optical polymers significantly exceeded the empirical boundary due to limited structural diversity from one-step synthesis restriction.
Unclear to what extent RadonPy-calculated properties are quantitatively predictive for other properties and polymer systems.
Synthesis failure of P2 due to insolubility.

source.title: Molecular property prediction using pretrained-BERT and Bayesian active learning: a data-efficient approach to drug design

initial_request.research_problem: Data-efficient molecular property prediction in drug discovery using active learning, where traditional methods with handcrafted features (ECFP) struggle with limited labeled data, leading to poor uncertainty estimation and suboptimal sample selection.
initial_request.known_context: Active learning typically trains models on labeled examples alone, while unlabeled data is only used for acquisition. This fully supervised approach neglects valuable information present in unlabeled molecular data, impairing both predictive performance and the molecule selection process.
constraints: Limited labeled data
Binary classification tasks
quantifiable_goals: Improve data efficiency in active learning for molecular property prediction
Achieve equivalent toxic compound identification with fewer iterations compared to conventional active learning

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Fully supervised active learning neglects unlabeled data, impairing predictive performance and molecule selection.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to leverage unlabeled data to improve active learning for molecular property prediction.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Integrating a pretrained BERT model (MolBERT) into active learning will disentangle representation learning and uncertainty estimation, leading to more reliable molecule selection.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Propose to use MolBERT embeddings in a semi-supervised active learning framework.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Pretrained BERT models can provide rich molecular representations that may improve uncertainty estimation.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Design the semi-supervised active learning pipeline with MolBERT embeddings.

evidence.quote_or_span: Active learning typically trains models on labeled examples alone, while unlabeled data is only used for acquisition. This fully supervised approach neglects valuable information present in unlabeled molecular data, impairing both predictive performance and the molecule selection process.

We address this limitation by integrating a transformer-based BERT model, pretrained on 1.26 million compounds, into the active learning pipeline. This effectively disentangles representation learning and uncertainty estimation, leading to more reliable molecule selection.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Hypothesis that MolBERT can improve active learning.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to define the architecture for semi-supervised active learning with MolBERT.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Using pretrained MolBERT embeddings (764-dim) and an MLP head will leverage large unlabeled data effectively.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Design semi-supervised active learning using MolBERT embeddings and an MLP head.
evolution_trajectory.observation: MolBERT provides 764-dimensional embeddings from SMILES sequences.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Design Bayesian neural network for uncertainty estimation.

evidence.quote_or_span: we propose to use molecular representations from a pretrained self-supervised learning model. Specifically, we encoded the molecular SMILES sequences into corresponding embeddings, utilizing a large transformer model MolBERT, pretrained on 1.6 million SMILES via masking, alongside physicochemical properties
The embedding of each SMILES sequence is a pooled output from the pretrained MolBERT with dimension 764.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: MolBERT embeddings defined.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need a method for uncertainty estimation in the active learning loop.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Design Bayesian neural network with MC dropout (dropout rate 0.5, 20 forward passes), skip connections, and batch normalization.

tool_or_method.name: Bayesian neural network with MC dropout

evolution_trajectory.observation: Network incorporates skip connections and batch normalization for enhanced information flow.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Set up experiments to compare BERT vs ECFP features with different acquisition functions.

evidence.quote_or_span: we use a Bayesian neural network to account for the model uncertainty.
we conduct 20 stochastic forward passes with dropout rate 0.5, each with a different dropout mask, to obtain a set of predictions.
the network incorporates a skip connection, merging the input and output of the hidden layer, enhancing information flow.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Designed semi-supervised active learning with MolBERT and Bayesian neural network.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How does the proposed approach compare to ECFP-based active learning with different acquisition functions?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: BERT-based features will outperform ECFP features in active learning, especially with EPIG acquisition.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Compare active learning performance using BERT vs ECFP features and EPIG, BALD, Random acquisition functions on Tox21 and ClinTox datasets with scaffold splitting and balanced initial set of 100 molecules.

tool_or_method.name: Active learning with BERT/ECFP features and EPIG/BALD/Random acquisition

evolution_trajectory.observation: Experiments conducted; results to be analyzed.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Analyze learning curves and statistical significance.

evidence.quote_or_span: Experiments on Tox21 and ClinTox datasets demonstrate that our approach achieves equivalent toxic compound identification with 50% fewer iterations compared to conventional active learning.
We evaluated the impact of molecular representations on active learning performance using three acquisition strategies (EPIG, BALD, and Random sampling) on two datasets (Tox21 and ClinTox).
Train and test sets are created using an 80/20 scaffold split, with the training set further divided into initial set, validation set, and pool set through random splitting.

BERT-based models efficiently extracted 70% of positive samples in approximately 100 iterations, while ECFP-based models required around 180 iterations—nearly a twofold increase in sample acquisition effort.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Active learning experiments completed.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to quantify the performance differences between BERT and ECFP features.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: BERT-EPIG will show steeper learning curve and earlier convergence than ECFP-EPIG; BERT-BALD will reach stable significance earlier than ECFP-BALD.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Analyze learning curves and statistical significance of BERT vs ECFP methods.
evolution_trajectory.observation: BERT-EPIG demonstrates superior learning dynamics with a steeper improvement slope compared to ECFP-EPIG. BERT-BALD diverges from random sampling, achieving stable significance (p-value < 0.05) at iteration 529, while ECFP-BALD reaches stable significance only after iteration 878. BERT-EPIG achieves convergence significantly earlier (300 iterations) compared to ECFP-EPIG (600 iterations).

tool_or_method.name: UMAP, PCA, Davies-Bouldin index, class purity, Fisher's ratio

evolution_trajectory.observation: BERT features exhibit more structured organization with distinct clusters; ECFP representations show scattered distribution with overlap. Davies-Bouldin index: BERT 6.046 vs ECFP 9.529 (36.5% better separation). Positive class purity: BERT 0.154 vs ECFP 0.091 (69.5% higher). Fisher's ratio: BERT 0.054 vs ECFP 0.021 (2.5 times higher).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Analyze model calibration to link structured representations to uncertainty quality.

evidence.quote_or_span: The BERT features exhibit more structured organization, where positive samples (red points, 6.8% of dataset) are distributed in distinct clusters
ECFP representations show a more scattered distribution with significant overlap between positive and negative regions
These metrics collectively demonstrate that BERT's structural advantages are fundamental rather than artifacts of visualization choices.

The Davies-Bouldin index, where lower values indicate better cluster separation, shows that BERT (6.046) provides 36.5% better separation between toxic and non-toxic compounds compared to ECFP (9.529).
Fisher's ratio-a direct measure of class separability that quantifies the ratio of between-class to within-class variance-is 2.5 times higher for BERT (0.054) compared to ECFP (0.021).
For the minority positive class (toxic compounds), BERT embeddings demonstrate 69.5% higher neighborhood purity (0.154 vs. 0.091)

evolution_trajectory.state_before: BERT features show structured clusters.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Does the structured representation lead to better uncertainty calibration?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: BERT-based methods will have lower Expected Calibration Error (ECE) early on, with BERT-EPIG achieving fastest ECE reduction.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Analyze model calibration using Expected Calibration Error (ECE) over active learning iterations.
evolution_trajectory.observation: All methods initially exhibit high ECE (0.30–0.38). BERT-based approaches demonstrate consistently lower ECE compared to ECFP counterparts throughout early stages (iterations 0-200). BERT-EPIG achieves fastest reduction in ECE. All methods eventually converge to ECE < 0.1 after 600-800 iterations.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Evaluate positive sample acquisition efficiency.

evidence.quote_or_span: All methods initially exhibit high ECE (0.30–0.38), indicating poor calibration due to limited training data.
the BERT-based approaches demonstrate consistently lower ECE compared to their ECFP counterparts throughout the early stages of AL (iterations 0-200).
Particularly noteworthy is the EPIG acquisition function with BERT features, which achieves the
All methods initially exhibit high ECE (0.30–0.38), indicating poor calibration due to limited training data.
While all methods eventually converge to well-calibrated uncertainties (ECE < 0.1) after 600-800 iterations, the path to achieving good calibration is markedly different.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: BERT-EPIG shows best calibration.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How does improved calibration translate to practical sample acquisition?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: BERT-EPIG will identify toxic compounds more efficiently than other methods.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Analyze positive sample acquisition on ClinTox dataset.
evolution_trajectory.observation: BERT-EPIG identifies 70% of toxic compounds in only 266 iterations, compared to 343 iterations for BERT-BALD and approximately 600 iterations for Random sampling. This represents a 2-fold improvement over Random sampling.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Quantify relative gains over random sampling.

evidence.quote_or_span: BERT-EPIG demonstrates superior acquisition efficiency, identifying 70% of toxic compounds in only 266 iterations, compared to 343 iterations for BERT-BALD and approximately 600 iterations for Random sampling.
BERT-EPIG demonstrated a 2-fold improvement over Random sampling in identifying toxic compounds.

BERT-EPIG demonstrated a 2-fold improvement over Random sampling by identifying 70% of toxic compounds (gray horizontal line) in only 266 iterations compared to approximately 600 iterations for Random sampling, demonstrating better exploration of the chemical space when starting with limited labeled data

evolution_trajectory.state_before: BERT-EPIG shows 2-fold improvement in positive sample acquisition.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How do relative gains over random sampling compare between BERT and ECFP, and between EPIG and BALD?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: BERT-EPIG will achieve higher gains over random than ECFP-EPIG; BALD will show delayed gains or initial degradation, especially with ECFP on ClinTox.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Analyze relative gain over random sampling for all methods.
evolution_trajectory.observation: BERT features enable more reliable uncertainty estimation. BALD shows positive gains after 400-600 iterations, while EPIG maintains consistent improvements from early stages. On ClinTox, ECFP-BALD initially degrades performance (-0.125) before recovery.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate findings on additional ADME datasets.

evidence.quote_or_span: This disparity suggests BERT features not only provide better base performance but also enable more reliable uncertainty estimation for superior sample selection.
While BALD shows positive gains after 400-600 iterations, EPIG maintains consistent improvements from early stages.
This difference is most pronounced in ClinTox, where ECFP-BALD initially degrades performance (-0.125) before recovery, while EPIG maintains stable gains.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: BERT features show consistent advantages on Tox21 and ClinTox.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Do the advantages of BERT features extend to other datasets like ADME properties?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Performance advantages of BERT over ECFP will extend to ADME datasets (PAMPA permeability, Pgp inhibition).
evolution_trajectory.decision: Validate on PAMPA permeability and Pgp inhibition datasets.
evolution_trajectory.observation: For PAMPA permeability, both BALD and EPIG with BERT representations achieve higher average precision compared to Random sampling; with ECFP, both perform nearly identically to Random. On Pgp inhibition, BERT methods quickly separate from Random (around iteration 50-100), while ECFP separation occurs much later (iteration 250-300) with smaller gap.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Note failure case on Pgp inhibition due to distribution mismatch.

evidence.quote_or_span: For PAMPA permeability (top row), both BALD and EPIG acquisition functions with BERT representations achieve higher average precision compared to Random sampling.
when using ECFP features for the same dataset, both BALD and EPIG perform nearly identically to Random sampling throughout the entire learning process.
With BERT representations, both BALD and EPIG quickly separate from Random sampling (around iteration 50-100).
with ECFP features, the separation from random sampling occurs much later (around iteration 250-300) and the performance gap remains considerably smaller throughout the learning process.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: BERT methods show advantages on ADME datasets, but a decline is observed on Pgp inhibition.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Why does performance decline after peaking on Pgp inhibition?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Distribution mismatch between pool and test sets causes the decline.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Analyze the cause of performance decline on Pgp inhibition.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Both EPIG and BALD with BERT features show decline in average precision after peaking at iterations 150-200. This decline stems from a distribution mismatch—the pool set has more positives (ratio 1.56) while the test set has more negatives (ratio 0.35). Uncertainty-based methods preferentially select minority negative samples from the pool, with BERT-BALD's positive-to-negative ratio dropping from 1.0 to 0.55 by iteration 300, creating harmful distribution shift.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Conclude with limitations and overall findings.

evidence.quote_or_span: Both EPIG and BALD with BERT features show decline in average precision after peaking at iterations 150-200.
This decline stems from a distribution mismatch-the pool set has more positives (ratio 1.56) while the test set has more negatives (ratio 0.35).
Figure 10 shows uncertainty-based methods preferentially select minority negative samples from the pool, with BERT-BALD's positive-to-negative ratio dropping from 1.0 to 0.55 by iteration 300.
This sampling initially improves performance but eventually creates harmful distribution shift.

validation_methods: Representation analysis (UMAP, PCA, Davies-Bouldin index, class purity, Fisher's ratio)
success_verification.final_conclusion: Integrating pretrained MolBERT embeddings into active learning with EPIG acquisition significantly improves data efficiency for molecular property prediction. BERT-EPIG achieves steeper learning curves, earlier convergence, better calibration, and 2-fold improvement in toxic compound identification over random sampling. However, BALD underperforms random on ClinTox, and distribution mismatch can cause performance decline on some datasets.
limitations: Binary classification only
Limited datasets (Tox21, ClinTox, PAMPA, Pgp)
One pretrained model (MolBERT)
Performance decline on Pgp inhibition due to distribution mismatch

source.title: Unveiling molecular moieties through hierarchical Grad-CAM graph explainability

initial_request.research_problem: Explaining GNN predictions in virtual screening for drug discovery by identifying molecular substructures (moieties) driving bioactivity, to guide medicinal chemists.
initial_request.known_context: GNN predictions for molecular bioactivity are not self-explainable; existing XAI methods (SHAP, GNNExplainer) fail to provide pharmacophoric-level explanations.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: GNN predictions for molecular bioactivity are not self-explainable; existing XAI methods (SHAP, GNNExplainer) fail to provide pharmacophoric-level explanations.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Existing XAI methods do not provide pharmacophoric-level explanations for GNN predictions.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Hierarchical Grad-CAM at multiple graph depths (atom, ring, whole-molecule) can capture multi-scale moieties for complete explanation.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Propose Hierarchical Grad-CAM (HGE) to obtain local explanations at atom, ring, and entire molecule level.
evolution_trajectory.observation: HGE leverages Grad-CAM to obtain local explanations at atom, ring, and the entire molecule level.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need to design a GNN architecture to apply Grad-CAM.

evidence.quote_or_span: the integration of explainable methods to elucidate the specific contributions of molecular substructures to biological activity remains a significant challenge

Even if the graph representation of a molecule is self-explainable, the precise relation between the relevant molecular properties and the network prediction is not, and it suffers from the same issues observed in classical Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) devoted to images.
SHAP was used to determine the significance of individual features; however, it did not provide insight into their pharmacophoric characteristics.
HGE leverages Grad-CAM to obtain local explanations at atom, ring, and the entire molecule level

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Hypothesis of hierarchical Grad-CAM requires a GNN architecture.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need a GNN architecture optimized for high sensitivity in virtual screening.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A GCNN with U-Net-like channel expansion and 1-WL kernel can achieve high sensitivity.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Design GCNN architecture with 7 GC blocks, channel sequence 32,64,128,256,128,64,32, optimized for high sensitivity.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Architecture designed with U-Net-like channel expansion to balance complexity and performance.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need datasets to train the GCNN classifiers.

evidence.quote_or_span: The architecture consists of seven GC blocks, with the number of channels in each GC layer following this sequence: 32, 64, 128, 256, 128, 64, and again 32.
the main aim of the network is to provide a very high sensitivity across all the protein targets analyzed in this study
To achieve this, we carefully optimized the network architecture, tailoring its structure to effectively balance complexity and performance.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: GCNN architecture designed; need training data.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need datasets with appropriate imbalance for training and stress testing.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: EMBER (1:100 imbalance) and ChEMBL_over (oversampled to 1:15) will serve as training and stress test sets.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Prepare EMBER dataset (89373 molecules, 1:100 ratio) and ChEMBL_over (oversampled inactives to 1:15 ratio).
evolution_trajectory.observation: Datasets prepared with specified imbalance ratios.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Train 20 single-target GCNN classifiers on these datasets.

evidence.quote_or_span: The final data set consists of 89373 small molecules and has a 1:100 active/inactive ratio for the smallest class
Overampling was applied on inactive molecules, until an active/inactive ratio of 1:15 was obtained

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Datasets prepared; GCNN architecture designed.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to train classifiers for 20 kinase targets.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Training with weighted BCE loss and soft F-score will yield high sensitivity.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Train 20 single-target GCNN classifiers on EMBER and ChEMBL_over with hyperparameter grid search, using weighted BCE loss and soft F-score.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 20 classifiers trained; hyperparameters selected via grid search.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Adapt Grad-CAM for hierarchical explanation.

evidence.quote_or_span: we built twenty GCNN classifiers, each dedicated to a specific protein target. These models were trained to optimize classification performance on a single target.
A separate GCNN binary classifier was trained to assess compounds activity for each target.
A grid search was performed to select the hyperparameters properly.
The training process was conducted on an NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3090 with 10,496 CUDA cores
our architecture outperforms in terms of sensitivity the two architectures used as a comparison on all 20 protein targets for the EMBER dataset
our architecture outperforms in terms of sensitivity the two architectures used as a comparison on all 20 protein targets for the EMBER dataset.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Trained GCNNs available; need explainability method.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: GNNExplainer is too fine-grained; need to capture negative contributions and multi-scale moieties.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Adapting Grad-CAM by removing ReLU and placing at layers 1,3,7 will capture both positive and negative contributions at multiple scales.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Reject GNNExplainer; adapt Grad-CAM by removing ReLU and placing explainers at GC layers 1, 3, and 7.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Grad-CAM chosen as best technique; ReLU removed to capture negative contributions; explainers placed at layers 1,3,7 for atom, ring, and whole-molecule levels.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Merge Grad-CAM outputs and threshold to identify relevant moieties.

evidence.quote_or_span: We chose Grad-CAM as the best performing technique for labeling pointwise contributions to positive predictions
the ReLU activation function has been removed to capture not only positive contributions to the prediction, but also negative ones.

we placed also a Grad-CAM explainer at the very first GC layer to consider the atoms' impact in a 1-neighborhood that is at a bond level. Another explainer was placed after the third layer to catch information related to 3-neighborhoods.
GNNExplainer detects as relevant the two arcs connecting C-5/C-6/O-16 and the arc connecting C-12/C-13

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Grad-CAM outputs from multiple layers need to be combined.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to merge multi-scale Grad-CAM outputs into a single relevance map.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Summing, min-max scaling, and thresholding at 0.7 will identify relevant moieties.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Merge Grad-CAM outputs by summing, min-max scaling, and thresholding at 0.7.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Relevant moieties identified as contributions above 0.7 after min-max scaling.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Evaluate GCNN performance against baselines on EMBER.

evidence.quote_or_span: The resulting $L_{Grad-CAM}$ distribution has been min-max scaled, and only contributions higher than 0.7 have been regarded as relevant.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: HGE method defined; need to evaluate GCNN performance.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to compare GCNN sensitivity against Chemprop and Attentive FP on EMBER.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: GCNN will outperform baselines in sensitivity due to architecture optimization.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Evaluate GCNN, Chemprop, and Attentive FP on EMBER dataset.
evolution_trajectory.observation: GCNN outperforms Chemprop and Attentive FP in sensitivity on all 20 targets (mean 0.899 vs 0.704 vs 0.395), with AUC 0.982 and F1 0.960.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Stress test on ChEMBL_over to assess robustness.

evidence.quote_or_span: our architecture outperforms in terms of sensitivity the two architectures used as a comparison on all 20 protein targets for the EMBER dataset.
our architecture outperforms in terms of sensitivity the two architectures used as a comparison on all 20 protein targets for the EMBER dataset
it also has an very good ability to prioritise True Positives in terms of TP/P and EF

evolution_trajectory.state_before: GCNN shows superior sensitivity on EMBER.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to test on oversampled dataset to check robustness.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: GCNN will maintain high sensitivity but may trade off F1 due to oversampling.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Evaluate on ChEMBL_over dataset.
evolution_trajectory.observation: GCNN has higher sensitivity (0.946 vs 0.897) but Chemprop has higher F1 (0.945 vs 0.920); GCNN better prioritizes true positives.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate on known DrugBank drugs.

evidence.quote_or_span: Our architectures outperform Chemprop on 18 out of 20 targets in terms of sensitivity
Chemprop's average F1-score is higher than our architectures
our networks outperform Chemprop at prioritising True Positive
This result may suggest a vulnerability in the Chemprop net, which appears more prone than our GCNNs to be misled in determining True Positives by the presence of over-sampled chemicals

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Performance characterized on benchmark datasets.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to validate HGE's ability to assign drugs to known targets.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: HGE will correctly assign most DrugBank kinase inhibitors to their known targets.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Validate on 143 known drugs from DrugBank for 20 kinases.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 116/143 (81%) drugs correctly assigned; consistent moiety identification (e.g., Apigenin on CDK6/CK2A1).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Analyze failure cases on multi-target drugs.

evidence.quote_or_span: 116 drugs out of 143 were correctly assigned to the target known in the literature
the prediction of the molecular moieties of Apigenin, a drug that is bioactive on CDK6 and CK2A1, remains congruous

To evaluate the explainability power of HGE, we selected a test set made by 143 known drugs from DrugBank $^{5}$ for the 20 kinases considered in this work to compare the predictions with experimental and clinical results.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Overall validation accuracy 81%.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Multi-target drugs may be harder to classify.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Multi-target drugs will have lower classification accuracy due to scaffold similarity and limited training data.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Analyze classification accuracy on multi-target drugs.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Only 30/44 (68%) multi-target drugs correctly classified; reasons include scaffold similarity and limited training data.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Compare HGE qualitatively with GNNExplainer.

evidence.quote_or_span: only 30/44 (0.6818) drugs with multi-target activity are correctly classified
the misclassification on the 14 multi-target drug molecules can be traced back to different reasons
for some targets, training datasets contain fewer known molecules

evolution_trajectory.state_before: HGE validated; GNNExplainer available as baseline.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to demonstrate HGE's superiority in providing richer moiety descriptions.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: HGE provides more complete and chemically meaningful explanations than GNNExplainer.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Qualitatively compare HGE and GNNExplainer on Apigenin and other molecules.
evolution_trajectory.observation: HGE combines layers for complete interpretation, consistently identifies aromatic regions and H-bond acceptors, while GNNExplainer only detects arcs.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Acknowledge limitations.

evidence.quote_or_span: GNNExplainer detects as relevant the two arcs connecting C-5/C-6/O-16 and the arc connecting C-12/C-13
Fig. 6 Results obtained with GNNExplainer compared with HGE on the Apigenin molecule
the predictive comparison with GNNExplainer in Fig. 6 shows the different ability of our explainer in labelling information in purely chemical terms.
The main strength of the presented approach is the combination of different explanation layers (from single atoms to complex structures).

evolution_trajectory.state_before: HGE shown to be effective but has limitations.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: HGE does not account for pharmacological effect or potency magnitude; single-target focus limits multi-target applicability.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: HGE identifies binding-relevant structures but not pharmacological effect.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Note limitations: HGE identifies binding-relevant structures but does not account for pharmacological effect or potency magnitude; single-target focus limits multi-target applicability.
evolution_trajectory.observation: HGE identifies chemical structures compatible with protein binding process, without considering pharmacological effect or drug potency.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Propose future work.

evidence.quote_or_span: HGE identifies chemical structures compatible with protein binding process, without considering pharmacological effect or drug potency.

success_verification.final_conclusion: HGE successfully provides hierarchical explanations of GNN predictions, identifying relevant molecular moieties. GCNN outperforms baselines in sensitivity, and HGE offers richer explanations than GNNExplainer. However, multi-target classification remains challenging, and pharmacological effect is not captured.
limitations: HGE identifies binding-relevant structures but does not account for pharmacological effect or potency magnitude.
Single-target focus limits multi-target applicability.

source.title: Role of Artificial Intelligence in Drug Discovery and Design: From Foundational Principles to Emerging Applications in Antiviral Therapeutics

initial_request.research_problem: Traditional antiviral drug discovery is too slow, expensive, and failure-prone to keep pace with rapidly mutating viruses, necessitating AI-driven acceleration.
initial_request.known_context: Traditional drug discovery takes >10 years, costs >$2 billion, and has <10% success rate.
constraints: Must reduce time and cost
Must handle rapid viral mutations
quantifiable_goals: Reduce cost and time by over 80%
Reduce screening space by 90-95%

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Traditional drug discovery is slow, costly, and low success rate, unable to keep up with viral mutations.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to accelerate drug discovery to match the pace of viral evolution.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: AI techniques (ML, DL, NLP, RL) can accelerate drug discovery by analyzing large datasets and predicting molecular properties.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Propose AI as a solution to replace traditional methods.
evolution_trajectory.observation: AI has shown impressive success in various domains and can speed up drug discovery.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Apply AI specifically to replace high-throughput screening with virtual screening.

evidence.quote_or_span: Traditional drug discovery methods are not able to compete with rapidly occurring viral mutations, since these are often time-consuming and labor-intensive.

the use of AI, which includes a variety of techniques such as machine learning (ML), deep learning (DL), natural language processing (NLP), and reinforcement learning (RL), has shown impressive success
AI techniques, such as machine learning (ML), deep learning (DL), and natural language processing (NLP), can speed up and enhance this process by analyzing vast datasets efficiently.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: AI techniques proposed but not yet applied to specific drug discovery stages.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to replace slow and expensive high-throughput screening.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: AI can replace high-throughput screening with rapid in silico virtual screening.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Implement AI-driven virtual screening.
evolution_trajectory.observation: AI replaces high-throughput screening with rapid in silico analysis.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate quantitative improvements in cost, time, and screening space.

evidence.quote_or_span: In virtual screening and hit identification, AI replaces high-throughput screening with rapid in silico analysis.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: AI-driven virtual screening proposed but quantitative benefits unknown.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to quantify cost, time, and screening space reductions.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: AI-driven screening pipelines can reduce cost and time by over 80% and pre-screening models can reduce screening space by 90-95%.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Evaluate AI-driven screening pipelines and pre-screening models.
evolution_trajectory.observation: AI-driven screening pipelines reduced cost and time by over 80%; AI pre-screening models reduced screening space by 90-95%.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Extend AI to generative chemistry for novel molecule design.

evidence.quote_or_span: Kwofie et al. [27] in a recent study, found that AI-driven screening pipelines reduced both cost and time by over $80\%$ , when applied to antiviral compounds.
Instead of testing all compounds, AI ranks top candidates, reducing screening space by 90-95% [30].

evolution_trajectory.state_before: AI screening validated, but need for novel molecule design.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to rapidly design novel drug-like molecules.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Generative chemistry with reinforcement learning can design novel drug-like molecules rapidly.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Apply generative chemistry approaches.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Generative chemistry approaches utilize reinforcement learning to design novel, drug-like molecules rapidly.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Address safety profiling to reduce adverse effects.

evidence.quote_or_span: Generative chemistry approaches utilize reinforcement learning to design novel, drug-like molecules rapidly.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: AI designed molecules need safety assessment.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to predict and reduce adverse effects.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: AI models like DeepTox can forecast unintended protein interactions and drug-drug interactions to improve safety profiles.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Use off-target profiling with DeepTox.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Through off-target profiling using models such as DeepTox, AI reduces adverse effects by forecasting unintended protein interactions and drug-drug interactions.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Apply AI to drug repurposing for existing antivirals.

evidence.quote_or_span: Through off-target profiling using models such as DeepTox, AI reduces adverse effects by forecasting unintended protein interactions and drug-drug interactions, improving safety profiles.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: AI methods established for screening and design, but repurposing potential unexplored.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can AI repurpose existing drugs for new viral targets?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: AI can identify existing drugs with antiviral activity against dengue and Ebola.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Test AI modeling for drug repurposing.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Quinine repurposed for dengue via AI modeling of viral protease inhibition; Tilorone predicted by deep learning as broad-spectrum antiviral for Ebola.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Extend repurposing to COVID-19.

evidence.quote_or_span: Quinine: Repurposed for dengue through AI modeling of viral protease inhibition.
Tilorone: Predicted by deep learning to act as a broad-spectrum antiviral, especially for Ebola.
- Antiviral drug development for specific viral diseases, including COVID-19, dengue, hepatitis, and Ebola.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: AI repurposing successful for dengue and Ebola, but COVID-19 pandemic emerges.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need rapid identification of COVID-19 treatments.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: AI analysis of gene expression profiles can identify existing drugs as potential COVID-19 treatments.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Apply AI model to identify baricitinib for COVID-19.

tool_or_method.name: AI model by Richardson et al.

evolution_trajectory.observation: An AI model identified baricitinib, an arthritis drug, as a potential COVID-19 treatment by analyzing gene expression profiles.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Further ML-based repurposing for SARS-CoV-2.

evidence.quote_or_span: An AI model by Richardson et al. [36] identified baricitinib, an arthritis drug, as a potential COVID-19 treatment by analyzing gene expression profiles.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Baricitinib identified, but more repurposing candidates needed for SARS-CoV-2.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can supervised ML predict additional FDA-approved drugs with antiviral activity against SARS-CoV-2?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Supervised ML can identify zafirlukast as a repurposing candidate against SARS-CoV-2.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Use supervised ML to screen FDA-approved drugs.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Zafirlukast has emerged as a promising repurposing candidate against SARS-CoV-2 infection.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Apply AI to influenza drug discovery.

evidence.quote_or_span: Zafirlukast has emerged as a promising repurposing candidate against SARS-CoV-2 infection

evolution_trajectory.state_before: AI repurposing successful for multiple viruses, but influenza remains a challenge.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can AI identify new influenza treatments and combinations?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: AI algorithms can flag baloxavir before FDA approval and DL-based screening can identify effective drug combinations against resistant strains.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Apply AI algorithms and DL-based screening to influenza.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Baloxavir was initially flagged by AI algorithms before becoming an FDA-approved influenza treatment; DL-based screening identified the combination of oseltamivir with specific antihistamines as particularly effective against resistant strains.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Explore network pharmacology for additional influenza treatments.

evidence.quote_or_span: Baloxavir was initially flagged by AI algorithms before becoming an FDA-approved influenza treatment
DL-based screening identified the combination of oseltamivir with specific antihistamines as particularly effective against resistant strains
Baloxavir was initially flagged by AI algorithms before becoming an FDA-approved influenza treatment [57].
DL-based screening identified the combination of oseltamivir with specific antihistamines as particularly effective against resistant strains [58].

evolution_trajectory.state_before: AI applied to influenza, but other mechanisms may exist.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can network pharmacology models discover new influenza treatments?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Network pharmacology models can discover that cholesterol-lowering medications may disrupt influenza virus replication.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Apply network pharmacology models.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Network pharmacology models discovered that certain cholesterol-lowering medications may disrupt influenza virus replication.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Acknowledge limitations of AI-driven datasets.

evidence.quote_or_span: Network pharmacology models discovered that certain cholesterol-lowering medications may disrupt influenza virus replication [59].

source.title: Multistate and functional protein design using RoseTTAFold sequence space diffusion

initial_request.research_problem: Developing a sequence-space diffusion model (ProteinGenerator) based on RoseTTAFold that simultaneously generates protein sequences and structures, enabling direct sequence-based guidance for multistate and functional protein design.
initial_request.known_context: Structure-based diffusion models (RFdiffusion, Chroma) provide limited opportunities for sequence-based guidance and multistate design.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Structure-based diffusion models (RFdiffusion, Chroma) provide limited opportunities for sequence-based guidance and multistate design.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to enable sequence-based guidance and multistate design in protein diffusion models.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Carrying out diffusion in sequence space rather than structure space could enable guidance using sequence-based features and explicit design of sequences populating multiple states.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Develop a sequence-space diffusion model (ProteinGenerator) based on RoseTTAFold.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Sequence-space diffusion could overcome limitations of structure-based models.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Proceed to design sequence representation and diffusion process.

evidence.quote_or_span: Although powerful, structure-based approaches, such as RFdiffusion $^{11}$ and Chroma $^{12}$ , provide limited opportunities to guide protein generation using sequence-based features and to identify sequences with more than one fold and/or function.

We reasoned that carrying out diffusion in sequence space rather than structure space could enable guidance of design using sequence-based features and explicit design of sequences populating multiple states.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Sequence representation and noise schedule defined.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to train the model to generate sequences and structures.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Multi-task learning with diffusion, structure prediction, and fixed backbone design will yield a model capable of sequence-structure generation.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Train RoseTTAFold-based model with multi-task learning (40% diffusion, 30% structure prediction, 30% fixed backbone design) using categorical cross-entropy, FAPE, and KL losses with self-conditioning.

parameters.task_weights: diffusion 40%, structure prediction 30%, fixed backbone design 30%

evolution_trajectory.observation: Model trained with multi-task learning.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Set up inference procedure with self-conditioning and logit clamping.

evidence.quote_or_span: Along with the standard diffusion task (40% of the time), the model was also challenged with structure prediction (seq2str) and fixed backbone sequence design (30% of the time each).

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Inference procedure defined.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Does unconditional generation produce viable proteins?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Unconditional generation will yield a fraction of high-confidence designs that are soluble and monomeric.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Generate proteins unconditionally and filter by AF2 pLDDT>90 and RMSD<2Å; experimentally test 42 designs.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 6% of designs have AF2 pLDDT>90 and RMSD<2Å; 32/42 tested proteins soluble and monomeric.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Explore sequence-based guidance with rare amino acid enrichment.

evidence.quote_or_span: 6% of designs have AF2 confidence pLDDT > 90 and RMSD < 2 Å
Of the 42 proteins experimentally tested, 32 were soluble and monomeric by SEC
Designs were filtered by AF2 pLDDT > 90 and AF2 RMSD to design < 2 Å for ordering final constructs.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Unconditional generation successful.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can sequence-based guidance enrich rare amino acids and induce disulfide bonds without structural conditioning?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Biasing amino acid composition during generation will yield soluble proteins enriched in rare amino acids, and high cysteine content will form disulfide bonds.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Generate proteins with biased amino acid composition (tryptophan, cysteine, valine, histidine, methionine) and test expression and solubility.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 68/96 expressed designs soluble; cysteine designs formed 3-4 disulfide bonds without structural conditioning.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Apply to repeat protein design.

evidence.quote_or_span: Of the expressed designs, 68 were soluble in Escherichia coli
Guiding for high cysteine content at the sequence level resulted in the formation of 3–4 disulfide bonds per protein without any structural conditioning
separate PG trajectories were carried out for each enriched amino acid.
SEC Normalized absorbance
Generated designs were filtered for high AF2 confidence (pLDDT > 90) and self-consistency (RMSD to design < 2 Å), and 96 were selected for experimental characterization.
For broad applicability, we carried out this optimization with standard settings and no extensive hyperparameter turning (96 designs generated and tested per round for three rounds).

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Rare amino acid enrichment demonstrated.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can PG generate diverse repeat proteins?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Secondary structure conditioning will enable diverse repeat protein designs beyond beta-solenoids.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Generate repeat proteins with and without secondary structure conditioning; test solubility and monomericity.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Unconditional generation biased toward beta-solenoids; secondary structure conditioning enabled diverse designs; 27/74 capped and 10/86 uncapped soluble and monomeric.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate atomic accuracy with crystal structure.

evidence.quote_or_span: Unconditional generation with this approach yielded largely beta-solenoid structures.

To encourage further exploration, we trained PG to condition on secondary structure (Supplementary Fig. 16) and specified secondary structure constraints to yield a wide range of all-alpha, all-beta and mixed alpha/beta designs
Of these, 27 repeats with caps and 10 repeats without helical caps were soluble and monomeric by SEC

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Repeat protein designs generated.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Are the designs atomically accurate?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: The designed repeat protein structure will match the crystal structure with high accuracy.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Solve crystal structure of a five-repeat four-helix bundle design.
evolution_trajectory.observation: C RMSD 1.38 Å overall, 0.47 Å for asymmetric unit.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Apply PG to cage bioactive peptides.

evidence.quote_or_span: We solved the crystal structure of a five-repeat unit design composed of a four-helix bundle asymmetric unit and found the design to have atomic accuracy: the C RMSD of design to the crystal structure was 1.38 Å for the whole structure and 0.47 Å for the asymmetric unit
Diffraction quality crystals formed in a mixture of 0.1 M PCB buffer (pH 4) and 25% PEG 1500.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Atomic accuracy validated.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can PG cage bioactive peptides for conditional activity?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Fixing a bioactive peptide sequence and diffusing the remainder with secondary structure conditioning and a furin cleavage site will yield a caged peptide that is released upon protease cleavage.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Design melittin cages by fixing melittin sequence, diffusing scaffold, and including furin cleavage site.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Melittin cage designs generated.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Experimentally validate melittin cages.

evidence.quote_or_span: We used PG to address this challenge by scaffolding bioactive peptide sequences within an inert protein cage (Fig. 4a) by designating a region of the protein chain (usually at the N or C terminus) to be fixed at the bioactive peptide sequence and freely diffusing the remainder of the sequence.
Proteins of 155 amino acids in length were generated in 25 steps with spans of helical DSSP conditioning to encourage the model to generate helical bundles and cleavage loops.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Melittin cage designs generated.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Do the designed cages express, fold, and release melittin upon furin cleavage?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A subset of designs will be soluble, monomeric, helical, thermostable, and show conditional hemolysis.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Express and characterize 13 designs; test furin cleavage and hemolysis.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 5/13 soluble, monomeric, helical, thermostable; furin cleavage confirmed; conditional hemolysis (P=0.002, P=0.005).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Note limitation of increased sampling and filtering.

evidence.quote_or_span: Of 13 experimentally characterized designs, five were soluble and monodisperse by SEC, folded with helical secondary structure by CD and thermostable
Upon addition of furin protease, a band shift from -18 kD to -15 kD indicated cleavage of our designed melittin cage
Mass spectrometry analysis confirmed release of intact melittin peptide
Of 13 experimentally characterized designs, five were soluble and monodisperse by SEC, folded with helical secondary structure by CD and thermostable

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Limitation noted.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can PG scaffold barcode peptides for protein identification?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: PG can scaffold short barcode sequences into a protein, enabling intrinsic barcoding.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Design proteins with C-terminal barcode sequences and test expression and monodispersity.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 64/84 expressed, 48/64 monodisperse, SEC-MS agreement for 41/58.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Apply PG to multistate design.

evidence.quote_or_span: PG could scaffold short barcode-like sequences (7–14 residues) into a protein of interest, thereby removing the need for multiple extrinsic barcodes
64 of 84 (76%) were expressed, and 48 of 64 (75%) of the expressed proteins exhibited monodisperse elution peaks
We observed close agreement between SEC-MS and iSEC peak elution volume for 41 of 58 (71%) designs
Proteins of 100 amino acids in length were generated in 50 steps with barcodes being set as fixed regions on the C-terminus.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Multistate designs generated.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Do the designs adopt the intended parent and child folds?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A subset of parent-child triples will be soluble and monodisperse, and NMR structures will match designs.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Characterize 72 triples; select 4 families for CD and NMR; solve NMR structures of MS2 and MS3 parents.
evolution_trajectory.observation: MS2 parent Cα RMSD 1.06 Å, MS3 parent Cα RMSD 1.61 Å.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Apply PG to iterative optimization with experimental data.

evidence.quote_or_span: We experimentally characterized 72 parent–child triples that AF2 predicted with high confidence and accuracy to be in the parent state when intact and the child states when split
we selected 4 (MS1–MS4) soluble and monodisperse sequence families for detailed CD and nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) studies
The solution NMR structure of MS2 parent is within 1.06 Å Cα RMSD of the design model
The solution NMR structure of MS3 parent is within 1.61 Å Cα RMSD of the design model

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Multistate designs validated.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can PG iteratively optimize sequences using experimental fitness data?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Gradient-based guidance from classifiers trained on experimental fitness will outperform Bayesian optimization.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Simulate 3 rounds of iterative design on GB1 fitness landscape; compare to Bayesian optimization.
evolution_trajectory.observation: PG outperforms Bayesian optimization in average and maximum fitness (P<0.05).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Attribute improved performance to RoseTTAFold prior.

evidence.quote_or_span: In silico demonstration of iterative design using GB1 fitness landscape for binding and comparison with Bayesian optimization (BO).
Mean activities for each round are statistically significant between the two populations (P < 0.05, two-sided Mann–Whitney U-test, n = 96 designs per round).
We simulated an iterative guidance process by exploring the fitness landscape of the protein GB1 via gradient-based optimization with IgG binding activity-guided diffusion trajectories.

We found that the average and maximum fitness of PG-generated designs increases each round, outperforming a Bayesian optimization baseline with the best identified acquisition function (batched upper confidence bound)

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Iterative optimization results.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Why does PG outperform Bayesian optimization?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: The rich sequence-structure prior in RoseTTAFold enables better exploration.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Attribute improved performance to RoseTTAFold prior.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Improved performance likely due to RoseTTAFold's prior understanding of sequence-structure relationships.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Conclude trajectory.

evidence.quote_or_span: The improved performance likely reflects the rich prior understanding of protein sequence–structure relationships implicit in RoseTTAFold compared to the baseline, which has access to only limited experimental data.

success_verification.final_conclusion: ProteinGenerator (PG) successfully generates sequences and structures with sequence-based guidance, enabling multistate design, peptide caging, barcode scaffolding, and iterative optimization, outperforming Bayesian optimization on GB1.
limitations: Considerable filtering required for complex designs (e.g., melittin cages).
PG better for soft sequence constraints; structure-based models better for global structural properties.

source.title: Biosensor and machine learning-aided engineering of an amaryllidaceae enzyme
