source.title: Designing of a multi-epitopes based vaccine against Haemophilius parainfluenzae and its validation through integrated computational approaches

initial_request.research_problem: Design a multi-epitope vaccine against Haemophilus parainfluenzae using computational methods to address antibiotic resistance and lack of existing vaccine.
initial_request.known_context: H. parainfluenzae is an opportunistic pathogen with increasing antibiotic resistance; no vaccine exists.
constraints: Computational design only
Must be non-allergenic, non-toxic, antigenic
Must cover broad population HLA alleles
quantifiable_goals: 90% worldwide population coverage
Stable 3D structure with >90% Ramachandran favored
Strong immune simulation response

evolution_trajectory.state_before: No vaccine exists for H. parainfluenzae; antibiotic resistance is a concern.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can a multi-epitope vaccine designed via bioinformatics elicit broad immune response?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A multi-epitope vaccine designed using immunoinformatics can elicit broad humoral and cellular immune responses against H. parainfluenzae.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Proceed with computational design of a multi-epitope vaccine.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Hypothesis formulated based on prior successes of multi-epitope vaccines for other pathogens.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need to select antigenic proteins from the proteome.

evidence.quote_or_span: The principal objective of this investigation is to utilize bioinformatics and immuno-informatic methodologies in the development of a candidate multi-epitope Vaccine.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: CTL epitopes selected; need B-cell epitopes.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which B-cell epitopes are antigenic, non-allergenic, non-toxic?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: B-cell epitopes predicted by ABCPred (score >0.5) and filtered will be suitable.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Predict B-cell epitopes using ABCPred, filter by antigenicity, allergenicity, toxicity.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Two B-cell epitopes selected (Table 4).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Construct multi-epitope vaccine with linkers and adjuvant.

evidence.quote_or_span: Similarly, among the selected 19 LBL epitopes, two were deemed suitable for vaccine development, considering predictions related to toxicity, allergenicity, and antigenicity (Table 4).
TABLE 4 Final selected B-cell epitope for the construction of vaccine.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: CTL and B-cell epitopes selected.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to assemble epitopes into a stable, immunogenic construct?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Using adjuvant (cholera enterotoxin subunit B) and specific linkers (EAAAK, AAY, GPGPG, KK) will yield a functional vaccine.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Construct MEV: adjuvant at N-terminal via EAAAK; CTL linked with AAY, HTL with GPGPG, B-cell with KK.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Final construct of 344 amino acids.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Analyze physicochemical and safety properties.

evidence.quote_or_span: Multi-epitope constructs were designed by using suitable linker sequences. To enhance the immunological potential, an adjuvant sequence was incorporated using the EAAAK linker.
The final vaccine construct, comprising 344 amino acids, was achieved after the addition of adjuvants and linkers.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: MEV construct validated for safety.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: What is the 3D structure of the MEV?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: AlphaFold2 can predict a reliable 3D model, which can be refined by GalaxyRefine.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Predict 3D structure with AlphaFold2, refine with GalaxyRefine.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Initial model generated and refined.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate refined structure.

evidence.quote_or_span: The initial vaccine 3D structure was generated using the Alpha-fold 2 server
The projected 3D structure was further refined and enhanced through the GalaxyRefine server

Your vaccine's tertiary structure was created using a multi-step process that guaranteed the protective structure's accuracy and predicted its function: The initial vaccine 3D structure was generated using the Alpha-fold 2 server (49).

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Refined 3D model obtained.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Is the refined structure stereochemically valid?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Ramachandran plot and ProSA-web will confirm structural quality.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Validate with RAMPAGE and ProSA-web.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Ramachandran: 90.3% favored, 8.3% allowed, 1.3% outliers; ProSA Z-score acceptable.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Enhance stability via disulfide engineering.

evidence.quote_or_span: Analysis of the improved model using the Ramachandran plot indicated that 90.3% of amino acids were situated in the favored regions, 8.3% were in the allowed regions, and only 1.3% were in the outlier regions
Ramachandran Plot was generated to assess quality of the vaccine construct, revealing that 90.3% of amino acids residues in favored regions.
The Ramachandran plot values provided evidence of substantial improvement and desired characteristics in the quality and stability of the resulting vaccine constructs

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Validated 3D structure.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can stability be further improved?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Introducing disulfide bonds at specific residue pairs will enhance stability.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Use Design v2.0/DbD2 to identify and introduce disulfide bonds.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Three pairs selected: PRO74-HIS78, GLY223-GLN245, PHE246-ALA264.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Dock stabilized MEV with TLR4.

evidence.quote_or_span: By creating disulfide connections between cysteine residues, disulfide engineering seeks to improve the stability of a protein's three-dimensional structure

Subsequent evaluation based on chi3 and B-factor energy parameters narrowed down the selection to three specific pairs: PRO74-HIS78, GLY223-GLN245, and PHE246-ALA264.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Stabilized MEV structure.

evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Analyze stability of MEV-TLR4 complex via normal mode analysis.

evidence.quote_or_span: To assess how well your MEV could bind to human immunological receptors, molecular docking research was conducted

evolution_trajectory.state_before: MEV-TLR4 docked complex.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Is the complex dynamically stable?

evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform normal mode analysis using iMODS.

evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Simulate immune response to MEV.

evidence.quote_or_span: In my research, the iMODS server was employed for this purpose

MD simulation of the docked complex of MEV, (A) deformability; (B) B-factor; (C) covariance index; (D) elastic network analysis; (E) eigenvalue.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Stable MEV-TLR4 complex predicted.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Will MEV elicit a robust immune response in silico?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: C-ImmSim simulation will show strong primary and secondary responses with memory development.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Simulate immune response using C-ImmSim.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Effective immune response observed; memory B and T cells developed.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Assess population coverage of selected epitopes.

evidence.quote_or_span: Through utilization of C-ImmSim, investigators have the capability to computationally simulate and forecast the immune reactions elicited by the MEV construct
the immunization produced an effective immune response, which resulted in the pathogen being eliminated on consecutive visits.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Immune simulation successful.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: What is the global population coverage of the selected epitopes?

evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform population coverage analysis.

evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Optimize codons for expression.

evidence.quote_or_span: Statistical analysis reveal that the shared coverage of 90.00% worldwide for chosen epitopes

the selected epitopes covered around 90% of the world's population combined.
Interestingly, the selected epitopes covered around 90% of the world's population combined.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Population coverage confirmed.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can the MEV be expressed in E. coli?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Codon optimization for E. coli K12 will enable high expression.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform codon optimization and in silico cloning.

evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Acknowledge need for experimental validation.

evidence.quote_or_span: The Codon Adaptation Index (CAI) was calculated to be 0.9, indicating a high level of alignment, and the GC content of the optimized DNA was determined to be 49.32%.
the resultant clone's overall size of 5788 base pairs

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Computational design complete.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Computational predictions require experimental validation.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Note limitations and future work.

evidence.quote_or_span: It is crucial to emphasize that despite these computational validations, experimental research in the laboratory is imperative to demonstrate the immunogenicity and protective efficacy of the developed vaccine.

success_verification.final_conclusion: The computationally designed MEV shows promising in silico immunogenicity, stability, and population coverage, but requires experimental validation.

source.title: Computational design and engineering of self-assembling multivalent microproteins with therapeutic potential against SARS-CoV-2

initial_request.research_problem: Need for broad-spectrum antivirals against evolving SARS-CoV-2 variants that evade existing monovalent drugs.
initial_request.known_context: SARS-CoV-2 mutations reduce efficacy of existing drugs and limit conserved epitopes; Omicron variants predominantly adopt 1-RBD-up conformation, evading many antibodies.
constraints: Design must achieve multivalent binding to all three RBDs
Constructs must self-assemble into stable trimers
Linkers must span calculated minimum distances
input_data: Structures of miniACE2, F-scaffold (T4 fibritin foldon), C-scaffold (coiled-coil)
quantifiable_goals: Achieve sub-nanomolar binding affinity (KD < 1 nM)
Broad neutralization of Omicron variants including cluster 2
Induce 3-RBD-up conformation

source.title: AI-based prediction of protein–ligand binding affinity and discovery of potential natural product inhibitors against ERK2

initial_request.research_problem: Accurate and efficient protein–ligand binding affinity (PLA) prediction is needed to accelerate drug discovery, but existing methods have limitations: sequence-based methods ignore receptor-ligand interactions, structure-based methods lack explicit physicochemical descriptions and are computationally expensive (e.g., 3D voxelization).
initial_request.known_context: PLA determination is time-consuming and costly experimentally; deep learning can help but needs comprehensive representation.
input_data: in-house library of 851 drug-like natural products
quantifiable_goals: Improve PLA prediction accuracy over existing methods
Identify potential ERK2 inhibitors from natural products

evolution_trajectory.state_before: PLA determination is time-consuming and costly experimentally.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to leverage deep learning to improve PLA prediction efficiency and accuracy.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Explore deep learning methods for PLA prediction.
evolution_trajectory.observation: PLA determination is time-consuming and costly; deep learning has potential to help.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Motivates investigation of existing DL methods and their limitations.

evidence.quote_or_span: Determination of protein–ligand binding affinity (PLA) is a key technological tool in hit discovery and lead optimization, which is critical to the drug development process. PLA can be determined directly by experimental methods, but it is time-consuming and costly.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Existing DL methods for PLA prediction have limitations: sequence-based methods ignore receptor-ligand interactions, structure-based methods lack explicit physicochemical descriptions and are computationally expensive due to 3D voxelization.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to integrate complementary information from ligand, pocket, and interactions to improve PLA prediction accuracy and efficiency.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Integrating ligand descriptors, pocket descriptors, and interaction graphs via early fusion can capture complementary information and improve prediction.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Propose DeepLIP, a novel DL architecture that employs an early fusion strategy for PLA prediction.
evolution_trajectory.observation: DeepLIP represents protein binding pockets and ligands as descriptors, mitigating redundancy of sequence encoding; departs from conventional voxelization by introducing spatial graphs; fuses three disparate levels of heterogeneous information.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Hypothesis leads to the design of the DeepLIP architecture.

evidence.quote_or_span: we proposed DeepLIP, a novel DL architecture that employs an early fusion strategy for PLA prediction.
DeepLIP represents protein binding pockets and ligands as descriptors, thereby mitigating the redundancy of sequence encoding.
this architecture departs from the conventional voxelization representation by introducing spatial graphs.
By fusing these three disparate levels of heterogeneous information, DeepLIP is able to capture multiple representations and combine them for complementary purposes

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Hypothesis of multi-modal fusion proposed.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to design a concrete architecture that implements the early fusion of ligand descriptors, pocket descriptors, and interaction graphs.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Using descriptor-based ligand and pocket representations and spatial graph for interactions, fused with self-attention, will effectively capture complementary information.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Design DeepLIP with descriptor-based ligand and pocket representations and spatial graph for interactions, fused early with self-attention.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Interactions represented as graphs; interaction features captured through PotentialNet; self-attention layer introduced to adaptively balance importance of each representation.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Design is implemented and trained.

evidence.quote_or_span: the interactions between the two are represented in the form of graphs
the interaction features are captured through the PotentialNet architecture
a self-attention layer is introduced to adaptively balance the importance of each representation

evolution_trajectory.state_before: DeepLIP architecture designed.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to train the model and optimize hyperparameters to achieve good performance.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Training on PDBbind v2016 refined set with SmoothL1Loss and hyperparameter optimization via Optuna will yield a robust model.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Train DeepLIP on PDBbind v2016 refined set, use SmoothL1Loss, optimize hyperparameters with Optuna, repeat 10 times with different random seeds.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Model trained; whole process repeated 10 times with different random seeds.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Training yields model evaluated on internal test set.

evidence.quote_or_span: we employed SmoothL1Loss as the loss function to train DeepLIP.
The whole process was repeated 10 times with different random seeds

After training the models on the refined set of PDBbind v2016, we directly conducted extensive experiments on the non-overlapping refined set of PDBbind v2020 and selected the best models for the subsequent benchmark test, which is significant in terms of more realistically reflecting the generalization performance and providing a fairer comparison.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: DeepLIP trained on PDBbind v2016.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to evaluate the trained model on an internal test set to select the best model.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Evaluate DeepLIP on the internal test set (PDBbind v2020 refined set) and select the best model based on PCC, MAE, RMSE.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Optimal performance on internal test set: PCC=0.698, MAE=1.112, RMSE=1.372.

evidence.quote_or_span: the optimal performance of DeepLIP on the internal test set achieved 0.698 (PCC), 1.112 (MAE), and 1.372 (RMSE)

After training the models on the refined set of PDBbind v2016, we directly conducted extensive experiments on the non-overlapping refined set of PDBbind v2020 and selected the best models for the subsequent benchmark test, which is significant in terms of more realistically reflecting the generalization performance and providing a fairer comparison.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Best DeepLIP model selected from internal test.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to compare DeepLIP against state-of-the-art methods on an external benchmark to assess generalization.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: DeepLIP will outperform existing methods on the CASF-2016 benchmark.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Evaluate DeepLIP on CASF-2016 benchmark and compare with SIGN, FAST, and other methods.
evolution_trajectory.observation: DeepLIP surpassed the state-of-the-art methods on CASF-2016.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Strong performance enables ablation studies and virtual screening validation.

evidence.quote_or_span: our proposed models surpassed the

More specifically, $20\%$ of the samples from the former were randomly selected as the validation set to optimize the hyperparameters of the pre-training models, and the latter was the benchmark dataset of CASF-2016, a scoring function evaluation platform [20].

evolution_trajectory.state_before: DeepLIP validated on external benchmark.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to confirm the contribution of each modality (ligand, pocket, interaction) to the model's performance.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Each local feature contributes positively, with pocket feature contributing the most.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Conduct ablation studies by removing each feature type (DeepLI, DeepIP, DeepLP) and evaluate on internal test set.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Removing pocket or ligand features caused substantial drop; DeepLI (no pocket) dropped to PCC=0.287, MAE=1.911, RMSE=2.357; pocket feature contributed the most.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Ablation confirms modality contributions, supporting the fusion design.

evidence.quote_or_span: removing either the pocket features or the ligand features resulted in a substantial drop in the performance of the model, while removing the interaction features significantly deteriorated the predictive robustness
DeepLI exhibited the most obvious decrease in performance, with a PCC of only 0.287, as well as high MAE and RMSE of 1.911 and 2.357
the results of the ablation studies showed that each of the local features contributed positively to the prediction of PLA, with the pocket feature contributing the most

evolution_trajectory.state_before: DeepLIP shows strong PLA prediction and modality contributions confirmed.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to validate DeepLIP's screening power on a real target (ERK2) compared to traditional docking.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: DeepLIP will outperform Autodock Vina in screening ERK2 inhibitors at various activity thresholds.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Evaluate DeepLIP on 145 ERK2 inhibitors with known activity and compare screening performance with Autodock Vina.
evolution_trajectory.observation: DeepLIP prediction performance at each threshold significantly better than Autodock Vina; overall accuracy ranging from 75.17 to 93.79%.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Screening power on known inhibitors supports application to natural product library.

evidence.quote_or_span: the prediction performance of DeepLIP at each threshold was significantly better than that of the traditional scoring function, with overall accuracy ranging from 75.17 to 93.79%
Comparison of the screening performance of DeepLIP and Autodock Vina at different ERK2 activity thresholds

To verify the application potential of the fusion model in real cases, we employed the trained model to perform PLA prediction on 145 ERK2 inhibitors with known activity (the dataset was derived from a previous study by our group), while the prediction results of Autodock Vina were used as a control to simulate the virtual screening process $[31]$ .

evolution_trajectory.decision: Screen in-house library of 851 natural products: first filter with trained ML model, then predict binding affinity with DeepLIP for 173 high-confidence molecules, select top five hits.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 173 molecules with high confidence selected; five hits with highest affinity chosen.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Top hits need validation of binding stability and affinity.

evidence.quote_or_span: Virtual screening workflow adopted in this study
we utilized the proposed DeepLIP model to perform PLA prediction on 173 molecules with high confidence and selected five hits with the highest affinity among them
we combined this model with other commonly used virtual screening tools to identify three potential ERK2 inhibitors from 851 drug-like natural products

In terms of drug discovery, we performed a first round of virtual screening of an in-house library of our group (containing 851 drug-like natural products) based on a previously trained ERK2 activity classification model.
In addition, an in-house library containing 851 drug-like natural products manually collected from the relevant literature was used in the overall workflow (Fig.
A["In-house Library (851 Natural Products)"] --> B["Trained Machine Learning Model"]

evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: MD simulation and MM/PBSA will confirm that DA, Sclareol, and Glaucine bind stably to ERK2 with higher binding free energies than previously identified actives.

the affinity of DA, Sclareol, and Glaucine for ERK2 was higher than that of the previously identified active compounds

The results of molecular dynamics simulation showed that three natural products were able to bind stably to ERK2 and had higher MM/PBSA binding free energies than our previously identified active natural products.
To further investigate the binding stability between the screened compounds and the target protein, we performed 100 ns molecular dynamics simulations.

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to acknowledge limitations of the approach and propose future work.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Limitations include reliance on binding pockets, untested large-scale screening performance, and black-box predictions.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Discuss limitations: binding pocket requirement limits applicability; large-scale virtual screening performance unevaluated; predictions are black-box.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Use of binding pockets limits application in many scenarios; performance in large-scale virtual screening needs evaluation; prediction results are a black box.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Limitations motivate future work directions.

evidence.quote_or_span: the use of binding pockets as local features limits the application of the model in many scenarios
the performance of DeepLIP in large-scale virtual screening needs to be evaluated, and only ERK2 was initially applied in this study
the prediction results are still a “black box”, and users cannot understand which residues in the interaction contribute more to the predicted binding affinity

source.title: Enzyme structure correlates with variant effect predictability

initial_request.research_problem: Variant effect prediction models have variable accuracy, and it is unclear what structural characteristics make some variants harder to predict.
initial_request.known_context: Prediction accuracy varies greatly across variants; structural characteristics may influence predictability.
constraints: Dataset must be partitionable by structural classes
Models must represent different encoding strategies
quantifiable_goals: Identify structural characteristics associated with prediction error
Compare predictability across structural classes

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Prediction accuracy varies greatly across variants, and it is unclear what characterizes variants with large prediction error.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Unknown whether structural characteristics influence predictability of variant effects.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Mutations that have large effects on structure and function are more difficult to model, and those mutations are largely defined by their structural characteristics (buriedness, contacts, active site proximity, secondary structure).
evolution_trajectory.decision: Design a framework to test structural characteristics by creating a combinatorial dataset partitionable by structural classes.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Hypothesis formulated based on known variability in prediction accuracy.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need to design a dataset and select models to test the hypothesis.

evidence.quote_or_span: prediction accuracy can vary greatly from one protein variant to the next. So far, it is unclear what characterizes variants that are associated with large prediction error.
We hypothesized that mutations that have large effects on the structure and function are more difficult to model and that those mutations are largely defined by their structural characteristics.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Hypothesis formulated but no dataset or models selected.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need a dataset that can be partitioned by structural classes and models to evaluate predictability.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A combinatorial dataset with variants sampled from structural bins will allow testing of structural influence on predictability.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Design a framework to select positions with different structural characteristics and create a balanced combinatorial dataset; select four ML models with different encoding strategies.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Framework designed to contrast variants with mutations exhibiting each characteristic vs. those that do not; models selected to represent different encoding strategies.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Create the combinatorial dataset according to the design.

evidence.quote_or_span: we designed a framework to select positions for mutation with different structural characteristics, and used these to contrast variants with mutations that exhibit those characteristics with variants harboring mutations that do not.

we compared variants with mutations that are 1) buried or exposed, 2) closely or loosely connected with other residues, 3) close to or distant from the active site and 4) part of helices/sheets or part of loops.

Four machine learning models were chosen for evaluation: two baseline models and two models representing the dominant subfields of supervised variant effect prediction, namely protein language models and alignment-based models

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Dataset design and model selection complete.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to generate a combinatorial variant library that covers structural bins.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Sampling variants from 16 structural bins with 75% ESM1v-guided and 25% uniform sampling will yield a balanced dataset.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Create a high-order combinatorial dataset of alpha-amylase variants.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 21,436 candidate variants designed; 3,706 successfully synthesized, cloned, and transformed, covering 389 of 425 residues.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Measure activity of the 3,706 variants.

evidence.quote_or_span: we created a novel high-order combinatorial dataset for an enzyme spanning 3,706 variants, that can be partitioned into subsets of variants with mutations at positions exclusively belonging to a particular structural class.

We sampled residues of 75% of the variants from protein language model (PLM) likelihood scores of ESM1v (esm1v\_t33\_650m\_ur90s\_1) in an effort to optimize the yield of the cloning pipeline, as such scores have been shown to correlate with expression [17] .
This yielded 21,436 candidate alpha-amylase variants, of which 3,706 were successfully synthesized, cloned and transformed (see subsection 4.1.
These sequences were later cloned and screened, resulting in a dataset of stain removal activity measurements of 3,706 variants with mutations covering 389 out of all 425 residues.

The remaining 25% of variants were sampled uniformly over all possible alternative residues, ensuring that some fraction of the variants are not constrained to diversity that is biased towards higher PLM likelihoods.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: 3,706 variants available.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need activity measurements to assess variant effects.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Stain removal assay will provide activity measurements for all variants.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Measure stain removal activity in triplicate; use non-normalized activity due to concentration normalization failure.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Activity values range from -0.0103 to 0.212; mean SD 0.00755. All structural classes contain some variants with higher activity than reference.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Analyze effect distributions across structural classes.

evidence.quote_or_span: all classes contain some variants with higher activity than the reference enzyme, which is indicated by the dashed line.
This resulted in activity values ranging between $-1.03 \times 10^{-2}$ and $2.
Enzyme solutions were screened for stain removal in triplicate on 96-well plates that were prepared with swatches of fabric with starch-based stains (Rice Starch, CS28 https://www.
3,706 of the variants matched the intended designs and were picked and grown in 96-well format.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Activity data collected.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Whether structural characteristics predict effect magnitude.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Structural characteristics may be weak predictors of effect magnitude.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Analyze activity distributions across structural classes.
evolution_trajectory.observation: All structural classes contain beneficial variants; structural characteristics are weak predictors of effect magnitude. Shift focus to predictability.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Train models on structurally partitioned subsets to assess predictability.

evidence.quote_or_span: the structural characteristics of mutations are weak predictors of their effects, and that using such characteristics to limit the scope of positions to mutate potentially restricts the identification of variants with improved properties.
It is therefore of interest to understand if predictability depends on these characteristics, such that we can focus model improvement on less predictable classes of variants.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Shifted focus to predictability.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Whether predictability depends on structural characteristics.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Predictability will differ across structural classes.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Train four models on structurally partitioned subsets using 10-fold cross-validation; evaluate with Spearman correlation.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Large predictability differences: buried, close-to-active-site, many-contact variants are harder to predict. Secondary structure shows no significant difference.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Check if predictability differences are model-independent.

evidence.quote_or_span: By training four different supervised variant effect prediction (VEP) models on structurally partitioned subsets of our data, we found that predictability strongly depended on all four structural characteristics we tested
Fig. 3 shows large differences in median performance between variants positive and negative for the characteristics buriedness, number of contact residues and distance to the active site.
Combinatorial variants in our dataset appear more difficult to model when their mutated sites are buried, close to the active site or in contact with many residues.
The models exhibit significantly worse prediction performance on combinatorial variants with mutations at sites that are buried, close to the active site, and in contact with many residues.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Predictability differences observed.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Whether differences are model-specific.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Predictability differences are model-independent.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Compare performance patterns across all four models.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Results almost identical across all models, indicating model-independent failure modes.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate findings on single mutation datasets.

evidence.quote_or_span: The results shown in Fig. 3 are almost identical across all models, which indicates that predictability differences are nearly independent of model type for the combinatorial dataset.
the mechanism by which mutations affect stain removal activity in less predictable variants is apparently not well accounted for by these models.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Model-independent patterns found in combinatorial dataset.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Whether patterns generalize to single mutation datasets.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Same structural dependencies exist in single mutation datasets.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Replicate analysis on SSM dataset of same enzyme, another protease, and 9 public ProteinGym datasets (≥1000 mutants, ≤600 residues).
evolution_trajectory.observation: Same structural dependencies found in most datasets, but with dataset-specific effect directions. Predictability differences highly similar across models.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Investigate opposite directions in amylase datasets.

evidence.quote_or_span: in most of the datasets, we identify the same structural dependencies we observe in the combinatorial datasets, but with different effect directions
predictability differences are highly similar across models, demonstrating that these differences are primarily driven by the assay, and less so by the models.

We selected nine additional SSM datasets of enzymes from the ProteinGym repository that measure activity. We constrained our selection to datasets that contain at least 1,000 single mutants, contain sequences no longer than 600 residues and have a corresponding reference structure that covers the mutated region.

We therefore repeat our analysis on a site saturation mutagenesis (SSM) dataset of the same enzyme with trisaccharide hydrolysis measurements $[20–22]$ , an SSM dataset of a serine protease of Cellulomonas bogoriensis (UniProt ID: A2RQE2\_9CELL) with keratin proteolysis measurements $[23]$ , and nine publicly available single mutation VEP datasets of different enzymes (S2) from the ProteinGym repository $[19]$ .

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Dataset-specific directions observed.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Why two amylase datasets show opposite predictability differences.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A moderator variable (interaction effect) determines the direction of the relationship.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Compare predictability differences between the two amylase datasets.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Opposite predictability differences in two amylase datasets involving the same enzyme, indicating a moderator variable.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Identify limitations and future directions.

evidence.quote_or_span: some pairs of datasets show opposite predictability differences. This is particularly notable in the two amylase datasets, which involve the same enzyme.

This observation indicates the presence of a moderator variable that depends on the dataset and determines the direction of the relationship between predictability and structural characteristics, a phenomenon knows as interaction in statistics

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Model-independent failures and interaction effects identified.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Current models lack mechanisms to capture less predictable variants.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: New inductive biases (e.g., physical features, physics-informed NNs) could improve performance.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Propose future directions: incorporate physical features or physics-informed neural networks.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Models make similar mistakes; different modalities and architectures with new inductive biases are needed.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Acknowledge dataset limitations and evaluation gaps.

evidence.quote_or_span: models generally make similar mistakes, suggesting that different modalities of protein variants and model architectures with new inductive biases will play important roles in further improvement of variant effect prediction.
A perfect model should therefore perform equally well on positive and negative classes of variants, which can be evaluated by explicitly using them as individual benchmarks.
This assertion is supported by our analysis, which establishes that predictability dependencies on structure depend less on model choice than on the training data at hand.

Adding explicit physical features to models, or imposing constraints directly into the model architecture with so-called physics informed neural networks, could be an approach to improve performance on less predictable classes of variants.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Dataset created and analyzed.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Limitations of existing datasets for studying structural determinants.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: This dataset is unique for stratification but smaller than some large-scale efforts.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Discuss dataset limitations: combinatorial nature hinders stratification; smaller size but covers large enzyme with high-order variants.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Majority of existing datasets are single mutation or limited to small subsets; this dataset enables stratification but is smaller than some efforts.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Highlight evaluation gaps.

evidence.quote_or_span: The majority are comprised of single mutation variants, although some datasets are combinatorial, i.e., contain variant sequences with more than one mutation.
Some protein variant datasets are limited to a small subset of residue positions

An inherent limitation of combinatorial protein variant datasets to address the research question we focused on, is that stratification by mutated positions is hindered by variants with overlapping mutated positions.
Our dataset is unique in its potential for stratification by mutation characteristic, while also being an exemplar of a typical protein engineering dataset encountered in industrial scenarios.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Predictability differences underscore extrapolation challenge.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Random holdout evaluation insufficient for extrapolation.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: New splitting strategies (e.g., modulo split, contiguous split) are needed to test extrapolation.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Propose evaluation strategies that test extrapolation beyond training data.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Central challenge is predicting fitness beyond training data; current random holdout does not address extrapolation.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Conclude with limitations and future work.

evidence.quote_or_span: One of the central challenges in protein engineering is predicting the fitness of variants that lie beyond the training data domain.
New methods in machine learning guided protein engineering are often evaluated by their rank correlation on randomly held out data, which does not satisfactorily address the capacity to extrapolate.
have proposed strategies of evaluating extrapolation by creating data splitting strategies based on the dependent variable and number of mutations $[34]$ , and recently, Notin et al.

validation_methods: Replication on single mutation datasets (same enzyme, another protease, 9 public datasets)
metrics: Spearman correlation between predicted and measured activity
success_verification.final_conclusion: Predictability of variant effects strongly depends on structural characteristics (buriedness, contacts, active site proximity) in a model-independent manner, but effect directions are dataset-specific, indicating a moderator variable. Current models lack necessary inductive biases; physical priors may improve performance.
limitations: Combinatorial dataset stratification hindered by overlapping mutations
Dataset smaller than some large-scale efforts
Random holdout evaluation insufficient for extrapolation
Concentration normalization failed, non-normalized activity used

risk_notes: All coverage requirements met; evidence IDs and quotes copied exactly.

source.title: Protein engineering using variational free energy approximation

initial_request.research_problem: Protein engineering is limited by the complexity of exploring vast design spaces and the difficulty of generating thermodynamically stable variants. Existing generative models often produce non-functional proteins because they cannot condition generation on thermodynamic stability.
initial_request.known_context: Generative models for protein design typically focus on sequence diversity but neglect thermodynamic stability, leading to many non-functional variants.
constraints: Variants must be thermodynamically stable
Variants must retain enzymatic function
Mutations should not disrupt binding sites
quantifiable_goals: Generate functional EcNAGK variants with improved or maintained stability
Achieve high experimental viability and functionality rates

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Protein engineering requires exploring a vast design space and ensuring thermodynamic stability, which is challenging. Existing generative models often produce non-functional variants because they do not condition on stability.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to generate protein variants that are both diverse and thermodynamically stable?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A variational autoencoder that jointly models sequence and free energy can learn a latent representation that captures the sequence-to-stability relationship, enabling the generation of stable variants.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Develop a model that extends VAE to two observed variables (sequence and ΔG) with separate decoders, trained by maximizing ELBO with reconstruction terms for both.
evolution_trajectory.observation: The PREVENT model is designed to allow controlled generation of variants with minimal free energy and prediction of free energy for generated variants.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Model design requires training data, so in silico mutagenesis and free energy estimation are performed.

evidence.quote_or_span: Engineering proteins is a challenging task requiring the exploration of a vast design space.
most models do not generate thermodynamically stable proteins, thus leading to many non-functional variants.

we developed the PRotein Engineering by Variational frEe eNergy approximation (PREVENT) model, which allows both the controlled generation of variants with minimal free energy as well as the prediction of the free energy associated with generated variants for downstream experimental prioritization.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Training dataset of 117,387 variants with sequences and FOLDX ΔG values.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can the transformer VAE learn a meaningful latent representation that captures the sequence-to-free-energy relationship?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Training a transformer VAE with 128-dim latent space on the full dataset will yield low reconstruction error and high Spearman correlation between predicted and true ΔG.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Train transformer VAE on full dataset with 128-dim latent space.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Reconstruction error 114.80, RMSE 9.27, Spearman correlation 0.96 on full dataset.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: After training, latent space is analyzed to validate the learned energy landscape.

evidence.quote_or_span: the reconstruction error was 114.80 (perplexity: 0.66) for the full dataset
obtained the best results when using the full dataset (RMSE = 9.27)
96$ when training on the full dataset and only dropping to $\rho = 0.92$ when using only 25K sequences, suggesting that predicted values are a robust metric for variants ranking.

With our procedure, we generated 117,387 unique variants, with each variant harbouring up to 38 mutations ( $\approx$ 15% of the residues), and then computed the associated free energy, $\Delta G$ , using FOLDX $^{14}$ .
the smaller model with 128-dimensional latent space achieving the lowest RMSE of 6.84 across all experiments
Here we found that the vast majority (126) of the 128 components to be active, albeit to a different level, suggesting that our model maps variants to different regions of the latent space.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Latent space validated; model performance metrics known.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can model performance be improved with different hyperparameters?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A smaller model with 128-dim latent space may achieve lower RMSE while maintaining high Spearman correlation.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform hyperparameter analysis across different model sizes.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Smaller model with 128-dim latent achieved lowest RMSE 6.84, but Spearman correlation remained high (0.97-0.98) across all models. Original model used for downstream tasks.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Trained model is used to generate variants via POE and SNE strategies.

evidence.quote_or_span: the smaller model with 128-dimensional latent space achieving the lowest RMSE of 6.84 across all experiments
the Spearman correlation remained high across all models, ranging from 0.97 to 0.98

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Trained PREVENT model available.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to generate novel variants with low free energy?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Two strategies can be used: POE (prior optimization of free energy) for exploring low-energy regions, and SNE (seeded non-optimal energy ranking) for generating variants near wildtype.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Generate 2000 variants using POE and 30,000 samples for SNE, then filter duplicates and binding site mutations.
evolution_trajectory.observation: After filtering, obtained 1282 new variants from POE (66%) and 13,323 from SNE (44%).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Generated variants are analyzed for diversity and mutation patterns.

evidence.quote_or_span: We generated 2000 variants using POE and 30,000 samples for SNE
we obtained 1282 new variants (66%)

We generated 2000 variants using POE and 30,000 samples for SNE, and then removed those sequences that were i) present in the training set, ii) have mutations in the binding site, or iii) are duplicated.
we obtained 1282 new variants (66%) for POE and 13,323 variants for SNE (44%)

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Generated variant pools from POE and SNE.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How diverse are the generated variants and what are their mutation patterns?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: POE variants will be more diverse and target specific regions with non-conservative mutations, while SNE variants will be more uniform.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Analyze mutation counts, BLOSUM62 scores, and positional mutation frequencies.
evolution_trajectory.observation: POE variants more diverse (avg 7.09 mutations) than SNE (3.57). Only ~25% mutations conservative. POE targets C-terminus with non-conservative mutations; SNE mutations uniform.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Generated variants are used to design a library for experimental validation.

evidence.quote_or_span: POE variants are more diverse (see Fig. 3A) than SNE, with an average of 7.09 and 3.57 mutations respectively.

POE predominantly mutates the C-terminus harboring the ATP-binding domain of the protein, which harbours highly non conservative mutations, such as the tryptophan introduced at position 212 in place of the aspartic acid residue
SNE introduced mutations almost uniformly across the wildtype sequence

Surprisingly, we observed that only $\approx$ 25% of mutations were conservative, as measured by BLOSUM62 substitution matrix; this result suggests that PREVENT might select energetically favorable mutations regardless of evolutionary information (see Fig.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Large pools of generated variants available.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which variants should be experimentally tested to validate the approach?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Selecting top variants by free energy from POE and SNE, and comparing with sequence-only selection (highest ELBO and highest identity), will demonstrate the advantage of energy-based design.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Design a library of 40 EcNAGK variants: 10 lowest free energy from POE, 10 from SNE, plus 10 highest ELBO (SNL) and 10 highest identity (SNI) for comparison.

parameters.selection_criteria: lowest free energy (POE, SNE), highest ELBO (SNL), highest identity (SNI)

evolution_trajectory.observation: Variants in the library have an average free energy ranging from -29.8 kcal/mol for the SNE group, to -18 kcal/mol for the SNL group.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Experimental validation begins, but initial expression system causes growth penalty.

evidence.quote_or_span: we took the 10 variants with lowest free energy obtained with the POE strategy and the 10 variants with the lowest free energy generated with the SNE strategy. We further augmented this library by adding 10 variants with the highest ELBO, denoted as SNL, and 10 with the highest identity with respect to the wildtype, hereby denoted as SNI
Variants in the library have an average free energy ranging from -29.8 kcal/mol for the SNE group, to -18 kcal/mol for the SNL group

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Library of 40 variants designed for expression in E. coli ΔargB strain.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can the variants be expressed and screened in a high-throughput manner?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Plasmid-based expression of wildtype argB will complement the auxotrophy, but overexpression may cause toxicity.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Transform wildtype argB plasmid into ΔargB strain and assess growth.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Initial plasmid-based expression of wildtype argB caused severe growth penalty (>24h lag) due to overexpression.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Growth penalty is resolved by codon optimization.

evidence.quote_or_span: we observed that argB knockouts required over 24 h of incubation to emerge from lag phase
we hypothesised that the strong EcNAGK overexpression was severely penalising cell growth in minimal media

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Overexpression of wildtype argB causes growth penalty.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to reduce expression to a tolerable level?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Recoding argB using E. coli codon bias will reduce expression and improve growth.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Recode argB using E. coli codon bias and apply codon optimization to all variants.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Achieved 25-fold reduction in expression and drastic growth improvement.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Codon-optimized variants are transformed and screened.

evidence.quote_or_span: 6) $^{16}$ , we measured an average 25-fold reduction in gene expression by RT-qPCR, which was associated with a drastic improvement in growth rate using both solid and liquid minimal media (see Supplementary Fig.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Codon-optimized variant library ready for transformation.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Will the designed variants be viable and functional in complementing the argB knockout?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A high-throughput transformation and screening protocol will allow assessment of viability and functionality.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Transform 40 variants into ΔargB strain using high-throughput OT-2 robot and screen on auxotrophic media.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 7 variants failed to produce viable transformants; 33 viable, 28 functional (85% of viable).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Screening results are analyzed to compare energy-based vs sequence-only strategies.

evidence.quote_or_span: 7 EcNAGK variants (namely poe4, snl3, snl6, snl7, sni10, sne1, sne3) did not produce viable transformants
the total count of active variants to 28
85% of the designed and transformed EcNAGK variants enabled survival on auxotrophic media
In conclusion, 85% of the designed and transformed EcNAGK variants enabled survival on auxotrophic media, with 67% permitting similar recovery levels to the wild-type sequence.
To this end, we developed a robust and high-throughput heat-shock transformation protocol using an Opentrons OT-2 robot, which allowed us to reliably and reproducibly transform E.
developed a transformation protocol for the Opentrons OT-2 robot.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Experimental screening results available.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Do energy-based strategies yield more diverse functional variants than sequence-only strategies?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Energy-based strategies (POE+SNE) will yield functional variants with more mutations than sequence-only (SNI+SNL), and ELBO prioritization (SNL) will perform worst.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Compare mutation counts and functionality across groups; perform growth assays.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Energy-based strategies (POE+SNE) yielded 14 functional variants with avg 5.64 mutations vs 1.64 for sequence-only (SNI+SNL). ELBO prioritization (SNL) performed worst.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Computational structural analysis validates that functional variants maintain wildtype-like structure.

evidence.quote_or_span: functional variants designed using free energy information (POE, SNE) harbour, on average, significantly more mutations (5.64) compared to the 1.64 mutations found on variants selected by sequence only information

177, $p$ -value = 3.151 × 10 $^{-5}$ ), suggesting that exploiting free energy information could yield more diverse library with minimal impact on the overall number of functional proteins obtained in a screen.

14 out of the 28 viable variants were obtained by using either POE (6 variants) or SNE (8 variants); as expected, since they carry only 1 mutation, 9 out of 10 SNI variants recovered the phenotype, whereas only 5 out of the 10 SNL variants were functional.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Functional variants identified, but structural integrity unknown.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Do the functional variants maintain the wildtype fold and stability?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Computational structural analysis (ESMFOLD, OPENMM, eNMA, ESM-IF) will confirm that variants closely resemble wildtype structure.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Predict structures, perform energy minimization, normal mode analysis, and inverse folding evaluation.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Variants closely resemble wildtype structure (avg pLDDT 89.64, avg RMSD 1.70Å) and are consistent with wildtype fold, though slightly less stable (wildtype log-likelihood: -0.991; average variants log-likelihood: -1.069).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Limitations of the approach are discussed based on experimental and computational findings.

evidence.quote_or_span: The predicted structures have an average pLDDT of 89.64
closely resembling the structure of the wildtype enzyme (average RMSD: 1.70Å)
the average inverse folding score of -0.077
wildtype log-likelihood: -0.991; average variants log-likelihood: -1.069

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Experimental and computational validation completed.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: What are the limitations of the PREVENT approach?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: FOLDX accuracy may be limited for large proteins or unfolded states, and model explainability remains an open challenge.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Discuss limitations regarding FOLDX reliability and model explainability.
evolution_trajectory.observation: FOLDX accuracy is limited for large proteins or unfolded states; relative free energy changes (ΔΔG) might be more robust. Model explainability not addressed.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: These limitations inform future work.

evidence.quote_or_span: FOLDX, a well-known and validated tool, which has been shown to be reliable mostly for small and medium size proteins, mostly due to the intrinsic difficulty of modeling proteins in unfolded state
identifying the best framework to make our model explainable will require further investigation.

validation_methods: Experimental complementation assay in E. coli ΔargB
Computational structural analysis (ESMFOLD, OPENMM, eNMA, ESM-IF)
metrics: Average mutations in functional energy-based variants: 5.64 vs 1.64 for sequence-only
Structural similarity: avg pLDDT 89.64, avg RMSD 1.70Å
success_verification.final_conclusion: PREVENT successfully generates functional EcNAGK variants with higher diversity when using free energy information, while maintaining wildtype-like structure. Energy-based strategies outperform sequence-only methods in yielding diverse functional variants.
limitations: FOLDX accuracy limited for large proteins or unfolded states; relative free energy changes (ΔΔG) might be more robust.
Model explainability not addressed.

source.title: Machine learning optimization of candidate antibody yields highly diverse sub-nanomolar affinity antibody libraries

initial_request.research_problem: Developing a machine learning approach to optimize antibody single-chain variable fragments (scFvs) for high affinity and diversity, outperforming conventional directed evolution.
initial_request.known_context: Candidate antibodies from phage display are often weak binders; the vast search space of antibody sequences makes exhaustive evaluation infeasible.
constraints: Cost limitations restrict training data to k=1,2,3 random mutations.
input_data: Training data: random k=1,2,3 mutations in CDRs of candidate scFv Ab-14, with high-throughput binding quantification via yeast mating assay.
quantifiable_goals: Engineer scFvs with significantly stronger binding than directed evolution.
Achieve high diversity and success rate in designed libraries.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Candidate antibodies from phage display are often weak binders; exhaustive evaluation of the vast antibody sequence space is infeasible.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to efficiently engineer scFvs with stronger binding than typical directed evolution approaches.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Integrating target-specific binding affinities with information from millions of natural protein sequences in a probabilistic machine learning framework can rapidly engineer scFvs that are significantly stronger binders than what typical directed evolution approaches would produce.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Develop a five-step process combining language models, Bayesian optimization, and high-throughput experimentation.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Hypothesis formulated based on the potential of ML to leverage natural sequence information and binding data.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Proceed to design the five-step process.

evidence.quote_or_span: the vast search space of antibody sequences renders exhaustive evaluation of the entire antibody space infeasible
the resultant candidate antibodies are often weak binders or suffer from developability issues

We hypothesized that by integrating target-specific binding affinities with information from millions of natural protein sequences in a probabilistic machine learning framework, we could rapidly engineer scFvs that are significantly stronger binders than what typical directed evolution approaches would produce.
we developed a five-step process that uniquely combines state-of-art language models, Bayesian optimization and high-throughput experimentation to generate high-affinity scFv libraries

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Training data available; need sequence representations.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to effectively represent antibody sequences for affinity prediction.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Pre-trained BERT masked language models on large protein sequence databases (Pfam, OAS) can capture useful features for downstream affinity prediction.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Pre-train four BERT masked language models: a protein language model, an antibody heavy chain model, an antibody light chain model, and a paired heavy-light chain model.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Four pre-trained language models obtained.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Build sequence-to-affinity models using these representations.

evidence.quote_or_span: We pre-trained four BERT masked language models, i.e., a protein language model, an antibody heavy chain model, an antibody light chain model and a paired heavy-light chain model.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Language models provide sequence representations.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to build sequence-to-affinity models with uncertainty quantification.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Ensemble and Gaussian Process (GP) models can predict binding affinities with uncertainty, enabling Bayesian optimization.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Investigate two approaches for affinity prediction with uncertainty: an ensemble method and Gaussian Process (GP).
evolution_trajectory.observation: Two sequence-to-affinity models with uncertainty quantification obtained.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Construct Bayesian fitness landscape using these models.

evidence.quote_or_span: we investigated two approaches to predict affinities with uncertainty quantification: an ensemble method and Gaussian Process (GP)

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Sequence-to-affinity models with uncertainty available.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to efficiently search the vast sequence space for improved binders.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A Bayesian fitness landscape mapping sequences to posterior probability of improved binding, combined with sampling strategies (hill climb, genetic algorithm, Gibbs), can identify high-affinity variants.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Construct Bayesian fitness landscape and apply three sampling strategies: hill climb (HC), genetic algorithm (GA), and Gibbs sampling.

tool_or_method.name: Bayesian fitness landscape and sampling strategies

evolution_trajectory.observation: Fitness landscape constructed; optimization performed.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Generate libraries for experimental validation.

evidence.quote_or_span: a Bayesian-based fitness landscape was constructed to map the entire scFv sequence to a posterior probability, i.e., the probability that the estimated binding affinity is better than the candidate scFv Ab-14
Three strategies were used: hill climb (HC), genetic algorithm (GA) and Gibbs sampling.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Optimized sequences identified in silico.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to experimentally validate the designed sequences.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: The top 6000 sequences per library, when synthesized and tested via yeast mating assay, will show improved binding over baseline.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Construct seven scFv libraries per chain: six from ML fitness functions (three ensemble, three GP) and one PSSM control; select top 6000 sequences per library for experimental testing.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Libraries constructed and binding affinities measured.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Analyze performance of ML designs vs. training data and PSSM.

evidence.quote_or_span: This resulted in seven scFv libraries per chain: three libraries from optimizing the ensemble-based fitness function (namely, En-HC, En-GA and En-Gibbs), three libraries from optimizing the GP-based fitness function (namely, GP-HC, GP-GA, GP-Gibbs), and one PSSM library
For each scFv chain (Ab-14-H variants and Ab-14-L variants), we constructed two fitness functions extrapolated from the ensemble and GP model, respectively.
For each fitness function, we performed optimization using three sampling strategies.

This resulted in 6 libraries per chain: 3 libraries from optimizing the ensemble-based fitness function (namely, En-HC, En-GA and En-Gibbs), and 3 libraries from optimizing the GP-based fitness function (namely, GP-HC, GP-GA, GP-Gibbs).
We then rank-ordered the generated sequences based on their fitness score per library and selected the top 6000 sequences per library for experimental validation.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Experimental binding data collected for all libraries.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Do ML-designed libraries outperform the training data and conventional methods?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: ML designs, especially ensemble-based, will have a higher fraction of variants surpassing the best training binder compared to PSSM.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Compare the fraction of designs with stronger binding than the best training binder across libraries.
evolution_trajectory.observation: >25% of ensemble-based Ab-14-H designs beat the best training binder vs. 0.9% for PSSM.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Evaluate success rates of different libraries.

evidence.quote_or_span: more than 25% of ensemble-based Ab-14-H variant designs have stronger measured binding affinities than the strongest measured binder in the training data, whereas only 0.9% of PSSM-based Ab-14-H variant designs outperform the strongest measured binder in the training data.

Notably, more than 25% of ensemble-based Ab-14-H variant designs have stronger measured binding affinities than the strongest measured binder in the training data, whereas only 0.9% of PSSM-based Ab-14-H variant designs outperform the strongest measured binder in the training data.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Overall improvement over training data confirmed.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How do different ML libraries compare in terms of success rate (fraction of improved binders)?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Ensemble-based libraries with HC and GA sampling will have high success rates, while Gibbs may underperform.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Calculate percent success (fraction of designs with improved binding over Ab-14) for each library.
evolution_trajectory.observation: En-HC: 94.3%, En-GA: 96% success; En-Gibbs heavy-chain: 12% success; PSSM: 23.8%.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Investigate diversity to explain the low success of En-Gibbs.

evidence.quote_or_span: The En-HC (94.3%) and En-GA (96%) libraries are particularly successful and outperform all GP-generated Ab-14H variant libraries (59.4–84.2%).
3%) and En-GA (96%) libraries are particularly successful and outperform all GP-generated Ab-14H variant libraries (59.4–84.

The En-HC (94.3%) and En-GA (96%) libraries are particularly successful and outperform all GP-generated Ab-14H variant libraries (59.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Success rates vary; En-Gibbs heavy-chain has low success.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Why does En-Gibbs heavy-chain have low success despite being ML-designed?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: En-Gibbs explores higher mutational distance (diversity) but at the cost of reduced affinity, indicating a diversity-affinity trade-off.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Analyze mutational distances (d_avg^Ab-14 and pairwise) for all libraries.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Ensemble-based libraries have d_avg^Ab-14 7.9–15.6 (En-Gibbs: 15.6) vs. GP 3.4–3.7 and PSSM 3.1; En-Gibbs sequences are on average 15.6 mutations from Ab-14-H and 14.9 from each other, but this high diversity reduces affinity.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Examine model extrapolation to understand why GP libraries have lower diversity.

evidence.quote_or_span: sequences in the En-Gibbs library are on average 15.6 distance away from Ab-14-H and 14.9 distance away from each other
this increase in mutational distance comes at the cost of reduced affinity
all ML-optimized libraries have higher $d_{avg}^{Ab-14}$ than the PSSM library (with $d_{avg}^{Ab-14}=3.1$ )
The ensemble-based libraries also have significantly higher $d_{avg}^{Ab-14}$ (7.9–15.6) than the GP-based libraries (3.4–3.7)

evolution_trajectory.state_before: GP libraries show lower diversity and success than ensemble.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Why does the GP model fail to extrapolate to higher-order mutants?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: GP model has poor extrapolation for sequences with many mutations, as indicated by a sharp increase in MAE.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Evaluate GP model MAE as a function of mutational distance from Ab-14.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Sharp increase in MAE for heavy-chain GP model at ≥6 mutations, and for light-chain GP model at ≥10 mutations.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Compare with ensemble model's extrapolation performance.

evidence.quote_or_span: we observed a sharp increase in MAE on sequences with six or more mutations away from Ab-14-H for the heavy-chain GP model, and on sequences with ten or more mutations away from Ab-14-L for the light-chain GP model

evolution_trajectory.state_before: GP model fails at high mutational distances.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Does the ensemble model generalize better?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Ensemble model maintains low MAE across mutational distances and shows better correlation with measured affinities on designed variants.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Evaluate ensemble model's Spearman correlation on designed Ab-14-H variants and MAE vs. mutational distance.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Ensemble model Spearman correlation 0.69 vs. GP -0.42 on designed heavy-chain variants; ensemble shows no notable increase in MAE with mutational distance.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Assess impact of sampling algorithm choice given the superior ensemble model.

evidence.quote_or_span: the heavy-chain ensemble model has a Spearman correlation of 0.69 but the heavy-chain GP model performs significantly worse (-0.42)
Ensemble models exhibit no notable increase in MAE as the mutational distance increases, indicating that the ensemble approach is more generalizable to higher-order mutants than the GP model

The most notable difference is when evaluating on designed Ab-14-H variants, where the heavy-chain ensemble model has a Spearman correlation of 0.69 but the heavy-chain GP model performs significantly worse (-0.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Ensemble model is superior; sampling strategies yield different outcomes.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How does the choice of sampling algorithm affect the quality of designed libraries when using the ensemble fitness landscape?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: HC and GA yield high success and large affinity improvements, while Gibbs yields high diversity but low success.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Compare fold improvement over PSSM and success rates for En-HC, En-GA, and En-Gibbs heavy-chain libraries.
evolution_trajectory.observation: En-HC: 28.7-fold improvement, 94.3% success; En-GA: 27.9-fold, 96% success; En-Gibbs: 2.9-fold improvement.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate the in silico performance metric for predicting library success.

evidence.quote_or_span: hill climb and genetic algorithms found scFvs with significant (28.7 and 27.9-fold, respectively) increases in binding over the best PSSM-sampled scFv
both methods were highly successful (94.3% and 96% success, respectively)
when combined with the Gibbs sampling algorithm, the best scFv sampled was only 2.9-fold better
When using the ensemble-extrapolated fitness landscape to engineer 14-Ab-H, hill climb and genetic algorithms found scFvs with significant (28.7 and 27.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Experimental results show varying success; need a predictive metric.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can an in silico metric predict the actual success of designed libraries?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: An in silico performance metric (estimated percent of success) will correlate with actual percent success on hold-out test data.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Compute in silico metric for each library and compare rankings with actual success rankings.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Predicted rankings match actual rankings with rank correlation 0.8.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Acknowledge limitations and broader implications.

evidence.quote_or_span: the predicted rankings match well to the actual rankings with a rank correlation of 0.8

validation_methods: Experimental validation via yeast mating assay
In silico performance metric correlation with actual success (rank correlation 0.8)
metrics: Percent success (fraction of designs with improved binding over Ab-14)
Fold improvement over best PSSM binder
Spearman correlation between predicted and measured affinities
success_verification.final_conclusion: The ML-driven approach, particularly using ensemble models with hill climb or genetic algorithm sampling, successfully engineers highly diverse scFv libraries with sub-nanomolar affinity, significantly outperforming directed evolution (PSSM). The best design achieved a 28.7-fold improvement over PSSM with 94.3% success rate. However, a diversity-affinity trade-off exists, and GP models fail to extrapolate to high-order mutants.
limitations: Cost limitations restricted training data to k=1,2,3 random mutations.
Diversity-affinity trade-off: high diversity (e.g., En-Gibbs) can reduce affinity.
GP model shows poor extrapolation for sequences with ≥6 (heavy) or ≥10 (light) mutations.

source.title: Lactate biosensors for spectrally and spatially multiplexed fluorescence imaging

initial_request.research_problem: Develop improved green extracellular and red intracellular L-lactate biosensors with higher ΔF/F, faster kinetics, and better localization for multiplexed imaging.
initial_request.known_context: eLACCO1.1 suffers from aggregation and limited fluorescence response on mammalian cell surface; no red fluorescent biosensors for intracellular L-lactate exist.
constraints: Must be genetically encoded
Must enable spectrally and spatially multiplexed imaging
quantifiable_goals: Higher ΔF/F than eLACCO1.1
Faster kinetics
Better surface localization
Red intracellular sensor with high ΔF/F

evolution_trajectory.state_before: eLACCO1.1 aggregates and has limited fluorescence response on mammalian cell surface; no red intracellular L-lactate biosensor exists.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need improved green extracellular sensor and a new red intracellular sensor for multiplexed imaging.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A spectrally and functionally orthogonal pair of green extracellular and red intracellular biosensors can be developed.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Design orthogonal pair: green extracellular eLACCO2.1 and red intracellular R-iLACCO1. Select eLACCO0.9 as starting point for green sensor evolution due to faster kinetics than eLACCO1.
evolution_trajectory.observation: eLACCO1.1 aggregation and limited response; no red intracellular sensor reported. eLACCO0.9 identified as promising candidate.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Proceed to directed evolution of eLACCO0.9.

evidence.quote_or_span: the first-in-class eLACCO1.1 suffers from aggregation and limited fluorescence response on the surface of mammalian cells
no red fluorescent biosensors for intracellular L-lactate have yet been reported, hindering imaging with other green fluorescent biosensors in spatially and spectrally multiplexed manner

a spectrally and functionally orthogonal pair of high-performance genetically encoded biosensors: the second-generation green fluorescent eLACCO2.1 for extracellular L-lactate and the first-generation red fluorescent R-iLACCO1 for intracellular L-lactate
we first identified eLACCO0.9 as a promising candidate due to a faster fluorescence response than eLACCO1

evolution_trajectory.state_before: eLACCO0.9 selected as starting point.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to improve ΔF/F and other properties.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Directed evolution can yield variants with higher ΔF/F.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform directed evolution: 8 rounds of random mutagenesis on eLACCO0.9 to produce eLACCO2 with ΔF/F 16.
evolution_trajectory.observation: eLACCO2 achieved ΔF/F of 16.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Tune affinity of eLACCO2.

evidence.quote_or_span: Leu79Ile mutation was added to eLACCO2 to tune the L-lactate affinity. The resulting mutant was designated as eLACCO2.1.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: eLACCO2 with ΔF/F 16.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to tune affinity for physiological lactate range.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Leu79Ile mutation will adjust Kd.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Introduce Leu79Ile into eLACCO2 to produce eLACCO2.1 (Kd 1.9 mM, ΔF/F 14).
evolution_trajectory.observation: eLACCO2.1 has Kd 1.9 mM and ΔF/F 14.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Optimize cell surface localization.

evidence.quote_or_span: Leu79Ile mutation was added to eLACCO2 to tune the L-lactate affinity. The resulting mutant was designated as eLACCO2.1.
Finally, Leu79Ile mutation was added to eLACCO2 to tune the L-lactate affinity.
