source.title: Polymerase-guided base editing enables in vivo mutagenesis and rapid protein engineering

initial_request.research_problem: Existing in vivo mutagenesis systems have limited mutation windows, require multiple gRNAs, inhibit mutation at binding sites, and have fixed mutation biases. The goal is to develop a targeted, continual, inducible in vivo mutagenesis platform with high mutation rates, broad windows, tunable spectra, and minimal off-target effects.
initial_request.known_context: CRISPR-based targeting systems (CRISPR-X, EvolvR) have narrow mutation windows (~40 bp), inhibit mutation at gRNA binding sites, and have fixed mutation bias. TRACE lacks chemically inducible tunability and has low diversity biased to C/G transitions; Moore et al. and Álvarez et al. systems have significant off-target mutation frequency.
constraints: Targeted mutagenesis
Inducible control
Minimal off-target effects
Broad mutation window
Tunable mutation spectrum
quantifiable_goals: High on:off-target mutation ratio
Broad mutation window (up to 2 kb)
Tunable mutation rate over 100-fold range
Increased mutational diversity beyond C>T and G>A

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Existing in vivo mutagenesis systems (CRISPR-X, EvolvR, TRACE) have limitations: narrow mutation windows (~40 bp), inhibition at gRNA binding sites, fixed mutation bias, lack of tunability, and significant off-target effects.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to achieve targeted, continual, inducible in vivo mutagenesis with high mutation rates, broad windows, tunable spectra, and minimal off-target effects.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A system mimicking B-cell somatic hypermutation (SHM) by targeting mutagenic enzymes via T7 RNAP could overcome these limitations.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Design TRIDENT platform with cytosine deaminase (PmCDA1), engineered adenosine deaminase (yeTadA1.0), and DNA repair factors (Apn2p, Msh6p) targeted via T7 RNAP.
evolution_trajectory.observation: TRIDENT is designed to consist of up to three components: cytosine deaminase, adenosine deaminase, and DNA-repair factors, targeted to a locus via T7 RNAP, inspired by SHM.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Test initial implementation of TRIDENT with PmCDA1-T7 RNAP fusion.

evidence.quote_or_span: mutations are limited to an approximately 40 bp window adjacent to the gRNA target site, mutation of the 20 bp gRNA binding site is inhibited, and mutagenesis is focused to just two or three base pairs

effective diversification of a 1000 bp gene can require more than 20 gRNAs, requiring redesign for each new target and inhibiting mutation at over 400 bp of sequence corresponding to gRNA binding sites

Moore et al. and Álvarez et al. also developed a cytidine deaminase T7 RNA Polymerase targeting system in E. coli, but did not utilize DNA-modifiers to increase mutation diversity and both systems, while more targeted than chemical mutagenesis, still retained significant off-target mutation frequency

TRIDENT consists of up to three components targeted to a locus of interest via T7 RNA polymerase: (1) a cytosine deaminase enzyme that introduces C>T and G>A substitutions, (2) an engineered adenosine deaminase enzyme that introduces A>G and T>C substitutions, and (3) DNA-repair factors that further enhance A/T/G mutational diversity

evolution_trajectory.state_before: TRIDENT design with PmCDA1-T7 RNAP fusion expressed from plasmid.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Will plasmid-based expression of PmCDA1-T7 RNAP achieve high on-target mutagenesis with low off-target effects?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Plasmid-based expression will provide sufficient targeting and low off-target mutagenesis.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Test plasmid-based expression of PmCDA1-T7 RNAP in YHM0.P_T7 KO strain.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Plasmid-based expression increased off-target mutagenesis by more than 2500-fold above background, resulting in a net on:off-target ratio of only 60-fold.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Optimize expression to reduce off-target effects.

evidence.quote_or_span: Plasmid-based expression of PmCDA1-T7 RNAP increased off-target mutagenesis by more than 2500-fold above the background rate, resulting in a net on:off-target ratio of 60-fold

evolution_trajectory.state_before: High off-target mutagenesis from plasmid-based expression.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to reduce off-target mutagenesis while maintaining on-target activity?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Genomic integration and inducible promoter control will reduce off-target effects and enable tunability.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Integrate PmCDA1-T7 RNAP expression cassette into genome and use β-estradiol inducible promoter.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Achieved up to 1800-fold on:off-target ratio and tunable mutation rate over 100-fold range (LOF rate from 2.5×10^-5 to 2.7×10^-3).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Characterize mutation window and rate of optimized system.

evidence.quote_or_span: The combined strategy of controlling the expression of PmCDA1-T7 RNAP from a synthetic, inducible promoter $^{17}$ and integrating the expression cassette for the fusion protein into the genome of the base strain YHM0 (resulting in YHM1) resulted in the highest on:off-target ratio of up to 1800-fold when induced with 2 nM $\beta$ -estradiol inducer for 16 h (Fig.
The improved system further enabled titratable control of on-target mutation rate over a 100-fold range from a LOF rate of $2.5 \times 10^{-5}$ to $2.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Optimized TRIDENT strain YHM1 with PmCDA1.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: What are the mutation window and rate characteristics of TRIDENT with PmCDA1?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: TRIDENT will achieve precise initiation and constant sustained mutation rate across a broad window.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Characterize mutation window and rate using URA3 reporter with P_T7 promoter.
evolution_trajectory.observation: TRIDENT with PmCDA1 achieves precise initiation of mutation and constant sustained mutation rate across a target region, with mutation window up to 2 kb; first increase at cytosines 9–12 bases from initiation site.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Assess mutation spectrum bias.

evidence.quote_or_span: TRIDENT with PmCDA1 achieves several important characteristics of engineered in vivo mutagenesis, including precise initiation of mutation and constant sustained mutation rate across a target region.
the first increase in mutation rate occurring at cytosines that are 9–12 bases from the RNA transcription initiation site of T7 RNAP

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Characterized mutation window and rate.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: What is the mutation spectrum of TRIDENT with PmCDA1?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: The spectrum may be biased towards C>T and G>A substitutions.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Analyze mutation spectrum from sequencing data.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Over 99% of induced mutations are C>T and G>A substitutions.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Expand platform to enhance mutational diversity by targeting DNA-repair factors.

evidence.quote_or_span: the overall mutation spectrum remained biased as over 99% of induced mutations are C>T and G>A substitutions

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Error-prone library of TadA domains fused to T7 RNAP.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Will directed evolution yield variants with increased TAA reversion rate?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Multiple rounds of evolution and selection will enrich for variants with higher A/T mutagenesis.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform five rounds of evolution and selection, measuring TAA reversion rate.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Substantial increase in TAA-reverting colonies after five rounds; engineered yeTadA1.0-T7 RNAP fusion exhibited 270-fold increase in TAA-reversion rate compared to parental TadA-TadA*-T7 RNAP.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate on/off-target mutation rates of strains with yeTadA1.0.

evidence.quote_or_span: We observed a substantial increase in the number of TAA reverting colonies on media lacking uracil after five rounds of evolution (Supplementary Fig. 12).
0-T7 RNAP fusion (pCS4333) exhibited a 270-fold increase in TAA-reversion rate compared with the TadA-TadA\*-T7 RNAP fusion (pCS4332).

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Engineered yeTadA1.0 strains YHM5 and YHM6.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Do YHM5 and YHM6 retain low off-target mutation rates and minimal fitness impact?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: The strains will have similar on/off-target rates to optimized YHM2 and no significant growth defect.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Measure on/off-target mutation rates and growth rates of YHM5 and YHM6 compared to YHM2.
evolution_trajectory.observation: YHM5 and YHM6 retained similar on/off-target mutation rates to YHM2; no statistically significant difference in growth rate (p < 0.05) up to 100 nM β-estradiol.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Apply TRIDENT to correct a TAA stop codon in mCherry.

evidence.quote_or_span: We also confirmed that the most advanced TRIDENT strains, YHM5 and YHM6, retained similar on and off-target mutation rates to YHM2, which had been optimized for lower off-target mutagenesis (Supplementary Fig. 13).
There was no statistically significant difference (p < 0.05) in growth rate for any level of β-estradiol induction up to 100 nM (Supplementary Table 3).

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Validated TRIDENT strain YHM6.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can TRIDENT correct a TAA stop codon in mCherry and recover fluorescence?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Induced mutagenesis will revert the stop codon, yielding mCherry-positive cells detectable by FACS.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Grow YHM6.mCherry.TAA with 10 nM β-estradiol for 16 h and sort mCherry-positive cells by FACS.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 0.14% mCherry-positive cells after one sort.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Evolve red-shifted mCherry variant.

evidence.quote_or_span: Strain YHM6.mCherry.TAA was grown with 10 nM β-estradiol for 16 h and mCherry-positive cells were sorted once yielding a 0.14% mCherry-positive population.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: mCherry-positive population from stop codon reversion.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can TRIDENT evolve a red-shifted mCherry variant?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Sorting for red-shifted fluorescence will enrich for variants with altered emission spectra.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Sort 1% of cells with highest 615:660 nm emission ratio over multiple rounds, then characterize isolated variant.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Evolved mCherry-F23S variant with 5 nm red-shift (max emission 615 nm vs 610 nm WT); all non-parent variants contained single F23S mutation (T>C substitution).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Apply TRIDENT to evolve pyrimethamine resistance in PfDHFR.

evidence.quote_or_span: TRIDENT-evolved mCherry variant, mCherry-F23S, is red-shifted by 5 nm.
mCherry had max emission of 610 nm and mCherry-F23S had max emission of 615 nm.
Of 12 sequenced colonies, all non-parent variants contained a single F23S mutation resulting from a T>C substitution of TTC to TCC.
The data indicate that mCherry-F23S is 5 nm red-shifted compared to wild-type mCherry $^{23}$ with an emission max of 615 nm compared to 610 nm (Fig.

From this initial library, we enriched for red-shifted variants by sorting the 1% of cells having the highest ratio of 615:660 nm emission and outgrowing for 24 h without additional mutagenesis before sorting again.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Pyrimethamine-resistant PfDHFR populations.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: What mutations confer pyrimethamine resistance?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Sequencing will reveal enriched mutations, likely D54N.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Sequence PfDHFR from resistant replicates and analyze mutation frequencies.
evolution_trajectory.observation: D54N was the most common mutation, present in >85% of sequences; D54 is within 3 Å of pyrimethamine binding pocket.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate functional impact of D54N on growth rate.

evidence.quote_or_span: the most common mutation, D54N, was present in over 85% of individual sequences.
the D54 residue is within 3 Å of the pocket where pyrimethamine binds

D54N and Y57H, have been identified in previous studies as impacting the interaction between pyrimethamine and PfDHFR $^{24,25}$ , and the most common mutation, D54N, was present in over 85% of individual sequences.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Identified D54N as dominant mutation.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Does D54N significantly improve growth rate in pyrimethamine?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: D54N will confer faster growth; other mutations may have weaker effects.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Express PfDHFR variants individually and in combination, measure growth rates in pyrimethamine.
evolution_trajectory.observation: All variants with D54N grew significantly faster than wild-type; Y57H and Q237* showed positive but not statistically significant improvement.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Note limitations of TRIDENT.

evidence.quote_or_span: All yeast with PfDHFR variants containing the D54N mutation grew significantly faster than yeast with wild-type PfDHFR.
Two other mutations (Y57H and Q237\*) appeared to have a positive impact on growth rate, but the improvement compared to wild-type was not statistically significant in the context of this experiment.
Validation experiments confirmed that the D54N mutation resulted in a faster growth rate.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Successful protein engineering demonstrations.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: What are the remaining limitations of TRIDENT?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: TRIDENT still has transition bias and potential downstream mutagenesis due to single T7 terminator.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Discuss limitations: transition bias and T7 terminator efficiency.
evolution_trajectory.observation: TRIDENT shows bias towards transition mutations; single T7 terminator may not fully prevent downstream mutagenesis (Moore et al. suggest ≥4 terminators).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Future work to address limitations.

evidence.quote_or_span: the system shows bias towards transition mutations that limitation can be addressed through further tuning of the DNA-repair modifying pathways
Moore et al. showed that four or more T7 terminators may be needed to efficiently terminate T7 RNAP transcription and prevent downstream mutagenesis

success_verification.final_conclusion: TRIDENT is a targeted, continual, inducible in vivo mutagenesis platform with high mutation rates, broad windows (up to 2 kb), tunable spectra, and minimal off-target effects. It successfully evolved a red-shifted mCherry variant and pyrimethamine-resistant PfDHFR, with D54N as the key resistance mutation.
limitations: Bias towards transition mutations
Single T7 terminator may not fully prevent downstream mutagenesis

source.title: Droplet-based microfluidic platform for high-throughput screening of Streptomyces

initial_request.research_problem: Traditional Streptomyces screening methods (microtiter plates, flow cytometry) are low-throughput or cannot screen mycelium and secreted products, limiting industrial strain engineering.
initial_request.known_context: Flow cytometry-based screening can only analyze spores or protoplasts, not filamentous mycelium or secreted products, which are critical for industrial Streptomyces cultivation.
constraints: Must screen filamentous mycelium
Must screen secreted products
High throughput required
quantifiable_goals: Enrichment ratio up to 334.2
Throughput at least 10,000 variants per hour

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Flow cytometry-based screening can only analyze spores or protoplasts, not filamentous mycelium or secreted products.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: No high-throughput method exists to screen Streptomyces in its mycelial form or to screen secreted products.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A droplet-based microfluidic platform can enable high-throughput screening of Streptomyces mycelium and secreted products, overcoming flow cytometry limitations.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Develop a droplet-based microfluidic platform for Streptomyces screening.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Hypothesis formulated based on known limitations of flow cytometry.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need to design spore preparation for droplet encapsulation.

evidence.quote_or_span: Previously reported flow cytometry-based screening methods can only screen spores or protoplasts released from mycelium, which do not represent the filamentous stationary phase Streptomyces used in industrial cultivation.
Here we show a droplet-based microfluidic platform to facilitate more relevant, reliable and rapid screening of Streptomyces mycelium

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Hypothesis formulated; need to encapsulate spores in droplets.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Spore clumping may hinder monodispersed encapsulation.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Using eight-layered sterile filter papers can obtain monodispersed spore suspension for droplet encapsulation.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Optimize spore preparation using eight-layered sterile filter papers.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Eight-layered sterile filter papers performed the best for obtaining monodispersed spores.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Test germination and growth in droplets.

evidence.quote_or_span: eight-layered sterile filter papers were found to perform the best

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Monodispersed spores obtained; need to verify droplet compatibility.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Unknown if spores can germinate and grow normally in droplets.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Spores will germinate and grow comparably in droplets as in suspension.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Compare germination and growth in droplets vs. suspension.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Spores germinated and proliferated at a comparable rate; droplets filled with mycelium by 24 h.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Determine fluorescence detection timeline for screening.

source.title: A broadly neutralizing humanized ACE2-targeting antibody against SARS-CoV-2 variants

initial_request.research_problem: The rapid emergence of SARS-CoV-2 variants with RBD mutations that escape existing antibody therapies necessitates broadly effective countermeasures.
initial_request.known_context: SARS-CoV-2 variants with RBD mutations (e.g., D614G, B.1.1.7, B.1.351) are spreading and can escape existing antibody therapies. ACE2 is the conserved host receptor for SARSr-CoVs.
constraints: Must block ACE2-RBD interaction
Must not downregulate ACE2 or impair its enzymatic activity
Must be safe in non-human primates
input_data: SARS-CoV and SARS-CoV-2 spike pseudotyped viruses
quantifiable_goals: Broad neutralization of SARS-CoV-2 variants
High binding affinity to hACE2 (KD < 10 nM)
In vivo efficacy in mouse model
Safety in cynomolgus monkeys

evolution_trajectory.state_before: SARS-CoV-2 variants with RBD mutations are emerging and escaping existing antibody therapies.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Existing antibody therapies target the RBD, which is prone to mutation. A host-directed approach may provide broader neutralization.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Targeting the conserved host receptor ACE2 with a blocking antibody may provide broad neutralization against SARSr-CoVs and variants.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Pursue isolation of ACE2-blocking monoclonal antibodies.
evolution_trajectory.observation: ACE2 is the conserved receptor for SARS-CoV and SARS-CoV-2; RBD mutations can escape RBD-targeting antibodies.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Hypothesis supported by known biology; proceed to antibody generation.

evidence.quote_or_span: The successive emergences and accelerating spread of novel severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2) lineages and evolved resistance to some ongoing clinical therapeutics increase the risks associated with the coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19) pandemic.
An urgent intervention for broadly effective therapies to limit the morbidity and mortality of COVID-19 and future transmission events from SARS-related coronaviruses (SARSr-CoVs) is needed.

evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Lead clone h11B11 identified for further characterization.

source.title: MTSplice predicts effects of genetic variants on tissue-specific splicing

initial_request.research_problem: Predicting tissue-specific effects of genetic variants on splicing is crucial for understanding tissue-specific diseases, but existing models like MMSplice only capture constitutive splicing and lack tissue-specificity.
initial_request.known_context: MMSplice is a tissue-agnostic model for variant effect prediction on splicing. SPANR does not allow tissue-specific predictions. Multi-task learning across tissues can capture shared regulatory sequences.
constraints: Limited tissue-specific sQTLs (7-21%)
GTEx from healthy donors may not capture disease-relevant tissue-specific effects
input_data: ASCOT dataset of tissue-specific percent spliced-in (Ψ) values
quantifiable_goals: Improve splicing variant effect prediction in a tissue-specific manner
Outperform MMSplice in tissue-specific benchmarks

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Existing tools like MMSplice and SPANR lack tissue-specific splicing predictions.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: No model predicts tissue-specific variant effects on splicing.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Multi-task learning across tissues can capture shared tissue-specific regulatory sequences.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Develop a multi-task neural network (TSplice) to predict tissue-specific splicing.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Multi-task learning has been successful in other splicing models.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need to analyze tissue-specific splicing data to inform model design.

evidence.quote_or_span: MMSplice has been trained in a tissue-agnostic fashion and therefore does not capture effects of variants affecting tissue-specific regulatory elements.
the publicly available SPANR does not allow performing tissue-specific predictions.

A common denominator of these models is that they are trained by multi-task learning, i.e., the models make joint predictions for all tissues or cell types using a common set of underlying predictive features. This strategy allows models to efficiently pool information about regulatory elements that are shared across cell types or tissues.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Hypothesis that multi-task learning can capture tissue-specific splicing.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to quantify tissue-specific splicing variation.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A substantial fraction of exons show tissue-specific splicing deviations.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Analyze ASCOT dataset for tissue-specific Ψ patterns.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 29% of exons show tissue-specific deviations >10% from average; brain and muscle/heart tissues cluster.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Data supports need for tissue-specific model; also reveals limitation of GTEx data.

evidence.quote_or_span: samples from the central nervous system (brain, spinal cord, and retina) have very distinct splicing patterns compared to other tissues
skeletal muscle and the two heart tissues (left ventrial and atrial appendage) also clustered together with shared splicing patterns.
only a limited fraction (between 7 and 21%) of splicing QTLs are tissue-specific
Since GTEx samples are derived from healthy donors, this observation, however, does not rule out the possibility that some disease-

evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to train and validate TSplice.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Train TSplice on ASCOT (excluding chr2,3,5) with KL divergence loss, Adam optimizer, and ensemble of 8 models.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 20 models trained, 8 selected via forward selection for ensemble.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Evaluate TSplice predictions on test set.

evidence.quote_or_span: TSplice was trained on the ASCOT dataset using all chromosomes except for chromosome 2, 3, and 5.
Missing values, which typically correspond to tissues in which the gene is not expressed, were masked out in the loss function.
After finding the best hyperparameter combination, 20 models were trained with the best hyperparameters but different random initialization.
A forward model selection strategy was used to select a set of models whose average predictions gives the smallest loss on the validation set.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: TSplice trained.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to assess TSplice prediction accuracy.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: TSplice predictions correlate with measured tissue-specific Ψ.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Compute Spearman correlation between TSplice predictions and ASCOT measurements per tissue.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Median Spearman ρ=0.22 across tissues, higher for CNS tissues.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate TSplice against baseline and known biology.

evidence.quote_or_span: The predictions positively correlated with the measurements in all tissues and showed a median Spearman correlation of 0.22
The performance was higher for tissues of the central nervous system

evolution_trajectory.observation: TSplice outperforms the baseline in all 56 tissues.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate that TSplice captures known tissue-specific regulatory motifs.

evidence.quote_or_span: TSplice outperforms the alternative model for all 56 tissues

evolution_trajectory.state_before: TSplice outperforms baseline.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to verify TSplice learns known tissue-specific regulatory motifs.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: TSplice activates at known tissue-specific splicing factor binding sites.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Visualize model gradients and perform in silico mutations to identify motifs.
evolution_trajectory.observation: TSplice activates at PTBP1/2, NOVA1/2, MBNL1 motifs; in silico mutations confirm tissue-specific effects.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Integrate TSplice with MMSplice to create MTSplice.

evolution_trajectory.observation: MTSplice combines constitutive and tissue-specific effects.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Benchmark MTSplice against MMSplice on GTEx variant effects.

evidence.quote_or_span: We next considered combining MMSplice, which models tissue-independent effects together with TSplice, which models differential effects between tissues, to predict the effects associated with genetic variants for any GTEx tissue

evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: MTSplice outperforms MMSplice in tissue-specific variant effect prediction, especially in brain.

evolution_trajectory.observation: MTSplice outperforms MMSplice in 39/51 tissues (P=1.76e-5), especially brain (10/12 tissues).

source.title: A dual-reporter system for investigating and optimizing protein translation and folding in E. coli

initial_request.research_problem: Heterologous protein expression in E. coli often leads to misfolding, aggregation, and degradation. Existing screening methods are low-throughput and cannot simultaneously monitor translation and folding at the single-cell level.
initial_request.known_context: E. coli is a valued host for industrial expression of peptides and proteins, but heterologous expression often results in misfolding, aggregation, and degradation.
constraints: Low-throughput existing screening methods
Inability to simultaneously monitor translation and folding at single-cell level
quantifiable_goals: Simultaneously monitor protein translation and folding at single-cell level
High-throughput screening of protein variants

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Heterologous protein expression in E. coli often results in misfolding, aggregation, and degradation. Existing screening methods are low-throughput and cannot simultaneously monitor translation and folding at the single-cell level.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: No system enables simultaneous monitoring of translation and folding at the single cell level.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A dual-reporter system can be developed to simultaneously monitor protein translation and folding at the single-cell level.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Design a dual-reporter system with a translation sensor and a folding sensor.
evolution_trajectory.observation: The problem of low-throughput screening and lack of simultaneous monitoring motivates the design of a dual-reporter system.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Proceed to design the dual-reporter system.

evidence.quote_or_span: heterologous expression of recombinant proteins in bacteria such as E. coli, a valued host for industrial expression of a wide range of peptides and proteins, often results in misfolding, aggregation and degradation.
no system enables simultaneous monitoring of translation and folding at the single cell level.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: No system exists for simultaneous monitoring of translation and folding at single-cell level.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to design sensors that can report on translation and folding independently.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A translation-coupling cassette with mCherry can report translation, and an IbpA promoter driving GFP can report folding stress.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Design a dual-reporter system consisting of a translation sensor (translation-coupling cassette with mCherry) and a folding sensor (IbpA promoter driving GFP).
evolution_trajectory.observation: The designed system should allow simultaneous monitoring of translation and folding at the single-cell level.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Optimize the folding sensor components for better sensitivity.

evidence.quote_or_span: we have developed a dual-reporter system that simultaneously monitors protein translation and protein folding at the single cell level (Fig. 1a).

The translation sensor consists of a translation-coupling cassette comprised of a strong secondary mRNA structure formed by a C-terminal hexa-histidine tag, a stop codon for the gene of interest and a ribosome binding site (RBS) for a downstream fluorescent reporter protein, mCherry.
The protein folding sensor is based on the naturally occurring heat shock response system in E. coli.
In our protein folding sensor, the RpoH inducible IbpA promoter is inserted upstream of a GFP reporter gene

evolution_trajectory.state_before: The folding sensor design needs optimization for dynamic range and sensitivity.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: The effect of plasmid copy number and GFP stability on the folding sensor performance is unknown.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Using a low-copy plasmid backbone (pBBR1) and an unstable GFP variant (GFP-ASV) will improve the signal-to-noise ratio of the folding sensor.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Test plasmid backbones (pBBR1 vs ColE1) and GFP variants (GFP-mut3 vs GFP-ASV) using heat shock induction.
evolution_trajectory.observation: pBBR1 with GFP-ASV gave two sharp well-defined peaks with a 3–5-fold increase in signal-to-noise ratio compared to other combinations.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate the optimized dual-reporter system with model proteins.

evidence.quote_or_span: To test whether the copy number affects the lbpAp-GFP activity, we have analyzed the heat shock response from two vector backbones with the pBBR1 or the ColE1 ORI, respectively.
The two ORIs were further tested in combination with two GFP-variants, GFP-mut3 and GFP-ASV.

Monitoring of the heat shock response signals of protein folding sensors (pSEVA441-IbpAp and pSEVA631(Sp)-IbpAp) with different origin of replications (ColE1 and pBBR1, respectively) and GFP variants (GFP-mut3 and GFP-ASV) after induction of the IbpA promoter.
two sharp well-defined peaks were observed from the heat induced and uninduced pBBR1 plasmids with a 3–5-fold increase in the signal-to-noise ratio.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Optimized dual-reporter system ready for validation with model proteins.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Whether the dual-reporter system can distinguish between soluble and insoluble proteins and report on their translation and folding status.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Soluble proteins will show high mCherry and low GFP, while insoluble proteins will show low mCherry and high GFP.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Validate the dual-reporter system with five model proteins: PARP1-BRCT (soluble), BRCA1-BRCT (insoluble), p19 (soluble), E6 (insoluble), and a control.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Soluble proteins gave high mCherry/low GFP; insoluble proteins gave low mCherry/high GFP, but GFP levels varied between insoluble proteins (BRCA1-BRCT showed 5–10-fold increase in GFP signal compared to PARP1-BRCT).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Test solubility tags to improve solubility and further validate the system.

evidence.quote_or_span: Overexpression of the insoluble BRCA1-BRCT, however, promoted binding of RpoH to the IbpA promoter region of the folding sensor, resulting in a 5–10-fold increase in GFP-signal compared to PARP1-BRCT.
Different levels of GFP fluorescence were observed for BRCA1-BRCT and E6 although they both were expressed as insoluble protein.
The results demonstrate that it is possible to combine both biosensors to simultaneously investigate translation and proper folding of proteins in E. coli.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: The dual-reporter system can distinguish soluble and insoluble proteins, but some proteins remain insoluble.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Whether solubility tags can improve solubility and reduce GFP signal for insoluble proteins.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: N-terminal solubility tags (NusA, SUMO) will improve solubility and reduce GFP signal for insoluble proteins.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Test NusA and SUMO solubility tags on PARP1-BRCT, BRCA1-BRCT, p19, and E6.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Solubility tags improved solubility and reduced GFP signal for some proteins, but SUMO decreased expression of BRCA1-BRCT.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Proceed to deep mutational scanning to screen for stability variants.

evidence.quote_or_span: The N-terminally linked solubility tags did not have a large impact on the translation level of PARP1-BRCT, BRCA1-BRCT, and E6, which all expressed also without the tags, and the SUMO tag even decreased the expression level of BRCA1-BRCT.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: The dual-reporter system is validated for monitoring translation and folding of individual proteins.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Whether the system can be used for high-throughput screening of protein variants with altered stability.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A random mutant library of PARP1-BRCT can be screened by FACS using the dual-reporter to enrich variants with impaired folding.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Generate a random mutant library of PARP1-BRCT (1–3 mutations per protein) and screen by FACS for high mCherry and high GFP.

parameters.sorting_gates: P1: high mCherry; P2: high mCherry + high GFP (upper 1%)

evolution_trajectory.observation: Cells sorted for high mCherry and high GFP showed enrichment of variants with impaired folding.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Analyze sorted bins by NGS and correlate with computational predictions.

evidence.quote_or_span: We thus demonstrate the use of the dual-reporter system to screen for variants with either reduced or increased stability in a high-throughput mode.

a randomly generated mutant library of pET22-PARP1-BRCT-mCherry was expressed with the protein folding sensor pSEVA631(Sp)-IbpAp-GFP-ASV to demonstrate the applicability of the dual-reporter system to screen for new variants with correct translation but altered stability.

The mutant library was prepared using the error-prone DNA polymerase Mutazyme II that provides a minimal mutational bias. By adjusting the amount of initial target DNA and the number of gene duplications, a mutation rate of 1–3 amino acid substitutions per protein was achieved.

The cell cultures were sorted using FACS for high mCherry signal (P1) alone and for both high mCherry signal (P1) and high GFP signal (P2). Gate 1 (P1) was defined as a mCherry signal higher than the control plasmid background, to ensure that only cells expressing correctly translated proteins were collected. Gate 2 (P2) was defined as the upper 1% of cells with the highest GFP fluorescence, in order to select for variants expressing proteins with decreased stability.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Sorted bins from deep mutational scanning are available.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Whether the experimental enrichment of variants correlates with computational stability predictions.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: NGS analysis of sorted bins will show correlation with FoldX and Rosetta stability predictions.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform NGS on sorted bins and compare enrichment scores with computational stability predictions (FoldX, Rosetta).
evolution_trajectory.observation: GFP signal shift indicated enrichment of variants with impaired folding; correlation with computational predictions gave AUC=0.61, suggesting reasonable but non-perfect correlation.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Validate selected variants individually.

evidence.quote_or_span: The PARP1-BRCT library sorted for both high mCherry signal and a high GFP signal (Lib. P2) shows a clear shift in GFP signal compared to PARP1-BRCT WT and the PARP1-BRCT library that was only sorted for correct translation (Lib. P1) (Fig. 5b). The shift in GFP signals indicates that protein variants with impaired folding properties had been enriched.
From this analysis, we obtained an area under the curve of 0.61 suggestive of a reasonable but non-perfect correlation between the calculated stabilities and the experimental sequencing data.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Deep mutational scanning identified potential misfolding and non-interfering variants.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Whether the identified variants indeed show the expected folding behavior when tested individually.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Synthesized variants will confirm the expected patterns: misfolding variants will show high GFP, non-interfering variants will show low GFP.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Synthesize 20 variants (14 misfolding, 6 non-interfering) and analyze by flow cytometry and Western blot.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 14 misfolding and 6 non-interfering variants confirmed expected patterns, validating the system.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Attempt to find suppressor mutations for a specific misfolding variant.

evidence.quote_or_span: The 20 variants were synthesized and introduced into the translation sensor plasmid and analyzed using flow cytometry, and the protein concentration was quantified using Western blots

From the deep mutational scanning we chose 20 variants for further analysis, of which 14 mutations (G20V, G20W, A31T, I33N, G37E, G37R, G37W, G38R, C50Y, S52I, S52N, I72N, V74F, H97L) were suggested by the deep mutational scan to result in misfolding and 6 mutations (I33T, V74I, D78V, Q81R, A96P, A96V) that were found not to interfere with protein folding.
The translation levels detected by the mCherry signal and by Western blotting was comparable for 6 (A31T, I33T, D78V, Q81R, A96P, A96V) of the 20 variants
The corresponding high GFP signal shows that the stability of the PARP1-BRCT I33N and G37E variants was decreased as also predicted from the library sequencing data and the FoldX analysis.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: PARP1-BRCT I33N is a misfolding variant identified from the deep mutational scan.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Whether suppressor mutations can restore folding of I33N.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A new mutant library based on I33N can be screened for variants with high translation and low GFP to identify suppressors.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Generate a random mutant library of PARP1-BRCT I33N and sort for high mCherry and low GFP.
evolution_trajectory.observation: After sorting, only WT sequences and one silent mutation (P10P) were found; likely due to reversion of the single nucleotide substitution.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Analyze the reason for failure and consider limitations.

evidence.quote_or_span: all the selected clones were found to encode wild type PARP1-BRCT, except for one silent mutation, P10P

Our observations that only WT sequences were found after sorting for proteins with increased stability is a likely result of the I33N mutation being a single nucleotide substitution making it likely to revert back to the WT PARP1-BRCT sequence.

Our observations that only WT sequences were found after sorting for proteins with increased stability is a likely result of the I33N mutation being a single nucleotide substitution making it likely to revert back to the WT PARP1-BRCT sequence.
64 single clones that show protein expression (Fig. 7A, Pool 1, Gate 1) in combination with a decreased GFP signal (Fig. 7A, Pool 1; Gate 2) were sorted

evolution_trajectory.state_before: GFP fluorescence depends on DnaK binding sites, which vary between proteins.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: GFP fluorescence may not be comparable between unrelated proteins.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: For variants of the same protein, GFP output can be used as a direct measure of stability.
evolution_trajectory.decision: State limitation and provide guidance for use.
evolution_trajectory.observation: GFP fluorescence may not be comparable between unrelated proteins; for variants of the same protein, GFP output can be used as a direct measure of stability.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Highlight system validation and remaining limitations.

evidence.quote_or_span: This suggests that the GFP fluorescence may not be comparable when investigating unrelated proteins.
For variants of the same protein, the GFP output can be used as a direct measure of high or low protein stability, as we have demonstrated for CI2.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: The dual-reporter system has been used for various applications.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Whether the system can detect misfolded proteins that would otherwise be characterized as having no expression.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: The translation sensor differentiates nonsense from missense mutations, enabling detection of misfolded proteins.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Validate that the system detects misfolded proteins and differentiates mutation types.
evolution_trajectory.observation: The system is capable of detecting misfolded proteins that would otherwise have been characterized as proteins with no expression; the translation sensor differentiates nonsense from missense mutations.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Note limitation: need for downstream activity assay.

evidence.quote_or_span: the system is capable of detecting misfolded proteins that would otherwise have been characterized as proteins with no expression.

The incorporation of the translation sensor makes it possible to differentiate between nonsense and missense mutations since the reporter signal will only be generated when complete translation of the target protein has been achieved.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: The dual-reporter system can identify variants with high translation and solubility.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Whether the identified variants retain enzyme activity.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A downstream activity assay is needed to ensure enzyme activity, as mutations near catalytic sites may reduce activity.
evolution_trajectory.decision: State limitation that a downstream activity assay is needed.
evolution_trajectory.observation: The dual-reporter system can be used to obtain protein variants with high translation levels and high or moderate solubility; however, a downstream activity assay is needed to ensure an active enzyme.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Conclude the trajectory.

evidence.quote_or_span: The dual-reporter system can be used to obtain protein variants with high translation levels and high or moderate solubility; however, a downstream activity assay is needed to ensure an active enzyme.

success_verification.final_conclusion: The dual-reporter system successfully enables simultaneous monitoring of protein translation and folding at the single-cell level, allowing high-throughput screening of protein variants. It can detect misfolded proteins and differentiate mutation types, but GFP signal is not comparable between unrelated proteins, and a downstream activity assay is needed for enzyme activity.
limitations: GFP fluorescence may not be comparable between unrelated proteins due to varying DnaK binding sites.
A downstream activity assay is needed to ensure enzyme activity.
Suppressor screening for single nucleotide substitutions may be confounded by reversion to wild type.

source.title: Improvements of the productivity and saccharification efficiency of the cellulolytic β-glucosidase D2-BGL in Pichia pastoris via directed evolution

initial_request.research_problem: Improve catalytic efficiency, substrate inhibition tolerance, and recombinant production of the fungal GH3 β-glucosidase D2-BGL for biomass saccharification via directed evolution.
initial_request.known_context: D2-BGL is a GH3 β-glucosidase from a metagenomic library with high substrate affinity but suffers from substrate inhibition and low recombinant production.
constraints: Maintain pH and thermostability
Use S. cerevisiae for library construction and P. pastoris for production
input_data: Homology model based on A. niger BGL1
quantifiable_goals: Increase specific activity at high cellobiose concentration
Increase recombinant enzyme production in P. pastoris
Improve glucose release from sugarcane bagasse

evolution_trajectory.state_before: D2-BGL has high substrate affinity but suffers from substrate inhibition and low recombinant production.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Unknown whether random mutagenesis can discover beneficial mutations not predictable from structure.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Random mutagenesis coupled with high-throughput screening can discover new beneficial mutations in D2-BGL.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Proceed with error-prone PCR and screening.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Random mutagenesis with high-throughput screening is a powerful tool for discovering beneficial mutations.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Hypothesis justifies experimental design choices.

evidence.quote_or_span: random mutagenesis coupled with an efficient high-throughput screening process represents a powerful molecular tool for discovering new beneficial mutations.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Need to construct mutant libraries and produce enzyme for characterization.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Choice of expression hosts for library construction and large-scale production.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: S. cerevisiae is suitable for library construction due to efficient homologous recombination; P. pastoris is better for large-scale production due to higher yields and appropriate glycosylation.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Use S. cerevisiae for mutant library construction and P. pastoris for large-scale production.
evolution_trajectory.observation: S. cerevisiae deemed suitable for library construction; P. pastoris deemed better producer for large-scale.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Host selection enables rational design and library construction.

evidence.quote_or_span: S. cerevisiae was deemed to be a suitable expression host for constructing the D2-BGL mutant library, whereas P. pastoris was deemed to be a better producer of recombinant enzyme in large-scale.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: F256M mutation designed but not tested.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Effect of F256 substitutions on activity and substrate inhibition.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: F256M will maintain activity at low substrate and reduce inhibition at high substrate.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Test F256C, F256Y, F256M mutants for specific activity at 10 and 50 mM cellobiose.
evolution_trajectory.observation: F256C and F256Y reduce activity; F256M shows similar activity at 10 mM but 26% higher at 50 mM, indicating reduced substrate inhibition.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: F256M identified as beneficial, used as template for second library.

source.title: Synergistic stabilization of a double mutant in chymotrypsin inhibitor 2 from a library screen in E. coli

initial_request.research_problem: Identifying and characterizing synergistic stabilizing mutations in CI2 using a bacterial folding sensor, overcoming limited dynamic range for stabilized variants.
initial_request.known_context: Wild-type CI2 already produces low GFP signal in the folding sensor, limiting detection of further stabilization. The I57A variant is significantly destabilized (ΔGf=14 kJ/mol) and yields high GFP signal.
constraints: Limited dynamic range of GFP sensor for stabilized variants
Error-prone PCR library diversity limited to 0-4 mutations per gene
quantifiable_goals: Identify mutations that increase thermodynamic stability beyond wild-type
Characterize synergistic effects between mutations

evolution_trajectory.state_before: The bacterial folding sensor has limited dynamic range for detecting stabilized CI2 variants because wild-type CI2 already produces low GFP signal.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to increase the dynamic range of the folding sensor to enable selection of stabilized CI2 variants.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Using a destabilized CI2 background (I57A) will increase the dynamic range of the GFP sensor, allowing selection of stabilizing mutations.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Adopt the I57A background for library screening.
evolution_trajectory.observation: The I57A variant is significantly destabilized (ΔGf=14 kJ/mol) and results in a high GFP signal, providing a larger dynamic range.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: The I57A background is expected to enable selection of stabilized variants by FACS sorting for low GFP signal.

evidence.quote_or_span: The dynamic range of the GFP signal towards discovering stabilized variants (i.e. less GFP) is thus very small.

evolution_trajectory.observation: 71 unique sequences without stop codons were found; 41 from WT library, 30 from I57A library.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Analyze sequences to identify common mutations in stabilized variants.

evidence.quote_or_span: In total we found 71 unique sequences without stop codons, deletions or insertions in the region encoding the 64 amino acid residues of CI2.
41 of the sequences were from the wild-type library and 30 sequences were from the I57A library (Supplementary Fig.
from the library starting from the wild-type sequence and 289 clones from the library starting from the I57A sequence by collection of single cells from the last rounds of FACS screening.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Compare sequences and identify recurring mutations.
evolution_trajectory.observation: All stabilized variants from the I57A library carry I57V; I57V alone is more stable than wild-type.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Measure thermodynamic stability of identified variants to quantify stabilization.

evidence.quote_or_span: All variants selected in the I57A background that are more stable than this background have a valine at position 57
All variants selected in the I57A background that are more stable than this background have a valine at position 57
it is known that I57V is slightly more stable than the wild-type protein
Also, all variants selected in the I57A background that are more stable than I57A carry I57V, suggesting that not many single variants in the library stabilize CI2.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: A set of 27 CI2 variants (13 from WT background, 12 from I57A background, plus wild-type and I57A) were selected for stability measurements.

evolution_trajectory.observation: Stabilities range from -7.4 kJ/mol to -38.5 kJ/mol; five variants more stable than wild-type.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Identify highly stabilized double mutants and analyze synergy.

evidence.quote_or_span: To measure the in vitro stability for folding we used our recently described combined two-dimensional thermal and chemical protein unfolding assay

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Stability data for 27 variants available.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Compare stabilities of double mutants to single mutants.
evolution_trajectory.observation: L49I/I57V and D55G/I57V are further stabilized than I57V; L49I/I57V shows strong positive synergy (stabilized by 5.1 kJ mol⁻¹ more than additive expectation).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Investigate structural basis of synergy.

evidence.quote_or_span: Both the L49I/I57V and D55G/I57V are even further stabilized than I57V.
mutants we found (L49I/I57V and D55G/I57V) were, however, highly stabilized, both relative to the I57A background but also more than the wild-type protein.

The other case is a double mutant, L49I/I57V, which is 5.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Calculate ΔΔG values using FoldX, Rosetta, and SEQ and compare to experiment.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Pearson's r: 0.81 (FoldX), 0.74 (Rosetta), 0.72 (SEQ). Methods fail to predict stabilized variants and synergistic effects; FoldX predicts D55G well but not I57V or L49I synergy.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Structural studies needed to understand the physical basis of synergy.

evidence.quote_or_span: The Pearson's correlation coefficients $(r)$ of the experimentally determined values with those calculated using FoldX, Rosetta and SEQ are 0.81, 0.74 and 0.72, respectively.
while the methods are relatively good at predicting destabilizing effects, they are generally not able to predict stabilized variants
they are generally not able to predict stabilized variants, though FoldX does predict D55G to be highly stabilizing.
FoldX does a good job in predicting the effects of the L49I and D55G mutations
FoldX predicts the effect of D55G/I57V rather well, but this can be attributed to the dominating effect of the D55G mutation that was well predicted on its own.
The prediction methods that we used were not able to pick up the effects of the I57V and L49I mutations that we observe.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Synergy in L49I/I57V cannot be explained computationally.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: What structural changes underlie the synergistic stabilization?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Crystal structures of wild-type, L49I, I57V, and L49I/I57V will reveal conformational differences.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Determine crystal structures of the four variants.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Structures are highly similar (RMSD 0.16, 0.45, and 0.17 Å). In L49I/I57V, Ile49 adopts two alternative conformations (70% major rotated ~120°).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Analyze interaction energies to explain synergy.

evidence.quote_or_span: We, therefore, determined the crystal structures of all four CI2 variants in the wild-type to L49I/I57V double mutant cycle (Table 2).
Overall, the structures of L49I, I57V and L49I/I57V are highly similar to the structure of the wild-type (Fig. 3c) with RMSDs for the backbone of 0.16, 0.45 and 0.17 Å.
d Structural details around positions 49 and 57 in the four CI2 variants in the wild-type to L49I/I57V double mutant cycle.
CI2 WT, L49I and L49I/I57V were crystallized at 293 K in 40% $(\mathrm{NH}_{4})_{2}\mathrm{SO}_{4}$ , 50 mM Tris-HCl, pH 8.
In the major conformation that accounts of the remaining 70% of the electron density, the γ2 methyl is rotated approximately 120°

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Crystal structures reveal alternative Ile49 conformations in L49I/I57V.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How do these structural changes translate to energetic stabilization?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Pairwise interaction energy analysis will show that I57V relieves an unfavorable I57-Q59 interaction, and L49I relieves long-range destabilizing effects on residues 57-62.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform pairwise interaction energy analysis on the structures.
evolution_trajectory.observation: I57V relieves unfavorable I57-Q59 interaction; L49I destabilizes wild-type but stabilizes I57V by relieving long-range effects on residues 57-62.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Conclude on the synergistic mechanism and note limitations.

evidence.quote_or_span: Analysis of the pairwise interaction energies at the residue level (Fig. 3e) suggests that much of the stabilizing effect of the I57V mutation originates from an unfavourable interaction between I57 and Q59
The effect of the L49I mutation, which destabilizes the wild-type, but stabilizes the I57V variant is more subtle
It appears that some of the destabilization of the L49I mutation is a long-range effect resulting in several less favourable interactions among residues 57-62.
These negative effects are relieved in the double mutant (Fig. 3e).

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Synergistic mechanism proposed.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: What are the limitations of the approach?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: The error-prone PCR library had limited diversity, and the folding sensor dynamic range remains limited even with I57A background.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Acknowledge limitations in library design and sensor dynamic range.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Most codons in selected variants are one base substitution from wild-type; I57V nearly minimizes GFP signal, hindering selection of further stabilized variants.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: These limitations inform future library design and sensor improvements.

evidence.quote_or_span: It also demonstrates that our settings in the error prone PCR did not result in a very broad library, and indeed most codons in the selected variants are just one base substitution from the wild-type sequence.
The large gain in stability of the I57V variant over the I57A background is a challenge to the folding sensor as the GFP signal reaches close to its minimal level with just one base substitution.

metrics: ΔΔGf (change in stability relative to wild-type)
success_verification.final_conclusion: L49I/I57V is a synergistic double mutant that stabilizes CI2 beyond wild-type through relief of unfavorable interactions and entropic effects from alternative Ile49 conformations. Computational methods fail to predict this synergy.
limitations: Error-prone PCR library had limited diversity; most codons one base substitution from wild-type.
Folding sensor dynamic range still limited; I57V nearly minimizes GFP signal, hindering selection of further stabilized variants.

evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: To maximize product activity and yield, prolong culture at low μ with methanol co-feeding; to maximize qp, use high μ glycerol-only fed-batch.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Provide design recommendations based on μ and methanol effects.

avoiding the use of methanol would allow to avoid the important methanol-associated operational drawbacks.
