validation_methods: temperature shift and colony formation assay
success_verification.final_conclusion: BHD-linked missense and in-frame deletion variants cause disease via structural destabilization and proteasomal degradation, classifying BHD as a protein misfolding disease.
limitations: Overexpression system may not fully recapitulate endogenous PQC pathways.
Computational model may not perfectly capture solvent-exposed side chain effects.
Other destabilized variants did not regain function at low temperature, likely due to irreversible misfolding.
V400I and K508R variants are stable and not degraded, questioning their pathogenicity.

source.title: Optimized CRISPR guide RNA design for two high-fidelity Cas9 variants by deep learning

initial_request.research_problem: Lack of knowledge governing gRNA activity for high-fidelity Cas9 variants (eSpCas9(1.1), SpCas9-HF1) limits their applications; existing gRNA design tools optimized for WT-SpCas9 may not work for these variants.
initial_request.target_object: gRNA activity prediction for eSpCas9(1.1) and SpCas9-HF1
initial_request.known_context: Highly specific Cas9 nucleases derived from SpCas9 are valuable for genome editing, but their wide applications are hampered by a lack of knowledge governing gRNA activity. Existing evidence reveals that some highly active gRNAs for WT-SpCas9 are poorly active for the highly specific Cas9 variants.
constraints: High-fidelity Cas9 variants are sensitive to 5' mismatches, limiting target site selection with standard hU6 promoter (requires G start).
quantifiable_goals: Develop a predictive model for gRNA activity for eSpCas9(1.1) and SpCas9-HF1 with high accuracy (Spearman correlation >0.85).

evolution_trajectory.state_before: No large-scale gRNA activity data for high-fidelity Cas9 variants; existing tools are for WT-SpCas9.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Unknown whether gRNA design rules differ between WT-SpCas9 and high-fidelity variants.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A genome-scale screen can generate sufficient gRNA activity data to model and predict activity for eSpCas9(1.1) and SpCas9-HF1.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform a genome-scale screen to measure gRNA activity for WT-SpCas9, eSpCas9(1.1), and SpCas9-HF1.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Highly specific Cas9 variants have limited applications due to lack of knowledge governing gRNA activity; existing tools are optimized for WT-SpCas9.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need to design a gRNA library compatible with high-fidelity variants' sensitivity to 5' mismatches.

evidence.quote_or_span: Highly specific Cas9 nucleases derived from SpCas9 are valuable tools for genome editing, but their wide applications are hampered by a lack of knowledge governing guide RNA (gRNA) activity.
Existing evidence reveals that some highly active gRNAs for WT-SpCas9 are poorly active for the highly specific Cas9 variants

Here, we perform a genome-scale screen to measure gRNA activity for two highly specific SpCas9 variants (eSpCas9(1.1) and SpCas9-HF1) and wild-type SpCas9 (WT-SpCas9) in human cells, and obtain indel rates of over 50,000 gRNAs for each nuclease, covering \~20,000 genes.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Standard hU6 promoter requires G at +1, limiting target sites for high-fidelity variants sensitive to 5' mismatches.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can mU6 promoter, which initiates with A or G, expand target sites without compromising activity?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: mU6 promoter will show similar activity to hU6 for gRNAs initiated with G, and can also initiate with A, expanding targetable sites.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Adopt mU6 promoter for gRNA expression in the library.
evolution_trajectory.observation: mU6 promoter showed activity similar to hU6 promoter for 12 tested gRNAs initiated with G.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: With mU6 validated, design a gRNA library targeting microRNAs and coding genes.

evidence.quote_or_span: We compared the activity of mU6 promoter and hU6 promoter by transient expression of gRNAs for genome editing in WT-SpCas9-expressing HEK293T cells (Fig. 1a). The mU6 promoter showed activity similar to hU6 promoter for 12 tested gRNAs initiated with G
Mutation of nucleotides around the +1 position of type 3 polymerase III promoters: the effect on transcriptional activity and start site usage.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: mU6 promoter validated; need to design gRNA library covering diverse targets.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to design gRNAs for microRNAs and coding genes that meet mU6 requirements?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: In-house script for miRNAs (start with A/G, no 4T) and Doench tool for coding genes will yield a comprehensive library.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Design gRNA library: 4951 gRNAs for 1549 miRNAs, 75,312 gRNAs for 19,037 coding genes.

tool_or_method.name: in-house Python script; Doench et al. online tool

evolution_trajectory.observation: Designed 4951 gRNAs covering 1549 human microRNAs and 75,312 gRNAs covering 19,037 human coding genes.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Library design complete; proceed to genome-scale screen.

evidence.quote_or_span: The gRNAs targeting microRNAs were designed using an in-house Python script.
The gRNAs were selected if they satisfied the following criteria: (1) initiated with either A or G; (2) without four consecutive thymines (T).
we designed a total of 4951 gRNAs covering 1549 human microRNAs.
The gRNAs targeting coding sequences in the library were designed by an online tool (https://portals.broadinstitute.org/gpp/public/analysis-tools/sgrna-design) developed by Doench et al.
A total of 75,312 gRNAs covering 19,037 human coding genes was designed.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: gRNA library designed; need to measure activity for three Cas9 variants.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: What are the indel rates for >50,000 gRNAs across WT-SpCas9, eSpCas9(1.1), and SpCas9-HF1?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Genome-scale screen with lentiviral library at 1000-fold coverage will yield reliable activity data.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform screen in HEK293T cells expressing each Cas9 variant, with puromycin selection, genomic DNA extraction, and deep sequencing.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Obtained indel rates for >50,000 gRNAs per nuclease, covering ~20,000 genes.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Data generated; assess quality and begin feature analysis.

evidence.quote_or_span: cells were infected with gRNA library with at least 1000-fold coverage of each gRNAs.
Cells were harvested and the genomic DNA was isolated using Blood & Cell Culture DNA Kits (Qiagen)

In this study, we perform a genome-scale screen in human cells to measure gRNA activity for WT-SpCas9, eSpCas9(1.1), and SpCas9-HF1 by using a high-throughput method and obtain an activity data set of over 50,000 gRNAs, covering \~20,000 genes.

Here, we perform a genome-scale screen to measure gRNA activity for two highly specific SpCas9 variants (eSpCas9(1.1) and SpCas9-HF1) and wild-type SpCas9 (WT-SpCas9) in human cells, and obtain indel rates of over 50,000 gRNAs for each nuclease, covering \~20,000 genes.
The library was packaged into lentiviruses and transduced into HEK293T cells expressing WT-SpCas9, eSpCas9(1.1), or SpCas9-HF1 at an MOI of 0.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Activity data obtained; plasmid library error rate noted.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Does the 36.5% error rate in plasmid library affect screen results?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Residual plasmid DNA has minimal influence on the library screen.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Analyze plasmid library error rate and its impact.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Plasmid library error rate 36.5%, but residual plasmid DNA had minimal influence on the library screen.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Despite error, data quality sufficient; proceed to feature importance analysis.

evidence.quote_or_span: Analysis of the plasmid library by deep sequencing revealed that the error rate (A read contains any mutation was considered as an error) induced by oligonucleotide synthesis or PCR amplification at guide RNA–target sequence region was 36.5%.
the residual plasmid DNA only had minimal influence on the library screen

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Activity data ready; need to identify important features for gRNA activity.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which features most influence gRNA activity for each Cas9 variant?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Position-dependent nucleotide composition strongly influences predicted scores.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Use Tree SHAP with XGBoost to evaluate feature importance of 1031 features.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Predicted scores strongly influenced by position-dependent nucleotide composition; G favored, T disfavored.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Feature importance informs model selection; evaluate conventional algorithms.

evidence.quote_or_span: We connected XGBoost with SHAP (called Tree SHAP) to evaluate the importance of 1031 features
the predicted scores were strongly influenced by position-dependent nucleotide composition for three Cas9 nucleases
G was generally favored, and T was generally disfavored

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Feature importance known; need to benchmark conventional regression algorithms.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which conventional algorithm best predicts gRNA activity?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: MLP will outperform linear, ridge, XGBoost; lasso and SVM may underperform or be too slow.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Evaluate Linear, Ridge, XGBoost, MLP, lasso, SVM on the dataset.
evolution_trajectory.observation: MLP most predictive with Spearman correlation coefficients of 0.8416, 0.8457, and 0.8440 for WT-SpCas9, eSpCas9(1.1), and SpCas9-HF1, respectively; lasso equivalent to linear; SVM failed to finish in 3 weeks.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: MLP performance promising but deep learning may improve further; explore CNN and RNN.

evidence.quote_or_span: MLP is the most predictive, with Spearman correlation coefficients of 0.8416, 0.8457, and 0.8440 for WT-SpCas9, eSpCas9(1.1), and SpCas9-HF1, respectively
the penalty coefficient for lasso regression is nearly zero, which made it equivalent to linear model
SVM regression failed to finish the benchmarking on the current scale of data set within a reasonable time (3 weeks)

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Conventional algorithms benchmarked; deep learning may capture sequence dependencies better.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can CNN or RNN outperform MLP for gRNA activity prediction?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: RNN will outperform CNN due to better modeling of sequential dependencies.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Train and evaluate CNN and RNN models.
evolution_trajectory.observation: RNN outperformed CNN with Spearman correlation coefficients of 0.8555, 0.8491, and 0.8512.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: RNN shows best performance; can biological features further improve it?

evidence.quote_or_span: RNN outperformed CNN and other algorithms for gRNA activity prediction with Spearman correlation coefficients of 0.8555, 0.8491, and 0.8512

evolution_trajectory.state_before: RNN + Biofeature model built; need to test on unseen data.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: What is the generalization performance of the RNN + Biofeature model?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: The model will achieve high Spearman correlations on test sets for all three Cas9 variants.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Evaluate RNN + Biofeature model on 15% held-out test sets.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Spearman correlations: 0.8670 (WT), 0.8624 (eSpCas9), 0.8603 (SpCas9-HF1).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Strong performance; validate on endogenous sites to confirm real-world applicability.

evidence.quote_or_span: Spearman correlation coefficients of 0.8670, 0.8624, and 0.8603 for WT-SpCas9, eSpCas9(1.1), and SpCas9-HF1, respectively

5% of the data set in each set was used as the validation set, and the reserved 15% of the data set in each set was used as the test set to measure the generalization ability of each model to predict unseen data.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Model validated on test sets; need to test on endogenous genomic sites.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Do the models predict activity accurately on endogenous sites?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: All seven models will work well, but linear and ridge regression will be less predictive.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Test seven models on 85 (WT), 81 (eSpCas9), 82 (SpCas9-HF1) endogenous sites.
evolution_trajectory.observation: All seven models worked considerably well, but linear regression and ridge regression were less predictive.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Model performance confirmed; attempt to improve with chromatin data.

evidence.quote_or_span: All seven models worked considerably well, but linear regression and ridge regression were less predictive based on Spearman correlation metrics

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Model validated; chromatin accessibility might further improve predictions.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Does incorporating DNase I chromatin accessibility data improve prediction?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Adding chromatin data will increase prediction ability.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Incorporate DNase I data into DeepWt_Chromatin model and evaluate.

parameters.data: DNase I from KBM-7 cell line

evolution_trajectory.observation: Spearman correlation scores were not improved; incorporation could not significantly increase prediction ability.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Chromatin data not beneficial; finalize model and deploy as tool.

evidence.quote_or_span: the Spearman correlation scores were not improved
incorporation of these data could not significantly increase the prediction ability

evolution_trajectory.state_before: RNN + Biofeature model is the best performer; ready for deployment.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to make the model accessible to the research community?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: An online tool will facilitate gRNA design for high-fidelity Cas9 variants.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Develop DeepHF web server based on RNN + Biofeature model.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Developed an online tool called DeepHF for gRNA design.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Tool deployed; trajectory complete.

evidence.quote_or_span: developed an online tool called DeepHF (Deep learning for High-Fidelity Cas9) based on RNN + biofeature model for gRNA design

success_verification.final_conclusion: The RNN + Biofeature model (DeepHF) accurately predicts gRNA activity for WT-SpCas9, eSpCas9(1.1), and SpCas9-HF1, with Spearman correlations of 0.8670, 0.8624, and 0.8603 on test sets, and performs well on endogenous sites.
limitations: Plasmid library error rate 36.5% (minimal impact)
Chromatin accessibility data did not improve predictions
Model trained on HEK293T data; transferability to other cell types not fully validated

source.title: Hidden bias in the DUD-E dataset leads to misleading performance of deep learning in structure-based virtual screening

initial_request.research_problem: CNN models trained on DUD-E may learn dataset biases (analogue and decoy) rather than protein-ligand interactions, leading to misleadingly high performance.
initial_request.target_object: DUD-E dataset biases in CNN-based virtual screening
initial_request.known_context: DUD-E is a widely-used benchmark for structure-based virtual screening. CNN models have shown high enrichment efficiency on DUD-E, but it is unclear whether this performance reflects learning of protein-ligand interactions or exploitation of dataset biases.
constraints: Use only DUD-E dataset and derived AD dataset
CNN architectures limited to receptor-ligand-water, receptor-ligand, and ligand-only variants
input_data: DUD-E dataset (102 targets with actives and decoys)
AD dataset (actives from other targets as decoys, docked with Vina)
quantifiable_goals: Determine whether CNN models learn protein-ligand interactions or dataset biases
Quantify contributions of analogue bias and decoy bias to model performance

evolution_trajectory.state_before: CNN models achieve high enrichment efficiency on DUD-E, but the source of this performance is unclear.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: It is unknown whether CNN models learn protein-ligand interactions or exploit hidden biases in the DUD-E dataset.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Superior enrichment efficiency in CNN models can be attributed to analogue and decoy bias hidden in the DUD-E dataset rather than successful generalization of protein-ligand interactions.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Design experiments to isolate contributions of analogue bias, decoy bias, and protein-ligand interactions.
evolution_trajectory.observation: DUD-E contains potential analogue bias (similar actives across targets) and decoy bias (decoys selected to be topologically dissimilar but physically similar).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need to design training/test set combinations to isolate these biases.

evidence.quote_or_span: we outline various sources of bias in one such widely-used dataset, the Directory of Useful Decoys: Enhanced (DUD-E).

We find that superior enrichment efficiency in CNN models can be attributed to the analogue and decoy bias hidden in the DUD-E dataset rather than successful generalization of the pattern of protein-ligand interactions.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Hypothesis that biases exist, but no experimental design to test them.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to isolate contributions of analogue bias, decoy bias, and protein-ligand interactions.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: By carefully constructing training and test set combinations, we can isolate or minimize the contributions of each bias.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Design experiments: (1) create AD dataset to minimize decoy bias, (2) build three CNN model types to test role of receptor and water information.
evolution_trajectory.observation: AD dataset uses actives from other targets as decoys, docked with Vina. CNN models include receptor-ligand-water, receptor-ligand, and ligand-only.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Proceed to create AD dataset and build CNN models.

evidence.quote_or_span: we carefully construct training and test set combinations that are designed to isolate or minimize the contributions of each of these biases.
The Actives as Decoys (AD) dataset was designed to minimize the decoy bias introduced by the selection criteria in the construction of the DUD-E dataset.
For each protein target, we docked (using Vina) all of the active compounds from the other 101 DUD-E proteins to that target.
We built three kinds of CNN models: 1) receptor-ligand-water model, 2) receptor-ligand model and 3) ligand-only model, distinguished by the binding information used for model training.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: CNN models built; water channels added to explore solvation effects.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Whether adding water thermodynamic information improves CNN performance on DUD-E.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Adding water channels will improve the performance of the receptor-ligand CNN model.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Train receptor-ligand and receptor-ligand-water CNN models on 10 DUD-E targets and compare AUC.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Both models achieved high AUC, but adding water channels resulted in no detectable increase in test set AUC.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Water channels do not help; investigate whether receptor information is used at all.

evidence.quote_or_span: Water thermodynamic information was not part of the originally published CNN model. It was added here to explore whether adding solvation effects to the protein-ligand system improves the performance of the CNN model.
we independently trained the protein-ligand and protein-ligand-water CNN models on 10 targets from the DUD-E dataset
Adding water information does not improve the performance of the protein-ligand CNN model
adding the water channels resulted in no detectable increase in the test set AUC

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Water channels do not improve performance; suspicion that receptor info may be ignored.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Whether the receptor-ligand model actually uses receptor information.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: The receptor-ligand model may not be using receptor information; a ligand-only model will perform equivalently.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Train and compare receptor-ligand and ligand-only CNN models on all 102 DUD-E targets.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Both models achieved an average AUC of 0.98, with R² = 0.98 between their scores.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Equivalence suggests receptor info is ignored; perform deeper analysis.

evidence.quote_or_span: Performances of the receptor-ligand and ligand-only CNN models are equivalent
Both the receptor-ligand model and ligand-only model achieved an average AUC of 0.98
for all 102 targets, the ligand-KNN models achieved AUC values greater than 0.82, 97 of which were greater than 0.90

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Receptor-ligand and ligand-only models perform equivalently.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to confirm that receptor information is indeed ignored.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: If receptor info is ignored, removing it at test time should not affect performance, and filter weights on receptor channels should be negligible.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Test receptor-ligand models with and without receptor info; analyze filter weights; correlate scores.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Average absolute AUC difference with/without receptor: 0.0006. Filter weights on receptor channels (atom types 0–15) much smaller than on ligand (atom types 16–34). For AA2AR, R² = 0.998 between scores with/without receptor.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Confirmed receptor info is ignored; now test if simple ligand-based methods can achieve high performance.

evidence.quote_or_span: the receptor-ligand models performed almost exactly the same regardless of whether information on the receptor was provided in the test set
the average absolute AUC difference between the two testing sets is 0.0006
the weights placed on the receptor (atom type 0 to atom type 15) are much smaller than those placed on the ligand (atom type 16 to atom type 34)

The average weight put on each atom type in the 32 filters from the first convolutional layer of the AA2AR receptor-ligand CNN model; atom types 0–15 are from the receptor, and atom types 16–34 are from the ligand.
Correlation between scores predicted by the AA2AR receptor-ligand CNN model on ligands with vs. without receptor information provided. $R^{2} = 0.998$

evolution_trajectory.state_before: CNN models ignore receptor; ligand-only model performs well.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Whether simple ligand-based methods can also achieve high performance, indicating easy separability of actives and decoys.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: A simple KNN classifier on ligand fingerprints will achieve high AUC, suggesting that actives and decoys are easily separable by ligand features alone.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Train KNN models on RDKit fingerprints for all 102 DUD-E targets.
evolution_trajectory.observation: All targets achieved AUC > 0.82, 97 > 0.90. Best K = 1 or 2 for 94% of targets. KNN AUC moderately correlated with CNN AUC (R=0.59).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: High KNN performance suggests CNN exploits similar ligand biases; investigate intra-target biases.

evidence.quote_or_span: for all 102 targets, the ligand-KNN models achieved AUC values greater than 0.82, 97 of which were greater than 0.90
96 (94%) targets have a best K equal to 1 or 2
the AUC values from the ligand-only CNN models are moderately correlated (Pearson correlation R = 0.59, average absolute difference 0.02)

evolution_trajectory.state_before: KNN shows easy ligand separability; CNN likely exploits biases.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to confirm that CNN models are learning analogue and decoy bias within targets.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Ligand-only CNN prediction scores will cluster near 1 for actives and 0 for decoys, and training on very few examples will still yield high AUC.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Analyze score distributions of ligand-only CNN; train on only 5 actives and 5 decoys per target.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Average active score 0.90 ± 0.24, average decoy score 0.04 ± 0.15. With only 5 actives and 5 decoys, ligand CNN still achieved high AUC for many targets. Varied standard deviations reflect different levels of analogue and decoy bias per target.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Intra-target biases confirmed; test cross-target analogue bias via inter-target prediction.

evidence.quote_or_span: the scores of most actives were higher than those of the decoys, and most of the actives had prediction scores clustered very closely 1, while the majority of the decoys had scores clustered very closely around 0
the average predicted score for all actives and decoys across all testing sets being $0.90 \pm 0.24$ and $0.04 \pm 0.15$ , respectively
the ligand CNN model still achieved high AUC values for many targets
The varied standard deviations reflect different levels of analogue and decoy bias for each target.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Intra-target biases confirmed; need to test if biases extend across targets.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Whether analogue bias exists across targets, i.e., models trained on one target can distinguish actives/decoys of another target.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Single-target ligand CNN models will achieve high AUC on ligands from other targets, indicating cross-target analogue bias.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Run each single-target ligand CNN model against ligands from all other 101 targets.
evolution_trajectory.observation: For 74 targets, actives and decoys were accurately distinguished (AUC>0.9) by one or more models trained on other targets. Sequence-similarity splits insufficient to prevent this.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: High cross-target AUC suggests analogue bias; test on AD dataset to isolate decoy bias.

evidence.quote_or_span: we ran each single-target ligand-trained CNN model against the ligands from all other 101 targets
high AUC values not only occurred within targets (diagonal line) but also
this is based on the untested assumption that targets in DUD-E with low sequence similarity have distinct actives
For 74 targets, the actives and decoys were accurately distinguished (AUC>0.9) by one or more models trained on the ligands of other targets

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Cross-target analogue bias detected; need to quantify decoy bias contribution.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How much of the cross-target performance is due to decoy bias versus analogue bias.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Testing on the AD dataset, which minimizes decoy bias, will reduce inter-target AUCs, and for targets with different functions, AUC will drop to random.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Repeat inter-target prediction using AD dataset as test set.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Number of models yielding high AUC significantly decreased. Average AUC for 'different function' group in AD dataset was 0.500 (random).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Decoy bias confirmed; test if multi-target models can overcome biases.

evidence.quote_or_span: As shown in Fig 9B, the number of models yielding a high AUC for each target is significantly decreased
the average AUC for the “different function” group in AD dataset was 0.500

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Single-target models fail to generalize; biases dominate.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Whether training on multiple targets simultaneously can force the model to learn protein-ligand interactions.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Multi-target CNN models trained on 10 targets will achieve moderate AUC on the remaining 92, but receptor info will still not be used, and performance will drop to random on AD dataset.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Train receptor-ligand and ligand-only multi-target CNN models on 10 targets, test on 92 targets and AD dataset.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Both receptor-ligand and ligand-only models achieved AUC 0.80 ± 0.13 on DUD-E test set. On AD dataset, AUCs shifted downward for all targets, average performance similar to random chance.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Multi-target models also fail; propose controls for future work.

evidence.quote_or_span: the receptor-ligand and ligand-only CNN model showed similar performance (both with AUC 0.80 ± 0.13) when tested on the remaining 92 targets
the AUCs shifted downward for all targets, and the average performance was similar to that of random chance

evolution_trajectory.state_before: All experiments indicate CNN models learn biases, not protein-ligand interactions.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to propose controls to test for these biases in future models.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Controls such as removing receptor info, using AD dataset, and inter-target validation can diagnose whether a model learns protein-ligand interactions or biases.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Propose three controls: (1) remove receptor info at test time, (2) test on AD dataset, (3) inter-target validation with homology/function removal.
evolution_trajectory.observation: These controls can determine if a model learns from protein-ligand interactions, analogue bias, or decoy bias.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Conclude and issue caution to community.

evidence.quote_or_span: we have introduced controls in datasets to test whether a model is learning from protein-ligand interactions, analogue bias or decoy bias
By removing receptor information from the test set for receptor-ligand models, we can determine how much the model is learning from the receptor and hence from protein-ligand interactions.
testing on a dataset that does not share decoy bias introduced by the decoy selection criteria (as we did with the AD dataset) helps identify how much a model is learning from decoy bias

Inter-target validation on test sets from which proteins that share homology and functional similarity with training set proteins have been removed controls for real analogue bias and constructed decoy bias.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Controls proposed; overall conclusion reached.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Final assessment of DUD-E biases and implications for deep learning in virtual screening.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: High performance on DUD-E may be due to biases; community should develop more robust models and be aware of dataset pitfalls.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Conclude that analogue and decoy bias are widespread in DUD-E, and models may only learn inherent bias rather than physically meaningful features.
evolution_trajectory.observation: High performance in test sets is not enough; hidden biases can mislead. Hope to raise awareness and improve future models.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: End of trajectory.

evidence.quote_or_span: high performance in test sets is not enough to make the model generally applicable, as hidden biases may exist in the training/testing datasets that can lead the model astray
we clearly showed here that analogue bias and decoy bias are widespread in DUD-E and, consequently, models may only learn the inherent bias in the dataset rather than physically meaningful features
We hope this work can help our community become more aware of the pitfalls of current databases and develop more robust and meaningful deep learning models for drug discovery.

validation_methods: Comparison of receptor-ligand and ligand-only CNN models
success_verification.final_conclusion: CNN models trained on DUD-E primarily learn analogue and decoy biases rather than protein-ligand interactions. High performance is misleading. Proposed controls (receptor removal, AD dataset, inter-target validation) can diagnose biases.
limitations: PDBbind-trained models did not outperform Autodock Vina on DUD-E; low data quality and paucity challenge generalization.
Study limited to DUD-E and AD datasets; findings may not generalize to all virtual screening benchmarks.

source.title: Functional rare and low frequency variants in BLK and BANK1 contribute to human lupus

initial_request.research_problem: SLE is a heterogeneous autoimmune disease with a strong genetic component, but common GWAS variants explain only a modest fraction of risk. The study investigates whether rare coding variants in known SLE-risk genes contribute to disease, focusing on functional consequences in BLK and BANK1.
initial_request.target_object: Rare coding variants in BLK and BANK1
initial_request.known_context: GWAS have identified common variants associated with SLE, but they explain only a modest fraction of heritability. Rare coding variants with strong effects may account for missing heritability.
constraints: Focus on rare coding variants (MAF < 0.01) in 76 SLE-risk genes
Functional validation required for prioritized variants
input_data: WES/WGS data from SLE1 cohort (69 probands), SLE2 cohort (64 probands), and 97 healthy controls
Targeted PCR-amplifluor assay for BANK1 W40C in 150 SLE patients and 222 controls
quantifiable_goals: Identify rare coding variants in SLE-risk genes
Determine functional impact of BLK variants on kinase activity and IFNβ repression
Validate findings in CRISPR-edited B-cell lines and mouse models

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Common GWAS variants explain only a modest fraction of SLE heritability.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Missing heritability in SLE may be due to rare coding variants with strong effects.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Rare coding variants in lupus-risk genes contribute to SLE susceptibility.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Screen for rare coding variants in 76 SLE-risk genes using WES/WGS in SLE patients and controls.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Hypothesis formulated based on prior knowledge of missing heritability in complex diseases.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need to design a study to test the hypothesis by screening for rare variants.

evidence.quote_or_span: Another hypothesis is that in some cases SLE arises from rare genetic variants with strong effects.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: BLK prioritized for further study.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Frequency of rare BLK missense variants in SLE vs. other cohorts.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Rare BLK missense variants are more frequent in SLE patients than in other disease cohorts.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Compare BLK variant frequencies in SLE1, SLE2, CVID/CID, and healthy controls.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Rare BLK missense variants found in 10.1% of SLE1 and 10.9% of SLE2 patients, significantly less in CVID/CID (2.8%) but comparable to healthy controls (7.2%).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: High frequency in controls suggests need for functional assays to distinguish pathogenic variants.

evidence.quote_or_span: 10.1% of our original SLE patients (SLE1, n = 7 of 69) (Fig. 2a) and 10.9% of our replication cohort (SLE2, n = 7 of 64) had a rare or novel missense SNV in BLK.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: BLK variants identified, but some also found in controls.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Do BLK variants interact with variants in other genes to cause disease?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Epistasis between BLK and BANK1 variants contributes to SLE, as suggested by co-segregation in families.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Investigate co-segregation of BLK and BANK1 variants in families with autoimmune disease.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Two families showed co-segregation of rare BLK SNVs with rare BANK1 mutations (D400G and W40C).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Search for BANK1 W40C in additional SLE cohort to assess frequency.

evidence.quote_or_span: we considered the possibility of additional SLE rare variants acting in epistasis with BLK being present in subjects with disease.

Expression of the BLK variants with an IFNb dual luciferase reporter demonstrated that all tested BLK variants ( $BLK^{R131W}$ , $BLK^{R238Q}$ , and $BLK^{Y350H}$ ) were unable to repress IRF5-mediated IFNb activity compared to wild-type BLK (Fig.

evolution_trajectory.observation: Ramos cells with R131W showed impaired repression of IFNβ after R848 stimulation (p<0.05, p<0.01).

IFNβ luciferase activity 24 h after co-transfection of HEK293T cells with IRF5, MyD88, and indicated BLK variants.

evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: BANK1 forms a complex with TRAF6, and W40C may affect this interaction.

Coimmunoprecipitation of BANK1-V5 and FLAG-TRAF6 from HEK293T cells demonstrating a two-way interaction.

Indeed, when we searched for this variant in a third cohort comprised of 150 SLE patients (for which the DNA yield and quality was insufficient for WES, but adequate for a targeted polymerase chain reaction (PCR)-amplifluor assay), we identified the $BANK1^{W40C}$ SNV in 9 SLE probands (6%) and in 3 out of 222 unaffected controls (n = 3 of 222, 1.

success_verification.final_conclusion: Rare coding variants in BLK and BANK1 contribute to SLE by impairing kinase activity and IFNβ repression, with epistatic interactions. BLK variants from patients, but not controls, fail to repress IFNβ. BANK1 W40C is a loss-of-function variant that promotes type I IFN signaling by altering TRAF6 localization and IRF5 activation.
limitations: Mouse model with Blk R125W did not show increased type I IFN response, possibly due to species differences or environmental factors.
Incomplete penetrance of autoimmunity in heterozygous individuals suggests additional genetic or environmental factors.
Functional assays primarily in overexpression systems (HEK293T) may not fully recapitulate endogenous B-cell context.

risk_notes: Mouse model phenotype not included as a step due to negative result, but noted in limitations.
Some evidence IDs from auto_specific were used to supplement quotes, but all are from the same paper.

source.title: A Quantum-Inspired Method for Three-Dimensional Ligand-Based Virtual Screening

initial_request.research_problem: Improving ligand-based virtual screening by incorporating 3D molecular features into a graph-based similarity method to achieve higher early enrichment than conventional 2D fingerprints.
initial_request.known_context: Conventional 2D fingerprint methods for molecular similarity do not consider all relevant molecular features, particularly 3D structural information.
constraints: Computational cost of GMS method
Quantum annealer limited to 66 fully connected variables
Sensitivity to 3D conformation
input_data: Modified DUD-E dataset with 13 target classes
MACCS and Morgan fingerprints as baselines
quantifiable_goals: Higher early enrichment (awROCE at 0.5%) than 2D fingerprints
Best GMS setting CS9WS_B-5 achieves mean awAUC 0.65, AUC 0.67, awROCE 0.5% 43.39

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Conventional 2D fingerprint methods for molecular similarity do not consider all relevant molecular features, particularly 3D structural information.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: 2D fingerprints miss 3D structural information that could improve virtual screening enrichment.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Including 3D atomic coordinates in the graph-based molecular similarity (GMS) method will yield higher early enrichment values in virtual screening.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Proceed to design GMS method incorporating 3D coordinates.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Previous studies comparing 2D vs 3D methods showed 2D outperforming 3D, but this work hypothesizes that a graph-based approach with 3D features can reverse this trend.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Design the GMS method to test the hypothesis.

evidence.quote_or_span: While similarity methods using fingerprint encodings are efficient, they do not consider all the relevant aspects of molecular structure.
Our results show that using three-dimensional atomic coordinates as features for comparison yields higher early enrichment values.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Hypothesis that 3D coordinates improve enrichment.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need a method to represent molecules as graphs with 3D and pharmacophore features.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Modeling molecules as graphs with atoms/rings as vertices and bonds as edges, incorporating pharmacophore features and 3D coordinates, will enable effective similarity comparison.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Design GMS method with graph modeling including pharmacophore features and 3D coordinates.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Designed graph representation with features summarized in Table 1.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Formulate similarity as a maximum weighted co-k-plex problem.

evidence.quote_or_span: Individual atoms and ring structures in the molecule are represented by individual vertices in the graph.
The set of features considered in this work is summarized in Table 1.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Graph representation designed.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to define similarity between two molecular graphs allowing partial matches.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Formulating similarity as a maximum weighted co-k-plex on a conflict graph with 3D distance constraints and criticality will capture relevant molecular similarities.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Use conflict graph and maximum co-k-plex with criticality for partial matching.

tool_or_method.name: Conflict graph and maximum co-k-plex formulation

parameters.co-k-plex: allows k missing edges per vertex
parameters.criticality: designates features as critical or non-critical

evolution_trajectory.observation: Formulation incorporates 3D distance violations and criticality for flexible matching.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Set k=1 and define similarity metric.

evidence.quote_or_span: Given two molecular graphs, we are interested in finding the maximum co-k-plex of a third graph. This third graph is called a conflict graph
Distance violation aims to incorporate 3D molecular information
we introduce the concept of criticality for each of the features described in Table 1. One is able to designate a feature as being “critical” or “non-critical” based on screening requirements.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Co-k-plex formulation defined.

source.title: Engineered bidirectional promoters enable rapid multi-gene co-expression optimization

initial_request.research_problem: Efficient and tunable co-expression of multiple genes is limited by monodirectional promoters; natural bidirectional promoters are scarce and lack diverse expression profiles.
initial_request.target_object: bidirectional promoters (BDPs) for multi-gene co-expression in P. pastoris
initial_request.known_context: Monodirectional promoters (MDPs) are limited for multi-gene co-expression. Natural bidirectional promoters (nBDPs) exist but are scarce and poorly characterized.
constraints: Must function in P. pastoris
Need diverse expression strengths and ratios
Should maintain regulatory profiles
quantifiable_goals: Create a library of synthetic BDPs with wide expression range
Achieve variable expression ratios between sides
Surpass natural BDP efficiency

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Monodirectional promoters (MDPs) are limited for multi-gene co-expression.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Lack of diverse, tunable bidirectional promoters for multi-gene co-expression.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Natural BDPs can serve as a parts repository for engineering synthetic BDPs.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Leverage naturally occurring BDPs as a starting point.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Natural BDPs exist and could be mined for parts.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Identify and characterize natural BDPs in P. pastoris.

evidence.quote_or_span: Classically, monodirectional promoters (MDPs) have been used for such applications, but MDPs are limited in terms of multi-gene co-expression capabilities.
we generate a library of 168 synthetic BDPs in the yeast Komagataella phaffii (syn. Pichia pastoris), leveraging naturally occurring BDPs as a parts repository.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Hypothesis that natural BDPs can be used as parts.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which natural BDPs are suitable for engineering?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Bioinformatics can identify putative BDPs in P. pastoris genome.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform bioinformatics search and select a subset for characterization.
evolution_trajectory.observation: 1462 putative BDPs identified; 40 selected for detailed characterization.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Characterize the 40 selected natural BDPs.

evidence.quote_or_span: Bioinformatics approaches (Supplementary Data 1, Supplementary Note 1) identified 1462 putative BDPs in P. pastoris' genome (Fig. 1b), with a subset of 40 BDPs selected for detailed characterization

evolution_trajectory.state_before: 40 natural BDPs selected for characterization.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Do the selected natural BDPs have suitable expression properties?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Some natural BDPs will show bidirectional activity with useful expression strengths.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Characterize the 40 natural BDPs experimentally.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Majority of natural BDPs had limited engineering applicability (weak or monodirectional).
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Need alternative strategy; focus on histone core promoters.

evidence.quote_or_span: Though the majority of nBDPs mined provided limited engineering applicability
Bioinformatics approaches (Supplementary Data 1, Supplementary Note 1) identified 1462 putative BDPs in P. pastoris' genome (Fig. 1b), with a subset of 40 BDPs selected for detailed characterization

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Most natural BDPs unsuitable.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to create synthetic BDPs with diverse expression?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Short histone core promoters can be used as parts for bidirectionalization and creation of synthetic hybrid promoters.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Use histone core promoters as engineering templates.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Histone core promoters identified as excellent parts repository.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Design truncation/deletion strategies on histone promoter P_HHX2.

evidence.quote_or_span: these short histone core promoters turned out to be an excellent repository of parts for promoter bidirectionalization and the creation of synthetic hybrid promoters

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Histone core promoters chosen as parts.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How to diversify expression strengths and ratios from a single histone BDP?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Truncation and deletion of P_HHX2 will create variants with varied expression.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Construct a synthetic BDP library by truncating/deleting P_HHX2.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Library of 31 variants constructed.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Characterize expression of the variants.

evidence.quote_or_span: we utilized truncation and deletion strategies of $P_{HHX2}$ (Fig. 2c, d) to construct a synthetic BDP (sBDP) library with diversified expression strengths and ratios
The 31 variants generated from HHX2 histone promoter deletions (Fig. 2c, d) spanned a >15-fold range in cumulative expression levels and up to 39-fold expression ratio between sides.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: 31 HHX2 variants constructed.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: What is the expression range of the variants?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Variants will show a range of cumulative expression and expression ratios.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Measure fluorescence of reporter genes driven by each variant.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Variants spanned >15-fold range in cumulative expression and up to 39-fold expression ratio between sides.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Use insights to hypothesize bidirectionalization of MDPs.

evidence.quote_or_span: The 31 variants generated from HHX2 histone promoter deletions (Fig. 2c, d) spanned a >15-fold range in cumulative expression levels and up to 39-fold expression ratio between sides.
2c, d) spanned a >15-fold range in cumulative expression levels and up to 39-fold expression ratio between sides.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Histone BDP variants show tunable expression.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Can monodirectional promoters be converted to bidirectional?

evolution_trajectory.decision: Test bidirectionalization by fusing histone core promoters to MDPs.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Hypothesis formulated for experimental testing.

evidence.quote_or_span: We hypothesized that adding a short, non-regulated core promoter in reverse orientation upstream of an MDP could duplicate the expression and regulation of the native orientation

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Hypothesis for bidirectionalization of MDPs.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Does fusing core promoters to MDPs create functional BDPs?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Fusion of histone core promoters to MDPs will yield bidirectional promoters.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Fuse 6 histone core promoters to 12 MDPs with varying lengths.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Two thirds of the 30 constructs were successfully bidirectionalized.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Analyze expression properties of successful fusions.

evidence.quote_or_span: we fused 6 histone core promoters to 12 monodirectional P. pastoris promoters, partly varying the lengths of the core promoters and the MDPs (Fig. 3a). Two thirds of the 30 constructs were successfully bidirectionalized

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Fusion promoters created.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Do fusion promoters maintain original regulation and expression levels?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Fusion promoters will retain each side's original regulation and expression.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Measure expression ratios and regulatory profiles.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Fusion promoters generally maintained each side's original regulation and expression levels, with expression ratios between sides ranging from 0.16 to 0.96.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Investigate synergistic/antagonistic effects.

evidence.quote_or_span: These fusions generally maintained each side's original regulation and individual expression levels, allowing for the creation of variably regulated BDPs with a range of expression ratios between sides (0.16–0.96).

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Fusion promoters show maintained regulation.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Are there interactions between the fused promoters?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Some fusions may exhibit synergistic or antagonistic effects.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Compare expression of fusion promoters to individual promoters.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Some fusion variants showed synergistic effects (e.g., 1.8-fold increase for GAP-DAS2), while others showed antagonistic effects (e.g., 40% repression of HTA1-TAL2), suggesting transcriptional spillover.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Evaluate overall efficiency of hybrid BDPs.

evidence.quote_or_span: Some fusion variants showed synergistic effects, such as the 1.8-fold increase in the expression for a GAP-DAS2 fusion promoter. Others showed antagonistic effects, such as the 40% repression of a HTA1-TAL2 fusion promoter, suggesting a transcriptional “spillover” between promoters

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Fusion promoters show variable interactions.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: How do hybrid BDPs compare to natural BDPs and MDPs?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Hybrid BDPs will be more efficient per bp than natural BDPs.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Calculate expression strength per bp of promoter length.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Hybrid BDPs showed up to 3.3-fold higher expression strength per bp than nMDPs and 2.1-fold more efficient than the most efficient nBDP.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Compile final library and validate overall performance.

evidence.quote_or_span: Hybrid BDPs showed up to 3.3-fold higher expression strengths per bp of promoter length than typically used nMDPs and were 2.1-fold more efficient than the most efficient nBDP
1-fold and nMDPs up to 3.3-fold.
c Expression strengths per bp of promoter length of sBDPs exceed nBDPs up to 2.1-fold and nMDPs up to 3.

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Hybrid BDPs show improved efficiency.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Does the final library meet design goals?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: The library of 168 BDPs provides a wide range of expression and ratios.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Characterize the complete library of 168 BDPs.
evolution_trajectory.observation: The library covers a 79-fold range of cumulative expression, variable expression ratios from parity to 61-fold difference, and different regulatory profiles per side.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Library ready for application testing.

evidence.quote_or_span: 2b–d, 3, and 4) produced a versatile library of 168 BDPs offering (1) different regulatory profiles, (2) providing a 79-fold range of

Our library covers a 79-fold range of cumulative expression, has variable expression ratios ranging from parity to a 61-fold difference between sides, and combines different regulatory profiles per side including the possibility for consecutive induction.
cumulative expression, and (3) up to 61-fold expression ratio between sides, meeting the intended design requirements for our library (Fig.
b The library of BDPs offers different ratios between the two sides of the promoters, ranging from equal expression to a 61-fold difference.

validation_methods: Comparison to natural BDPs and MDPs
metrics: Expression strength per bp (up to 3.3-fold over nMDPs)
success_verification.final_conclusion: A library of 168 synthetic BDPs was created, providing a 79-fold range of cumulative expression, variable expression ratios up to 61-fold, and different regulatory profiles, surpassing natural BDPs in efficiency.
limitations: Properties of fusion promoters are difficult to predict due to potential transcriptional spillover and unknown insulator effects.

source.title: Creating a more robust 5-hydroxymethylfurfural oxidase by combining computational predictions with a novel effective library design

initial_request.research_problem: Engineering a more robust 5-hydroxymethylfurfural oxidase (HMFO) for industrial FDCA production by enhancing thermostability while maintaining activity.
initial_request.known_context: Wild-type HMFO has low thermostability (Tm_app = 48.5 °C) and inefficient final oxidation step, limiting industrial application.
constraints: Avoid active-site residues (within 5 Å of FAD) to preserve activity
quantifiable_goals: Increase thermostability (Tm_app)
Maintain or improve catalytic activity for FDCA production

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Wild-type HMFO has low thermostability (Tm_app = 48.5 °C) and inefficient final oxidation step.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need to identify stabilizing mutations without compromising activity.
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: FRESCO computational method can predict stabilizing mutations in HMFO by avoiding active-site residues.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Apply FRESCO to HMFO structure (PDB:4UDQ) to predict stabilizing point mutations.
evolution_trajectory.observation: FRESCO is a recently developed method for identifying thermostabilizing mutations.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Proceed to in silico screening of all possible point mutations.

evidence.quote_or_span: the enzyme displays only a modest stability
it is rather inefficient in the last step
we decided to enhance its thermostability using the recently developed FRESCO method: a computational approach to identify thermostabilizing mutations in a protein structure

The results of the ThermoFAD analysis showed that the 8xHMFO (I73V, H74Y, Q187E, G356H, V367L, T414K, A419Y, and A435E) was the most thermostable variant with an improvement of 13 °C compared with the WT ( $T_{m}^{app} = 48.5$ °C).

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Hypothesis that FRESCO can predict stabilizing mutations.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which specific point mutations are predicted to be stabilizing?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Mutations with predicted ΔΔG_Fold < -5 kJ/mol and favorable MD simulations will stabilize HMFO.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Perform in silico screening: model all point mutations (excluding residues within 5 Å of FAD), filter by FoldX and Rosetta free energy predictions, then MD simulations and visual inspection.
evolution_trajectory.observation: All possible point mutations were modeled excluding residues within 5 Å of FAD; those with predicted ΔΔG_Fold < -5 kJ/mol were submitted to MD simulations.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Experimentally test the selected single mutants for thermostability.

evidence.quote_or_span: all possible point mutations were modeled (excluding residues that are within 5 Å from the FAD cofactor)
The single point mutations with a predicted $\Delta\Delta G^{Fold} < -5$ kJ mol $^{-1}$ were subsequently submitted to MD simulations under Yasara as previously described [18, 19].

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Single mutants selected from computational screening.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which of these predicted mutations actually increase thermostability?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Some of the selected mutants will show increased Tm_app in ThermoFAD assay.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Express single point variants in E. coli and measure thermostability by ThermoFAD assay.

parameters.expression_system: E. coli BL21(DE3) with pET SUMO-HMFO
parameters.assay: ThermoFAD in phosphate buffer 50 mM pH 8.0

evolution_trajectory.observation: ThermoFAD assay was used to determine unfolding temperature of HMFO variants.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Select top mutations for combination based on ΔTm_app and activity.

evidence.quote_or_span: The single point variants were expressed in E. coli BL21(DE3) cells with the pET SUMO-HMFO vector.

The melting temperature of HMFO cell-free extract or purified variants was tested by the ThermoFAD method, which allows to determine the unfolding temperature based on the release of the flavin cofactor
$T_{m}^{app}$ values of HMFO variants (30 $\mu$ M) obtained by ThermoFAD in phosphate buffer 50 mM pH 8.0

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Stabilizing single mutants identified.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Which mutations to combine for additive stabilization without losing activity?
evolution_trajectory.hypothesis: Top 8 mutations (I73V, H74Y, Q187E, G356H, V367L, T414K, A419Y, A435E) will combine additively; I73V and H74Y are neighboring and may show additive effect.
evolution_trajectory.decision: Select top 8 mutations based on highest Tm_app and activity (vanillyl alcohol assay); test additivity of I73V and H74Y.
evolution_trajectory.observation: I73V and H74Y were combined to verify additive effect; all 8 mutations retained activity.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Design gene shuffling method to combine the 7 mutation sites (I73V-H74Y as one module).

evidence.quote_or_span: For the gene shuffling library, we chose the eight mutations which resulted in the highest $T_{m}^{app}$ values: I73V, H74Y, Q187E, G356H, V367L, T414K, A419Y, and A435E.
Before performing gene shuffling, the mutations I73V and H74Y, being neighboring residues, were combined to verify whether they display an additive effect.
Activity towards vanillyl alcohol of HMFO mutants I73V, H74Y, Q187E, G356H, V367L, T414K, A419Y, and A435E.
The HMFO mutants I73V, H74Y, Q187E, G356H, V367L, T414K, A419Y, A435E were tested using enzyme activity towards vanillyl alcohol as reported previously $[8]$ .

evolution_trajectory.state_before: Top 8 mutations selected; I73V-H74Y combined as one module.
evolution_trajectory.gap_or_uncertainty: Need a rapid method to combine 7 mutation sites (128 combinations) without step-wise mutagenesis.

evolution_trajectory.decision: Design two donor vectors (WT and 8-fold mutant) with 8 BsaI sites flanking 7 gene modules, and an acceptor vector; perform one-pot restriction-ligation.
evolution_trajectory.observation: Method designed to generate all 128 combinations in a single reaction.
evolution_trajectory.next_step_reason: Generate and analyze the shuffled library.

evidence.quote_or_span: we now developed a new and facile gene shuffling approach to rapidly combine stabilizing mutations in a one-pot reaction
The aim of the gene shuffling approach was to create a library of all the possible combinations (128) of the selected 7 sites
The Golden Gate gene shuffling which we developed for the FRESCO strategy is a one-pot reaction that involves only three designed vectors and a single restriction-ligation reaction
The first step was the design of two synthetic genes, one wild-type version and one with the selected 7 mutations; each containing 8 BsaI restriction sites that flank the 7 gene modules

one mutated region (the first module containing 2 mutations, and the 8 mutations are: I73V-H74Y, Q187E, G356H, V367L, T414K, A419Y, A435E) and the other synthetic gene with the same modules arrangement but without mutations, corresponding to the wild-type DNA sequence.

the thermocycler program was: incubation at 37 °C for 5 min and at 16 °C for 10 min repeated 50 times, followed by a final incubation at 50 °C for 10 min (final digestion) and at 80 °C for 10 min (enzyme inactivation).
The restriction-ligation reaction (5 $\mu$ L) were used to transform 100 $\mu$ L of chemically competent NEB 10 $\beta$ cells plated on 100 $\mu$ g mL $^{-1}$ ampicillin LB agar plates.
The hmfo variants were verified first by colony PCR (DreamTaq Green PCR Master Mix, Thermo Fisher) to determine the inserts size and then by sequencing.

The gene single point variants, the double mutant I73V H74Y, the 8AxHMFO, and the 8BxHMFO were obtained from pET SUMO-HMFO or pBAD SUMO-7xHMFO by whole-plasmid PCR with PfuUltra II Hotstart PCR Master Mix (Agilent).

Therefore, the best multiple mutant was considered to be the 7xHMFO mutant (I73V, H74Y, G356H, V367L, T414K, A419Y, and A435E) with a $T_{m}^{app}$ of 60.5 °C and $k_{cat}$ and $K_{m}$ values comparable with the wild-type enzyme (Table 1) [8].

success_verification.final_conclusion: 7xHMFO is a highly thermostable scaffold (Tm_app 60.5 °C) with WT-like activity; 8BxHMFO achieves >95% FDCA conversion in 9 h at 40 °C due to improved operational stability, despite lower catalytic efficiency.
